(7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Concept (2 marks):** - Arrays are **ordered, zero-indexed, heterogeneous** collections stored as objects with a `length` property. - Arrays are **dynamic**; they grow/shrink automatically. - Arrays are **reference types**; assignment copies the reference, not contents. ```javascript const arr = [10, 20, 30]; arr[0]; // 10 arr.length; // 3 arr[5] = 50; // creates a sparse array, length becomes 6 ``` **Mutating Methods (3 marks):** | Method | Purpose | Example | Result | |--------|---------|---------|--------| | `push(x)` | Add to end | `[1,2].push(3)` | `[1,2,3]`, returns new length `3` | | `pop()` | Remove from end | `[1,2,3].pop()` | `[1,2]`, returns `3` | | `shift()` | Remove from start | `[1,2,3].shift()` | `[2,3]`, returns `1` | | `unshift(x)` | Add to start | `[2,3].unshift(1)` | `[1,2,3]`, returns `3` | | `splice(i, n, ...items)` | Generic insert/remove | `[1,4].splice(1,0,2,3)` | `[1,2,3,4]` | **Non-mutating / Functional Methods (2 marks):** | Method | Purpose | Example | Result | |--------|---------|---------|--------| | `slice(start,end)` | Sub-array copy | `[1,2,3,4].slice(1,3)` | `[2,3]` | | `map(fn)` | Transform each | `[1,2,3].map(x => x*2)` | `[2,4,6]` | | `filter(fn)` | Keep matching | `[1,2,3,4].filter(x=>x%2===0)` | `[2,4]` | | `reduce(fn, init)` | Accumulate | `[1,2,3].reduce((a,b)=>a+b,0)` | `6` | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse `splice` (mutates original) with `slice` (does NOT mutate). KTU marks award 1 mark for explicitly mentioning the mutation behaviour. --- #### (b) What are expressions in JavaScript? Differentiate between expression statements and control statements. Evaluate the following step by step: ```javascript let a = 10, b = "5", c = true; let result = a + b - c && true || false; console.log(result); ``` (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Expressions (2 marks):** An *expression* is any unit of code that **resolves to a value**. JavaScript has 4 categories: primary, arithmetic, assignment, and conditional. **Expression Statement vs Control Statement (2 marks):** | Property | Expression Statement | Control Statement | |----------|----------------------|-------------------| | Returns a value? | Yes (the expression's value) | Usually no | | Purpose | Compute & apply side effects | Direct program flow | | Example | `counter++;` | `if(x>0) return;` | | Example | `x = 5 + 3;` | `for(let i=0;i<5;i++){}` | **Step-by-Step Evaluation (3 marks):** ```javascript let a = 10, b = "5", c = true; let result = a + b - c && true || false; ``` $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: } & a + b && \rightarrow 10 + \text{"5"} = \text{"105"} \quad \text{(string concat)}\\[4pt] \text{Step 2: } & \text{"105"} - c && \rightarrow 105 - 1 = 104 \quad \text{(numeric coercion by -)}\\[4pt] \text{Step 3: } & 104 \;\&\&\; \text{true} && \rightarrow \text{true} \quad \text{(104 is truthy, return RHS)}\\[4pt] \text{Step 4: } & \text{true} \;\|\|\; \text{false} && \rightarrow \text{true} \quad \text{(short-circuit)}\\[4pt] \text{Final: } & \mathbf{\text{true}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget that the `-` operator **always coerces** its operands to numbers, while `+` is overloaded. This 1-mark distinction is a favourite KTU differentiator. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist — Module 1** - ✅ A **statement** is a semicolon-terminated command; an **expression** resolves to a value. They are not the same. - ✅ Use `let` for mutable variables, `const` for immutable bindings; **avoid `var`** in modern code. - ✅ JavaScript has **7 primitive types** (`number, string, boolean, undefined, null, bigint, symbol`) and **1 object type**. - ✅ `typeof null === "object"` (a famous bug); `typeof NaN === "number"`; `NaN !== NaN`. - ✅ Always prefer **`===`** (strict) over `==` (loose) to avoid type-coercion bugs. - ✅ The `+` operator is **overloaded** for strings; the `-` operator is **not** (it always coerces to number). - ✅ **Operator precedence** rules determine evaluation order — exponentiation binds tighter than multiplication, which binds tighter than addition. - ✅ **Nullish coalescing `??`** differs from logical OR `||`: `??` only triggers on `null`/`undefined`, not on falsy values like `0` or `""`. - ✅ **Arrays are objects**; use `Array.isArray(x)` to test, not `typeof`. - ✅ Array methods `splice` and `slice` are different — splice mutates, slice returns a new array. - ✅ `push`/`pop` work at the **end**; `shift`/`unshift` work at the **start**; both mutate. - ✅ `map`, `filter`, `reduce` are **non-mutating** and return **new arrays / values** — favourites for KTU functional-programming questions. - ✅ **TDZ (Temporal Dead Zone)**: accessing a `let`/`const` before its declaration throws `ReferenceError`. - ✅ ASI (Automatic Semicolon Insertion) is unsafe; **always write `;` explicitly** in exam answers. - ✅ **Short-circuit logic**: `A && B` returns `A` if `A` is falsy, else returns `B`. `A || B` returns `A` if `A` is truthy, else returns `B`. > **End of Module 1 Notes — PBITT504 (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

topics.title: Functions, Methods, and Objects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Functions, Methods, and Objects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Functions in JavaScript

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
A **function** in JavaScript is a **first-class, reusable block of code** designed to perform a specific task. It is executed when *invoked* (called). Functions are treated as **objects** (Function objects), meaning they can be assigned to variables, passed as arguments, and returned from other functions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In JavaScript, functions are *first-class citizens* (or *first-class objects*). This is a core concept tested in Module 1 and is the foundation of **functional programming**, **closures**, and **asynchronous callbacks** in web development.

### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)
Think of a **function** as a **vending machine**.
- You insert something (input / *parameters* / *arguments*).
- You press a button (invoke / *call* the function).
- The machine processes the input using a fixed internal mechanism (the function *body*).
- It dispenses an output (a *return value*).

You do not need to know *how* the machine works internally — you just use the interface. Functions are the same: they hide complexity (encapsulation) and provide a clean, reusable interface.

```javascript
// Example: A simple function
function addNumbers(a, b) {
    return a + b; // returns the output
}
let sum = addNumbers(5, 3); // invocation
console.log(sum); // Output: 8
```

---

## 1.2 Methods in JavaScript

### Formal Definition
A **method** is a **function that is a property of an object** (i.e., a function stored as a value within an object). When a function is called as a property of an object, it is referred to as a method of that object, and the `this` keyword inside the method refers to the owning object.

### Conceptual Analogy
A **method** is like a **skill possessed by a person (object)**.
- The person is the *object* (e.g., `doctor`).
- The skill (e.g., `prescribeMedicine()`) is the *method*.
- When the doctor uses the skill, the doctor themselves (the `this` keyword) is implicitly involved.

```javascript
// Example: A method
const person = {
    firstName: "John",
    lastName: "Doe",
    fullName: function() { // method
        return this.firstName + " " + this.lastName;
    }
};
console.log(person.fullName()); // Output: "John Doe"
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Shorthand Method Syntax (ES6):** Modern JavaScript allows the shorthand `fullName() { ... }` instead of `fullName: function() { ... }`.

---

## 1.3 Objects in JavaScript

### Formal Definition
An **object** is a **collection of related data and/or functionality**, typically consisting of *named properties* (variables) and *methods* (functions). In JavaScript, almost everything is an object (or behaves like one), with the two primary exceptions being `null` and `undefined`.

### Conceptual Analogy
An **object** is like a **real-world entity described in an ID card**.
- The ID card holds information: *name, age, address* (these are **properties**).
- It also lists actions the person can perform: *sign, verify, update* (these are **methods**).
- A single ID card is one **object instance**; the *template* used to create it is the **class** (or **constructor**).

```javascript
// Example: An object literal
const car = {
    brand: "Toyota",
    model: "Camry",
    year: 2022,
    start: function() {
        return "Engine started";
    }
};
console.log(car.brand);     // Access property: "Toyota"
console.log(car.start());   // Invoke method: "Engine started"
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Point:** JavaScript uses **prototypal inheritance** (not classical inheritance like Java/C++). Objects inherit directly from other objects via the prototype chain. The ES6 `class` keyword is *syntactic sugar* over this prototype mechanism.

---

## 1.4 Summary Snapshot

| Concept | Core Identity | Triggered By | `this` Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Standalone, reusable code | Function name / variable | Global (or `undefined` in strict mode) |
| **Method** | Function as object property | `object.methodName()` | The owning object |
| **Object** | Container of `key: value` pairs | `{}` or `new` | Refers to the instance |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Functions in JavaScript (KTU Module 1 Focus)

JavaScript provides **four** primary ways to define functions. Each has distinct behavior regarding *hoisting*, *`this` binding*, and *syntax suitability*.

### A. Function Declaration (Statement)
- Hoisted completely (both name and definition).
- Has its own `this` binding (depending on caller).
- Can be used before declaration in code.

```javascript
hoistedFunction(); // Works because of hoisting
function hoistedFunction() {
    return "I am a function declaration";
}
```

### B. Function Expression
- NOT hoisted (variable is hoisted, but the assignment is not).
- Can be **named** (for recursion) or **anonymous**.
- Useful for callbacks.

```javascript
const greet = function(name) {
    return "Hello, " + name;
};
```

### C. Arrow Functions (ES6)
- Introduced in ES6; concise syntax.
- **Does NOT have its own `this`** — inherits from the enclosing lexical scope.
- Cannot be used as a constructor (no `new`).
- Has no `arguments` object.

```javascript
const add = (a, b) => a + b;
```

### D. Function Constructor (rare, unsafe)
- Creates functions from a string. Not recommended (security/performance issues).

```javascript
const subtract = new Function('a', 'b', 'return a - b;');
```

### E. IIFE — Immediately Invoked Function Expression
- Runs once at definition; creates a private scope.
- Used to avoid polluting the global scope.

```javascript
(function() {
    console.log("I run immediately!");
})();
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequent Question:** "Differentiate between function declarations and function expressions." — Focus on **hoisting** and **named vs. anonymous** behavior.

---

## 2.2 Object Creation Techniques (KTU High-Yield)

| Technique | Syntax | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Object Literal** | `let obj = { key: value };` | Simple, one-off objects |
| **Constructor Function** | `new ConstructorFn(args)` | Multiple similar instances |
| **Object.create()** | `Object.create(protoObj)` | Prototypal inheritance setup |
| **ES6 Class** | `class MyClass { ... }` | Modern OOP-style code |
| **Factory Function** | Returns an object literal | Functional programming style |

---

## 2.3 The `this` Keyword — Decision Table

The value of `this` depends on **how a function is called**, not where it is defined.

| Invocation Pattern | `this` Value |
|---|---|
| `fn()` (plain call) | Global object (`window` in browser) or `undefined` in strict mode |
| `obj.method()` | The `obj` |
| `new Constructor()` | The newly created instance |
| `fn.call(ctx)` / `fn.apply(ctx)` | The `ctx` argument (explicit binding) |
| `fn.bind(ctx)` | A new function permanently bound to `ctx` |
| Arrow function | Lexical `this` (inherited from enclosing scope) |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax Pattern | Hoisted? | Own `this`? | Can be Constructor? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Function Declaration | `function fn(){}` | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Function Expression | `const fn = function(){}` | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (if named) |
| Arrow Function | `const fn = () =>{}` | ❌ No | ❌ Lexical | ❌ No |
| Method (shorthand) | `{ fn(){} }` | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| Class Method | `class C { fn(){} }` | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| IIFE | `(function(){})()` | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |

| Access Operator | Syntax | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Dot notation** | `obj.key` | Standard property access |
| **Bracket notation** | `obj["key"]` | Dynamic / variable key access |

| Iteration Method | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `Object.keys(obj)` | Returns array of own enumerable keys |
| `Object.values(obj)` | Returns array of own enumerable values |
| `Object.entries(obj)` | Returns array of `[key, value]` pairs |
| `for...in` | Iterates over enumerable keys (incl. inherited) |
| `for...of` (with entries) | Iterates over values |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Single Page Applications (SPAs):** React/Angular components are essentially objects whose methods handle state, lifecycle, and event responses.
- **API Development:** Express.js routers use method chains (e.g., `app.get(path, handler)`) where handlers are functions/methods.
- **Asynchronous Web Operations:** Arrow functions are the de-facto standard for `.then()` and `.catch()` callbacks.
- **Modular Code:** Constructor functions and classes enable scalable, maintainable codebases for large teams.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — How a Method is Resolved at Runtime

Let us analyze the step-by-step mechanism of how JavaScript resolves a method call such as `user.greet()`. This logical breakdown is essential for understanding the `this` binding process.

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

**Given the code:**

```javascript
const user = {
    name: "Alice",
    greet: function() {
        return "Hello, " + this.name;
    }
};

const fn = user.greet;   // Step 1: Reference extraction
console.log(fn());       // Step 2: Plain function invocation
```

**Step 1 — Reference Extraction (Reference Type Creation)**
When `user.greet` is evaluated, JavaScript creates a **Reference Record** containing two parts:
- The base object: `user`
- The property name: `"greet"`

**Step 2 — Reference Assignment**
This reference is assigned to `fn`. The base object (`user`) is **not** stored in `fn` — only the function object is.

**Step 3 — Plain Invocation**
Calling `fn()` triggers a function reference with **no base object**. Therefore, the `this` value at call site is:
- In non-strict mode: the global object (`window` in browser).
- In strict mode: `undefined`.

**Resulting Runtime Trace:**

```javascript
function traceThis() {
    "use strict";
    return this;
}
console.log(traceThis()); // Output: undefined
```

**Final Output of the Original Code:**

```javascript
const fn = user.greet;
console.log(fn());
// Output: "Hello, undefined"   (strict mode)
// Output: "Hello, "             (non-strict — this.name becomes window.name)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** This is why **arrow functions** or **explicit `.bind(this)`** are often used in React class components to preserve `this` across event handlers.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Arrow vs. Regular Function `this`

**Code to analyze:**

```javascript
const obj = {
    value: 42,
    regular: function() { return this.value; },
    arrow:   ()      => { return this.value; }
};
console.log(obj.regular()); // ?
console.log(obj.arrow());   // ?
```

**Logical Deduction:**

For `obj.regular()`:
- Invocation: `obj.regular()` — base object is `obj`.
- `this` binding: `obj`.
- Output: `42`.

For `obj.arrow()`:
- Arrow functions **do not have their own `this`**.
- The lexical enclosing scope of the arrow function is the **module/global scope** (where the `obj` literal is defined).
- In strict mode, `this` at module top-level is `undefined`.
- Output: `undefined` (or throws a TypeError if `this.value` is accessed on `undefined`).

**Final Output Trace:**

```javascript
console.log(obj.regular()); // Output: 42
console.log(obj.arrow());   // Output: undefined  (or TypeError in strict mode)
```

**Summary Equation (KTU Exam Style):**

$$this_{method} = \text{BaseObject at call site}$$
$$this_{arrow} = this_{\text{lexical scope at definition}}$$

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Building an Object via Constructor

**Problem:** Create multiple `Student` objects with shared behavior.

**Step 1 — Define the Constructor Function:**

```javascript
function Student(name, rollNo) {
    this.name   = name;     // instance property
    this.rollNo = rollNo;   // instance property
}
```

**Step 2 — Add a Method to the Prototype:**

```javascript
Student.prototype.introduce = function() {
    return "I am " + this.name + ", Roll No: " + this.rollNo;
};
```

**Step 3 — Instantiate Objects:**

```javascript
const s1 = new Student("Anu", 1);
const s2 = new Student("Bala", 2);
```

**Step 4 — What happens when `new` is invoked (the 4-step algorithm):**

$$S_{new} = \begin{cases} 1. & \text{Create a new empty object } O. \\ 2. & \text{Set } O.\text{\_\_proto\_\_} = \text{Constructor.prototype}. \\ 3. & \text{Execute Constructor with } this = O. \\ 4. & \text{Return } O \text{ (if Constructor does not return an object explicitly).} \end{cases}$$

**Step 5 — Verify Prototype Chain:**

```javascript
console.log(s1.__proto__ === Student.prototype); // Output: true
console.log(s1.introduce());                     // Output: "I am Anu, Roll No: 1"
```

**Memory Efficiency Note:** All instances share **one copy** of `introduce` (in the prototype), rather than each instance having its own copy. This is the prototypal inheritance advantage tested in KTU exams.

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: ES6 Class Equivalence

The same logic using modern ES6 syntax:

```javascript
class Student {
    constructor(name, rollNo) {
        this.name   = name;
        this.rollNo = rollNo;
    }
    introduce() { // method on the class prototype
        return `I am ${this.name}, Roll No: ${this.rollNo}`;
    }
}
const s = new Student("Anu", 1);
console.log(s.introduce()); // Output: "I am Anu, Roll No: 1"
```

**Verification of Equivalence:**

$$Class_{ES6} \equiv \text{Constructor Function} + \text{Prototype Methods}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When asked "What is the difference between a class and a constructor function?", the answer must mention that **classes are NOT hoisted**, must be called with `new`, and have a `constructor` method for initialization.

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step: Object Destructuring (ES6 — KTU 2024 Relevant)

**Problem:** Extract multiple properties from an object efficiently.

**Step 1 — Traditional Approach:**

```javascript
const user = { name: "Anu", age: 21, city: "Kochi" };
const name = user.name;
const age  = user.age;
```

**Step 2 — Destructuring Approach:**

```javascript
const { name, age, city } = user;
console.log(name, age, city); // Output: "Anu" 21 "Kochi"
```

**Step 3 — Destructuring with Renaming:**

```javascript
const { name: userName, age: userAge } = user;
console.log(userName, userAge); // Output: "Anu" 21
```

**Step 4 — Destructuring with Default Values:**

```javascript
const { name, country = "India" } = user;
console.log(country); // Output: "India"
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Coverage:** Destructuring is part of ES6 syntax and is fair game for short-answer questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Function Definition & Invocation Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Programmer writes function declaration] --> B{Is it a Declaration or Expression?}
    B -- Declaration --> C[Function hoisted to top of scope]
    B -- Expression --> D[Variable hoisted as undefined, function assigned at runtime]
    C --> E[Function available for invocation]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Caller invokes function: fn args]
    F --> G[Create Execution Context]
    G --> H[Bind this keyword based on call site]
    H --> I[Execute function body line by line]
    I --> J{Is there a return statement?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Return value to caller]
    J -- No --> L[Return undefined implicitly]
    K --> M[Pop Execution Context from Call Stack]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Execution continues at call site]
```

---

## 4.2 Object–Method–`this` Relationship Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O[Object: user] -->|owns property| P1[name: 'Anu']
    O -->|owns property| P2[age: 21]
    O -->|owns method| M1[regular: function]
    O -->|owns method| M2[arrow: function]

    M1 -->|this is bound to| T1[user object]
    M2 -->|this is inherited from lexical scope| T2[Global or enclosing scope]
    T1 -->|accesses| P1
    T2 -->|does NOT access| P1

    classDef objStyle fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef methodStyle fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef thisStyle fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    class O objStyle
    class M1,M2 methodStyle
    class T1,T2 thisStyle
```

---

## 4.3 Prototype Chain Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Instance: s1] -->|__proto__| B[Student.prototype]
    A -->|has own properties| A1[name, rollNo]
    B -->|contains| B1[introduce method]
    B -->|__proto__| C[Object.prototype]
    C -->|contains| C1[hasOwnProperty, toString, valueOf]
    C -->|__proto__| D[null]

    classDef instStyle fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000
    classDef protoStyle fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#558b2f,color:#000
    classDef objProtoStyle fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    classDef nullStyle fill:#eeeeee,stroke:#616161,color:#000
    class A,A1 instStyle
    class B,B1 protoStyle
    class C,C1 objProtoStyle
    class D nullStyle
```

---

## 4.4 Object Creation Techniques Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need to create an object] --> Q1{One-off or Multiple instances?}
    Q1 -- One-off --> A1[Object Literal: {}]
    Q1 -- Multiple similar --> Q2{Need OOP style with inheritance?}
    Q2 -- Yes, modern --> A2[ES6 class with new]
    Q2 -- Yes, legacy --> A3[Constructor function with new]
    Q2 -- No, functional --> A4[Factory function returning {}]
    A2 --> Q3{Need prototypal inheritance only?}
    A3 --> Q3
    Q3 -- Yes --> A5[Object.create proto]
    Q3 -- No --> End[Use chosen approach]
    A1 --> End
    A4 --> End
    A5 --> End

    classDef startEnd fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef action fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    class Start,End startEnd
    class Q1,Q2,Q3 decision
    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5 action
```

---

## 4.5 ES6 Class vs. Constructor Function — Visual Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ClassApproach [ES6 Class Approach]
        C1[class Student] --> C2[constructor name, rollNo]
        C1 --> C3[introduce method on prototype]
    end

    subgraph ConstructorApproach [Constructor Function Approach]
        F1[function Student] --> F2[this.name, this.rollNo]
        F1 --> F3[Student.prototype.introduce]
    end

    ClassApproach ===|Equivalent Under The Hood| ConstructorApproach

    classDef classStyle fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,color:#000
    classDef funcStyle fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    class C1,C2,C3 classStyle
    class F1,F2,F3 funcStyle
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
Define a *function* in JavaScript. List any **two** differences between **function declarations** and **function expressions**.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **function** in JavaScript is a reusable block of code designed to perform a specific task, defined using the `function` keyword and invoked (called) by name. It can accept **parameters** and **return** a value.

| Aspect | Function Declaration | Function Expression |
|---|---|---|
| **Hoisting** | Fully hoisted (name + body) | Only variable is hoisted (as `undefined`) |
| **Syntax** | `function fn() {}` | `const fn = function() {};` |
| **Usage before definition** | Allowed | Throws `TypeError` (Cannot access before initialization) |
| **Naming** | Always named | May be anonymous or named |

**(1 mark for definition, 1 mark per difference = 3 marks)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
What is the significance of the `this` keyword inside a JavaScript method? How does it differ when used inside an **arrow function** vs. a **regular function**?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The `this` keyword inside a method refers to the **object that owns the method** (the base object on which the method was called). It allows methods to access the data (properties) of the object they belong to.

- **Regular function / method:** Has its **own `this`**, bound to the calling object (`obj.method()` → `this = obj`).
- **Arrow function:** Does **not** have its own `this`. It uses **lexical scoping** — `this` is inherited from the enclosing scope at the time of definition.

**(1 mark for `this` in method, 1 mark for regular function, 1 mark for arrow function = 3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the different ways to create objects in JavaScript with suitable examples. Compare object literals, constructor functions, and ES6 classes. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain how **prototypal inheritance** works in JavaScript. How does it differ from classical inheritance? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**1. Object Literal (2 marks)**
The simplest way to create a single object. Uses `{}` syntax with `key: value` pairs.

```javascript
const car = {
    brand: "Honda",
    model: "City",
    start: function() {
        return "Engine started";
    }
};
console.log(car.start());
```

**2. Constructor Function (2 marks)**
Used to create **multiple similar objects**. Convention: capitalize function name.

```javascript
function Car(brand, model) {
    this.brand = brand;
    this.model = model;
    this.start = function() {
        return "Engine started";
    };
}
const myCar = new Car("Honda", "City");
```

**3. ES6 Class (2 marks)**
Modern, cleaner syntax for OOP. Internally uses prototypes.

```javascript
class Car {
    constructor(brand, model) {
        this.brand = brand;
        this.model = model;
    }
    start() {
        return "Engine started";
    }
}
const myCar = new Car("Honda", "City");
```

**4. Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Feature | Object Literal | Constructor Function | ES6 Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| Syntax | `{}` | `function Fn(){}` | `class C {}` |
| Reusability | Low (one object) | High (multiple instances) | High (multiple instances) |
| Hoisting | No | Function only | Not hoisted |
| Method location | On object itself | On object itself (unless prototype) | On class prototype |

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Prototypal Inheritance — Definition (2 marks)**
In JavaScript, every object has a hidden property called `__proto__` (accessible via `Object.getPrototypeOf()`) that points to another object — its **prototype**. Objects inherit properties and methods from their prototype. This is called **prototypal inheritance**.

**Example (3 marks):**

```javascript
const animal = {
    isAlive: true,
    speak: function() {
        return "I make a sound";
    }
};

const dog = Object.create(animal);
dog.breed = "Labrador";
dog.bark = function() {
    return "Woof!";
};

console.log(dog.isAlive);  // Inherited: true
console.log(dog.speak());   // Inherited: "I make a sound"
console.log(dog.bark());    // Own: "Woof!"
console.log(dog.__proto__ === animal); // true
```

**How it works:**
- `dog` does not have `isAlive` or `speak` as own properties.
- JavaScript traverses the prototype chain: `dog → animal → Object.prototype → null`.
- The chain lookup finds `isAlive` and `speak` on `animal`.

**Difference from Classical Inheritance (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Classical (Java/C++) | Prototypal (JavaScript) |
|---|---|---|
| Basis | Class-based | Object-based |
| Inheritance | Class inherits from class | Object inherits from object |
| Copying | Copies fields/methods | Links via `__proto__` reference |
| Flexibility | Rigid hierarchy | Dynamic, can change prototype at runtime |

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** What is a **callback function**? Explain with a real-world example. How are **arrow functions** used as callbacks in modern JavaScript? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **call**, **apply**, and **bind** methods in JavaScript. Provide one example for each. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition (2 marks):**
A **callback function** is a function passed as an **argument** to another function, which is then **invoked inside the outer function** to complete some action. Callbacks are the foundation of asynchronous JavaScript (e.g., event handlers, `setTimeout`, API calls).

**Real-world Example (3 marks):**
Imagine ordering food at a restaurant:
- You place an order (call a function).
- The kitchen prepares it (some processing happens).
- When the food is ready, the waiter calls you back (the **callback** is invoked).
- You eat the food (the callback executes its logic).

```javascript
function processOrder(orderId, callback) {
    console.log("Processing order: " + orderId);
    setTimeout(function() {
        console.log("Order ready!");
        callback(orderId);
    }, 2000);
}

processOrder(101, function(id) {
    console.log("Serving order: " + id);
});
```

**Arrow Functions as Callbacks (2 marks):**
ES6 arrow functions provide concise syntax, ideal for short callbacks.

```javascript
const numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4];
const doubled = numbers.map((n) => n * 2);
console.log(doubled); // Output: [2, 4, 6, 8]

// In an event listener (browser context):
// document.getElementById("btn").addEventListener("click", () => {
//     console.log("Button clicked!");
// });
```

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**1. `call()` Method (2.3 marks)**
Invokes the function **immediately** with a given `this` value and arguments passed **individually**.

```javascript
function greet(greeting) {
    return greeting + ", " + this.name;
}
const person = { name: "Anu" };
console.log(greet.call(person, "Hello")); // Output: "Hello, Anu"
```

**2. `apply()` Method (2.3 marks)**
Invokes the function **immediately** with a given `this` value and arguments passed as an **array**.

```javascript
function introduce(greeting, city) {
    return greeting + ", I am " + this.name + " from " + city;
}
const user = { name: "Bala" };
console.log(introduce.apply(user, ["Hi", "Kochi"])); // Output: "Hi, I am Bala from Kochi"
```

**3. `bind()` Method (2.4 marks)**
Returns a **new function** permanently bound to the given `this` value (and optionally preset arguments). The function is **not** invoked immediately.

```javascript
const moduleObj = {
    x: 42,
    getX: function() { return this.x; }
};

const unboundGetX = moduleObj.getX;
console.log(unboundGetX()); // undefined (this lost)

const boundGetX = unboundGetX.bind(moduleObj);
console.log(boundGetX()); // Output: 42
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Confusing `call`/`apply`:** Students often interchange the argument styles. Remember: `call` uses **comma-separated** arguments, `apply` uses an **array**. (Lose 1 mark per mistake.)
> 2. **`bind` does NOT invoke:** Writing `boundGetX()` correctly is mandatory — many students write `moduleObj.getX.bind(moduleObj)()` and lose a mark for failing to explain that `bind` returns a new function.
> 3. **`this` in arrow functions:** Stating that arrow functions can be re-bound with `call`/`apply`/`bind` is **wrong** — arrow functions ignore explicit binding. (Heavy penalty if not clarified.)
> 4. **Hoisting of `class`:** Writing `new MyClass()` before the class declaration throws `ReferenceError` — classes are NOT hoisted like function declarations.
> 5. **Method without `()`:** Accessing `obj.method` (without parentheses) gives the **function reference**, not its return value. A common KTU trap in code-trace questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Functions, Methods, and Objects**

- 🔹 **Functions** are **first-class objects** in JavaScript — assignable, passable, returnable.
- 🔹 Four ways to define: **declaration, expression, arrow, constructor** — know their **hoisting** and **`this`** differences.
- 🔹 **Arrow functions** have **lexical `this`** — they **cannot** be used as constructors and **ignore** `call`/`apply`/`bind`.
- 🔹 **Methods** are functions stored as object properties; their `this` refers to the **calling object**.
- 🔹 **Objects** are `key: value` collections — use **dot** or **bracket notation** for access.
- 🔹 **Three main object creation methods:** object literal, constructor function (with `new`), ES6 `class` (with `new`).
- 🔹 **`new` keyword algorithm:** create object → link prototype → bind `this` → return object.
- 🔹 **Prototype chain** is the basis of inheritance in JS — traversal goes `obj → obj.__proto__ → Object.prototype → null`.
- 🔹 **ES6 `class`** is **syntactic sugar** over constructor functions + prototypes — it is **NOT hoisted**.
- 🔹 **Callbacks** are functions passed as arguments — they enable asynchronous behavior.
- 🔹 **`call` / `apply` / `bind`:** all set `this` explicitly; `call` and `apply` invoke immediately, `bind` returns a new function.
- 🔹 **Destructuring** (`const {a, b} = obj`) is the modern ES6 way to extract object properties.
- 🔹 **`Object.keys()` / `Object.values()` / `Object.entries()`** are essential enumeration utilities.
- 🔹 Strict mode (`"use strict";`) affects `this` in plain function calls (becomes `undefined`).
- 🔹 KTU 2024 emphasis: Be able to **trace code output** involving `this`, arrow vs. regular, and prototype chain lookups.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arrays of Objects and Objects in Arrays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Arrays of Objects and Objects in Arrays # 📘 Module 1: Arrays of Objects and Objects in Arrays ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > An **Array of Objects** is a homogeneous indexed collection (data structure) in which each element of the array is an **instance of an object (class/constructor)**. Conversely, an **Object in an Array** (more precisely, an *Object containing Arrays*) refers to an object whose property values are arrays. In JavaScript — the canonical KTU reference language for this module — both concepts are expressed through **object literals** and **array literals** since the language treats objects and arrays as **reference-type composite structures** stored on the **heap memory**. In classical Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) languages such as **Java, C++, and C#**, an *array of objects* literally means an array whose elements are heap-allocated objects instantiated from a class. In **JavaScript**, the equivalent is achieved through arrays holding object literals or constructor/prototype instances. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in KTU exams:** > The Web Application Development paper (PBITT504) tests this concept because nearly every real-world web response (API JSON, React `state`, database rows fetched via `fetch()`/`Axios`) is structurally an **Array of Objects**. Mastery of iteration, mutation, and transformation patterns on this structure is a **CO1 / CO2** (Apply / Analyze) cognitive skill. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Catalogue 📚 Imagine a **library catalogue card drawer**: - The **drawer itself** is the *Array* (an ordered, indexed collection). - Each **card inside the drawer** is an *Object* — containing multiple labelled fields such as `title`, `author`, `ISBN`, `year`, `genre`, `availableCopies`. So an *Array of Objects* = a drawer full of structured cards. The **drawer order** (index 0, 1, 2 …) gives positional access, while the **card labels** (keys) give semantic access to data fields. | Real-World Analogy | Programming Equivalent | |---|---| | Card Drawer | `Array` | | Single Card | `Object` | | Card Field (e.g., "Author") | Object Property (e.g., `book.author`) | | Position in Drawer | Array Index (e.g., `books[0]`) | | Cards-within-cards (e.g., a "Borrower Log" inside a card) | **Object containing Arrays** (Nested Structure) | > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Summary:** If you have a *list* of *structured records* — you almost certainly need an **Array of Objects**. --- ## 1.3 Memory Model & Reference Semantics In JavaScript (and Java/C++), arrays of objects do **not** store the object data *inside* the array slots. Instead: - The array stores **references (memory addresses / pointers)** to objects allocated on the **heap**. - Two different array slots can point to the **same** object → mutations through one are visible through the other (a classic exam pitfall). > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake (KTU Board):** Students often write `let arr2 = arr1` thinking it copies the array. In reality, both variables now point to the **same heap reference**. To deep-copy an array of objects, you must use `structuredClone()`, `JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(arr))`, or the spread operator **plus** property-level copying. --- ## 1.4 Distinguishing the Two Directions | Direction | Syntax Shape (JS) | Real Meaning | |---|---|---| | **Array of Objects** | `[ {…}, {…}, {…} ]` | A list where each entry is a structured record. | | **Object in Array** | Same syntax (viewpoint differs) | Focus is on *one specific object* that lives inside a list. | | **Object containing Arrays** | `{ skills: ["JS","HTML"], marks: [90,85] }` | A single record that holds lists as field values. | | **Nested / Hybrid** | `[{tags:["a","b"]}, {tags:["c"]}]` | Array of objects, where each object internally has arrays. | > [!NOTE] > The phrase **"Objects in Arrays"** in the KTU 2024 module descriptor is best interpreted as the *Array of Objects* pattern, but the discussion naturally extends to **Object of Arrays** and **Nested hybrid** forms, all of which appear in `JSON` payloads. # 🔬 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Theory — Anatomy of an Array of Objects Let us dissect the structure layer by layer. ### 2.1.1 The Object Layer An object in JavaScript is a **collection of key-value pairs** (also called *properties*). Each key is a string (or Symbol), and each value can be a primitive (`number`, `string`, `boolean`, `null`, `undefined`) or another reference type (object, array, function). $$ \text{Object} \equiv \{(k_1, v_1),\ (k_2, v_2),\ \dots,\ (k_n, v_n)\} $$ where each $k_i$ is a unique property name and $v_i$ is its value. ### 2.1.2 The Array Layer An array is an **ordered, integer-indexed** collection. The index set is: $$ I = \{0, 1, 2, \dots, n-1\} \quad \text{for an array of length } n $$ ### 2.1.3 Composition — Array of Objects Combining both: $$ A = [O_0,\ O_1,\ O_2,\ \dots,\ O_{n-1}] $$ where each $O_i$ is an object reference, and the array stores these references in contiguous memory slots (in low-level languages) or in a hash-indexed structure (JavaScript engines optimize this internally). ### 2.1.4 Composition — Object Containing Arrays A dual structure: $$ O = \{ p_1 : A_1,\ p_2 : A_2,\ \dots,\ p_m : A_m \} $$ where each property $p_i$ maps to an array $A_i$. --- ## 2.2 Why and How — The Engineering Rationale - **Why use Array of Objects?** - To represent **tabular/relational data** (rows of a database table). - To model **collections of similar entities** (list of users, products, posts). - To enable **batch processing** via iteration methods (`forEach`, `map`, `filter`). - **How does the JavaScript engine store it?** - V8 (and other engines) store array elements in a **linear backing store** for small homogeneous arrays (PACKED_SMI_ELEMENTS, PACKED_DOUBLE_ELEMENTS, PACKED_ELEMENTS). - When an array contains **objects**, it transitions to `PACKED_ELEMENTS` containing **pointers** to the actual objects on the heap. - Property access `arr[i].prop` is a **two-step dereference**: first index into the array to fetch the pointer, then dereference the pointer and look up the property. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Operation | Syntax (JavaScript) | Time Complexity | Returns | |---|---|---|---| | Create empty array of objects | `let arr = [];` | $O(1)$ | New array | | Add object to end | `arr.push(obj);` | $O(1)$ amortized | New length | | Remove from end | `arr.pop();` | $O(1)$ | Removed object | | Insert at start | `arr.unshift(obj);` | $O(n)$ | New length | | Access by index | `arr[i]` | $O(1)$ | Object reference | | Access property | `arr[i].key` or `arr[i]["key"]` | $O(1)$ | Value | | Iterate (traditional) | `for (let i=0; i …)` | $O(n)$ | — | | Transform | `arr.map(obj => newObj)` | $O(n)$ | New array | | Filter | `arr.filter(obj => condition)` | $O(n)$ | New (sub)array | | Reduce | `arr.reduce((acc, obj) => …, init)` | $O(n)$ | Accumulated value | | Find first match | `arr.find(obj => condition)` | $O(n)$ | Object or `undefined` | | Check existence | `arr.some(obj => condition)` | $O(n)$ | Boolean | | Check all | `arr.every(obj => condition)` | $O(n)$ | Boolean | | Sort by property | `arr.sort((a,b) => a.key - b.key)` | $O(n \log n)$ | Sorted array (in-place) | | Deep clone | `structuredClone(arr)` | $O(n)$ | New deep copy | | JSON round-trip clone | `JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(arr))` | $O(n)$ | New deep copy (lossy for dates/functions) | | Spread shallow copy | `[...arr]` | $O(n)$ | New array (objects shared) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** The notation $O(n)$ means *linear time*. For an array of $n$ objects each having $k$ properties, a full traversal + property access is $O(n \cdot k)$. Most KTU questions that ask "complexity" expect the **dominant term** — state it in Big-O. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Use Case | |---|---| | **Frontend Frameworks (React, Angular, Vue)** | Component lists rendered from API data | | **Backend APIs (REST, GraphQL)** | JSON responses are almost always arrays of objects | | **Databases (MongoDB)** | Documents are objects; collections are arrays of objects | | **Data Science (Pandas DataFrames)** | Each row is an object; the DataFrame is an array of objects | | **Configuration Files (JSON/YAML)** | Hierarchical nested structures mix both patterns | | **Testing (Jest, Mocha)** | Test suites are arrays of test-case objects | --- ## 2.5 Reference vs. Value — The Critical Distinction In JavaScript: - **Primitives** (`number`, `string`, `boolean`, `null`, `undefined`, `symbol`, `bigint`) are passed **by value**. - **Objects and Arrays** are passed **by reference**. $$ \text{When } A \text{ and } B \text{ are objects},\ A === B \iff \text{they reference the same heap location}. $$ This produces the **aliasing effect**: $$ A = [\{x:1\}];\ B = A; \quad \Rightarrow \quad B[0].x = 5;\ A[0].x === 5 \ \text{(true)} $$ > [!WARNING] > In KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) questions, the examiner *deliberately* tests this. Marks are lost when students mutate a "copy" that is in fact a shared reference. --- ## 2.6 Mutation vs. Immutability Patterns - **Mutating methods:** `push`, `pop`, `shift`, `unshift`, `splice`, `sort`, `reverse`. - **Non-mutating methods:** `map`, `filter`, `slice`, `concat`, `reduce`, `find`, `every`, `some`, `forEach` (forEach does not mutate, but the callback can). > [!TIP] > Modern JavaScript favours **immutability** (especially in React). Always prefer the non-mutating trio: `map` / `filter` / `spread` over `push` / `splice` / `sort`. # 🧮 3. Step-by-Step Derivation, Code Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > Since PBITT504 is a **Computer Science / Web Development** course, code walkthroughs are **explicitly permitted** under the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 *adaptive complexity* rule. All derivations here are pedagogical; the focus is on operational semantics, not on syntax memorization. ## 3.1 Construction — Building an Array of Objects (JS) ### Step 1: Define a class / constructor (Optional, but recommended for clarity) ```javascript class Student { constructor(rollNo, name, marks) { this.rollNo = rollNo; this.name = name; this.marks = marks; // array inside the object } } ``` **Explanation of logic:** - `class Student` declares a blueprint. - The `constructor` is invoked with `new Student(...)`. - `this.rollNo`, `this.name`, `this.marks` attach three properties to the newly allocated heap object. - `this.marks` is itself an array — this gives us an **Object containing an Array** (the *Object of Arrays* pattern, in reverse nesting). ### Step 2: Instantiate and push into the array ```javascript let students = []; // Step 2a: create empty array (heap allocation) students.push(new Student(1, "Arjun", [85, 90, 78])); // Step 2b students.push(new Student(2, "Meera", [92, 88, 95])); // Step 2c students.push(new Student(3, "Rahul", [70, 75, 80])); // Step 2d ``` **Logic behind each line:** - `let students = []` → allocates a new empty array on the heap. The reference is stored in the stack variable `students`. - `new Student(...)` → calls the constructor, allocates a new object on the heap, returns a reference. - `students.push(...)` → appends that reference to the next free slot. Array length becomes $3$. ### Step 3: Verify the structure ```javascript console.log(students); // [ // Student { rollNo: 1, name: 'Arjun', marks: [85, 90, 78] }, // Student { rollNo: 2, name: 'Meera', marks: [92, 88, 95] }, // Student { rollNo: 3, name: 'Rahul', marks: [70, 75, 80] } // ] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > `students` is an **array of objects**, where each object is also an **object containing an array** (`marks`). This is a **hybrid nested structure** — extremely common in KTU exam questions. --- ## 3.2 Accessing Elements — Indexing & Property Lookup ### Derivation of Access Pattern For an array $A$ of length $n$ and an object $O_i$ at index $i$: $$ A[i] \rightarrow O_i \rightarrow O_i[\text{key}] $$ This is a **two-step pointer dereference**. ### Code Walkthrough ```javascript // (a) Access the second student object let second = students[1]; // → Student { rollNo: 2, name: 'Meera', marks: [92, 88, 95] } // (b) Access the name property of the first student let firstName = students[0].name; // → "Arjun" // (c) Access the second mark of the third student let mark = students[2].marks[1]; // → 75 // (d) Use bracket notation (dynamic key) let key = "rollNo"; let r = students[1][key]; // → 2 ``` **Logic notes:** - `students[0]` returns the reference to the first `Student` object. - Chaining `.name` accesses the `name` property on that object. - Chaining `.marks[1]` performs **three dereferences**: array → object → inner array → element. --- ## 3.3 Iteration Patterns — The KTU-Favourite Section ### Pattern 1: Classical `for` loop ```javascript for (let i = 0; i < students.length; i++) { console.log(`Roll ${students[i].rollNo}: ${students[i].name}`); } ``` - **Logic:** Loop variable `i` walks indices $0, 1, 2$. At each step, the object at $A[i]$ is fetched, and properties are read. - **Complexity:** $O(n)$ where $n$ is the array length. ### Pattern 2: `for...of` (value-based) ```javascript for (const student of students) { console.log(student.name, "->", student.marks.join(", ")); } ``` - **Logic:** `student` is bound to each object reference in sequence. The `of` operator uses the array's **iterator protocol** internally. ### Pattern 3: `for...in` on the object (key-based) ```javascript for (const key in students[0]) { console.log(key, "=", students[0][key]); } ``` - **Logic:** Iterates enumerable property names of the *object*, not the array index. - ⚠️ Not used to iterate the array itself. ### Pattern 4: `forEach` ```javascript students.forEach((student, index) => { console.log(`${index}: ${student.name}`); }); ``` ### Pattern 5: `map` → Transform to a new array ```javascript let names = students.map(s => s.name); // → ["Arjun", "Meera", "Rahul"] ``` ### Pattern 6: `filter` → Subset ```javascript let toppers = students.filter(s => { let avg = s.marks.reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0) / s.marks.length; return avg >= 85; }); ``` ### Pattern 7: `reduce` → Aggregate ```javascript let classAverage = students.reduce((sum, s) => { let total = s.marks.reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0); return sum + total / s.marks.length; }, 0) / students.length; ``` ### Pattern 8: `find` → First match ```javascript let meera = students.find(s => s.name === "Meera"); ``` ### Pattern 9: `sort` → In-place ordering ```javascript students.sort((a, b) => a.rollNo - b.rollNo); ``` ### Pattern 10: `some` and `every` ```javascript let anyFail = students.some(s => s.marks.some(m => m < 40)); let allPass = students.every(s => s.marks.every(m => m >= 40)); ``` --- ## 3.4 Derivation of Deep Copy vs. Shallow Copy Let $A = [O_0, O_1]$ where each $O_i$ is an object. **Shallow copy** (spread / `Array.from` / `slice`): $$ B = [...A] \implies B[0] \equiv A[0] \ \text{(same reference)} $$ A mutation to $B[0].x$ is visible through $A[0].x$. **Deep copy** (`structuredClone` / JSON round-trip): $$ B = \text{structuredClone}(A) \implies B[0] \not\equiv A[0] \ \text{(fresh object)} $$ ### Demonstration ```javascript let original = [{ a: 1, nested: { b: 2 } }]; // Shallow copy let shallow = [...original]; shallow[0].a = 99; console.log(original[0].a); // → 99 (aliasing!) shallow[0].nested.b = 88; console.log(original[0].nested.b); // → 88 (nested object also shared) // Deep copy let deep = structuredClone(original); deep[0].a = 100; console.log(original[0].a); // → 99 (unchanged) deep[0].nested.b = 77; console.log(original[0].nested.b); // → 88 (unchanged) ``` **Logical explanation:** - Spread (`...`) copies the **top-level array slots** but the slot *values* (object references) are duplicated only as pointers, not as the objects they point to. - `structuredClone` recursively walks the entire object graph and allocates new memory for every nested structure, producing a fully independent copy. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Operation Behaviour Summary | Operation | Mutates Original? | Creates New Array? | Touches Nested Objects? | KTU Probability | |---|---|---|---|---| | `push` | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | N/A | ⭐⭐⭐ | | `pop` | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | N/A | ⭐⭐ | | `map` | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | Only via callback | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | `filter` | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | Only via callback | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | `reduce` | ❌ No | ❌ No | Yes | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | `sort` | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | N/A | ⭐⭐⭐ | | `forEach` | ❌ No (can via callback) | ❌ No | Yes (via callback) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | Spread `[...arr]` | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (shallow) | ❌ No | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | `structuredClone(arr)` | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (deep) | ✅ Yes | ⭐⭐⭐ | --- ## 3.6 Worked Example — Object Containing Arrays (Reverse Pattern) ```javascript let department = { name: "CSE", hod: "Dr. Iyer", faculty: ["Asha", "Binu", "Chitra"], labs: ["OS Lab", "Networks Lab", "AI Lab"] }; console.log(department.faculty[1]); // → "Binu" department.faculty.push("Deepak"); console.log(department.faculty.length); // → 4 ``` **Stepwise logic:** - `department` is a single object allocated on the heap. - The properties `faculty` and `labs` hold array references. - `department.faculty[1]` dereferences: object → array property → index 1 → value `"Binu"`. - `.push("Deepak")` mutates the array in place (the object property now references an array of length 4). > [!NOTE] > The KTU 2024 syllabus often asks: *"Differentiate between an array of objects and an object containing arrays."* — Use the comparison above to construct a 2-column answer. # 🗺️ 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Memory Layout — Array of Objects (Pointer Model) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Stack Variable: students] --> B[Heap: Array Object] B --> B0[Index 0: Pointer to Obj0] B --> B1[Index 1: Pointer to Obj1] B --> B2[Index 2: Pointer to Obj2] B0 --> C0[Heap: Student Object 0
rollNo:1 name:Arjun marks:ref] B1 --> C1[Heap: Student Object 1
rollNo:2 name:Meera marks:ref] B2 --> C2[Heap: Student Object 2
rollNo:3 name:Rahul marks:ref] C0 --> D0[Heap: Inner Array
85 90 78] C1 --> D1[Heap: Inner Array
92 88 95] C2 --> D2[Heap: Inner Array
70 75 80] ``` **Interpretation:** - The stack variable holds a single reference to the array object on the heap. - The array object contains three slots, each holding a **pointer** to a `Student` object (also on the heap). - Each `Student` object, in turn, holds a pointer to its inner `marks` array. - Mutations to any inner object are visible through *all* references that point to it. --- ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — CRUD on Array of Objects ```mermaid flowchart TD R[REST API / Database] --> P[Parser: JSON.parse] P --> S[In-memory Array of Objects] S --> C1[CREATE: arr.push newObj] S --> R1[READ: arr.find filter] S --> U1[UPDATE: arr.map to new values] S --> D1[DELETE: arr.filter to exclude] C1 --> S2[Updated Array] R1 --> S2 U1 --> S2 D1 --> S2 S2 --> V[View: render to DOM / Template] S2 --> T[Transform: map reduce sort] S2 --> P2[Persistence: JSON.stringify for storage] ``` **Reading the diagram:** - Data flows from a source (API/DB) into an in-memory **Array of Objects**. - Four CRUD operations (Create, Read, Update, Delete) act on the same data structure. - The result is either rendered to a view, transformed, or persisted back. --- ## 4.3 Hybrid Structure — Array of Objects Containing Arrays ```mermaid graph TD subgraph OuterArray[Outer Array: students] direction TB S0[Object: Arjun] S1[Object: Meera] S2[Object: Rahul] end S0 --> M0[marks: 85 90 78] S0 --> R0[rollNo: 1] S0 --> N0[name: Arjun] S1 --> M1[marks: 92 88 95] S1 --> R1[rollNo: 2] S1 --> N1[name: Meera] S2 --> M2[marks: 70 75 80] S2 --> R2[rollNo: 3] S2 --> N2[name: Rahul] ``` This block-level architecture shows the **two-level nesting**: - **Level 1:** Outer array `students` indexed $0, 1, 2$. - **Level 2:** Each object has scalar fields (`name`, `rollNo`) and an array field (`marks`). --- ## 4.4 State Transition — Shallow vs. Deep Copy ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SHALLOW[Shallow Copy via Spread] A1[Original Array] -->|arr0 ptr| O1[Object X] A1 -->|arr1 ptr| O2[Object Y] A2[Copied Array] -->|arr0 ptr| O1 A2 -->|arr1 ptr| O2 end subgraph DEEP[Deep Copy via structuredClone] A3[Original Array] -->|arr0 ptr| O3[Object X original] A3 -->|arr1 ptr| O4[Object Y original] A4[Copied Array] -->|arr0 ptr| O5[Object X clone] A4 -->|arr1 ptr| O6[Object Y clone] end ``` **Interpretation:** - In the **shallow** case, the original and copy share *the same* object instances. - In the **deep** case, the copy has *entirely new* object instances — no aliasing. --- ## 4.5 Iteration Method Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Q{Need to transform
each element?} Q -->|Yes| Q1{Need a new array?} Q1 -->|Yes| M[Use map] Q1 -->|No, in-place| F[Use forEach and mutate] Q -->|No| Q2{Need to select subset?} Q2 -->|Yes| F2[Use filter] Q2 -->|No| Q3{Need to find one?} Q3 -->|Yes| F3[Use find] Q3 -->|No| Q4{Need to aggregate?} Q4 -->|Yes| R[Use reduce] Q4 -->|No| Q5{Need to test condition?} Q5 -->|Yes, any| SM[Use some] Q5 -->|Yes, all| EV[Use every] ``` Use this decision tree during KTU viva to justify the choice of iteration method. # 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] **Differentiate between an *array of objects* and an *object containing arrays*. Give a JavaScript example for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Array of Objects | Object Containing Arrays | |---|---|---| | Definition | A list/array whose elements are objects. | A single object whose properties hold arrays. | | Shape | `[ {…}, {…}, {…} ]` | `{ key1: […], key2: […] }` | | Use Case | Tabular data, list of records | Configuration, grouped data | | Access | `arr[i].prop` | `obj.key[i]` | **JavaScript Examples:** ```javascript // Array of Objects let products = [ { id: 1, name: "Pen", price: 10 }, { id: 2, name: "Book", price: 50 } ]; // Object containing Arrays let store = { storeName: "Kerala Books", categories: ["Stationery", "Books", "Toys"], prices: [10, 50, 200] }; ``` *(Award full marks only if both definitions AND examples are provided.)* --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] **Explain the difference between *shallow copy* and *deep copy* of an array of objects in JavaScript. Why does the spread operator (`...`) not produce a deep copy?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Shallow copy** duplicates only the **top-level array slots**. The element values — which are object references — are copied as pointers, not as the objects themselves. Hence, the original and the copy *share* nested objects. - **Deep copy** recursively duplicates **every nested object**, producing an entirely independent clone with no shared references. - The spread operator (`[...arr]`) only iterates the array's top-level elements and copies their values. Since the values are reference types (objects), only the references are copied — yielding a **shallow** copy. - To deep-copy, use `structuredClone(arr)` or `JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(arr))` (with caveats for dates/functions). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > [!IMPORTANT] > As per **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern**, Module 1 questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Each question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts. --- ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) [Module 1 Choice – Set 1] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **With a suitable JavaScript program, demonstrate the creation, access, and iteration of an array of objects. Use the example of a `library` containing `Book` objects with properties `title`, `author`, `year`, and `available`.** **Model Solution:** ```javascript // (i) Creation class Book { constructor(title, author, year, available) { this.title = title; this.author = author; this.year = year; this.available = available; } } let library = [ new Book("The Alchemist", "Paulo Coelho", 1988, true), new Book("Wings of Fire", "APJ Abdul Kalam", 1999, true), new Book("Introduction to Algorithms", "CLRS", 1990, false) ]; // (ii) Access console.log("First book title:", library[0].title); console.log("Third book author:", library[2]["author"]); // (iii) Iteration console.log("\n--- All Books ---"); library.forEach((book, i) => { console.log(`${i+1}. "${book.title}" by ${book.author} (${book.year}) — ${book.available ? "Available" : "Issued"}`); }); // (iv) Filter to find available books let availableBooks = library.filter(b => b.available); console.log("\nAvailable books count:", availableBooks.length); // (v) Map to titles only let titles = library.map(b => b.title); console.log("All titles:", titles); ``` **Explanation of logic (7 marks distribution):** 1. Class definition with constructor — 2 marks. 2. Array instantiation with multiple objects — 1 mark. 3. Property access by index and by key — 1 mark. 4. Iteration using `forEach` — 1 mark. 5. Filter and map demonstration — 2 marks. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Consider the following array of objects representing students:** ```javascript let students = [ { name: "Arjun", marks: [85, 90, 78] }, { name: "Meera", marks: [92, 88, 95] }, { name: "Rahul", marks: [70, 75, 80] } ]; ``` **Write JavaScript code to:** 1. Compute the **average marks** of each student and store it in a new array of objects `{name, average}`. 2. Find the **topper** (student with highest average). 3. **Sort** the original array by total marks in descending order. **Model Solution:** ```javascript // (1) Compute averages let withAvg = students.map(s => { let total = s.marks.reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0); let avg = total / s.marks.length; return { name: s.name, average: avg }; }); console.log("With averages:", withAvg); // → [ // { name: 'Arjun', average: 84.33 }, // { name: 'Meera', average: 91.67 }, // { name: 'Rahul', average: 75.00 } // ] // (2) Find topper let topper = withAvg.reduce((best, curr) => curr.average > best.average ? curr : best ); console.log("Topper:", topper); // → { name: 'Meera', average: 91.67 } // (3) Sort by total marks descending let sorted = [...students].sort((a, b) => { let totalA = a.marks.reduce((x, y) => x + y, 0); let totalB = b.marks.reduce((x, y) => x + y, 0); return totalB - totalA; }); console.log("Sorted by total:", sorted); // → Meera (275), Arjun (253), Rahul (225) ``` **Logic walkthrough (7 marks):** - `map` + `reduce` for averages — 2 marks. - `reduce` for topper identification — 2 marks. - `sort` with comparator using `reduce` for totals — 2 marks. - Use of spread (`...`) to avoid mutating original — 1 mark. --- ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) [Module 1 Choice – Set 2 — Alternative] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain the concept of *reference semantics* in JavaScript. With a code example, show how two different array variables can refer to the *same* object, and demonstrate the aliasing effect. How would you correctly create a deep copy?** **Model Solution:** **Concept (2 marks):** In JavaScript, arrays and objects are stored on the heap, and variables hold *references* (memory addresses) to them. Assigning one array variable to another does **not** copy the contents — it copies only the reference. **Code demonstrating aliasing (3 marks):** ```javascript let original = [ { id: 1, value: "A" }, { id: 2, value: "B" } ]; // Aliasing — both point to the SAME array of objects let alias = original; alias[0].value = "Z"; console.log(original[0].value); // → "Z" (proves aliasing!) console.log(original === alias); // → true (same reference) // Shallow copy still shares nested objects let shallow = [...original]; shallow[0].value = "Y"; console.log(original[0].value); // → "Y" (still aliased at object level) ``` **Deep copy (2 marks):** ```javascript let deep = structuredClone(original); deep[0].value = "Fresh"; console.log(original[0].value); // → "Y" (unchanged) console.log(deep[0].value); // → "Fresh" ``` `structuredClone` recursively copies every nested structure, eliminating aliasing. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Consider an object `classroom` that contains arrays of student names, their subject marks, and attendance records. Demonstrate, with code, how to:** 1. Compute the **class average** in each subject. 2. Find students with **attendance below 75%**. 3. Generate a new object containing the **topper of each subject**. ```javascript let classroom = { className: "S5 CSE-A", students: ["Arjun", "Meera", "Rahul", "Diya"], subjects: ["Maths", "DS", "OS"], marks: [ // [Maths, DS, OS] per student [85, 90, 78], // Arjun [92, 88, 95], // Meera [70, 75, 80], // Rahul [95, 92, 89] // Diya ], attendance: [82, 95, 68, 90] // percentage }; ``` **Model Solution:** ```javascript // (1) Class average per subject let subjectAverages = classroom.subjects.map((sub, j) => { let total = classroom.marks.reduce((sum, stuMarks) => sum + stuMarks[j], 0); let avg = total / classroom.students.length; return { subject: sub, average: avg.toFixed(2) }; }); console.log("Subject averages:", subjectAverages); // → [ // { subject: 'Maths', average: '85.50' }, // { subject: 'DS', average: '86.25' }, // { subject: 'OS', average: '85.50' } // ] // (2) Students with attendance < 75% let lowAttendance = classroom.students .map((name, i) => ({ name, att: classroom.attendance[i] })) .filter(s => s.att < 75); console.log("Low attendance:", lowAttendance); // → [{ name: 'Rahul', att: 68 }] // (3) Topper of each subject let subjectToppers = classroom.subjects.map((sub, j) => { let topIdx = classroom.marks.reduce((bestIdx, stuMarks, i) => stuMarks[j] > classroom.marks[bestIdx][j] ? i : bestIdx , 0); return { subject: sub, topper: classroom.students[topIdx] }; }); console.log("Subject toppers:", subjectToppers); // → [ // { subject: 'Maths', topper: 'Diya' }, // { subject: 'DS', topper: 'Diya' }, // { subject: 'OS', topper: 'Meera' } // ] ``` **Marks distribution (7):** - Subject average computation — 2 marks. - Low-attendance filter — 2 marks. - Subject-wise topper using nested `reduce` — 3 marks. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Confusing array-of-objects with object-of-arrays** (1–2 marks lost) — always state the *outermost* data structure first. > 2. **Mutating the original when asked not to** (2 marks lost) — use spread (`[...arr]`) before `sort`/`reverse`. > 3. **Forgetting `return` inside `map`/`filter` callbacks** (entire 7-mark answer invalidated if the question depends on it) — always test your code mentally. > 4. **Confusing `for...in` (object keys) with `for...of` (array values)** (1 mark lost). > 5. **Assuming spread does a deep copy** (1–2 marks lost) — be explicit about shallow vs. deep. > 6. **Writing `arr[i].marks` when the structure is `obj.marks[i]`** (the *reverse* nesting) — read the structure diagram first. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀 - [x] **Array of Objects** = list of structured records. Access via `arr[i].prop`. - [x] **Object containing Arrays** = single record with list fields. Access via `obj.key[i]`. - [x] Arrays and objects in JS are **reference types** stored on the **heap**. - [x] Two variables can point to the **same** object → **aliasing** risk. - [x] **Shallow copy** (`[...arr]`, `slice`, `Array.from`) duplicates slots but **not** nested objects. - [x] **Deep copy** requires `structuredClone()` or `JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(...))`. - [x] **Mutating methods:** `push`, `pop`, `shift`, `unshift`, `splice`, `sort`, `reverse`. - [x] **Non-mutating methods:** `map`, `filter`, `slice`, `concat`, `reduce`, `find`. - [x] **Iteration patterns:** `for`, `for...of` (values), `for...in` (object keys), `forEach`, `map`, `filter`, `reduce`. - [x] **Time complexity** for full traversal is $O(n)$; for property access is $O(1)$. - [x] **JSON payloads** from APIs are virtually always arrays of objects — this is the *de facto* data interchange format of the web. - [x] In KTU 2024 ESE, expect questions framed as: *"Given a dataset, write code to compute X / filter Y / sort Z"* — these test **CO1 (Apply)** and **CO2 (Analyze)**. - [x] Always **diagram the structure first** (outer array → inner objects → inner arrays) before writing code — this is what separates 12-mark answers from 14-mark answers. > [!TIP] > **Last-minute KTU revision mantra:** > *"Arrays hold objects by reference. Objects hold arrays by reference. Know the difference, copy wisely, and iterate cleanly."* ✅

topics.title: Built-in Objects: Browser Object Model and Document Object Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Built-in Objects: Browser Object Model and Document Object Model # 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are "Built-in Objects" in JavaScript? In JavaScript, **Built-in Objects** are pre-defined objects provided by the runtime environment (the **Web Browser** in client-side scripting). They are ready to use without the programmer having to define them. Built-in objects are broadly classified into three families: 1. **Standard Built-in Objects** (Core JS): `Object`, `Array`, `String`, `Math`, `Date`, `Number`, `Boolean`, etc. 2. **Browser Object Model (BOM)** Objects: Provided by the browser to interact with the browser window itself. 3. **Document Object Model (DOM)** Objects: Provided by the browser to interact with the HTML document content. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The focus of this topic is specifically on the **BOM** and the **DOM**, which form the backbone of all dynamic, interactive web pages. The JavaScript `window` object acts as the **root** for both models. --- ## 1.2 Browser Object Model (BOM) — Definition > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (W3C-aligned):** The **Browser Object Model (BOM)** is a browser-specific convention that refers to all the objects exposed by the **web browser** to JavaScript. It represents the browser window as a hierarchical tree of objects, with the `window` object at the top, allowing scripts to interact with the browser itself (not the document content). The BOM is **not standardized by the W3C**, but most modern browsers follow a de-facto structure. ### 🏠 Conceptual Analogy: The "House and its Neighborhood" Imagine you are inside a **house**: - The **DOM** is the **interior of the house** — the furniture, walls, doors, rooms (HTML elements). You can rearrange them. - The **BOM** is the **neighborhood surrounding the house** — the street address (URL/location), the history of previous houses you've lived in, the size of the land (screen dimensions), and the mailbox (alert/popups). You use **BOM** when you want to talk to the browser (e.g., redirect to a new URL, go back, check screen size), and **DOM** when you want to change the page content (e.g., change text color, add a button). --- ## 1.3 Document Object Model (DOM) — Definition > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (W3C Standard):** The **Document Object Model (DOM)** is a cross-platform, language-independent **tree-structured representation** of an HTML or XML document. It defines the logical structure of documents and the way a document is accessed and manipulated by programming languages like JavaScript. The W3C DOM standard is divided into 3 parts: **Core DOM**, **XML DOM**, and **HTML DOM**. ### 🏠 Conceptual Analogy: The "Architectural Blueprint" When an architect designs a house, the **blueprint** is not the house itself, but a structured drawing that represents every room, door, and window. The **DOM is the JavaScript blueprint of the webpage**: - The `` tag is the **outermost wall**. - `` and `` are the **floors**. - `
`, `

`, `

` are **rooms** inside. - Text, attributes, and comments are the **furniture and labels**. JavaScript uses this blueprint to **find elements**, **read values**, **change content**, **add new elements**, or **delete existing ones** — without reloading the page. This is the foundation of **Dynamic HTML (DHTML)**. --- ## 1.4 Why are BOM & DOM Critical for KTU/Web Development? - They enable **client-side interactivity** (validations, animations, AJAX). - They form the **core foundation** of front-end frameworks like React, Angular, and Vue. - Mastering these models is a prerequisite for understanding **Event Handling**, which is the next sub-topic in Module 1. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory KTU Note:** The root of both BOM and DOM is the **`window` object**. In browser JavaScript, typing `alert("hi")` is actually the same as typing `window.alert("hi")`. The `document` object (root of DOM) is a **property of `window`**. # 📐 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 The BOM Hierarchy (Tree Structure) The Browser Object Model organizes all browser-related objects under a single root: the `window` object. ``` window (Root) ├── document → (Entry point to the DOM) ├── navigator → Information about the browser (userAgent, platform, language) ├── screen → Information about the user's display (width, height, colorDepth) ├── history → Browser session history (back, forward, length) ├── location → Current URL information (href, pathname, search, reload) ├── frames[] → All

topics.title: Global Objects: String, Number, Math, Date
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Global Objects: String, Number, Math, Date # Module 1: Global Objects: String, Number, Math, Date ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** > *"In JavaScript, the standard built-in objects are often referred to as global objects because they are accessible from anywhere in the script. The most commonly used built-in objects in client-side JavaScript are: **String**, **Number**, **Math**, and **Date**."* In the JavaScript runtime environment, an **object** is a composite data type that bundles together **properties** (named values) and **methods** (functions attached to that object). Unlike user-defined objects, JavaScript ships with a curated set of pre-built objects, which the ECMAScript specification (the formal blueprint of JavaScript, standardized under **ECMA-262**) defines as the **Global Objects**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction for Exams:** > In a web browser environment, the global scope is the `window` object, and every global property of JavaScript (including our four targets) is a property of `window`. This is why they are often called *Global Objects* — you can call `window.Math.PI` or just `Math.PI` interchangeably. ### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Swiss Army Knife" Toolkit Imagine you walk into a well-stocked hardware store. You don't have to manufacture a screwdriver, a measuring tape, or a calculator from raw metal every time you need one — they are sitting on the shelf, ready to use, universally available, and standardized. JavaScript's **Global Objects** function exactly like that hardware store shelf: | Real World Analogy | JavaScript Equivalent | Purpose | |---|---|---| | Pre-printed date calendar | `Date` object | Handle dates and times | | Pocket calculator with scientific functions | `Math` object | Perform mathematical operations | | Universal ruler / measuring tape | `Number` object | Handle numeric constants, parsing, formatting | | Word processor with text editing tools | `String` object | Manipulate text (strings) | > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Takeaway:** The String, Number, Math, and Date objects provide the JavaScript engine with pre-built, highly optimized tools. As a developer, you do not need to write a function to calculate the square root of a number (`Math.sqrt(9)` gives `3` instantly) or figure out how to find the length of a string (`"Hello".length` gives `5`). These objects are your **productivity multipliers**. ### 1.2 Standard Categories of Global Objects The four global objects under study can be functionally classified: 1. **Wrapper Objects (String, Number):** These wrap primitive values (a string literal, a number literal) so that you can call methods on them. JavaScript automatically performs this conversion — a process called **autoboxing**. 2. **Static Utility Object (Math):** Cannot be instantiated. All its properties and methods are accessed directly via the object name (e.g., `Math.PI`). 3. **Constructor Object (Date):** Must be instantiated using the `new` keyword (e.g., `new Date()`) to create a specific date instance. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** All four objects are part of JavaScript's *standard built-in objects*. They are guaranteed to be available in every JavaScript environment (browser or Node.js) without needing any imports. --- ## 1.3 High-Yield Classification Diagram (Text-Based Schematic) ``` ┌────────────────────────────────────┐ │ JAVASCRIPT GLOBAL OBJECTS │ │ (Standard Built-in / ECMA-262) │ └─────────────────┬──────────────────┘ │ ┌───────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ ┌────▼─────┐ ┌──────▼──────┐ ┌──────▼──────┐ │ String │ │ Number │ │ Date │ │(Wrapper) │ │ (Wrapper) │ │(Constructor)│ └────┬─────┘ └──────┬──────┘ └──────┬──────┘ │ │ │ Text operations Numeric constants, Date/Time creation length, slice, parsing, formatting getFullYear, getMonth indexOf, toUpperCase toFixed, parseInt, setHours, toISOString parseFloat, NaN, Infinity │ ┌─────▼──────┐ │ Math │ │ (Static) │ └─────┬──────┘ │ Math.PI, Math.E sqrt, pow, random floor, ceil, round sin, cos, log ``` # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — The Four Global Objects ## 2.1 The `String` Object > [!NOTE] > **Definition:** The `String` object is a wrapper around the JavaScript string primitive data type, enabling access to properties and methods for text manipulation. ### 2.1.1 Why Does a Wrapper Object Exist? In JavaScript, primitives (`"hello"`, `42`, `true`) are *immutable* and do not have methods of their own. However, when you write `"hello".toUpperCase()`, JavaScript *temporarily* wraps the string primitive into a `String` object, executes the method, and returns the result. This invisible conversion is called **autoboxing**. ### 2.1.2 Two Ways to Create a String | Method | Syntax | Output Type | Mutability | |---|---|---|---| | Primitive literal | `let s = "Hello";` | Primitive | Immutable | | Object constructor | `let s = new String("Hello");` | Object (Wrapper) | Mutable reference | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Using `new String("Hello") === "Hello"` returns `false` because one is an object and the other is a primitive. KTU examiners frequently test this with the `typeof` operator (`typeof new String("a")` returns `"object"`; `typeof "a"` returns `"string"`). ### 2.1.3 Complete String Methods Reference The most important String methods for KTU exams are categorized below: **A. Length & Character Access** - `str.length` → Number of characters (zero-based, not one-based) - `str.charAt(index)` → Returns character at given position - `str.charCodeAt(index)` → Returns UTF-16 code (0–65535) of the character **B. Searching & Extracting** - `str.indexOf(substring, start)` → Returns the first index of occurrence, or **-1** if not found - `str.lastIndexOf(substring)` → Returns the last index of occurrence - `str.includes(substring)` → Returns `true` or `false` (ES6) - `str.startsWith(prefix)` / `str.endsWith(suffix)` → Boolean checks - `str.slice(start, end)` → Extracts a section; supports negative indices (counts from end) - `str.substring(start, end)` → Similar to slice, but negatives are treated as 0 - `str.substr(start, length)` → **Deprecated**, length-based extraction **C. Transformation Methods** - `str.toUpperCase()` → Converts to UPPERCASE - `str.toLowerCase()` → Converts to lowercase - `str.trim()` → Removes whitespace from both ends - `str.replace(search, replace)` → Replaces first match - `str.replaceAll(search, replace)` → Replaces all matches (ES2021) - `str.split(separator)` → Splits into an Array - `str.concat(str2)` → Joins two strings - `str.repeat(count)` → Repeats the string `count` times **D. Padding & Modern Methods (ES2017)** - `str.padStart(targetLength, padString)` → Pads from the start - `str.padEnd(targetLength, padString)` → Pads from the end > [!TIP] > **Real-world Engineering Use:** String methods are heavily used in form validation (`email.includes("@")`), data sanitization (`input.trim()`), URL parsing (`url.split("/")`), and building dynamic HTML (`"

" + name + "
