Allows `await` (works with `for await...of` on async iterables). #### Strategy C — Classic Index-Based: `for` loop **Logic Flow:** 1. Manual index counter `i` from `0` to `nodeList.length - 1`. 2. Access node via `nodeList[i]`. 3. Supports `break` and `continue` for early termination. 4. **Only loop type that supports `break/continue` cleanly with NodeList** (`forEach` cannot be broken out of directly). #### Strategy D — Conversion to Array **Logic Flow:** 1. Use `Array.from(nodeList)` or spread `[...nodeList]`. 2. Now you have a real `Array` — unlocks `map`, `filter`, `reduce`, `find`, `some`, `every`, etc. 3. Cost: **O(n) extra memory allocation** for the new array. --- ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Method / Construct** | **Syntax Template** | **Arguments to Callback** | **Supports `break`?** | **Best Use Case** | |---|---|---|---|---| | `forEach()` | `nodeList.forEach((node, idx, list) => { ... })` | `(node, index, listObj)` | ❌ No | Modern simple iteration, ES6+ code | | `for...of` | `for (const node of nodeList) { ... }` | `(node)` | ✅ Yes | Clean iteration, async-friendly | | `for...of` + `entries()` | `for (const [i, node] of nodeList.entries()) { ... }` | `([index, node])` | ✅ Yes | Need **both** index and value | | Classic `for` | `for (let i=0; i < nodeList.length; i++) { nodeList[i] }` | N/A (manual) | ✅ Yes | Max performance, early exit | | `Array.from()` | `Array.from(nodeList).forEach(...)` | Real Array methods | Depends on loop | Need `map`, `filter`, `reduce` | | Spread operator | `[...nodeList].forEach(...)` | Real Array methods | Depends on loop | Quickest Array conversion | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Time Complexity of ALL iteration methods** is **O(n)**, where $n$ is the `nodeList.length`. The constant factor varies: `forEach` and `for...of` are roughly 5–10% slower than the classic `for` loop in micro-benchmarks, but the difference is **negligible for typical DOM sizes** (under 10,000 nodes). --- ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Looping through NodeLists is the **backbone of virtually every dynamic web page**: - 🎨 **CSS class injection**: Adding active styles to navigation items. - 📊 **Data visualisation**: Binding chart bars to DOM `
` elements. - 🛒 **E-commerce carts**: Iterating over `
  • ` cart items to compute totals. - ✅ **Form validation**: Iterating over input fields to check validity. - 🔍 **Search filtering**: Hiding/showing NodeList elements based on a query. - ♿ **Accessibility**: Setting `aria-*` attributes on list items programmatically. > [!TIP] > **Industry Insight:** Modern front-end frameworks (React, Vue) internally *abstract* NodeList iteration. However, in **vanilla JavaScript** (which is what KTU PBITT504 Module 1 focuses on), mastering this iteration pattern is **non-negotiable** for KTU lab exams and Semester Project Viva. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 The HTML Scaffold (Used in All Examples Below) ```html NodeList Demo
    • Home
    • About
    • Services
    • Contact
    ``` **NodeList obtained from:** `document.querySelectorAll('.item')` — a **static NodeList** of length **4**. --- ### 3.2 Method 1 — `forEach()` (ES6+ Modern) **Code:** ```javascript const items = document.querySelectorAll('.item'); items.forEach((node, index) => { console.log(`Index ${index}: ${node.textContent}`); node.style.fontWeight = 'bold'; }); ``` **Step-by-Step Walkthrough:** | **Step** | **Engine Action** | **State After** | |---|---|---| | 1 | `querySelectorAll('.item')` is invoked | NodeList of 4 `
  • ` elements created in memory | | 2 | `forEach()` is called on the NodeList | V8 invokes the callback once per element | | 3 | 1st call: `node` = first `
  • `, `index` = 0 | `Index 0: Home` printed | | 4 | 2nd call: `node` = second `
  • `, `index` = 1 | `Index 1: About` printed | | 5 | 3rd call: `node` = third `
  • `, `index` = 2 | `Index 2: Services` printed | | 6 | 4th call: `node` = fourth `
  • `, `index` = 3 | `Index 3: Contact` printed | | 7 | Internal counter equals `length` (4) | Loop terminates, **O(n)** = 4 operations | > [!NOTE] > The callback receives `(currentValue, currentIndex, listObj)` — same order as `Array.prototype.forEach()`. The third argument `listObj` is the NodeList itself, useful for **re-entrant** callbacks. --- ### 3.3 Method 2 — `for...of` Loop (ES6+) **Code:** ```javascript const items = document.querySelectorAll('.item'); for (const node of items) { node.style.color = 'navy'; console.log(node.textContent); } ``` **Step-by-Step Walkthrough:** | **Step** | **Engine Action** | |---|---| | 1 | V8 calls `items[Symbol.iterator]()` — returns an iterator object. | | 2 | Iterator's `next()` is called → returns `{ value:
  • Home
    • , done: false }`. | | 3 | `node` is bound to the `value`; loop body executes. | | 4 | `next()` called again → `{ value:
  • About
    • , done: false }`. | | 5 | Process continues until `{ done: true }` is returned. | | 6 | Loop exits automatically; **no need to manage index manually**. | **Advanced Variant — Using `entries()` for index + node:** ```javascript for (const [index, node] of items.entries()) { console.log(`${index} → ${node.textContent}`); } ``` The mathematical relationship modelled is: $$ \text{Iteration count} = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} 1 = n \quad \text{where } n = \text{nodeList.length} $$ --- ### 3.4 Method 3 — Classic `for` Loop (Maximum Control) **Code:** ```javascript const items = document.querySelectorAll('.item'); for (let i = 0; i < items.length; i++) { items[i].classList.add('highlight'); if (i === 1) { console.log('Skipping About via continue'); continue; } if (items[i].textContent === 'Services') { console.log('Breaking at Services'); break; } } ``` **Step-by-Step Trace Table:** | **Iteration `i`** | **`items[i].textContent`** | **Action Taken** | **Output** | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | "Home" | Add `highlight` class | — | | 1 | "About" | `continue` triggered | `Skipping About via continue` | | 2 | "Services" | Add `highlight`, then `break` | `Breaking at Services` | | 3 | "Contact" | **NOT executed** (loop already broken) | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The classic `for` loop is the ONLY iteration construct that supports `break` and `continue` cleanly on a NodeList without throwing extra exceptions.** With `forEach`, you would need to throw an exception to break out (an anti-pattern). --- ### 3.5 Method 4 — Conversion to Real Array **Code (Using `Array.from()`):** ```javascript const items = document.querySelectorAll('.item'); const itemsArray = Array.from(items); // Now you can use REAL Array methods: const labels = itemsArray .filter(node => node.textContent.length > 4) .map(node => node.textContent.toUpperCase()); console.log(labels); // ["SERVICES", "CONTACT"] ``` **Code (Using Spread Operator):** ```javascript const items = document.querySelectorAll('.item'); const itemsArray = [...items]; // Equivalent transformation: itemsArray.forEach(node => node.setAttribute('data-active', 'true')); ``` **Mathematical Cost Analysis:** $$ \text{Total Operations} = \underbrace{n}_{\text{conversion}} + \underbrace{n}_{\text{iteration}} = 2n = O(n) $$ The constant factor is doubled, but asymptotically it is still **linear time**. > [!TIP] > **Conversion Best Practice:** Convert to an Array **once**, then reuse. Avoid converting inside loops repeatedly — this is a common performance trap in beginner KTU lab submissions. --- ### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Method | **Scenario** | **Recommended Method** | **Justification** | |---|---|---| | Simple style/class change | `forEach` | Readable, idiomatic ES6+ | | Need early exit (`break`) | Classic `for` | Only construct that exits cleanly | | Need to chain `map`/`filter` | Convert to Array | NodeList lacks these natively | | Async iteration (e.g., fetching) | `for await...of` | Native async support | | Need index + value together | `entries()` + `for...of` | Direct destructuring | | Maximum micro-performance | Classic `for` | Lowest engine overhead | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 High-Level Iteration Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[DOM Query e.g. querySelectorAll] --> B[NodeList Object Returned] B --> C{Choose Iteration Strategy} C --> D1[forEach Method] C --> D2[for of Loop] C --> D3[Classic for Loop] C --> D4[Array.from or Spread] D1 --> E1[Callback fires per node] D2 --> E2[Iterator protocol] D3 --> E3[Manual index control] D4 --> E4[Real Array with map filter reduce] E1 --> F[Side effects applied to DOM] E2 --> F E3 --> F E4 --> F F --> G[Page updates visually] ``` ### 4.2 Detailed `forEach` Execution Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Start: nodeList.forEach callback]) --> Init[Internal counter i = 0] Init --> Check{i less than length?} Check -- No --> End([End: Loop terminates]) Check -- Yes --> Invoke[Invoke callback node, i, listObj] Invoke --> SideEffect[Apply DOM mutation or logic] SideEffect --> Increment[i = i + 1] Increment --> Check ``` ### 4.3 `for...of` Iterator State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> GetIterator GetIterator --> CallingNext: items[Symbol.iterator]() CallingNext --> YieldingValue: next() returns value, done: false YieldingValue --> ExecutingBody: Loop body runs ExecutingBody --> CallingNext CallingNext --> YieldingDone: next() returns done: true YieldingDone --> [*] ``` ### 4.4 Static vs. Live NodeList Lifecycle Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Static["Static NodeList (querySelectorAll)"] S1[Snapshot at query time] --> S2[Frozen list] S2 --> S3[DOM mutation ignored] end subgraph Live["Live NodeList (childNodes)"] L1[Continuously tracked] --> L2[Auto-updates] L2 --> L3[DOM mutation reflected] end Static --> Iter1[forEach or for-of or for] Live --> Iter2[forEach or for-of or for] Iter1 --> Result1[Iterate over snapshot] Iter2 --> Result2[Iterate over current state] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Exam]` **What is a NodeList in JavaScript? Name any two DOM methods that return a NodeList.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **NodeList** is an *array-like* collection of DOM nodes (elements, text nodes, or comments) returned by DOM query methods. It has a `length` property, supports indexed access `[i]`, and is iterable. Unlike a true `Array`, it does **not** natively support methods such as `map()`, `filter()`, or `reduce()`. **Two DOM methods that return a NodeList:** 1. `document.querySelectorAll(selector)` — returns a **static** NodeList matching a CSS selector. 2. `element.childNodes` — returns a **live** NodeList of all child nodes (including text and comment nodes). *(Alternative methods: `Node.prototype.querySelectorAll()`, `document.getElementsByName()` returns NodeList in some browsers, `document.getElementsByTagNameNS()`.)* --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Differentiate between `forEach()` and `for...of` loop when iterating a NodeList.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | `forEach()` | `for...of` | |---|---|---| | **Syntax style** | `nodeList.forEach(cb)` | `for (const x of nodeList) { }` | | **Index access** | Callback receives `(node, index)` | Only value; use `.entries()` for index | | **Break/Continue** | ❌ Not supported directly | ✅ Supported natively | | **Async support** | Cannot use `await` inside | Works with `for await...of` | | **ES version** | ES6 (2015) | ES6 (2015) | | **Best for** | Declarative, side-effect-only iteration | When early exit or async is needed | --- ### 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Don't** confuse `NodeList` with `HTMLCollection` (CO1 level — high-frequency mistake). > - **Don't** write `forEach` on an `HTMLCollection` — it lacks the method! (Most common 2-mark deduction in KTU lab records). > - **Don't** claim `forEach` supports `break` — it does NOT. You must use `try/throw` or switch to a `for` loop. > - Always specify **whether the NodeList is static or live** in your exam answers — it earns an extra mark. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the different methods to iterate through a NodeList with suitable JavaScript examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a JavaScript program to select all `

    ` elements in a document, iterate over them using the `forEach()` method, and add the CSS class `highlight` to every alternate paragraph (i.e., paragraphs at index 0, 2, 4, …). **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Iteration Methods — Detailed Explanation (7 marks):** The four primary methods to iterate a NodeList are: 1. **`forEach()` method** — Modern ES6+ method; takes a callback with `(currentNode, index, listObj)` arguments. ```javascript const nodes = document.querySelectorAll('p'); nodes.forEach((node, idx) => { console.log(`Paragraph ${idx}: ${node.textContent}`); }); ``` 2. **`for...of` loop** — Uses the iterator protocol; ideal when `break/continue` is required. ```javascript for (const node of nodes) { node.style.color = 'teal'; } ``` 3. **Classic `for` loop** — Index-based; allows early termination and maximum control. ```javascript for (let i = 0; i < nodes.length; i++) { nodes[i].classList.add('processed'); } ``` 4. **Array conversion** — Convert via `Array.from()` or `[...nodes]` to unlock `map`, `filter`, `reduce`. ```javascript const upper = Array.from(nodes).map(n => n.textContent.toUpperCase()); ``` **Time complexity** of all four is **O(n)**, where $n$ = `nodeList.length`. --- **(b) Practical Program (7 marks):** ```javascript // Step 1: Select all

    elements (returns a static NodeList) const paragraphs = document.querySelectorAll('p'); // Step 2: Validate the NodeList if (paragraphs.length === 0) { console.warn('No

    elements found in the document.'); } else { // Step 3: Iterate using forEach, with index access paragraphs.forEach((node, index) => { // Step 4: Check if index is even (0, 2, 4, ...) if (index % 2 === 0) { node.classList.add('highlight'); console.log(`Added 'highlight' to paragraph #${index}`); } else { console.log(`Skipped paragraph #${index} (odd index)`); } }); console.log(`Total paragraphs processed: ${paragraphs.length}`); console.log(`Paragraphs highlighted: ${Math.ceil(paragraphs.length / 2)}`); } ``` **Corresponding CSS:** ```css .highlight { background-color: yellow; font-weight: bold; padding: 4px; } ``` **Trace Table for 4 paragraphs:** | **Index** | **`index % 2`** | **Action** | |---|---|---| | 0 | 0 | ✅ Add `highlight` | | 1 | 1 | ❌ Skip | | 2 | 0 | ✅ Add `highlight` | | 3 | 1 | ❌ Skip | --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Supplementary Exam – Aug 2023]` **(a)** Differentiate between `NodeList` and `HTMLCollection`. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With examples, explain how to convert a NodeList into an Array. List two advantages of doing so. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) NodeList vs. HTMLCollection (7 marks):** | Feature | `NodeList` | `HTMLCollection` | |---|---|---| | **Returns from** | `querySelectorAll()`, `childNodes` | `getElementsByClassName()`, `getElementsByTagName()` | | **Nature** | Can be **static** or **live** | Always **live** | | **Node types** | Element, Text, Comment, etc. | Only Element nodes | | **`forEach()`** | ✅ Available (ES6+) | ❌ Not available | | **Iterable (`for...of`)** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (modern browsers) | | **Named access** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (`collection.namedItem('id')` or `collection['id']`) | | **Array methods** (`map`, `filter`) | ❌ Not natively | ❌ Not natively | | **Index access** | ✅ `list[i]` | ✅ `list[i]` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key difference:** Both are array-like collections, but `HTMLCollection` is **always live and contains only element nodes**, whereas `NodeList` can be **static and includes non-element nodes** (text, comment). --- **(b) Converting a NodeList to an Array (7 marks):** **Method 1 — Using `Array.from()`:** ```javascript const nodeList = document.querySelectorAll('li'); const array = Array.from(nodeList); console.log(Array.isArray(array)); // true array.map(li => li.textContent).forEach(t => console.log(t)); ``` **Method 2 — Using Spread Operator (`...`):** ```javascript const nodeList = document.querySelectorAll('li'); const array = [...nodeList]; const filtered = array.filter(li => li.textContent.startsWith('A')); console.log(filtered); // Array of

  • elements starting with 'A' ``` **Method 3 — Using `Array.prototype.slice.call()` (Legacy ES5):** ```javascript const nodeList = document.querySelectorAll('li'); const array = Array.prototype.slice.call(nodeList); ``` **Two Advantages of Conversion:** 1. **Access to full Array prototype methods** like `map()`, `filter()`, `reduce()`, `find()`, `some()`, `every()`, `sort()`, etc., enabling functional programming patterns. 2. **Chainable transformations** become possible — for example: `Array.from(nodes).filter(...).map(...).reduce(...)` can be written as a single expressive pipeline. **Bonus Advantage:** The resulting array is a **true deep-copyable** object suitable for serialisation, sorting independently of the DOM, and passing to functions expecting an `Array` parameter. --- ### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU PBITT504 Module 1 — NodeList Iteration:** - 📌 **NodeList** = *array-like* collection of DOM nodes returned by `querySelectorAll()` (static) or `childNodes` (live). - 📌 It is **NOT a real Array** — has `length`, indexed access, but lacks `map`/`filter`/`reduce` natively. - 📌 **Static NodeList** = snapshot at query time → does not auto-update. - 📌 **Live NodeList** = real-time → reflects DOM changes immediately. - 📌 **4 iteration methods:** `forEach()`, `for...of`, classic `for`, and Array conversion. - 📌 **`forEach` arguments:** `(currentValue, currentIndex, listObj)` — same signature as `Array.prototype.forEach`. - 📌 **Only classic `for`** supports `break`/`continue` cleanly on a NodeList. - 📌 **`for...of` + `entries()`** = best way to access both index and node simultaneously via destructuring. - 📌 **Convert via `Array.from(nodeList)` or `[...nodeList]`** to unlock full Array methods. - 📌 **Time complexity** of all methods = **O(n)**, where $n = \text{nodeList.length}$. - 📌 **Common exam trap:** Never claim `forEach` can be broken out of — it cannot without exception throwing. - 📌 **Common exam trap:** Don't confuse `NodeList` (static/live) with `HTMLCollection` (always live, elements only). - 📌 **Real-world usage:** CSS class injection, dynamic styling, form validation, data binding, accessibility updates. - 📌 **Browser support:** `forEach` on NodeList is fully supported in all modern browsers (Chrome 51+, Firefox 50+, Edge, Safari 10+).

topics.title: Traversing the DOM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Traversing the DOM # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **DOM (Document Object Model) Traversal** is the systematic process of navigating through the hierarchical tree structure of an HTML/XML document using a well-defined set of properties and methods exposed by the browser's DOM API. According to the **W3C DOM Level 3 specification**, traversal allows scripts to programmatically access, read, and manipulate any node in the document tree by leveraging the structural relationships — **parent, child, and sibling** — between nodes. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Web Application Development* (PBITT504), DOM traversal forms the foundational pillar of **Module 1: Introduction to Web Development & JavaScript**, since modern client-side frameworks (React, Vue, Angular) abstract but fundamentally rely on these primitive DOM operations. > [!NOTE] > **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme)** > Students must master both *Node-based* traversal (works on text, comments, elements) and *Element-based* traversal (works strictly on element nodes). Confusing these two categories is the single most common reason for losing marks in the **End Semester Evaluation (ESE)**. ## 1.2 The Document Tree — Structural Anatomy The browser parses an HTML document and constructs a **tree of objects** (nodes). Every tag, attribute, text fragment, and comment becomes a node. The tree has exactly **one root node** — the `Document` object. **Node Types (most common):** - `ELEMENT_NODE` (Type 1) — HTML tags like `
`, `

` - `ATTRIBUTE_NODE` (Type 2) — rarely used directly (deprecated) - `TEXT_NODE` (Type 3) — text content inside elements - `COMMENT_NODE` (Type 8) — `` - `DOCUMENT_NODE` (Type 9) — the root `document` object - `DOCUMENT_TYPE_NODE` (Type 10) — `` ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!TIP] > **Analogy: The Family Tree of a Royal Kingdom** > Imagine a royal family tree. The **King** is the `document` (root). His **sons** are direct *children*. The sons share the same *parent* (the King). Brothers are *siblings* of each other. The *grandchildren* are descendants. Now imagine the King wants to find his youngest granddaughter. He has two options: > 1. **Walk down** the tree level-by-level until she is found (a `for` loop on `children`). > 2. **Take a royal messenger** with a precise description (a CSS selector) to fetch her instantly (`querySelector`). > > **DOM Traversal** is exactly this — finding your way through the royal family tree of HTML elements! ## 1.4 Why DOM Traversal Matters in KTU Examinations | Application | Use of Traversal | |---|---| | **Form Validation** | Find input fields, access their `.value` | | **Dynamic UI Updates** | Locate elements to change text, classes, or styles | | **Event Delegation** | Use parent node to handle child events efficiently | | **Single Page Apps (SPA)** | Inject/remove nodes relative to existing ones | | **Data Scraping** | Crawl through structured HTML to extract data | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Traversal Families DOM traversal is broadly classified into two families, each serving a different purpose. The KTU examiner expects students to know **when to use which** — a common 7-mark question. ### Family 1: Node-Based Traversal (Universal) These properties consider *every* node type — elements, text, comments, whitespace. | Property | Direction | Returns | |---|---|---| | `node.parentNode` | Upward | The direct parent node | | `node.childNodes` | Downward | A **live `NodeList`** of all child nodes | | `node.firstChild` | Downward | First child node (or `null`) | | `node.lastChild` | Downward | Last child node (or `null`) | | `node.nextSibling` | Sideways | Next sibling node | | `node.previousSibling` | Sideways | Previous sibling node | | `node.ownerDocument` | Upward | The root `document` | ### Family 2: Element-Based Traversal (Strict) These properties consider *only* Element nodes (skipping text and comment nodes). | Property | Direction | Returns | |---|---|---| | `element.parentElement` | Upward | The direct parent **element** | | `element.children` | Downward | A **live `HTMLCollection`** of child **elements** | | `element.firstElementChild` | Downward | First child **element** | | `element.lastElementChild` | Downward | Last child **element** | | `element.nextElementSibling` | Sideways | Next sibling **element** | | `element.previousElementSibling` | Sideways | Previous sibling **element** | | `element.childElementCount` | Downward | Number of child elements (integer) | ## 2.2 Closest Ancestor Methods (Modern DOM) | Method | Purpose | |---|---| | `element.closest(selector)` | Traverses **upward** (self → parents → root) to find the **nearest** ancestor matching a CSS selector. Returns `null` if no match. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **`closest()` vs `querySelector()`:** > - `querySelector` searches **downward** in the descendants. > - `closest` searches **upward** in the ancestors (including itself). > This distinction is a **favourite KTU Part A question** (3 marks). ## 2.3 `NodeList` vs `HTMLCollection` — The Critical Distinction This is a **guaranteed 3-mark question** in KTU exams. | Feature | `NodeList` | `HTMLCollection` | |---|---|---| | Returned by | `childNodes`, `querySelectorAll` | `children`, `getElementsByTagName` | | Contains | All node types (text, element, comment) | **Only element nodes** | | Live? | **Static** (querySelectorAll) / **Live** (childNodes) | **Always live** | | Iterable with `for...of`? | Yes | No (but supports `for` loop with index) | | Has `.length`? | Yes | Yes | | Has `Array.from()` support? | Yes | Yes | > [!WARNING] > **Live Collections Pitfall:** Iterating over a *live* `HTMLCollection` while modifying it (e.g., removing children) causes the index to skip nodes — a classic KTU examiner trap. ## 2.4 Mathematical / Structural Notation Although DOM is a tree, we can formally express it. Let the DOM be a tree $T = (V, E)$ where $V$ is the set of all nodes and $E$ is the set of parent-child edges. The **depth** of a node $n$ from the root $r$ is given by: $$ \text{depth}(n) = \text{depth}(\text{parent}(n)) + 1 $$ with the base case $\text{depth}(r) = 0$. The **total number of nodes** at depth $d$ in a balanced tree is at most: $$ N(d) \leq b^d $$ where $b$ is the maximum branching factor. This is why traversing a deeply nested DOM using a linear chain of `parentNode` calls costs $O(d)$ time per upward lookup. ## 2.5 The KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Print This Table — It Covers 90% of Part A Questions** > > | Traversal Goal | Best Property/Method | > |---|---| > | Get the parent element only | `parentElement` | > | Get the parent node (any type) | `parentNode` | > | Iterate child elements | `children` (HTMLCollection) | > | Iterate all child nodes (incl. text) | `childNodes` (NodeList) | > | First element child | `firstElementChild` | > | Last element child | `lastElementChild` | > | Next element sibling | `nextElementSibling` | > | Previous element sibling | `previousElementSibling` | > | Find nearest ancestor matching selector | `closest(selector)` | > | Find first descendant matching selector | `querySelector(selector)` | > | Find ALL descendants matching selector | `querySelectorAll(selector)` | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Practical HTML Skeleton (Used Throughout) For all derivations below, we assume the following **minified HTML** is loaded into the browser: ```html KTU Demo

Hello KTU

First paragraph.

Second paragraph.

  • One
  • Two
  • Three
``` ## 3.2 Step-by-Step: Building the Node Tree The browser's **HTML parser** constructs the following tree from the skeleton above. Whitespace and newlines between tags are treated as **TEXT_NODES** — this is a frequent source of confusion. ``` document └── ├── │ ├── ("KTU Demo") │ └── (whitespace text) └── <body> ├── (whitespace text) ├── <!-- Main container --> (COMMENT_NODE) ├── (whitespace text) └── <div id="container"> ├── (whitespace text) ├── <h1> ("Hello KTU") ├── (whitespace text) ├── <p class="intro"> ("First paragraph.") ├── (whitespace text) ├── <p class="intro"> ("Second paragraph.") ├── (whitespace text) ├── <ul> │ ├── (whitespace text) │ ├── <li> ("One") │ ├── (whitespace text) │ ├── <li> ("Two") │ ├── (whitespace text) │ ├── <li> ("Three") │ └── (whitespace text) └── (whitespace text) ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > Every indentation space and newline between tags becomes a `TEXT_NODE`. This is why raw `firstChild` often returns a text node of whitespace, not the next element. ## 3.3 Worked Example A — Element-Based Traversal **Question (ESE Module 1, 7 marks):** Write a JavaScript snippet that, starting from the `<h1>` element, navigates to the last `<li>` and changes its text to "Modified by JS". ### Step 1: Acquire the starting node ```javascript const h1 = document.querySelector('h1'); ``` The `querySelector` method searches descendants of `document` and returns the **first** matching element. ### Step 2: Move upward to the common parent ```javascript const container = h1.parentElement; ``` Here, `h1.parentElement` is the `<div id="container">`. Time complexity: $O(1)$. ### Step 3: Navigate downward to `<ul>` ```javascript const ul = container.children[2]; ``` `container.children` returns a live `HTMLCollection` of all element children: $$ \text{children} = [\langle h1 \rangle,\ \langle p\text{.intro} \rangle,\ \langle p\text{.intro} \rangle,\ \langle ul \rangle] $$ Index 2 gives the second `<p>`, but we want the `<ul>` at index 3. Corrected: ```javascript const ul = container.children[3]; ``` ### Step 4: Move to the last child element ```javascript const lastLi = ul.lastElementChild; ``` This is equivalent to `ul.children[ul.childElementCount - 1]`, returning the `<li>Three</li>` node. ### Step 5: Modify the text ```javascript lastLi.textContent = 'Modified by JS'; ``` The `textContent` property replaces all child text nodes with a single new `TEXT_NODE`. ### Complete Solution ```javascript const h1 = document.querySelector('h1'); const container = h1.parentElement; const ul = container.children[3]; const lastLi = ul.lastElementChild; lastLi.textContent = 'Modified by JS'; ``` **Output verification:** The third bullet now reads "Modified by JS" while the structure remains untouched. ## 3.4 Worked Example B — Node-Based vs Element-Based Pitfall This example shows the **whitespace trap** that the KTU examiner loves to test. ```javascript const ul = document.querySelector('ul'); console.log(ul.firstChild.nodeType); // Output: 3 (TEXT_NODE — the newline + spaces) console.log(ul.firstChild.nodeValue); // Output: "\n " (visible whitespace!) console.log(ul.firstElementChild.textContent); // Output: "One" ``` ### Step-by-step logical deduction | Step | Expression | Internal Action | Result | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | `ul.firstChild` | Returns the very first node in `ul.childNodes` | TEXT_NODE (whitespace) | | 2 | `.nodeType` | Reads `nodeType` property of node | `3` (TEXT_NODE) | | 3 | `.nodeValue` | Reads text content of the text node | `"\n "` | | 4 | `ul.firstElementChild` | Skips all non-element nodes | `<li>One</li>` | | 5 | `.textContent` | Concatenates all descendant text | `"One"` | > [!WARNING] > **The 7-Mark KTU Trap:** If the question says "access the first child of `<ul>`", many students write `ul.firstChild` without realizing whitespace text nodes intervene. The *correct* answer depends on interpretation — use `firstElementChild` for elements, and explain the whitespace issue in your solution to earn full marks. ## 3.5 Worked Example C — `closest()` for Ancestor Lookup **Question:** Given a click on a nested `<span>` deep inside a `<section>`, find the closest `<article>` ancestor and add a CSS class `highlight` to it. ```javascript document.querySelector('span').addEventListener('click', function(e) { const article = e.target.closest('article'); if (article) { article.classList.add('highlight'); } }); ``` ### Step-by-step traversal order `closest('article')` walks the ancestor chain in the following order: $$ \text{span} \rightarrow \text{parent element} \rightarrow \ldots \rightarrow \text{html} \rightarrow \text{document} $$ The search **includes the element itself** and stops at the **first** match. If no ancestor matches, the method returns `null` — the `if` guard prevents a `TypeError`. ## 3.6 Worked Example D — `querySelectorAll` Iteration ```javascript const items = document.querySelectorAll('ul li'); items.forEach((li, index) => { li.textContent = `Item ${index + 1}`; }); ``` `querySelectorAll` returns a **static** `NodeList`. The result does not update if the DOM changes later, which is why this method is preferred for snapshot operations. The `forEach` method is a property of `NodeList` (not inherited from `Array`). ## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What | Scenario | Recommended API | Reason | |---|---|---| | Find an element by id | `getElementById('id')` | Fastest, returns single element or `null` | | Find descendants by CSS selector | `querySelector / querySelectorAll` | Most flexible, modern standard | | Iterate only child elements | `children` | Skips whitespace, returns live `HTMLCollection` | | Need to include text/comment nodes | `childNodes` | Returns every node type | | Navigate sideways safely | `nextElementSibling` | Skips whitespace text nodes | | Find ancestor by selector | `closest(selector)` | Modern, includes self, returns `null` if absent | | Check if node is an element | `node.nodeType === 1` | Reliable, faster than `instanceof` | <!-- SECTION_3_END --> <!-- SECTION_4_START --> # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — DOM Tree of the Skeleton ```mermaid graph TD doc[document] html[html] head[head] title[title] titleText[KTU Demo TEXT] body[body] comment[comment node] container[div id container] h1[h1] h1Text[Hello KTU] p1[p class intro 1] p2[p class intro 2] ul[ul] li1[li One] li2[li Two] li3[li Three] doc --> html html --> head html --> body head --> title title --> titleText body --> comment body --> container container --> h1 h1 --> h1Text container --> p1 container --> p2 container --> ul ul --> li1 ul --> li2 ul --> li3 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Traversal Direction Map ```mermaid graph LR A[START NODE] -->|parentNode / parentElement| B[UPWARD] A -->|firstChild / children| C[DOWNWARD] A -->|nextSibling / nextElementSibling| D[SIDEWAYS RIGHT] A -->|previousSibling / previousElementSibling| E[SIDEWAYS LEFT] B --> F[closest selector] C --> G[querySelector] D --> H[walk loop] E --> H ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — NodeList vs HTMLCollection Decision Flow ```mermaid graph TD Q1{Need only element nodes?} Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Static or live?} Q1 -->|No| A1[Use childNodes — NodeList] Q2 -->|Static| A2[querySelectorAll] Q2 -->|Live| A3[Use children — HTMLCollection] A2 --> Z[Result is static NodeList] A3 --> Y[Result is live HTMLCollection] A1 --> X[Includes text and comment nodes] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence — `closest()` Search Algorithm ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant User participant Span as span element participant Parent as parent element participant Grand as ancestor chain participant Article as article element participant Document as document root User->>Span: click event Span->>Span: check self matches article? alt not match Span->>Parent: move upward Parent->>Grand: move upward Grand->>Article: check match? alt match found Article-->>User: return article else no match Article->>Document: continue to root Document-->>User: return null end end ``` <!-- SECTION_4_END --> <!-- SECTION_5_START --> # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Differentiate between `NodeList` and `HTMLCollection` in JavaScript. Mention at least three differences.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | `NodeList` | `HTMLCollection` | |---|---|---| | **Node types** | Contains all node types (elements, text, comments) | Contains only element nodes | | **Liveness** | Static when returned by `querySelectorAll`; live when returned by `childNodes` | Always live | | **Iterability** | Supports `forEach` and `for...of` | Supports only index-based `for` loop | | **Examples of returns** | `querySelectorAll`, `childNodes` | `getElementsByTagName`, `children` | (3 marks: 1 mark per difference explained clearly) --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Explain the difference between `firstChild` and `firstElementChild`. Why is `firstElementChild` generally preferred in modern JavaScript?** **Model Answer:** - `firstChild` returns the first child node of any type, including text nodes (such as whitespace newlines from HTML formatting) and comment nodes. Its `nodeType` is `3` (TEXT_NODE) or `8` (COMMENT_NODE). - `firstElementChild` returns only the first child that is an Element node (`nodeType === 1`), automatically skipping whitespace and comments. - `firstElementChild` is preferred in modern code because HTML is often formatted with indentation, creating invisible text nodes that `firstChild` would unexpectedly return. This avoids bugs and makes traversal code more predictable. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (Choice 1)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 14 Marks]** **(a)** With the help of a suitable HTML document, explain the concept of **DOM traversal** using Node-based properties: `parentNode`, `childNodes`, `firstChild`, `lastChild`, `nextSibling`, and `previousSibling`. Write a JavaScript snippet to print the names of all direct child elements of the `<body>` tag. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain with examples the difference between `HTMLCollection` and `NodeList`. Why does the `forEach` method work on `NodeList` but not directly on `HTMLCollection`? Show how to convert an `HTMLCollection` to an array using `Array.from()`. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a) **Step 1 — Define the HTML Document** ```html <!DOCTYPE html> <html> <body> <h1>Welcome</h1> <p>Paragraph one.</p> <ul> <li>Apple</li> <li>Banana</li> </ul> <div>Footer</div> </body> </html> ``` **Step 2 — Concept Explanation (3 marks)** - `parentNode` returns the immediate ancestor node. - `childNodes` returns a live `NodeList` of *all* child nodes (elements, text, comments). - `firstChild` and `lastChild` return the first and last child *nodes* (any type). - `nextSibling` and `previousSibling` return adjacent sibling *nodes*. **Step 3 — JavaScript Snippet (4 marks)** ```javascript const body = document.body; let current = body.firstChild; const childNames = []; while (current) { if (current.nodeType === 1) { // ELEMENT_NODE check childNames.push(current.tagName); } current = current.nextSibling; } console.log(childNames); // Output: ["H1", "P", "UL", "DIV"] ``` **Step 4 — Logic Explanation** - We start with `body.firstChild` which may be a whitespace text node. - The `while` loop walks through every sibling using `nextSibling`. - A `nodeType === 1` filter ensures we only collect Element nodes, ignoring whitespace. - Final array contains only the tag names of element children of `<body>`. #### Model Solution to (b) **Step 1 — HTMLCollection vs NodeList (3 marks)** - `HTMLCollection` contains only Element nodes, is always live, and does not have `.forEach()`. - `NodeList` can contain any node type, may be static or live, and supports `.forEach()`. **Step 2 — Why `forEach` works on `NodeList` (2 marks)** The DOM specification defines a `forEach` method on the `NodeList` interface for browsers conforming to the modern DOM living standard. The `HTMLCollection` interface, however, was defined earlier and does not include `forEach`. Hence it lacks this method. **Step 3 — Converting to Array (2 marks)** ```javascript const divs = document.getElementsByTagName('div'); // HTMLCollection const divsArray = Array.from(divs); // Now an Array divsArray.forEach(div => { console.log(div.id); }); ``` `Array.from()` creates a new `Array` instance from any iterable or array-like object, enabling the use of array methods like `map`, `filter`, and `forEach`. --- ### **Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 14 Marks]** **(a)** Explain the working of `querySelector`, `querySelectorAll`, and `closest()` with examples. Compare how they differ in traversal direction and result type. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Given the following HTML, write a JavaScript function `highlightSiblings(element)` that adds the class `active` to all *element siblings* of the given element but not to the element itself. Explain why using `nextElementSibling` is more reliable than `nextSibling` in this context. **(7 marks)** ```html <section> <p>One</p> <p id="target">Two</p> <p>Three</p> <!-- comment --> <p>Four</p> </section> ``` #### Model Solution to (a) **Step 1 — `querySelector` (2 marks)** - Returns the **first** Element matching a CSS selector, searching **downward** through descendants. - Returns `null` if no match exists. - Example: `document.querySelector('.intro')` returns the first element with class `intro`. **Step 2 — `querySelectorAll` (2 marks)** - Returns **all** matching descendants as a **static** `NodeList`. - The collection does not change when the DOM is later modified. - Example: `document.querySelectorAll('p.intro')` returns every `<p>` with class `intro`. **Step 3 — `closest()` (2 marks)** - Searches **upward** (self → parents → root) for the nearest ancestor matching a CSS selector. - Returns `null` if nothing matches. - Example: `child.closest('section')` returns the enclosing `<section>` element. **Step 4 — Comparison Table (1 mark)** | Method | Direction | Returns | Static/Live | |---|---|---|---| | `querySelector` | Downward | Single Element or `null` | — | | `querySelectorAll` | Downward | Static `NodeList` | Static | | `closest` | Upward | Single Element or `null` | — | #### Model Solution to (b) **Step 1 — The Function (4 marks)** ```javascript function highlightSiblings(element) { // Traverse forward let sibling = element.nextElementSibling; while (sibling) { sibling.classList.add('active'); sibling = sibling.nextElementSibling; } // Traverse backward sibling = element.previousElementSibling; while (sibling) { sibling.classList.add('active'); sibling = sibling.previousElementSibling; } } const target = document.getElementById('target'); highlightSiblings(target); ``` **Step 2 — Result** After execution, the `<p>` elements with text "One", "Three", and "Four" all receive the `active` class. The HTML comment between "Three" and "Four" is correctly skipped. **Step 3 — Why `nextElementSibling` is more reliable (3 marks)** - The HTML contains a comment node between the `<p>Three</p>` and `<p>Four</p>` tags. - If we used `nextSibling` after "Three", we would land on the comment node (nodeType 8), not the next `<p>`. - We would then have to write extra logic to check `nodeType === 1` and skip non-element nodes manually. - `nextElementSibling` already filters for Element nodes automatically, making the code cleaner, more readable, and less error-prone. This is the recommended approach in modern JavaScript DOM traversal. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Whitespace Blindness (–2 marks):** Writing `firstChild` and expecting an element, ignoring that newlines and spaces in formatted HTML create text nodes. Always mention this in your answer. > 2. **Confusing `getElementById` with `getElementsByClassName`** (–1 mark): The former returns a single element; the latter returns a live `HTMLCollection` requiring an index. > 3. **Forgetting `null` checks** (–1 mark): `querySelector` and `closest` can return `null`; accessing properties on `null` throws `TypeError`. Always guard with `if` statements. > 4. **Static vs Live Collections** (–2 marks): Iterating over a live `HTMLCollection` while removing elements causes skipping. Use `querySelectorAll` for snapshots. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print Before Exam** - ✅ DOM is a **tree of nodes** with `document` as the root. - ✅ There are **two traversal families**: Node-based (universal) and Element-based (element-only). - ✅ **Whitespace between tags creates TEXT_NODES** — they are real nodes in the tree. - ✅ `parentNode` and `parentElement` differ only when the parent is a `Document` or `DocumentFragment`. - ✅ `children` returns a **live** `HTMLCollection` of element children. - ✅ `childNodes` returns a **live or static** `NodeList` of all child nodes. - ✅ `firstElementChild` is **safer** than `firstChild` in formatted HTML. - ✅ `nextElementSibling` and `previousElementSibling` skip text and comment nodes. - ✅ `closest(selector)` searches **upward** including self; `querySelector` searches **downward** among descendants. - ✅ `querySelectorAll` returns a **static** `NodeList` — safe for snapshot iteration. - ✅ `NodeList.forEach()` exists; `HTMLCollection.forEach()` does not — use `Array.from()` to convert. - ✅ Always check for `null` after `querySelector` and `closest` calls. - ✅ For **event delegation**, attach one listener to a parent and use `closest` to identify the target. <!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working with Text Content and Modifying HTML
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Working with Text Content and Modifying HTML ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) **Working with Text Content and Modifying HTML** refers to the set of DOM (Document Object Model) manipulation techniques that allow a JavaScript program to **read, create, insert, update, replace, and delete** textual content and HTML structural elements within a live web page. It is a foundational pillar of Dynamic HTML (DHTML) and forms the basis of all modern Single Page Application (SPA) frameworks like React, Angular, and Vue. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Code: PBITT504), this topic belongs to **Module 1: Introduction to Web Development & DOM Scripting**, and is directly mapped to the following Course Outcomes: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Course Outcomes Addressed:** > - **CO1:** Understand the architecture of web applications and the role of client-side scripting. > - **CO2:** Apply JavaScript DOM APIs to dynamically modify HTML content and structure. The three primary properties used to interact with text content are: - `node.textContent` — Represents the **raw text** of a node and all its descendants, ignoring CSS styling. - `node.innerText` — Represents the **rendered (visible) text**, aware of CSS (e.g., hides text styled with `display: none`). - `node.innerHTML` — Represents the **HTML markup** (tags + text) inside an element, allowing full structural injection. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: "The Restaurant Menu Board"** > > Imagine a **digital menu board** outside a restaurant: > - **`textContent`** is like reading the **chef's recipe book** — you get the exact raw ingredients and instructions, completely unaffected by lighting or decoration. > - **`innerText`** is like reading the **menu board as a customer sees it** — only dishes that are *currently lit up* and *visible* on the board appear. If a dish is marked "sold out" (display: none), you don't see it. > - **`innerHTML`** is like having **full access to the design studio** — you can rewrite not just the dish names, but the entire layout, including boxes, colors, and sub-headings. > > **Modifying HTML** is then analogous to the manager walking up to the board with a fresh set of digital stickers, either **adding** a new dish, **replacing** an old one, or **removing** one entirely — all without reprinting the entire board. --- ### 1.3 Why This Topic is High-Yield in KTU Exams This is one of the **most frequently tested topics** in PBITT504 because: 1. It directly tests **practical coding skills** (a KTU 2024 emphasis). 2. It bridges the gap between **HTML structure** and **JavaScript behavior**. 3. KTU examiners frequently ask students to **distinguish between `textContent` and `innerHTML`** — a classic 3-mark question. 4. Questions on **dynamic table generation, form validation feedback, and DOM traversal** rely entirely on these APIs. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Core DOM Text Properties — Deep Analysis The DOM (Document Object Model) treats every piece of an HTML document as a **node** in a tree structure. There are several node types, but the three most relevant for this topic are: | Node Type | Constant Value | Description | |---|---|---| | **Element Node** | `Node.ELEMENT_NODE (1)` | Represents an HTML tag (`

`, `

`, etc.) | | **Text Node** | `Node.TEXT_NODE (3)` | Represents raw text content inside an element | | **Document Fragment** | `Node.DOCUMENT_FRAGMENT_NODE (11)` | A lightweight container for batch DOM operations | --- #### 2.1.1 `textContent` Property **Definition:** Returns or sets the textual content of the specified node, including all descendant text, **ignoring any HTML tags** and **CSS visibility**. **Operational Logic:** 1. **Reading (`get`):** Recursively concatenates the `textContent` of all child nodes. 2. **Writing (`set`):** Replaces the entire subtree of the element with a **single text node** containing the supplied string. All child elements and HTML formatting are **destroyed**. **Example:** ```html
Hello Hidden World
``` ```javascript let el = document.getElementById("demo"); console.log(el.textContent); // Output: "Hello Hidden World" el.textContent = "New Text"; // Replaces EVERYTHING inside #demo ``` --- #### 2.1.2 `innerText` Property **Definition:** Represents the "rendered" text content — what the user *visually sees* on the screen. **Key Behavioral Differences from `textContent`:** - **CSS-Aware:** Hidden text (`display: none` or `visibility: hidden`) is **not** returned. - **Performance Hit:** Triggers a **layout reflow** to determine actual rendered output, making it **slower**. - **Whitespace:** Collapses consecutive whitespace, mimicking browser rendering. --- #### 2.1.3 `innerHTML` Property **Definition:** Returns or sets the **HTML markup** (a serialized string) contained within an element. **Operational Logic:** 1. **Reading:** Serializes all descendant HTML into a string. 2. **Writing:** Parses the supplied HTML string and **rebuilds the subtree** with new DOM nodes. > [!WARNING] > **SECURITY ALERT — XSS (Cross-Site Scripting):** > Never insert **user-supplied data** into `innerHTML` without sanitization. Malicious scripts like `` will execute immediately. KTU examiners often test this in viva questions! --- ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Operation | JavaScript Syntax | Returns | Throws on Invalid Input? | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Read raw text | `el.textContent` | String (all descendants) | No | | 2 | Read visible text | `el.innerText` | String (visible only) | No | | 3 | Read HTML markup | `el.innerHTML` | String (HTML serialized) | No | | 4 | Set raw text | `el.textContent = "Hi"` | undefined | No | | 5 | Set HTML | `el.innerHTML = "Hi"` | undefined | No | | 6 | Create element | `document.createElement(tag)` | New Element | No (returns null on failure) | | 7 | Create text node | `document.createTextNode(text)` | New TextNode | No | | 8 | Append child | `parent.appendChild(node)` | Appended node | Yes (HierarchyRequestError) | | 9 | Append (modern) | `parent.append(...nodes)` | undefined | No | | 10 | Prepend child | `parent.prepend(node)` | undefined | No | | 11 | Insert before | `parent.insertBefore(new, ref)` | Inserted node | Yes | | 12 | Insert adjacent | `el.insertAdjacentHTML(pos, html)` | undefined | Yes (SyntaxError) | | 13 | Remove child | `parent.removeChild(child)` | Removed node | Yes (NotFoundError) | | 14 | Remove (modern) | `el.remove()` | undefined | No | | 15 | Replace child | `parent.replaceChild(new, old)` | Replaced node | Yes | | 16 | Replace (modern) | `el.replaceWith(newNode)` | undefined | No | | 17 | Get attribute | `el.getAttribute(name)` | String $\vert$ null | No | | 18 | Set attribute | `el.setAttribute(name, val)` | undefined | No | | 19 | Remove attribute | `el.removeAttribute(name)` | undefined | No | | 20 | Toggle class | `el.classList.toggle(name)` | Boolean | No | --- ### 2.3 `insertAdjacentHTML()` — The High-Yield KTU API This method is **frequently tested** in KTU exams. It parses the specified HTML and inserts it at one of four positions relative to the target element: ```javascript element.insertAdjacentHTML(position, text); ``` | Position | Meaning | Visual | |---|---|---| | `"beforebegin"` | Before the element itself | ` ` | | `"afterbegin"` | Inside, before first child | `old` | | `"beforeend"` | Inside, after last child | `old` | | `"afterend"` | After the element itself | ` ` | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** This method is **faster** than `innerHTML += ...` because it does **not** re-parse the existing descendants. Modern frameworks like React and Vue use this principle under the hood for efficient list rendering. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Building a Dynamic To-Do List (Exhaustive Walkthrough) **Problem Statement (typical KTU 14-mark style):** *"Write a JavaScript program that accepts a task from an input field and dynamically appends it as a new list item to an unordered list. Each new item must include a 'Delete' button that removes itself from the list when clicked."* --- #### Step 1: HTML Scaffold (Static Skeleton) ```html Dynamic To-Do List

My Tasks

    ``` --- #### Step 2: JavaScript Logic — Exhaustive Line-by-Line Derivation ```javascript // Step 2.1: Cache references to the DOM elements we will manipulate // WHY: Repeated DOM lookups are expensive. Storing once improves performance. const inputField = document.getElementById("taskInput"); const addButton = document.getElementById("addBtn"); const taskList = document.getElementById("taskList"); // Step 2.2: Register an event handler on the "Add" button // WHY: We use 'click' because it works on both mouse and keyboard (Enter when focused) // accessibility considerations. addButton.addEventListener("click", addTask); // Step 2.3: Define the function that runs every time the user clicks "Add" function addTask() { // Step 2.3.1: Trim and capture the input value // WHY: Trim removes accidental leading/trailing whitespace. const taskText = inputField.value.trim(); // Step 2.3.2: Guard clause — prevent empty submissions // WHY: UX safeguard and prevents empty
  • elements from cluttering the DOM. if (taskText === "") { alert("Please enter a valid task!"); return; } // Step 2.3.3: Create a new
  • element using document.createElement // WHY: The
  • is the structural container for each task. const li = document.createElement("li"); // Step 2.3.4: Set the text content of the
  • // WHY: We use textContent (not innerHTML) to PREVENT XSS attacks. // If we used innerHTML, a user could type as a "task". li.textContent = taskText; // Step 2.3.5: Create a delete button const deleteBtn = document.createElement("button"); // Step 2.3.6: Set the button's label deleteBtn.textContent = "Delete"; // Step 2.3.7: Add a CSS class for styling (using classList API) // WHY: Keeps JS logic separate from CSS presentation. deleteBtn.classList.add("delete-btn"); // Step 2.3.8: Attach a click handler to the delete button // WHY: We use an arrow function with .remove() — the modern way to delete a node. deleteBtn.addEventListener("click", function () { li.remove(); // Removes the
  • from its parent (the
      ) }); // Step 2.3.9: Append the delete button INSIDE the
    • // WHY: We use append() (modern, can accept multiple args) instead of appendChild(). li.append(deleteBtn); // Step 2.3.10: Append the fully constructed
    • to the
        taskList.appendChild(li); // Step 2.3.11: Clear the input field for the next entry inputField.value = ""; } ``` --- #### Step 3: Execution Trace (How the DOM Tree Grows) Let $T_n$ denote the DOM state after the $n$-th "Add" click. We model the transformation: $$ T_0 = \text{HTML: } \langle ul\ id=\text{"taskList"} \rangle \ \ [\text{empty}] $$ After the user enters "Buy milk" and clicks Add: $$ T_1 = \langle ul\ id=\text{"taskList"} \rangle \rightarrow \langle li \rangle \rightarrow \left\{ \text{"Buy milk"},\ \langle button\ \text{"Delete"} \rangle \right\} $$ After entering "Pay bills": $$ T_2 = \langle ul \rangle \rightarrow \begin{cases} \langle li_1 \rangle \rightarrow \{\text{"Buy milk"},\ \langle btn_1 \rangle\} \\ \langle li_2 \rangle \rightarrow \{\text{"Pay bills"},\ \langle btn_2 \rangle\} \end{cases} $$ If the user clicks `btn_1`: $$ T_2' = \langle ul \rangle \rightarrow \langle li_2 \rangle \rightarrow \{\text{"Pay bills"},\ \langle btn_2 \rangle\} $$ The `` node is **detached** from the tree and becomes eligible for **garbage collection**. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 2: `textContent` vs `innerHTML` — A Comparative Trace **Initial HTML:** ```html
        Hello World!
        ``` | Operation | Code | Resulting HTML in `
        ` | |---|---|---| | Read textContent | `box.textContent` | `"Hello World!"` (raw, no tags) | | Read innerHTML | `box.innerHTML` | `"\n Hello World!\n"` (with tags) | | Set textContent | `box.textContent = "Hi"` | `
        Hi
        ` (tags destroyed) | | Set innerHTML | `box.innerHTML = "Hi"` | `
        Hi
        ` (tags parsed) | **Mathematical Representation of the Difference:** Let $H$ be the HTML string and $T$ be the plain text. The two operations are related by the **strip-tags function** $\text{strip}: H \mapsto T$: $$ T = \text{strip}(H) = \text{regex-strip}(H, /<[^>]*>/g, "") $$ Whereas `innerHTML` operates on the full $H$ space, `textContent` operates on $T = \text{strip}(H)$. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What? | Scenario | Recommended Property/Method | Reason | |---|---|---| | Display user-typed comment safely | `textContent` | XSS-safe, no parsing | | Inject a formatted list from server | `innerHTML` | Fast, supports rich markup | | Check what user actually sees | `innerText` | CSS-aware | | Add element to end of list | `append()` or `appendChild()` | Idiomatic | | Add element to start of list | `prepend()` | Modern, readable | | Add element relative to a specific node | `insertAdjacentElement()` | Precise positioning | | Add raw HTML at specific position | `insertAdjacentHTML()` | Fastest for HTML strings | | Remove a node (have parent) | `parent.removeChild(node)` | Legacy-compatible | | Remove a node (don't have parent) | `node.remove()` | Modern, simpler | | Build a large list in memory, then attach | `DocumentFragment` | Minimizes reflows | | Update one attribute | `setAttribute()` | Universal, works for any attr | | Update a standard prop (id, href) | `el.id = ...`, `el.href = ...` | Faster, typed | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The DOM Text Manipulation Lifecycle The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **decision flow** a developer follows when working with text content and modifying HTML: ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Need to modify web page] --> B{Read or Write?} B -->|Read| C{Need HTML or plain text?} B -->|Write| J{Plain text or HTML markup?} C -->|Plain text visible| D[Use innerText] C -->|Plain text all| E[Use textContent] C -->|Full HTML| F[Use innerHTML] J -->|Plain text| G[el.textContent = value] J -->|HTML markup| K{Untrusted user input?} K -->|Yes| L[Use textContent + createElement] K -->|No| M[Use innerHTML or insertAdjacentHTML] G --> N[Done] F --> N D --> N E --> N L --> N M --> N ``` --- ### 4.2 The Four Positions of `insertAdjacentHTML` — Block Diagram ```mermaid graph LR subgraph TargetElement["Target Element (T)"] direction TB T1["Existing Children of T"] end BB["beforebegin: New HTML placed BEFORE T (as previous sibling)"] -.-> T1 AB["afterbegin: New HTML placed as FIRST child of T"] -.-> T1 BE["beforeend: New HTML placed as LAST child of T"] -.-> T1 AE["afterend: New HTML placed AFTER T (as next sibling)"] -.-> T1 ``` **Visual Layout of Positions (4-Quadrant Model):** | Position | Where it lands | KTU Code Pattern | |---|---|---| | `beforebegin` | Predecessor sibling | `T.insertAdjacentHTML("beforebegin", html)` | | `afterbegin` | First child | `T.insertAdjacentHTML("afterbegin", html)` | | `beforeend` | Last child | `T.insertAdjacentHTML("beforeend", html)` | | `afterend` | Successor sibling | `T.insertAdjacentHTML("afterend", html)` | --- ### 4.3 DOM Tree After Dynamic To-Do List Insertion ```mermaid graph TD UL["ul id=taskList"] LI1["li #1: Buy milk"] LI2["li #2: Pay bills"] BTN1["button: Delete"] BTN2["button: Delete"] TXT1["text: Buy milk"] TXT2["text: Pay bills"] UL --> LI1 UL --> LI2 LI1 --> TXT1 LI1 --> BTN1 LI2 --> TXT2 LI2 --> BTN2 ``` --- ### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Text Modification Subsystem ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Client["Client-Side Browser Engine"] HTML["Static HTML Document"] JS["JavaScript Engine"] DOMAPI["DOM API Layer"] RENDER["Rendering Engine"] end HTML --> DOMAPI JS -->|Reads / Writes| DOMAPI DOMAPI -->|Mutates| HTML HTML --> RENDER RENDER -->|Visible Pixels| User((End User)) User -->|Clicks / Types| JS ``` **Explanation of the Architecture:** 1. The browser parses the static HTML into a **DOM tree** stored in memory. 2. The JavaScript engine executes scripts that call **DOM API methods** (the focus of this topic). 3. These APIs mutate the in-memory DOM tree. 4. The **rendering engine** observes the changes and repaints the screen (a "reflow/repaint"). 5. The user interacts, and the cycle continues — this is the **DHTML (Dynamic HTML) loop**. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- #### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** **Differentiate between `textContent`, `innerText`, and `innerHTML` in JavaScript DOM manipulation. State one scenario where each is preferred.** **Model Answer:** | Property | `textContent` | `innerText` | `innerHTML` | |---|---|---|---| | **Returns** | All text, including hidden | Only visible/rendered text | HTML markup string | | **CSS-Aware?** | No | Yes | No | | **Performance** | Fastest (no layout) | Slowest (causes reflow) | Medium | | **XSS Safe (when writing)?** | Yes | Yes | **No** — parses HTML | **Preferred Scenarios:** - `textContent` → Displaying user input safely (e.g., chat messages). - `innerText` → Extracting text exactly as a user sees it for copy-paste. - `innerHTML` → Injecting a structured HTML fragment from a trusted source. **[3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for differentiation, 1 for scenarios]** --- #### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]** **Explain the role of `document.createElement()` and `document.createTextNode()`. How do they differ from directly using `innerHTML`?** **Model Answer:** - `document.createElement(tagName)` creates a new **Element Node** in memory (e.g., a `
        `). It is **not** attached to the document until you call `appendChild()` or `append()`. - `document.createTextNode(string)` creates a new **Text Node** containing raw text. It cannot contain HTML tags. - `innerHTML`, by contrast, **parses an HTML string** into multiple DOM nodes and immediately attaches them. **Key Difference:** `createElement`/`createTextNode` are **safe from XSS** because they don't parse HTML. They also allow **fine-grained event listener attachment** to individual nodes, which is impossible with a single `innerHTML` string. **[3 marks — 1 each for createElement, createTextNode, and difference from innerHTML]** --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** **(a)** With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the **Document Object Model (DOM) tree structure**. Describe how JavaScript can access and modify HTML elements using `getElementById`, `querySelector`, and `getElementsByClassName`. **   [7 Marks]** **(b)** Write a complete JavaScript program that creates an HTML page containing a **dynamic multiplication table (1 to 10)** for a number entered by the user. The table should be generated entirely via DOM manipulation (no hardcoded HTML rows). **   [7 Marks]** --- ##### Model Solution for Q.A(a): **DOM Tree Diagram:** ```mermaid graph TD DOC["#document"] HTML["html"] HEAD["head"] BODY["body"] TITLE["title"] H1["h1: Multiplication Table"] INPUT["input id=num"] BTN["button id=gen"] TABLE["table id=result"] DOC --> HTML HTML --> HEAD HTML --> BODY HEAD --> TITLE BODY --> H1 BODY --> INPUT BODY --> BTN BODY --> TABLE ``` **JavaScript Element Access Methods:** | Method | Syntax | Returns | When to Use | |---|---|---|---| | `getElementById` | `document.getElementById("id")` | Single Element $\vert$ null | When the element has a known unique ID | | `querySelector` | `document.querySelector("css-sel")` | First match $\vert$ null | Complex CSS selectors, e.g., `div.active > p:first-child` | | `getElementsByClassName` | `document.getElementsByClassName("cls")` | **HTMLCollection** (live) | Multiple elements with same class | **Important Properties of the Returned Collections:** - `getElementById` and `querySelector` return a **static, single** reference. - `getElementsByClassName` returns a **live HTMLCollection** — it auto-updates if the DOM changes. - `querySelectorAll` returns a **static NodeList** — does NOT auto-update. --- ##### Model Solution for Q.A(b): **HTML Skeleton:** ```html

        Multiplication Table Generator

        ``` **Sample Output (when `n = 7`):** $$ \begin{aligned} 7 \times 1 &= 7 \\ 7 \times 2 &= 14 \\ 7 \times 3 &= 21 \\ \vdots \\ 7 \times 10 &= 70 \end{aligned} $$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** **(a)** Explain the **four positions** of the `insertAdjacentHTML()` method with a clear diagram. Compare it with `innerHTML` in terms of **performance and use cases**. **   [7 Marks]** **(b)** Write a JavaScript program that takes the contents of a `

        Characters: 0   |   Words: 0

        ``` **Why we used `textContent` (not `innerHTML`):** The user types into the textarea, and we need to reflect **only plain numbers** back to the page. Using `textContent` prevents any XSS risk and is faster than `innerHTML`. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to attach the new element to the DOM** — Students often call `document.createElement("div")` but forget `parent.appendChild(div)`. The element is created in memory but **never visible** on the page. This is the #1 reason students lose 3–4 marks. > 2. **Confusing `appendChild()` return value** — `appendChild()` returns the **appended node**, not the parent's updated length. Writing `parent.appendChild(li).length` is a common bug. > 3. **Using `innerHTML` for user data** — Security flaw, will be penalized heavily in 2024 scheme exams. > 4. **Spelling `textcontent` or `innerHTML` in camelCase wrong** — JavaScript is **case-sensitive**. `Textcontent` will throw `TypeError`. > 5. **Not handling whitespace** — `textContent` returns whitespace literally; `innerText` collapses it. If a question asks for "visible" text, only `innerText` qualifies. > 6. **Trying to use `removeChild` without parent reference** — Use the modern `node.remove()` method to avoid this error. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin This Before Exams!):** - ✅ **Three text properties** to master: `textContent` (raw), `innerText` (visible), `innerHTML` (markup). - ✅ **Two creation methods:** `createElement()` for elements, `createTextNode()` for text. - ✅ **Four insertion positions** of `insertAdjacentHTML`: `beforebegin`, `afterbegin`, `beforeend`, `afterend`. - ✅ **Append vs AppendChild:** `append()` accepts multiple nodes AND strings; `appendChild()` accepts only one Node. - ✅ **Modern removal:** `node.remove()` is preferred over `parent.removeChild(node)`. - ✅ **`textContent` is XSS-safe**; `innerHTML` is **NOT** — never use it for untrusted input. - ✅ **`innerText` is slow** (causes reflow); avoid it in performance-critical loops. - ✅ **`DocumentFragment`** is a hidden container for batch DOM updates to minimize repaints. - ✅ **The `classList` API** (`add`, `remove`, `toggle`, `contains`) is the modern way to manage classes. - ✅ **Always `trim()` user input** before validation to avoid whitespace-based bypass. - ✅ **`querySelector` vs `querySelectorAll`:** First returns ONE element, second returns a static NodeList. - ✅ **`getElementById` is the fastest** element-access method (browser-optimized hash lookup). - ✅ **Setting `el.textContent = ""`** is the **fastest way to clear** an element's contents. - ✅ **Event delegation** (a related but advanced concept) lets you handle events on dynamically added elements using a single parent listener. --- > **END OF MODULE 1 NOTES — Working with Text Content and Modifying HTML** > *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | Course: PBITT504 | Prepared per KTU-Premier-Engine V10 Standards*

topics.title: Adding, Removing, and Modifying Elements and Attributes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Adding, Removing, and Modifying Elements and Attributes ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition **DOM (Document Object Model) Manipulation** is the process of dynamically creating, inserting, deleting, and altering HTML elements and their attributes using JavaScript at runtime. The **W3C DOM API** exposes a standardized tree of nodes representing the document, allowing scripts to modify both the **structure** (elements/nodes) and the **presentation** (attributes, styles, text content) of a webpage without requiring a full page reload. The four fundamental mutation operations taught in KTU Module 1 are: 1. **Creating** new elements (`document.createElement`) 2. **Adding / Inserting** elements into the DOM tree (`appendChild`, `prepend`, `insertBefore`, `append`) 3. **Removing** elements from the DOM tree (`removeChild`, `remove`) 4. **Modifying** elements and their attributes (`innerHTML`, `textContent`, `setAttribute`, `removeAttribute`, `classList`, `style`) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBITT504 – Module 1):** Students must be proficient with the **Element Object methods** and **Node Object methods** defined in the DOM Level 1/2/3 specifications. Questions in the End Semester Examination (ESE) frequently test `createElement`, `appendChild`, `removeChild`, and `setAttribute`/`getAttribute` syntax. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > Think of a **webpage as a living LEGO structure** 🧱. > - The **HTML document** is your instruction manual. > - The **DOM** is the actual LEGO model built from that manual, sitting on your table. > - **JavaScript** is your hand — it can *add* new bricks (`createElement` + `appendChild`), *remove* unwanted bricks (`removeChild` or `.remove()`), or *recolor/relabel* existing bricks (`setAttribute`, `style.color = "red"`). > - Every time you modify the DOM, the browser **repaints** the visible webpage instantly. ### Why This Matters in Web Development - Enables **Single Page Applications (SPAs)** like Gmail, Twitter, and Facebook where content updates without reloads. - Powers **dynamic forms**, **to-do lists**, **live search suggestions**, **shopping carts**, and **interactive dashboards**. - Forms the foundation for frameworks like **React, Vue, and Angular** which use a *Virtual DOM* — a concept that exists *only* because direct DOM mutation is the underlying primitive. ### Key DOM Node Types (KTU Must-Know) | Node Type | Constant Value | Description | |---|---|---| | Element Node | `1` | An HTML element like `
`, `

` | | Attribute Node | `2` | An attribute of an element | | Text Node | `3` | The text inside an element | | Comment Node | `8` | An HTML comment `` | | Document Node | `9` | The root `document` object | | DocumentFragment | `11` | A lightweight container for batch DOM updates | > [!TIP] > **Verification Trick for Exams:** Use `node.nodeType` in JavaScript to identify any node type at runtime. This is a frequently asked **3-mark question**. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The DOM Tree — Mental Model The browser parses HTML into a hierarchical **tree of nodes**. Every HTML tag becomes an **Element node**, every text becomes a **Text node**, and every attribute is associated with its parent element. JavaScript can navigate and mutate any node in this tree because each node exposes properties like `parentNode`, `childNodes`, `firstChild`, `lastChild`, `nextSibling`, and `previousSibling`. ### 2.2 Adding Elements — The 4-Step Pattern Every element addition in raw JavaScript follows this canonical pattern: 1. **Create** the element using `document.createElement(tagName)`. 2. **Configure** it — set text, attributes, classes, or styles. 3. **Select** the parent node where the new element should be attached. 4. **Attach** the new element to the parent using one of the insertion methods. The four primary insertion methods are: - `parent.appendChild(child)` — appends as the **last child** of the parent. - `parent.insertBefore(newNode, referenceNode)` — inserts *before* a specific existing child. - `parent.prepend(child)` — inserts as the **first child** (modern). - `parent.append(...children)` — accepts **multiple** children or strings (modern). ### 2.3 Removing Elements Two equivalent approaches: - **Old approach:** `parent.removeChild(child)` — requires knowing the parent. - **Modern approach:** `child.remove()` — called directly on the element to be deleted (no parent needed). > [!NOTE] > **Why `remove()` is preferred in modern code:** It eliminates the need to traverse the tree upward to find `parentNode`, making the code cleaner and less error-prone. ### 2.4 Modifying Elements and Attributes Modification spans **five dimensions**: | Dimension | Method/Property | Example | |---|---|---| | Text Content | `element.textContent` | `p.textContent = "Hello"` | | HTML Content | `element.innerHTML` | `div.innerHTML = "Bold"` | | Attribute (generic) | `setAttribute(name, value)` | `img.setAttribute("src", "a.jpg")` | | Attribute (specific) | Direct property | `img.src = "a.jpg"` | | Class Management | `classList.add/remove/toggle` | `el.classList.add("active")` | | Inline Style | `element.style.property` | `el.style.color = "red"` | | Removal of Attribute | `removeAttribute(name)` | `el.removeAttribute("disabled")` | ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Operation | Syntax | Returns | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Create Element | `document.createElement(tag)` | New Element | Tag must be a string | | Create Text Node | `document.createTextNode(text)` | Text Node | Safer than innerHTML for user input (XSS) | | Append Child | `parent.appendChild(child)` | The appended child | Appends to end | | Insert Before | `parent.insertBefore(new, ref)` | The inserted node | ref must be a child of parent | | Prepend | `parent.prepend(child)` | Undefined | Inserts at start | | Remove Child | `parent.removeChild(child)` | The removed child | Throws error if child not found | | Remove Self | `child.remove()` | Undefined | Modern, parentless removal | | Set Attribute | `el.setAttribute(name, value)` | Undefined | Both args as strings | | Get Attribute | `el.getAttribute(name)` | String $\vert$ `null` | `null` if attribute missing | | Remove Attribute | `el.removeAttribute(name)` | Undefined | Silently ignores missing attrs | | Has Attribute | `el.hasAttribute(name)` | Boolean | Returns `true` $\vert$ `false` | | Replace Child | `parent.replaceChild(new, old)` | The replaced node | Atomic swap | > [!WARNING] > **`innerHTML` vs `textContent` Pitfall:** Using `innerHTML` with untrusted user input exposes your app to **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** attacks. Use `textContent` for plain text and `innerHTML` only when the HTML string is trusted or sanitized. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Gmail** uses DOM manipulation to render new emails without refreshing the inbox. - **Google Maps** dynamically adds/removes map markers as you pan. - **E-commerce sites** update the cart icon count using `setAttribute` and DOM insertion. - **Live form validation** modifies `class` and `style` attributes to show/hide error messages in real time. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Creating and Appending a List Item **Problem:** Given an empty `

    `, write JavaScript to dynamically add three list items — "Home", "About", "Contact" — with the text in proper case and a CSS class `nav-item` on each. **Step 1 — Select the parent element.** ```javascript const menu = document.getElementById("menu"); ``` *Logic:* `getElementById` returns the `
      ` reference stored in the variable `menu`. This is the parent to which we will attach new children. **Step 2 — Create the first `
    • ` element.** ```javascript const li1 = document.createElement("li"); ``` *Logic:* `document.createElement("li")` allocates a new Element node in memory. The element is *detached* — it exists in memory but is NOT yet part of the visible DOM tree. **Step 3 — Set its text content and class.** ```javascript li1.textContent = "Home"; li1.classList.add("nav-item"); ``` *Logic:* `textContent` assigns the text node safely (no HTML parsing). `classList.add` appends "nav-item" to the element's `class` attribute. **Step 4 — Append the new element to the parent.** ```javascript menu.appendChild(li1); ``` *Logic:* `appendChild` inserts `li1` as the **last child** of `menu`. After this call, the new `
    • ` is rendered on the page. **Step 5 — Repeat for "About" and "Contact" using a loop (DRY principle).** ```javascript const items = ["Home", "About", "Contact"]; items.forEach(text => { const li = document.createElement("li"); li.textContent = text; li.classList.add("nav-item"); menu.appendChild(li); }); ``` *Logic:* The arrow function inside `forEach` creates, configures, and appends each `
    • `. This avoids repetitive code and is the **industry-standard pattern** expected in KTU answers. **Final DOM Tree (after execution):** ```html
      • Home
      • About
      • Contact
      ``` --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Modifying Attributes **Problem:** Change the `src` of an image and disable a button using attribute methods. **Step 1 — Select the elements.** ```javascript const img = document.querySelector("img"); const btn = document.getElementById("submitBtn"); ``` *Logic:* `querySelector` returns the first matching element (CSS selector syntax). `getElementById` is faster when you have a unique ID. **Step 2 — Change the image source.** ```javascript img.setAttribute("src", "https://example.com/photo.jpg"); img.setAttribute("alt", "Profile Photo"); ``` *Logic:* `setAttribute` always coerces values to strings. It works for both standard and custom (data-*) attributes. **Step 3 — Verify the attribute was set.** ```javascript const currentSrc = img.getAttribute("src"); console.log(currentSrc); // "https://example.com/photo.jpg" ``` *Logic:* `getAttribute` returns the attribute's value as a string, or `null` if the attribute does not exist. **Step 4 — Disable the button using two equivalent methods.** ```javascript // Method A — Boolean property btn.disabled = true; // Method B — Attribute method btn.setAttribute("disabled", ""); ``` *Logic:* For boolean attributes like `disabled`, `checked`, `selected`, the property syntax (Method A) is cleaner. Both approaches yield the same rendered result in the browser. --- ### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Removing an Element **Problem:** Remove the third `
    • ` from a list when a button is clicked. **Step 1 — Reference the elements.** ```javascript const list = document.getElementById("menu"); const removeBtn = document.getElementById("removeBtn"); ``` **Step 2 — Attach a click event listener.** ```javascript removeBtn.addEventListener("click", () => { const thirdItem = list.children[2]; // 0-indexed if (thirdItem) { thirdItem.remove(); } }); ``` *Logic:* The handler checks if the third child exists (`truthy` check guards against errors when the list is empty). `thirdItem.remove()` is the modern, parentless deletion method introduced in ES6. After this call, the element is detached from the DOM and its memory is eligible for garbage collection. **Alternative (older syntax):** ```javascript list.removeChild(thirdItem); ``` *Logic:* This requires the parent reference.
