> 4. Iteration order is **not guaranteed** in older engines (although modern engines do preserve it).
>
> `Map` and `Set` provide **deterministic insertion-order iteration**, **explicit size tracking**, and **arbitrary typed keys** — fulfilling the requirements of a robust web application.

### 1.4 The Companion Variants: WeakSet and WeakMap

The specification also defines **WeakSet** and **WeakMap**, which hold *weak references* to their keys/values. They are not enumerable and exist primarily to **prevent memory leaks** in long-running Single Page Applications (SPAs) — a KTU-favoured viva question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of a `Set`

A `Set` internally maintains a **hash table** of unique entries. The browser engine guarantees the following behavioural contract:

**Construction**
- `new Set([iterable])` — creates a Set, optionally initialized from any iterable (array, string, Map, etc.). Duplicates are **silently de-duplicated**.

**Core Mutation Methods (in order of typical usage)**

1. **`set.add(value)`** — Appends a new element. Returns the `Set` object itself (enabling chaining). **Time Complexity: O(1) amortized**.
2. **`set.has(value)`** — Tests for the presence of a value. Uses the **SameValueZero** algorithm for comparison. **Time Complexity: O(1) amortized**.
3. **`set.delete(value)`** — Removes a specific element. Returns `true` if successful, `false` otherwise. **Time Complexity: O(1) amortized**.
4. **`set.clear()`** — Removes all elements. **Time Complexity: O(n)**.
5. **`set.size`** — A read-only integer accessor returning the number of unique elements.

**Iteration Methods**
- `set.keys()`, `set.values()`, `set.entries()` — All return the **same iterator** (because Set values are their own keys). This design symmetry allows Sets to be drop-in compatible with the `Map` interface.
- `set.forEach((value, valueAgain, set) => {})` — Callback receives `(value, value, set)`.

> [!NOTE]
> **SameValueZero Algorithm:** This is the equality algorithm used by Set/Map. It treats `NaN` as equal to `NaN` (unlike `===`), but treats `+0` and `-0` as equal (unlike `Object.is`). This is the *de facto* standard for collection membership checks.

### 2.2 Operational Breakdown of a `Map`

A `Map` is conceptually a **dictionary** (associative array) with object-typed safety.

**Construction**
- `new Map([iterable])` — accepts an iterable of `[key, value]` pairs.

**Core Methods**

1. **`map.set(key, value)`** — Sets the value for the key. Returns the `Map` (chainable). **Time Complexity: O(1) amortized**.
2. **`map.get(key)`** — Returns the value associated with the key, or `undefined`. **Time Complexity: O(1) amortized**.
3. **`map.has(key)`** — Boolean existence check. **Time Complexity: O(1) amortized**.
4. **`map.delete(key)`** — Removes an entry. **Time Complexity: O(1) amortized**.
5. **`map.clear()`** — Empties the map. **Time Complexity: O(n)**.
6. **`map.size`** — Number of entries.

**Iteration Methods**
- `map.keys()`, `map.values()`, `map.entries()` — Each returns a dedicated iterator.
- `map.forEach((value, key, map) => {})` — Notice the parameter order: **value comes first**, matching `Array.prototype.forEach`.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Operation | Set Method | Map Method | Time Complexity (Avg) | Time Complexity (Worst) | Return Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Insert / Update | `add(v)` | `set(k,v)` | **O(1)** | O(n) | Collection (chainable) |
| Read | N/A | `get(k)` | **O(1)** | O(n) | Value or `undefined` |
| Existence Test | `has(v)` | `has(k)` | **O(1)** | O(n) | Boolean |
| Delete One | `delete(v)` | `delete(k)` | **O(1)** | O(n) | Boolean |
| Delete All | `clear()` | `clear()` | O(n) | O(n) | `undefined` |
| Count Elements | `size` | `size` | **O(1)** | O(1) | Integer |
| Iterate | `forEach` / `for..of` | `forEach` / `for..of` | O(n) | O(n) | Iterator / `undefined` |

### 2.4 The Object vs Map Decision Rule (Engineering Utility)

In professional web applications (e.g., React state management, Redux selectors, or REST API caching layers), the rule is:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Use a **`Map`** when **keys are not known until runtime**, when **keys are not strings**, or when you frequently **add/remove** entries.
> - Use a **plain `Object`** when there is a **fixed, known set of keys** (e.g., a configuration record) and you want native JSON serialization support (`JSON.stringify` ignores Map; you must use a replacer array).

### 2.5 Set Theory Operations Derived in JavaScript

Classical set algebra can be implemented directly using Set properties:

| Set Operation | JavaScript Idiom (with `Set A`, `Set B`) | Engineering Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Union $A \cup B$ | `new Set([...A, ...B])` | Merge unique tags in a blog system |
| Intersection $A \cap B$ | `[...A].filter(x => B.has(x))` | Common permissions of two users |
| Difference $A \setminus B$ | `[...A].filter(x => !B.has(x))` | Roles user A has but user B does not |
| Superset $A \supseteq B$ | `[...B].every(x => A.has(x))` | Permission validation (RBAC) |
| **Time Complexity** | All O($n \cdot m$) without optimization | Filter pipelines in front-end dashboards |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why `Set` Eliminates Duplicates (NaN Case Study)

The classical `===` operator in JavaScript fails the equation `NaN === NaN`, which evaluates to `false`. This is a notorious source of bugs. Let us derive how `Set` resolves this.

**Step 1 — Naive Array Deduplication (Fails):**

$$
\text{ArrayUnique} = \text{Array.filter}((v, i, arr) \Rightarrow \text{arr.indexOf}(v) \equiv i)
$$

**Step 2 — Test the Naive Algorithm with Input Containing NaN:**

$$
\text{Input} = [1, 2, 2, \text{NaN}, \text{NaN}]
$$

Applying the filter:
- Index 0, value 1: `arr.indexOf(1) = 0` $\equiv$ `0` $\rightarrow$ **keep**.
- Index 1, value 2: `arr.indexOf(2) = 1` $\equiv$ `1` $\rightarrow$ **keep**.
- Index 2, value 2: `arr.indexOf(2) = 1` $\not\equiv$ `2` $\rightarrow$ **drop**. (Correct so far.)
- Index 3, value NaN: `arr.indexOf(NaN) = -1` $\not\equiv$ `3` $\rightarrow$ **keep**. (First NaN kept.)
- Index 4, value NaN: `arr.indexOf(NaN) = -1` $\not\equiv$ `4` $\rightarrow$ **keep**. (Duplicate NaN incorrectly kept!)

**Step 3 — Set Resolves via SameValueZero Algorithm:**

$$
\text{SetDedup}(x, y) = 
\begin{cases}
\text{true} & \text{if } x \text{ and } y \text{ are both NaN} \\
x \equiv y & \text{otherwise (with string/number coercion rules)}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Result with Set:**

$$
\text{new Set}([1, 2, 2, \text{NaN}, \text{NaN}]) = \text{Set}\{1, 2, \text{NaN}\}
$$

The same Set object handed to `JSON.stringify` produces `{"1":1,"2":2,"NaN":null}` due to JSON's inability to represent NaN — an important KTU viva point.

### 3.2 Derivation: Converting a `Map` to an Object and Back (Lossless Round-Trip)

A common KTU exam question asks: *"How do you serialize a Map?"* JSON does not support Maps natively. The derivation of a **lossless** conversion is as follows:

**Step 1 — Map → Array of Pairs:**

$$
\text{map.entries()} \rightarrow \text{Iterator}\langle [k_1, v_1], [k_2, v_2], \dots, [k_n, v_n] \rangle
$$

**Step 2 — Serialize via `JSON.stringify` with a Replacer:**

$$
\text{json} = \text{JSON.stringify}(\text{Array.from(map)}, \text{null}, 2)
$$

**Step 3 — Deserialize and Reconstruct:**

$$
\text{new Map}(\text{JSON.parse(json}))
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lossless Constraint:** This round-trip is lossless **only if** all keys in the Map are JSON-serializable types (string, number, boolean, null, or an array/object of these). An object key (e.g., `map.set({}, 'value')`) will be silently coerced to the string `"[object Object]"` during `JSON.stringify`, causing a collision warning.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Collection

The following decision matrix is **high-yield for KTU 2024 ESE** and is frequently tested under *"Compare and contrast"* questions.

| Criterion | Array | Plain Object | Map | Set |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Ordered (insertion) | Yes | Partial (engine-dependent) | **Yes (guaranteed)** | **Yes (guaranteed)** |
| Allows Duplicate Values | Yes | Yes (in values) | Values: Yes | **No** |
| Key Type Constraints | N/A (indexed) | String / Symbol | **Any type** | N/A |
| Native `size` property | No (manual) | No (manual) | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Native JSON Support | Yes | Yes | No (replacer needed) | No |
| Iterable by Default | Yes | No (Object.keys needed) | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Best Use Case | Ordered lists, sequences | Static config records | Dynamic keyed caches | Tag lists, unique IDs |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Set Theory on Tags

**Problem Statement (Module Internal Style):** A blog platform has 50 posts tagged as `["javascript", "web", "es6"]` and 30 posts tagged as `["web", "react", "frontend"]`. Compute the union, intersection, and exclusive tags.

**Step 1 — Initialize Sets:**

$$
A = \text{new Set}([\text{"javascript", "web", "es6"}])
$$

$$
B = \text{new Set}([\text{"web", "react", "frontend"}])
$$

**Step 2 — Intersection $A \cap B$:**

$$
A \cap B = \{x \mid x \in A \land x \in B\} = \{\text{"web"}\}
$$

**Step 3 — Union $A \cup B$:**

$$
A \cup B = \{x \mid x \in A \lor x \in B\} = \{\text{"javascript", "web", "es6", "react", "frontend"}\}
$$

**Step 4 — Symmetric Difference $(A \cup B) \setminus (A \cap B)$:**

$$
(A \cup B) \setminus (A \cap B) = \{\text{"javascript", "es6", "react", "frontend"}\}
$$

This derivation (count = 4) is the standard KTU-validated methodology.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: Internal Architecture of a `Map`

The following diagram visualizes the *bucket-based hash table* logic that the V8 engine (used in Chrome and Node.js) uses to implement Map. Each key is hashed to a bucket index; collisions are resolved via chaining.

```mermaid
graph TD
    inputKey[User Key e.g. Object userId]
    hashFn[Hash Function Murmur or FNV]
    bucket1[Bucket 0]
    bucket2[Bucket 1]
    bucket3[Bucket 2]
    bucket4[Bucket N]
    collision[Collision Chain Linked List]
    valA[Value Pointer 1]
    valB[Value Pointer 2]
    orderTable[Insertion Order Table Array]
    
    inputKey --> hashFn
    hashFn --> bucket1
    hashFn --> bucket2
    hashFn --> bucket3
    hashFn --> bucket4
    bucket2 --> collision
    collision --> valA
    collision --> valB
    orderTable -.tracks.-> valA
    orderTable -.tracks.-> valB
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Functional Flow of a Set Operation Pipeline

This block represents the **End-to-End Web Application Flow** of how a Set is used to dedupe a user-input tag list from a React form.

```mermaid
graph TD
    UI[React Form Component]
    rawTags[Raw String e.g. react, react, js]
    splitOp[String split comma]
    arrayTags[Array of Strings]
    setInit[New Set array]
    dedupSet[Deduplicated Set]
    renderUI[Re render UI badges]
    
    UI --> rawTags
    rawTags --> splitOp
    splitOp --> arrayTags
    arrayTags --> setInit
    setInit --> dedupSet
    dedupSet --> renderUI
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block: Map Use Case in REST API Caching Layer

A typical *frequently-asked 14-mark question* scenario: build a client-side cache for GitHub user profiles using Map.

```mermaid
graph TD
    clientReq[Client Request userId 42]
    cacheCheck{Has Map.has 42}
    cacheHit[Map.get 42 Return Cached Profile]
    apiCall[Fetch github api users 42]
    network[Network Round Trip]
    mapSet[Map.set 42 profile]
    cacheMiss[Return Fresh Profile]
    
    clientReq --> cacheCheck
    cacheCheck -- True --> cacheHit
    cacheCheck -- False --> apiCall
    apiCall --> network
    network --> mapSet
    mapSet --> cacheMiss
```

### 4.4 WeakMap Reference Lifecycle (Memory Leak Prevention)

WeakMap and WeakSet are not enumerable; their keys must be objects. When the only reference to a key is the WeakMap, the Garbage Collector (GC) may reclaim it.

```mermaid
graph LR
    domNode[DOM Node Button]
    weakMap[WeakMap privateData]
    gc[Garbage Collector]
    reclaimed[Memory Reclaimed]
    
    domNode -->|strong ref| weakMap
    domNode -.->|detached from DOM| gc
    weakMap -.->|weak ref only| gc
    gc --> reclaimed
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** The most common mark-deduction reasons in this topic are:
> 1. **Confusing `forEach` parameter order** between Set (value, value, set) and Map (value, key, map). Examiners **specifically deduct 1 mark** if you write `(key, value)` for a Set's `forEach`.
> 2. **Forgetting that `add()` returns the Set itself**, not the added value. Writing `set.add(x) + set.add(y)` produces `NaN` or `"[object Set][object Set]"`.
> 3. **Claiming that `Map` keys are ordered alphabetically** — they are ordered by **insertion sequence**, not sort order. This is a recurring 2-mark trap.
> 4. Using `delete` as a method on a plain object literal `{ delete: ... }` triggers a SyntaxError in strict mode (used by ES Modules). The KTU ES6 module expects you to know `Map.prototype.delete` is the correct method, not the `delete` operator on objects.

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the JavaScript `Map` object. List any three of its properties and briefly explain how it differs from a plain JavaScript object when used as a hash map.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A `Map` is a built-in ES6 collection that stores **key-value pairs** where both keys and values may be of any data type, and the iteration order of entries follows the **insertion sequence**.

| # | Property | Plain Object | Map |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | Key types allowed | Strings, Symbols, (numbers coerced to strings) | **Any type** (object, function, NaN, etc.) |
| 2 | `size` property | Not available (manual counting) | **Built-in `size` accessor** |
| 3 | Iteration order | Guaranteed in modern engines, but not by spec | **Guaranteed by ECMAScript specification** |
| 4 | Prototype pollution risk | Yes (e.g., `obj["toString"]`) | **None** (uses internal slots) |
| 5 | JSON serializability | Native | Requires replacer |

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for any three valid differences.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Explain the `SameValueZero` algorithm used by Set and Map in JavaScript. Why was it chosen over `===` and `Object.is`?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **SameValueZero** algorithm is the equality comparison used internally by `Set.has`, `Map.has`, `Set.add`, and `Map.get` to test for membership.

**Truth Table:**

| Comparison $x = y$ | `===` | `Object.is` | **SameValueZero** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| `NaN === NaN` | false | **true** | **true** |
| `+0 === -0` | true | false | **true** |
| `"5" === 5` | false | false | **false** |

**Rationale (the "Why" — 2 marks):**
- It treats `NaN` as equal to `NaN`, which is essential because deduplication must collapse repeated NaN values, but the loose `===` operator fails this. (1 mark)
- It treats `+0` and `-0` as equal, removing the ambiguity of `Object.is` which differentiates them. This simplifies the mental model for the developer — "two zeros are always the same key." (1 mark)
- It is therefore a *pragmatic middle-ground* that prioritizes **developer intuition over mathematical purity**.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the internal architecture of a `Map`. Explain how the insertion-order iteration guarantee is maintained. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a JavaScript program that uses a `Map` to build a **client-side LRU (Least Recently Used) cache** for a REST API. Demonstrate `set`, `get`, and a capacity-eviction policy. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Internal Architecture of Map (7 Marks):**

A `Map` is implemented by the JavaScript engine (e.g., V8) as a **hash table with separate chaining** for collision resolution. It comprises two cooperating data structures:

1. **The Hash Table** — An array of *buckets*. When a key is inserted, the engine invokes an internal hash function to compute an integer index, and the entry is placed in the corresponding bucket.
2. **The Insertion-Order Table** — A secondary linear array that records the **sequence** in which entries were added.

**Maintenance of Order:** When `for...of` is invoked on the Map, the engine iterates the linear *order table*, **not** the hash buckets. This means even if two keys hash to the same bucket (collision), their iteration order is the order in which they were originally inserted, not the order in which the hash table array stores them. (3 marks for diagram + 2 marks for explanation + 2 marks for order guarantee.)

*Refer to the Mermaid block in Section 4.1 for the diagram.*

**(b) LRU Cache Implementation (7 Marks):**

The Map's *insertion-order* property is the foundation of a remarkably simple LRU cache: whenever a key is read or written, we **re-insert** it, pushing it to the "most recent" end. When the size exceeds capacity, we delete the **first** entry (the oldest).

```javascript
class LRUCache {
  constructor(capacity) {
    this.capacity = capacity;
    this.cache = new Map();
  }

  get(key) {
    if (!this.cache.has(key)) return -1;
    const value = this.cache.get(key);
    this.cache.delete(key);     // Remove old position
    this.cache.set(key, value); // Re-insert at the end
    return value;
  }

  set(key, value) {
    if (this.cache.has(key)) {
      this.cache.delete(key);
    } else if (this.cache.size >= this.capacity) {
      const oldestKey = this.cache.keys().next().value;
      this.cache.delete(oldestKey);
    }
    this.cache.set(key, value);
  }
}

// Demonstration
const apiCache = new LRUCache(3);
apiCache.set('user/1', { name: 'Arjun' });
apiCache.set('user/2', { name: 'Rahul' });
apiCache.set('user/3', { name: 'Meera' });
apiCache.get('user/1');           // Promotes user/1
apiCache.set('user/4', { name: 'Sneha' }); // Evicts user/2 (oldest)
console.log([...apiCache.keys()]); 
// Output: [ 'user/3', 'user/1', 'user/4' ]
```

**Mark Allocation:**
- Class structure with `capacity` and `Map`: 2 marks
- Correct `get` re-insertion logic: 2 marks
- Correct `set` with capacity check and oldest-key eviction: 2 marks
- Working demo and explanation: 1 mark

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast the `Set` and `WeakSet` objects. In what real-world web development scenario is a `WeakSet` preferred over a `Set`? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Demonstrate the implementation of **classical set operations** (union, intersection, difference) on JavaScript `Set` objects. Write a function `isSuperset(setA, setB)` that returns `true` if setA contains all elements of setB. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Set vs WeakSet (7 Marks):**

| Feature | `Set` | `WeakSet` |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Value Types | Any (primitives + objects) | **Objects only** |
| Size Enumerable | Yes (`size` property) | **No** (no `size` property) |
| Iterable | Yes (`forEach`, `for...of`) | **No** (not enumerable) |
| Garbage Collection | Holds **strong** references | Holds **weak** references |
| `clear()` method | Yes | **No** |
| Methods Available | `add, has, delete` | `add, has, delete` only |

**Real-World Scenario — DOM Node Tracking (3 marks):**

In a Single Page Application (SPA) like a React dashboard, you may want to track *which DOM nodes are currently marked as "favourited"* without preventing the browser's Garbage Collector from reclaiming nodes that have been removed from the DOM (e.g., when a user navigates to another page).

A `Set` would **leak memory** because it holds a strong reference to the DOM node forever, even after the node is detached. A `WeakSet` allows automatic cleanup, eliminating the leak.

```javascript
const favoritedNodes = new WeakSet();
function toggleFavorite(node) {
  if (favoritedNodes.has(node)) {
    favoritedNodes.delete(node);
    node.classList.remove('favorited');
  } else {
    favoritedNodes.add(node);
    node.classList.add('favorited');
  }
}
```

When a node is removed from the DOM and no other code references it, the GC reclaims the memory automatically — a *zero-overhead* design pattern.

**(b) Classical Set Operations (7 Marks):**

```javascript
// Union: A ∪ B
function union(setA, setB) {
  return new Set([...setA, ...setB]);
}

// Intersection: A ∩ B
function intersection(setA, setB) {
  return new Set([...setA].filter(x => setB.has(x)));
}

// Difference: A \ B
function difference(setA, setB) {
  return new Set([...setA].filter(x => !setB.has(x)));
}

// Superset Test: A ⊇ B
function isSuperset(setA, setB) {
  for (const element of setB) {
    if (!setA.has(element)) return false;
  }
  return true;
}

// Demonstration (e-commerce permissions system)
const adminPerms    = new Set(['read', 'write', 'delete', 'audit']);
const editorPerms   = new Set(['read', 'write']);
const viewerPerms   = new Set(['read']);

console.log(isSuperset(adminPerms, editorPerms));  // true
console.log(isSuperset(editorPerms, adminPerms));  // false
console.log(difference(adminPerms, editorPerms)); 
// Set { 'delete', 'audit' }
```

**Mark Allocation:**
- Union/Intersection/Difference definitions: 3 marks
- `isSuperset` early-exit loop: 2 marks
- Working demo and real-world use (RBAC): 2 marks

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Set** = unique values, any type; uses **SameValueZero**; insertion-ordered. **(1 mark booster)**
- **Map** = key-value pairs, **any key type**, insertion-ordered, has built-in `size`. **(1 mark booster)**
- **`size` is a property, not a method** — write `map.size`, never `map.size()`. **(Common 1-mark trap)**
- **`add()` / `set()` return the collection itself** — enables method chaining.
- **`forEach` parameter order differs** — Set: `(value, value, set)`; Map: `(value, key, map)`.
- **`WeakSet` / `WeakMap`** hold **weak references**; keys must be **objects only**; not iterable; no `size`, no `clear`. **(Frequently asked 3-mark question)**
- **Plain object keys are coerced to strings**; Map keys are not.
- **JSON.stringify ignores Map and Set** — use a replacer or convert to array first.
- **Set deduplicates `NaN`** — `===` cannot.
- **Insertion order is guaranteed** by the ES2015 specification (Section 23.1 / 24.2) for both Map and Set.
- **Time complexity** for `add`, `set`, `get`, `has`, `delete` is **O(1) amortized** — write this verbatim in exams.
- **Application domains** to mention in answers: REST API caching (Map), DOM node tracking (WeakSet), tag deduplication (Set), Redux state slices (Map), user-permission RBAC (Set operations).
- **LRU cache trick** — re-insert on access; delete the first key when capacity is exceeded. **High-yield for 7-mark questions.**
- **Never use `delete` operator** on a Map (use `Map.prototype.delete`); never write `map.delete` without parentheses.
- The Map's **internal slot name** is `[[MapData]]` (per ECMAScript spec) — bonus mark in viva.
- WeakMap is the official way to attach **private data** to DOM nodes in library code (e.g., the jQuery `$.data` pattern).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Array Methods: find and findIndex
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Array Methods: find and findIndex

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 2 — Array Methods: `find` and `findIndex`

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> The `Array.prototype.find()` and `Array.prototype.findIndex()` methods are higher-order array iteration methods introduced in **ECMAScript 2015 (ES6)**. They execute a user-supplied **callback (predicate) function** on every element of an array until the predicate returns a **truthy** value, and then terminate execution (short-circuit evaluation).

### 1.1 Formal Definition

- **`Array.prototype.find(callback[, thisArg])`** — Returns the **value of the first element** in the array for which the callback function returns a truthy value. If no element satisfies the predicate, it returns **`undefined`**.

- **`Array.prototype.findIndex(callback[, thisArg])`** — Returns the **index of the first element** in the array for which the callback function returns a truthy value. If no element satisfies the predicate, it returns **`-1`**.

Both methods belong to the category of **non-mutating** (pure) iteration methods, meaning they **do not modify** the original array on which they are called.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🎯

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: Searching a Library**

Imagine you walk into a library with **10,000 books** and you want to find the **first book** whose title contains the word "JavaScript".

- You start scanning from **shelf 1, shelf 2, shelf 3**...
- The moment you find the first matching book, you **stop searching** — you don't look through the remaining 9,997 books. This is exactly what `find` does — it returns the **book** (value).
- Now imagine you don't want the book itself, but rather its **shelf number** so you can come back later. That is what `findIndex` does — it returns the **shelf number** (index).

> **Key Insight:** Both methods are **short-circuiting** — they stop iterating the moment a match is found, making them highly efficient for large datasets.

### 1.3 The Callback Function Signature

Both `find` and `findIndex` accept a callback with **three parameters**:

| Parameter | Description | Required |
|-----------|-------------|----------|
| `element` | The current element being processed in the array | ✅ Yes |
| `index` | The index of the current element being processed | ❌ Optional |
| `array` | The array on which `find` / `findIndex` was called | ❌ Optional |

The `thisArg` parameter (second argument to the method itself) allows you to set the value of `this` inside the callback.

### 1.4 Reference Standard

- **ECMAScript Specification:** ECMA-262, 6th Edition (ES2015), Section **23.1.3.8** (`find`) and **23.1.3.9** (`findIndex`)
- **Browser Support:** All modern browsers (Chrome 45+, Firefox 25+, Safari 8+, Edge 12+)
- **Node.js Support:** Version **4.0.0+**

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 expects students to know the exact difference between `find` (returns value or `undefined`) and `findIndex` (returns index or `-1`). A very common 3-mark question!

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2. Operational Breakdown — The Internal Algorithm

### 2.1 Execution Flow of `find` (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. The engine initializes a `found` flag to `false`.
2. A `result` accumulator is set to `undefined`.
3. The engine iterates from `index = 0` to `array.length - 1`.
4. For each index, the **callback function is invoked** with `(element, index, array)`.
5. If the callback returns a **truthy** value:
   - Set `result = element` (for `find`) or `result = index` (for `findIndex`).
   - Set `found = true`.
   - **Break the loop** (short-circuit).
6. After the loop, if `found` is `false`, return `undefined` (for `find`) or `-1` (for `findIndex`).
7. Otherwise, return `result`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule:** The callback must return a value that is **coerced to `true`**. This includes non-zero numbers, non-empty strings, objects, arrays, and the boolean `true`. Returning `0`, `""`, `null`, `undefined`, `NaN`, or `false` will be treated as **falsy**, and the search will continue.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Matters |
|------|----------------|
| Short-circuit on match | **Performance** — O(n) worst case, but O(1) best case (element at index 0) |
| Return value vs. index | **Use case differentiation** — Do you need the data or its position? |
| `undefined` vs `-1` | **Sentinel value** — Both are unambiguous "not found" indicators for their return types |
| Non-mutating | **Functional purity** — Safe to use in Redux/Vuex state management, React reducers |

### 2.3 Edge Cases & Boundary Conditions

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently confuse these edge cases. Memorize them!

| Edge Case | Behavior of `find` | Behavior of `findIndex` |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------------|
| Empty array `[]` | Returns `undefined` | Returns `-1` |
| Array with all `undefined` elements | Returns the first `undefined` element (since `===` is the implicit check if no callback) | Returns index of first `undefined` element |
| Sparse array `[1, , 3]` | Callback is **NOT invoked** for empty slots | Callback is **NOT invoked** for empty slots |
| `thisArg` is omitted | `this` will be `undefined` in strict mode | `this` will be `undefined` in strict mode |
| No element matches | Returns `undefined` | Returns `-1` |
| First match at `index 0` | Returns that element (note: `0` is falsy, but the element VALUE is returned, not the truthiness of index) | Returns `0` (which is valid — a valid index!) |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📝

| Property | `Array.prototype.find` | `Array.prototype.findIndex` |
|----------|------------------------|-----------------------------|
| **Return Type** | Element value (any type) | Integer index |
| **Not Found Return** | `undefined` | `-1` |
| **Mutates Array?** | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| **Short-circuits?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Callback Invoked For** | Each index, including those with assigned `undefined` | Each index, including those with assigned `undefined` |
| **Empty Slots** | Skipped (not invoked) | Skipped (not invoked) |
| **Time Complexity** | O(n) worst, O(1) best | O(n) worst, O(1) best |
| **Space Complexity** | O(1) auxiliary | O(1) auxiliary |
| **ES Version** | ES6 (2015) | ES6 (2015) |
| **Predicate Form** | `callback(el) → Boolean` | `callback(el) → Boolean` |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility 🌐

| Domain | Use Case |
|--------|----------|
| **E-commerce** | Find a product in a cart by `productId` |
| **Authentication** | Find a user object in a users array by `email` |
| **React/Vue State** | Find an item to toggle, update, or delete in an immutable way |
| **Form Validation** | Find the first invalid field to focus on |
| **Game Development** | Find the nearest enemy to the player |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Code Derivations

## 3. Exhaustive Code Derivations

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Finding a Primitive Value (Number)

> **Problem:** Given the array `[5, 12, 8, 130, 44]`, find the first element greater than 10.

**Step 1:** Define the array.

```javascript
const numbers = [5, 12, 8, 130, 44];
```

**Step 2:** Define the predicate (testing) function. This function takes `element` and returns a boolean.

```javascript
const predicate = (element) => element > 10;
```

**Step 3:** Apply the `find` method.

```javascript
const result = numbers.find(predicate);
```

**Step 4:** Trace the execution step-by-step.

| Iteration | `element` | `index` | `element > 10` | Action |
|-----------|-----------|---------|----------------|--------|
| 1 | 5 | 0 | `false` | Continue |
| 2 | 12 | 1 | `true` | ✅ **Match found!** Return `12`. Stop. |

**Step 5:** Final result.

```javascript
console.log(result); // Output: 12
```

> [!NOTE]
> Notice how the engine **stopped at index 1**. It did NOT process `8`, `130`, or `44`. This is the **short-circuit** behavior.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Finding an Object in an Array

> **Problem:** Given an array of user objects, find the user with `userId === 103`.

**Step 1:** Define the array of objects.

```javascript
const users = [
  { userId: 101, name: "Arun",   role: "admin" },
  { userId: 102, name: "Priya",  role: "user"  },
  { userId: 103, name: "Rahul",  role: "user"  },
  { userId: 104, name: "Sneha",  role: "admin" }
];
```

**Step 2:** Define the predicate using the standard arrow function syntax with two parameters.

```javascript
const targetUser = users.find((element, index, array) => {
  return element.userId === 103;
});
```

**Step 3:** Detailed trace.

| Iteration | `element.userId` | `index` | `array` | Predicate Result |
|-----------|------------------|---------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | 101 | 0 | `users` (ref) | `false` |
| 2 | 102 | 1 | `users` (ref) | `false` |
| 3 | 103 | 2 | `users` (ref) | `true` ✅ → Return `{userId:103, name:"Rahul", role:"user"}` |

**Step 4:** Verify the output.

```javascript
console.log(targetUser); 
// Output: { userId: 103, name: 'Rahul', role: 'user' }
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** The callback received three parameters (`element`, `index`, `array`), but we only used `element`. This is perfectly valid — extra parameters are simply ignored by the engine.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Using `findIndex` to Locate a Position

> **Problem:** Find the index of the first prime number in `[4, 6, 8, 9, 11, 15]`.

**Step 1:** Define a helper function `isPrime(n)`.

```javascript
const isPrime = (n) => {
  if (n < 2) return false;
  for (let i = 2; i <= Math.sqrt(n); i++) {
    if (n % i === 0) return false;
  }
  return true;
};
```

**Step 2:** Apply `findIndex`.

```javascript
const arr = [4, 6, 8, 9, 11, 15];
const primeIndex = arr.findIndex(isPrime);
```

**Step 3:** Trace the execution.

| Iteration | `element` | `isPrime(element)` | Action |
|-----------|-----------|---------------------|--------|
| 1 | 4 | `false` (4 % 2 === 0) | Continue |
| 2 | 6 | `false` (6 % 2 === 0) | Continue |
| 3 | 8 | `false` (8 % 2 === 0) | Continue |
| 4 | 9 | `false` (9 % 3 === 0) | Continue |
| 5 | 11 | `true` ✅ | Return index `4`. Stop. |

**Step 4:** Final result.

```javascript
console.log(primeIndex); // Output: 4
console.log(arr[primeIndex]); // Output: 11 (using the index to retrieve the value)
```

> [!TIP]
> **Pattern Tip:** `findIndex` + bracket access is functionally equivalent to `find` for most cases, but `findIndex` is preferred when you need to **mutate** the array later (e.g., `splice`).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Using `thisArg` Parameter

> **Problem:** Search for a value using a configurable threshold stored in an external object.

**Step 1:** Define the context object.

```javascript
const config = { minValue: 50 };
```

**Step 2:** Define the array and predicate.

```javascript
const scores = [10, 45, 67, 23, 89, 100];
```

**Step 3:** Use `thisArg` to bind `this` to `config`.

```javascript
const firstHighScore = scores.find(function(element) {
  return element > this.minValue; // 'this' refers to 'config'
}, config);
```

**Step 4:** Trace the execution.

| Iteration | `element` | `this.minValue` | `element > this.minValue` | Action |
|-----------|-----------|-----------------|--------------------------|--------|
| 1 | 10 | 50 | `false` | Continue |
| 2 | 45 | 50 | `false` | Continue |
| 3 | 67 | 50 | `true` ✅ | Return `67`. Stop. |

**Step 5:** Final result.

```javascript
console.log(firstHighScore); // Output: 67
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Arrow functions **do not have their own `this`**! If you use an arrow function for the callback, `thisArg` will be **silently ignored**. Always use a regular `function` expression when you need `thisArg`.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: The "Not Found" Scenario

> **Problem:** What happens when no element matches?

**Step 1:** Define an array where no element matches the predicate.

```javascript
const data = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5];
const notFound = data.find((el) => el > 100);
```

**Step 2:** Trace.

| Iteration | `element` | `element > 100` |
|-----------|-----------|-----------------|
| 1-5 | 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 | All `false` |

After the loop completes with no match, the method returns the **sentinel value**.

```javascript
console.log(notFound);       // Output: undefined
console.log(notFound === undefined); // Output: true (be careful — 0 is also falsy!)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Bug Warning:** If your array can contain valid `undefined` or `null` values, you cannot distinguish "not found" from "found a `undefined`" when using `find`. Use `findIndex` and check for `-1` in such cases.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Internal Execution Flow of `find` / `findIndex`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: arr.find or arr.findIndex called] --> B[Initialize found = false, result = undefined]
    B --> C[Loop: index = 0 to array.length - 1]
    C --> D{Is current index a hole in sparse array?}
    D -->|Yes| H[Skip: do not invoke callback]
    D -->|No| E[Invoke callback element, index, array]
    E --> F{Does callback return a truthy value?}
    F -->|No| G[Increment index]
    G --> C
    F -->|Yes| I[Set result = element OR index]
    I --> J[Set found = true]
    J --> K[Break out of loop]
    H --> C
    K --> L{Is found true?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Return result]
    L -->|No| N{Method Type?}
    N -->|find| O[Return undefined]
    N -->|findIndex| P[Return -1]
    M --> Q[End]
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Decision Matrix: `find` vs `findIndex` vs `filter` vs `indexOf`

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SCENARIO [I need to search an array]
        A[Start] --> B{Do I need the value or the position?}
        B -->|Value| C{Could the value be undefined or null?}
        B -->|Position| D{Is my search condition complex?}
        C -->|No| E[Use Array.find]
        C -->|Yes| F[Use Array.findIndex then check for -1]
        D -->|No, simple equality| G[Use Array.indexOf or Array.includes]
        D -->|Yes, complex predicate| H[Use Array.findIndex]
    end
    E --> I[End]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Callback Invocation Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant Engine as JS Engine
    participant CB as Callback Function
    User->>Engine: arr.find(callback, thisArg)
    Engine->>Engine: Validate array and callback
    loop For each index i from 0 to length-1
        Engine->>Engine: Check if arr i is a hole
        alt Not a hole
            Engine->>CB: callback arr i, i, arr
            CB-->>Engine: Return truthy or falsy
            alt Returned truthy
                Engine-->>User: Return arr i
                Note over Engine: Loop breaks immediately
            else Returned falsy
                Engine->>Engine: Continue to next index
            end
        else Is a hole
            Engine->>Engine: Skip this index
        end
    end
    Engine-->>User: Return undefined or -1
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Architecture — Use Case Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DATA [Data Source Layer]
        D1[Array of Products]
        D2[Array of Users]
        D3[Array of Orders]
        D4[Array of Form Fields]
    end
    subgraph METHODS [Method Layer]
        M1[Array.find]
        M2[Array.findIndex]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT [Application Layer]
        O1[Render Product Card]
        O2[Display User Profile]
        O3[Edit Order]
        O4[Focus First Invalid Field]
    end
    D1 --> M1 --> O1
    D2 --> M2 --> O2
    D3 --> M1 --> O3
    D4 --> M2 --> O4
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank]`
> Differentiate between `Array.prototype.find()` and `Array.prototype.findIndex()` in JavaScript. Mention the values returned by each method when no matching element is found.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | `Array.prototype.find()` | `Array.prototype.findIndex()` |
|--------|--------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Return on match** | The **value** of the first matching element | The **index** of the first matching element |
| **Return on no match** | `undefined` | `-1` |
| **Purpose** | Retrieve the element itself | Retrieve the position of the element |
| **ES Version** | ES6 (2015) | ES6 (2015) |

Both methods execute a predicate callback and **short-circuit** (stop iterating) on the first match. They do **not mutate** the original array. **(1 mark for each correct difference = 3 marks)**

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank]`
> What happens when you pass an empty array to the `find` method? What is the return value of `find` when the callback is provided but no element satisfies the predicate?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- When an **empty array** `[]` is passed to `find`, the callback is **never invoked** (since there are no elements to iterate over) and the method returns **`undefined`**. **(1.5 marks)**
- When the callback is provided but no element satisfies the predicate (i.e., the callback returns a falsy value for every element), the loop completes without finding a match, and the method returns **`undefined`**. This is the same sentinel value, and care must be taken if the array legitimately contains `undefined` values. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Pattern]`**
>
> **(a)** Explain the syntax, parameters, and return value of the `Array.prototype.find()` method in JavaScript with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write a JavaScript program to find the first employee object from an array of employee records whose salary is greater than ₹50,000. Also explain the concept of short-circuit evaluation in this context. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Syntax:**

```javascript
arr.find(callback(element[, index[, array]])[, thisArg])
```

**Parameters Table:**

| Parameter | Type | Description |
|-----------|------|-------------|
| `callback` | Function | Predicate function executed on each element |
| `element` | Any | Current element being processed |
| `index` | Integer (optional) | Index of current element |
| `array` | Array (optional) | The array `find` was called upon |
| `thisArg` | Any (optional) | Value bound to `this` inside callback |

**Return Value:** The **value of the first element** for which `callback` returns a truthy value. If no such element exists, returns `undefined`. **(2 marks)**

**Example Code:**

```javascript
const fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry", "date"];
const result = fruits.find((fruit) => fruit.startsWith("c"));
console.log(result); // Output: "cherry"
```

**Explanation:** The callback checks if each fruit name starts with the letter "c". The first match is `"cherry"` at index 2, so the method returns `"cherry"` and stops iterating. **(5 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Program:**

```javascript
const employees = [
  { empId: 201, name: "Anand",   salary: 45000 },
  { empId: 202, name: "Bhavna",  salary: 55000 },
  { empId: 203, name: "Chirag",  salary: 70000 },
  { empId: 204, name: "Deepa",   salary: 40000 }
];

// Using Array.find() with arrow function
const highEarner = employees.find((employee) => employee.salary > 50000);

if (highEarner) {
  console.log(`First employee earning > ₹50,000: ${highEarner.name} (₹${highEarner.salary})`);
} else {
  console.log("No employee found matching the criteria.");
}

// Output: First employee earning > ₹50,000: Bhavna (₹55000)
```

**Step-by-Step Trace:**

| Iteration | `employee.name` | `employee.salary` | `salary > 50000` | Action |
|-----------|----------------|-------------------|------------------|--------|
| 1 | Anand | 45000 | `false` | Continue |
| 2 | Bhavna | 55000 | `true` ✅ | Return `{empId:202, name:"Bhavna", salary:55000}`. **Stop.** |

**Short-Circuit Evaluation Explanation:** **(3 marks)**
Short-circuit evaluation means that as soon as the callback returns a truthy value for an element, the `find` method **immediately terminates the iteration** and returns that element. It does not process the remaining elements (`Chirag` and `Deepa` were never evaluated in this example). This provides:
1. **Performance optimization** — best case O(1) when the match is at index 0.
2. **Determinism** — guarantees the **first** match (left-to-right traversal).

---

### 📘 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **`[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Pattern]`**
>
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the internal working of `Array.prototype.findIndex()`. Compare its return value semantics with `Array.prototype.indexOf()`. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write a JavaScript program using `findIndex` to locate the position of a specific book in a library array based on its ISBN. Handle the "book not found" scenario appropriately. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**Internal Working Diagram (Text Representation):**

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Array.findIndex(callback[, thisArg])                        │
│  ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────    │
│  Input: [10, 25, 30, 45, 50], target: value > 20            │
│                                                              │
│  Step 1: index = 0, arr[0] = 10  → 10 > 20? NO  → continue │
│  Step 2: index = 1, arr[1] = 25  → 25 > 20? YES → RETURN 1  │
│                                                              │
│  Output: 1                                                   │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Comparison Table: `findIndex` vs `indexOf`:**

| Feature | `findIndex()` | `indexOf()` |
|---------|---------------|-------------|
| **Search criteria** | Custom predicate callback | Strict equality (`===`) |
| **Return on match** | Index of first match | Index of first match |
| **Return on no match** | `-1` | `-1` |
| **Supports complex search?** | ✅ Yes (e.g., `obj.id === 5`) | ❌ No (primitives only via `===`) |
| **Works on object arrays?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (object reference comparison only) |
| **ES Version** | ES6 (2015) | ES5 (2009) |
| **NaN handling** | ✅ Can find NaN (with `isNaN` check) | ❌ `indexOf` cannot find NaN |

**(5 marks for diagram + 2 marks for comparison)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Program:**

```javascript
const library = [
  { title: "JavaScript: The Good Parts", isbn: "978-0596517748" },
  { title: "Clean Code",               isbn: "978-0132350884" },
  { title: "You Don't Know JS",        isbn: "978-1491924464" },
  { title: "Eloquent JavaScript",      isbn: "978-1593275846" }
];

const searchISBN = "978-0132350884";

// Using findIndex to locate the book
const bookIndex = library.findIndex((book) => book.isbn === searchISBN);

// Handling "not found" scenario
if (bookIndex !== -1) {
  console.log(`Book found at index ${bookIndex}: "${library[bookIndex].title}"`);
  // Output: Book found at index 1: "Clean Code"
} else {
  console.log(`No book found with ISBN: ${searchISBN}`);
}
```

**Trace Table:**

| Iteration | `book.isbn` | `book.isbn === searchISBN` | Action |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------------|--------|
| 0 | 978-0596517748 | `false` | Continue |
| 1 | 978-0132350884 | `true` ✅ | Return `1`. **Stop.** |

**Handling Not Found:** If the `searchISBN` is changed to `"000-0000000000"`, the loop completes without a match, `findIndex` returns `-1`, and the `else` block executes. **(2 marks for not-found handling + 5 marks for core logic)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing `find` with `filter`:** `find` returns **one** element; `filter` returns an **array of all matches**. This is the #1 mistake.
> 2. **Forgetting the `===` strict equality:** JavaScript uses `===` (strict) for object property checks, not `==` (loose). Writing `book.isbn = searchISBN` (single `=`) is an **assignment**, not a comparison — it will always evaluate to truthy!
> 3. **Arrow functions and `thisArg`:** Using an arrow function for the callback makes `thisArg` **non-functional**. Use a regular `function` expression.
> 4. **Misidentifying the return for index 0:** `findIndex` returning `0` is a **valid** index, not "not found". Only `-1` means not found.
> 5. **Sparse array oversight:** The callback is **skipped** for holes in sparse arrays. Don't assume every index is visited.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ `find` returns the **element value**; `findIndex` returns the **index** of the first matching element.
- ✅ Both return early on the first truthy callback result (**short-circuit**).
- ✅ `find` returns **`undefined`** when not found; `findIndex` returns **`-1`**.
- ✅ Neither method **mutates** the original array (they are pure/non-destructive).
- ✅ The callback signature is `callback(element, index, array)`.
- ✅ The second argument to the method (`thisArg`) sets the `this` value inside the callback — but **only works with regular `function` expressions**, not arrow functions.
- ✅ Both methods are **ES6 (ES2015)** features.
- ✅ The callback must return a **truthy** value; `0`, `""`, `null`, `undefined`, `NaN`, and `false` are falsy.
- ✅ For **empty arrays**, both return the sentinel value immediately without calling the callback.
- ✅ For **sparse arrays** (e.g., `[1, , 3]`), the callback is **not invoked** for holes.
- ✅ `find` is equivalent to `findIndex` + bracket access **only when the value cannot be `undefined`**.
- ✅ Use `findIndex` over `find` when you need to **mutate** the array later (e.g., `arr.splice(idx, 1)`).
- ✅ `indexOf` uses `===` (strict equality) and cannot search objects or NaN; `findIndex` accepts a custom predicate and is more flexible.
- ✅ Time complexity is **O(n)** worst case, **O(1)** best case (match at index 0).
- ✅ Space complexity is **O(1)** auxiliary (constant extra space).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Template Strings and JavaScript Classes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Template Strings and JavaScript Classes ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Template Strings (Template Literals) **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** Template Strings, formally known as **Template Literals**, are a feature introduced in **ECMAScript 2015 (ES6)** that provides a more powerful and readable way to construct strings. They are enclosed in **backticks (`` ` ``)** instead of single or double quotes, and they support **string interpolation**, **multi-line strings**, and **tagged templates**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Template Literals are an ES6 feature, NOT plain strings. The backtick character (`` ` ``) is mandatory. Students often confuse these with regular strings enclosed in quotes — never use `' '` or `" "` for template literals. **Conceptual Analogy — The "Custom Form Letter" Analogy:** Imagine you are a bank manager sending out 10,000 welcome letters to new customers. Writing each letter by hand is impossible. Instead, you create a **template letter** with **placeholders** like `{{CUSTOMER_NAME}}` and `{{ACCOUNT_NUMBER}}`. You then feed in the actual customer data, and the template automatically fills in the blanks. **Template Literals work exactly like this** — they are form letters for code, where `${expression}` is the placeholder, and JavaScript automatically replaces it with the actual value at runtime. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Terminology Mapping:** > - **Backticks** = the template letter envelope > - **`${...}`** = the placeholder slot inside the letter > - **Interpolated Value** = the final merged string --- ### 1.2 JavaScript Classes **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** **JavaScript Classes** are syntactic sugar over JavaScript's existing **prototype-based inheritance** model, introduced in **ES6 (ECMAScript 2015)**. A class is a **blueprint** for creating objects that share common properties and methods, encapsulating data and behavior into reusable, organized structures. **Conceptual Analogy — The "Architect's Blueprint" Analogy:** Think of a class as an **architect's blueprint for a house**. The blueprint itself is not a house — you cannot live in it. However, the blueprint defines: - **Properties** (4 bedrooms, 2 bathrooms, white walls) - **Behavior** (doors open, lights switch on) From this single blueprint, you can build **100 different houses** (objects/instance) on **100 different plots of land** (memory locations). Each house has the same structure but **unique data** (different family living in each). A **JavaScript Class** behaves identically — define the class once, then `new` it up as many times as needed. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition Recap:** > - **Class** = Blueprint (the recipe) > - **Object/Instance** = The actual house baked from the recipe > - **Constructor** = The build phase executed when `new` is called > - **Method** = An action the house can perform --- ### 1.3 Why Were These Introduced? (The "Why" Question) > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** Questions often ask "Why were classes introduced in JavaScript if it already had prototypes?" The answer has two parts: > 1. **Readability** — `class` syntax is cleaner and more familiar to developers from OOP languages like Java/C++. > 2. **Strictness** — Classes run in **strict mode** by default and provide clearer error messages. **Before ES6 (The Old Way):** ```javascript function Car(brand, speed) { this.brand = brand; this.speed = speed; } Car.prototype.accelerate = function() { this.speed += 10; }; ``` **After ES6 (The Modern Way):** ```javascript class Car { constructor(brand, speed) { this.brand = brand; this.speed = speed; } accelerate() { this.speed += 10; } } ``` The output is **functionally identical** — the class version is just a more pleasant way to write it. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of a Template Literal A Template Literal is built from three foundational building blocks: **1. The Backtick Delimiter** Enclose the entire string within backticks. Example: `` `Hello World` ``. **2. String Interpolation `${...}`** Any valid JavaScript expression can be embedded inside `${ }`. This includes variables, arithmetic, function calls, even ternary operators. **3. Multi-line Capability** Template literals preserve **newlines and whitespace** as written, eliminating the need for `\n` or `+` concatenation. **Syntax Form:** ```javascript `string text ${expression} string text` ``` --- ### 2.2 Three Core Features of Template Literals | # | Feature | Syntax Token | Purpose | Real-World Engineering Use | |---|---------|--------------|---------|---------------------------| | 1 | **Basic Interpolation** | `${variable}` | Embed variable values into strings | Generating dynamic HTML emails in web apps | | 2 | **Expression Interpolation** | `${a + b}`, `${ternary}` | Evaluate JS expressions inline | Building real-time dashboards (price × quantity) | | 3 | **Multi-line Strings** | `` `line1\nline2` `` (literal newline) | Write strings spanning multiple lines | Writing SQL queries, JSON templates, code generators | | 4 | **Nested Templates** | `` `outer ${`inner`}` `` | Embed a template inside another | Building recursive component trees in React | | 5 | **Tagged Templates** | `` tagFn`text` `` | Pass template parts + values to a function | Sanitizing HTML, internationalization (i18n), styled-components | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** Tagged Templates are a **favourite KTU question**. Remember — the tag function receives an array of string parts as its first argument, followed by the interpolated values. --- ### 2.3 Anatomy of a JavaScript Class A class definition contains the following components: **1. Class Declaration** ```javascript class ClassName { ... } ``` The class name must follow PascalCase convention (KTU coding standard). **2. Constructor Method** A special method automatically invoked when `new ClassName()` is called. Used to initialize instance properties. **Only ONE constructor is allowed per class**. **3. Instance Methods** Functions defined inside the class body. They are added to the class's prototype and are shared across all instances (memory efficient). **4. Static Methods** Methods prefixed with the `static` keyword. They are called on the **class itself**, not on instances. Useful for utility/helper functions tied to the class conceptually. **5. Getters and Setters** Special accessor methods using `get` and `set` keywords. They allow controlled read/write access to a property. **6. Inheritance via `extends`** A child class can inherit from a parent class, gaining all its methods and properties. --- ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **KTU Cheat Sheet for Module 2 (JavaScript Modern Features)** | Concept | Syntax Pattern | Returns / Behaviour | KTU Exam Frequency | |---------|----------------|---------------------|---------------------| | **Template Literal** | `` `Hello ${name}` `` | String with embedded value | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Multi-line String** | `` `Line 1\nLine 2` `` (raw newline) | String preserving newlines | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Tagged Template** | `` highlight`text ${x}` `` | First arg: array of string parts; rest: values | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Class Declaration** | `class Foo { ... }` | Defines a blueprint | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Constructor** | `constructor(params) { this.x = params; }` | Initializes new instance | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Instance Method** | `methodName() { ... }` | Called via `instance.methodName()` | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Static Method** | `static helper() { ... }` | Called via `Class.helper()` | ⭐⭐⭐ | | **Getter** | `get propName() { return this._x; }` | Read-only style access | ⭐⭐⭐ | | **Setter** | `set propName(v) { this._x = v; }` | Validated write access | ⭐⭐⭐ | | **Inheritance** | `class Child extends Parent { ... }` | Child inherits Parent's methods | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **`super` in constructor** | `super(args)` | Calls parent constructor (must be first line) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **`super` in methods** | `super.methodName()` | Calls parent version of method | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Class Expression** | `const X = class { ... };` | Anonymous class assigned to variable | ⭐⭐ | --- ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility **Template Literals** are used extensively in: - **React/JSX frameworks** for building component strings - **Styled-components** library (Tagged Templates for CSS-in-JS) - **GraphQL clients** (e.g., Apollo's `gql` tagged template) - **Build tools** (Webpack, Vite config generators) - **Server-side rendering** (Next.js) for dynamic HTML generation **JavaScript Classes** are used in: - **Frontend frameworks**: React class components (legacy), Angular services - **Backend frameworks**: TypeORM entities, NestJS controllers, Sequelize models - **Game development**: Phaser.js entity blueprints - **Testing libraries**: Jest test suite organization > [!IMPORTANT] > **Industrial Relevance:** In a 2024 stack overflow survey, ES6 classes and template literals are listed among the **top 5 most-used modern JavaScript features** in production code. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From String Concatenation to Template Literals **Step 1: The Old Pre-ES6 Method (Concatenation)** ```javascript const name = "Ananya"; const score = 95; const result = "Student " + name + " scored " + score + " marks."; ``` **Step 2: Identify the Pain Points** - **P1:** Visual noise — too many `+` operators and quote pairs - **P2:** Error-prone — forgetting a `+` or quote breaks the string - **P3:** No expression evaluation — must pre-compute every value - **P4:** Multi-line is verbose — requires `\n` and array `.join()` **Step 3: The ES6 Solution** ```javascript const name = "Ananya"; const score = 95; const result = `Student ${name} scored ${score} marks.`; ``` **Step 4: Confirm Equivalence** $$ \text{result} = \text{"Student Ananya scored 95 marks."} $$ The output is **byte-for-byte identical** to the concatenation method, but the template literal is **40% fewer characters** and far more readable. **Step 5: Expression Interpolation Upgrade** ```javascript const price = 100; const tax = 0.18; const message = `Final price: ₹${price + (price * tax)}`; ``` The expression `price + (price * tax)` is evaluated **before** string substitution, giving: $$ \text{message} = \text{"Final price: ₹118"} $$ **Step 6: Multi-line Capability** ```javascript const html = `

${title}

${body}

`; ``` The whitespace and indentation are **preserved exactly as written** — no `\n` escape sequences required. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — From Constructor Function to ES6 Class **Step 1: The ES5 Constructor Function Pattern** ```javascript function Student(name, rollNo) { this.name = name; this.rollNo = rollNo; } Student.prototype.greet = function() { return `Hello, I am ${this.name}`; }; const s1 = new Student("Rahul", 23); ``` **Step 2: Trace What Happens in Memory** When `new Student("Rahul", 23)` executes: 1. A new empty object `{}` is created. 2. The object's prototype is linked to `Student.prototype`. 3. The constructor is called with `this` bound to the new object. 4. The new object is returned automatically. **Step 3: The ES6 Class Equivalent** ```javascript class Student { constructor(name, rollNo) { this.name = name; this.rollNo = rollNo; } greet() { return `Hello, I am ${this.name}`; } } const s1 = new Student("Rahul", 23); ``` **Step 4: Verify the Output is Identical** ```javascript s1.greet(); // Returns: "Hello, I am Rahul" typeof s1; // Returns: "object" s1 instanceof Student; // Returns: true ``` **Step 5: Prove Class Methods Live on the Prototype** ```javascript console.log(Student.prototype.greet); // Function exists on prototype console.log(s1.hasOwnProperty("name")); // true (instance property) console.log(s1.hasOwnProperty("greet")); // false (prototype method) ``` This proves the class is **syntactic sugar** — the underlying prototype-based mechanism is unchanged. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Inheritance with `extends` and `super` **Step 1: Define the Parent Class** ```javascript class Vehicle { constructor(brand, wheels) { this.brand = brand; this.wheels = wheels; } describe() { return `${this.brand} has ${this.wheels} wheels.`; } } ``` **Step 2: Define the Child Class with `extends`** ```javascript class Car extends Vehicle { constructor(brand, wheels, fuelType) { super(brand, wheels); this.fuelType = fuelType; } honk() { return `${this.brand} says Beep!`; } } ``` **Step 3: Explain the Role of `super()`** The `super(brand, wheels)` call: 1. **Must be the first line** of the child constructor (or omitted entirely). 2. Invokes the parent class's constructor. 3. Initializes `this.brand` and `this.wheels` inside the parent context. 4. Only after `super()` returns can we safely use `this` to add new properties. **Step 4: Instantiate and Test** ```javascript const myCar = new Car("Toyota", 4, "Petrol"); myCar.describe(); // "Toyota has 4 wheels." (inherited from Vehicle) myCar.honk(); // "Toyota says Beep!" (defined in Car) ``` **Step 5: Method Overriding with `super.methodName()`** ```javascript class ElectricCar extends Car { constructor(brand, wheels, batteryCapacity) { super(brand, wheels, "Electric"); this.batteryCapacity = batteryCapacity; } describe() { return `${super.describe()} Battery: ${this.batteryCapacity} kWh.`; } } const tesla = new ElectricCar("Tesla", 4, 75); tesla.describe(); ``` The output is constructed as: $$ \text{output} = \underbrace{\text{super.describe()}}_{\text{"Tesla has 4 wheels."}} + \text{" Battery: "} + \text{this.batteryCapacity} + \text{" kWh."} $$ $$ \therefore \text{output} = \text{"Tesla has 4 wheels. Battery: 75 kWh."} $$ --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What? | Scenario | Recommended Approach | Reason | |----------|---------------------|--------| | Building a string with 1-2 variables | Template Literal | More readable than concatenation | | Generating multi-line HTML/SQL | Template Literal | Preserves whitespace, no `\n` mess | | Sanitizing user input in HTML | Tagged Template | Pre-built libraries (lit-html) handle it | | Creating a single utility object | Object literal `{}` | Class is overkill | | Creating many similar objects | **Class** | DRY principle, shared methods | | Modeling real-world entities (User, Product) | **Class** | Maps naturally to OOP thinking | | Inheriting shared behavior | **Class with extends** | Promotes code reuse | | One-off helper function | Static method | No need to instantiate | | Validated property access | Getter/Setter | Encapsulation & control | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Template Literal Evaluation Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Template Literal Code] --> B{Backtick Delimiter} B --> C[Scanner Walks Character by Character] C --> D{Encountered Dollar Brace Dollar} D -->|No| E[Append Character to String Buffer] D -->|Yes| F[Extract Expression Inside Braces] F --> G[Evaluate Expression in JS Engine] G --> H[Convert Result to String via ToString] H --> I[Append Converted Value to Buffer] E --> J[Continue Scanning] I --> J J --> K[Reached End of Literal] K --> L[Return Final Concatenated String] style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#fff style L fill:#16a34a,color:#fff style G fill:#ea580c,color:#fff ``` **Flow Explanation:** 1. The JavaScript engine encounters `` ` `` (backtick) — enters template-literal-parsing mode. 2. It scans each character one by one. 3. When it sees `${`, it switches to **expression-evaluation mode**. 4. It extracts the expression, evaluates it, converts to string, and concatenates. 5. On reaching the closing `` ` ``, the final string is returned. --- ### 4.2 JavaScript Class Architecture & Memory Model ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph classDef[Class Definition] A[class Student] B[constructor name, rollNo] C[Method greet lives on prototype] D[Static method create lives on class itself] end subgraph instanceDef[Instance Objects] E[Instance s1 name=Ananya] F[Instance s2 name=Rahul] G[Instance s3 name=Priya] end A -.extends.-> H[class Person] H --> I[Inherited method introduce] A -->|new| E A -->|new| F A -->|new| G E -.prototype link.- C F -.prototype link.- C G -.prototype link.- C style A fill:#0ea5e9,color:#fff style H fill:#a855f7,color:#fff style C fill:#facc15 style D fill:#fb923c style E fill:#22c55e,color:#fff style F fill:#22c55e,color:#fff style G fill:#22c55e,color:#fff ``` **Memory Model Insight:** - The **class itself** exists once in memory. - The **prototype** (containing methods) exists once in memory. - Each **instance** has its own memory for properties (name, rollNo). - All instances **share** the same methods via the prototype chain — this is the key to memory efficiency. --- ### 4.3 Inheritance Hierarchy Diagram ```mermaid classDiagram class Vehicle { +String brand +int wheels +describe() String } class Car { +String fuelType +honk() String } class ElectricCar { +int batteryCapacity +describe() String OVERRIDDEN } class Motorcycle { +boolean hasSidecar +wheelie() String } Vehicle <|-- Car Car <|-- ElectricCar Vehicle <|-- Motorcycle style Vehicle fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff style Car fill:#10b981,color:#fff style ElectricCar fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff style Motorcycle fill:#ef4444,color:#fff ``` **Hierarchy Explanation:** - `Vehicle` is the **base/parent class** with brand and wheels. - `Car` extends Vehicle, adding fuelType. - `ElectricCar` extends Car, adding batteryCapacity AND overriding `describe()`. - `Motorcycle` extends Vehicle directly (sibling to Car). --- ### 4.4 Tagged Template Function Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User Code participant T as Tag Function highlight participant E as JS Engine U->>T: highlight`Name: ${name}, Age: ${age}` T->>E: Receive args Note over T: strings = ["Name: ", ", Age: ", ""] values = [name, age] T->>T: Iterate over values T->>E: Apply formatting logic E-->>T: Return processed pieces T->>U: Return final styled string style U fill:#6366f1,color:#fff style T fill:#ec4899,color:#fff style E fill:#14b8a6,color:#fff ``` **Use Cases of Tagged Templates:** - HTML escaping libraries (e.g., lit-html) - Internationalization (i18n) - CSS-in-JS (styled-components) - GraphQL query parsing (Apollo's `gql` tag) ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Explain template literals in JavaScript. List any three advantages over traditional string concatenation.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Template literals (also called template strings) are string literals introduced in ES6 that are enclosed in backticks (`` ` ``) instead of quotes. They support **string interpolation** using `${expression}` syntax, allowing variables and expressions to be embedded directly within strings. **Three Advantages:** 1. **Readability** — Embedding values with `${}` is cleaner than using multiple `+` operators and quote pairs. 2. **Multi-line support** — Strings can span multiple lines naturally, with newlines and whitespace preserved without `\n` escape sequences. 3. **Expression evaluation** — Any valid JavaScript expression (arithmetic, function calls, ternaries) can be placed inside `${}` and evaluated automatically. ```javascript const name = "Sara"; console.log(`Hello, ${name.toUpperCase()}!`); // Output: Hello, SARA! ``` --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **What is a class in JavaScript? Explain the role of the `constructor` method.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **class** in JavaScript is a blueprint for creating objects, introduced in ES6 as syntactic sugar over the prototype-based inheritance system. A class encapsulates data (properties) and behavior (methods) into a single reusable structure. **Role of the Constructor:** - The `constructor` is a **special method** automatically invoked when `new ClassName()` is called. - It is used to **initialize instance properties** with values passed as arguments. - **Only one constructor is allowed** per class. - If no constructor is defined, JavaScript provides a default empty constructor. ```javascript class Employee { constructor(id, name) { this.id = id; this.name = name; } } const e1 = new Employee(101, "Arjun"); ``` --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **(a) (7 Marks)** With a suitable example, explain template literals in JavaScript. Also explain tagged template literals with a working code example. **(b) (7 Marks)** What are static methods and getters/setters in a JavaScript class? Demonstrate with a complete code example that includes inheritance using `extends` and `super`. --- **Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks):** **Template Literals Definition:** Template literals are ES6 strings enclosed in backticks. They support: 1. **Interpolation** with `${expression}` 2. **Multi-line** strings natively 3. **Expression evaluation** inline **Example — Basic Interpolation and Multi-line:** ```javascript const student = { name: "Diya", marks: 92 }; const report = ` === STUDENT REPORT === Name : ${student.name} Grade : ${student.marks >= 90 ? "A+" : "A"} Status: ${student.marks >= 50 ? "PASS" : "FAIL"} ====================== `; console.log(report); ``` **Output:** ``` === STUDENT REPORT === Name : Diya Grade : A+ Status: PASS ====================== ``` **Tagged Template Literals Explanation:** A **tagged template** uses a function to process the template literal. The function receives: - First argument: an **array of string parts** - Remaining arguments: the **interpolated values** **Working Example:** ```javascript function highlight(strings, ...values) { let result = ""; strings.forEach((str, i) => { result += str; if (i < values.length) { result += `[${values[i].toUpperCase()}]`; } }); return result; } const name = "kerala"; const capital = "thiruvananthapuram"; const output = highlight`State: ${name}, Capital: ${capital}`; console.log(output); ``` **Output:** `State: [KERALA], Capital: [THIRUVANANTHAPURAM]` The function `highlight` is the **tag**. It receives `["State: ", ", Capital: ", ""]` as `strings` and `["kerala", "thiruvananthapuram"]` as `values`. --- **Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks):** **Static Methods:** - Defined using the `static` keyword. - Called on the **class itself**, NOT on instances. - Cannot access `this` referring to an instance. - Commonly used for utility/helper functions related to the class. **Getters and Setters:** - Defined using `get` and `set` keywords. - Allow **controlled access** to class properties. - Useful for validation and computed properties. **Inheritance with `extends` and `super`:** ```javascript class Account { constructor(holder, balance) { this.holder = holder; this._balance = balance; } get balance() { return `₹${this._balance}`; } set balance(amount) { if (amount < 0) { console.log("Invalid amount!"); } else { this._balance = amount; } } static bankName() { return "KTU Federal Bank"; } } class SavingsAccount extends Account { constructor(holder, balance, interestRate) { super(holder, balance); this.interestRate = interestRate; } calculateInterest() { return `Interest: ₹${this._balance * this.interestRate}`; } } const acc = new SavingsAccount("Meera", 10000, 0.05); console.log(acc.balance); // Getter: "₹10000" console.log(SavingsAccount.bankName()); // Static: "KTU Federal Bank" acc.balance = 15000; // Setter accepts console.log(acc.balance); // "₹15000" acc.balance = -500; // Setter rejects console.log(acc.calculateInterest()); // "Interest: ₹750" ``` **Key Concepts Demonstrated:** - `extends` — `SavingsAccount` inherits from `Account`. - `super(holder, balance)` — calls parent constructor (must be first line). - `get balance()` — property-style read access. - `set balance(amount)` — property-style write with validation. - `static bankName()` — called on the class itself, not on instance. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **(a) (7 Marks)** Explain the difference between ES5 constructor functions and ES6 classes. Rewrite the following ES5 code using ES6 class syntax. ```javascript function Book(title, author, price) { this.title = title; this.author = author; this.price = price; } Book.prototype.getDiscountedPrice = function(discount) { return this.price - (this.price * discount / 100); }; ``` **(b) (7 Marks)** Implement a multi-level inheritance hierarchy in JavaScript with classes for `Shape`, `Rectangle`, and `Square`. Each class should have a method to compute area. Use `super` properly and demonstrate method overriding. --- **Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks):** **Differences Table:** | Aspect | ES5 Constructor Function | ES6 Class | |--------|--------------------------|-----------| | Definition | `function Foo() {}` | `class Foo {}` | | Method placement | On prototype explicitly: `Foo.prototype.method` | Inside class body, auto on prototype | | Strict mode | Optional | Always in strict mode | | Hoisting | Function declarations are hoisted | Class declarations are NOT hoisted (TDZ) | | Calling without `new` | Allowed (errors at runtime) | Throws `TypeError` immediately | | Inheritance | `Object.create()` or prototype chaining | `extends` and `super` | **Rewritten ES6 Class:** ```javascript class Book { constructor(title, author, price) { this.title = title; this.author = author; this.price = price; } getDiscountedPrice(discount) { return this.price - (this.price * discount / 100); } } const b1 = new Book("JS Basics", "Kyle Simpson", 500); console.log(b1.getDiscountedPrice(10)); // 450 ``` **Memory Insight:** The `getDiscountedPrice` method is automatically placed on `Book.prototype`, identical to the ES5 version — confirming that **class is syntactic sugar**. --- **Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks):** **Multi-level Inheritance Code:** ```javascript class Shape { constructor(color) { this.color = color; } describe() { return `A ${this.color} shape`; } area() { return 0; } } class Rectangle extends Shape { constructor(color, length, width) { super(color); this.length = length; this.width = width; } area() { return this.length * this.width; } describe() { return `${super.describe()} that is a rectangle`; } } class Square extends Rectangle { constructor(color, side) { super(color, side, side); this.side = side; } area() { return this.side * this.side; } } const sq = new Square("blue", 5); console.log(sq.describe()); // "A blue shape that is a rectangle" console.log(sq.area()); // 25 ``` **Multi-level Chain:** `Square` → `Rectangle` → `Shape` - `super(color, side, side)` in `Square` calls `Rectangle`'s constructor. - `super(color)` in `Rectangle` calls `Shape`'s constructor. - `super.describe()` calls the parent's version of `describe()`. - `area()` is **overridden** in both `Rectangle` and `Square`. **Method Resolution Order (MRO):** JavaScript looks up `sq.area()` first in `Square`, then `Rectangle`, then `Shape`. The closest definition wins. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting `super()` in the child constructor** — Causes a `ReferenceError: Must call super constructor in derived class before accessing 'this'`. This is the **#1 most common mistake** and costs 2-3 marks. > 2. **Using `this` before `super()`** — JavaScript will throw a runtime error. Always put `super()` first. > 3. **Defining methods with `function` keyword inside classes** — Wrong syntax. Use shorthand: `methodName() { ... }` NOT `methodName: function() { ... }`. > 4. **Hoisting classes** — `new MyClass()` BEFORE its declaration will throw an error (Temporal Dead Zone). Unlike function declarations, classes are NOT hoisted. > 5. **Forgetting backticks for template literals** — Using `'${}'` with single quotes results in a literal string with no interpolation. > 6. **Static methods on instances** — Calling `instance.staticMethod()` will throw `TypeError`.
