Always call on the class itself. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Save This!)** **Template Literals:** - ☐ Use backticks `` ` `` — NEVER single or double quotes - ☐ `${expression}` for interpolation of variables, math, function calls - ☐ Multi-line strings work natively (no `\n` needed) - ☐ Tagged templates: `tagFn\`text ${val}\`` — function receives `(strings, ...values)` - ☐ Nested templates allowed: `` `outer ${`inner ${x}`}` `` **JavaScript Classes:** - ☐ `class ClassName { ... }` — PascalCase convention - ☐ `constructor()` — auto-called by `new`, initializes `this` - ☐ Methods go on the prototype automatically (memory efficient) - ☐ `static methodName()` — called on class, not instance - ☐ `get propName()` / `set propName(v)` — controlled property access - ☐ `class Child extends Parent` — inheritance - ☐ `super(args)` — **must be first line** in child constructor - ☐ `super.methodName()` — call parent version of overridden method - ☐ Classes are NOT hoisted (Temporal Dead Zone) - ☐ Classes always run in strict mode - ☐ Classes are **syntactic sugar** over prototype-based inheritance **Common Exam Traps:** - ☐ One class = one constructor (multiple constructors not allowed) - ☐ `this` cannot be used before `super()` in derived classes - ☐ Method overriding requires exact same signature - ☐ `instanceof` checks the prototype chain — works with class hierarchy > [!IMPORTANT] > **One-Line Takeaway:** Template Literals give JavaScript a powerful, readable string-building syntax, and ES6 Classes provide a clean, OOP-friendly way to structure code — both being syntactic sugar layers over the existing prototype-based engine.

topics.title: Callbacks, Promises, and Async/Await
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Callbacks, Promises, and Async/Await

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 JavaScript Execution Model — The Foundation

JavaScript is a **single-threaded, synchronous, blocking** scripting language that executes one statement at a time on a single call stack. To perform time-consuming operations (network requests, file I/O, timers) without freezing the user interface, JavaScript relies on the **event loop** and **asynchronous programming patterns**.

The three foundational asynchronous patterns in modern JavaScript are:

### 1.2 Callbacks — The Original Pattern

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
> A **Callback** is a function passed as an argument to another function, which is then invoked *inside* the outer function to complete some kind of routine or action. It is the original mechanism JavaScript used to handle asynchronous operations.

- **Synchronous Callback**: Executed immediately during the execution of the enclosing function (e.g., `Array.prototype.map`, `Array.prototype.filter`).
- **Asynchronous Callback (Deferred Callback)**: Executed *after* the completion of an asynchronous operation (e.g., the callback inside `setTimeout`, `fs.readFile`, `XMLHttpRequest.onload`).

### 1.3 Promises — The Structured Successor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
> A **Promise** is an object representing the *eventual completion* (or failure) of an asynchronous operation. It acts as a proxy for a value that is not necessarily known when the promise is created. A Promise is in one of three states: **pending**, **fulfilled**, or **rejected**.

### 1.4 Async/Await — The Syntactic Sugar Layer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
> **`async/await`** is ES2017 syntactic sugar built on top of Promises. The `async` keyword declares a function as asynchronous (it implicitly returns a Promise), and the `await` keyword pauses execution *within* that function until a Promise settles, without blocking the main call stack.

---

## 1.5 Real-World Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Restaurant Model):**

Imagine ordering food at a restaurant:

- **Callback** = You give the waiter your **phone number** and sit down. When the food is ready, the waiter *calls you back*. If you ordered three dishes this way, you juggle three phone numbers. If one dish depends on the previous one, you must chain the calls inside the calls (Callback Hell).
- **Promise** = You receive a **token with a number** (a Promise object). You can sit down, and the number will be called when ready. The token has built-in methods: `then()` for "when ready" and `catch()` for "if something went wrong". You can chain tokens and even `Promise.all` to wait for multiple tokens.
- **Async/Await** = You place your order, **sit down, and wait politely at the table**. The kitchen works in the background, and the food is brought to you when ready. Your code reads like a *linear, synchronous recipe*, but it is asynchronous underneath.

This analogy is a high-yield KTU concept — examiners frequently test the **evolution** from callbacks to promises to async/await in viva and short-answer questions.

---

## 1.6 The Three States of a Promise (Exhaustive)

A Promise is a **state machine** with exactly **three mutually exclusive states**:

| State | Description | Transition |
|---|---|---|
| `pending` | Initial state, neither fulfilled nor rejected | Can transition to fulfilled or rejected |
| `fulfilled` | Operation completed successfully, has a `value` | Terminal (cannot transition again) |
| `rejected` | Operation failed, has a `reason` | Terminal (cannot transition again) |

Once a promise is *settled* (fulfilled or rejected), its state and value are **immutable** — this property is called *immutability of settled promises*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Callbacks — Operational Mechanics

A callback is just a function reference. The mechanism is straightforward:

1. A higher-order function accepts a function as a parameter.
2. The function is stored or scheduled for later execution.
3. When an event completes (timer, network response, file read), the callback is pushed onto the **task queue** (or **microtask queue** depending on origin).
4. The **event loop** picks it up when the call stack is empty.

### The Famous Problem: **Callback Hell** (Pyramid of Doom)

When multiple async operations depend on each other, callbacks are nested, producing unreadable, unmaintainable code:

```javascript
// CALLBACK HELL (Pyramid of Doom)
getUser(userId, function(user) {
    getOrders(user.id, function(orders) {
        getOrderDetails(orders[0].id, function(details) {
            getShippingStatus(details.shipId, function(status) {
                console.log(status);
            }, handleError);
        }, handleError);
    }, handleError);
}, handleError);
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Inversion of Control**: Callbacks suffer from a critical design flaw — you hand control of *when* and *how* your callback runs to another function. If that function calls your callback twice, or never calls it, the program breaks. This is a known KTU viva question.

---

## 2.2 Promises — Operational Mechanics

A Promise is created using the `Promise` constructor or factory functions like `fetch()`.

```javascript
const myPromise = new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
    // Async work here
    if (success) resolve(value);
    else reject(error);
});
```

The `executor function` runs *synchronously* inside the constructor, but the `resolve()` and `reject()` calls schedule the callbacks for the **next microtask tick**.

### 2.2.1 Key Promise Methods (KTU High-Yield)

- **`.then(onFulfilled, onRejected)`** — Schedules callbacks for the success/failure of the promise. **Returns a new promise**, enabling chaining.
- **`.catch(onRejected)`** — Sugar for `.then(null, onRejected)`. Handles errors anywhere in the chain.
- **`.finally(onFinally)`** — Runs whether the promise was fulfilled or rejected (e.g., for hiding a loading spinner).
- **`Promise.resolve(value)`** — Returns a promise resolved with `value`.
- **`Promise.reject(reason)`** — Returns a promise rejected with `reason`.
- **`Promise.all(iterable)`** — Waits for **all** promises; rejects if **any** reject.
- **`Promise.race(iterable)`** — Resolves/rejects as soon as the **first** promise settles.
- **`Promise.allSettled(iterable)`** — Waits for all to settle; never rejects.
- **`Promise.any(iterable)`** — Resolves when **any** fulfills; rejects only if all reject.

### 2.2.2 Chaining Promises

```javascript
fetch('/api/user')
    .then(response => response.json())
    .then(user => fetch(`/api/orders/${user.id}`))
    .then(res => res.json())
    .then(orders => console.log(orders))
    .catch(err => console.error(err));
```

Each `.then()` returns a *new* promise, so errors automatically propagate down the chain to the nearest `.catch()`.

---

## 2.3 Async/Await — Operational Mechanics

`async/await` does **not replace** promises — it **consumes** them. It is purely a syntactic transformation.

```javascript
async function getUserOrders(userId) {
    try {
        const response = await fetch(`/api/user/${userId}`);
        const user = await response.json();
        const ordersRes = await fetch(`/api/orders/${user.id}`);
        const orders = await ordersRes.json();
        return orders;
    } catch (error) {
        console.error('Failed:', error);
    }
}
```

### 2.3.1 The Transformation Rules (KTU High-Yield)

| Async/Await Construct | Equivalent Promise Construct |
|---|---|
| `async function f(){}` | `function f(){ return new Promise(...); }` |
| `await promise` | `.then(value => ...)` inside the function body |
| `return value` (in async) | `resolve(value)` |
| `throw error` (in async) | `reject(error)` |

### 2.3.2 Error Handling

Async/await uses the **traditional `try...catch`** block — the same syntax used in synchronous code. This unifies error handling, which is a major reason for its adoption.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight**: The `await` keyword only pauses the *current async function*. The main thread, event loop, and other tasks continue running. This is **cooperative non-blocking** behavior.

---

## 2.4 The Event Loop, Microtasks, and Macrotasks (KTU Critical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Microtask Queue** has **higher priority** than the **Task Queue (Macrotask Queue)**. After every macrotask, the engine drains *all* microtasks before moving to the next macrotask.

- **Microtasks**: Promise callbacks (`.then`, `.catch`, `.finally`), `queueMicrotask()`, `MutationObserver`.
- **Macrotasks**: `setTimeout`, `setInterval`, `setImmediate` (Node.js), I/O operations, UI rendering.

**Execution Order Rule**: Call Stack → Drain Microtask Queue → Take one Macrotask → Drain Microtask Queue → ... (repeat).

This explains why `Promise.then` always fires *before* `setTimeout(..., 0)`.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Callbacks | Promises | Async/Await |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Introduced In** | Original JavaScript | ES6 (ES2015) | ES8 (ES2017) |
| **Readability** | Poor (nested) | Good (chainable) | Excellent (linear) |
| **Error Handling** | Per-callback error param | `.catch()` chain | `try...catch` block |
| **Return Value** | None (side effect) | Promise (chainable) | Promise (implicit) |
| **Inversion of Control** | Yes (problematic) | No (resolved once) | No (managed by engine) |
| **Parallel Composition** | Manual (complex) | `Promise.all` etc. | `Promise.all` + await |
| **Stack Trace** | Often lost | Preserved | Preserved |
| **Supported in** | All engines | ES6+ | ES2017+ |
| **Debugging** | Difficult | Moderate | Easy (step through) |
| **Cancel Support** | Custom logic | Custom $\vert$ `AbortController` | Custom $\vert$ `AbortController` |
| **Common API Example** | `setTimeout`, `fs.readFile` | `fetch()` | N/A (consumes Promises) |

> [!NOTE]
> The `$\vert$` symbol above uses LaTeX math-mode to avoid breaking the markdown table — a real KTU question may ask you to compare these three side-by-side.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Transformation Analysis: Callback → Promise → Async/Await

The same task (fetch user, then fetch their orders) expressed in all three styles, with **explicit operational reasoning**.

### 3.1.1 Version 1 — Callback Form (Base Case)

```javascript
function getUserOrdersCallback(userId, onSuccess, onError) {
    // STEP 1: Define the operation to retrieve the user
    fetch(`/api/user/${userId}`)
        // STEP 2: parse the JSON response
        .then(response => response.json())
        // STEP 3: receive parsed user, then trigger next async op
        .then(user => {
            // STEP 4: nested call to fetch orders
            fetch(`/api/orders/${user.id}`)
                .then(res => res.json())
                // STEP 5: final callback fires with the orders
                .then(orders => onSuccess(orders))
                // STEP 6: error path of the inner call
                .catch(err => onError(err));
        })
        // STEP 7: error path of the outer call
        .catch(err => onError(err));
}
```

**Analytical Observation**: Notice the *indentation drift to the right* with each dependency. This is the **Pyramid of Doom**, and at scale, the code becomes unmaintainable. The control flow is also harder to trace because errors must be caught at *every level*.

---

### 3.1.2 Version 2 — Promise Form (Flat Chain)

```javascript
function getUserOrdersPromise(userId) {
    // STEP 1: Return a promise that resolves to the orders array
    return fetch(`/api/user/${userId}`)      // Promise<Response>
        .then(res => res.json())              // Promise<User>
        .then(user => fetch(`/api/orders/${user.id}`)) // Promise<Response>
        .then(res => res.json())              // Promise<Orders[]>
        // STEP 2: All errors from any .then() propagate to a single .catch
        .catch(err => {
            console.error(err);
            return []; // graceful fallback
        });
}
```

**Analytical Observation**: Code is now *flat*. The chain reads top-to-bottom. A *single* `.catch()` at the end handles errors from any preceding step — this is **error propagation through the promise chain**. The function returns a promise, so the caller can compose it further.

---

### 3.1.3 Version 3 — Async/Await Form (Linear Synchronous Style)

```javascript
async function getUserOrdersAsync(userId) {
    // STEP 1: try block wraps the entire async pipeline
    try {
        // STEP 2: await pauses THIS function until fetch resolves
        const res     = await fetch(`/api/user/${userId}`);
        // STEP 3: another await — still inside the same try
        const user    = await res.json();
        // STEP 4: value of the awaited expression becomes the resolved value
        const ordersRes = await fetch(`/api/orders/${user.id}`);
        // STEP 5: final await — return is implicit
        const orders  = await ordersRes.json();
        // STEP 6: returning a value wraps it in a resolved Promise automatically
        return orders;
    } catch (err) {
        // STEP 7: any rejected promise (or thrown error) lands here
        console.error('Pipeline failed:', err);
        return []; // graceful fallback
    }
}
```

**Analytical Observation**: The logic is now *indistinguishable from synchronous code*. The `await` keyword is the syntactic marker that signals "pause this function, do not block the thread". Behind the scenes, every `await` is a *microtask checkpoint*.

---

## 3.2 Mathematical Formalization of the Event Loop

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **high-yield KTU derivation** frequently asked in 14-mark questions on asynchronous JavaScript.

Let the call stack be $C$, the microtask queue be $M$, and the macrotask queue be $T$. At any tick $n$ of the event loop, the system obeys the following recurrence:

$$
\text{State}_{n+1} \;=\; \mathcal{F}(\text{State}_n)
$$

where the transition function $\mathcal{F}$ is defined as:

$$
\mathcal{F}(C, M, T) \;=\; \begin{cases}
\text{Execute}(C_{\text{top}}), & \text{if } C \neq \emptyset \\
\text{Drain}(M) \cup \text{Pop}(T), & \text{if } C = \emptyset \text{ and } M \neq \emptyset \\
\text{Idle}, & \text{if } C = \emptyset, \, M = \emptyset, \, T = \emptyset
\end{cases}
$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1** — Initialize the state.
Let $C_0$ contain the initial script. Let $M_0 = \emptyset$ and $T_0 = \emptyset$.

**Step 2** — Run the synchronous script.
The engine pops frames off $C$ until it is empty.

**Step 3** — Enqueue side effects.
If the script schedules a `setTimeout(fn, 0)`, the engine pushes $fn$ onto $T$ (macrotask). If it calls `Promise.resolve().then(fn)`, the engine pushes $fn$ onto $M$ (microtask).

**Step 4** — Apply the transition function.
Since $C = \emptyset$ and $M \neq \emptyset$ (promise resolved *during* execution), $\mathcal{F}$ drains $M$ first. Each microtask is executed; any newly enqueued microtasks are drained *before* moving to $T$.

**Step 5** — Take one macrotask.
Once $M$ is empty, the engine pops the oldest item from $T$ and pushes it onto $C$.

**Step 6** — Recurse.
Apply $\mathcal{F}$ again. This loop continues until all queues are empty.

### Worked Execution-Order Example

```javascript
console.log('1');
setTimeout(() => console.log('2'), 0);
Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log('3'));
console.log('4');
```

**Step-by-step trace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{`console.log('1')`} \rightarrow \text{prints 1} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{`setTimeout` schedules callback into } T \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{`Promise.resolve().then(...)` schedules callback into } M \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{`console.log('4')`} \rightarrow \text{prints 4} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & C = \emptyset, \, M = \{ \text{log 3} \}, \, T = \{ \text{log 2} \} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \mathcal{F} \text{ drains } M \rightarrow \text{prints 3} \\
\text{Step 7:} \quad & M = \emptyset, \, T = \{ \text{log 2} \} \rightarrow \text{pop and print 2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Output**: `1, 4, 3, 2` — the famous KTU question that tests whether you understand microtask priority.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each Pattern

| Scenario | Recommended Pattern | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Simple one-shot async (e.g., timer) | Callback / `setTimeout` | Minimal overhead |
| Multiple independent fetches | `Promise.all` + `await` | Parallel execution, fails fast |
| Sequential dependent fetches | Async/Await | Readable, linear flow |
| Library exposing event-based API | Callback (wrapped in Promise) | Legacy compatibility |
| Top-level `await` (ES2022) | Async/Await | Cleanest module-level async |
| Streaming data (e.g., file read) | Callbacks $\vert$ EventEmitter | Push-based, not pull-based |
| Mixed sync + async logic | Async/Await with `try/catch` | Unified error handling |
| Cancelling a request | `AbortController` with `fetch` | Modern cancellation standard |

> [!NOTE]
> The $\vert$ symbol is rendered in LaTeX math-mode to prevent breaking the markdown table — this dual-form pattern (Math-mode pipes inside tables) is part of the KTU-Premier-Engine V10 specification.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Callback Flow — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Main Script Executes] --> B[Call getData with callback fn]
    B --> C{Is operation sync?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Execute callback immediately]
    C -- No --> E[Register callback in Task Queue]
    D --> F[Continue main script]
    E --> G[Operation runs in background]
    G --> H[Event Loop polls Task Queue]
    H --> I[Call Stack empty?]
    I -- No --> H
    I -- Yes --> J[Push callback to Call Stack]
    J --> K[Execute callback with result]
    K --> L[Callback completes]
```

**Reading the diagram**: The event loop continually checks if the call stack is empty. When the async operation (e.g., `setTimeout`) finishes, the callback is moved to the task queue, then to the call stack, then executed.

---

## 4.2 Promise State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> pendingState: new Promise executor runs
    pendingState --> fulfilledState: resolve value called
    pendingState --> rejectedState: reject reason called
    fulfilledState --> [*]: value locked, immutable
    rejectedState --> [*]: reason locked, immutable
    note right of pendingState: thenable callbacks queued
    note right of fulfilledState: .then onFulfilled fires
    note right of rejectedState: .catch onRejected fires
```

**Reading the diagram**: A Promise is a strict state machine. It begins in `pending`. The `resolve()` and `reject()` functions (called inside the executor) transition it to a *terminal* state. Once terminal, the state cannot change.

---

## 4.3 Async/Await Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start async function] --> T[Execute code up to first await]
    T --> U{Awaited promise settled?}
    U -- No --> V[Suspend function, return control to event loop]
    V --> W[Event loop processes other tasks]
    W --> U
    U -- Yes --> X[Resume function with resolved value]
    X --> Y{More code after await?}
    Y -- Yes --> T
    Y -- No --> Z[Return value as resolved promise]
    Z --> E[End]
```

**Reading the diagram**: An `async` function is *paused* at every `await` and *resumed* when the awaited promise settles. The function's caller receives a Promise that resolves with the function's return value.

---

## 4.4 Event Loop & Queue Priority Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EngineCore[JavaScript Engine]
        CS[Call Stack]
    end
    subgraph AsyncWorld[Asynchronous APIs]
        TIMER[setTimeout/setInterval]
        IO[fs.readFile / fetch]
        PROM[Promise.resolve / queueMicrotask]
    end
    subgraph Queues[Priority Queues]
        MQ[Microtask Queue - HIGH priority]
        TQ[Task Queue - LOWER priority]
    end
    EL[Event Loop Tick]

    TIMER --> TQ
    IO --> TQ
    PROM --> MQ

    CS --> EL
    MQ --> EL
    TQ --> EL
    EL --> CS
```

**Reading the diagram**: Microtasks (Promise callbacks) are drained completely before any macrotask is processed. This is why `Promise.then` fires before `setTimeout(..., 0)`.

---

## 4.5 Comparative Pattern Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CB[Callback Pattern]
        CB1[getUser id, fn1] --> CB2[fn1 user, fn2]
        CB2 --> CB3[fn2 orders, fn3]
        CB3 --> CB4[fn3 details, fn4]
    end
    subgraph PM[Promise Pattern]
        PM1[fetch user] --> PM2[.then fetch orders]
        PM2 --> PM3[.then fetch details]
        PM3 --> PM4[.then console.log]
        PM4 --> PM5[.catch handleError]
    end
    subgraph AA[Async Await Pattern]
        AA1[const user = await fetch user] --> AA2[const orders = await fetch orders]
        AA2 --> AA3[const details = await fetch details]
        AA3 --> AA4[console.log details]
    end

    style CB fill:#ffe0e0
    style PM fill:#e0f0ff
    style AA fill:#e0ffe0
```

**Reading the diagram**: Visual proof of the **pyramid of doom** in callbacks (nested to the right), the **flat chain** in promises, and the **linear synchronous style** of async/await.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a Promise in JavaScript. List and explain its three states.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Promise** is an object in JavaScript that represents the eventual completion (or failure) of an asynchronous operation. It acts as a placeholder for a value that may not yet be available.

The three states are:

1. **Pending**: The initial state — the async operation has not yet completed. Neither fulfilled nor rejected. **(1 mark)**
2. **Fulfilled**: The operation completed successfully. The promise has a resolved `value` that cannot change. **(1 mark)**
3. **Rejected**: The operation failed. The promise has a `reason` (error) that cannot change. **(1 mark)**

Once a promise transitions out of `pending`, it is said to be *settled* and its state is immutable.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between `async/await` and Promises. When would you prefer one over the other?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

`async/await` is **syntactic sugar built on top of Promises** — it does not replace them. **(1 mark)**

| Aspect | Promises | Async/Await |
|---|---|---|
| Syntax | Chainable `.then()/.catch()` | Linear, synchronous-style |
| Error Handling | `.catch()` at chain end | `try...catch` block |
| Readability | Good for parallel | Excellent for sequential |

**When to prefer**: Use **Async/Await** for sequential async workflows (cleaner code, easier debugging). Use **raw Promises with `Promise.all`** for parallel independent operations. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Callback Hell**. Rewrite the following nested callback code using **Promises** and then using **async/await**. Show the output flow clearly. **(7 marks)**

**Given Code (Callback Hell):**
```javascript
function processUser(id, callback) {
    setTimeout(() => {
        const user = { id: id, name: 'Alice' };
        setTimeout(() => {
            const orders = [{ id: 1, item: 'Book' }];
            setTimeout(() => {
                const total = 50;
                callback({ user, orders, total });
            }, 100);
        }, 100);
    }, 100);
}
```

**Model Solution:**

**Part 1 — Identifying Callback Hell (2 marks):**

The code suffers from **Callback Hell** (Pyramid of Doom). Issues:
- Deeply nested callbacks (3 levels).
- Indentation drifts to the right.
- Error handling must be repeated at each level.
- Inversion of control — each `setTimeout` controls *when* the next callback fires.

**Part 2 — Refactored to Promises (3 marks):**

```javascript
function processUserPromise(id) {
    return new Promise((resolve) => {
        setTimeout(() => resolve({ id: id, name: 'Alice' }), 100);
    })
    .then(user => new Promise(resolve => {
        setTimeout(() => resolve({ user, orders: [{ id: 1, item: 'Book' }] }), 100);
    }))
    .then(data => new Promise(resolve => {
        setTimeout(() => resolve({ ...data, total: 50 }), 100);
    }));
}
```

**Explanation**: Each `setTimeout` is wrapped in a new Promise. The `.then()` chain keeps the code flat. A single `.catch()` at the end would handle all errors. **(1 mark)**

**Part 3 — Refactored to Async/Await (2 marks):**

```javascript
function delay(ms) {
    return new Promise(resolve => setTimeout(resolve, ms));
}

async function processUserAsync(id) {
    await delay(100);
    const user = { id: id, name: 'Alice' };
    await delay(100);
    const orders = [{ id: 1, item: 'Book' }];
    await delay(100);
    const total = 50;
    return { user, orders, total };
}
```

**Explanation**: The helper `delay()` is a Promise wrapper for `setTimeout`. The `async` function reads like synchronous code. The `await delay(100)` lines make the intent clear. **(1 mark)**

---

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain the **JavaScript Event Loop**, the **Call Stack**, the **Microtask Queue**, and the **Task Queue**. Predict the output of the following code with proper justification. **(7 marks)**

```javascript
console.log('A');
setTimeout(() => console.log('B'), 0);
Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log('C'));
Promise.resolve().then(() => {
    console.log('D');
    setTimeout(() => console.log('E'), 0);
});
console.log('F');
```

**Model Solution:**

**Part 1 — Event Loop Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CS[Call Stack] --> EL[Event Loop Tick]
    EL --> CS
    MQ[Microtask Queue - Promise .then callbacks] --> EL
    TQ[Task Queue - setTimeout, setInterval, I/O] --> EL
```

**Explanation**:
- **Call Stack**: LIFO structure holding currently executing functions.
- **Microtask Queue**: Holds Promise callbacks. **Higher priority.**
- **Task Queue (Macrotask Queue)**: Holds `setTimeout`, I/O, UI events. **Lower priority.**
- **Event Loop**: After every macrotask, it *drains* the entire microtask queue before the next macrotask.

**Part 2 — Step-by-Step Execution Trace (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tick 1:} \quad & \text{`console.log('A')`} \rightarrow \text{prints A} \\
\text{Tick 2:} \quad & \text{`setTimeout(...)` schedules `log B` into Task Queue} \\
\text{Tick 3:} \quad & \text{`Promise.resolve().then(...)` schedules `log C` into Microtask Queue} \\
\text{Tick 4:} \quad & \text{`Promise.resolve().then(...)` schedules block into Microtask Queue} \\
\text{Tick 5:} \quad & \text{`console.log('F')`} \rightarrow \text{prints F} \\
\text{Tick 6:} \quad & \text{Call Stack empty. Drain Microtask Queue.} \\
\text{Tick 6a:} \quad & \text{Execute `log C`} \rightarrow \text{prints C} \\
\text{Tick 6b:} \quad & \text{Execute block: `log D`} \rightarrow \text{prints D; \texttt{setTimeout} schedules `log E` into Task Queue} \\
\text{Tick 7:} \quad & \text{Microtask Queue empty. Pop one macrotask: `log B`} \rightarrow \text{prints B} \\
\text{Tick 8:} \quad & \text{No more microtasks. Pop `log E`} \rightarrow \text{prints E}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Output**: `A, F, C, D, B, E` **(1 mark)**

**Part 3 — Justification (1 mark):**

The output order is `A, F, C, D, B, E`. Sync code runs first (`A`, `F`). The microtask queue is drained before any macrotask, so `C` and `D` (both Promise callbacks) fire before `B`. The nested `setTimeout` in `D`'s callback (`E`) is a new macrotask, so it runs last.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper] (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the **Promise Chaining** mechanism in JavaScript. Write a complete program that fetches a list of users from an API, then for the first user fetches their posts, then for the first post fetches the comments — all using **promise chaining with `.then()`**. Handle errors using a single `.catch()`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part 1 — Promise Chaining Theory (2 marks):**

Promise chaining is the pattern of calling `.then()` (or `.catch()`) on a promise, where each handler **returns a new value or a new promise**. The new value is wrapped in a promise that is then passed to the next `.then()`. This allows multiple async operations to be sequenced **without nesting**.

Key rules:
- Each `.then()` returns a *new* promise.
- If a handler returns a value, the next `.then()` receives that value.
- If a handler returns a promise, the next `.then()` waits for it.
- If a handler throws or returns a rejected promise, control jumps to the nearest `.catch()`.

**Part 2 — Complete Program (4 marks):**

```javascript
function fetchSequential() {
    // Step 1: Fetch all users
    return fetch('https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users')
        // Step 2: Parse JSON response
        .then(response => {
            if (!response.ok) throw new Error('Users fetch failed');
            return response.json();
        })
        // Step 3: Take the first user, fetch their posts
        .then(users => {
            if (users.length === 0) throw new Error('No users found');
            return fetch(`https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts?userId=${users[0].id}`)
                .then(res => res.json())
                .then(posts => ({ user: users[0], posts }));
        })
        // Step 4: Take the first post, fetch its comments
        .then(({ user, posts }) => {
            if (posts.length === 0) throw new Error('No posts found');
            return fetch(`https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/comments?postId=${posts[0].id}`)
                .then(res => res.json())
                .then(comments => ({ user, post: posts[0], comments }));
        })
        // Step 5: Log the final nested data
        .then(({ user, post, comments }) => {
            console.log('User:', user.name);
            console.log('Post:', post.title);
            console.log('Comments:', comments.length);
            return { user, post, comments };
        })
        // Step 6: Single catch for the entire chain
        .catch(error => {
            console.error('Pipeline failed at some step:', error.message);
            return null; // graceful fallback
        });
}

fetchSequential();
```

**Explanation**:
- The chain has **5 sequential `.then()` handlers** + 1 `.catch()`.
- Errors from any step (network failure, empty data, JSON parse error) all propagate to the single `.catch()`. **(1 mark)**
- The `throw new Error(...)` statements *convert* a normal flow into a rejected promise, so the `.catch()` can handle them uniformly. **(1 mark)**

---

**(b)** Refactor the above program using **`async/await`**. Compare both approaches in terms of **readability, error handling, and debuggability**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part 1 — Async/Await Refactor (3 marks):**

```javascript
async function fetchSequentialAsync() {
    try {
        // Step 1: Fetch users
        const usersRes = await fetch('https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users');
        if (!usersRes.ok) throw new Error('Users fetch failed');
        const users = await usersRes.json();
        if (users.length === 0) throw new Error('No users found');

        // Step 2: Fetch first user's posts
        const postsRes = await fetch(`https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts?userId=${users[0].id}`);
        const posts = await postsRes.json();
        if (posts.length === 0) throw new Error('No posts found');

        // Step 3: Fetch first post's comments
        const commentsRes = await fetch(`https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/comments?postId=${posts[0].id}`);
        const comments = await commentsRes.json();

        // Step 4: Log the result
        console.log('User:', users[0].name);
        console.log('Post:', posts[0].title);
        console.log('Comments:', comments.length);
        return { user: users[0], post: posts[0], comments };
    } catch (error) {
        console.error('Pipeline failed at some step:', error.message);
        return null;
    }
}

fetchSequentialAsync();
```

**Explanation**: The code reads **top-to-bottom** like a synchronous recipe. The `try...catch` block catches *any* error (network, parse, logical). Each `await` pauses only this function — the event loop is free to handle other work.

**Part 2 — Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Promise Chain | Async/Await |
|---|---|---|
| **Readability** | Good, but `return fetch(...).then(...)` patterns clutter intent. | Excellent — looks like synchronous code. |
| **Error Handling** | Single `.catch()` at end; throw to skip. | `try...catch` block — familiar from sync JS. |
| **Debugging** | Stack traces can be obscure; stepping through `.then` is awkward. | Native debugger support — step through line-by-line. |
| **Conditional Logic** | Hard to insert `if/else` mid-chain without nested `.then`. | Natural — use `if/else` directly. |
| **Composability** | Easy to compose via `Promise.all`/`Promise.race`. | Same — can still use `Promise.all` inside async. |
| **Cognitive Load** | Higher — must mentally flatten the chain. | Lower — code is linear. |
| **Nesting** | Can re-nest if not careful (return-in-then pattern). | Almost impossible to re-nest. |

**Verdict**: For **sequential** workflows, async/await is the modern preferred style. For **parallel** workflows (`Promise.all`), both work, but the `await Promise.all([...])` pattern is cleanest.

**Part 3 — Conclusion (1 mark):**

Async/await is **syntactic sugar over Promises** — they are not mutually exclusive. Modern JavaScript code uses async/await for sequential logic and raw `Promise.all`/`Promise.race` for parallel composition. Understanding both is essential for KTU's outcome-based assessment on asynchronous programming.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing `await` with blocking**: Many students write `await` thinking it "stops the page" — it does not. It only pauses the *current async function*. The main thread and event loop continue. **(-2 marks)**
> 2. **Forgetting `try...catch` in async functions**: Unhandled rejections become `unhandled promise rejection` warnings in the console and may crash Node.js processes. **(-1 mark)**
> 3. **Mixing callback and promise styles**: Students sometimes pass a callback to a function that returns a promise, or vice versa. KTU examiners specifically test this confusion. **(-2 marks)**
> 4. **Event Loop output questions**: Students often answer `1, 2, 3, 4` instead of the correct `1, 4, 3, 2` (or similar) because they forget microtask priority. **(-3 marks)**
> 5. **Forgetting that `async` functions always return a Promise**: Even if you `return 42` from an async function, the caller gets a `Promise<42>`, not the number. **(-1 mark)**
> 6. **Promise state mutability**: Claiming a promise can be "reset" or "un-rejected" — once settled, a promise is **frozen forever**. **(-1 mark)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

### Core Concepts
- **JavaScript is single-threaded** with one call stack; async patterns exist to avoid blocking it.
- The **Event Loop** continuously checks: is the call stack empty? If yes, drain microtasks, then take one macrotask.
- **Microtask Queue** (Promise callbacks, `queueMicrotask`) has **higher priority** than the **Task Queue** (`setTimeout`, I/O).

### Callbacks
- A function passed as an argument to be invoked later.
- **Synchronous callbacks** run immediately (e.g., `Array.map`).
- **Asynchronous callbacks** run after an event (e.g., `setTimeout`).
- Suffer from **Callback Hell** (Pyramid of Doom) and **Inversion of Control**.

### Promises
- Object representing the **eventual** result of an async operation.
- Three states: **pending → fulfilled** (via `resolve`) or **pending → rejected** (via `reject`).
- Once settled, **immutable**.
- `.then()` returns a *new* promise, enabling chaining.
- `.catch()` handles any error in the chain.
- Static methods: `Promise.all` (all-or-nothing), `Promise.race` (first-to-settle), `Promise.allSettled` (wait for all, never reject), `Promise.any` (first-to-fulfill).

### Async/Await
- ES2017 syntactic sugar over Promises — **not a replacement**.
- `async function` implicitly returns a Promise.
- `await` pauses **only the current async function**; the main thread is unblocked.
- Errors handled with **traditional `try...catch`** blocks.
- `await` is valid only inside `async` functions (except in ES2022 top-level await in modules).

### The Three Patterns Side-by-Side
- **Callbacks** — original, error-prone, but still in legacy APIs (`fs`, DOM events).
- **Promises** — structured, chainable, composable via static methods.
- **Async/Await** — readable, debuggable, idiomatic modern JavaScript.

### Practical Engineering Wisdom
- For **parallel** independent fetches, use `await Promise.all([...])`.
- For **sequential** dependent fetches, use `await` in sequence.
- For **cancellation**, use `AbortController` with `fetch`.
- For **legacy callback APIs**, wrap them in `new Promise((resolve, reject) => api(...args, (err, result) => err ? reject(err) : resolve(result)))` — this is called **promisification**.

### Common KTU Question Triggers
- "Explain Callback Hell and how Promises solve it." → 7 marks.
- "Differentiate between Promises and async/await with examples." → 7 marks.
- "Predict the output of the following code involving `setTimeout` and `Promise.then`." → 7 marks (event loop).
- "Write a program using `async/await` to fetch data from multiple APIs." → 14 marks.
- "What are the three states of a Promise? Can a Promise change state after being fulfilled?" → 3 marks.

### The One-Sentence Summary
> *Callbacks, Promises, and Async/Await are three evolutionary stages of handling asynchronous operations in single-threaded JavaScript — from nested function pointers, to structured stateful objects, to readable synchronous-style code that is built on top of those same objects.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AJAX: Concepts, Working, and Data Formats (XML, JSON)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** AJAX: Concepts, Working, and Data Formats (XML, JSON)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**AJAX (Asynchronous JavaScript and XML)** is a set of interrelated web development techniques used on the **client-side** to create **asynchronous** web applications. With AJAX, web applications can send and retrieve data from a server **asynchronously** (in the background) without interfering with the display and behavior of the existing page.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** AJAX stands for **Asynchronous JavaScript and XML**. It is not a single technology but a combination of technologies — *HTML/XHTML and CSS* for presentation, the *Document Object Model (DOM)* for dynamic display, *XML/JSON* for data interchange, *XMLHttpRequest (XHR)* or the *Fetch API* for asynchronous data retrieval, and *JavaScript* to bind everything together.

The acronym AJAX was coined by **Jesse James Garrett** in his 2005 essay *"Ajax: A New Approach to Web Applications"*, where he described the technique of loading data in the background to avoid full page reloads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Highlight:** Note that modern AJAX implementations frequently use **JSON** instead of XML, because JSON is **lighter, faster, and natively parsed by JavaScript**. However, the name "AJAX" is retained for historical reasons.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting in a **restaurant**:

- **Traditional Web Model (Synchronous / Full Page Reload):** You order food. The waiter takes your order to the kitchen and **stands there waiting** until the dish is ready. During this time, **you cannot order anything else, talk to your friends, or even drink water**. The whole restaurant (your browser tab) is "frozen" until the waiter returns with the food. This is exactly what happens during a full HTTP page request — the browser locks up and shows a blank/loading state.

- **AJAX Model (Asynchronous / Background Data Loading):** You place your order. The waiter notes it down and goes to the kitchen, but **immediately returns to your table** so you can continue ordering drinks, asking for the menu, or chatting with friends. When the food is ready, the waiter **brings it to you** while you are still doing other things. Your dining experience (the page) is never interrupted. This is the essence of AJAX — the browser continues to be interactive while data is being fetched in the background.

## 1.3 Why AJAX is Important — KTU Board Perspective

| Aspect | Traditional Web App | AJAX-Based Web App |
|---|---|---|
| Page Reload | Full page reload on every request | **No full reload** — only data is fetched |
| User Experience | Slow, choppy, and disruptive | Smooth, fast, and desktop-like |
| Server Load | Heavy (reloads entire HTML/CSS/images) | Light (only data payload is transferred) |
| Network Usage | High bandwidth consumption | **Low bandwidth** (small data payloads) |
| State Management | Stateless, form-driven | Stateful, partial updates |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Metric to Remember:** A typical AJAX call reduces transferred data by approximately **60–80%** compared to a full page reload, because only the *delta* (changed data) is exchanged, not the entire HTML document, CSS, and static assets.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Technologies That Make Up AJAX

AJAX is **not a single technology** — it is an **architectural pattern** that combines five existing technologies. Understanding each layer is critical for KTU exam answers.

### Layer 1: Presentation — HTML/XHTML + CSS
- Provides the **structure and styling** of the page.
- Defines what the user sees and interacts with.

### Layer 2: Dynamic Display — DOM (Document Object Model)
- A tree-structured representation of the page.
- JavaScript uses the DOM to **dynamically update parts of the page** without reloading the whole page.
- Example: `document.getElementById("result").innerHTML = newData;`

### Layer 3: Data Interchange — XML or JSON
- **XML (eXtensible Markup Language):** Structured, tag-based data format (heavyweight, verbose).
- **JSON (JavaScript Object Notation):** Lightweight, key-value data format (modern standard).
- The server returns data in one of these formats, which JavaScript parses.

### Layer 4: Asynchronous Data Retrieval — XMLHttpRequest (XHR) / Fetch API
- **XMLHttpRequest (XHR):** The traditional JavaScript object used to send HTTP requests to the server asynchronously.
- **Fetch API:** The modern, promise-based replacement for XHR, introduced in ES6.
- Both work *behind the scenes* — the user does not see a new page being loaded.

### Layer 5: Binding Logic — JavaScript
- The "**glue**" that ties everything together.
- Listens to user events, fires HTTP requests, parses responses, and updates the DOM.

## 2.2 How AJAX Works — Step-by-Step Operational Flow

Let us break down the **complete lifecycle** of an AJAX request:

1. **User Event Occurs:** The user performs an action in the browser — clicking a button, typing in a search box, selecting a dropdown, or the page finishes loading (`onload`).
2. **JavaScript Event Handler Triggers:** An event listener (e.g., `onclick`, `onkeyup`) invokes a JavaScript function.
3. **XMLHttpRequest / Fetch Object is Created:** JavaScript instantiates an XHR object: `var xhttp = new XMLHttpRequest();`
4. **Request is Configured:** The request method (`GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`), the target URL, and async flag (`true` for asynchronous) are set: `xhttp.open("GET", "server/data.json", true);`
5. **Request is Sent:** `xhttp.send();` is invoked. The browser sends the HTTP request *in the background*.
6. **Server Processes the Request:** The web server (e.g., Node.js, PHP, Python, Java) receives the request, queries the database, and prepares a response in XML or JSON.
7. **Server Sends Response Back:** The response data travels over the network as an HTTP response packet.
8. **Browser Receives Response:** The XHR object's `readyState` changes and the `onreadystatechange` callback fires when `readyState == 4` and `status == 200`.
9. **JavaScript Parses the Response:** The raw response string is parsed using `responseXML` (for XML) or `JSON.parse(responseText)` (for JSON).
10. **DOM is Updated:** JavaScript uses the parsed data to modify specific elements of the page via the DOM, providing **seamless, partial updates** to the user.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The keyword **"Asynchronous"** means the browser does **NOT wait** for the server's response. It continues executing other JavaScript code and rendering the page. When the response arrives, a **callback function** handles it. This is the core philosophical difference from the traditional synchronous model.

## 2.3 Synchronous vs Asynchronous — The Critical Distinction

| Property | Synchronous Request | Asynchronous Request (AJAX) |
|---|---|---|
| Browser Behavior | **Frozen** until response arrives | Continues normal operation |
| `xhttp.open()` 3rd Argument | `false` | `true` |
| User Experience | Poor (button freezes, page hangs) | Excellent (seamless) |
| Default in AJAX | — | **Always `true` (asynchronous)** |
| Use Cases | Form submissions, file downloads | Search suggestions, live feeds, chat |

## 2.4 The XMLHttpRequest Object — Lifecycle States (readyState)

The XHR object exposes a property called `readyState` that tracks the status of the request. This is **extremely high-yield** for KTU exams.

| readyState Value | Status Name | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **0** | UNSENT | The `open()` method has not been called yet. |
| **1** | OPENED | The `open()` method has been called, but `send()` has not. |
| **2** | HEADERS_RECEIVED | The `send()` method has been called, and headers + status are available. |
| **3** | LOADING | The response body is being downloaded; `responseText` contains partial data. |
| **4** | DONE | The entire operation is **complete** — the response is fully received. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** The KTU paper commonly asks: *"Which readyState value indicates the completion of an AJAX request?"* The correct answer is **readyState == 4 (DONE)**.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Key Value / Property | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| AJAX Full Form | **Asynchronous JavaScript and XML** | Coined by Jesse James Garrett (2005) |
| Core XHR Methods | `open()`, `send()`, `abort()` | `open()` configures, `send()` dispatches |
| Core XHR Properties | `readyState`, `status`, `responseText`, `responseXML` | `responseText` for JSON, `responseXML` for XML |
| Completion Condition | `readyState == 4` **AND** `status == 200` | Both conditions must be true |
| Async Flag (3rd arg of `open`) | `true` (asynchronous) / `false` (synchronous) | AJAX always uses `true` |
| HTTP Status 200 | **OK** — request successful | Most common success code |
| HTTP Status 404 | **Not Found** — resource missing | Client-side error |
| HTTP Status 500 | **Internal Server Error** | Server-side error |
| JSON MIME Type | `application/json` | Required in server response header |
| XML MIME Type | `application/xml` or `text/xml` | Required in server response header |
| Data Format Lightweight Winner | **JSON** | ~30–40% smaller than equivalent XML |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The XML Data Format — Structure, Rules, and Example

**XML (eXtensible Markup Language)** is a markup language that defines a set of rules for encoding documents in a format that is both **human-readable** and **machine-readable**. It was the original data interchange format used by AJAX applications.

### XML Structural Rules

1. Every XML document **must have a single root element** that contains all other elements.
2. Every opening tag `<tag>` **must have a corresponding closing tag** `</tag>`.
3. XML tags are **case-sensitive** (`<Name>` and `<name>` are different).
4. XML attributes must always be **quoted**: `<book id="1">`.
5. XML must be **well-formed** — no overlapping or improperly nested tags.

### Worked Example — XML Document Representing a Bookstore

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<bookstore>
    <book category="fiction">
        <title lang="en">The Alchemist</title>
        <author>Paulo Coelho</author>
        <year>1988</year>
        <price>299.99</price>
    </book>
    <book category="non-fiction">
        <title lang="en">Clean Code</title>
        <author>Robert C. Martin</author>
        <year>2008</year>
        <price>549.00</price>
    </book>
</bookstore>
```

**Step-by-step parsing logic in JavaScript:**

Let us trace how a browser parses this XML and extracts a value.

Step 1: The server returns the above XML string with `Content-Type: application/xml`.
Step 2: In the XHR callback, the response is accessed via `xhttp.responseXML`, which is automatically parsed into a DOM tree.
Step 3: To extract the title of the first book, JavaScript navigates the DOM:

$$
\text{title}_{1} = \texttt{xhttp.responseXML.getElementsByTagName("title")[0].childNodes[0].nodeValue}
$$

Step 4: Result: `title₁ = "The Alchemist"`.

### Drawbacks of XML for AJAX

- **Verbose:** Every piece of data is wrapped in opening AND closing tags, roughly doubling the payload size.
- **Parsing Overhead:** Requires a full XML parser (DOMParser), which is slower than JSON's native `JSON.parse()`.
- **No Native JavaScript Support:** JavaScript must convert XML DOM nodes into JS objects manually.
- **Schema Dependency:** Often requires an XSD/DTD for validation, adding complexity.

## 3.2 The JSON Data Format — Structure, Rules, and Example

**JSON (JavaScript Object Notation)** is a **lightweight, text-based, language-independent** data interchange format. It is derived from JavaScript object literal syntax, but it is now the **de facto standard** for AJAX data exchange.

### JSON Structural Rules

1. Data is written as **name/value pairs**: `"name": "value"`.
2. Name must be a **double-quoted string**.
3. Values can be: a string, number, boolean (`true`/`false`), array, object, or `null`.
4. Objects are enclosed in curly braces `{ }` and arrays in square brackets `[ ]`.
5. Members are separated by **commas**, and a comma is **NEVER** placed after the last member.
6. JSON must be **well-formed** — a single syntax error invalidates the entire document.

### Worked Example — JSON Document Representing the Same Bookstore

```json
{
    "bookstore": {
        "books": [
            {
                "category": "fiction",
                "title": "The Alchemist",
                "author": "Paulo Coelho",
                "year": 1988,
                "price": 299.99
            },
            {
                "category": "non-fiction",
                "title": "Clean Code",
                "author": "Robert C. Martin",
                "year": 2008,
                "price": 549.00
            }
        ]
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step parsing logic in JavaScript:**

Step 1: The server returns the above JSON string with `Content-Type: application/json`.
Step 2: In the XHR callback, the raw string is accessed via `xhttp.responseText`.
Step 3: The string is converted to a native JavaScript object using `JSON.parse()`:

$$
\texttt{const data = JSON.parse(xhttp.responseText);}
$$

Step 4: To extract the first book's title:

$$
\text{title}_{1} = \texttt{data.bookstore.books[0].title}
$$

Step 5: Result: `title₁ = "The Alchemist"`.

### Why JSON Dominates Modern AJAX

- **Native JavaScript Support:** `JSON.parse()` and `JSON.stringify()` are built into every modern browser.
- **Compact:** No closing tags; data structure is minimal.
- **Faster Parsing:** Tokenization-based parsing is significantly faster than XML DOM tree construction.
- **Direct Object Mapping:** JSON maps directly to JavaScript objects — no conversion needed.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — XML vs JSON Head-to-Head Comparison

| Comparison Axis | XML | JSON |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | eXtensible Markup Language | JavaScript Object Notation |
| **Syntax Style** | Tag-based (`<tag>value</tag>`) | Key-value pair (`"key": value`) |
| **Verbosity** | **High** — closing tags duplicate payload | **Low** — minimal syntax overhead |
| **Data Types** | All data is text (must parse manually) | Native: string, number, boolean, array, null |
| **Parsing in JS** | Manual DOM traversal | `JSON.parse()` (one line) |
| **Schema Support** | XSD, DTD (strict validation) | JSON Schema (optional, less strict) |
| **Comments Allowed** | Yes (`<!-- comment -->`) | **No** — JSON forbids comments |
| **Arrays Support** | No native arrays (workaround needed) | **Native** first-class support |
| **Namespace Support** | Yes (XML namespaces) | No namespace concept |
| **MIME Type** | `application/xml`, `text/xml` | `application/json` |
| **File Extension** | `.xml` | `.json` |
| **Readability** | Moderate | **High** |
| **Speed** | Slower (DOM construction) | **Faster** (direct tokenization) |
| **Best Suited For** | Document-style data, SOAP, RSS | API responses, configuration, mobile apps |
| **Modern AJAX Usage** | Declining | **Dominant** (REST APIs standard) |

## 3.4 Complete AJAX Request — Annotated Trace

Below is a fully annotated walkthrough of a typical AJAX call, mapping every line to its conceptual purpose.

```javascript
// STEP 1: Create the XHR object
var xhttp = new XMLHttpRequest();

// STEP 2: Bind the callback event listener
// This function fires every time readyState changes
xhttp.onreadystatechange = function() {
    // STEP 3: Wait until the request is fully complete (readyState == 4)
    //          AND the server returned HTTP 200 (OK)
    if (this.readyState == 4 && this.status == 200) {
        // STEP 4: Parse the JSON response into a JavaScript object
        var data = JSON.parse(this.responseText);
        
        // STEP 5: Update the DOM with the received data
        document.getElementById("output").innerHTML = data.message;
    }
};

// STEP 6: Configure the request
// open(method, url, async) — async is TRUE for AJAX
xhttp.open("GET", "https://api.example.com/data", true);

// STEP 7: Send the request to the server
xhttp.send();
```

**Trace of the `readyState` value during execution:**

$$
\text{readyState}(t) = \begin{cases} 0 & t < t_{\text{open}} \\ 1 & t_{\text{open}} \leq t < t_{\text{send}} \\ 2 & t_{\text{send}} \leq t < t_{\text{headers}} \\ 3 & t_{\text{headers}} \leq t < t_{\text{body}} \\ 4 & t \geq t_{\text{body}} \end{cases}
$$

Where $t_{\text{open}}$, $t_{\text{send}}$, $t_{\text{headers}}$, and $t_{\text{body}}$ are the moments the `open()`, `send()`, response headers, and response body complete, respectively.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Traditional Web Model vs AJAX Model

The diagram below contrasts the **synchronous (traditional)** request flow with the **asynchronous (AJAX)** request flow. This is a high-yield visual for KTU answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[User clicks link or submits form] --> B1[Browser sends full HTTP request]
    B1 --> C1[Server processes and generates new HTML page]
    C1 --> D1[Server sends ENTIRE new page back]
    D1 --> E1[Browser RE-RENDERS the whole page]
    E1 --> F1[User sees updated page]
    
    A2[User clicks button or types in box] --> B2[JavaScript event handler fires]
    B2 --> C2[XHR object sends request in BACKGROUND]
    C2 --> D2[Server processes and returns data ONLY]
    D2 --> E2[JavaScript parses XML or JSON response]
    E2 --> F2[DOM updated partially]
    F2 --> G2[User sees updated section]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Detailed AJAX Internals (XMLHttpRequest Lifecycle)

This diagram maps the **internal state machine** of the XHR object. Note that the node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to avoid Mermaid reserved keyword conflicts.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> state0: xhttp = new XMLHttpRequest
    state0: state0_readyState_0_UNSENT
    state0 --> state1: xhttp.open called
    state1: state1_readyState_1_OPENED
    state1 --> state2: xhttp.send called
    state2: state2_readyState_2_HEADERS_RECEIVED
    state2 --> state3: Downloading response body
    state3: state3_readyState_3_LOADING
    state3 --> state4: Response fully received
    state4: state4_readyState_4_DONE
    state4 --> [*]: Callback executes, DOM updated
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of an AJAX System

This block diagram shows the **five technological layers** of AJAX and how they interact. The labels are kept clean (no markdown formatting inside double quotes) to satisfy Mermaid rendering rules.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph presentationLayer["LAYER 1 PRESENTATION"]
        htmlNode["HTML and CSS page structure and styling"]
    end
    
    subgraph domLayer["LAYER 2 DYNAMIC DISPLAY"]
        domNode["DOM tree for partial page updates"]
    end
    
    subgraph bindingLayer["LAYER 3 BINDING LOGIC"]
        jsNode["JavaScript event handlers and callbacks"]
    end
    
    subgraph dataLayer["LAYER 4 DATA INTERCHANGE"]
        xmlNode["XML format server response"]
        jsonNode["JSON format server response"]
    end
    
    subgraph transportLayer["LAYER 5 TRANSPORT"]
        xhrNode["XMLHttpRequest object or Fetch API"]
    end
    
    userNode["USER BROWSER"] --> presentationLayer
    presentationLayer --> domLayer
    domLayer --> bindingLayer
    bindingLayer --> transportLayer
    transportLayer --> serverNode["REMOTE SERVER database or API"]
    serverNode --> transportLayer
    transportLayer --> dataLayer
    dataLayer --> bindingLayer
    bindingLayer --> domLayer
    domLayer --> presentationLayer
    presentationLayer --> userNode
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — XML vs JSON Payload Visualization

This diagram visually compares how the **same logical data** is represented in XML and JSON, making the verbosity difference immediately obvious.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph xmlRep["XML REPRESENTATION verbose"]
        xmlBlock["bookstore book title The Alchemist title author Paulo Coelho author year 1988 year price 299 price book bookstore"]
    end
    
    subgraph jsonRep["JSON REPRESENTATION compact"]
        jsonBlock["title The Alchemist author Paulo Coelho year 1988 price 299"]
    end
    
    sameData["Same Logical Data 1 book record"] --> xmlRep
    sameData --> jsonRep
    
    xmlRep -.->|"Approx 1.6x larger payload"| xmlMetric["XML heavier"]
    jsonRep -.->|"Lighter and faster to parse"| jsonMetric["JSON lighter"]
```
