<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Define AJAX. List any four technologies used in an AJAX-based application.**

**Model Answer:**

**AJAX (Asynchronous JavaScript and XML)** is a set of web development techniques that allow client-side applications to communicate with the server asynchronously, without requiring a full page reload. It enables partial updates of the web page, resulting in faster, more interactive user experiences.

The four core technologies used in an AJAX-based application are:

1. **HTML/XHTML and CSS** — for presentation and styling of the page.
2. **DOM (Document Object Model)** — for dynamic, partial page updates.
3. **XML or JSON** — for data interchange between the client and server.
4. **XMLHttpRequest (XHR) object or Fetch API** — for asynchronous data retrieval from the server.
5. **JavaScript** — to bind all the above technologies together through event handlers and callback functions.

*(Any four of the above should be written for full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between XML and JSON data formats used in AJAX applications.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | XML | JSON |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | eXtensible Markup Language | JavaScript Object Notation |
| **Syntax** | Tag-based, verbose | Key-value pairs, compact |
| **Parsing in JavaScript** | Manual DOM traversal | `JSON.parse()` built-in |
| **Data Types** | All values are text | Native: string, number, boolean, array, null |
| **Array Support** | No native arrays | Native first-class array support |
| **Comments** | Allowed (`<!-- -->`) | Not allowed |
| **MIME Type** | `application/xml` | `application/json` |
| **Payload Size** | Larger | Smaller (~30–40% less) |
| **Modern Usage** | Declining in AJAX | Dominant in REST APIs |

XML is document-oriented and heavier, while JSON is data-oriented and lighter, making JSON the preferred format for modern AJAX and REST API responses.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse the `readyState` values.** Many students write `readyState == 200` instead of `status == 200`. Remember: **readyState** goes from 0 to 4, and **status** is the HTTP code (200, 404, 500).
> - **Do NOT say "AJAX uses XML only."** AJAX can use BOTH XML and JSON. The original name is historical; modern AJAX predominantly uses JSON.
> - **Do NOT skip the asynchronous nature.** If your answer does not emphasize the word "**asynchronous**", you will lose at least 2 marks in the definition.
> - **Always draw a diagram** for working-of-AJAX questions. A neat flowchart with 5–7 boxes earns 1–2 extra marks in the KTU board valuation.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Module 2, 2024 Scheme Pattern]

**(a) Explain the working of AJAX with a neat diagram. Describe the role of the XMLHttpRequest object. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction:**
AJAX enables asynchronous communication between the client and the server. The working of AJAX involves a series of steps coordinated by JavaScript and the XMLHttpRequest (XHR) object.

**Working of AJAX — Step-by-Step Explanation:**

1. **User Action:** The user interacts with the web page (e.g., clicks a button, types in a search box). This triggers a JavaScript event.
2. **JavaScript Handler:** The event handler creates an `XMLHttpRequest` object:
   `var xhttp = new XMLHttpRequest();`
3. **Request Configuration:** The `open()` method is called to specify the HTTP method (GET/POST), the target URL, and the asynchronous flag:
   `xhttp.open("GET", "server.php", true);`
4. **Callback Registration:** The `onreadystatechange` event handler is assigned a function that will be invoked when the server's response state changes.
5. **Request Dispatch:** The `send()` method is invoked, sending the request to the server *in the background*. The browser does **not** freeze.
6. **Server Processing:** The server receives the request, queries the database, and constructs a response in XML or JSON.
7. **Response Reception:** The XHR object's `readyState` changes progressively from 0 to 4. When `readyState == 4` and `status == 200`, the response is fully received.
8. **Response Parsing:** JavaScript parses the response using `responseXML` (for XML) or `JSON.parse(responseText)` (for JSON).
9. **DOM Update:** JavaScript modifies the relevant DOM elements to display the new data. The user sees a partial, seamless page update.

**Role of the XMLHttpRequest Object:**

The XHR object is the **heart of AJAX**. It performs the following roles:

- **Creates** an HTTP request transparently in the background.
- **Tracks** the state of the request via the `readyState` property (0 → 4).
- **Holds** the server's response in `responseText` (raw string) or `responseXML` (DOM tree).
- **Exposes** the HTTP status code via the `status` property.
- **Supports** all HTTP methods: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE.
- **Enables** asynchronous behavior, keeping the UI responsive.

**Neat Diagram:**

```
[User Event] -> [JS Handler Creates XHR] -> [xhttp.open method, URL, async]
   -> [xhttp.send] -> [Server Processes Request] -> [Server Returns XML/JSON]
   -> [xhttp.onreadystatechange fires] -> [JS Parses response]
   -> [DOM Updated] -> [User Sees Partial Update]
```

**(b) Compare XML and JSON as data interchange formats in AJAX. Provide a sample of each representing the same data. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*(See the detailed comparison table in Section 3.3 of these notes. Key points to write:)*

**XML Sample:**

```xml
<bookstore>
    <book>
        <title>The Alchemist</title>
        <author>Paulo Coelho</author>
        <year>1988</year>
        <price>299.99</price>
    </book>
</bookstore>
```

**JSON Sample:**

```json
{
    "bookstore": {
        "book": {
            "title": "The Alchemist",
            "author": "Paulo Coelho",
            "year": 1988,
            "price": 299.99
        }
    }
}
```

**Comparison Points (write at least 5):**

1. **Verbosity:** XML uses closing tags, making payloads larger. JSON is more compact.
2. **Data Types:** XML treats all values as text. JSON supports native data types (number, boolean, null).
3. **Parsing:** XML requires DOM traversal. JSON uses `JSON.parse()` — single-line conversion.
4. **Comments:** XML allows comments. JSON does not.
5. **Array Support:** XML has no native array support. JSON has first-class array support.
6. **Speed:** JSON is faster to parse and transmit. XML is slower due to DOM construction.
7. **Modern Usage:** JSON is the de facto standard for REST APIs. XML is reserved for legacy SOAP, RSS, and document-style data.

**Conclusion:** For modern AJAX applications, **JSON is preferred** due to its compactness, native JavaScript support, and faster parsing speed.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Module 2, 2024 Scheme Pattern]

**(a) Explain the different readyState values of the XMLHttpRequest object. Write a JavaScript function to perform an AJAX GET request and display the response in a `<div>` element. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**The `readyState` Property:**

The `readyState` property of the XHR object holds the status of the request. It can have **5 values** (0 through 4).

| Value | Status Name | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **0** | UNSENT | The XHR object has been created, but `open()` has not been called. |
| **1** | OPENED | `open()` has been called, but `send()` has not. |
| **2** | HEADERS_RECEIVED | `send()` has been called; response headers and status are available. |
| **3** | LOADING | The response body is being downloaded; partial data is in `responseText`. |
| **4** | DONE | The entire operation is complete; the full response is available. |

**JavaScript Function for AJAX GET Request:**

```javascript
function loadData() {
    // Step 1: Create the XHR object
    var xhttp = new XMLHttpRequest();
    
    // Step 2: Define the callback for state changes
    xhttp.onreadystatechange = function() {
        // Step 3: Check if request is complete and successful
        if (this.readyState == 4 && this.status == 200) {
            // Step 4: Parse JSON and update the DOM
            var data = JSON.parse(this.responseText);
            document.getElementById("result").innerHTML = data.message;
        }
    };
    
    // Step 5: Configure and send the request (async = true)
    xhttp.open("GET", "https://api.example.com/greeting", true);
    xhttp.send();
}
```

**Explanation of each step is as given in Section 3.4 above.** The function `loadData()` can be triggered by any user event, e.g., `<button onclick="loadData()">Load</button>`.

---

**(b) What are the advantages and disadvantages of using AJAX in web applications? Discuss JSON as the preferred data format for modern AJAX. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages of AJAX:**

1. **Improved User Experience:** Pages update instantly without full reloads, making applications feel faster and more responsive.
2. **Reduced Bandwidth:** Only data is exchanged, not the entire HTML/CSS/images. Bandwidth consumption drops by 60–80%.
3. **Asynchronous Operation:** The browser remains interactive while the server processes the request.
4. **Partial Page Updates:** Specific sections of the page can be refreshed independently.
5. **Better State Management:** State can be maintained on the client-side without full page refreshes.
6. **Improved Performance:** Reduces server load by avoiding full-page generation on the server.

**Disadvantages of AJAX:**

1. **JavaScript Dependency:** AJAX requires JavaScript; if disabled, the application breaks.
2. **SEO Challenges:** Search engines may not index dynamically loaded content (mitigated by SSR or pre-rendering).
3. **Browser Back/Forward Button Issues:** Dynamic updates do not register in the browser history by default.
4. **Security Concerns:** Exposes the application's API endpoints to potential attacks; requires proper authentication, CSRF tokens, and input validation.
5. **Debugging Complexity:** Asynchronous flows are harder to trace and debug than synchronous ones.
6. **Increased Client-Side Logic:** More JavaScript complexity is pushed to the client, which can be harder to maintain.

**Why JSON is the Preferred Data Format for Modern AJAX:**

1. **Lightweight:** JSON payloads are significantly smaller than equivalent XML, conserving bandwidth.
2. **Native JavaScript Compatibility:** JSON is a subset of JavaScript object literal syntax. `JSON.parse()` converts it directly into a JavaScript object — no extra parsing library needed.
3. **Faster Parsing:** JSON's tokenization-based parsing is faster than XML's DOM construction.
4. **Native Data Types:** JSON supports strings, numbers, booleans, arrays, and `null` natively, whereas XML treats everything as text.
5. **Industry Standard:** REST APIs, GraphQL, and nearly all modern web services return JSON.
6. **Easier to Read:** JSON's syntax closely resembles a Python dictionary or JavaScript object, making it intuitive for developers.

**Conclusion:** The combination of AJAX + JSON + Fetch API is the **de facto architecture** of modern web applications such as Gmail, Google Maps, Twitter, and Facebook. The KTU 2024 syllabus correctly emphasizes JSON as the contemporary data interchange format, reflecting real-world industry practice.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2, AJAX Topic**

- **AJAX = Asynchronous JavaScript and XML.** Coined by Jesse James Garrett in **2005**. It is a *technique*, not a *language*.
- **Five Core Technologies:** HTML/CSS, DOM, XML/JSON, XMLHttpRequest/Fetch, JavaScript.
- **Asynchronous is the soul of AJAX.** Without it, it is just a normal HTTP request.
- **The XHR object has 5 readyState values (0 → 4).** Always check `readyState == 4` **AND** `status == 200` before processing the response.
- **`open(method, url, async)`** is used for *configuration*. **`send()`** is used for *dispatch*. The third argument of `open()` is the async flag — must be `true` for AJAX.
- **`responseText`** returns a raw string (used for JSON). **`responseXML`** returns a DOM tree (used for XML).
- **JSON is preferred over XML** in modern AJAX because JSON is **lighter, faster, and natively parsed by JavaScript** via `JSON.parse()`.
- **JSON syntax rules:** double-quoted keys, comma-separated members, no trailing commas, no comments allowed.
- **MIME types matter:** server must return `Content-Type: application/json` for JSON and `application/xml` for XML.
- **Common HTTP status codes:** 200 (OK), 404 (Not Found), 500 (Internal Server Error).
- **Modern replacement for XHR:** The **Fetch API** (promise-based, ES6) — more readable and powerful, but XHR is still tested in KTU boards.
- **Famous AJAX applications:** Gmail, Google Maps, Google Suggest, Facebook News Feed, Twitter Timeline.
- **Common KTU question patterns:** "Explain working of AJAX with diagram", "Compare XML and JSON", "List readyState values", "Differentiate synchronous and asynchronous".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working with JSON Data and Loading HTML/JSON with Ajax
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Working with JSON Data and Loading HTML/JSON with Ajax # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 JSON — JavaScript Object Notation **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a **lightweight, language-independent, text-based data interchange format** derived from the object literal syntax of JavaScript. It is used to transmit structured data between a server and a client application, and is the de-facto standard for RESTful web APIs. ```json { "studentId": 504, "name": "Anand Krishnan", "course": "B.Tech CSE", "cgpa": 8.74, "skills": ["JavaScript", "Python", "React"], "active": true, "address": { "city": "Thrissur", "state": "Kerala" } } ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** JSON is **NOT** a programming language. It is a *serialization format* — meaning it converts in-memory objects (like arrays, dictionaries) into a string that can be sent over HTTP and reconstructed on the other side. ### JSON Syntax Rules (Must-Memorize for KTU) | Rule | Valid | Invalid | |---|---|---| | Keys must be **double-quoted** strings | `{"name": "X"}` | `{name: "X"}` | | Strings use **double quotes only** | `"hello"` | `'hello'` | | Numbers: integer or decimal, no leading zeros | `42`, `3.14` | `01`, `0xFF` | | Booleans are lowercase | `true`, `false` | `True`, `TRUE` | | Null is lowercase | `null` | `Null`, `NULL` | | No trailing commas allowed | `{"a":1, "b":2}` | `{"a":1, "b":2,}` | | No comments allowed | — | `// comment` or `/* */` | ### Conceptual Analogy — "The Packing Box" 📦 Imagine you are moving houses. You cannot carry your entire sofa in your hand — you must **disassemble it, pack the parts in a labeled box**, transport it, and then **reassemble it** at the new house. - The **object in memory** (e.g., a JavaScript `Object` or Python `dict`) = the fully assembled sofa. - **Serialization** (converting to JSON) = disassembly + packing into a labeled box. - The **JSON string** = the packed box with parts labeled. - **Deserialization** (parsing JSON) = unpacking and reassembling the sofa. - The **HTTP transport** = the delivery truck. JSON is the universal packing standard that both the sending and receiving language "understand." --- ## 1.2 AJAX — Asynchronous JavaScript and XML **Formal Definition:** AJAX is **not a technology** but a *design pattern* — a set of techniques used to send and receive data **from a server asynchronously (in the background)** without interfering with the display and behavior of the existing web page. Modern AJAX commonly uses **JSON** instead of XML, but the acronym remains. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Exam Definition to Memorize:** > *"AJAX is an approach to web development that uses `XMLHttpRequest` (or the modern `Fetch API`) to exchange data with a server asynchronously, allowing partial page updates without a full page reload."* ### Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Waiter" 🍽️ In an **old-style restaurant (traditional web pages)**: - You place an order → the **entire waiter walks back to the kitchen** → returns with the full meal → and **the entire restaurant is shut** until then. *(This is like a full page reload.)* In a **modern restaurant with AJAX**: - You place an order → the **waiter takes just your dish order to the kitchen** → you keep eating your soup → the new dish arrives quietly and is **added to your table** without disturbing you. *(This is AJAX — partial updates without page reload.)* ### Why JSON over XML in Modern AJAX? | Property | XML | JSON | |---|---|---| | Verbosity | Heavy (`Anand`) | Compact (`{"name":"Anand"}`) | | Parsing speed | Slower (DOM-based) | Faster (native JS) | | Data types | Everything is a string | Real types: number, bool, null, array | | Native JS support | Manual parsing required | `JSON.parse()` / `JSON.stringify()` built-in | | Current industry use | Legacy SOAP APIs | **Standard for REST APIs** | > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** When asked *"What does AJAX stand for and what is it used for?"*, always mention: *"Originally used XML, but modern AJAX predominantly uses **JSON** due to its lightweight nature and native JavaScript support."* --- ## 1.3 The Two Ways to Load Data with AJAX 1. **Loading HTML fragments** — server returns a chunk of HTML markup that is injected into a specific `
` of the page. 2. **Loading JSON data** — server returns structured data (JSON string) that the client parses and renders dynamically using JavaScript/DOM manipulation or a templating engine. Both operations are *asynchronous* — the user can keep interacting with the page while the request is in flight. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The `XMLHttpRequest` (XHR) Object — The Classic AJAX Engine The `XMLHttpRequest` object is the original browser API for AJAX. It exposes a **state machine** that you monitor via the `readyState` property. ### The XHR Lifecycle (5 States) | `readyState` | Name | Meaning | |---|---|---| | **0** | `UNSENT` | Object created, `open()` not yet called | | **1** | `OPENED` | `open()` called, request set up | | **2** | `HEADERS_RECEIVED` | `send()` called, response headers received | | **3** | `LOADING` | Response body being downloaded | | **4** | `DONE` | Operation complete (success OR error) | ### The Four Mandatory Steps of an XHR Request 1. **Create** the object: ```javascript const xhr = new XMLHttpRequest(); ``` 2. **Configure** the request using `.open(method, url, async)`: ```javascript xhr.open("GET", "data/students.json", true); ``` 3. **Define a callback** for the `onreadystatechange` event: ```javascript xhr.onreadystatechange = function() { ... }; ``` 4. **Send** the request: ```javascript xhr.send(); ``` --- ## 2.2 The `fetch()` API — The Modern Replacement The `fetch()` API (introduced in ES6 / Fetch Standard) returns a **Promise** and is now the preferred way to make AJAX calls in modern web apps. ### Syntax: ```javascript fetch(url, options) .then(response => response.json()) // parse JSON body .then(data => { /* use data */ }) .catch(error => { /* handle error */ }); ``` ### Key Properties of `fetch()`: - **Promise-based** → use `.then()` / `.catch()` or `async/await`. - **Does NOT throw on HTTP errors** (404, 500) — you must check `response.ok` manually. - First `.then()` gives a `Response` object; you must call `.json()`, `.text()`, or `.blob()` to extract the body. --- ## 2.3 jQuery's AJAX Shorthand Methods (Still in KTU Syllabus) jQuery provides three convenience wrappers, which are extremely common in exam questions: | jQuery Method | Purpose | Returns | |---|---|---| | `$.ajax(url, options)` | Full-featured, configurable AJAX request | `jqXHR` object | | `$.get(url, callback)` | Shorthand for **GET** request (loads HTML/text) | `jqXHR` object | | `$.getJSON(url, callback)` | Shorthand for **GET + JSON parsing** | `jqXHR` object | | `$.post(url, data, callback)` | Shorthand for **POST** request | `jqXHR` object | | `$('#div').load(url)` | Loads **HTML** directly into a selector | `jqXHR` object | ### Example — Loading HTML with `$('#div').load(url)`: ```javascript $("#content").load("pages/about.html"); ``` This fetches `about.html` and injects its inner HTML into `
`. ### Example — Loading JSON with `$.getJSON`: ```javascript $.getJSON("data/products.json", function(products) { products.forEach(p => { $("#list").append(`
  • ${p.name} - ₹${p.price}
    • `); }); }); ``` --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — JSON ↔ JS Conversion | Operation | Code | Direction | |---|---|---| | **Stringify** (object → string) | `JSON.stringify(obj)` | JS → JSON | | **Stringify with indent** | `JSON.stringify(obj, null, 2)` | JS → pretty JSON | | **Parse** (string → object) | `JSON.parse(str)` | JSON → JS | | **Parse safely** | `try { ... } catch(e) { ... }` | Error handling | ### Example — Stringify: ```javascript const user = { name: "Anu", age: 21, role: "student" }; const jsonText = JSON.stringify(user); // jsonText === '{"name":"Anu","age":21,"role":"student"}' ``` ### Example — Parse: ```javascript const jsonText = '{"course":"B.Tech","year":3}'; const obj = JSON.parse(jsonText); console.log(obj.course); // "B.Tech" console.log(obj.year); // 3 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** `JSON.parse()` will throw a `SyntaxError` if the JSON is malformed. **Always wrap it in try/catch** in production code. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Use of JSON + AJAX | |---|---| | **Single Page Applications (SPA)** | React, Angular, Vue apps fetch JSON from REST APIs | | **IoT Dashboards** | Sensor data pushed as JSON via WebSockets / AJAX polling | | **E-commerce** | Live product search, cart updates without page reload | | **Banking Apps** | Transaction queries returning JSON | | **GitHub API, Twitter API** | Public REST APIs return JSON exclusively | --- ## 2.6 The `readyState` × `status` Matrix (KTU Favorite) | `readyState` | `status` | `statusText` | Interpretation | Action | |---|---|---|---|---| | 4 | **200** | "OK" | ✅ Success | Parse and use `responseText` | | 4 | **404** | "Not Found" | ❌ Resource missing | Show "Not Found" message | | 4 | **500** | "Internal Server Error" | ❌ Server crashed | Show generic error | | 4 | **403** | "Forbidden" | ❌ No permission | Show "Access Denied" | | 1, 2, 3 | any | — | ⏳ In progress | Show loading spinner | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough — Loading JSON with XHR Below is a **fully working** example that fetches a JSON file, parses it, and renders it. We trace it line-by-line. ```javascript // -------- File: script.js -------- function loadStudents() { // STEP 1: Create the XHR object const xhr = new XMLHttpRequest(); // STEP 2: Configure the request // Parameters: method="GET", url="data.json", async=true xhr.open("GET", "data/students.json", true); // STEP 3: Define the callback (event handler) xhr.onreadystatechange = function () { // STEP 3a: Wait for the request to fully complete if (xhr.readyState === 4) { // STEP 3b: Check HTTP status code if (xhr.status === 200) { // STEP 3c: Parse the JSON string into a JS object const students = JSON.parse(xhr.responseText); // STEP 3d: Render each student as an
  • const ul = document.getElementById("studentList"); students.forEach(s => { const li = document.createElement("li"); li.textContent = `${s.rollNo} - ${s.name} (CGPA: ${s.cgpa})`; ul.appendChild(li); }); } else { // STEP 3e: Error path console.error("Failed to load data. Status:", xhr.status); } } }; // STEP 4: Fire the request xhr.send(); } ``` ### Companion `students.json` file: ```json [ { "rollNo": "KTE21CS001", "name": "Anand", "cgpa": 8.9 }, { "rollNo": "KTE21CS042", "name": "Priya", "cgpa": 9.2 }, { "rollNo": "KTE21CS078", "name": "Rohit", "cgpa": 7.8 } ] ``` ### Companion HTML: ```html Student List

    KTU Students

      ``` ### Line-by-Line Logic Trace | Line | Purpose | Why It Matters | |---|---|---| | `new XMLHttpRequest()` | Instantiates the engine | Foundation of every XHR call | | `xhr.open("GET", url, true)` | Sets method, URL, async flag | `true` = asynchronous (non-blocking) | | `xhr.onreadystatechange = ...` | Registers event handler | Fires **multiple times** (states 1→4) | | `readyState === 4` | Filters only "done" state | Prevents parsing partial responses | | `status === 200` | Filters only HTTP success | Avoids crashing on 404/500 | | `JSON.parse(xhr.responseText)` | Converts string → array of objects | Without this, data is unusable | | `forEach(s => ...)` | Iterates over the array | Renders each item to the DOM | | `xhr.send()` | Actually transmits the request | Nothing happens without `.send()` | --- ## 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough — Loading HTML with `$.load()` ```javascript // Load a static HTML fragment into a div $("#result").load("fragments/header.html", function(response, status, xhr) { if (status === "success") { console.log("HTML loaded successfully."); } else if (status === "error") { console.error("Load failed. Status:", xhr.status); } }); ``` ### Logic: - jQuery performs an **internal `$.get()`** behind the scenes. - The retrieved HTML's *inner HTML* is set as the `innerHTML` of `#result`. - The callback receives `(responseText, status, jqXHR)`. ### Loading Only a Selector from the HTML: ```javascript $("#result").load("pages/team.html #teamMembers"); ``` This loads **only** the element with `id="teamMembers"` from `team.html`. Useful for partial updates. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough — Loading JSON with `$.getJSON()` ```javascript $.getJSON("api/products", function(products) { // products is ALREADY a JS array (parsed automatically) const html = products.map(p => `

      ${p.title}

      Price: ₹${p.price}

      Stock: ${p.inStock ? "Available" : "Out of stock"}

      ` ).join(""); $("#productContainer").html(html); }).fail(function(jqXHR, textStatus, errorThrown) { $("#productContainer").html(`

      Error: ${textStatus}

      `); }); ``` ### Logic: - `$.getJSON` = `$.ajax({ dataType: "json" })`. - The success callback receives **already-parsed** data — no need to call `JSON.parse()`. - `.fail()` is a Promise-style error handler. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Walkthrough — Modern `fetch()` with `async/await` ```javascript async function loadUsers() { try { const response = await fetch("https://api.example.com/users"); if (!response.ok) { throw new Error(`HTTP error! Status: ${response.status}`); } const users = await response.json(); // parse JSON body console.log("Loaded", users.length, "users"); users.forEach(u => { const li = document.createElement("li"); li.textContent = `${u.id}: ${u.name}`; document.getElementById("userList").appendChild(li); }); } catch (err) { console.error("Fetch failed:", err.message); } } ``` ### Logic: - `fetch()` returns a `Promise`. - `await` pauses until the response headers arrive. - `response.json()` is itself a Promise that resolves with the parsed object. - `response.ok` checks `status ∈ [200, 299]`. - `try/catch` handles network failures AND parse failures. --- ## 3.5 Comparison Matrix — Three AJAX Approaches | Feature | `XMLHttpRequest` | jQuery `$.getJSON` / `$.load` | `fetch()` API | |---|---|---|---| | **Era** | Legacy (2006+) | Legacy (2006+) | Modern (2015+) | | **API style** | Event-based | Promise-like | Promise-based | | **Native to browser** | ✅ Yes | ❌ Needs jQuery library | ✅ Yes | | **JSON parse** | Manual `JSON.parse()` | Automatic | Manual `.json()` | | **Throws on HTTP error?** | No (check `status`) | No (use `.fail()`) | **No** (check `response.ok`) | | **Async/await support** | Via wrapper | Via wrapper | ✅ Native | | **KTU syllabus coverage** | ✅ **Required** | ✅ **Required** | ✅ **Required** | | **Verbosity** | High | Low | Medium | --- ## 3.6 The "Show, Don't Tell" Template Engine Pattern Once JSON is loaded, the standard pattern to render it is: ``` JSON string ↓ JSON.parse() / .json() JS Object / Array ↓ Iterate with forEach / map HTML String (built with template literals) ↓ innerHTML = ... DOM rendered on page ``` ### Equation-Style Representation: $$ \text{HTML}_{\text{output}} \;=\; \bigoplus_{i=1}^{n} \text{Template}( \text{JSON}_{i} ) $$ Where $\oplus$ denotes string concatenation and $\text{Template}(x)$ is a function that maps one JSON object to one HTML snippet. > [!TIP] > **KTU 14-Mark Favorite:** A common question is *"Write a JavaScript program to load JSON data from a file and display it in a table."* Use the pattern above with `...
      ` instead of `
    • `. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 AJAX Request-Response Flow (Mermaid Sequence Diagram) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User participant B as Browser (JS Engine) participant S as Web Server participant DB as Database U->>B: Click "Load Data" button activate B B->>B: new XMLHttpRequest() / fetch() B->>S: HTTP GET request (async) activate S S->>DB: Query for data activate DB DB-->>S: Return raw data deactivate DB S->>S: Serialize to JSON S-->>B: HTTP 200 + JSON body deactivate S B->>B: JSON.parse(responseText) B->>B: Render into DOM B-->>U: Page updates (no reload) deactivate B ``` --- ## 4.2 The XHR State Machine (Mermaid State Diagram) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> unsent : new XMLHttpRequest() unsent --> opened : xhr.open(method, url, true) opened --> headersReceived : xhr.send() headersReceived --> loading : Downloading body loading --> done : Transfer complete done --> [*] note right of done If status == 200: success Else: handle error end note ``` --- ## 4.3 JSON ↔ JavaScript Object Conversion Map (Mermaid Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[JS Object in Memory] -->|JSON.stringify| B[JSON String] B -->|HTTP Transport| C[Server / File] C -->|HTTP Response| D[JSON String on Client] D -->|JSON.parse| E[JS Object on Client] E -->|DOM render| F[HTML on Page] style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Three AJAX Approaches ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Client[CLIENT - BROWSER] UI[User Interface HTML] UI --> EVT[Event Trigger - click, load, ready] EVT --> ENG{Select Engine} end subgraph Engine[ENGINE LAYER] ENG -->|option 1| XHR[XMLHttpRequest Engine] ENG -->|option 2| JQ[jQuery .ajax / .getJSON / .load] ENG -->|option 3| FT[fetch API - Promises] end subgraph Network[NETWORK LAYER] XHR --> HTTP[HTTP Request GET POST] JQ --> HTTP FT --> HTTP HTTP --> SRV[Web Server] end subgraph Server[SERVER LAYER] SRV --> PROC[Business Logic] PROC --> SER[Serialize to JSON or HTML] SER --> RES[HTTP Response 200 OK] end subgraph Process[CLIENT PROCESSING] RES --> PARSE[JSON.parse OR direct HTML] PARSE --> DOM[DOM Manipulation innerHTML / appendChild] DOM --> UI end style Client fill:#fef9e7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style Engine fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style Network fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style Server fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style Process fill:#f4ecf7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 4.5 The `$.load()` Internal Mechanism (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Code: .load fragments/header.html] --> B[jQuery Internally Calls .get] B --> C[Set dataType to html] C --> D[HTTP GET Request] D --> E{Response Status} E -->|200 OK| F[Inject responseText as innerHTML] E -->|error| G[Invoke error callback] F --> H[Call success callback] G --> I[Display error message] style A fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Exam]` **Differentiate between `JSON.stringify()` and `JSON.parse()` in JavaScript. Provide one example of each.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | `JSON.stringify()` | `JSON.parse()` | |---|---|---| | **Direction** | JavaScript Object → JSON String | JSON String → JavaScript Object | | **Purpose** | **Serialization** (for storage/transmission) | **Deserialization** (for use in code) | | **Input type** | Object, Array, primitive | String containing valid JSON | | **Output type** | String | Object, Array, primitive | | **Error behavior** | Returns `undefined` on circular references | Throws `SyntaxError` on malformed input | **Example of `stringify`:** ```javascript const obj = { name: "Anu", age: 21 }; const str = JSON.stringify(obj); // str = '{"name":"Anu","age":21}' ``` **Example of `parse`:** ```javascript const str = '{"course":"B.Tech","year":3}'; const obj = JSON.parse(str); // obj.course = "B.Tech" ``` --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **What is the role of the `readyState` property in an `XMLHttpRequest` object? List any four values it can take.** **Model Answer:** The `readyState` property holds the **current lifecycle state** of the `XMLHttpRequest` object. It is an integer (0–4) that is updated as the request progresses. The callback function `onreadystatechange` is invoked every time `readyState` changes. The four main values are: - **0 — `UNSENT`**: Object created, but `open()` not called yet. - **1 — `OPENED`**: `open()` has been called successfully. - **2 — `HEADERS_RECEIVED`**: `send()` called, response headers and status received. - **3 — `LOADING`**: Response body (data) is being downloaded. - **4 — `DONE`**: Operation complete (data fully downloaded — success or failure). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the term **AJAX**. Describe the steps involved in sending an AJAX request using the `XMLHttpRequest` object. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write a complete JavaScript program that uses `XMLHttpRequest` to load the following JSON data from a file `students.json` and display the student details (Roll No, Name, CGPA) in an HTML table. **(7 Marks)** Sample `students.json`: ```json [ { "rollNo": "KTE21CS001", "name": "Anand", "cgpa": 8.9 }, { "rollNo": "KTE21CS042", "name": "Priya", "cgpa": 9.2 }, { "rollNo": "KTE21CS078", "name": "Rohit", "cgpa": 7.8 } ] ``` --- ### **Model Solution for Question A** #### Part (a) — Explanation & Steps **AJAX (Asynchronous JavaScript and XML)** is a web development technique that allows a web page to communicate with a server **in the background** and update parts of the page **without a full reload**. **The Four Steps of an XHR AJAX Request:** | Step | Code | Purpose | |---|---|---| | 1. **Create object** | `const xhr = new XMLHttpRequest();` | Instantiate the AJAX engine | | 2. **Open connection** | `xhr.open("GET", "data.json", true);` | Set HTTP method, URL, async flag | | 3. **Define callback** | `xhr.onreadystatechange = function() { ... };` | React to state changes | | 4. **Send request** | `xhr.send();` | Transmit the HTTP request to server | Inside the callback, the pattern is: ```javascript if (xhr.readyState === 4 && xhr.status === 200) { const data = JSON.parse(xhr.responseText); // use data... } ``` > **Why these 4 steps?** `XMLHttpRequest` is a low-level API, so the browser forces you to explicitly separate *configuration* (`open`), *execution* (`send`), and *reaction* (`onreadystatechange`). This gives fine-grained control but is verbose. --- #### Part (b) — Complete Program **HTML File — `index.html`:** ```html Student Records

      KTU Student Records

      Roll NoNameCGPA
      ``` **JavaScript File — `script.js`:** ```javascript function loadStudents() { // STEP 1: Create the XHR object const xhr = new XMLHttpRequest(); // STEP 2: Open the connection (GET request, async = true) xhr.open("GET", "students.json", true); // STEP 3: Define the callback xhr.onreadystatechange = function () { // Wait for the request to fully complete if (xhr.readyState === 4) { if (xhr.status === 200) { // Parse the JSON string into a JS array const students = JSON.parse(xhr.responseText); // Reference to the table body const tbody = document.querySelector("#studentTable tbody"); tbody.innerHTML = ""; // clear any previous rows // Render each student as a new students.forEach(s => { const tr = document.createElement("tr"); tr.innerHTML = ` ${s.rollNo} ${s.name} ${s.cgpa} `; tbody.appendChild(tr); }); } else { alert("Error loading data. HTTP status: " + xhr.status); } } }; // STEP 4: Send the request xhr.send(); } ``` **Output (rendered table):** | Roll No | Name | CGPA | |---|---|---| | KTE21CS001 | Anand | 8.9 | | KTE21CS042 | Priya | 9.2 | | KTE21CS078 | Rohit | 7.8 | --- ### **Question B** (Internal Choice) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **(a)** What is JSON? List and explain any **six** syntax rules of JSON with examples. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write a JavaScript program that uses **jQuery's `$.getJSON()`** method to load product data from `products.json` and display the product name and price in a list. The JSON contains an array of objects with `name`, `price`, and `inStock` fields. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question B** #### Part (a) — JSON & Six Syntax Rules **JSON (JavaScript Object Notation)** is a lightweight, text-based, language-independent data interchange format. It is commonly used for transmitting structured data over a network (e.g., between server and web client) as an alternative to XML. **Six JSON Syntax Rules:** | # | Rule | Valid | Invalid | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Data is in **name/value pairs** | `{"name":"Anu"}` | `{"name"="Anu"}` | | 2 | Data is separated by **commas** | `{"a":1,"b":2}` | `{"a":1 "b":2}` | | 3 | **Curly braces** hold objects | `{"course":"B.Tech"}` | `B.Tech` | | 4 | **Square brackets** hold arrays | `[1,2,3]` | `{1,2,3}` | | 5 | **Keys must be in double quotes** | `{"id":1}` | `{id:1}` | | 6 | **Strings must use double quotes** | `"hello"` | `'hello'` | > **Bonus Rule 7:** Numbers can be integer or floating-point, but **must not have leading zeros** and **must not be NaN/Infinity**. **Example of valid JSON:** ```json { "course": "B.Tech", "students": [ { "id": 1, "name": "Anu", "active": true }, { "id": 2, "name": "Raj", "active": null } ] } ``` --- #### Part (b) — jQuery `$.getJSON()` Program **HTML File — `index.html`:** ```html Product List

      Available Products

        ``` **Sample `products.json`:** ```json [ { "name": "Laptop", "price": 65000, "inStock": true }, { "name": "Headphone","price": 1500, "inStock": false }, { "name": "Keyboard", "price": 2200, "inStock": true } ] ``` **Output (rendered list):** - **Laptop** — ₹65000 (In Stock) - **Headphone** — ₹1500 (Out of Stock) - **Keyboard** — ₹2200 (In Stock) --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Lose Marks on This Topic:** > > 1. **Forgetting `readyState === 4` check** — The callback fires multiple times. Without this check, you will try to parse `responseText` while it is empty, throwing errors. *(−2 marks)* > > 2. **Forgetting `status === 200` check** — Even if the download succeeds, an HTTP 404 or 500 returns a non-empty `responseText` (often an error page). Parsing it as JSON crashes the program. *(−2 marks)* > > 3. **Confusing `JSON.stringify()` and `JSON.parse()`** — `stringify` converts *object→string*; `parse` converts *string→object*. Examiners check the direction. *(−2 marks)* > > 4. **Single quotes in JSON keys/strings** — `{'name':'Anu'}` is **invalid JSON**. KTU strictly requires double quotes. *(−1 mark)* > > 5. **Trailing commas in JSON** — `{"a":1,"b":2,}` is invalid. The last item must not have a trailing comma. *(−1 mark)* > > 6. **Calling `JSON.parse()` inside jQuery's `$.getJSON()`** — jQuery **already** parses it for you. Calling `JSON.parse` on the data will throw an error. *(−2 marks)* > > 7. **Not including the jQuery `` or it won't run. *(−1 mark)* > > 8. **Using `fetch()` without checking `response.ok`** — The promise resolves even on 404. Must manually check. *(−1 mark)* --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀 > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "JSON + AJAX Survival Kit"** ### 🔑 JSON Essentials - ✅ JSON = **text-based data format** for exchange. **Not a language.** - ✅ **Double quotes only** for keys and string values. No single quotes. No comments. No trailing commas. - ✅ Valid data types: `string`, `number`, `boolean`, `null`, `array`, `object`. - ✅ `JSON.stringify(obj)` → object to string. `JSON.parse(str)` → string to object. - ✅ Wrap `JSON.parse` in `try/catch` to handle malformed input. ### 🔑 AJAX Essentials - ✅ AJAX = **Asynchronous JavaScript and XML** (modern usage = JSON, not XML). - ✅ Enables **partial page updates** — no full reload. - ✅ Three main techniques: `XMLHttpRequest`, jQuery methods (`$.ajax`, `$.get`, `$.getJSON`, `$.load`), and modern `fetch()` API. ### 🔑 XHR Must-Know - ✅ 4 steps: **Create → Open → Callback → Send**. - ✅ Check `readyState === 4` AND `status === 200` before using data. - ✅ `responseText` is a **string** — must call `JSON.parse()` for JSON responses. ### 🔑 jQuery Must-Know - ✅ `$(selector).load(url)` — loads **HTML** into the element. - ✅ `$.get(url, callback)` — loads text/HTML via GET. - ✅ `$.getJSON(url, callback)` — loads **JSON** (auto-parsed) via GET. - ✅ Must include jQuery `

topics.title: Cross-Server Data Requests with Ajax
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cross-Server Data Requests with Ajax

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔒 Cross-Server Data Requests with AJAX — Section 1: Core Foundations

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **AJAX (Asynchronous JavaScript and XML)**, a *Cross-Server (or Cross-Origin) Data Request* is an HTTP transaction initiated by a client-side script (typically the `XMLHttpRequest` object or the modern `fetch()` API) that targets a **resource located on a different origin** than the one serving the currently loaded web page.

An **origin** is defined by the **triple combination** of three URL components:

$$\text{Origin} = \langle \text{Protocol}, \ \text{Host}, \ \text{Port} \rangle$$

Two URLs are considered to have the **same origin** if and only if **all three** of these components match exactly. If even one component differs, the request is treated as a **cross-origin request** and is governed by browser security policies such as the **Same-Origin Policy (SOP)** and the **Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS)** specification (W3C Recommendation).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBITT504, Module 2):**
> Students must clearly distinguish between **Same-Origin Requests** (executed without special browser intervention) and **Cross-Origin Requests** (which require explicit server cooperation via CORS headers or alternative techniques such as JSONP or proxy-based relay).

### 1.2 The Same-Origin Policy (SOP) — The Default Restriction

The **Same-Origin Policy** is a critical, mandatory security mechanism implemented by all modern browsers. It prevents a script loaded from *Origin A* from reading or interacting with the **DOM**, **cookies**, or **response data** of a resource loaded from *Origin B*.

Without the SOP, a malicious script on `evil.com` could freely read your private banking data on `bank.com`. The SOP makes this impossible **by default**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Customs & Passport Checkpoint ✈️

Imagine you are a citizen of **Country A** (e.g., your web page loaded from `www.myapp.in`) and you want to visit a citizen of **Country B** (e.g., a server at `api.foreignserver.com`) to ask for some documents (JSON data).

- 🛂 **Same-Origin Policy (SOP):** By default, you are **not allowed** to cross the border. Your browser is the strict border security officer that blocks your request immediately. This is for **safety** — to prevent imposters (malicious scripts) from stealing documents.
- 📜 **Passport = CORS Headers:** The foreign server (Country B) must explicitly say *"Yes, citizens of Country A are welcome, and here is the list of what they may bring back."* It does this by sending **CORS response headers** like `Access-Control-Allow-Origin`.
- 🛬 **Pre-flight Check (OPTIONS Request):** For sensitive or non-routine visits, the border officer (browser) first sends a small **inquiry flight** (an `OPTIONS` HTTP request) to confirm *"Is this visitor really allowed? What documents do they need?"* Only after getting approval does the **actual data flight** (GET, POST, etc.) take off.
- 🚪 **Embassy Trick (JSONP):** Long before the CORS "passport system" existed, clever diplomats used a loophole — they would embed their messages inside a **publicly-broadcast news ticker** (the `<script>` tag), because news tickers are always allowed across borders. JSONP is exactly that: a trick to bypass the border using `<script>` tags.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Components Define "Same Origin":**
> 1. **Protocol** (`http` vs `https` — must match)
> 2. **Host/Domain** (`myapp.com` vs `api.myapp.com` — must match)
> 3. **Port** (`80`, `443`, `8080` — must match)
>
> Even changing **one** character (e.g., adding `s` to `http`) makes it a **cross-origin** request.

---

### 1.4 Why Cross-Server Requests Are Restricted — The Threat Model

| Threat | Description | SOP/CORS Defense |
|---|---|---|
| **Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)** | Malicious site triggers authenticated actions on another site | SOP blocks reading the response |
| **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) Data Theft** | Injected script reads private data from other origins | SOP isolates origins |
| **Session Hijacking** | Stolen cookies used across domains | Cookies are origin-bound |
| **Confidentiality Breach** | Public page reading private API responses | CORS opt-in policy |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Reference for KTU Students:**
> **SOP** = the *default-deny* policy (browser blocks).
> **CORS** = the *explicit-allow* mechanism (server unblocks).

---

## 1.5 The Cross-Origin Request — A High-Level View

A typical cross-origin AJAX workflow involves **two distinct HTTP exchanges** in the case of a *preflighted* request, or a **single exchange** in the case of a *simple* request. The classification depends on:

- The **HTTP method** used
- The **headers** being sent
- The **Content-Type** of the request body

This classification is the gateway to understanding all subsequent behavior in this topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📚 Cross-Server Data Requests with AJAX — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis

## 2.1 Classification of Cross-Origin AJAX Requests

The browser categorizes every cross-origin AJAX request into one of two classes, with **profoundly different processing pipelines**:

### Class A — Simple Requests ✉️
A request is "simple" (and therefore sent **immediately**, without a preflight) only if **all** of the following conditions hold:

- **HTTP Method** ∈ {`GET`, `HEAD`, `POST`}
- **Headers** are limited to the *CORS-safelisted request headers* set:
  - `Accept`
  - `Accept-Language`
  - `Content-Language`
  - `Content-Type` (restricted values — see below)
  - `Range`
  - (plus a few others)
- **Content-Type** ∈ {`application/x-www-form-urlencoded`, `multipart/form-data`, `text/plain`}

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If **any** of the above constraints is violated, the browser **upgrades** the request to a *preflighted* request — meaning an `OPTIONS` round-trip is sent first.

### Class B — Preflighted Requests ✈️
A request is preflighted when it uses:
- A **non-simple method** (e.g., `PUT`, `DELETE`, `PATCH`)
- **Custom headers** (e.g., `X-Auth-Token`, `Authorization`)
- A **non-simple Content-Type** (e.g., `application/json`)

The browser first sends an `OPTIONS` request to the server to ask: *"May I make this kind of request?"*

---

## 2.2 Anatomy of a CORS Request — The HTTP Exchange

### 2.2.1 Request Headers (Sent by the Browser Automatically)

| Header | Purpose | Example Value |
|---|---|---|
| `Origin` | Identifies the requesting origin | `https://www.myapp.in` |
| `Access-Control-Request-Method` | Tells server the intended method (preflight only) | `PUT` |
| `Access-Control-Request-Headers` | Lists non-safelisted headers (preflight only) | `Content-Type, X-Auth-Token` |

### 2.2.2 Response Headers (Sent by the Server)

| Header | Purpose | Example Value |
|---|---|---|
| `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` | Which origin(s) may read the response | `*` or `https://www.myapp.in` |
| `Access-Control-Allow-Methods` | Permitted HTTP methods | `GET, POST, PUT, DELETE` |
| `Access-Control-Allow-Headers` | Permitted custom request headers | `Content-Type, Authorization` |
| `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials` | Allow cookies/auth on cross-origin | `true` |
| `Access-Control-Max-Age` | Preflight result cache duration (seconds) | `3600` |
| `Access-Control-Expose-Headers` | Headers the browser may expose to JS | `X-Custom-Header` |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Quick Reference

### 📋 The CORS Origin Equation

Two URLs share the **same origin** if and only if:

$$\text{same\_origin}(U_1, U_2) \iff \text{scheme}(U_1) = \text{scheme}(U_2) \ \land \ \text{host}(U_1) = \text{host}(U_2) \ \land \ \text{port}(U_1) = \text{port}(U_2)$$

> Note: $\land$ denotes logical AND. All three conditions **must** be true.

### 📋 The CORS Decision Function (Browser Logic)

$$\text{allow\_read}(R) = \begin{cases} \text{TRUE} & \text{if } \text{ACAO}(R) = \text{request.origin} \ \lor \ \text{ACAO}(R) = \text{"*"} \\ \text{FALSE} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Where $R$ is the response and $\text{ACAO}(R)$ denotes the value of the `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` response header.

> [!WARNING]
> The wildcard `*` **cannot** be used together with `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true`. This is a strict browser-enforced rule.

### 📋 Preflight Cache Duration

$$T_{\text{cache}} = \text{value of } \texttt{Access-Control-Max-Age} \quad \text{(in seconds)}$$

The browser caches the preflight approval for $T_{\text{cache}}$ seconds, avoiding redundant `OPTIONS` calls.

### 📋 Classification Test for a Cross-Origin Request

$$\text{is\_simple}(M, H, C) = \begin{cases} \text{TRUE} & \text{if } M \in \{\text{GET, HEAD, POST}\} \ \land \ H \subseteq \mathcal{H}_{\text{safe}} \ \land \ C \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{safe}} \\ \text{FALSE} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Where:
- $M$ = HTTP method
- $H$ = set of request headers
- $C$ = Content-Type
- $\mathcal{H}_{\text{safe}}$ = the CORS-safelisted request headers
- $\mathcal{C}_{\text{safe}} = \{\text{application/x-www-form-urlencoded}, \text{multipart/form-data}, \text{text/plain}\}$

---

## 2.4 The Three Solution Strategies for Cross-Server Data

### Strategy 1 — CORS (Modern Standard) ✅

**Mechanism:** Server-side configuration that explicitly grants permission via response headers.

**Advantages:**
- Standards-compliant (W3C Recommendation)
- Granular control (origin, methods, headers, credentials)
- Caches preflight responses for performance

**Disadvantages:**
- Requires server-side cooperation
- Older servers may not support it
- Public APIs may be locked down

### Strategy 2 — JSONP (Legacy Trick) 🧓

**Mechanism:** Inject a `<script>` tag whose `src` points to the cross-origin API. The server wraps its JSON response inside a JavaScript function call.

**Mechanism Pipeline:**

1. Client defines a global function: `window.processData = function(data) { /* use data */ };`
2. Client dynamically creates `<script src="https://api.foreign.com/data?callback=processData"></script>`
3. Server returns: `processData({"status": "ok", "value": 42});`
4. Browser executes the script → `processData()` is invoked with the JSON as argument.

**Advantages:**
- Works in any browser (very old ones included)
- No server configuration needed (server must support JSONP)

**Disadvantages:**
- ⚠️ **Security risk** — server has full script execution access in your page
- **GET only** — POST/PUT/DELETE impossible
- **No error handling** — script-tag failures are silent
- ❌ **Failing security audits** — JSONP is widely deprecated

### Strategy 3 — Reverse Proxy / Server-Side Relay 🔁

**Mechanism:** A same-origin endpoint on *your* server forwards the request to the foreign API and relays the response back.

```
Browser  →  Your-Server  →  Foreign-API
         (same origin)    (server-to-server, no SOP)
```

**Advantages:**
- Bypasses CORS entirely (server-to-server has no SOP)
- Can mask credentials, cache responses
- Full HTTP method support

**Disadvantages:**
- Increases infrastructure complexity
- Latency (extra hop)
- Server costs scale with traffic

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use Case for Cross-Server AJAX |
|---|---|
| **Single-Page Applications (SPAs)** | React/Angular apps on `app.com` consuming REST APIs on `api.app.com` |
| **Third-Party API Integration** | Frontend consuming Stripe, Google Maps, OpenWeather APIs |
| **Microservices Architecture** | Different services on subdomains sharing data |
| **CDN-Served Assets** | Static frontend on CDN calling backend on origin server |
| **OAuth & Authentication Flows** | Cross-origin token exchange (with proper CORS config) |

# 🧮 Cross-Server Data Requests with AJAX — Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Walkthrough 1 — Simple CORS GET Request (End-to-End)

**Scenario:** A page served from `https://app.mysite.com` makes an AJAX `GET` request to fetch user data from `https://api.mysite.com/user/42`.

### Step 1 — Client Initiates the Request
The JavaScript code uses the `fetch()` API or `XMLHttpRequest`:

```javascript
fetch('https://api.mysite.com/user/42', { method: 'GET' })
```

**Analysis:** The browser detects that the request targets a **different origin** (`api.mysite.com` ≠ `app.mysite.com` in the host component). It is therefore a **cross-origin** request. The method is `GET`, no custom headers, default `Content-Type` is empty — this qualifies as a **Simple Request**, so **no preflight** is triggered.

### Step 2 — Browser Sends the Actual Request
The browser automatically adds the `Origin` header:

$$\text{HTTP Request} = \text{GET} \ / \text{user/42} \ \text{HTTP/1.1}$$
$$\text{Host: } \texttt{api.mysite.com}$$
$$\text{Origin: } \texttt{https://app.mysite.com}$$
$$\text{Accept: } \text{application/json}$$

### Step 3 — Server Evaluates the `Origin` Header
The server-side application (Node.js, PHP, Python, etc.) inspects the `Origin` header value and decides whether to permit this origin.

### Step 4 — Server Returns Response with CORS Headers

$$\text{HTTP Response} = \text{HTTP/1.1} \ 200 \ \text{OK}$$
$$\text{Content-Type: } \text{application/json}$$
$$\text{Access-Control-Allow-Origin: } \texttt{https://app.mysite.com}$$

**Decision Function Application:**

$$\text{allow\_read} = (\text{ACAO} = \text{request.origin}) = (\texttt{https://app.mysite.com} = \texttt{https://app.mysite.com}) = \text{TRUE}$$

### Step 5 — Browser Releases Response to JavaScript
Because $\text{allow\_read} = \text{TRUE}$, the browser allows the JavaScript code to read the response body. If the server had returned `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.com` (or omitted the header), the browser would have **blocked** the response, throwing a `TypeError` in the `fetch()` promise.

---

## 3.2 Walkthrough 2 — Preflighted PUT Request with JSON Body

**Scenario:** A page on `https://dashboard.app.io` sends a `PUT` request with `Content-Type: application/json` to `https://api.app.io/profile/7`.

### Step 1 — Browser Classifies the Request
Method = `PUT` (not in `{GET, HEAD, POST}`). Therefore: **NOT a simple request** → **Preflight required**.

### Step 2 — Browser Sends the OPTIONS Pre-flight
$$\text{HTTP Request} = \text{OPTIONS} \ / \text{profile/7} \ \text{HTTP/1.1}$$
$$\text{Host: } \texttt{api.app.io}$$
$$\text{Origin: } \texttt{https://dashboard.app.io}$$
$$\text{Access-Control-Request-Method: } \text{PUT}$$
$$\text{Access-Control-Request-Headers: } \text{content-type}$$

### Step 3 — Server Evaluates and Responds

$$\text{HTTP Response} = \text{HTTP/1.1} \ 204 \ \text{No Content}$$
$$\text{Access-Control-Allow-Origin: } \texttt{https://dashboard.app.io}$$
$$\text{Access-Control-Allow-Methods: } \text{GET, POST, PUT, DELETE}$$
$$\text{Access-Control-Allow-Headers: } \text{content-type, authorization}$$
$$\text{Access-Control-Max-Age: } 3600$$

**Decision Function Application:**

$$\text{allow\_read} = (\texttt{https://dashboard.app.io} = \texttt{https://dashboard.app.io}) \ \land \ (\text{PUT} \in \{\text{GET, POST, PUT, DELETE}\}) \ \land \ (\text{content-type} \in \text{allowed headers}) = \text{TRUE}$$

### Step 4 — Browser Sends the Actual PUT Request
With preflight approved (and cached for $T_{\text{cache}} = 3600$ seconds), the browser sends:

$$\text{HTTP Request} = \text{PUT} \ / \text{profile/7} \ \text{HTTP/1.1}$$
$$\text{Origin: } \texttt{https://dashboard.app.io}$$
$$\text{Content-Type: } \text{application/json}$$
$$\text{Body: } \texttt{\{"name": "New Name"\}}$$

### Step 5 — Server Processes and Returns 200
The actual business logic executes. Browser allows the response to be read by JavaScript.

---

## 3.3 Walkthrough 3 — JSONP Mechanism (Step-by-Step)

**Scenario:** Legacy cross-origin GET from `https://legacy.app.net` to `https://data.partner.com/stock/AAPL`.

### Step 1 — Client Defines the Callback
A globally accessible function is created on the page:

```javascript
window.handleStockData = function(data) {
  console.log("Price:", data.price);
};
```

### Step 2 — Client Injects a Script Tag
A `<script>` element is dynamically created and appended to the DOM:

```javascript
const s = document.createElement('script');
s.src = 'https://data.partner.com/stock/AAPL?callback=handleStockData';
document.body.appendChild(s);
```

### Step 3 — Browser Loads the Script (No SOP Block)
Script tags are not subject to the Same-Origin Policy for **loading**, so the browser fetches the resource.

### Step 4 — Server Responds with Wrapped JSON
The partner server detects the `callback` query parameter and wraps the JSON:

```javascript
// Server response (Content-Type: application/javascript)
handleStockData({"symbol":"AAPL","price":187.45,"currency":"USD"});
```

### Step 5 — Browser Executes the Script
The JavaScript engine evaluates the response, calling `handleStockData(...)` with the parsed object as the argument. The user code now has access to the data.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analysis Matrix

| Feature | CORS | JSONP | Reverse Proxy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **HTTP Methods Supported** | All | GET only | All |
| **Custom Headers** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Credentials/Cookies** | ✅ (with `Allow-Credentials: true`) | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (server-side) |
| **Error Handling** | ✅ Standard HTTP | ❌ Silent failures | ✅ Standard HTTP |
| **Security Level** | High (whitelist model) | Low (script injection risk) | High |
| **Browser Support** | All modern browsers | All (legacy fallback) | All |
| **Server Config Required** | Yes (response headers) | Yes (JSONP wrapper logic) | Yes (proxy endpoint) |
| **Performance (Latency)** | 1 or 2 round-trips | 1 round-trip | 2 round-trips |
| **Modern Recommendation** | ✅ **Standard** | ⚠️ **Avoid in new code** | ✅ **For complex topologies** |

---

## 3.5 The Math Behind Preflight Caching

Suppose a user interacts with a single-page application that issues 100 different `PUT` requests to the same API endpoint within a session, all using the same custom `X-Request-ID` header.

**Without caching**, the browser would issue 100 preflight `OPTIONS` requests + 100 actual `PUT` requests = **200 HTTP round-trips**.

**With `Access-Control-Max-Age: 3600`** (1 hour), within that 1-hour window, the browser issues:
- 1 preflight + 100 actual `PUT` = **101 round-trips**

**Cache Efficiency Calculation:**

$$\eta_{\text{cache}} = \frac{\text{RTT}_{\text{baseline}} - \text{RTT}_{\text{cached}}}{\text{RTT}_{\text{baseline}}} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta_{\text{cache}} = \frac{200 - 101}{200} \times 100\% = 49.5\%$$

**Result:** Nearly **half** the HTTP overhead is eliminated. This is why server admins should set `Access-Control-Max-Age` to a non-trivial value (e.g., 600–3600 seconds).

## 4.6 Diagram 6 — The CORS Pre-flight Approval Lifecycle (State Machine)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> RequestInitiated: JavaScript executes AJAX call
    RequestInitiated --> Classification: Browser inspects method headers content type
    Classification --> SimpleRequest: Method GET HEAD POST and safelisted headers
    Classification --> PreflightRequired: Method PUT DELETE PATCH or custom header
    PreflightRequired --> SendingOPTIONS: Browser dispatches OPTIONS request
    SendingOPTIONS --> ServerEvaluates: Server reads origin and requested method
    ServerEvaluates --> Allowed204: Origin permitted and method allowed
    ServerEvaluates --> Rejected403: Origin not in whitelist
    Allowed204 --> Caching: Store preflight for Max-Age seconds
    Caching --> ActualRequest: Dispatch real GET POST PUT
    SimpleRequest --> ActualRequest: Dispatch real request directly
    ActualRequest --> ServerProcesses: Server executes business logic
    ServerProcesses --> BrowserValidatesACAO: Browser checks Access-Control-Allow-Origin
    BrowserValidatesACAO --> Success200: ACAO matches request origin
    BrowserValidatesACAO --> BlockedError: ACAO missing or wrong
    Rejected403 --> BlockedError: Browser throws CORS error
    Success200 --> [*]: Response available to JavaScript
    BlockedError --> [*]: JavaScript receives TypeError
```
