&\texttt{mkdir myfirst-node-app} \quad \text{// Creates project directory} \\
&\texttt{cd myfirst-node-app} \quad \text{// Enters the directory} \\
&\texttt{npm init -y} \quad \text{// Generates package.json with default values}
\end{aligned}
$$

The `npm init -y` flag (`-y` for "yes") auto-accepts all default prompts, producing a minimal `package.json`:

$$
\texttt{\{ "name": "myfirst-node-app", "version": "1.0.0", "main": "index.js", ... \}}
$$

### Step 6 — Write the First Server (`hello.js`)

Create a file named `hello.js` with the following content:

```javascript
const http = require('http');

const hostname = '127.0.0.1';
const port = 3000;

const server = http.createServer((req, res) => {
  res.statusCode = 200;
  res.setHeader('Content-Type', 'text/plain');
  res.end('Hello World from Node.js!\n');
});

server.listen(port, hostname, () => {
  console.log(`Server running at http://${hostname}:${port}/`);
});
```

### Step 7 — Execute the Server

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\texttt{node hello.js} \quad \text{// Starts the server} \\
&\text{Output: } \texttt{Server running at http://127.0.0.1:3000/}
\end{aligned}
$$

Open a browser and navigate to `http://localhost:3000/`. The response "Hello World from Node.js!" confirms the entire installation stack is operational.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Behind Each Line (Exam-Friendly Explanation):**
> - `require('http')` → imports the built-in HTTP module, eliminating the need for an external server like Apache or Nginx.
> - `http.createServer()` → factory function returning a server object.
> - `server.listen(port, hostname, callback)` → binds the server to a TCP port.
> - The callback function handles every incoming request (here simplified to return a plain-text greeting).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Installation Variants Across Operating Systems

| OS | Installation Method | Binary Location | Verification Command | KTU Lab Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Windows** | `.msi` installer from nodejs.org | `C:\Program Files\nodejs\` | `node -v` in CMD | Most common in KTU computer centers |
| **macOS** | `.pkg` installer or `brew install node` | `/usr/local/bin/node` | `node -v` in Terminal | Homebrew is preferred by developers |
| **Ubuntu/Debian** | NodeSource APT repository | `/usr/bin/node` | `node -v` in Bash | Used in cloud/server environments |
| **Fedora/RHEL** | `dnf install nodejs` | `/usr/bin/node` | `node -v` in Bash | Enterprise Linux distros |
| **Docker** | `docker pull node:20` | Inside container | `node -v` (in container) | Modern CI/CD pipelines |

## 3.3 npm Command Hierarchy (Depth-of-Knowledge)

The **npm command structure** follows a layered hierarchy that examiners frequently test:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Level 1 (Initialize):} \quad & \texttt{npm init / npm init -y} \\
\text{Level 2 (Install Local):} \quad & \texttt{npm install <package>} \rightarrow \text{node\_modules/} \\
\text{Level 3 (Install Global):} \quad & \texttt{npm install -g <package>} \rightarrow \text{global prefix} \\
\text{Level 4 (Dev Dependency):} \quad & \texttt{npm install --save-dev <package>} \\
\text{Level 5 (Uninstall):} \quad & \texttt{npm uninstall <package>} \\
\text{Level 6 (Update):} \quad & \texttt{npm update <package>}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Local vs Global — KTU's most-asked distinction:**
> - **Local packages** are scoped to a single project, stored in `./node_modules/`, and listed in `package.json` under `dependencies`.
> - **Global packages** are installed once system-wide and are typically CLI tools (e.g., `nodemon`, `yarn`).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Node.js Installation Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: User Decision] --> B{Choose OS}
    B -->|Windows| C[Download .msi from nodejs.org]
    B -->|macOS| D[Download .pkg or use Homebrew]
    B -->|Linux| E[Add NodeSource APT repository]

    C --> F[Run installer wizard]
    D --> F1[Run pkg or brew install]
    E --> E1[apt-get install nodejs]

    F --> G[Auto PATH configuration]
    F1 --> G
    E1 --> G

    G --> H[Open Terminal or CMD]
    H --> I{Verification}
    I -->|node -v| J[Prints v20.11.0]
    I -->|npm -v| K[Prints 10.2.4]

    J --> L[Create project folder]
    K --> L
    L --> M[npm init -y generates package.json]
    M --> N[Write hello.js using http module]
    N --> O[Run node hello.js]
    O --> P[Server listens on port 3000]
    P --> Q[Browser shows Hello World]
    Q --> R[Installation Validated]
```

## 4.2 Node.js Runtime Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UserLayer [User Space]
        U1[JavaScript Code hello.js]
    end

    subgraph NodeRuntime [Node.js Runtime]
        N1[V8 JavaScript Engine]
        N2[libuv Asynchronous I/O Library]
        N3[Core Modules http fs path crypto]
        N4[npm Package Manager]
    end

    subgraph OSLayer [Operating System]
        O1[File System]
        O2[Network Stack]
        O3[Thread Pool]
    end

    U1 --> N1
    N1 --> N2
    N2 --> N3
    N4 --> N1
    N2 --> O1
    N2 --> O2
    N2 --> O3
```

## 4.3 npm Workflow Diagram (Local vs Global Installation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[npm install express] --> B{Includes -g flag}
    B -->|No Local| C[node_modules/ in project]
    B -->|Yes Global| D[System-wide path npm prefix -g]

    C --> E[Updates package.json dependencies]
    D --> F[Available as CLI tool globally]

    E --> G[Project scoped]
    F --> H[Machine scoped]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Node.js. Mention any two features of it.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**
Node.js is an open-source, cross-platform JavaScript runtime environment built on Google Chrome's V8 engine. It allows JavaScript to run on the server side, outside the browser.

**Two Features:**
1. **Asynchronous and Event-Driven:** All APIs of Node.js are asynchronous (non-blocking), meaning the server does not wait for the API to return data.
2. **Single-Threaded but Highly Scalable:** Node.js uses a single-threaded model with event looping, enabling it to handle thousands of concurrent connections efficiently.
3. **Fast Execution:** Built on the V8 engine, code is executed at high speed via JIT compilation.
4. **No Buffering:** Node.js applications never buffer data; they simply output the data in chunks.

*(Any two valid features — 2 marks. Definition — 1 mark. Total = 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. What is npm? How is it different from a Local Installation versus a Global Installation?
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
**npm (Node Package Manager):** It is the default package manager for Node.js, bundled automatically with the Node.js installation. It is used to install, share, and manage dependencies in Node.js projects.

**Local Installation:**
- Command: `npm install <package>`
- Installed inside the project's `node_modules/` directory.
- Listed in `package.json` under `dependencies`.
- Scoped to a single project.

**Global Installation:**
- Command: `npm install -g <package>`
- Installed at a system-wide location (e.g., `C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Roaming\npm\node_modules`).
- Typically used for CLI tools (e.g., `nodemon`, `yarn`).
- Available across all projects on the machine.

*(npm definition — 1 mark. Local vs Global distinction — 2 marks. Total = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the step-by-step procedure to install Node.js on a Windows operating system. List the verification commands used. (7 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Download the Installer:**
Visit the official Node.js website at `https://nodejs.org/en/download/`. Select the **LTS** (Long-Term Support) version, which is recommended for most users. Click on the **Windows Installer (.msi)** for 64-bit architecture. The file is approximately 30 MB.

**Step 2 — Run the Installer:**
Locate the downloaded `.msi` file (typically in the `Downloads` folder) and double-click to launch the installation wizard. Click *Next* on the welcome screen, accept the license agreement, and choose the installation destination (default: `C:\Program Files\nodejs\`).

**Step 3 — Custom Setup (Critical Step):**
Ensure the following components are selected for installation:
- Node.js runtime
- npm package manager
- **Add to PATH** (this is crucial — it allows the `node` and `npm` commands to work from any directory)

Optionally check *"Tools for Native Modules"* to install Chocolatey and Python for compiling native C++ addons.

**Step 4 — Install:**
Click *Install*. If prompted by User Account Control (UAC), click *Yes* to allow the installer to make system changes. Wait for the installation to complete (typically 1–2 minutes). Click *Finish*.

**Step 5 — Verify the Installation:**
Open **Command Prompt** or **PowerShell** and execute the following commands:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\texttt{C:\textbackslash Users\textbackslash Student> node -v} \\
&\texttt{v20.11.0} \\
&\texttt{C:\textbackslash Users\textbackslash Student> npm -v} \\
&\texttt{10.2.4}
\end{aligned}
$$

If both commands return version numbers, the installation is successful.

**Step 6 — First-Run Test:**
Create a file `test.js` containing `console.log("Node.js is working!");` and run `node test.js` in the terminal. If "Node.js is working!" is printed, the runtime is fully operational.

*(Steps 1–3: 3 marks. Step 4–5 verification: 2 marks. Step 6 validation: 2 marks. Total = 7 marks)*

---

#### (b) Write a Node.js program using the built-in `http` module to create a server that responds with "Welcome to KTU Web Development" on `localhost:3000`. Explain each line of code. (7 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

```javascript
// Step 1: Import the built-in http module
const http = require('http');

// Step 2: Define hostname and port
const hostname = '127.0.0.1';
const port = 3000;

// Step 3: Create the server using createServer()
const server = http.createServer((req, res) => {
  res.statusCode = 200;                       // HTTP 200 OK
  res.setHeader('Content-Type', 'text/plain'); // Response is plain text
  res.end('Welcome to KTU Web Development\n'); // Send response body
});

// Step 4: Start listening on the specified port and hostname
server.listen(port, hostname, () => {
  console.log(`Server running at http://${hostname}:${port}/`);
});
```

**Line-by-Line Explanation:**

- `const http = require('http');` → Imports Node.js's built-in **HTTP module**, which provides utilities to create HTTP servers and clients without any external dependencies.
- `const hostname = '127.0.0.1';` → Sets the host as `localhost` (IPv4 loopback address), meaning the server is accessible only from the local machine.
- `const port = 3000;` → Declares the TCP port (3000) on which the server will listen for incoming connections.
- `http.createServer((req, res) => { ... });` → The factory function returns a server object. The callback receives two arguments: `req` (incoming request) and `res` (outgoing response).
- `res.statusCode = 200;` → Sets the HTTP status code to **200 OK**, indicating a successful response.
- `res.setHeader('Content-Type', 'text/plain');` → Specifies that the response body is plain text, instructing the browser to render it as such.
- `res.end('Welcome to KTU Web Development\n');` → Finalizes the response, sending the message string to the client.
- `server.listen(port, hostname, callback);` → Binds the server to the port and host. The callback is invoked once the server is ready.

**Execution:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\texttt{C:\textbackslash node hello.js} \\
&\texttt{Server running at http://127.0.0.1:3000/}
\end{aligned}
$$

Open a browser and visit `http://localhost:3000/` to see the message.

*(Code: 4 marks. Line-by-line explanation: 3 marks. Total = 7 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) Differentiate between LTS and Current versions of Node.js. Which one is recommended for production deployment, and why? (7 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | LTS (Long-Term Support) | Current |
|---|---|---|
| **Stability** | Highly stable; bug fixes prioritized | Cutting-edge; may contain experimental features |
| **Support Duration** | **30 months** (active + maintenance) | Approximately 6 months until next Current release |
| **Release Cadence** | Every ~12 months | Every 6 months |
| **Target Audience** | Enterprise, production, students | Early adopters, framework authors |
| **New Features** | Includes only stable, tested features | Includes all new features (some may be experimental) |
| **Recommended For** | Production, KTU lab projects | Learning, experimentation |

**Recommendation:** **LTS is recommended for production deployment.**

**Reasons:**
1. **Long-term security patches:** LTS versions receive security updates for ~30 months, crucial for protecting deployed applications.
2. **Stability guarantee:** New features are thoroughly battle-tested before being merged into an LTS line, reducing the risk of breaking changes.
3. **Predictable upgrade path:** Enterprises can plan upgrades without sudden incompatibilities in dependencies.
4. **Ecosystem compatibility:** Most npm packages and frameworks (Express, Koa, NestJS) optimize their support for the active LTS line.

*(Comparison table: 4 marks. Recommendation with reasoning: 3 marks. Total = 7 marks)*

---

#### (b) Explain the role of the `package.json` file in a Node.js project. Describe the purpose of the following fields: `name`, `version`, `main`, `scripts`, `dependencies`, and `devDependencies`. (7 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

The **`package.json`** file is the **heart (manifest)** of any Node.js project. It is a JSON-formatted metadata file that describes the project's identity, dependencies, scripts, configuration, and entry points. It is generated (or pre-exists) when `npm init` is run, and it is the single source of truth for project information.

**Sample `package.json`:**
```json
{
  "name": "ktu-web-app",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "main": "index.js",
  "scripts": {
    "start": "node index.js",
    "test": "echo \"No tests yet\""
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "express": "^4.18.2"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "nodemon": "^3.0.1"
  }
}
```

**Field-by-Field Explanation:**

- **`name`** → The unique name of the project. Must be lowercase, no spaces. Used when publishing to npm registry.
- **`version`** → Follows the **Semantic Versioning (SemVer)** format: `MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH` (e.g., `1.0.0`). Tracks release history.
- **`main`** → The entry-point file executed when the package is `require()`-ed or when `npm start` is run.
- **`scripts`** → Custom CLI commands defined by the developer. For example, `npm start` runs `node index.js`. `npm test` runs the test command.
- **`dependencies`** → Production dependencies required for the app to run. Installed via `npm install <package>`. Recorded with version ranges (e.g., `^4.18.2`).
- **`devDependencies`** → Development-only dependencies (testing tools, linters, build tools). Not installed in production. Added via `npm install --save-dev <package>`.

**Importance:**
1. Enables reproducible installations via `npm install` (reads the file and installs all listed dependencies).
2. Acts as a project documentation card.
3. Required for publishing the package to the npm registry.
4. Facilitates collaboration — any developer can clone the project and run `npm install` to get the exact same environment.

*(Definition + sample: 2 marks. Field explanations: 4 marks. Importance: 1 mark. Total = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Node.js with a framework** — Students often write "Node.js is a framework like Angular or React." This is **wrong**. It is a *runtime environment*.
> 2. **Forgetting `require('http')`** — A very common mistake is writing `http.createServer()` without first importing the http module.
> 3. **Wrong command for global vs local** — Writing `npm install express -g` in a project-specific scenario (or vice versa) loses marks.
> 4. **Mixing up `dependencies` and `devDependencies`** — `devDependencies` are for development tools only (e.g., testing libraries), not runtime packages.
> 5. **Not mentioning LTS** — Examiners often award a bonus mark for explicitly stating that LTS is recommended for production.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Installing Node.js:**

- ✅ **Node.js** is a **JavaScript runtime environment**, NOT a language and NOT a framework — built on Chrome's **V8 engine**.
- ✅ The **V8 engine** compiles JavaScript to native machine code using **Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation** for high-speed execution.
- ✅ Node.js is **single-threaded** but uses an **event-driven, non-blocking I/O model** for high scalability.
- ✅ Installation sources: `https://nodejs.org` (official); supports **Windows (.msi)**, **macOS (.pkg / Homebrew)**, **Linux (APT / NodeSource)**.
- ✅ **LTS (Long-Term Support)** versions are recommended for production; **Current** versions are for experimentation.
- ✅ LTS support duration: **~30 months** (active + maintenance).
- ✅ Verification commands: `node -v` and `npm -v` — both must return version strings.
- ✅ **PATH** environment variable is auto-configured by the installer to make `node` and `npm` globally callable.
- ✅ `npm` = **Node Package Manager**, bundled automatically with Node.js.
- ✅ `npm init -y` creates a default `package.json`; `-y` accepts all default values.
- ✅ `npm install <pkg>` = **local** installation (project-scoped, in `node_modules/`).
- ✅ `npm install -g <pkg>` = **global** installation (system-wide, typically CLI tools).
- ✅ `npm install --save-dev <pkg>` adds to **`devDependencies`** (development-only).
- ✅ `package.json` is the project's manifest: contains `name`, `version`, `main`, `scripts`, `dependencies`, `devDependencies`.
- ✅ The built-in **`http` module** is used to create web servers without any external framework: `require('http')`, `http.createServer()`, `server.listen(port, host, callback)`.
- ✅ Default development port: **3000**; default localhost IP: **127.0.0.1**.
- ✅ The `res.statusCode = 200` indicates **HTTP OK**; `res.setHeader('Content-Type', ...)` configures response MIME type.
- ✅ Node.js forms the **N** in the popular **MERN** and **MEAN** full-stack technology stacks.
- ✅ **JavaScript executes client-side (browser)** traditionally; **Node.js enables server-side JavaScript execution**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating First Server
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Creating First Server # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Web Server (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *web server* is a software application (or hardware system) that accepts incoming **HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol)** requests from client applications (browsers, mobile apps, IoT devices) over a network, processes them, and returns **HTTP responses** containing the requested resources (HTML pages, JSON data, images, files) along with appropriate **status codes** and **headers**. In the context of **Node.js** (which is the typical stack used in the KTU PBITT504 syllabus), a server is created using the built-in **`http`** module, which is part of Node's standard library and requires no external installation. The two most fundamental methods used are: - **`http.createServer(callback)`** → Creates an instance of the server. - **`server.listen(port, hostname, callback)`** → Binds the server to a specific port and starts listening for incoming connections. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 3):** *Creating First Server* is the foundational building block of backend development. The student must understand how a **request event** triggers a callback, how the **response object** is constructed, and how the server is **listening** on a specific port number (default: **3000** or **8080**). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Waiter" Imagine a busy restaurant: | Real-World Element | Web Server Equivalent | |---|---| | 🍽️ The **Restaurant** itself | The **Node.js Server Process** running in memory | | 👨‍🍳 The **Waiter** who takes orders | The **HTTP Server** created by `http.createServer()` | | 📝 The **Customer's Order** | The **HTTP Request** (`req`) from a browser | | 🍕 The **Food Delivered** | The **HTTP Response** (`res`) sent back | | 🚪 The **Front Door** | The **Port Number** (e.g., 3000) where the server "stands" | | 🗣️ The **Waiter's Notepad** | The **Callback Function** triggered per request | > **The flow:** A customer walks in (request hits the port) → the waiter takes the order (callback executes) → food is prepared (server processes) → food is served back (response sent). The waiter then **waits again** for the next customer — this is the **event loop** in action. --- ## 1.3 Key Terminology (Syllus Vocabulary) - **Host / Hostname:** The address where the server runs. For local machines, this is typically `127.0.0.1` (loopback) or simply `localhost`. - **Port:** A **16-bit number (0–65535)** that identifies a specific process on a host. Web servers commonly use **port 80 (HTTP)**, **443 (HTTPS)**, or development ports like **3000**, **8000**, **8080**. - **Request Object (`req`):** Contains metadata about the incoming request — URL, HTTP method, headers, and body. - **Response Object (`res`):** Used by the server to construct and send data back to the client. - **Event-Driven Architecture:** Node.js servers do not run sequentially for each user; they listen for **events** (like `request`, `connection`, `close`) and react to them asynchronously. > [!TIP] > **Why Node.js for servers?** Traditional servers (like Apache) create a new thread per request. Node.js uses a **single-threaded event loop**, making it extremely efficient for **I/O-heavy** web applications — a key reason it dominates modern backend development. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Request–Response Lifecycle (Operational Breakdown) When a user types `http://localhost:3000/` in a browser, the following sequence occurs: 1. **DNS Resolution** → The browser resolves `localhost` to the IP `127.0.0.1`. 2. **TCP Handshake** → A TCP connection is established between client and server on **port 3000**. 3. **HTTP Request Sent** → The browser sends an HTTP GET request: ```http GET / HTTP/1.1 Host: localhost:3000 User-Agent: Chrome/120.0 Accept: text/html ``` 4. **Server Event Triggered** → Node.js fires the `request` event → your callback executes. 5. **Response Constructed** → The server builds an HTTP response with status code, headers, and body. 6. **Data Streamed Back** → Response is sent over the same TCP connection. 7. **Connection Handled** → Either closed or kept alive (HTTP keep-alive). --- ## 2.2 The `http.createServer()` Mechanism — Stepwise Logic ### Why `createServer()`? It returns a new instance of `http.Server` — an `EventEmitter` that emits a `'request'` event **every time** a client connects. ### Core Components: | Component | Purpose | Mandatory? | |---|---|---| | `http` module | Provides HTTP server & client functionality | ✅ Yes | | `createServer(reqListener)` | Creates server with optional request listener | ✅ Yes | | `req` (IncomingMessage) | Readable stream of the incoming request | ✅ Yes | | `res` (ServerResponse) | Writable stream used to send the response | ✅ Yes | | `res.writeHead(status, headers)` | Sets HTTP status code & response headers | Recommended | | `res.end(data)` | Sends response body and closes the stream | ✅ Yes | | `server.listen(port, host, callback)` | Binds to port and starts listening | ✅ Yes | --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is **exam-critical**. Memorize the status codes and standard ports. | Concept | Value / Rule | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---| | Port Number Range | $0 \leq \text{port} \leq 65535$ | 16-bit unsigned integer ($2^{16} - 1$) | | Reserved Ports | $0$ to $1023$ | Requires root/admin privileges | | Registered Ports | $1024$ to $49151$ | For user applications | | Dynamic/Private Ports | $49152$ to $65535$ | Ephemeral, OS-assigned | | Standard HTTP Port | **80** | Default for web servers | | Standard HTTPS Port | **443** | TLS/SSL encrypted | | Common Dev Ports | **3000, 8000, 8080** | Used in tutorials & frameworks | | Localhost IP | **127.0.0.1** | Loopback address (`\vert x \vert$-style notation in IPv4) | | HTTP Methods | `GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH, HEAD, OPTIONS` | 7 standard verbs (RFC 7231) | | Status Code 2xx | **Success** | e.g., 200 OK | | Status Code 3xx | **Redirection** | e.g., 301 Moved Permanently | | Status Code 4xx | **Client Error** | e.g., 404 Not Found | | Status Code 5xx | **Server Error** | e.g., 500 Internal Server Error | | Response MIME Type | `text/html`, `application/json`, `text/plain` | Set via `Content-Type` header | --- ## 2.4 Status Code Reference (High-Yield for KTU) | Code | Name | Meaning | |---|---|---| | 200 | OK | Request succeeded | | 201 | Created | Resource successfully created (POST) | | 301 | Moved Permanently | Resource moved to a new URL | | 400 | Bad Request | Malformed client request | | 401 | Unauthorized | Authentication required | | 403 | Forbidden | Server refuses to authorize | | 404 | Not Found | Resource does not exist | | 500 | Internal Server Error | Generic server-side failure | | 503 | Service Unavailable | Server overloaded or under maintenance | > [!TIP] > **Engineering Utility:** Status codes allow the client (browser, API consumer) to programmatically decide what to do next — render an error page, retry the request, or redirect to a login screen. They form the **contract** of the HTTP protocol. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Utility Creating a first server is the entry point to: - **REST API Development** (Express.js, Fastify frameworks build on top of this) - **Microservices Architecture** (each service runs its own lightweight Node server) - **Real-time Applications** (chat apps using WebSockets, built on `http.Server`) - **Serverless & Edge Computing** (AWS Lambda, Cloudflare Workers all start with the same HTTP fundamentals) - **IoT Backends** (Node servers handle millions of lightweight device requests efficiently) # 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Walkthrough ## 3.1 Environment Setup (Prerequisite Steps) Before writing the server, the following must be in place: 1. **Install Node.js** from [nodejs.org](https://nodejs.org). Verify with: ```bash node -v npm -v ``` 2. **Create a project folder** and initialize: ```bash mkdir my-first-server cd my-first-server npm init -y ``` 3. **Create the main file** `server.js`. --- ## 3.2 Building the First Server — Exhaustive Line-by-Line Derivation Below is the **canonical "Hello World" Node.js server**, with every single line explained. ```javascript // server.js // Step 1: Import the built-in http module const http = require('http'); // Step 2: Define the port number as a constant const PORT = 3000; const HOST = '127.0.0.1'; // Step 3: Create the server using createServer() const server = http.createServer((req, res) => { // Step 3a: Set the HTTP status code and response headers res.writeHead(200, { 'Content-Type': 'text/html' }); // Step 3b: Construct the response body res.write('

Welcome to My First Node.js Server!

'); res.write('

You requested: ' + req.url + '

'); // Step 3c: End the response and send it to the client res.end('

Server running successfully.

'); }); // Step 4: Start the server and make it listen on the specified port server.listen(PORT, HOST, () => { console.log(`Server is running at http://${HOST}:${PORT}/`); }); ``` --- ### 3.3 Step-by-Step Analytical Explanation #### **Step 1: `const http = require('http');`** - `require()` is Node.js's **CommonJS module loading function**. - It loads the `http` module from Node's standard library (no `npm install` needed). - The returned object contains methods like `createServer`, `request`, `get`, etc. #### **Step 2: Defining Constants** - `PORT = 3000` and `HOST = '127.0.0.1'` are extracted as constants for **readability, maintainability, and configurability** (a 12-factor app principle). - Using a constant avoids **magic numbers** in the code. #### **Step 3: `http.createServer((req, res) => { ... })`** - This function takes a **request listener callback**. - The callback is invoked **every time** a new HTTP request hits the server. - It receives two arguments: - `req` → An `IncomingMessage` object (Readable Stream). - `res` → A `ServerResponse` object (Writable Stream). #### **Step 3a: `res.writeHead(200, { 'Content-Type': 'text/html' });`** - `200` is the **HTTP status code** indicating success. - The second argument is an object of **response headers**. - `Content-Type: text/html` tells the browser to interpret the body as HTML. #### **Step 3b: `res.write(...)`** - Writes a chunk of data to the response body. - Can be called **multiple times** before `res.end()`. - Each `write()` appends to the response stream. #### **Step 3c: `res.end(...)`** - **Mandatory.** Signals the end of the response. - Flushes any buffered data to the client. - Closes the response stream. - May optionally take a final chunk of data to write. > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall:** Forgetting to call `res.end()` will cause the browser to **hang indefinitely** waiting for the response to complete. This is one of the most common bugs for beginners. #### **Step 4: `server.listen(PORT, HOST, callback)`** - **Three-argument form**: `(port, hostname, callback)`. - The server begins listening for TCP connections. - The callback fires **once**, when the server has successfully bound to the port. - If the port is **already in use**, an `EADDRINUSE` error is thrown. --- ## 3.4 Mathematical Model of Port Binding The port binding operation can be modeled as a function: $$ \text{bind}: (\text{server}, \text{port}, \text{host}) \rightarrow \text{ListeningSocket} $$ Where the OS kernel maintains a mapping: $$ \text{ListeningSocket} = \{ (host_i, port_i) \mid \text{active server } i \} $$ For uniqueness: $$ \forall i \neq j : (host_i, port_i) \neq (host_j, port_j) $$ This is why two servers cannot share the same port on the same host simultaneously. --- ## 3.5 Extended Example: Routing Based on URL A more practical server distinguishes URLs: ```javascript const http = require('http'); const server = http.createServer((req, res) => { res.writeHead(200, { 'Content-Type': 'text/plain' }); if (req.url === '/' || req.url === '/home') { res.end('Welcome to the Home Page'); } else if (req.url === '/about') { res.end('About Us Page'); } else if (req.url === '/api/data') { res.writeHead(200, { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }); res.end(JSON.stringify({ name: 'KTU', scheme: '2024' })); } else { res.writeHead(404, { 'Content-Type': 'text/plain' }); res.end('404 - Page Not Found'); } }); server.listen(3000, '127.0.0.1', () => { console.log('Server live on http://127.0.0.1:3000'); }); ``` ### Line-by-Line Logic: - `req.url` contains the **path portion** of the URL (e.g., `/about`). - The `if-else` chain performs **basic routing**. - `JSON.stringify()` converts a JavaScript object into a JSON-formatted string for API responses. - Setting `Content-Type: application/json` is **mandatory** for JSON APIs — browsers and API clients rely on this header to parse correctly. - For unknown URLs, the server returns a **404** status — a hallmark of proper HTTP behavior. --- ## 3.6 Running the Server ```bash node server.js ``` **Expected Output:** ``` Server is running at http://127.0.0.1:3000/ ``` Open a browser and navigate to `http://localhost:3000/` to see the response. **To stop the server:** Press `Ctrl + C` in the terminal. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Client-Server Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Client Browser] -->|1. HTTP Request via TCP| B[Network Layer] B -->|2. Routed to Port 3000| C[Node.js HTTP Server] C -->|3. Trigger request event| D[Request Listener Callback] D -->|4. Process Request| E[Application Logic] E -->|5. Build Response| F[res object] F -->|6. HTTP Response sent back| B B -->|7. Render in Browser| A style A fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style C fill:#F5A623,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style D fill:#7ED321,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style E fill:#9013FE,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style F fill:#D0021B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` --- ## 4.2 HTTP Request-Response Cycle (Detailed Sequence) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Browser as Client Browser participant TCP as TCP/IP Stack participant Server as Node.js HTTP Server participant Callback as Request Callback participant FS as File System / Logic Browser->>TCP: GET /index.html HTTP/1.1 TCP->>Server: Connection on port 3000 Server->>Callback: Emit 'request' event Callback->>FS: Read file / Process data FS-->>Callback: Return data Callback->>Server: res.writeHead(200) + res.end(data) Server->>TCP: HTTP Response TCP->>Browser: Render HTML Note over Browser,Server: Connection closed (or kept alive) ``` --- ## 4.3 Server Lifecycle Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Node Process Starts]) --> Import[Import http module] Import --> Create[http.createServer with callback] Create --> Listen[server.listen PORT HOST] Listen --> Bind{Port Available?} Bind -->|No| Error[EADDRINUSE Error] Bind -->|Yes| Active[Server Listening] Active --> Wait[Wait for request event] Wait --> Trigger{New Request?} Trigger -->|Yes| Execute[Execute Callback with req, res] Execute --> Process[Process logic and build response] Process --> Send[res.end and send to client] Send --> Wait Trigger -->|No| Wait Error --> Stop([Server Crashed]) Send --> CtrlC[User presses Ctrl+C] CtrlC --> Close[server.close] Close --> End([Server Stopped]) style Start fill:#9013FE,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style Active fill:#7ED321,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style Execute fill:#F5A623,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style Error fill:#D0021B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style End fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` --- ## 4.4 Request Object Structure (Internal Anatomy) ```mermaid graph TB ReqObj[req: IncomingMessage Object] ReqObj --> URL[req.url: /about] ReqObj --> Method[req.method: GET / POST / PUT] ReqObj --> Headers[req.headers: Host, User-Agent, Accept] ReqObj --> HTTPVersion[req.httpVersion: HTTP/1.1] ReqObj --> Socket[req.socket: TCP connection info] ReqObj --> Body[req.on data: For POST body] ResObj[res: ServerResponse Object] ResObj --> WriteHead[res.writeHead status, headers] ResObj --> SetHeader[res.setHeader name, value] ResObj --> Write[res.write chunk] ResObj --> End[res.end finalChunk] ResObj --> StatusCode[res.statusCode] style ReqObj fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style ResObj fill:#F5A623,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Explain the role of the `http` module in Node.js. How is `http.createServer()` used to instantiate a server?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The `http` module is a **built-in Node.js module** that provides functionality to create HTTP servers and clients. It does not require any external installation via npm. `http.createServer()` is used to create a new HTTP server instance. It accepts an optional **request listener callback** with the signature `(req, res) => { ... }`. The callback is invoked for every incoming HTTP request, where `req` is the `IncomingMessage` object representing the client's request and `res` is the `ServerResponse` object used to construct and send the response. The returned server object is then bound to a port using `server.listen()`. **(3 marks)** --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is the significance of the `res.end()` method in a Node.js HTTP server? What happens if it is not called?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The `res.end()` method **finalizes the HTTP response**. It signals to the client that no more data will be sent, flushes any buffered content, and closes the response stream. It may also accept an optional final data chunk. **(2 marks)** If `res.end()` is not called, the response is never completed, and the client (browser) will **hang indefinitely** waiting for the response, eventually leading to a **request timeout error**. This is one of the most common bugs in Node.js server code. **(1 mark)** --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **client-server architecture** and the **HTTP request-response cycle** in web applications. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a Node.js program to create a basic HTTP server that runs on **port 3000** and responds differently to the following URLs: `/`, `/about`, and `/contact`. For any other URL, it should return a **404 Not Found** page. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for (a):** The **client-server architecture** is a distributed model in which the **client** (typically a web browser) sends requests to a **server** (a remote machine running server software), which processes the request and returns the appropriate response. **Components:** - **Client:** Initiates requests. Examples: Chrome, Firefox, Postman. - **Server:** Listens on a specific port and handles requests. Example: Node.js HTTP server. - **Network:** The communication medium (TCP/IP). - **Protocol:** HTTP/HTTPS defines the rules of communication. **HTTP Request-Response Cycle:** 1. The user enters a URL in the browser. 2. The browser resolves the hostname via DNS to obtain the IP address. 3. A **TCP connection** is established via a three-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK). 4. The browser sends an **HTTP request** (method, URL, headers, optional body). 5. The server receives the request and triggers its **request event handler**. 6. The server processes the request and constructs an **HTTP response** (status code, headers, body). 7. The response is sent back over the same TCP connection. 8. The browser renders the response (HTML, JSON, etc.). 9. The connection is closed or kept alive for further requests. *(Attach the diagram from Section 4.1 or 4.2 for full marks.)* **(7 marks — 2 for diagram, 5 for explanation)** --- #### **Model Solution for (b):** ```javascript // server.js const http = require('http'); const PORT = 3000; const HOST = '127.0.0.1'; const server = http.createServer((req, res) => { res.setHeader('Content-Type', 'text/html'); if (req.url === '/' || req.url === '/home') { res.writeHead(200); res.end('

Welcome to Home Page

'); } else if (req.url === '/about') { res.writeHead(200); res.end('

About Us

We are a KTU student project.

'); } else if (req.url === '/contact') { res.writeHead(200); res.end('

Contact Page

Email: student@ktu.ac.in

'); } else { res.writeHead(404); res.end('

404 - Page Not Found

'); } }); server.listen(PORT, HOST, () => { console.log(`Server is running at http://${HOST}:${PORT}/`); }); ``` **Explanation of Key Parts:** - `require('http')` → Loads the HTTP module. **(0.5 marks)** - `http.createServer((req, res) => {...})` → Creates the server with a request listener. **(1 mark)** - `req.url` → Extracts the path component from the request URL. **(1 mark)** - `res.setHeader('Content-Type', 'text/html')` → Sets the MIME type so the browser renders HTML correctly. **(0.5 marks)** - `res.writeHead(200)` and `res.writeHead(404)` → Set the appropriate HTTP status codes. **(1 mark)** - `res.end(...)` → Sends the final HTML content to the client. **(0.5 marks)** - `server.listen(PORT, HOST, callback)` → Starts the server on the specified port and host. **(1 mark)** - The fallback `else` block ensures proper **404 handling** for unknown routes. **(1 mark)** - Proper code formatting and comments. **(0.5 marks)** **(Total: 7 marks)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the **event-driven architecture** of Node.js. How does it relate to the HTTP server's request handling? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a Node.js HTTP server that: - Listens on **port 8080** - Responds to `GET /` with the message **"Hello, KTU Student!"** in **plain text** - Responds to `GET /json` with a **JSON object** containing your name, course, and semester - Logs **every incoming request** (method + URL) to the console **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for (a):** Node.js uses an **event-driven, non-blocking I/O architecture** built on top of Google's V8 JavaScript engine. The core concept is the **event loop**, a continuously running loop that: 1. Listens for events (e.g., incoming HTTP requests, file system changes, timer expirations). 2. When an event fires, executes the corresponding **callback function** registered for that event. 3. Returns to waiting for the next event. **Relation to HTTP Server:** - When `http.createServer()` is called, an `EventEmitter` object is created. - The `server.listen()` call registers the server with the OS as listening for incoming connections. - Each incoming HTTP request triggers a `'request'` event. - The callback function registered via `createServer()` is invoked **asynchronously** by the event loop. - This allows Node.js to handle **thousands of concurrent connections** with a single thread, making it highly efficient for I/O-bound web workloads. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for (b):** ```javascript // server2.js const http = require('http'); const PORT = 8080; const HOST = '127.0.0.1'; const server = http.createServer((req, res) => { // Log every incoming request console.log(`[${new Date().toISOString()}] ${req.method} ${req.url}`); if (req.url === '/' && req.method === 'GET') { res.writeHead(200, { 'Content-Type': 'text/plain' }); res.end('Hello, KTU Student!'); } else if (req.url === '/json' && req.method === 'GET') { const data = { name: 'Your Name Here', course: 'B.Tech CSE', semester: 'S5' }; res.writeHead(200, { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }); res.end(JSON.stringify(data)); } else { res.writeHead(404, { 'Content-Type': 'text/plain' }); res.end('404 Not Found'); } }); server.listen(PORT, HOST, () => { console.log(`Server live at http://${HOST}:${PORT}/`); }); ``` **Explanation:** - `console.log(...)` is placed at the start of the callback so **every** request is logged. **(1 mark)** - `req.method === 'GET'` ensures the routes only respond to GET requests. **(1 mark)** - For `/json`, the object is converted to a string using `JSON.stringify()`. **(1 mark)** - `Content-Type: application/json` is set for proper JSON handling. **(1 mark)** - For `/`, plain text response is sent. **(1 mark)** - 404 fallback for all other routes. **(1 mark)** - Proper use of `server.listen()` with port 8080. **(1 mark)** **(Total: 7 marks)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting `res.end()`** → The browser will time out. Examiners will deduct **1–2 marks** if you mention writing the code but not ending the response. > 2. **Wrong port number** → Students often write `port 80` or `port 443` without understanding that these require admin/root privileges. Always use `3000`, `8000`, or `8080` for development. > 3. **Missing `Content-Type` header** → Without it, browsers may display raw HTML or refuse to render JSON. Always set it explicitly. > 4. **Confusing `res.write()` with `res.end()`** → `res.write()` is for **partial** data; `res.end()` is **mandatory** to terminate the response. > 5. **Not handling 404 routes** → Examiners look for graceful handling of unknown URLs with proper status codes. > 6. **Synchronous thinking** → Some students use blocking calls (like `fs.readFileSync`) inside the server, defeating the purpose of Node.js's event-driven model. Always use **async/non-blocking** operations. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Creating First Server":** - ⭐ The `http` module is a **core, built-in** Node.js module — no `npm install` required. - ⭐ `http.createServer(callback)` returns a new HTTP server instance and **registers** the request listener. - ⭐ The callback signature is `(req, res)` where `req` is `IncomingMessage` and `res` is `ServerResponse`. - ⭐ `server.listen(port, host, callback)` **binds** the server to a port and starts listening. - ⭐ Default dev ports: **3000, 8000, 8080**. Avoid reserved ports (0–1023) unless running as root. - ⭐ **`res.end()` is mandatory** — omitting it causes the client to hang. - ⭐ `res.writeHead(statusCode, headers)` sets the status code and headers in one call. - ⭐ `Content-Type` header is **critical** for proper rendering: `text/html`, `text/plain`, `application/json`. - ⭐ HTTP status code groups: **2xx (success), 3xx (redirect), 4xx (client error), 5xx (server error)**. - ⭐ Node.js uses a **single-threaded event loop** — do not perform blocking operations inside the request handler. - ⭐ The `request` event is emitted for **every** incoming HTTP request automatically by the server. - ⭐ Common request properties: `req.url`, `req.method`, `req.headers`, `req.httpVersion`. - ⭐ Always handle **404 Not Found** for unmatched routes — it's good HTTP citizenship and earns marks. - ⭐ Use `JSON.stringify()` to send JSON responses and set `Content-Type: application/json`. - ⭐ Stop the server with `Ctrl + C` in the terminal, or programmatically with `server.close()`. - ⭐ KTU exam tip: **Draw the client-server diagram** whenever the question involves servers — visual aids earn easy partial credit. --- > **End of Module 3 Notes — Creating First Server** ✅

topics.title: Request and Response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Request and Response # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **HTTP Request and Response** form the fundamental transactional unit of the **HyperText Transfer Protocol (HTTP)** — an application-layer request-response protocol operating over **TCP/IP** (default port **80** for HTTP, **443** for HTTPS) that governs how clients (typically web browsers) and servers exchange hypermedia documents and structured data. In the **client-server model**, every web interaction consists of a pair: - An **HTTP Request** — the message sent *from* the client *to* the server. - An **HTTP Response** — the message sent *back* from the server *to* the client. The **Request-Response Cycle** is a **stateless**, **synchronous**, and **half-duplex** exchange. HTTP is officially standardized by the **IETF (Internet Engineering Task Force)** under **RFC 9110** (HTTP Semantics, 2022) and **RFC 9112** (HTTP/1.1 Message Framing). > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 of PBITT504 focuses on server-side scripting, where mastering request intake and response generation is the *core competency* tested in the End Semester Examination (ESE). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Order System > [!TIP] > Imagine a **restaurant**: > - **You (the Client)** sit at a table and call the **waiter (the network/HTTP layer)**. > - You hand over a written **order slip (the HTTP Request)** specifying: dish name (URL), preparation method (HTTP Method), special instructions (Headers), and any items you already brought (Request Body). > - The **kitchen (the Web Server)** processes the order and sends back a **tray (the HTTP Response)** with the food (Response Body), a **bill (Status Code: 200 OK / 404 Not Found)**, and **receipt details (Headers)** like "Prepared by Chef" (Server) or "Allergy info" (Content-Type). Just as a restaurant cannot prepare a meal without a clear order, a web server cannot generate a page without a well-formed HTTP Request. ## 1.3 Anatomy of the Request-Response Pair — At a Glance | Component | In Request | In Response | |---|---|---| | **Start Line** | Request Line: Method + URI + HTTP-Version | Status Line: HTTP-Version + Status-Code + Reason-Phrase | | **Headers** | Host, User-Agent, Accept, Cookie, Authorization | Content-Type, Set-Cookie, Location, Cache-Control | | **Empty Line** | `\r\n\r\n` separator | `\r\n\r\n` separator | | **Body** | Optional (POST/PUT/PATCH data) | Optional (HTML, JSON, file, etc.) | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **CRLF (Carriage Return + Line Feed, i.e., `\r\n`)** sequence is the official HTTP message separator as mandated by **RFC 9112**. The empty line between headers and body is *non-negotiable* — without it, servers treat headers as part of the body and parsing fails. ## 1.4 HTTP Version Evolution (KTU Must-Know) > [!NOTE] > **Version Comparison Table** | Version | Year | Key Feature | Transport | |---|---|---|---| | **HTTP/0.9** | 1991 | Single-line GET only, HTML only | TCP | | **HTTP/1.0** | 1996 | Headers, status codes, multiple methods | TCP | | **HTTP/1.1** | 1997 | Persistent connections, chunked transfer, pipelining | TCP | | **HTTP/2** | 2015 | Binary framing, multiplexing, header compression (HPACK) | TCP + TLS | | **HTTP/3** | 2022 | **QUIC** protocol (UDP-based), 0-RTT handshakes | QUIC (UDP) | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The HTTP Request — Structural Breakdown > [!NOTE] > **Definition:** An HTTP Request is a text-based (in HTTP/1.1) message sent by the client to a server to perform an action on a resource identified by a **URI (Uniform Resource Identifier)**. ### 2.1.1 The Request Line The first line of any HTTP request has three space-separated components: ``` METHOD SP REQUEST-TARGET SP HTTP-VERSION CRLF ``` For example: ``` GET /index.html HTTP/1.1 ``` ### 2.1.2 Request Methods (Verbs) — The Action Vocabulary > [!IMPORTANT] > **RFC 9110 (HTTP Semantics)** classifies methods as **Safe**, **Idempotent**, and **Cacheable**. KTU exams frequently test these classifications. | Method | Purpose | Safe? | Idempotent? | Has Body? | KTU Frequency | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **GET** | Retrieve a resource representation | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ Typically no | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **POST** | Submit data / create resource / trigger action | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **PUT** | Replace a resource entirely | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **DELETE** | Remove a resource | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ❌ Usually no | ⭐⭐⭐ | | **PATCH** | Partial modification of a resource | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ⭐⭐⭐ | | **HEAD** | Same as GET but no response body | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ⭐⭐ | | **OPTIONS** | Describe communication options (CORS preflight) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ⭐⭐ | | **TRACE** | Echo-back diagnostic | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ⭐ | | **CONNECT** | Establish tunnel (used for HTTPS proxy) | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | ⭐ | > [!TIP] > **Engineering Mnemonic — "Get Safe Pizza"** → **GET** = **S**afe, **POST** = not idempotent, **PUT** = **I**dempotent but not safe. Memorize this for the 3-mark definition questions. ### 2.1.3 The Request URI (Request-Target) Four forms are permitted by **RFC 9112 §3.2**: 1. **Origin Form** — `/path?query` (most common, used by clients) 2. **Absolute Form** — `https://example.com/path` (used by proxies) 3. **Authority Form** — `host:port` (used with CONNECT method) 4. **Asterisk Form** — `*` (used with OPTIONS, applies to whole server) ### 2.1.4 Request Headers — The Metadata Envelope Headers are key-value pairs (case-insensitive in HTTP/1.1+). They are grouped into categories: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Major Request Header Categories (KTU High-Yield)** | Category | Examples | Function | |---|---|---| | **General** | `Date`, `Connection` | Apply to both request/response | | **Representation** | `Accept`, `Accept-Language`, `Accept-Encoding` | Client's content negotiation | | **Request Context** | `Host`, `User-Agent`, `Referer`, `From` | Info about the client | | **Authentication** | `Authorization`, `Cookie` | Credentials / session | | **Conditional** | `If-Match`, `If-None-Match`, `If-Modified-Since` | Cache validation | | **Body-related** | `Content-Type`, `Content-Length`, `Transfer-Encoding` | Describe payload | ### 2.1.5 The Request Body (Payload / Entity) Carried in methods like **POST**, **PUT**, **PATCH**. Common content types: | MIME Type | Use Case | |---|---| | `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` | HTML form submission (default) | | `multipart/form-data` | File uploads | | `application/json` | REST API payloads | | `application/xml` | SOAP, older APIs | | `text/plain` | Raw text | ## 2.2 The HTTP Response — Structural Breakdown > [!NOTE] > **Definition:** An HTTP Response is the message a server sends back to a client after processing a request, comprising a status line, headers, an empty line, and an optional body. ### 2.2.1 The Status Line ``` HTTP-VERSION SP STATUS-CODE SP REASON-PHRASE CRLF ``` For example: ``` HTTP/1.1 200 OK ``` ### 2.2.2 Status Code Classification (The Five Classes) > [!IMPORTANT] > **RFC 9110 §15 — Status Code Classes** | Class | Range | Category | KTU Priority | |---|---|---|---| | **1xx** | 100–199 | **Informational** — interim response | ⭐⭐ | | **2xx** | 200–299 | **Success** — request received and accepted | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **3xx** | 300–399 | **Redirection** — further action needed | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **4xx** | 400–499 | **Client Error** — fault in the request | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **5xx** | 500–599 | **Server Error** — server failed to fulfill valid request | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | > [!TIP] > **The "1-2-3-4-5 Success-Redirection-Client-Server" Rule:** 1=inform, 2=success, 3=redirect, 4=client's fault, 5=server's fault. Examiners love asking students to *classify* a code into its class. ### 2.2.3 The Top 15 Status Codes KTU Will Test (with Meanings) | Code | Reason Phrase | Meaning | KTU Marks Weight | |---|---|---|---| | **200** | OK | Standard success for GET, PUT, PATCH | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **201** | Created | Resource successfully created (POST) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **204** | No Content | Success but no body to return (e.g., DELETE) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **301** | Moved Permanently | Resource URI changed forever (SEO redirect) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **302** | Found | Temporary redirect | ⭐⭐⭐ | | **304** | Not Modified | Use cached copy (cache validation) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **307** | Temporary Redirect | Preserves HTTP method on redirect | ⭐⭐⭐ | | **308** | Permanent Redirect | Preserves method, permanent | ⭐⭐ | | **400** | Bad Request | Malformed syntax | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **401** | Unauthorized | Authentication required | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **403** | Forbidden | Server refuses (auth not enough) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **404** | Not Found | Resource does not exist | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **405** | Method Not Allowed | Method not supported on resource | ⭐⭐⭐ | | **409** | Conflict | State conflict (e.g., duplicate entry) | ⭐⭐⭐ | | **500** | Internal Server Error | Generic server crash | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **503** | Service Unavailable | Server overloaded / maintenance | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Student Pitfall:** Confusing **401 Unauthorized** with **403 Forbidden**. **401 = "Who are you?" (Authenticate first)**, **403 = "I know who you are, but you can't do this" (Authorized but not permitted).** ### 2.2.4 Response Headers — The Server's Side of Negotiation | Header | Function | |---|---| | `Content-Type` | MIME type of the body (e.g., `text/html; charset=utf-8`) | | `Content-Length` | Size of the body in bytes | | `Set-Cookie` | Server instructs client to store a cookie | | `Location` | Used in 3xx redirects — where to go next | | `Cache-Control` | Caching directives (e.g., `no-cache`, `max-age=3600`) | | `Server` | Software identification (e.g., `Apache/2.4`, `nginx/1.25`) | | `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` | CORS policy (e.g., `*` or specific origin) | | `WWW-Authenticate` | Required in 401 responses — tells client how to auth | | `ETag` | Unique fingerprint for caching | ### 2.2.5 The Response Body The actual content: HTML document, JSON payload, image binary stream, file download, etc. The body is **optional** — code **204 No Content** and **304 Not Modified** explicitly MUST NOT have a body. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet > [!TIP] > **This is the dense revision block — memorize before the exam.** | Concept | Definition / Formula | Exam Use | |---|---|---| | **HTTP Default Port** | **80** (HTTP), **443** (HTTPS) | 1-mark direct | | **CRLF Separator** | `\r\n` (0x0D 0x0A) | Code snippet tracing | | **Message Format** | `Start-Line + Headers + \r\n + Body` | Diagram questions | | **Safe Methods** | GET, HEAD, OPTIONS, TRACE | 3-mark comparison | | **Idempotent Methods** | GET, HEAD, PUT, DELETE, OPTIONS, TRACE | 3-mark comparison | | **Cacheable Methods** | GET, HEAD (and POST/PATCH under conditions) | 3-mark | | **5xx = Server Error** | The request was valid, server failed | Status code Q | | **4xx = Client Error** | The request was invalid | Status code Q | | **2xx = Success** | Action completed as expected | Status code Q | | **3xx = Redirection** | Client must take additional action | Status code Q | | **Stateless Property** | Each request carries all needed info; server retains nothing | 14-mark essay | | **Content-Type for JSON** | `application/json` | API design Q | | **Content-Type for form POST** | `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` | Form handling Q | ## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility > [!NOTE] > The Request-Response paradigm powers: > - **REST APIs** (used by 90%+ of modern web services) > - **Server-Side Rendered (SSR)** frameworks (Express, Django, Spring) > - **Microservices** communication (gRPC uses similar semantics over HTTP/2) > - **Serverless Functions** (AWS Lambda triggered by API Gateway requests) > - **Webhooks** (reverse-direction: server-to-server request) > - **CDN Edge Logic** (Cloudflare Workers inspect/modify requests before origin) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Performance Engineering Insight:** The number of round trips in a request-response cycle directly impacts **TTFB (Time To First Byte)**. HTTP/2 multiplexing reduces this dramatically. KTU may ask: "Why is HTTP/2 faster?" → Answer: **multiplexed streams over a single TCP connection, eliminating head-of-line blocking.** # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > This section walks through **worked analytical scenarios** typical of KTU 14-mark questions. Each derivation is fully expanded, with every step shown explicitly. --- ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Constructing a Complete HTTP/1.1 Request (Trace-Based Question) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Question Pattern:** *"A user types `http://shop.example.com/products?id=42&q=phone` in a browser. Construct the HTTP/1.1 request the browser sends. Assume the browser sends User-Agent, Accept, and Connection headers."* ### Step-by-Step Construction **Step 1: Identify the Request Line components.** - **Method** → User is *viewing* a page → **GET** - **Request-Target** → `/products?id=42&q=phone` (origin form, path + query string) - **HTTP-Version** → `HTTP/1.1` **Step 2: Determine the mandatory headers.** - `Host: shop.example.com` (mandatory in HTTP/1.1 per **RFC 9112 §5.3**) - `User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 ...` (client identification) - `Accept: text/html,application/xhtml+xml,...` (content negotiation) - `Connection: keep-alive` (request persistent connection) **Step 3: No request body is needed for GET.** **Step 4: Assemble the final message.** ```text GET /products?id=42&q=phone HTTP/1.1\r\n Host: shop.example.com\r\n User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/120.0.0.0 Safari/537.36\r\n Accept: text/html,application/xhtml+xml,application/xml;q=0.9,*/*;q=0.8\r\n Accept-Language: en-US,en;q=0.5\r\n Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate\r\n Connection: keep-alive\r\n \r\n ``` **Step 5: Verify with byte count.** The final `\r\n\r\n` (empty line) signals end of headers. There is **no body** since GET is bodyless. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Constructing an HTTP Response for a JSON API > [!IMPORTANT] > **Scenario:** A REST API endpoint `POST /api/users` successfully creates a new user with id 17. Construct the response. ### Step-by-Step Construction **Step 1: Status Line.** - Resource was **created** → use **201 Created** (not 200, since this is the standard for successful creation) - `HTTP/1.1 201 Created` **Step 2: Required headers for a JSON response.** $$Content\text{-}Type: application/json$$ $$Content\text{-}Length: \vert body\vert$$ **Step 3: Body — the new user representation.** ```json { "id": 17, "username": "alice", "email": "alice@example.com", "createdAt": "2025-01-15T10:30:00Z" } ``` **Step 4: Compute Content-Length.** Let body string be: ``` {"id":17,"username":"alice","email":"alice@example.com","createdAt":"2025-01-15T10:30:00Z"} ``` For the derivation, if we let $B$ be the JSON body and $L = \text{byte length of } B$, then: $$L = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{bytes}(B_i)$$ Where $B_i$ is each character. (Examiners often give a fixed string and ask for $L$.) **Step 5: Complete response.** ```text HTTP/1.1 201 Created\r\n Content-Type: application/json\r\n Content-Length: 116\r\n Location: /api/users/17\r\n Server: Express\r\n Date: Wed, 15 Jan 2025 10:30:00 GMT\r\n \r\n {"id":17,"username":"alice","email":"alice@example.com","createdAt":"2025-01-15T10:30:00Z"} ``` --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Status Code Reasoning (Analytical Decision Tree) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Scenario:** A client GETs `/orders/9999`, but no order with id 9999 exists. What status code does the server return? Justify with the decision matrix. ### Step-by-Step Decision Trace **Step 1: Identify the operation.** - Method = **GET** → user wants to **read** a resource. **Step 2: Classify the failure.** - Was the request well-formed? → Yes (no syntax error) - Is the issue that the resource does not exist? → **YES** - Is the issue that the user lacks permission? → No (assuming open endpoint) **Step 3: Apply the 4xx decision matrix.** $$ \text{Code} = \begin{cases} 400, & \text{if request is syntactically malformed} \\ 401, & \text{if authentication is required and missing} \\ 403, & \text{if user is authenticated but not authorized} \\ 404, & \text{if the resource does not exist on the server} \\ 405, & \text{if the resource exists but method is wrong} \\ 409, & \text{if state conflict (e.g., duplicate)} \\ 410, & \text{if the resource is permanently gone (deleted)} \end{cases} $$ Since the request is valid, the user is authorized, and the resource simply **does not exist**, the correct code is: $$\boxed{\text{HTTP/1.1 404 Not Found}}$$ **Step 4: Construct the response.** ```text HTTP/1.1 404 Not Found\r\n Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8\r\n Content-Length: 167\r\n Server: nginx/1.25\r\n \r\n 404 Not Found

404 Not Found

nginx/1.25
``` --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Header Parsing and Field Classification > [!IMPORTANT] > **Scenario:** A request arrives with the following raw headers. Classify each into its category and explain its role. ``` Host: api.example.com User-Agent: curl/8.5.0 Accept: application/json Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9... Cookie: session=abc123; theme=dark Content-Type: application/json Content-Length: 248 If-None-Match: "e1b7c2" ``` ### Classification Matrix | Header | Category | Role / Engineering Utility | |---|---|---| | `Host` | Request Context | **Mandatory** in HTTP/1.1; supports virtual hosting (one IP, many domains) | | `User-Agent` | Request Context | Identifies client software; used for analytics and adaptive content | | `Accept` | Representation (Content Negotiation) | Tells server the client wants JSON | | `Authorization` | Authentication | Bearer token (JWT) — proves identity for protected API | | `Cookie` | Authentication / State | Re-sends previously stored session ID — works around HTTP's statelessness | | `Content-Type` | Body-related | Tells server the body is JSON | | `Content-Length` | Body-related | Tells server exactly how many bytes the body has | | `If-None-Match` | Conditional | Cache validation — "send new version only if ETag changed" | --- ## 3.5 Comparative Derivation — GET vs POST > [!TIP] > **KTU favourite 14-mark topic: "Compare GET and POST with examples."** | Property | GET | POST | |---|---|---| | **Purpose** | Retrieve (read) | Submit (write/create) | | **Data location** | URL query string | Request body | | **Visibility** | Visible in URL/bookmarks/history | Not visible in URL | | **Caching** | Yes (default) | No (default) | | **Bookmarkable** | Yes | No | | **Idempotent** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (each call may create new resource) | | **Safe** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (mutates state) | | **Length limit** | ~2–8 KB (URL length limits) | No practical limit | | **Back-button safety** | Yes | May trigger re-submit warning | | **Use case** | Search queries, page loads | Form submissions, API writes | --- ## 3.6 The Complete Request-Response Lifecycle (Engineer's Trace) $$ \text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{DNS resolves}} \text{IP} \xrightarrow{\text{TCP 3-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK)}} \text{Established} $$ $$ \xrightarrow{\text{TLS handshake (if HTTPS)}} \text{Secure channel} $$ $$ \xrightarrow{\text{HTTP Request over socket}} \text{Server parses} $$ $$ \xrightarrow{\text{Route + handler executes}} \text{Server logic runs} $$ $$ \xrightarrow{\text{HTTP Response generated}} \text{Bytes flushed to socket} $$ $$ \xrightarrow{\text{Connection: keep-alive or close}} \text{End of transaction} $$ > [!NOTE] > Each variable in the lifecycle can be instrumented for observability: DNS time, TCP time, TLS time, TTFB, content download time. Sum them to get total page load time. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Sequence Diagram — Complete Request-Response Flow > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mermaid sequence diagram** showing the synchronous nature of HTTP. ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant U as User/Browser (Client) participant D as DNS Resolver participant S as Web Server participant DB as Database / Backend U->>U: 1. User types URL or clicks link U->>D: 2. Resolve hostname to IP (DNS Query) D-->>U: 3. Return IP address (e.g., 93.184.216.34) U->>S: 4. TCP SYN (open connection on port 80/443) S-->>U: 5. TCP SYN-ACK U->>S: 6. TCP ACK (connection established) Note over U,S: Optional TLS handshake (HTTPS only) U->>S: 7. HTTP Request (Start-Line + Headers + Body) S->>S: 8. Parse method, URI, headers S->>DB: 9. Query data (e.g., SELECT * FROM products) DB-->>S: 10. Return result set S->>S: 11. Render HTML / build JSON S-->>U: 12. HTTP Response (Status-Line + Headers + Body) S-->>U: 13. Connection close or keep-alive U->>U: 14. Render the page in the browser ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Anatomy of an HTTP Message ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph REQ["HTTP REQUEST MESSAGE"] R1["REQUEST LINE: METHOD REQUEST-TARGET HTTP-VERSION"] R2["HEADER 1: Host: example.com"] R3["HEADER 2: User-Agent: ..."] R4["HEADER N: ..."] R5["EMPTY LINE: CRLF"] R6["BODY (optional): POST data, JSON, form fields"] end subgraph RES["HTTP RESPONSE MESSAGE"] S1["STATUS LINE: HTTP-VERSION STATUS-CODE REASON-PHRASE"] S2["HEADER 1: Content-Type: text/html"] S3["HEADER 2: Set-Cookie: ..."] S4["HEADER N: ..."] S5["EMPTY LINE: CRLF"] S6["BODY (optional): HTML, JSON, file bytes"] end REQ -.sent over network.-> RES ``` ## 4.3 Decision Tree — Status Code Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Server receives request] --> B{Is request syntactically valid?} B -- No --> C[400 Bad Request] B -- Yes --> D{Does it need authentication?} D -- Missing --> E[401 Unauthorized] D -- Present --> F{Is user authorized for this resource?} F -- No --> G[403 Forbidden] F -- Yes --> H{Does the resource exist?} H -- No --> I[404 Not Found] H -- Yes --> J{Is the method allowed?} J -- No --> K[405 Method Not Allowed] J -- Yes --> L{Did server successfully process?} L -- No, server fault --> M[5xx Server Error] L -- Yes, redirect needed --> N[3xx Redirection] L -- Yes, completed --> O[2xx Success] ``` ## 4.4 Functional Architecture — How Server-Side Code Maps Request to Response ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CLIENT["CLIENT SIDE"] C1[Browser / Mobile App / curl] end subgraph SERVER["SERVER SIDE"] R1[Request Listener
e.g., Express app] R2[Router / Middleware
match URL + method] R3[Authentication
check token / cookie] R4[Parser
body, cookies, headers] R5[Controller / Handler
business logic] R6[Data Layer
DB or external API] R7[Response Builder
set status, headers, body] end C1 -- HTTP Request --> R1 R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 --> R5 --> R6 --> R5 R5 --> R7 R7 -- HTTP Response --> C1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering interpretation:** Each block above corresponds to a typical layer in a web framework (e.g., **Express.js**, **Django**, **Spring Boot**). The handler is where your application logic lives — everything else is plumbing. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` > **Differentiate between HTTP and HTTPS. Mention the default port numbers.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol)** is the unencrypted, plain-text application-layer protocol used for transferring web resources. **HTTPS (HTTP Secure)** is HTTP layered on top of **TLS/SSL** encryption, ensuring **confidentiality, integrity, and authentication** of data in transit. - HTTP operates on **port 80**; HTTPS operates on **port 443**. - HTTPS uses digital certificates (X.509) issued by a Certificate Authority (CA), whereas HTTP has no such mechanism. - *(1 mark each for the 3 distinctions)* --- > **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` > **Explain the difference between status codes 401 Unauthorized and 403 Forbidden with suitable scenarios.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **401 Unauthorized:** The client has **not provided valid authentication credentials**. The server is asking *"Who are you?"* — the response must include a `WWW-Authenticate` header. **Example:** A user accesses `/admin` without logging in. - **403 Forbidden:** The client **is authenticated**, but the account **lacks permission** to access the resource. The server is saying *"I know who you are, but you cannot do this."* **Example:** A normal user tries to delete another user's post. - Both are 4xx (client-error) codes, but they communicate *different* failure stages in the access-control pipeline. - *(1 mark definition + 1 mark scenario + 1 mark distinction)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Request & Response Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting the empty CRLF line** between headers and body — a 1-mark loss in any "draw the request" question. > 2. **Confusing `Content-Length` (in bytes) with character count** — always specify *bytes*. > 3. **Writing `200 OK` for a POST that creates a resource** — should be `201 Created` per **RFC 9110**. > 4. **Not specifying HTTP version** in the request/response line — examiners deduct for vagueness. > 5. **Mixing up Safe and Idempotent** — these are orthogonal properties; a method can be one, both, or neither. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — CHOICE 1 > **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`** > **(a)** Explain the structure of an HTTP request message with a neat diagram. List any **five** commonly used request headers and state their purpose. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Explain the five classes of HTTP status codes with **two examples per class**. Describe the differences between `301 Moved Permanently` and `302 Found`. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question A(a) **1. Structure of HTTP Request (4 marks):** An HTTP/1.1 request has four parts in this order: 1. **Request Line** — `METHOD SP REQUEST-TARGET SP HTTP-VERSION CRLF` - Example: `GET /index.html HTTP/1.1` 2. **Header Fields** — Zero or more key-value pairs, each terminated by CRLF - Example: `Host: www.example.com` 3. **Empty Line** — A single CRLF (`\r\n`) that separates headers from the body 4. **Message Body** — Optional payload (used in POST, PUT, PATCH) **Diagram:** ``` +---------------------------------------------+ | GET /index.html HTTP/1.1 (Line 1) | | Host: www.example.com (Header) | | User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Header) | | Accept: text/html (Header) | | Connection: keep-alive (Header) | | (Blank Line)| | [Optional Body] | +---------------------------------------------+ ``` **2. Five Common Request Headers (3 marks):** | Header | Purpose | |---|---| | `Host` | Specifies the domain name and port of the server (mandatory in HTTP/1.1) | | `User-Agent` | Identifies the client software (browser, version, OS) | | `Accept` | Tells the server which content types the client can handle (content negotiation) | | `Authorization` | Carries credentials like `Bearer ` or `Basic ` | | `Cookie` | Sends previously stored session/cookie data back to the server | *(Any 5 valid headers with correct purpose = 3 marks)* --- #### Model Solution — Question A(b) **1. The Five Classes of Status Codes (5 marks):** | Class | Range | Meaning | Example 1 | Example 2 | |---|---|---|---|---| | **1xx Informational** | 100–199 | Interim response; client should wait | `100 Continue` | `101 Switching Protocols` | | **2xx Success** | 200–299 | Request was successfully received, understood, and accepted | `200 OK` | `201 Created` | | **3xx Redirection** | 300–399 | Further action must be taken (usually redirect) | `301 Moved Permanently` | `304 Not Modified` | | **4xx Client Error** | 400–499 | Request contains bad syntax or cannot be fulfilled | `400 Bad Request` | `404 Not Found` | | **5xx Server Error** | 500–599 | Server failed to fulfill an apparently valid request | `500 Internal Server Error` | `503 Service Unavailable` | **2. `301` vs `302` (2 marks):** | Property | 301 Moved Permanently | 302 Found (Temporary) | |---|---|---| | **Cache behaviour** | Browsers cache the redirect aggressively | Browsers do not cache; may re-check later | | **SEO impact** | Passes ~90–99% of link equity to new URL | Does **not** pass full link equity | | **Use case** | Domain change, HTTPS migration, URL restructure | A/B testing, temporary maintenance, login redirects | | **RFC name** | "Moved Permanently" | "Found" (historically "Moved Temporarily") | --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — CHOICE 2 > **`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]`** > **(a)** Compare **GET** and **POST** HTTP methods in **at least eight** aspects. Explain why GET is considered *safe* and POST is *not*. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** With a suitable example, describe the complete **request-response cycle** for a login form submission over HTTPS. Include the request line, at least four request headers, the response status line, and at least three response headers. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question B(a) **Comparison Table (5 marks for the table, 2 marks for the safety explanation):** | Aspect | GET | POST | |---|---|---| | **Purpose** | Retrieve a resource | Submit / create / modify data | | **Data location** | URL query string | Request body | | **URL visibility** | Data visible in address bar | Data hidden from address bar | | **Bookmarkable** | Yes | No | | **Cacheable** | Yes (by default) | No (by default) | | **Idempotent** | ✅ Yes — same effect on repeated calls | ❌ No — repeated calls may create duplicates | | **Safe** | ✅ Yes — does not mutate server state | ❌ No — mutates server state | | **Body allowed** | Generally no | Yes | | **Length limit** | Constrained by URL length (~2–8 KB) | No practical limit | | **Browser back button** | Re-submits safely | Re-submit warning | | **Use case** | Search, page load, filters | Login, signup, checkout, file upload | **Why GET is Safe (1 mark):** A method is **safe** if it is intended only for information retrieval and **must not cause any side effects** on the server state (no creation, modification, or deletion). GET meets this definition because it is read-only — calling it 1 time or 100 times produces the same observable state on the server. **Why POST is Not Safe (1 mark):** POST is **not safe** because it is designed to *change* server state. Calling POST multiple times may create multiple new resources, charge a credit card repeatedly, or send multiple emails. Hence, browsers display a "Confirm form re-submission" warning if you re-load a POST page. --- #### Model Solution — Question B(b) **Example:** A user submits a login form with username `alice` and password `secret123` to `https://app.example.com/login`. **Step 1: Browser initiates connection.** DNS resolves `app.example.com` → IP. TCP 3-way handshake on port **443**. TLS handshake (certificate exchange, key agreement). **Step 2: Browser sends the HTTPS-encrypted HTTP request (5 marks for the request):** ```text POST /login HTTP/1.1\r\n Host: app.example.com\r\n User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64) Chrome/120.0\r\n Accept: text/html,application/xhtml+xml,application/xml;q=0.9,*/*;q=0.8\r\n Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded\r\n Content-Length: 32\r\n Referer: https://app.example.com/login\r\n Connection: keep-alive\r\n \r\n username=alice&password=secret123 ``` **Key request headers explained:** - `Host` — required in HTTP/1.1 for virtual hosting - `Content-Type` — declares form-encoded body - `Content-Length` — exact byte count of the body - `Referer` — tells server the previous page **Step 3: Server processes the credentials, looks up the user, verifies the password hash, and creates a session.** **Step 4: Server sends the HTTP response (5 marks for the response):** If credentials are valid: ```text HTTP/1.1 302 Found\r\n Location: /dashboard\r\n Set-Cookie: sessionId=eyJ1c2VyIjoiYWxpY2UiLCJyb2xlIjoidXNlciJ9; HttpOnly; Secure; Path=/; SameSite=Lax\r\n Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8\r\n Content-Length: 0\r\n Server: nginx/1.25\r\n Date: Wed, 15 Jan 2025 12:45:30 GMT\r\n \r\n ``` If credentials are invalid: ```text HTTP/1.1 401 Unauthorized\r\n WWW-Authenticate: Basic realm="Login Required"\r\n Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8\r\n Content-Length: 168\r\n \r\n

401 Unauthorized

Invalid username or password.

``` **Key response headers explained:** - `Location` — where the browser should go next (302 redirect) - `Set-Cookie` — issues a session cookie with security flags - `Content-Type` — declares the body format - `WWW-Authenticate` — only present on 401, instructs client how to authenticate --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam** - ✅ HTTP is **request-response**, **stateless**, **text-based** (HTTP/1.1), and operates over **TCP**. - ✅ **Default ports:** HTTP = **80**, HTTPS = **443**. - ✅ Every HTTP message has four parts: **Start Line → Headers → Empty CRLF Line → Body**. - ✅ **CRLF (`\r\n`)** is the official line terminator.
