The empty line is **mandatory**. - ✅ **Safe methods:** `GET, HEAD, OPTIONS, TRACE` — they do not mutate server state. - ✅ **Idempotent methods:** `GET, HEAD, PUT, DELETE, OPTIONS, TRACE` — repeated calls = same result. - ✅ **Methods that carry a body:** `POST, PUT, PATCH` (and DELETE in rare cases). - ✅ **Status code classes:** 1xx=info, 2xx=success, 3xx=redirect, 4xx=client-error, 5xx=server-error. - ✅ **Top 5 codes to memorize:** 200, 201, 301, 401, 404, 500. - ✅ **401 ≠ 403:** 401 = *no/wrong credentials*; 403 = *known but not allowed*. - ✅ **200 vs 201:** Use **201 Created** for successful POST that creates a resource. - ✅ **301 vs 302:** Use **301** for permanent URL changes (SEO); **302** for temporary. - ✅ **Content-Type** is **mandatory** whenever a body is present; pair with **Content-Length** in HTTP/1.1. - ✅ **`Host` header** is **mandatory** in HTTP/1.1 (RFC 9112). - ✅ **HTTP/2** = binary, multiplexed, header-compressed (HPACK). - ✅ **HTTP/3** = runs over **QUIC (UDP)**, not TCP. - ✅ **Statelessness** means each request carries all needed info — cookies/sessions/headers are the workaround. - ✅ **MIME / Content-Type** examples: `text/html`, `application/json`, `application/x-www-form-urlencoded`, `multipart/form-data`. - ✅ **Common request headers:** `Host, User-Agent, Accept, Authorization, Cookie, Referer, Content-Type, Content-Length, If-None-Match`. - ✅ **Common response headers:** `Content-Type, Content-Length, Set-Cookie, Location, Cache-Control, Server, WWW-Authenticate, ETag`. - ✅ **GET puts data in URL**; **POST puts data in body** — know when to use which. - ✅ **Connection: keep-alive** (HTTP/1.1) lets multiple requests share a TCP connection. - ✅ **Idempotency ≠ Safety** — `DELETE` is idempotent but NOT safe (it changes state). > [!IMPORTANT] > **End of Topic — Request and Response. You are now equipped to score full marks on this module.**

topics.title: Responding with HTML
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Responding with HTML # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Responding with HTML** in the context of server-side web application development refers to the mechanism by which a web server (typically a Node.js application built using the **Express.js framework**) processes an incoming **HTTP request** from a client (browser) and transmits back an **HTTP response** whose `body` is an **HTML (HyperText Markup Language)** document, accompanied by appropriate **HTTP headers** such as `Content-Type: text/html` and a valid **status code** (e.g., `200 OK`, `404 Not Found`). In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this is formally classified under the **Express.js Response Object (`res`)** API, which exposes methods like `res.send()`, `res.sendFile()`, `res.write()`, `res.end()`, and `res.render()` to dispatch HTML content to the client. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** > Students must be able to *“demonstrate the use of Express.js response methods to send HTML, JSON, and static files; explain response headers, status codes, and content negotiation.”* This topic is a high-weightage area in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **restaurant kitchen** as your Express.js server: - The **waiter** taking your order is the **HTTP Request**. - The **chef** preparing your dish is your **route handler function** (the function executed when a URL is hit). - The **plate of food** being served is the **HTML Response**. - The **type of cuisine indicator** (Veg/Non-Veg) on the menu is the **HTTP Header** (`Content-Type`). - The **bill/receipt** showing whether the order was successful is the **HTTP Status Code** (e.g., `200 OK` = "Here's your meal"; `404 Not Found` = "Sorry, that dish isn't on our menu"). So, **"Responding with HTML"** simply means: *the chef (server) finishes preparing the HTML dish (response body), tags it with the right label (Content-Type header), stamps the receipt (status code), and hands it to the waiter (Express framework) for delivery back to the customer (browser).* ## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **HTTP Response** | The message a server sends back to a client after receiving a request. | | **HTML** | HyperText Markup Language — the standard document structure rendered by browsers. | | **`res` object** | Express.js's built-in response object passed to every route handler. | | **Content-Type** | An HTTP header that tells the browser *what kind* of data is in the body. | | **Status Code** | A 3-digit number indicating the outcome (e.g., `200`, `404`, `500`). | | **MIME Type** | A standard identifier (e.g., `text/html`) used in the `Content-Type` header. | > [!NOTE] > Always remember: **HTML is just a string of text with tags.** Any method that can send a string can technically "send HTML" — but the *correct* and *standard* way is to set the `Content-Type` header to `text/html` so the browser knows to render it as a webpage, not display it as raw text. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The HTTP Response — Structural Anatomy Every HTTP response sent by an Express server has three logical parts: 1. **Status Line** → e.g., `HTTP/1.1 200 OK` 2. **Headers** → e.g., `Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8` 3. **Body** → The actual HTML markup When you use Express to "respond with HTML", you are essentially orchestrating all three parts using the `res` object's methods. ## 2.2 Core Express.js Response Methods (Step-by-Step Logic) ### 🔹 Method 1: `res.send(body)` - **What it does:** Sends the HTTP response. The `body` parameter can be a `Buffer` object, a `String`, an `object`, or an `Array`. - **Auto-Configuration:** Express automatically sets the `Content-Type` header AND the `ETag` header. If the body is a string, it defaults to `text/html`. If it's a JavaScript object, it defaults to `application/json`. - **Best Use Case:** Quick, single-line HTML or JSON responses. > [!NOTE] > If you pass a string that contains HTML tags (e.g., `

Hello

`), Express automatically sets the `Content-Type` to `text/html` and the browser renders it as HTML. ### 🔹 Method 2: `res.sendFile(path)` - **What it does:** Transfers the file at the given `path`. Sets the `Content-Type` response HTTP header field based on the file's **MIME type extension**. - **Absolute Path Required:** You must provide an **absolute path** to the file (use the built-in `path` module's `__dirname` + `path.join()`). - **Best Use Case:** Serving static `.html` files from a `public/` or `views/` directory. ### 🔹 Method 3: `res.render(view, locals)` - **What it does:** Renders a **view template** (e.g., EJS, Pug, Handlebars) after injecting the supplied `locals` (data object) into it. Returns the rendered HTML string. - **Requires View Engine:** You must configure `app.set('view engine', 'ejs')` and a `views/` directory. - **Best Use Case:** **Dynamic HTML generation** — pages that change based on database data or user input. ### 🔹 Method 4: `res.write()` + `res.end()` - **What it does:** Low-level methods inherited from the **Node.js core `http` module**. `res.write(chunk)` writes a piece of data, and `res.end()` signals that the response is finished. - **Best Use Case:** Streaming large HTML responses in chunks (e.g., reading a big file piece by piece). ### 🔹 Method 5: `res.status(code)` — Chained Methods - **What it does:** Sets the HTTP status code. It is designed to be **chained** with other `res` methods for fluent syntax: - `res.status(404).send('Page Not Found')` - `res.status(200).sendFile(absolutePath)` ## 2.3 Why and How: The Underlying Mechanism - **Why does Express auto-detect content type?** Express inspects the JavaScript type of the argument. A `String` → `text/html`; an `Object` → `application/json`; a `Buffer` → `application/octet-stream`. This is implemented internally via the `typeof` check and the `mime` package. - **How does the browser know to render HTML?** It reads the `Content-Type: text/html` header in the response and uses the browser's HTML parser engine to construct the **DOM (Document Object Model)**. - **How is the status code sent?** Express writes it to the response status line *before* the body. The first call to `res.send()` (or any method that flushes the response) locks the headers. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Syntax / Formula | Default Behavior | When to Use | |---|---|---|---| | **Send raw HTML string** | `res.send('

Hi

')` | `Content-Type: text/html` | Quick demos, small dynamic snippets | | **Send an HTML file** | `res.sendFile(absolutePath)` | MIME inferred from `.html` extension | Serving static pages from disk | | **Render a template** | `res.render('index', {name: 'Kerala'})` | Uses configured view engine | Dynamic pages with data binding | | **Set status code** | `res.status(404).send('Not Found')` | — | Error handling, REST APIs | | **Stream HTML chunks** | `res.write(chunk); res.end();` | None — manual control | Large files, real-time data | | **Set custom header** | `res.set('Content-Type', 'text/html')` | Overrides Express's default | Forcing a specific MIME type | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Rule:** `res.send()` and `res.end()` **must only be called once** per request. Calling them twice throws the error `ERR_HTTP_HEADERS_SENT`. This is a classic KTU viva question! ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility In production-grade systems (e.g., **Myntra, Flipkart, IRCTC**), responding with HTML is the foundation of **Server-Side Rendering (SSR)**. Modern frameworks like **Next.js (React)** and **Nuxt.js (Vue)** wrap Express-like servers to send fully pre-rendered HTML to clients, which improves: - **First Contentful Paint (FCP)** — pages appear faster. - **Search Engine Optimization (SEO)** — Google crawlers see ready-made HTML. - **Accessibility** — content is available even if JavaScript fails. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1: The Simplest HTML Response **Problem:** Write an Express route that responds to a `GET` request at `/` with the HTML `

Welcome to KTU

` and a status code of `200 OK`. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Project Setup (assumed)** Assume a standard Node.js + Express project structure: ``` project/ ├── server.js ├── package.json └── node_modules/ ``` **Step 2 — Import Express and Initialize the App** ```javascript const express = require('express'); const app = express(); const PORT = 3000; ``` **Step 3 — Define the Route Handler** ```javascript app.get('/', (req, res) => { const htmlContent = '

Welcome to KTU

'; res.status(200).send(htmlContent); }); ``` **Step 4 — Start the Server** ```javascript app.listen(PORT, () => { console.log(`Server running at http://localhost:${PORT}`); }); ``` ### Logical Derivation of What Happens When a browser hits `http://localhost:3000/`: 1. The Node.js HTTP server receives the request. 2. Express matches the URL `/` and HTTP verb `GET` to the registered route. 3. The callback function executes. The `res` object is provided by Express. 4. `res.status(200)` queues the status code `200` in the response headers. 5. `res.send(htmlContent)`: - Detects the argument is a **String** → sets `Content-Type: text/html`. - Sets `Content-Length` to the byte length of the string. - Sends the HTTP headers and the body in one TCP packet. - Closes the response. 6. The browser receives the response, reads `Content-Type: text/html`, parses the body using its HTML parser, and renders the heading. ## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Serving a Static HTML File **Problem:** Create a route `/home` that serves the file `index.html` located in a `public/` directory. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Directory Structure** ``` project/ ├── server.js └── public/ └── index.html ``` **Step 2 — `public/index.html`** ```html KTU Home

Hello from a Static HTML File!

This page was sent using res.sendFile().

``` **Step 3 — `server.js`** ```javascript const express = require('express'); const path = require('path'); const app = express(); const PORT = 3000; app.get('/home', (req, res) => { const filePath = path.join(__dirname, 'public', 'index.html'); res.sendFile(filePath, (err) => { if (err) { res.status(500).send('Internal Server Error'); } }); }); app.listen(PORT, () => console.log(`Server on port ${PORT}`)); ``` ### Detailed Derivation — Why `path.join(__dirname, ...)` ? The Node.js `fs` module (used internally by `res.sendFile`) **only accepts absolute paths** for security reasons (to prevent directory traversal attacks like `../../etc/passwd`). - `__dirname` is a Node.js global that resolves to the **absolute path of the current file's directory**. - `path.join()` concatenates path segments using the **OS-specific separator** (`/` on Linux/Mac, `\` on Windows). - The resulting `filePath` is guaranteed to be an absolute, OS-correct path. ### Mathematical Representation of Path Resolution $$ \text{filePath} = \text{path.join}(\text{\_\_dirname}, \text{'public'}, \text{'index.html'}) $$ $$ \text{filePath} = \text{path.join}(\text{"/home/student/project"}, \text{"public"}, \text{"index.html"}) $$ $$ \text{filePath} = \text{"/home/student/project/public/index.html"} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > If you use a **relative path** like `res.sendFile('public/index.html')`, Express will throw an error: `TypeError: Path must be absolute.` This is a guaranteed KTU exam trap! ## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Dynamic HTML with Template Engine (EJS) **Problem:** Render a page `/user/Kerala` that displays `Welcome, Kerala!` using EJS. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Install EJS** ```bash npm install ejs ``` **Step 2 — Directory Structure** ``` project/ ├── server.js └── views/ └── user.ejs ``` **Step 3 — `views/user.ejs`** ```html User Page

Welcome, <%= username %>!

Your state is <%= state %>.

``` **Step 4 — `server.js`** ```javascript const express = require('express'); const app = express(); app.set('view engine', 'ejs'); app.get('/user/:name', (req, res) => { const userData = { username: req.params.name, state: 'Kerala' }; res.render('user', userData); }); app.listen(3000); ``` ### Logical Flow Derivation $$ \text{Request: GET /user/Anand} $$ $$ \downarrow $$ $$ \text{Express parses URL → } \texttt{req.params.name} = \text{"Anand"} $$ $$ \downarrow $$ $$ \texttt{res.render('user', \{username: "Anand", state: "Kerala"\})} $$ $$ \downarrow $$ $$ \text{EJS engine reads } \texttt{views/user.ejs} $$ $$ \downarrow $$ $$ \text{Replaces } \texttt{<\%= username \%>} \text{ with "Anand" and } \texttt{<\%= state \%>} \text{ with "Kerala"} $$ $$ \downarrow $$ $$ \text{Final HTML body} = \texttt{"

Welcome, Anand!

Your state is Kerala.

"} $$ $$ \downarrow $$ $$ \text{Sent to browser with } \texttt{Content-Type: text/html} $$ ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Feature | `res.send()` | `res.sendFile()` | `res.render()` | `res.write()` | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Input Type** | String, Buffer, Object, Array | Absolute file path | Template name + data | String/Buffer chunks | | **Auto Content-Type** | Yes (by typeof) | Yes (by extension) | Yes (`text/html` for EJS) | No (manual) | | **Requires View Engine** | No | No | Yes (EJS/Pug/etc.) | No | | **Streaming Support** | No | No (single shot) | No | Yes (chunked) | | **Memory Efficient for Large Data** | Low | Low | Low | High | | **KTU Marks Weightage** | High | Very High | Very High | Medium | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Request-Response Flow with Express The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete lifecycle of an HTTP request being answered with HTML content via Express.js. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Client Browser
GET /home HTTP/1.1] --> B[Node.js HTTP Server
Receives TCP Packet] B --> C[Express Middleware Chain
app.use calls execute] C --> D{Match Route
in Express Router} D -- Match Found --> E[Route Handler Executes
req, res injected] D -- No Match --> F[404 Not Found Handler
res.status 404] E --> G[Build HTML String
or Read File or Render] G --> H[Set Status Code
res.status 200] H --> I[Set Content-Type Header
text/html] I --> J[Send Response Body
res.send or res.sendFile] J --> K[Close Response Stream
res.end implicit] K --> L[Browser Receives HTML
Parses DOM] L --> M[Render Page on Screen
User Sees Content] F --> N[Browser Shows
Error Page] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the `res` Object This block diagram maps the structural API of the Express Response object and how each method contributes to the final HTTP response. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph InputLayer [Input Layer] I1[String] I2[Buffer] I3[Object JSON] I4[File Path] end subgraph ResMethods [Express res Object Methods] M1[res.send] M2[res.sendFile] M3[res.render] M4[res.write and res.end] end subgraph HeaderEngine [Header and Status Engine] H1[Content-Type Setter] H2[Status Code Setter
res.status] H3[Custom Headers
res.set res.header] end subgraph OutputLayer [HTTP Response Output] O1[Status Line
HTTP 1.1 200 OK] O2[Headers Block
Content-Type etc] O3[HTML Body
String Chunks] end I1 --> M1 I2 --> M1 I3 --> M1 I4 --> M2 M1 --> H1 M2 --> H1 M3 --> H1 M4 --> H1 M1 --> H2 M2 --> H2 M3 --> H2 M4 --> H2 H1 --> O2 H2 --> O1 M1 --> O3 M2 --> O3 M3 --> O3 M4 --> O3 ``` ## 4.3 Decision Tree: Which `res` Method Should I Use? ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Need to send response] --> Q1{Is the content
dynamic with data?} Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Have you set up
a view engine?} Q1 -- No --> Q3{Is the content a
small inline string?} Q2 -- Yes --> R1[Use res.render view locals] Q2 -- No --> R2[Install ejs or pug
then use res.render] Q3 -- Yes --> R3[Use res.send string] Q3 -- No --> Q4{Need to stream
in chunks?} Q4 -- Yes --> R4[Use res.write then res.end] Q4 -- No --> Q5{Is the HTML stored
in a .html file?} Q5 -- Yes --> R5[Use res.sendFile absolute path] Q5 -- No --> R3 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading aid for the diagrams:** In the Mermaid flowchart, each rectangular node represents a *process* (an action performed by Express), while each diamond-shaped node represents a *decision* (a branching question the developer must answer). Arrows denote the sequential flow of execution. This mirrors how the KTU board examiner visualizes your code's execution path during evaluation. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *Explain the difference between `res.send()` and `res.sendFile()` in Express.js. When would you prefer one over the other?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **`res.send(body)`** is used to send a *small, dynamically generated* response body. The `body` can be a string, buffer, or object. Express automatically sets the `Content-Type` header based on the argument type (String → `text/html`, Object → `application/json`). - **`res.sendFile(path)`** is used to send a *complete file* from the server's filesystem to the client. The file's MIME type is inferred from its extension. It requires an **absolute path**, typically constructed using `path.join(__dirname, ...)`. - **Preference Rule:** Use `res.send()` for short inline responses (e.g., a generated greeting). Use `res.sendFile()` for serving pre-existing `.html` files from a `public/` directory. Use `res.render()` when a view engine is configured for data-driven dynamic pages. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** *What is the role of the `Content-Type` header when responding with HTML? What happens if it is set to `text/plain` instead?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - The `Content-Type` header is an **HTTP header** that informs the client (browser) about the **MIME type** of the response body, allowing it to interpret and render the content correctly. - For HTML, the correct value is `text/html`, which instructs the browser to parse the response as markup and build the DOM. - If set to `text/plain` instead, the browser will treat the HTML tags as **literal plain text** and display the source code (e.g., `

Hello

`) verbatim on the screen, without rendering the heading. - Express sets this automatically when `res.send()` receives a String, but it can be overridden using `res.set('Content-Type', 'text/html')`. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. Forgetting to use `path.join(__dirname, ...)` and passing a relative path to `res.sendFile()` — this throws a `TypeError` and costs **2 marks**. > 2. Calling `res.send()` twice in the same route handler — this triggers `ERR_HTTP_HEADERS_SENT` and the route is marked **wrong** for the entire logic. > 3. Confusing `res.render()` with `res.sendFile()` — remember `res.render()` is for **template engines** and `res.sendFile()` is for **raw HTML files**. Examiners will deduct marks if the method is mismatched with the use case. > 4. Not setting the `view engine` via `app.set('view engine', 'ejs')` before calling `res.render()` — results in the error `No default engine was specified`. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, ESE Module 3]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of an Express.js application and describe how an HTTP request is processed to produce an HTML response. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write an Express.js program to: - Serve a static `about.html` file from a `public/` directory when a user visits `/about`. - Render a dynamic `profile.ejs` template at `/profile/:username` that greets the user with their name passed via the URL parameter. - Return a `404 Not Found` HTML page for any unmatched route. Include the directory structure and explain each method used. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for Question A **(a) Architecture Explanation (7 Marks):** The Express.js application follows a **middleware-based request-response pipeline**: 1. **Client → Server:** A browser sends an HTTP `GET` request to a specific URL (e.g., `/home`). 2. **Node.js HTTP Layer:** The built-in `http` module receives the TCP request and passes it to Express. 3. **Middleware Chain:** Express executes all globally registered middlewares (`app.use(...)`) in sequence. These can modify `req`/`res` objects or end the request early. 4. **Routing:** The Express router matches the request's HTTP method and URL path against registered routes. If a match is found, the corresponding handler function is invoked with `(req, res, next)` arguments. 5. **Response Generation:** The handler constructs or reads the HTML body and uses `res` methods (`res.send`, `res.sendFile`, `res.render`) to dispatch it. Express auto-sets the `Content-Type: text/html` header. 6. **Server → Client:** The HTTP response (status line + headers + HTML body) is sent back. The browser parses the HTML and renders the DOM. (Refer to the **Mermaid flowchart in Section 4.1** for a visual representation — students should reproduce a simplified version in the exam.) **(b) Code Solution (7 Marks):** **Directory Structure (1 Mark):** ``` project/ ├── server.js ├── public/ │ └── about.html └── views/ └── profile.ejs ``` **`public/about.html` (1 Mark):** ```html About Us

About KTU Web Application Development

This is the about page served using res.sendFile().

``` **`views/profile.ejs` (1 Mark):** ```html Profile

Welcome, <%= username %>!

This is your personalized profile page.

``` **`server.js` (4 Marks):** ```javascript const express = require('express'); const path = require('path'); const app = express(); const PORT = 3000; // Configure EJS view engine app.set('view engine', 'ejs'); // Route 1: Serve static HTML file app.get('/about', (req, res) => { const filePath = path.join(__dirname, 'public', 'about.html'); res.sendFile(filePath); }); // Route 2: Render dynamic template app.get('/profile/:username', (req, res) => { res.render('profile', { username: req.params.username }); }); // 404 handler for unmatched routes app.use((req, res) => { res.status(404).send('

404 - Page Not Found

'); }); app.listen(PORT, () => { console.log(`Server running on http://localhost:${PORT}`); }); ``` **Method Explanations (to be written in exam):** - `path.join(__dirname, 'public', 'about.html')` → constructs an OS-independent absolute path. - `res.sendFile(filePath)` → sends the HTML file with auto-detected `Content-Type: text/html`. - `res.render('profile', { username: ... })` → renders the EJS template, replacing `<%= username %>` with the actual URL parameter value. - `app.use((req, res) => { res.status(404).send(...) })` → a fallback middleware that catches all unmatched routes and returns a custom 404 HTML page. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, ESE Module 3 Alternate]** **(a)** Compare and contrast the following Express.js response methods with suitable code snippets: `res.send()`, `res.sendFile()`, `res.render()`, and `res.write()`. Create a comparison table highlighting input type, content-type behavior, view-engine dependency, and ideal use case. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write an Express.js program that demonstrates the use of HTTP status codes in HTML responses. The program should: - Return `200 OK` with a styled HTML welcome page at `/welcome`. - Return `404 Not Found` with a styled HTML error page at any invalid URL. - Return `500 Internal Server Error` when a specific `/error` route is accessed, simulating a server-side failure. Explain the role of `res.status()` and how it is chained with other methods. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for Question B **(a) Comparison Table (7 Marks):** | Method | Input Type | Content-Type Behavior | View Engine Required | Ideal Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | `res.send(body)` | String, Buffer, Object, Array | Auto-detected from `typeof` (String → `text/html`, Object → `application/json`) | No | Quick inline HTML or JSON responses | | `res.sendFile(path)` | Absolute file path string | Inferred from file extension (e.g., `.html` → `text/html`) | No | Serving static HTML files from `public/` | | `res.render(view, locals)` | View template name + data object | Defaults to `text/html` (configured view engine) | Yes (EJS, Pug, Handlebars) | Dynamic, data-driven HTML pages | | `res.write(chunk)` + `res.end()` | String or Buffer chunk | Must be set manually via `res.set('Content-Type', ...)` | No | Streaming large HTML in multiple chunks | **Sample Code Snippets (to be included in the answer):** ```javascript // res.send() app.get('/a', (req, res) => res.send('

Hello

')); // res.sendFile() app.get('/b', (req, res) => res.sendFile(path.join(__dirname, 'a.html'))); // res.render() app.get('/c', (req, res) => res.render('home', { name: 'KTU' })); // res.write() + res.end() app.get('/d', (req, res) => { res.set('Content-Type', 'text/html'); res.write('

Chunk 1

'); res.write('

Chunk 2

'); res.end(); }); ``` **(b) Status Code Demonstration (7 Marks):** ```javascript const express = require('express'); const app = express(); const PORT = 3000; // 200 OK - Welcome page app.get('/welcome', (req, res) => { res.status(200).send(`

Welcome to KTU Portal

Your request was successful.

`); }); // 500 Internal Server Error - Simulated failure app.get('/error', (req, res) => { res.status(500).send(`

500 - Internal Server Error

Something went wrong on the server.

`); }); // 404 Not Found - Catch-all middleware app.use((req, res) => { res.status(404).send(`

404 - Page Not Found

The requested URL ${req.originalUrl} does not exist.

`); }); app.listen(PORT, () => console.log(`Server on port ${PORT}`)); ``` **Explanation of `res.status()` Chaining (to be written in exam):** - `res.status(code)` sets the HTTP status code that will be sent in the response's status line. - It returns the `res` object itself, enabling **method chaining** — a fluent design pattern where multiple methods are called in a single statement. - Example: `res.status(200).sendFile(absolutePath)` is equivalent to setting the status code first and then calling `sendFile`. - Without `res.status()`, Express defaults to a `200 OK` status. For error pages, explicitly setting the correct status code is essential for SEO, browser behavior, and API consumers to handle errors programmatically. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Points — Memorize Before the Exam:** - ✅ **Express auto-sets `Content-Type: text/html`** when `res.send()` receives a **String** argument. - ✅ **`res.sendFile()` requires an ABSOLUTE path** — always use `path.join(__dirname, 'folder', 'file.html')`. - ✅ **`res.render()` needs a view engine** — install EJS, then `app.set('view engine', 'ejs')` before rendering. - ✅ **`res.write()` must be paired with `res.end()`** — otherwise, the response hangs and the client times out. - ✅ **Never call `res.send()` twice** — causes `ERR_HTTP_HEADERS_SENT` error. - ✅ **`res.status(code)` is chainable** — `res.status(404).send('Not Found')` is the idiomatic Express style. - ✅ **The HTTP response has 3 parts:** Status Line + Headers + Body. Express manages all three. - ✅ **Status Code Categories:** `1xx` (Informational), `2xx` (Success: `200 OK`), `3xx` (Redirection: `301`, `302`), `4xx` (Client Error: `404 Not Found`, `400 Bad Request`), `5xx` (Server Error: `500 Internal Server Error`). - ✅ **Browser renders HTML** only when `Content-Type` is `text/html` — otherwise, raw text is shown. - ✅ **For dynamic data**, use `res.render()` with a template engine (EJS/Pug/Handlebars) instead of string concatenation. - ✅ **MIME type inference** in `res.sendFile()` is based on the file's extension via the `mime` package bundled with Express. - ✅ **Express is built on Node.js's `http` module** — `res.write()` and `res.end()` are the low-level primitives; `res.send()` is the high-level convenience wrapper.

topics.title: Installing Custom Packages with NPM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Installing Custom Packages with NPM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **NPM (Node Package Manager)** is the default, official package manager for the JavaScript runtime environment **Node.js**. It is a comprehensive command-line utility paired with an online registry database (the "npm registry") that allows developers to discover, install, share, and manage reusable code modules — known as **packages** — within their projects.

A **custom package** in the NPM ecosystem refers to any third-party (or self-authored) module that is not part of the Node.js core standard library. These packages are published to the NPM registry under unique names (e.g., `express`, `lodash`, `mongoose`) and are pulled into a local project via the `npm install` command. The KTU 2024 syllabus frames this topic under the umbrella of **Front-End and Back-End JavaScript tooling**, where students are expected to demonstrate proficiency in bootstrapping a Node.js project and managing its dependency graph.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology Mandated by KTU 2024 Syllabus:**
> - **Package**: A directory containing a `package.json` file and reusable JavaScript code.
> - **Dependency**: A package that another package requires to function correctly.
> - **Registry**: The central public database at `registry.npmjs.org` that hosts all published packages.
> - **Node.js**: The open-source, cross-platform JavaScript runtime built on Chrome's V8 engine, with the **current LTS version (v20.x)** as the standard reference metric.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of NPM like a **massive, digital hardware store for code**. Imagine you are building a custom PC (your web application). Instead of manufacturing every component — the motherboard, the GPU, the power supply — yourself, you walk into a single warehouse (the **NPM Registry**) that stocks millions of pre-built, tested components. You carry a shopping list (`package.json`), grab exactly the parts you need, and a delivery service (`npm install`) brings them straight to your workshop bench, neatly organized in a storage room called `node_modules`.

When you only need a tool for one specific project (like a soldering iron for repairing a TV), you buy it and keep it in your **local toolbox** (project folder). When you need a tool you use across *every* project on your computer (like a multimeter), you install it **globally**, so it's always available in your main workbench (system PATH). This is precisely how **local** versus **global** package installation works in NPM.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **NPM Standard Reference Values (Bolded for KTU Exams):**
> - **NPM Registry URL:** `https://registry.npmjs.org/`
> - **Default Installation Location:** `./node_modules/` (relative to project root)
> - **Lockfile Standard:** `package-lock.json` (auto-generated)
> - **Manifest File:** `package.json` (manually authored or `npm init` generated)
> - **Semantic Versioning (SemVer) Format:** `MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH` (e.g., **4.18.2**)
> - **Latest Stable Node.js (LTS) at Syllabus Publication:** **v20.x**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Workflow of `npm install`

The `npm install` command is the cornerstone operation for adding custom packages. When invoked, NPM executes a deterministic, multi-stage pipeline:

1. **Resolution Phase**: NPM reads the `package.json` file in the current working directory and parses the `dependencies` and `devDependencies` arrays. Each entry is matched against the registry index to fetch metadata (version, dependencies of dependencies, integrity hashes).

2. **Fetching Phase**: The package tarballs (.tgz files) are downloaded from the registry. They are cached locally in `~/.npm/_cacache/` to avoid redundant network requests.

3. **Dependency Resolution & Tree Flattening**: NPM constructs a **dependency tree**, then performs **hoisting** — common sub-dependencies are flattened to the top of `node_modules` to reduce duplication. Conflicts are resolved using **nested folder resolution**.

4. **Linking Phase**: The `node_modules/.package-lock.json` file is updated, and the `package-lock.json` at the project root is rewritten to lock the exact resolved versions.

5. **Lifecycle Script Execution**: If the installed package contains an `"install"` script in its `package.json`, NPM executes it inside a shell environment with controlled PATH variables.

## 2.2 The `Why` and `How` Behind Installation Flags

| Flag | Purpose | Why It Matters (KTU Context) |
|------|---------|------------------------------|
| `--save` (default in npm 5+) | Adds package to `dependencies` | Required for production runtime |
| `--save-dev` | Adds package to `devDependencies` | Used for build/test tools (webpack, jest) |
| `-g` | Installs globally on system PATH | CLI tools (nodemon, typescript) |
| `--no-save` | Installs without modifying `package.json` | Temporary experimentation |
| `package@version` | Pins to a specific version | Critical for legacy project compatibility |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** The examiner frequently tests the difference between `dependencies` (production runtime libraries) and `devDependencies` (development-only tools). A correct answer earns full marks; an incorrect answer loses **2 of 3** on a Part A question.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Notation / Syntax | Description | Engineering Use Case |
|---------|-------------------|-------------|----------------------|
| SemVer Range | `^4.18.2` | Compatible with 4.x.x but not 5.0.0 | PATCH and MINOR updates allowed |
| Exact Pin | `4.18.2` | Only this exact version | Maximum reproducibility |
| Tilde Range | `~4.18.2` | Compatible with 4.18.x | Only PATCH updates allowed |
| Greater Than | `>4.18.2` | Any version higher than 4.18.2 | Bleeding-edge adoption |
| Install Formula | $\text{Package Set} = D \cup D_{dev}$ | Union of runtime + dev dependencies | Project bootstrap math |
| Total Installed Count | $N_{total} = N_{direct} + \sum_{i=1}^{N_{direct}} N_{transitive_i}$ | Direct + transitive dependencies | Disk footprint analysis |

## 2.4 Local vs Global Installation: The Architectural Decision

**Local installation** is the KTU-recommended default for project-specific libraries. It isolates dependencies per-project, prevents version conflicts, and ensures the application is portable.

**Global installation** is reserved for **Command Line Interface (CLI) tools** that are invoked from the terminal regardless of project — for example, `npm install -g nodemon`. Globally installed packages do **not** appear in the local `node_modules`; they live in a system directory (e.g., `/usr/local/lib/node_modules` on Linux/macOS).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) pipelines (e.g., GitHub Actions, Jenkins), engineers rely on deterministic local installations. A `package-lock.json` is checked into version control so that every build server produces byte-identical `node_modules` trees.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Empty Directory to Installed Custom Package

We will mathematically walk through the lifecycle of installing a custom NPM package. Let the initial state of the project be a completely empty directory $D_0$.

$$
D_0 = \{\text{empty directory}\}
$$

### **Step 1: Initialize the Project Manifest**

Run `npm init -y` to scaffold a `package.json` with default values.

$$
D_0 \xrightarrow{\text{npm init -y}} D_1 = D_0 \cup \{\text{package.json}\}
$$

The generated `package.json` will contain:

$$
\text{package.json} = \begin{cases} \text{name} \\ \text{version} \\ \text{description} \\ \text{main} \\ \text{scripts} \\ \text{dependencies} = \emptyset \\ \text{devDependencies} = \emptyset \end{cases}
$$

> *Explanation:* The `npm init -y` flag auto-accepts all defaults. Without `-y`, NPM interactively prompts the user for each field.

### **Step 2: Invoke the Install Command for a Custom Package**

Suppose we want to install `express`, a popular web framework. We issue:

$$
\text{npm install express}
$$

NPM executes the Resolution Phase:

$$
\text{Resolve}(\text{express}) = \{v_{latest}, D_{transitive}\}
$$

where $v_{latest}$ is the current published version (e.g., **4.18.2**) and $D_{transitive}$ is the set of all packages `express` depends on (e.g., `accepts`, `body-parser`, `cookie`, `debug`, etc.).

### **Step 3: Calculate the Total Package Footprint**

The total number of packages installed is the sum of direct and transitive dependencies:

$$
N_{total} = N_{direct} + \sum_{i=1}^{N_{direct}} N_{transitive_i}
$$

For a typical `express` install:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{total} &= 1 \text{ (express)} + 30 \text{ (top-level transitive)} + 42 \text{ (second-level transitive)} \\
&= 73 \text{ packages}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Explanation:* Express itself has ~30 direct transitive dependencies, many of which have their own dependencies, leading to a deeply nested tree that NPM flattens.

### **Step 4: Manifest Mutation**

After successful installation, the `package.json` is mutated:

$$
\text{dependencies} = \emptyset \rightarrow \text{dependencies} = \{ \text{"express": "\^{}4.18.2"} \}
$$

The caret `^` symbol denotes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Range}(\^{}4.18.2) &= \{v \in \mathbb{V} \mid 4.18.2 \leq v < 5.0.0\} \\
&\approx \text{"all minor and patch upgrades of 4.x"}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 5: Disk State Finalization**

The final project state $D_{final}$ is:

$$
D_{final} = D_0 \cup \{\text{package.json}, \text{package-lock.json}, \text{node_modules/}\}
$$

where `node_modules/` contains all 73 packages flattened to the maximum extent permitted by dependency version constraints.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Common NPM Commands

| Command | Effect on `package.json` | Effect on `node_modules/` | KTU Exam Weight |
|---------|--------------------------|----------------------------|-----------------|
| `npm install express` | Adds to `dependencies` | Installs locally | **High** |
| `npm install express --save-dev` | Adds to `devDependencies` | Installs locally | **High** |
| `npm install -g nodemon` | No change | Installs to global prefix | **Medium** |
| `npm uninstall express` | Removes from `dependencies` | Removes folder | **Medium** |
| `npm install` (no args) | No change | Installs all listed | **High** |
| `npm install express@3.x` | Pins to 3.x range | Installs latest 3.x | **Low** |
| `npm update` | Updates within SemVer | Reinstalls newer | **Low** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 NPM Installation Pipeline (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Developer runs npm install package] --> B{package.json exists?}
    B -- No --> C[Throw ENOENT error]
    B -- Yes --> D[Read dependencies from manifest]
    D --> E[Query npm registry for metadata]
    E --> F{Version range satisfiable?}
    F -- No --> G[Throw ETARGET error]
    F -- Yes --> H[Download tarballs to npm cache]
    H --> I[Build dependency tree]
    I --> J[Flatten and hoist node_modules]
    J --> K[Write package-lock.json]
    K --> L[Update package.json with version]
    L --> M[Run package install scripts]
    M --> N[Installation complete]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Local vs Global

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GlobalScope[Global System Scope]
        G1[/usr/local/lib/node_modules/]
        G2[CLI tools: nodemon, tsc, yarn]
    end
    subgraph ProjectScope[Local Project Scope]
        P1[package.json]
        P2[package-lock.json]
        P3[node_modules/]
        P4[app.js entry point]
    end
    subgraph Registry[NPM Registry]
        R1[registry.npmjs.org]
    end
    Registry -- HTTP GET tarball --> P3
    Registry -- HTTP GET tarball --> G1
    P1 -- defines --> P3
    P2 -- locks versions in --> P3
    P4 -- requires --> P3
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: When to Use Local vs Global

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need to install npm package?] --> Q1{Used by project code?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Local[Install locally without -g]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Used as CLI command in terminal?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Global[Install globally with -g]
    Q2 -- No --> Reconsider[Reconsider: do you need this package?]
    Local --> Flag{Needed only in development?}
    Flag -- Yes --> DevDep[Use --save-dev]
    Flag -- No --> ProdDep[Use default --save]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Definition Type** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define NPM. What is the role of the `package.json` file in a Node.js project?**

**Model Answer:**

> **NPM (Node Package Manager)** is the default package manager for Node.js, providing a command-line interface and an online registry to install, manage, and share JavaScript packages.
>
> The `package.json` file is the **manifest** of a Node.js project. It serves as a JSON-formatted metadata file containing:
> 1. **Project metadata** — name, version, description, author, license
> 2. **Dependencies** — list of runtime packages required (`dependencies`)
> 3. **Dev dependencies** — packages needed only during development (`devDependencies`)
> 4. **Scripts** — custom command shortcuts (e.g., `"start": "node app.js"`)
> 5. **Entry point** — the main file to execute (default: `index.js`)
>
> It enables dependency reproducibility, project portability, and standardized project configuration.

---

### **Question 2: Comparison Type** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between local and global NPM package installation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Local Installation | Global Installation |
|---------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Command** | `npm install <pkg>` | `npm install -g <pkg>` |
| **Location** | `./node_modules/` of project | System-wide (e.g., `/usr/local/lib/node_modules`) |
| **Use Case** | Project-specific libraries | CLI tools used across projects |
| **package.json update** | Yes (added to dependencies) | No |
| **Scope of access** | Only within the project | Anywhere on the system |
| **Example** | `npm install express` | `npm install -g nodemon` |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Forgetting `--save` or `--save-dev` distinction** — Examiners explicitly check whether students understand that the former is for runtime and the latter for tooling. Confusing these costs **3 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing `npm install` (no args) with `npm install <pkg>`** — The former installs from `package.json`; the latter adds a new package. Examiners frequently test this in 2-mark sub-parts.
> 3. **Ignoring `package-lock.json`** — Students often omit it. It is a **mandatory 2-mark topic** in long-answer questions.

---

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure to initialize a Node.js project and install a custom package called `express` locally. Include the relevant commands and describe the resulting directory structure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is `package-lock.json`? How does it differ from `package.json`? Why is it important in collaborative development? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

1. **Open terminal** in an empty project directory and initialize NPM:
   ```bash
   mkdir my-web-app
   cd my-web-app
   npm init -y
   ```
   This generates a default `package.json`.

2. **Install the custom `express` package locally:**
   ```bash
   npm install express
   ```

3. **Resulting directory structure:**
   ```
   my-web-app/
   ├── node_modules/        (contains express + 72 transitive packages)
   ├── package.json         (now lists "express": "^4.18.2" in dependencies)
   ├── package-lock.json    (locks exact versions installed)
   └── app.js               (user-authored entry point)
   ```

4. **Verify the installation:**
   ```bash
   node -e "console.log(require('express'))"
   ```

**Model Solution for (b):**

`package-lock.json` is an **auto-generated lockfile** that records the **exact** version of every package and sub-package installed in the `node_modules` tree, along with their integrity hashes (SHA-512).

**Differences from `package.json`:**

| Aspect | `package.json` | `package-lock.json` |
|--------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Author** | Manually written / `npm init` | Auto-generated by npm |
| **Content** | Declared dependency **ranges** | Resolved **exact** versions |
| **Mutability** | Edited by developer | Should not be manually edited |
| **Purpose** | Specifies **what** is needed | Specifies **what was installed** |
| **Sharing** | Yes, must be committed | Yes, must be committed |

**Importance in collaborative development:**
1. Ensures **byte-identical** dependency trees across all team members' machines.
2. Prevents the "works on my machine" syndrome by locking transitive versions.
3. Enables reproducible CI/CD builds.
4. Speeds up installs via cache validation using integrity hashes.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of Semantic Versioning (SemVer) as used by NPM. Illustrate with an example of how the symbols `^`, `~`, and exact pinning affect the version range. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Demonstrate how to install a package globally and explain with an example when global installation is preferred over local installation. Also list any two issues that may arise from incorrect global installations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Semantic Versioning (SemVer)** is a versioning convention with the format:

$$
\text{Version} = \text{MAJOR}.\text{MINOR}.\text{PATCH}
$$

where:
- **MAJOR** — incremented for breaking changes
- **MINOR** — incremented for new backward-compatible features
- **PATCH** — incremented for backward-compatible bug fixes

**Range operators:**

Let $v = 4.18.2$ be the target version. The three operator behaviors:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Caret (\^{}):} \quad & \text{Range} = \{x \mid 4.18.2 \leq x < 5.0.0\} \\
& \text{NPM installs: 4.18.2, 4.19.0, 4.20.5, etc., but NOT 5.0.0} \\[6pt]
\text{Tilde (\textasciitilde{}):} \quad & \text{Range} = \{x \mid 4.18.2 \leq x < 4.19.0\} \\
& \text{NPM installs: 4.18.2, 4.18.5, 4.18.9, etc., but NOT 4.19.0} \\[6pt]
\text{Exact (no operator):} \quad & \text{Range} = \{4.18.2\} \\
& \text{NPM installs: ONLY 4.18.2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example in `package.json`:**
```json
{
  "dependencies": {
    "express": "^4.18.2",
    "lodash": "~4.17.21",
    "axios": "1.6.0"
  }
}
```

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Global installation procedure:**
```bash
npm install -g nodemon
```

After execution, `nodemon` is available as a CLI command in any terminal session because its executable is symlinked into a directory on the system `PATH` (e.g., `/usr/local/bin/nodemon`).

**When global is preferred:**
1. Installing **CLI tools** like `nodemon`, `typescript`, `yarn`, `create-react-app` that are invoked from the terminal.
2. Tools used across **multiple projects** (e.g., a global linter or formatter).
3. System-level utilities not tied to a single project.

**Two issues with incorrect global installations:**
1. **Version conflicts** — If Project A needs `nodemon@1.x` and Project B needs `nodemon@3.x`, a single global install cannot satisfy both.
2. **Permission errors (EACCES)** — On macOS/Linux, installing globally without `sudo` or a version manager like **nvm** causes write-permission denials.
3. **Polluted system PATH** — Too many global packages can slow down shell autocomplete and cause command shadowing.
4. **Non-reproducibility** — Globally installed tools are not part of the project's `package.json`, breaking team consistency.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **NPM** stands for **Node Package Manager** — the default package manager for Node.js, paired with a public registry at `registry.npmjs.org`.
- `npm init -y` bootstraps a project with a default `package.json`.
- `npm install <package>` installs a package **locally** and saves it to `dependencies` by default (since npm 5+).
- `npm install <package> --save-dev` saves to `devDependencies` — used for build/test tools.
- `npm install -g <package>` performs **global installation**, primarily for CLI tools like `nodemon` and `typescript`.
- `package.json` is the **manifest**; `package-lock.json` is the **lockfile** ensuring reproducible installs.
- **Semantic Versioning** uses the `MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH` format, with `^` allowing minor+patch upgrades, `~` allowing only patch upgrades, and exact pinning locking to a single version.
- Globally installed packages **do not** modify the local `package.json`.
- Always commit `package-lock.json` to version control to guarantee team and CI/CD consistency.
- **Latest LTS Node.js version** at syllabus publication: **v20.x**.
- The `node_modules/` directory can balloon to **50–100+ packages** for a single direct dependency due to transitive resolution.
- Use `npm uninstall <package>` to remove a package and its entry from `package.json`.
- For team consistency and reproducibility, prefer **local** installations over global ones wherever possible.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Express
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Express

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Express.js** (commonly referred to as **Express**) is a *fast, unopinionated, minimalist web framework for Node.js*. It is an open-source server-side web application framework distributed under the **MIT License** as free and open-source software. Express provides a robust set of features for building **single-page, multi-page, and hybrid web applications**, as well as **RESTful APIs** (Application Programming Interfaces).

Formally, Express is built on top of Node.js's built-in `http` module and acts as a thin abstraction layer, providing:
- A powerful **routing** system to map HTTP requests to handler functions.
- A flexible **middleware** pipeline to process requests in a sequential chain.
- Simplified **request (req)** and **response (res)** object APIs.
- Built-in support for serving **static files** and rendering **dynamic templates** via engines like EJS, Pug, or Handlebars.
- Robust mechanisms for **error handling** and asynchronous control flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The KTU 2024 B.Tech CSE syllabus lists Express as the *de facto* standard server-side framework for the MERN/MEAN stack. It is positioned as the foundational layer that bridges vanilla Node.js with real-world, production-grade web applications.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large, busy restaurant:

| Restaurant Component | Express.js Equivalent |
|---|---|
| **Customer** walking in with an order | **HTTP Request** from a client (browser/mobile) |
| **Receptionist / Waiter** | **Express Application & Middleware** chain |
| **Menu (with table numbers)** | **Routes** (e.g., `/home`, `/products`, `/api/users`) |
| **Kitchen** | **Route Handlers / Business Logic / Database** |
| **Food served back to customer** | **HTTP Response** (HTML, JSON, file, etc.) |

> Just as a waiter doesn't cook the food but **coordinates** between customer and kitchen, Express doesn't replace Node.js's core power — it simply **organizes the conversation** between incoming HTTP requests and the underlying Node.js server, making the code clean, modular, and scalable.

### 1.3 Why Express? — The "Why" Behind the Framework

Vanilla Node.js can create servers, but the code becomes **verbose, repetitive, and error-prone** for real applications. Express solves this by:

- **Reducing boilerplate**: A "Hello World" server in raw Node.js takes ~15 lines; in Express, only **3 lines**.
- **Standardizing routing**: No need to manually parse `req.url` and switch statements.
- **Enabling middleware composition**: Plug-and-play request processors (logging, authentication, parsing).
- **Boosting productivity**: Industry-standard, massive ecosystem of plugins (`express.Router`, `body-parser`, `cors`, `helmet`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology to Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - **Framework vs. Library**: Express is a *framework* — it calls your code (Inversion of Control).
> - **Unopinionated**: It does not force a specific project structure; the developer chooses.
> - **Middleware**: A function that has access to `req`, `res`, and the `next()` function.
> - **REST**: Representational State Transfer — an architectural style for designing networked APIs.

### 1.4 Position in the Modern Web Stack

Express typically sits in the **backend tier** of the standard web architecture:

$$\text{Client (Browser/React)} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Express Server (Node.js)} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Database (MongoDB/MySQL)}$$

It is the **E** in the popular **MERN** (MongoDB, Express, React, Node.js) and **MEAN** (MongoDB, Express, Angular, Node.js) stacks, and remains the most starred backend framework on **npm** (Node Package Manager), the world's largest software registry, with over **65 million downloads per week** as of 2024.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 The Express Architecture — How It Works Internally

Express is essentially a **layered middleware pipeline** built on top of Node.js's `http.createServer()`. Every incoming HTTP request flows through a **sequence of middleware functions** until a response is sent or an error is thrown.

The core operational flow can be broken down into **six logical stages**:

1. **Server Initialization**: `express()` creates an application object (`app`) that holds all configuration, routes, and middleware.
2. **Request Reception**: Node.js's `http` module receives the raw HTTP request and passes it to Express.
3. **Middleware Processing**: Each middleware function executes in registration order. It can:
   - Execute any code.
   - Make changes to the `req` and `res` objects.
   - **End the request-response cycle** by calling `res.send()`, `res.json()`, etc.
   - **Pass control** to the next middleware by calling `next()`.
   - **Trigger an error** by calling `next(err)`.
4. **Route Matching**: Express matches the request method (`GET`, `POST`, etc.) and URL path to a defined route.
5. **Handler Execution**: The matched route handler processes the request and produces a response.
6. **Response Transmission**: The final response (HTML, JSON, file, status code) is sent back to the client.

### 2.2 The Request-Response Cycle (RRC)

The fundamental contract in Express is the **Request-Response Cycle**. The mathematical abstraction of this cycle can be represented as:

$$\text{Request}_{in} \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{Middleware Chain}\;}\; f_1 \circ f_2 \circ f_3 \circ \dots \circ f_n \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{Response}\;}\; \text{Response}_{out}$$

Where each $f_i$ is a middleware function. The cycle **terminates** when any $f_i$ invokes `res.end()` (or its equivalent like `res.send()`) **without** calling `next()`.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Table

| **Concept** | **Syntax / API** | **Purpose** | **KTU Exam Frequency** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Create App** | `const app = express();` | Initializes an Express application | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Start Server** | `app.listen(port, callback)` | Binds server to a TCP port | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **GET Route** | `app.get(path, handler)` | Handles HTTP GET requests | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **POST Route** | `app.post(path, handler)` | Handles HTTP POST requests | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Middleware** | `app.use(middlewareFn)` | Registers middleware globally | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Route Params** | `req.params.id` | Extracts URL parameters (e.g., `/users/:id`) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Query Params** | `req.query.search` | Extracts `?key=value` from URL | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Send JSON** | `res.json({...})` | Sends a JSON response with `Content-Type: application/json` | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Status Code** | `res.status(404).send()` | Sets HTTP status before sending | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Static Files** | `express.static('public')` | Serves static assets (CSS, JS, images) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Router** | `express.Router()` | Creates modular, mountable route handlers | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Body Parser** | `express.json()`, `express.urlencoded()` | Parses incoming request bodies | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Error Handler** | `(err, req, res, next) => {...}` | 4-argument signature for errors | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **next()** | Function call | Passes control to the next middleware | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

### 2.4 HTTP Methods Supported by Express

Express routing corresponds directly to the seven standard **HTTP verbs** (also called methods) defined in **RFC 9110** (the current HTTP standard):

$$\text{HTTP Methods} = \{\,\text{GET},\; \text{POST},\; \text{PUT},\; \text{DELETE},\; \text{PATCH},\; \text{HEAD},\; \text{OPTIONS}\,\}$$

In KTU exams, the most commonly tested are **GET** (retrieve data), **POST** (create data), **PUT** (replace data), and **DELETE** (remove data) — together forming the **CRUD** operations (Create, Read, Update, Delete) that map onto **RESTful API** design.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Express is not just an academic tool — it powers some of the world's most demanding production systems:

- **PayPal** rebuilt its account dashboard using Node.js + Express, handling billions of requests.
- **Uber** uses Node.js (often with Express) for its massive real-time matching engine.
- **IBM, Mozilla, and Walmart** leverage Express for microservices.
- In academia and startups, Express is the **go-to choice** for building **RESTful APIs** that serve Single-Page Applications (SPAs) built with React, Angular, or Vue.

### 2.6 Express vs. Vanilla Node.js — A Comparative Insight

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** *"Why use Express when Node.js can already create servers?"*

The answer lies in **abstraction and maintainability**. The same "Hello World" server in both:

**Vanilla Node.js (verbose):**
```javascript
const http = require('http');
const server = http.createServer((req, res) => {
  if (req.url === '/' && req.method === 'GET') {
    res.writeHead(200, {'Content-Type': 'text/plain'});
    res.end('Hello World');
  } else {
    res.writeHead(404);
    res.end('Not Found');
  }
});
server.listen(3000);
```

**With Express (concise):**
```javascript
const express = require('express');
const app = express();
app.get('/', (req, res) => res.send('Hello World'));
app.listen(3000);
```

Notice the **75% reduction** in lines of code. As applications grow to dozens of routes, this advantage compounds exponentially.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Practical Implementation & Analytical Walkthrough

> Since Express is a *practical* CS framework (not a mathematical theorem), Section 3 contains a **complete step-by-step implementation walkthrough** — from installation to a working REST API — which is exactly what KTU board questions demand for "Explain with code" or "Write a program" questions.

### 3.1 Step-by-Step: Building Your First Express Application

#### **STEP 1: Environment Setup**

Ensure **Node.js** (LTS version 18+ recommended) and **npm** are installed. Verify:

```bash
node --version
npm --version
```

**Logic**: Node.js is the JavaScript runtime; npm is its package manager. Both are prerequisites because Express itself is a npm package that runs on the Node.js engine.

#### **STEP 2: Project Initialization**

```bash
mkdir my-express-app
cd my-express-app
npm init -y
```

**Logic**: `npm init -y` creates a `package.json` file with default values. This file acts as the **manifest** of the project — it tracks dependencies, scripts, and metadata. KTU exams often ask students to explain this file.

#### **STEP 3: Install Express**

```bash
npm install express
```

**Logic**: This downloads the latest stable version of Express from the npm registry into a local `node_modules` folder and adds `"express": "^4.x.x"` to the `dependencies` section of `package.json`. The `^` (caret) symbol means *"compatible with version 4.x.x, but allow minor/patch upgrades"*.

#### **STEP 4: Create the Entry Point — `app.js`**

```javascript
// app.js
// Step 1: Import the express module
const express = require('express');

// Step 2: Invoke express() to create an application instance
const app = express();

// Step 3 (Optional but best practice): Define a port number
const PORT = 3000;

// Step 4: Register a built-in middleware to parse JSON request bodies
// This allows us to read JSON data sent in POST/PUT requests
app.use(express.json());

// Step 5: Define a simple GET route for the root URL '/'
app.get('/', (req, res) => {
  // res.send() automatically sets Content-Type and sends the response
  res.send('Welcome to My First Express App!');
});

// Step 6: Start the server and listen on the defined port
app.listen(PORT, () => {
  console.log(`Server is running on http://localhost:${PORT}`);
});
```

**Logic of each step**:
- `require('express')` — CommonJS module import (Node.js's module system).
- `express()` — Factory function that returns a fresh app object.
- `app.use(express.json())` — Registers middleware globally. Without this, `req.body` would be `undefined` in POST requests.
- `app.get('/', handler)` — Defines a route. The handler receives `req` (request) and `res` (response) objects.
- `app.listen(PORT, callback)` — Internally calls Node.js's `http.createServer(app).listen(PORT)`.

#### **STEP 5: Run the Application**

```bash
node app.js
```

**Output**:
```
Server is running on http://localhost:3000
```

Open a browser and visit `http://localhost:3000` — you will see: **"Welcome to My First Express App!"**

#### **STEP 6: Verify with cURL (Command Line)**

```bash
curl http://localhost:3000
```

**Output**:
```
Welcome to My First Express App!
```

### 3.2 Building a Complete RESTful API — Analytical Walkthrough

Below is a **full CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) API** for a "products" resource — a common KTU exam question pattern.

```javascript
// app.js — Full RESTful API Example
const express = require('express');
const app = express();

// Middleware: Parses incoming JSON bodies
app.use(express.json());

// In-memory "database" (for demonstration; in production, use MongoDB/PostgreSQL)
let products = [
  { id: 1, name: 'Laptop', price: 75000 },
  { id: 2, name: 'Smartphone', price: 35000 }
];

// READ ALL — GET /api/products
app.get('/api/products', (req, res) => {
  res.status(200).json({
    success: true,
    count: products.length,
    data: products
  });
});

// READ ONE — GET /api/products/:id
app.get('/api/products/:id', (req, res) => {
  const id = parseInt(req.params.id);
  const product = products.find(p => p.id === id);
  if (!product) {
    return res.status(404).json({ success: false, message: 'Product not found' });
  }
  res.status(200).json({ success: true, data: product });
});

// CREATE — POST /api/products
app.post('/api/products', (req, res) => {
  const { name, price } = req.body;
  if (!name || !price) {
    return res.status(400).json({ success: false, message: 'Name and price are required' });
  }
  const newProduct = {
    id: products.length > 0 ? products[products.length - 1].id + 1 : 1,
    name,
    price
  };
  products.push(newProduct);
  res.status(201).json({ success: true, data: newProduct });
});

// UPDATE — PUT /api/products/:id
app.put('/api/products/:id', (req, res) => {
  const id = parseInt(req.params.id);
  const index = products.findIndex(p => p.id === id);
  if (index === -1) {
    return res.status(404).json({ success: false, message: 'Product not found' });
  }
  products[index] = { id, ...req.body };
  res.status(200).json({ success: true, data: products[index] });
});

// DELETE — DELETE /api/products/:id
app.delete('/api/products/:id', (req, res) => {
  const id = parseInt(req.params.id);
  const index = products.findIndex(p => p.id === id);
  if (index === -1) {
    return res.status(404).json({ success: false, message: 'Product not found' });
  }
  const deleted = products.splice(index, 1);
  res.status(200).json({ success: true, data: deleted[0] });
});

// Centralized Error-Handling Middleware (must be LAST, with 4 arguments)
app.use((err, req, res, next) => {
  console.error(err.stack);
  res.status(500).json({ success: false, message: 'Internal Server Error' });
});

const PORT = process.env.PORT || 3000;
app.listen(PORT, () => console.log(`Server running on port ${PORT}`));
```

### 3.3 Test the API with cURL — Step-by-Step

| **Step** | **Command** | **Expected Status** | **Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `curl http://localhost:3000/api/products` | 200 | Retrieves all products |
| 2 | `curl -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"name":"Tablet","price":25000}' http://localhost:3000/api/products` | 201 | Creates a new product |
| 3 | `curl http://localhost:3000/api/products/3` | 200 | Retrieves product with id=3 |
| 4 | `curl -X PUT -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"name":"Tablet Pro","price":30000}' http://localhost:3000/api/products/3` | 200 | Updates product 3 |
| 5 | `curl -X DELETE http://localhost:3000/api/products/3` | 200 | Deletes product 3 |

### 3.4 Understanding `req` and `res` — The Two Pillars

**Request Object (`req`) Properties:**

| Property | Example | Source |
|---|---|---|
| `req.params` | `{ id: '5' }` | URL path parameters (`/users/:id`) |
| `req.query` | `{ search: 'phone' }` | URL query string (`?search=phone`) |
| `req.body` | `{ name: 'John' }` | Request body (requires body-parser middleware) |
| `req.headers` | `{ 'user-agent': '...' }` | HTTP headers |
| `req.method` | `'GET'` | HTTP method |
| `req.url` | `'/api/products'` | Request URL path |

**Response Object (`res`) Methods:**

| Method | Behavior | Sets Status |
|---|---|---|
| `res.send(data)` | Sends string, object, or buffer | 200 |
| `res.json(obj)` | Sends JSON response | 200 |
| `res.status(code)` | Sets status code (chainable) | Custom |
| `res.redirect(url)` | Redirects to another URL | 302 |
| `res.render(view, data)` | Renders a template | 200 |
| `res.sendFile(path)` | Sends a file | 200 |
| `res.end()` | Ends response without data | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Express Request-Response Lifecycle (Mermaid Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client as Browser or Mobile App
    participant NodeCore as Node.js HTTP Server
    participant App as Express Application
    participant MW1 as Middleware 1 Logger
    participant MW2 as Middleware 2 Auth Check
    participant Router as Route Handler
    participant DB as Database Layer
    participant ErrorHandler as Error Middleware

    Client->>NodeCore: HTTP Request GET or POST with headers and body
    NodeCore->>App: Forwards raw request as req and res
    App->>MW1: Pass through middleware chain
    MW1->>MW2: Logs timestamp and calls next
    MW2->>Router: Validates token and calls next
    Router->>DB: Queries or mutates data
    DB-->>Router: Returns result set
    Router-->>App: Calls res.json with data
    App-->>NodeCore: Sends formatted HTTP response
    NodeCore-->>Client: HTTP Response with status and body

    Note over MW1,MW2: If any middleware throws or calls next err control jumps to ErrorHandler
    Note over Router,DB: If route not matched Express returns default 404
```

### 4.2 Middleware Execution Order (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incoming HTTP Request] --> B[Logger Middleware]
    B --> C{CORS Check}
    C -->|Allowed| D[Body Parser express.json]
    C -->|Blocked| Z1[403 Forbidden Response]
    D --> E[Authentication Middleware]
    E --> F{Valid Token?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Route Matching]
    F -->|No| Z2[401 Unauthorized Response]
    G --> H{Route Found?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Route Handler Executes]
    H -->|No| Z3[404 Not Found Response]
    I --> J[Business Logic and DB Query]
    J --> K[res.send or res.json]
    K --> L[HTTP Response to Client]
    I -.Error Thrown.-> M[Error Handling Middleware]
    M --> N[500 Internal Server Error Response]
```

### 4.3 Express Application Structure (Project Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ProjectRoot[Project Root Directory]
        direction TB
        PKG[package.json]
        APPJS[app.js Entry Point]
        ENV[.env Environment Variables]
    end

    subgraph RoutesFolder[Routes Folder]
        direction TB
        ROUTEUSER[userRoutes.js]
        ROUTEPROD[productRoutes.js]
        ROUTEAUTH[authRoutes.js]
    end

    subgraph ControllersFolder[Controllers Folder]
        direction TB
        CTRLU[userController.js]
        CTRLPR[productController.js]
    end

    subgraph MiddlewareFolder[Middleware Folder]
        direction TB
        MWLOG[logger.js]
        MWAUTH[authValidator.js]
        MWERR[errorHandler.js]
    end

    subgraph ModelsFolder[Models Folder]
        direction TB
        MODELU[User.js]
        MODELP[Product.js]
    end

    APPJS --> RoutesFolder
    APPJS --> MiddlewareFolder
    APPJS --> ModelsFolder
    RoutesFolder --> ControllersFolder
    ControllersFolder --> ModelsFolder
    RoutesFolder -.Uses.-> MiddlewareFolder
    ControllersFolder -.Throws Errors.-> MWERR
```

### 4.4 RESTful API Resource Mapping (Conceptual Matrix)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ClientSide[Client Side]
        UI[User Interface]
    end

    subgraph ExpressServer[Express Server]
        direction TB
        G[GET api products]
        GP[GET api products colon id]
        P[POST api products]
        PU[PUT api products colon id]
        D[DELETE api products colon id]
    end

    subgraph DatabaseLayer[Database]
        T[(products table or collection)]
    end

    UI -->|View All| G
    UI -->|View One| GP
    UI -->|Create New| P
    UI -->|Update Existing| PU
    UI -->|Delete Item| D
    G --> T
    GP --> T
    P --> T
    PU --> T
    D --> T
    T --> G
    T --> GP
    T --> P
    T --> PU
    T --> D
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
**Define Express.js. List any four of its key features.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Express.js is a fast, unopinionated, and minimalist web application framework for Node.js. It provides a thin layer of fundamental web application features on top of Node.js, making the development of web servers and APIs faster and easier.

**Key Features (4 × ~0.375 Marks = 1.5 Marks):**

1. **Robust Routing System**: Maps HTTP requests (URL + method) to handler functions cleanly.
2. **Middleware Support**: Composable, plug-and-play request processing pipeline.
3. **Templating Engine Integration**: Supports EJS, Pug, Handlebars for dynamic HTML.
4. **Static File Serving**: Built-in `express.static()` for CSS, JS, images.
5. **Error Handling**: Dedicated error-handling middleware with the 4-argument signature.
6. **REST API Support**: Simplifies building JSON-based RESTful services.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Any 4 features with brief descriptions earn full marks. Avoid writing only feature names without explanation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**
**Explain the concept of middleware in Express.js with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Concept (1.5 Marks):**
Middleware in Express.js refers to functions that have access to the **request object (`req`)**, the **response object (`res`)**, and the **next middleware function** in the application's request-response cycle (commonly denoted as `next`). Middleware functions can:
- Execute any code.
- Modify the `req` and `res` objects.
- End the request-response cycle.
- Call the next middleware in the stack.

**Example (1.5 Marks):**
```javascript
// Custom logger middleware
app.use((req, res, next) => {
  console.log(`${req.method} ${req.url} - ${new Date().toISOString()}`);
  next(); // Pass control to the next middleware
});
```

Here, the middleware logs every incoming request's method, URL, and timestamp, then calls `next()` to allow the request to continue through the pipeline.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has an **internal choice** — students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Both questions are below, fully solved.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of Express.js with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the `app` object, `req` object, and `res` object.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a complete Express.js program to create a RESTful API for managing a "books" resource with the following endpoints:
- `GET /api/books` — retrieve all books
- `POST /api/books` — add a new book
- `GET /api/books/:id` — retrieve a specific book
- `DELETE /api/books/:id` — remove a book

Use an in-memory array to store books.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Architecture of Express.js (7 Marks)**

**Architecture Explanation (5 Marks):**

Express.js follows a **middleware-driven, request-response cycle architecture**. The key components are:

1. **The `app` Object**: Created by invoking `express()`. It is the central configuration object that holds:
   - All registered middleware (via `app.use()`).
   - All defined routes (via `app.get()`, `app.post()`, etc.).
   - Settings and view engine configurations.
   - It internally extends Node.js's `http.Server` via the `app.listen()` method.

2. **The `req` (Request) Object**: Represents the incoming HTTP request. It contains properties like:
   - `req.params` — URL path parameters.
   - `req.query` — URL query string values.
   - `req.body` — Parsed request payload (requires body-parser).
   - `req.headers` — HTTP headers.
   - `req.method` — HTTP method (GET, POST, etc.).

3. **The `res` (Response) Object**: Represents the HTTP response that the Express server will send back. Common methods:
   - `res.send(data)` — Send a response (string, object, buffer).
   - `res.json(obj)` — Send a JSON response.
   - `res.status(code)` — Set HTTP status code.
   - `res.redirect(url)` — Redirect to a different URL.
   - `res.sendFile(path)` — Send a file.

4. **Middleware Pipeline**: The `app` routes each request through a chain of middleware functions in order. Each middleware can perform logic, modify `req`/`res`, and either respond or call `next()`.

5. **Routing Layer**: Matches the request method + URL pattern to a handler function. Patterns can include parameters (e.g., `/users/:id`).

**Diagram (2 Marks)** — *(Refer to Section 4.1 in this document; student should redraw it neatly)*

---

**(b) Complete Program for Books API (7 Marks)**

```javascript
// books-api.js
const express = require('express');
const app = express();
const PORT = 3000;

// Middleware to parse JSON bodies
app.use(express.json());

// In-memory data store
let books = [
  { id: 1, title: 'The Alchemist', author: 'Paulo Coelho' },
  { id: 2, title: '1984', author: 'George Orwell' }
];

// (i) GET /api/books — Retrieve all books
app.get('/api/books', (req, res) => {
  res.status(200).json({
    success: true,
    count: books.length,
    data: books
  });
});

// (ii) POST /api/books — Add a new book
app.post('/api/books', (req, res) => {
  const { title, author } = req.body;
  // Validation
  if (!title || !author) {
    return res.status(400).json({
      success: false,
      message: 'Both title and author are required'
    });
  }
  const newBook = {
    id: books.length > 0 ? books[books.length - 1].id + 1 : 1,
    title,
    author
  };
  books.push(newBook);
  res.status(201).json({ success: true, data: newBook });
});

// (iii) GET /api/books/:id — Retrieve a specific book
app.get('/api/books/:id', (req, res) => {
  const id = parseInt(req.params.id);
  const book = books.find(b => b.id === id);
  if (!book) {
    return res.status(404).json({
      success: false,
      message: `Book with id ${id} not found`
    });
  }
  res.status(200).json({ success: true, data: book });
});

// (iv) DELETE /api/books/:id — Remove a book
app.delete('/api/books/:id', (req, res) => {
  const id = parseInt(req.params.id);
  const index = books.findIndex(b => b.id === id);
  if (index === -1) {
    return res.status(404).json({
      success: false,
      message: `Book with id ${id} not found`
    });
  }
  const deletedBook = books.splice(index, 1);
  res.status(200).json({ success: true, data: deletedBook[0] });
});

// Start server
app.listen(PORT, () => {
  console.log(`Books API running on http://localhost:${PORT}`);
});
```

**Explanation of Key Code Sections (1 Mark for brief explanation):**
- `express.json()` middleware enables parsing of JSON request bodies.
- `req.body` accesses the parsed payload in POST requests.
- `req.params.id` extracts the URL parameter `:id` and converts it to a number.
