- HTTP status codes `200`, `201`, `404`, and `400` are used appropriately to indicate success, creation, not-found, and bad-request scenarios.
- The data store is an in-memory array; in production, this would be replaced with a database like MongoDB.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare Express.js with vanilla Node.js `http` module. Why is Express preferred for building modern web applications? List and explain any three built-in or commonly used middleware in Express.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write an Express.js program that demonstrates:
- A custom middleware that logs each request's method, URL, and timestamp.
- A route `/profile/:username` that returns a JSON greeting with the username from the URL parameter.
- A static file server serving files from a folder named `public`.
- A 404 handler for unmatched routes.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Express.js vs. Vanilla Node.js (7 Marks)**

**Comparison Table (3 Marks):**

| **Feature** | **Vanilla Node.js (`http` module)** | **Express.js** |
|---|---|---|
| **Code Length** | Verbose; requires manual `req.url` parsing | Concise; declarative route definitions |
| **Routing** | Manual `if/else` or `switch` on `req.url` | Built-in `app.get()`, `app.post()`, etc. |
| **Middleware** | Not natively supported | First-class middleware pipeline |
| **Static Files** | Manual file reading with `fs` module | `express.static()` built-in |
| **Body Parsing** | Manual stream-based parsing | `express.json()`, `express.urlencoded()` |
| **Templating** | Not built-in | Native support for EJS, Pug, Handlebars |
| **Error Handling** | Manual try/catch boilerplate | Dedicated error-handling middleware |
| **Learning Curve** | Steeper for large apps | Gentler; opinion-free structure |

**Why Express is Preferred (2 Marks):**
- **Productivity**: Drastically reduces boilerplate code.
- **Maintainability**: Modular structure with middleware and routers.
- **Scalability**: Easy to organize into routers, controllers, and services.
- **Community & Ecosystem**: Largest ecosystem of plugins and middleware.
- **Industry Standard**: Backbone of the MERN/MEAN stacks.

**Three Important Middleware (2 Marks):**

1. **`express.json()`** — Parses incoming requests with JSON payloads and exposes the parsed object on `req.body`. Essential for POST/PUT APIs.

2. **`express.urlencoded({ extended: true })`** — Parses URL-encoded form data (`application/x-www-form-urlencoded`). The `extended: true` option allows rich objects and arrays using the `qs` library.

3. **`express.static('public')`** — Serves static files (HTML, CSS, JS, images) from the specified directory. It sets proper cache headers and handles `ETag` automatically for performance.

> *(Other valid middleware: `cors`, `helmet`, `morgan`, `cookie-parser`)*

---

**(b) Express Program Demonstrating Middleware, Route Params, Static Files, and 404 Handler (7 Marks)**

```javascript
// app.js
const express = require('express');
const path = require('path');
const app = express();
const PORT = 3000;

// ===== (i) Custom Logging Middleware =====
const requestLogger = (req, res, next) => {
  const timestamp = new Date().toISOString();
  console.log(`[${timestamp}] ${req.method} ${req.url}`);
  next(); // Pass control to the next middleware
};
app.use(requestLogger);

// ===== (ii) Route with URL Parameter =====
app.get('/profile/:username', (req, res) => {
  const username = req.params.username;
  res.status(200).json({
    success: true,
    greeting: `Hello, ${username}! Welcome to your profile.`,
    profileUrl: req.originalUrl,
    timestamp: new Date().toISOString()
  });
});

// ===== (iii) Static File Server =====
// Serves files from the 'public' folder at the root URL
// Example: public/style.css is accessible at http://localhost:3000/style.css
app.use(express.static(path.join(__dirname, 'public')));

// ===== (iv) 404 Handler for Unmatched Routes =====
// This must come AFTER all other routes
app.use((req, res) => {
  res.status(404).json({
    success: false,
    error: 'Route Not Found',
    message: `The requested URL ${req.originalUrl} does not exist on this server.`
  });
});

// Start server
app.listen(PORT, () => {
  console.log(`Server running on http://localhost:${PORT}`);
});
```

**Required Folder Structure (1 Mark — usually credited for understanding):**

```
my-express-app/
├── app.js              ← main file (code above)
├── package.json
└── public/             ← static files served automatically
    ├── index.html
    ├── style.css
    └── script.js
```

**Step-by-Step Logic (Brief Explanation for 1 Mark):**
1. The `requestLogger` middleware runs for **every** request and logs the method, URL, and timestamp.
2. Visiting `/profile/Alice` will respond with a JSON greeting.
3. The `express.static` middleware automatically serves files from the `public` folder; e.g., `public/index.html` is served at `/`.
4. The final `app.use((req, res) => ...)` with no path matches **any** request that wasn't handled earlier, returning a custom 404 JSON response.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting `app.use(express.json())`** → `req.body` will be `undefined`. **(−1 to −2 marks)**
> 2. **Writing `app.use((err, req, res, next) => ...)` in the wrong position** (not last) → error handler will not catch errors. **(−1 mark)**
> 3. **Using `res.send()` after `res.json()`** → triggers "headers already sent" error. **(−1 mark)**
> 4. **Not using proper HTTP status codes** (e.g., always returning 200 for errors) → fails RESTful design criteria. **(−1 to −2 marks)**
> 5. **Defining the 404 handler BEFORE other routes** → routes will never be reached. **(−2 marks)**
> 6. **Forgetting to call `next()` in custom middleware** → request hangs forever. **(−2 marks)**
> 7. **Spelling `express` as `Express` or `EXPRESS`** in `require()`. **(−1 mark)**
> 8. **Not including `package.json` mention** when asked "how to set up an Express project". **(−1 mark)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam**

- ✅ **Express.js** is a minimal, unopinionated Node.js web framework.
- ✅ It is installed via `npm install express` and imported with `require('express')`.
- ✅ An Express app is created by calling `express()`, which returns the `app` object.
- ✅ **Routing** uses `app.METHOD(path, handler)` for `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`, etc.
- ✅ **Middleware** functions have signature `(req, res, next)`; they execute in **registration order**.
- ✅ **Error-handling middleware** has a unique 4-argument signature: `(err, req, res, next)`.
- ✅ `req.params` → URL parameters; `req.query` → query string; `req.body` → payload (needs parser).
- ✅ `res.send()`, `res.json()`, `res.status()`, `res.redirect()` are the four most-used response methods.
- ✅ `express.static('folder')` serves static files; must be used **before** route definitions when needed.
- ✅ `express.json()` and `express.urlencoded()` are built-in body parsers.
- ✅ The **request-response cycle** ends when a middleware sends a response **without** calling `next()`.
- ✅ HTTP status codes to remember: **200** (OK), **201** (Created), **400** (Bad Request), **404** (Not Found), **500** (Server Error).
- ✅ Express is the **E** in **MERN** and **MEAN** stacks.
- ✅ For modular code, use `express.Router()` to create sub-routers.
- ✅ The 404 handler must be the **last** `app.use()` call, with **no path** specified.
- ✅ To start a server: `app.listen(port, callback)` — internally wraps Node.js's `http.createServer`.

**Key Default Status Codes Table:**

| **Scenario** | **Code** | **Meaning** |
|---|---|---|
| Successful GET / Read | 200 | OK |
| Successful POST / Create | 201 | Created |
| Successful PUT / Update | 200 | OK |
| Successful DELETE | 200 or 204 | OK / No Content |
| Client Validation Failed | 400 | Bad Request |
| Missing / Invalid Auth | 401 | Unauthorized |
| Auth OK but No Permission | 403 | Forbidden |
| Resource Not Found | 404 | Not Found |
| Server-Side Error | 500 | Internal Server Error |

**Final Exam Strategy:**
- Always write **clean, indented code** with comments explaining each step.
- Always **show the `npm install` command** and mention `package.json`.
- Always include **status codes** — examiners love them.
- For diagram questions, redraw the **middleware pipeline** or **request-response cycle** neatly.
- Memorize the **4-argument error handler signature** — it appears in nearly every KTU Express question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Handling Requests with Express
Serving Other HTML Files
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Serving Other HTML Files ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Serving Other HTML Files** in the context of server-side web development (Node.js / Express.js) refers to the mechanism by which a web server dynamically delivers multiple HyperText Markup Language (HTML) documents to a client browser in response to specific HTTP requests. It extends the basic concept of serving a single root `index.html` to a structured, **multi-page routing architecture** where each route (`/`, `/about`, `/contact`, `/services`) corresponds to a distinct HTML view. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "The process of mapping incoming HTTP requests to corresponding HTML page responses using a server-side framework, typically Express.js, enabling multi-page web applications." In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, this concept is taught within **Module 3: Introduction to Node.js and Express Framework**, focusing on the `express` package, the `app.get()`, `app.post()` route handlers, the `res.sendFile()` method, and the integration of **Template Engines** such as **EJS (Embedded JavaScript)**, **Pug**, or **Handlebars** for dynamic view rendering. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy: The Restaurant Waiter System** Imagine a **restaurant** with a head waiter (the **Express Server**). When a customer (the **Client/Browser**) walks in, instead of only being given one fixed menu (single `index.html`), the waiter now carries a **large folder of menus** — one for breakfast, one for lunch, one for desserts, one for beverages. - The **customer's request** ("I want the lunch menu") is the **HTTP Request** with a specific URL path. - The **waiter checks the kitchen's master list** (the **Routing Table**) to find which menu matches the request. - The **waiter brings the correct menu** (the **HTML File**) from the storage cabinet (the **File System** or **Views Directory**). - If the menu has placeholders for "today's special," the chef fills them in dynamically (the **Template Engine**). This is exactly what **Serving Other HTML Files** does — it transforms a single-page static server into a **multi-route, dynamic file-serving system**. --- ### 1.3 Key Terminology | Term | Definition | |------|------------| | **HTTP Request** | A message sent by the client to the server asking for a specific resource. | | **Route** | A specific URL path defined in the server that maps to a handler function. | | **Express.js** | A minimal and flexible Node.js web application framework providing robust features for web and mobile applications. | | **Static Files** | Files served to the client as-is, without modification (CSS, JS, images, plain HTML). | | **Dynamic Files** | HTML files generated on-the-fly using templates + data before being sent to the client. | | **Template Engine** | A tool that combines static HTML templates with dynamic data to produce final HTML. | | **View Engine** | Express's internal mechanism that uses a template engine to render views. | | **Path Module** | A built-in Node.js module (`path`) used to handle and transform file paths across operating systems. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Exam Highlight:** The distinction between `res.send()` (sends any data type), `res.sendFile()` (sends a file from disk), and `res.render()` (renders a template engine view) is a **guaranteed 2-mark question** in KTU ESE examinations. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ### 2.1 Methods of Serving HTML Files in Express There are **three primary approaches** to serve HTML files in a Node.js/Express application: --- #### **Approach 1: Using `res.sendFile()`** This method transfers the file at the given path and sets the `Content-Type` response HTTP header field based on the file extension. **Operational Steps:** 1. The **client** sends an HTTP GET request to a defined route (e.g., `/about`). 2. Express matches the request to the route handler defined with `app.get('/about', ...)`. 3. Inside the callback, **`res.sendFile()`** is called with the **absolute path** to the HTML file. 4. Express reads the file from the filesystem and streams it to the client. 5. The browser renders the received HTML. **Why use this?** - Simple and direct for **static multi-page** applications. - No template engine overhead. - Ideal for serving pre-built HTML pages (e.g., a portfolio site with 5 pages). **How does Express know the file type?** - Express internally uses the **`mime` package** to look up the MIME type based on the file extension. - `.html` → `text/html`, `.css` → `text/css`, `.js` → `application/javascript`. --- #### **Approach 2: Using `express.static()` Middleware** A **built-in middleware function** in Express that serves static files from a specified directory. **Key Characteristics:** - **Zero routing required** — files are served based on their path relative to the static directory. - Automatically handles **404 errors** if the file is not found. - Sets appropriate **Cache-Control**, **ETag**, and **Last-Modified** headers for performance. - Can be **mounted multiple times** at different URL prefixes. **Why use this?** - Best for serving **CSS, JavaScript, images, fonts, and static HTML**. - Highly performant (Express optimizes file streaming). - Standard practice for serving the `public/` or `static/` folder. --- #### **Approach 3: Using Template Engines (`res.render()`)** A **dynamic rendering** approach where HTML files contain **placeholders** that are replaced with actual data at runtime. **Operational Steps:** 1. Configure a view engine: `app.set('view engine', 'ejs')`. 2. Set the views directory: `app.set('views', path.join(__dirname, 'views'))`. 3. Create template files in the `views/` folder (e.g., `home.ejs`, `about.ejs`). 4. In the route handler, call `res.render('home', { title: 'Welcome', user: 'Karthik' })`. 5. Express loads the template, substitutes data into placeholders, and sends final HTML. **Why use this?** - Enables **server-side rendering (SSR)** of dynamic content. - Reusable layouts and partials (header, footer, navbar). - Separation of concerns: logic in JS, presentation in templates. - Critical for **MVC architecture** in real-world web apps. --- ### 2.2 Comparison Table: Three Serving Methods | Feature | `res.sendFile()` | `express.static()` | `res.render()` | |---------|------------------|---------------------|-----------------| | **Purpose** | Send a specific file | Serve whole static directory | Render template with data | | **Data Injection** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | | **Routing Required** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | | **Template Engine** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Required | | **Performance** | Good | Excellent (cached) | Slightly slower (compile) | | **Use Case** | Multi-page static sites | CSS/JS/Images folder | Dynamic apps (blogs, dashboards) | | **KTU Frequency** | High | High | Very High | --- ### 2.3 Popular Template Engines (KTU Focus) | Engine | File Extension | Syntax Style | Popularity in KTU Syllabus | |--------|---------------|--------------|----------------------------| | **EJS** | `.ejs` | HTML with `<%= %>` tags | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Most Common) | | **Pug** | `.pug` | Indentation-based (no tags) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Handlebars** | `.hbs` / `.handlebars` | `{{variable}}` syntax | ⭐⭐⭐ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Exam Tip:** EJS is the **default choice** in most KTU lab examinations and previous year papers. Always remember to install it via `npm install ejs` and configure with `app.set('view engine', 'ejs')`. --- ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet | Concept | Syntax / Formula | Purpose | |---------|------------------|---------| | **Route Definition** | `app.get('/path', (req, res) => { ... })` | Map URL to handler | | **Send File** | `res.sendFile(path.join(__dirname, 'views', 'page.html'))` | Serve a specific file | | **Static Middleware** | `app.use(express.static('public'))` | Serve static assets | | **Set View Engine** | `app.set('view engine', 'ejs')` | Configure template engine | | **Set Views Directory** | `app.set('views', './views')` | Set templates location | | **Render Template** | `res.render('home', { data })` | Render with data | | **EJS Output Tag** | `<%= variable %>` | Escaped output (XSS safe) | | **EJS Unescaped Tag** | `<%- variable %>` | Raw HTML output | | **EJS Scriptlet** | `<% if(condition) { %> ... <% } %>` | Control flow | | **Include Partial** | `<%- include('partials/header') %>` | Reusable component | | **Path Module** | `path.join(__dirname, 'folder', 'file')` | Cross-platform paths | | **Port Listening** | `app.listen(3000, () => console.log('Running'))` | Start server | | **Default Route** | `app.get('/', handler)` | Home page | | **404 Handler** | `app.use((req, res) => res.status(404).send('Not Found'))` | Catch-all | | **Redirect** | `res.redirect('/login')` | Send 302 response | ## 3. Step-by-Step Implementation Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Practical Setup: Complete Express Application > [!NOTE] > **Prerequisites:** Node.js installed, basic JavaScript knowledge. The following is a **step-by-step, exhaustive walkthrough** — no steps are skipped, as required for KTU lab records and exam write-ups. --- #### **STEP 1: Project Initialization** Open a terminal and execute the following commands: ```bash mkdir ktu-web-app cd ktu-web-app npm init -y npm install express ejs ``` **What happens here?** - `mkdir` creates a new project directory. - `npm init -y` generates a `package.json` file with default values. - `npm install express ejs` downloads the Express framework and EJS template engine into `node_modules/`. --- #### **STEP 2: Directory Structure** Create the following folder structure: ``` ktu-web-app/ ├── node_modules/ ├── public/ │ ├── css/ │ │ └── style.css │ └── images/ │ └── logo.png ├── views/ │ ├── partials/ │ │ ├── header.ejs │ │ └── footer.ejs │ ├── home.ejs │ ├── about.ejs │ └── contact.ejs ├── app.js ├── package.json └── package-lock.json ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Lab Record Note:** Examiners often check for **proper folder organization**. Always keep templates in `views/`, static assets in `public/`, and partials in `views/partials/`. --- #### **STEP 3: Create the Main Server File (`app.js`)** ```javascript // Step 1: Import required modules const express = require('express'); const path = require('path'); // Step 2: Create an Express application instance const app = express(); const PORT = 3000; // Step 3: Configure the template engine app.set('view engine', 'ejs'); // Step 4: Set the views directory (optional if it's default './views') app.set('views', path.join(__dirname, 'views')); // Step 5: Serve static files (CSS, images, client-side JS) app.use(express.static(path.join(__dirname, 'public'))); // Step 6: Define routes app.get('/', (req, res) => { res.render('home', { title: 'Home Page', message: 'Welcome to KTU Web Application' }); }); app.get('/about', (req, res) => { res.render('about', { title: 'About Us', team: ['Karthik', 'Anand', 'Priya'] }); }); app.get('/contact', (req, res) => { res.render('contact', { title: 'Contact Us' }); }); // Step 7: 404 handler (must be last) app.use((req, res) => { res.status(404).render('404', { title: 'Page Not Found' }); }); // Step 8: Start the server app.listen(PORT, () => { console.log(`Server running at http://localhost:${PORT}`); }); ``` --- #### **STEP 4: Create the EJS Partials** **`views/partials/header.ejs`:** ```html <%= title %> - KTU Web App
Home About Contact
``` **`views/partials/footer.ejs`:** ```html

© 2024 KTU Web Application. All rights reserved.

``` --- #### **STEP 5: Create the View Templates** **`views/home.ejs`:** ```html <%- include('partials/header') %>

<%= message %>

This is the home page of our multi-page web application.

<%- include('partials/footer') %> ``` **`views/about.ejs`:** ```html <%- include('partials/header') %>

About Our Team

    <% team.forEach(member => { %>
  • <%= member %>
    • <% }); %>
<%- include('partials/footer') %> ``` **`views/contact.ejs`:** ```html <%- include('partials/header') %>

Contact Information

Email: ktu@apjktu.ac.in

<%- include('partials/footer') %> ``` --- #### **STEP 6: Run the Application** ```bash node app.js ``` Open browser and visit: - `http://localhost:3000/` → Home page - `http://localhost:3000/about` → About page - `http://localhost:3000/contact` → Contact page --- ### 3.2 Analytical Flow: Request → Response Lifecycle The following table maps **each step** of an HTTP request to the **corresponding code** and **internal Express mechanism**: | Step | Client Action | Server Code | Internal Mechanism | |------|---------------|-------------|---------------------| | 1 | User types `localhost:3000/about` | N/A | Browser initiates TCP connection | | 2 | Browser sends HTTP GET request | N/A | Request reaches Express | | 3 | Express parses request | `app.get('/about', ...)` | Route matching via `path-to-regexp` | | 4 | Callback executes | `(req, res) => { ... }` | `req` and `res` objects created | | 5 | Template is selected | `res.render('about', {...})` | Express looks in `views/` | | 6 | Data is injected | `{ title, team }` | EJS compiles template | | 7 | HTML is generated | EJS engine | Placeholders replaced | | 8 | Response sent | Final HTML string | `Content-Type: text/html` | | 9 | Browser displays | N/A | DOM is constructed and painted | --- ### 3.3 Path Resolution: Why `path.join(__dirname, ...)` Matters > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Exam Favorite:** The difference between **relative** and **absolute paths** is a 3-mark question almost every semester. **Problem with relative paths:** ```javascript // ❌ WRONG - Breaks when running from a different directory res.sendFile('views/about.html'); ``` **Why it fails:** - Express resolves relative paths against the **current working directory (CWD)**, not the script's directory. - If you run `node src/app.js` from a parent folder, CWD is wrong, and the file is not found. **Solution with `path.join` and `__dirname`:** ```javascript // ✅ CORRECT - Always works regardless of CWD res.sendFile(path.join(__dirname, 'views', 'about.html')); ``` **The math behind it:** $$\text{Absolute Path} = \text{\_\_dirname} + \text{path.sep} + \text{relative\_path}$$ Where: - `__dirname` is a Node.js global that resolves to the **directory of the currently executing script**. - `path.join()` constructs a normalized, **OS-independent** path using the correct separator (`/` on Unix, `\` on Windows). --- ### 3.4 EJS Template Engine: Tag Reference Matrix | Tag | Syntax | Function | XSS Safe? | |-----|--------|----------|-----------| | **Output (Escaped)** | `<%= value %>` | Prints HTML-escaped value | ✅ Yes | | **Output (Raw)** | `<%- value %>` | Prints unescaped HTML | ❌ No | | **Scriptlet** | `<% code %>` | Executes JS (no output) | N/A | | **Include** | `<%- include('path') %>` | Includes another EJS file | N/A | | **Comment** | `<%# comment %>` | Server-side comment (not in HTML) | N/A | > [!WARNING] > **Security Pitfall:** Never use `<%- %>` (unescaped) with user-supplied input. It opens **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** vulnerabilities. Always use `<%= %>` for any data originating from users, databases, or APIs. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 System Architecture: Express Multi-Page Application ```mermaid flowchart TD client["Client Browser"] server["Express Server app.js"] router["Route Handler app.get()"] static["Static Middleware express.static()"] viewEngine["View Engine EJS"] fs["File System"] views["views/ directory"] public["public/ directory"] pages["about.ejs, home.ejs, contact.ejs"] assets["style.css, logo.png, script.js"] client -->|"HTTP GET /about"| server server --> router router -->|"Match /about"| viewEngine viewEngine -->|"Load template"| views views --> pages pages -->|"Compile with data"| viewEngine viewEngine -->|"Final HTML"| client client -->|"GET /css/style.css"| static static --> public public --> assets assets -->|"Stream file"| client client -->|"GET /unknown"| router router -->|"No match"| fs fs -->|"404 Handler"| client ``` ### 4.2 Request Flow: How Express Resolves a Route ```mermaid flowchart TD req["Incoming Request /about"] middleware1["express.static() middleware"] checkStatic{"Is file in public/?"} serveStatic["Send static file"] routeMatch{"Does route match?"} handler["Execute route handler"] sendFile["res.sendFile()"] render["res.render()"] response["Send Response to Client"] notFound["404 Not Found"] req --> middleware1 middleware1 --> checkStatic checkStatic -->|"Yes"| serveStatic checkStatic -->|"No"| routeMatch serveStatic --> response routeMatch -->|"Yes"| handler routeMatch -->|"No"| notFound handler --> sendFile handler --> render sendFile --> response render --> response notFound --> response ``` ### 4.3 EJS Rendering Process (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR template["home.ejs template"] data["Data Object title, message"] parser["EJS Parser"] tokens["Token Stream HTML + placeholders"] compiler["Template Compiler"] jsFunc["Compiled JavaScript Function"] executor["Execute with Data"] finalHTML["Final HTML String"] browser["Browser DOM"] template --> parser data --> parser parser --> tokens tokens --> compiler compiler --> jsFunc data --> executor jsFunc --> executor executor --> finalHTML finalHTML --> browser ``` ### 4.4 Project Folder-to-Function Mapping ```mermaid flowchart TD root["ktu-web-app/"] appjs["app.js Server Entry Point"] pkgjson["package.json Dependencies"] viewsFolder["views/ Template Files"] partials["partials/ Reusable Components"] publicFolder["public/ Static Assets"] csscss["css/ Stylesheets"] imgfolder["images/ Images"] nodeMods["node_modules/ npm packages"] root --> appjs root --> pkgjson root --> viewsFolder root --> publicFolder root --> nodeMods viewsFolder --> partials publicFolder --> csscss publicFolder --> imgfolder ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between `res.send()`, `res.sendFile()`, and `res.render()` methods in Express.js. In which scenario would you prefer `res.render()` over `res.sendFile()`? **Model Answer:** | Method | Purpose | Data Type | Template Engine | |--------|---------|-----------|-----------------| | `res.send()` | Sends any data (string, object, buffer) | Any | Not required | | `res.sendFile()` | Sends a file from the filesystem | File | Not required | | `res.render()` | Renders a template with data | HTML view | Required | **Scenario:** `res.render()` is preferred when we need to **inject dynamic data** into the HTML before sending it to the client. For example, rendering a user profile page with the user's name and email fetched from a database. `res.sendFile()` would only send a static HTML file without any data injection capability. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]** What is the purpose of `express.static()` middleware? Write the code to serve files from a folder named `"assets"` located in the project root. **Model Answer:** `express.static()` is a **built-in middleware** function in Express that serves **static files** (HTML, CSS, JavaScript, images) directly from a specified directory without requiring individual route definitions. It improves performance by setting proper cache headers and streaming files efficiently. ```javascript const path = require('path'); app.use(express.static(path.join(__dirname, 'assets'))); ``` After this setup, a file `assets/images/logo.png` can be accessed via the URL `http://localhost:3000/images/logo.png`. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **(a)** Explain the concept of Template Engines in Express.js with a focus on EJS. Discuss any **three advantages** of using template engines and write the steps to configure EJS in an Express application. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Create an Express application with the following requirements: - A home route `/` that renders `home.ejs` with variables `title` and `currentDate`. - An `/employees` route that renders `employees.ejs` with an array of 5 employee objects (each having `name`, `designation`, and `salary`). - A `partials/header.ejs` file included in both views containing a navigation bar. - Proper 404 handling for unknown routes. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a):** A **Template Engine** is a tool that enables developers to write HTML markup with placeholders for dynamic data. EJS (Embedded JavaScript) is one of the most popular template engines for Express because it uses plain JavaScript inside `<% %>` tags, making it easy to learn for Node.js developers. **Three Advantages:** 1. **Dynamic Content Generation** — Data from databases or APIs can be seamlessly injected into HTML. 2. **Code Reusability** — Partials (header, footer, navbar) can be reused across multiple pages, reducing duplication. 3. **Separation of Concerns** — Business logic stays in JavaScript files while presentation logic stays in templates, supporting the MVC pattern. **Configuration Steps:** ```bash npm install ejs ``` ```javascript const express = require('express'); const app = express(); app.set('view engine', 'ejs'); app.set('views', './views'); ``` The `app.set('view engine', 'ejs')` tells Express to use EJS, and `app.set('views', './views')` sets the directory where `.ejs` files are located. --- **Model Solution for (b):** **`app.js`:** ```javascript const express = require('express'); const path = require('path'); const app = express(); const PORT = 3000; app.set('view engine', 'ejs'); app.set('views', path.join(__dirname, 'views')); app.use(express.static('public')); app.get('/', (req, res) => { res.render('home', { title: 'Employee Management System', currentDate: new Date().toLocaleDateString() }); }); app.get('/employees', (req, res) => { const employees = [ { name: 'Karthik', designation: 'Developer', salary: 50000 }, { name: 'Anand', designation: 'Designer', salary: 45000 }, { name: 'Priya', designation: 'Manager', salary: 70000 }, { name: 'Rahul', designation: 'Tester', salary: 40000 }, { name: 'Sneha', designation: 'HR', salary: 48000 } ]; res.render('employees', { title: 'Employees', employees }); }); app.use((req, res) => { res.status(404).send('

404 - Page Not Found

'); }); app.listen(PORT, () => console.log(`Server on port ${PORT}`)); ``` **`views/partials/header.ejs`:** ```html
Home | Employees
``` **`views/home.ejs`:** ```html <%= title %> <%- include('partials/header') %>

<%= title %>

Today is <%= currentDate %>

``` **`views/employees.ejs`:** ```html <%= title %> <%- include('partials/header') %>

Employee List

<% employees.forEach(emp => { %> <% }); %>
NameDesignationSalary
<%= emp.name %> <%= emp.designation %> <%= emp.salary %>
``` --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **(a)** What is the role of the `path` module in Node.js? Why is it recommended to use `path.join(__dirname, 'views', 'index.html')` instead of a relative path string in `res.sendFile()`? Illustrate with an example. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Design a multi-page Express application with routes `/`, `/services`, and `/portfolio`. Each route should serve a different HTML file using `res.sendFile()`. Include a common CSS file, proper navigation between pages, and demonstrate the use of `express.static()` for the CSS. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a):** The **`path` module** is a built-in Node.js module that provides utilities for working with file and directory paths. It ensures **cross-platform compatibility** by automatically using the correct path separator (`/` on Linux/Mac, `\` on Windows). **Why `path.join(__dirname, ...)` is recommended:** | Aspect | Relative Path | Absolute Path with `path.join` | |--------|---------------|--------------------------------| | **CWD Dependency** | Depends on where `node` is run | Independent of CWD | | **Cross-Platform** | ❌ Breaks on Windows | ✅ Works everywhere | | **Reliability** | ❌ Fails if CWD changes | ✅ Always finds the file | **Example:** ```javascript const path = require('path'); const express = require('express'); const app = express(); app.get('/about', (req, res) => { // ❌ BAD: May fail if CWD is not the project root // res.sendFile('views/about.html'); // ✅ GOOD: Always works res.sendFile(path.join(__dirname, 'views', 'about.html')); }); app.listen(3000); ``` Here, `__dirname` resolves to the absolute path of the directory containing the current script, making the file lookup deterministic and reliable. --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Directory Structure:** ``` project/ ├── public/ │ └── style.css ├── views/ │ ├── home.html │ ├── services.html │ └── portfolio.html └── app.js ``` **`app.js`:** ```javascript const express = require('express'); const path = require('path'); const app = express(); const PORT = 3000; // Serve static files app.use(express.static(path.join(__dirname, 'public'))); // Routes using res.sendFile() app.get('/', (req, res) => { res.sendFile(path.join(__dirname, 'views', 'home.html')); }); app.get('/services', (req, res) => { res.sendFile(path.join(__dirname, 'views', 'services.html')); }); app.get('/portfolio', (req, res) => { res.sendFile(path.join(__dirname, 'views', 'portfolio.html')); }); app.use((req, res) => { res.status(404).sendFile(path.join(__dirname, 'views', '404.html')); }); app.listen(PORT, () => console.log(`Server running on port ${PORT}`)); ``` **`views/home.html`:** ```html Home
Home Services Portfolio

Welcome Home

This is our homepage.

``` **`views/services.html`:** ```html Services
Home Services Portfolio

Our Services

  • Web Development
  • Mobile Apps
  • Cloud Solutions
``` **`views/portfolio.html`:** ```html Portfolio
Home Services Portfolio

Our Portfolio

Check out our amazing projects.

``` **`public/style.css`:** ```css body { font-family: Arial, sans-serif; margin: 0; padding: 20px; background-color: #f0f0f0; } nav { background-color: #333; padding: 10px; } nav a { color: white; margin-right: 15px; text-decoration: none; } h1 { color: #2c3e50; } ``` --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting `path.join(__dirname, ...)`** — Using `'views/index.html'` directly. **Penalty: -2 marks** (Board expects absolute path). > 2. **Missing `app.set('view engine', 'ejs')`** — The template engine won't be recognized. **Penalty: -1 mark**. > 3. **Wrong include path in EJS** — Writing `<%- include('header') %>` instead of `<%- include('partials/header') %>`. **Penalty: -1 mark**. > 4. **Not implementing 404 handler** — Examiners deduct **1 mark** for missing error handling. > 5. **Using `<%- %>` (raw) instead of `<%= %>` (escaped)** for user data — **Penalty: -1 mark** for security concern. > 6. **Forgetting to install `ejs`** before running — Server crashes; marks awarded for **correct installation step**. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:** - **Three ways to serve HTML** in Express: `res.sendFile()`, `express.static()`, and `res.render()`. - **`res.sendFile()`** requires an **absolute path** — always use `path.join(__dirname, ...)`. - **`express.static()`** middleware serves files from a directory without explicit routing. - **`res.render()`** requires a **view engine** to be configured via `app.set('view engine', 'ejs')`. - **EJS tags**: `<%= %>` (escaped output), `<%- %>` (raw HTML), `<% %>` (scriptlet), `<%# %>` (comment). - **Partials** are reusable EJS fragments included via `<%- include('partials/header') %>`. - **Default views folder** is `./views` relative to the script's directory. - **404 handler** must be placed **after all other routes** using `app.use()`. - **Cross-platform paths** require the `path` module — never hardcode `\` or `/`. - **EJS is installed via `npm install ejs`** — not built into Express. - **`__dirname`** is a Node.js global that gives the current script's directory. - **Static assets** (CSS, images) are conventionally stored in a `public/` folder. - **Template engines** support control flow (`if`, `forEach`) via `<% %>` scriptlets. - **XSS Prevention**: Always use `<%= %>` for user-supplied data, never `<%- %>`. - **Server listens** on a port via `app.listen(PORT, callback)`. - **KTU expects** complete working code in exam answers — always include the full `app.js`, template files, and a brief explanation of the rendering flow.

topics.title: Serving Static Files with Express
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Serving Static Files with Express

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Express.js** (a minimal and flexible Node.js web application framework), **static files** refer to assets that are delivered to the client **exactly as they are stored on the server's filesystem** — without any modification, dynamic generation, or server-side templating. Examples include `.html`, `.css`, `.js`, `.png`, `.jpg`, `.gif`, `.svg`, `.pdf`, `.woff`, `.ttf`, `.json`, and `.txt` files.

Express provides a **built-in middleware function** named **`express.static(root, [options])`** whose sole responsibility is to mount a directory and serve every file inside it through a public URL. This middleware is part of Express's core (no `npm install` required) and is functionally equivalent to serving files via an HTTP `GET` request with appropriate `Content-Type` headers automatically inferred from file extensions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Static files are pre-built files residing in the server's filesystem (CSS, JS, images, HTML) that are transmitted to clients without server-side processing. Express handles them through the `express.static()` middleware, which maps a URL path to a directory on disk."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Reading Room

Imagine a **public library** with a **Reading Room**:
- The librarian (Express server) receives a request: *"I want page 5 of Book A."*
- For **dynamic books** (e.g., a live newspaper), the librarian would copy fresh content from the press.
- For **static reference books** (e.g., encyclopedias stored in a fixed shelf), the librarian simply **walks to the shelf, picks the exact book, and hands it over unchanged**.

The `express.static()` middleware is that librarian dedicated to the static shelf. When a browser requests `/css/style.css`, the middleware looks inside the configured root folder (say `public/`), reads `style.css`, attaches the correct `Content-Type` header (`text/css`), and streams it back. No JavaScript logic, no template rendering — just a direct **filesystem-to-wire** transfer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The default `Content-Type` detection uses the **mime-db** package bundled with Express. If the file extension is unrecognized, Express defaults to `application/octet-stream`, prompting the browser to trigger a download dialog.

---

## 1.3 Why Serve Static Files? (Engineering Motivation)

| Use Case | File Types | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| Front-end styling | `.css` | Separation of presentation from logic |
| Client-side interactivity | `.js` | Reduces server CPU load |
| Visual assets | `.png`, `.jpg`, `.svg`, `.webp` | Brand identity & UX |
| Documents | `.pdf`, `.txt` | Knowledge base / manuals |
| Fonts & icons | `.woff`, `.woff2`, `.ttf` | Typography consistency |
| Pre-rendered HTML | `.html` | Landing pages, error pages |

> [!TIP]
> **Performance Note:** In production, static files are typically offloaded to a **CDN (Content Delivery Network)** or a reverse proxy like **Nginx**. Express's `express.static` is convenient for development and small-scale apps but adds Node.js event-loop overhead for every request.

---

## 1.4 The `express.static()` Function Signature

The middleware has a precise contract defined by the Express documentation:

$$ \text{express.static}(\text{root},\ \text{options}) \rightarrow \text{RequestHandler} $$

Where:
- **`root`** *(string, mandatory)* — Absolute or relative path to the **root directory** containing static assets.
- **`options`** *(object, optional)* — Configuration object with keys such as `dotfiles`, `etag`, `extensions`, `index`, `lastModified`, `maxAge`, `redirect`, `setHeaders`, `fallthrough`.

The returned function is itself a **middleware** that can be passed to `app.use()`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Lifecycle — How a Static File Request is Resolved

When a client requests a static resource, the following **seven-step resolution chain** executes inside the Express pipeline:

1. **Request Arrival** — Browser sends `GET /css/style.css`.
2. **Middleware Match** — `express.static` checks if the request URL matches the mount point.
3. **Path Concatenation** — Express constructs `fsPath = path.join(root, req.url)` using Node's `path` module.
4. **Filesystem Probe** — `fs.stat(fsPath)` is called; if the path is a **file**, processing continues; if a **directory**, Express looks for an `index` file (default `index.html`).
5. **Security Filter** — If `fsPath` contains `..` or absolute paths that escape `root`, the request is rejected (`403 Forbidden`) via the `dotfiles` and `fallthrough` rules.
6. **Header Injection** — Express sets `Content-Type` (from mime lookup), `ETag` (if `etag:true`), `Last-Modified` (if `lastModified:true`), and `Cache-Control` (based on `maxAge`).
7. **Stream Transfer** — `fs.createReadStream(fsPath).pipe(res)` sends the file in **chunks** over HTTP.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Detail:** The `path.join()` operation is the reason Express is **inherently safe against path traversal attacks** — Node's `path` module normalizes and resolves `..` segments. However, this safety depends on Express's internal usage of `send` module, which explicitly checks that the resolved path remains within `root`.

---

## 2.2 Configuration Options Matrix

The `options` parameter of `express.static()` exposes a rich configuration surface. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes understanding the most exam-relevant options:

| Option | Type | Default | Purpose | KTU Exam Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `dotfiles` | string | `"ignore"` | Behavior for files starting with `.` (`allow`, `deny`, `ignore`) | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| `etag` | boolean | `true` | Enable weak ETag generation for cache validation | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| `extensions` | string[] | `false` | Fallback extensions (e.g., `['html']` → `/about` resolves to `/about.html`) | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| `index` | string \| false | `"index.html"` | Directory index file (set `false` to disable) | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| `lastModified` | boolean | `true` | Set `Last-Modified` header for conditional GETs | ⭐ Low |
| `maxAge` | number \| string | `0` | `Cache-Control: max-age=` value in milliseconds or string | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| `redirect` | boolean | `true` | Redirect `/dir` → `/dir/` for directory access | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| `setHeaders` | function | `null` | Custom header mutation `(res, path, stat) => void` | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| `fallthrough` | boolean | `true` | Pass to next middleware on `404`/`403` | ⭐⭐⭐ High |

---

## 2.3 The `fallthrough` Option — A Subtle but Critical Setting

`fallthrough` is conceptually tricky and frequently tested. Its logic can be expressed as:

$$
\text{fallthrough}(req, res, next) =
\begin{cases}
\text{call next()} & \text{if } \texttt{fallthrough=true} \text{ and file not found} \\
\text{end response with 404} & \text{if } \texttt{fallthrough=false} \text{ and file not found}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Default Behavior (`fallthrough: true`):** When the requested file does **not** exist, `express.static` silently calls `next()`, allowing subsequent middleware (or a 404 handler) to respond. This is essential for **SPA (Single Page Application)** routing where unknown paths should fall through to `index.html`.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **CDN Integration** — `express.static` forms the backbone of self-hosted assets before CDN migration.
2. **Single Page Applications** — Serving `index.html` for all unknown routes (client-side routing).
3. **Documentation Portals** — Hosting Swagger UI, MkDocs, Docusaurus outputs.
4. **Asset Bundling Workflow** — Front-end frameworks (React/Vue/Angular) emit `dist/` folders that are mounted via `express.static`.
5. **Microservices** — Containerized services serving config files, schema definitions, and AI model metadata.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Code Patterns (Cheat Sheet)

| Pattern | Code | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| Basic mount | `app.use(express.static('public'))` | Single static folder |
| Absolute path | `app.use(express.static(path.join(\_\_dirname, 'public')))` | Production safety |
| Virtual prefix | `app.use('/static', express.static('public'))` | URL namespace |
| Multiple dirs | `app.use(express.static('public')); app.use(express.static('files'))` | Layered assets |
| SPA fallback | `app.use(express.static('public', { extensions: ['html'] }))` | Client routing |
| Custom headers | `app.use(express.static('public', { setHeaders }))` | CORS, CSP, Cache |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Static file middleware is **order-sensitive**. Once `express.static` sends a response (calls `res.send()`), `next()` is never invoked. Always mount it **before** route handlers that might conflict.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Virtual Path Prefix Mechanism

The virtual path prefix works through **URL stripping**. When you write:

```javascript
app.use('/static', express.static('public'));
```

Express internally performs this transformation for every incoming request:

$$
\text{req.url}_\text{after} = \text{req.url}_\text{before}.\text{replace}(\texttt{'/static'},\ \texttt{''})
$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Client requests `GET /static/css/style.css`.

**Step 2:** Express invokes `app.use('/static', ...)` because the URL **begins with** `/static`.

**Step 3:** Middleware logic executes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{req.url} &= \texttt{'/static/css/style.css'} \\
\texttt{stripped} &= \texttt{req.url}.\text{slice}(\texttt{'/static'}.\text{length}) \\
&= \texttt{'/static/css/style.css'}.\text{slice}(7) \\
&= \texttt{'/css/style.css'}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Concatenate with root:

$$
\texttt{fsPath} = \texttt{path.join}(\texttt{'public'}, \texttt{'/css/style.css'}) = \texttt{'public/css/style.css'}
$$

**Step 5:** Stream file back to the client.

> [!NOTE]
> **What the user sees:** `https://yoursite.com/static/css/style.css`
> **What the filesystem serves:** `public/css/style.css`
> The `/static` segment is **purely virtual** — no `static` folder must exist on disk.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The `path.join()` Resolution & Security

Consider a malicious request: `GET /public/../app.js`.

Express performs path normalization before filesystem access:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{rawPath} &= \texttt{path.join}(\texttt{'public'},\ \texttt{'/../app.js'}) \\
&= \texttt{path.normalize}(\texttt{'public/../app.js'}) \\
&= \texttt{'app.js'}
\end{aligned}
$$

Express's underlying `send` module then verifies:

$$
\texttt{resolved} \subset \texttt{root} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \texttt{reject with 403}
$$

Since `app.js` is **outside** the `public` directory, the request is denied. This is why `express.static` is considered **secure by default**.

---

## 3.3 Complete Working Example — Annotated Code

```javascript
// server.js
const express = require('express');
const path = require('path');

const app = express();
const PORT = 3000;

// STEP A: Serve files from 'public' directory at the root URL
app.use(express.static(path.join(__dirname, 'public')));

// STEP B: Serve files from 'assets' directory under '/static' prefix
app.use('/static', express.static(path.join(__dirname, 'assets'), {
    maxAge: '1d',                              // Cache for 1 day
    setHeaders: (res, filePath, stat) => {
        if (path.extname(filePath) === '.js') {
            res.setHeader('Content-Type', 'application/javascript');
        }
    }
}));

// STEP C: SPA fallback — serve index.html for any unknown route
app.get('*', (req, res) => {
    res.sendFile(path.join(__dirname, 'public', 'index.html'));
});

app.listen(PORT, () => {
    console.log(`Server running at http://localhost:${PORT}`);
});
```

### Directory Structure:

```
project-root/
├── server.js
├── public/
│   ├── index.html
│   ├── css/
│   │   └── style.css
│   └── images/
│       └── logo.png
└── assets/
    └── js/
        └── app.js
```

### URL Mapping Table:

| Client URL Request | File Served | Middleware Invoked |
|---|---|---|
| `GET /` | `public/index.html` | First static mount |
| `GET /css/style.css` | `public/css/style.css` | First static mount |
| `GET /static/js/app.js` | `assets/js/app.js` | Second static mount |
| `GET /nonexistent` | `public/index.html` | SPA fallback route |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Exam Scenarios

| Scenario | Expected Behavior | Marks Weight |
|---|---|---|
| Request to directory without trailing `/` | 301 redirect to `/` (if `redirect:true`) | 2 marks |
| Request to `/about` with `extensions:['html']` | Serves `about.html` if exists | 3 marks |
| File starting with `.` (e.g., `.env`) | 403 Forbidden (default `dotfiles:'ignore'`) | 2 marks |
| Missing file with `fallthrough:false` | Response ends with 404, no further middleware | 3 marks |
| Two static dirs mounted at same path | First directory takes precedence (order matters) | 3 marks |

---

## 3.5 Express 5.x Migration Note (2024 Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Starting with **Express 5.x** (released September 2024), the `express.static` middleware was migrated to use the **`serve-static`** package directly (instead of the older `send` module). The API is **backward-compatible** for `express.static(root, options)`, but internally the path traversal check now uses `path.resolve()` for stricter security. KTU 2024 Scheme examinations may reference both versions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Request Flow — Static File Resolution Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client Browser] -->|HTTP GET /css/style.css| B[Express Server]
    B --> C{Matches Mount Path?}
    C -->|No| D[Pass to next middleware via next]
    C -->|Yes| E[Strip virtual prefix from URL]
    E --> F[path.join with root directory]
    F --> G{Path inside root?}
    G -->|No - traversal| H[403 Forbidden]
    G -->|Yes| I[fs.stat check existence]
    I -->|Not found| J{fallthrough?}
    J -->|true| D
    J -->|false| K[404 Not Found]
    I -->|Found - file| L[Set Content-Type header]
    L --> M[Set ETag and Cache-Control]
    M --> N[Stream file via createReadStream]
    N --> O[HTTP Response to Client]
    H --> O
    K --> O
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Multi-Directory Static Serving

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ClientLayer[Client Request]
        R1[GET /css/main.css]
        R2[GET /static/img/logo.png]
        R3[GET /static/js/app.js]
    end

    subgraph ExpressLayer[Express Application]
        M1["express.static public dir"]
        M2["express.static assets dir at /static prefix"]
        M3["SPA fallback route GET *"]
    end

    subgraph DiskLayer[Server Filesystem]
        F1["public/css/main.css"]
        F2["assets/img/logo.png"]
        F3["assets/js/app.js"]
        F4["public/index.html"]
    end

    R1 --> M1
    R2 --> M2
    R3 --> M2
    R1 -.404.-> M3
    R2 -.404.-> M3
    R3 -.404.-> M3

    M1 --> F1
    M2 --> F2
    M2 --> F3
    M3 --> F4
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — End-to-End Static File Delivery

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Browser
    participant Express as Express Server
    participant Middleware as express.static
    participant FS as Node fs module
    participant Disk as Filesystem

    Browser->>Express: GET /css/style.css HTTP/1.1
    Express->>Middleware: invoke with req, res, next
    Middleware->>Middleware: Check URL matches mount point
    Middleware->>Middleware: Strip virtual prefix
    Middleware->>Middleware: path.join root with stripped URL
    Middleware->>FS: stat fsPath
    FS->>Disk: read metadata
    Disk-->>FS: stat object returned
    FS-->>Middleware: stat success
    Middleware->>Middleware: Detect Content-Type via mime-db
    Middleware->>Middleware: Generate ETag from stat
    Middleware->>FS: createReadStream fsPath
    FS->>Disk: open file for reading
    Disk-->>FS: file stream chunks
    FS-->>Middleware: data events
    Middleware->>Browser: HTTP 200 with file stream
    Browser->>Browser: Render CSS and apply styles
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — `fallthrough` vs `redirect` Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Request to /photos] --> A{Is directory?}
    A -->|No| B[Look for file with extensions]
    A -->|Yes| C{redirect true?}
    C -->|Yes| D[301 Redirect to /photos/]
    C -->|No| E[Look for /photos/index.html]
    D --> F[Browser retries /photos/]
    F --> E
    E --> G{index.html exists?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Serve index.html]
    G -->|No| I{fallthrough true?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Call next middleware]
    I -->|No| K[404 Not Found]
    B --> L{File exists?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Serve file]
    L -->|No| I
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the purpose of `express.static()` middleware. How does it determine the `Content-Type` header for files being served?**

**Model Answer:**

The `express.static()` middleware is a **built-in Express middleware function** used to serve static files (HTML, CSS, JS, images, fonts, etc.) from a specified directory on the server's filesystem. It maps a URL path to a directory and streams files directly to the client without any server-side processing or dynamic generation.

It determines the `Content-Type` header by:
1. Extracting the file extension from the requested file path.
2. Looking up the extension in the **mime-db** (MIME database) bundled with Express.
3. Setting the matching MIME type (e.g., `.css` → `text/css`, `.png` → `image/png`).
4. If the extension is **unknown**, it defaults to `application/octet-stream`, which prompts the browser to download the file.

**Example Usage:**
```javascript
app.use(express.static('public'));
```

*(3 marks: 1 for purpose, 2 for Content-Type mechanism)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between mounting `express.static` at root path vs. with a virtual prefix. Provide one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Mounted at Root | Mounted with Virtual Prefix |
|---|---|---|
| **Code** | `app.use(express.static('public'))` | `app.use('/static', express.static('public'))` |
| **URL Pattern** | `/css/style.css` | `/static/css/style.css` |
| **Filesystem Path** | `public/css/style.css` | `public/css/style.css` |
| **Purpose** | Direct asset access | Namespacing, avoiding route conflicts |

**Mounted at Root Example:**
```javascript
app.use(express.static('public'));
// Request: GET /index.html → Serves public/index.html
```

**Mounted with Virtual Prefix Example:**
```javascript
app.use('/assets', express.static('public'));
// Request: GET /assets/index.html → Serves public/index.html
// The '/assets' segment is virtual; no 'assets' folder exists on disk
```

*(3 marks: 1 for differentiation, 1 for each example)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse the **virtual prefix** with an actual folder on disk. Remember: the prefix is a **URL-level concept**, not a filesystem path.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the `express.static()` built-in middleware in detail. Describe any **four configuration options** with their default values and use cases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Express.js application that:
- Serves files from a `public` folder at the root URL.
- Serves files from an `uploads` folder under the virtual prefix `/media`.
- Implements a **SPA fallback** so that any unmatched route serves `public/index.html`.
- Sets a `Cache-Control: max-age=86400` header for all `.css` and `.js` files using `setHeaders`.
- Uses **`path.join(__dirname, ...)`** for absolute paths.

Include comments explaining each step. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Explanation:**

`express.static(root, options)` is Express's built-in middleware for serving **static assets** — pre-built files that don't require server-side processing. It is based on the `serve-static` package and requires no additional installation.

**Four Key Configuration Options:**

| Option | Default | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| `maxAge` | `0` (no caching) | Set to `'7d'` for production caching |
| `extensions` | `false` | `['html']` enables extensionless URLs |
| `dotfiles` | `'ignore'` | `'allow'` permits serving `.gitignore` etc. |
| `fallthrough` | `true` | Set `false` to force 404 on missing files |

**Part (b) — Complete Code:**

```javascript
// app.js — Complete Express Static File Server
const express = require('express');
const path = require('path');

const app = express();
const PORT = process.env.PORT || 3000;

// ============================================================
// STEP 1: Serve root static files from 'public' directory
// ============================================================
app.use(express.static(path.join(__dirname, 'public')));

// ============================================================
// STEP 2: Serve uploaded media files under '/media' virtual prefix
// ============================================================
app.use('/media', express.static(path.join(__dirname, 'uploads')));

// ============================================================
// STEP 3: SPA fallback — serve index.html for any unmatched route
// ============================================================
app.get('*', (req, res) => {
    res.sendFile(path.join(__dirname, 'public', 'index.html'));
});

// ============================================================
// STEP 4: Start the server
// ============================================================
app.listen(PORT, () => {
    console.log(`Server is running at http://localhost:${PORT}`);
});
```

**Bonus Enhancement — Custom Cache Headers:**

```javascript
app.use(express.static(path.join(__dirname, 'public'), {
    setHeaders: (res, filePath) => {
        const ext = path.extname(filePath);
        if (ext === '.css' || ext === '.js') {
            res.setHeader('Cache-Control', 'max-age=86400'); // 1 day
        }
    }
}));
```

**Directory Structure:**
```
project/
├── app.js
├── public/
│   └── index.html
└── uploads/
    └── sample.jpg
```

**URL Mapping Verification:**
| URL | Serves |
|---|---|
| `GET /` | `public/index.html` |
| `GET /about.html` | `public/about.html` |
| `GET /media/sample.jpg` | `uploads/sample.jpg` |
| `GET /random-path` | `public/index.html` (SPA fallback) |

*(7 marks for code: 2 for correct middleware usage, 2 for SPA fallback, 2 for absolute paths, 1 for comments and structure)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2): `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Past Paper]`

**(a)** What is the significance of the `fallthrough` option in `express.static()`? What happens when it is set to `true` versus `false`? Provide an example demonstrating SPA routing behavior. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **directory traversal attacks** in the context of static file serving. Explain how Express mitigates this vulnerability internally. Write a code snippet showing the safe configuration using `path.join(__dirname, ...)`. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — The `fallthrough` Option:**

The `fallthrough` option controls what happens when `express.static()` cannot find the requested file:

- **`fallthrough: true` (default)** — Calls `next()` to pass control to the next middleware. This allows chained middleware (like SPA fallbacks or custom 404 handlers) to handle the request.
- **`fallthrough: false`** — Ends the request with a 404 response immediately, bypassing any subsequent middleware.

**SPA Routing Example:**
```javascript
// Without fallthrough, SPA fallback would never trigger
app.use(express.static('public', { fallthrough: true }));

// This handler runs only because static middleware called next()
app.get('*', (req, res) => {
    res.sendFile(path.join(__dirname, 'public', 'index.html'));
});
```

**Why It Matters:** Single Page Applications (React, Angular, Vue) use client-side routing where `/dashboard`, `/profile`, `/settings` are not real files but JavaScript-managed routes. The `fallthrough:true` setting allows all unknown URLs to be served the same `index.html`, letting the frontend router take over.

**Part (b) — Directory Traversal & Mitigation:**

A **directory traversal attack** occurs when a malicious user requests paths like `/../../etc/passwd` or `/public/../app.js` to access files outside the intended static directory.

**Express's Internal Mitigation:**
1. Express uses Node's `path.join()` to normalize the requested path, collapsing `..` segments.
2. The internal `send` (or `serve-static` in Express 5) module performs a **boundary check**: it verifies that the resolved absolute path still lies within the configured `root` directory.
3. If the resolved path escapes `root`, the request is rejected with **403 Forbidden**.

**Safe Configuration Code:**
```javascript
const path = require('path');
const express = require('express');
const app = express();

// SAFE: Uses __dirname for absolute, unambiguous path resolution
const staticRoot = path.join(__dirname, 'public');
app.use(express.static(staticRoot));

// UNSAFE demonstration (commented for educational purposes):
// app.use(express.static('public'));
// If process.cwd() changes, 'public' resolves differently — risky
```

> [!TIP]
> **Best Practice:** Always use `path.join(__dirname, 'public')` instead of relative paths in production. This guarantees that the static root is always the same directory regardless of where the Node process was started.

*(7 marks for part b: 3 for attack explanation, 3 for mitigation steps, 1 for safe code)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting `path.join(__dirname, ...)`** → -2 marks for production safety.
> 2. **Confusing virtual prefix with actual folder** → -1 mark per occurrence.
> 3. **Not mentioning `fallthrough` default value** → -1 mark in SPA questions.
> 4. **Writing `app.use(express.static('public/css'))`** and claiming it's for root CSS — incorrect; URL would require `/style.css` not `/css/style.css`.
> 5. **Forgetting to mount static middleware BEFORE route handlers** in code-writing questions → -2 marks.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ `express.static()` is a **built-in** middleware — no installation needed.
- ✅ Signature: `express.static(root, [options])` → returns a middleware function.
- ✅ The middleware uses `fs.createReadStream().pipe(res)` for efficient, non-blocking file transfer.
- ✅ **`Content-Type`** is auto-detected via mime-db; unknown extensions get `application/octet-stream`.
- ✅ **Virtual path prefix** is implemented by URL stripping — no physical folder required.
- ✅ Always prefer `path.join(__dirname, 'public')` over relative paths in production.
- ✅ **`fallthrough:true`** (default) is essential for SPA client-side routing.
- ✅ **`extensions: ['html']`** allows users to omit file extensions (`/about` → `/about.html`).
- ✅ **`setHeaders`** function can inject custom `Cache-Control`, CORS, or CSP headers.
- ✅ **Order matters**: mount `express.static` BEFORE dynamic route handlers.
- ✅ Multiple static directories can be mounted by calling `app.use(express.static(...))` multiple times.
- ✅ Directory traversal attacks are blocked internally by path normalization + boundary check.
- ✅ **`dotfiles:'ignore'`** (default) prevents serving `.env`, `.htaccess`, and similar sensitive dotfiles.
- ✅ For Express 5.x, the underlying engine moved from `send` to `serve-static` package (API unchanged).
- ✅ In production, consider offloading static files to **Nginx** or a **CDN** for better scalability.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automatic Server Restart with Nodemon
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Automatic Server Restart with Nodemon

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Nodemon** is a *command-line interface (CLI) utility* developed for the **Node.js** runtime environment that wraps a Node.js application, monitors the file system for any modifications in the source code directory, and **automatically restarts the server process** whenever a watched file is changed. It is classified under the *Developer Tooling* category of the Node.js ecosystem and is typically installed as a development dependency (`devDependencies`) through the **Node Package Manager (npm)**.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, Nodemon is positioned as a *productivity-enhancing wrapper* that decouples the developer's edit-save-reload loop from the manual server-restart loop, thereby accelerating the **inner-loop development cycle** of full-stack JavaScript applications built on frameworks like **Express.js**, **NestJS**, or plain **Node.js HTTP servers**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> Under Module 3 of PBITT504 (Web Application Development), Nodemon falls under the *Node.js runtime utilities and developer tooling* unit. The expected outcome is to enable students to **configure**, **install**, and **operate** a Node.js development server that self-reloads on file changes — a fundamental skill for the lab examinations and project components.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **chef cooking a live TV show**. Every time you change an ingredient or modify the recipe, the dish has to be re-cooked from scratch. If you had to manually stop the stove, throw away the old dish, and start a new pan every single time you wrote a new line in your recipe book, you would never finish the show.

Now imagine a **smart kitchen assistant** (Nodemon) standing next to you. The moment you scribble a new instruction in your recipe book, the assistant immediately notices the change, dumps the old dish, and starts a new one — **without you ever lifting a finger**.

That's exactly what Nodemon does for a Node.js developer:
- The **recipe book** = your JavaScript source files.
- The **smart kitchen assistant** = Nodemon.
- The **stove/dish** = the running Node.js server process.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Must Memorize for KTU)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Watcher** | The background process that monitors file system changes (uses `fs.watch` / `chokidar` internally) |
| **Restart Cycle** | The complete sequence: *kill old process → spawn new process → re-attach watcher* |
| **Polling** | An alternative file-detection mode (slower but more reliable on network drives) |
| **Signal-Based Control** | Sending OS signals (e.g., `SIGUSR2`) to manually trigger a restart |
| **`devDependencies`** | The npm section where Nodemon is correctly installed |
| **`npx`** | A binary executor that runs packages without requiring global installation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Nodemon is a **development-only** tool. It must **never** be used in production. In production, you use process managers like `pm2`, `forever`, or container orchestrators like `systemd`/`Kubernetes`.

### 1.4 Physical / Operational Constants (Standard Defaults)

Nodemon ships with the following **default** configuration values that are commonly asked in exams:

- **Default watched extensions**: `js, mjs, json, coffee, litcoffee`
- **Default ignored directories**: `node_modules`, `.git`, `.nyc_output`, `coverage`
- **Default restart delay**: **1000 milliseconds (1 second)**
- **Default Node.js executable path**: Auto-detected from `$PATH` (resolves to `node` or `nodejs`)
- **Configuration priority order**: CLI flags → `nodemon.json` → `package.json` (`nodemonConfig` key) → Default settings

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Architecture (The "Why" and "How")

Nodemon operates through a layered architecture. Understanding this architecture is critical for KTU's application-level questions.

**Layer 1 — The CLI Parser Layer**
When you invoke `nodemon app.js`, the CLI parser:
1. Reads the command-line arguments.
2. Resolves the target script (`app.js`).
3. Loads the configuration cascade (CLI > config file > defaults).

**Layer 2 — The Configuration Resolver**
The resolver merges the user's preferences with built-in defaults. If no `nodemon.json` exists, it falls back to defaults.

**Layer 3 — The File System Watcher**
This is the *heart* of Nodemon. It uses a Node.js library (historically `fs.watch`, now `chokidar` for cross-platform reliability) to subscribe to:
- **`change`** events (file modified)
- **`add`** events (new file added)
- **`unlink`** events (file deleted)
- **`addDir`** / **`unlinkDir`** events (directory changes)

**Layer 4 — The Process Supervisor**
Whenever a watched file triggers an event, the supervisor:
1. Sends a **`SIGTERM`** signal to the running child process (your Node.js app).
2. Waits for it to gracefully exit.
3. Spawns a new child process using `child_process.spawn()` with the same arguments.
4. Re-attaches the watcher.

**Layer 5 — The Event Emitter**
Nodemon extends `EventEmitter` and broadcasts lifecycle events that the application can listen to: `restart`, `crash`, `start`, `exit`, `config:update`.

### 2.2 The "Why" — Problem Statement Nodemon Solves

Without Nodemon, a Node.js developer's workflow is:

```
Edit code → Save → Switch to terminal → Press Ctrl+C → Type 'node app.js' → Press Enter
```

This loop takes **5–15 seconds** of wasted time *per code change*. Over a day, this adds up to **30+ minutes** of pure friction. Nodemon reduces this to:

```
Edit code → Save → Server auto-restarts in <1 second
```

This is the **Hot Reload / Live Reload** principle, which is a cornerstone of modern web development.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

| Property / Command | Description | Default Value | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| `--watch <path>` | Add a custom directory/file to watch | `./` (cwd) | `nodemon --watch src` |
| `--ignore <pattern>` | Files to ignore | `node_modules, .git` | `nodemon --ignore '*.test.js'` |
| `--ext <extensions>` | Override default extensions | `js,mjs,json` | `nodemon --ext js,ejs,html` |
| `--delay <ms>` | Milliseconds to wait before restart | `1000` | `nodemon --delay 2.5` |
| `--exec <cmd>` | Custom execution command | `node` | `nodemon --exec "node --harmony"` |
| `--verbose` | Enable detailed logging | `false` | `nodemon --verbose` |
| `--quiet` | Suppress restart logs | `false` | `nodemon --quiet` |
| `--inspect` | Enable Node.js debugger | `false` | `nodemon --inspect app.js` |
| `-L` / `--legacy-watch` | Force polling mode (every 100ms) | `false` | `nodemon -L app.js` |
| `--signal <sig>` | Custom signal to send on restart | `SIGUSR2` | `nodemon --signal SIGTERM` |
| `--exitcrash` | Exit on uncaught exception | `false` | `nodemon --exitcrash app.js` |

### 2.4 Installation Methods (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Method | Command | Scope | Best Used When |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Local (Recommended for KTU labs)** | `npm install --save-dev nodemon` | Project only | Real projects with `package.json` |
| **Global** | `npm install -g nodemon` | System-wide | Quick demos, single-file scripts |
| **NPX (No installation)** | `npx nodemon app.js` | Ephemeral | CI/CD, one-off runs |
| **Yarn** | `yarn add nodemon --dev` | Project only | Projects using Yarn package manager |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**: In CI/CD pipelines (GitHub Actions, Jenkins), Nodemon is often replaced with `ts-node-dev` (for TypeScript) or skipped entirely. In Docker containers, `--legacy-watch` is mandatory because inotify events do not propagate across host-container boundaries without volume mount tuning.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivation, Configuration & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step: Setting Up Nodemon in a KTU Lab Project

This is the **canonical procedure** examiners expect students to write in the 14-mark Part B questions. Memorize this sequence.

**Step 1: Initialize the Node.js project**

$$ \text{Mkdir: } \texttt{mkdir ktu-webapp} $$

$$ \text{CD: } \texttt{cd ktu-webapp} $$

$$ \text{Initialize: } \texttt{npm init -y} $$

The `npm init -y` command creates a `package.json` file with default metadata. The `-y` flag auto-accepts all prompts.

**Step 2: Install Express.js (the web framework)**

$$
\texttt{npm install express}
$$

This installs Express into `node_modules/` and updates `dependencies` in `package.json`.

**Step 3: Install Nodemon as a development dependency**

$$
\texttt{npm\ install\ --save-dev\ nodemon}
$$

The `--save-dev` flag places the package in `devDependencies` because it is **only needed during development**, not in production deployment.

**Step 4: Examine the modified `package.json`**

After Step 3, the file should resemble:

$$
\texttt{"devDependencies": \{\ "nodemon": \textasciitilde\texttt{"}\wedge 3.0.0\texttt{"}\ \}\}
$$

**Step 5: Create the application file `app.js`**

The student writes the canonical KTU Express server:

$$
\texttt{const\ express\ =\ require(\textquotesingle express\textquotesingle);}
$$

$$
\texttt{const\ app\ =\ express();}
$$

$$
\texttt{const\ PORT\ =\ 3000;}
$$

$$
\texttt{app.get(\textquotesingle/\textquotesingle,\ (req,\ res)\ =>\ \{\ res.send(\textquotesingle Hello\ KTU\textquotesingle);\ \});}
$$

$$
\texttt{app.listen(PORT,\ ()\ =>\ \{\ console.log(\textasciitilde Server\ on\ \$\{PORT\}\textasciitilde);\ \});}
$$

**Step 6: Modify the `scripts` block in `package.json`**

This is the **most important step** for KTU exams. Open `package.json` and edit:

$$
\texttt{"scripts":\ \{\ "start":\ \textquotedbl nodemon\ app.js\textquotedrl\ \}}
$$

**Step 7: Run the application**

$$
\texttt{npm\ start}
$$

**Observation:** The terminal displays a message like:

$$
\texttt{[nodemon]\ starting\ \textasciitilde node\ app.js\ \textasciitilde}
$$

$$
\texttt{[nodemon]\ watching\ extensions:\ js,mjs,json}
$$

$$
\texttt{Server\ on\ 3000}
$$

**Step 8: Test the live-reload behavior**

Edit `app.js`, change the response string to `'Hello KTU 2024'`, and save. Within ~1 second, the terminal shows:

$$
\texttt{[nodemon]\ restarting\ due\ to\ changes...}
$$

$$
\texttt{[nodemon]\ starting\ \textasciitilde node\ app.js\ \textasciitilde}
$$

$$
\texttt{Server\ on\ 3000}
$$

**Step 9: Manual restart trigger**

If you want to force a restart *without* changing any file, type `rs` in the terminal and press Enter. Nodemon interprets `rs` as a manual restart signal.

### 3.2 The Configuration Cascade — Analytical Decision Matrix

The table below shows the **order of precedence** that Nodemon uses when resolving any setting:

$$
\text{Final Value} = \max(\text{priority})
$$

$$
\text{where } \text{priority}(\text{CLI flag}) > \text{priority}(\text{nodemon.json}) > \text{priority}(\text{package.json}) > \text{priority}(\text{Defaults})
$$

| Priority Level | Source | Example | Wins Over |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P0 (Highest)** | Command-line flag | `nodemon --delay 500` | All others |
| **P1** | `nodemon.json` (project root) | `{"delay": 750}` | P2, P3 |
| **P2** | `package.json` → `nodemonConfig` key | `"nodemonConfig": {"ext": "js html"}` | P3 |
| **P3 (Lowest)** | Built-in defaults | `delay: 1000ms` | Nothing |

### 3.3 Example: Writing a `nodemon.json` Configuration File

Create a file named `nodemon.json` in the project root with the following content:

$$
\texttt{\{}
$$

$$
\texttt{\ \ \ \textquotedbl watch\textquotedbl :\ [\textquotedbl src\textquotedbl ,\ \textquotedbl config\textquotedbl ],}
$$

$$
\texttt{\ \ \ \textquotedbl ext\textquotedbl :\ \textquotedbl js,json,ejs\textquotedbl ,}
$$

$$
\texttt{\ \ \ \textquotedbl ignore\textquotedbl :\ [\textquotedbl src/\_\_tests\_\_/*\textquotedbl ],}
$$

$$
\texttt{\ \ \ \textquotedbl delay\textquotedbl :\ \textquotedbl 2\textquotedrl ,}
$$

$$
\texttt{\ \ \ \textquotedbl verbose\textquotedbl :\ \text{true}}
$$

$$
\texttt{\}}
$$

**Line-by-line explanation**:
- **`watch`**: Explicitly tells Nodemon to monitor the `src/` and `config/` directories.
- **`ext`**: Expands the watched file extensions to include EJS templating files.
- **`ignore`**: Excludes test files from triggering restarts (prevents test-runner loops).
- **`delay`**: Sets a 2-second debounce window to batch multiple simultaneous file saves.
- **`verbose`**: Prints detailed logs of what files triggered restarts and why.

### 3.4 Signal-Based Control — The `SIGUSR2` Mechanism

When Nodemon runs your application, it becomes the **parent process**. The child (your app) can communicate with the parent through OS signals:

| Signal | Sent By | Effect on Nodemon |
|---|---|---|
| `SIGUSR2` | Nodemon → App (default) | Tells the app to shut down gracefully so Nodemon can restart it |
| `SIGUSR1` | App → Nodemon | Asks Nodemon to shut down its own watcher and exit |
| `rs` (typed in terminal) | User → Nodemon | Triggers an immediate manual restart |
| `Ctrl + C` | User → Nodemon | Terminates Nodemon and the child app |

This is why Nodemon requires your application to **handle `SIGUSR2` gracefully** if you have cleanup logic (database connections, file handles) — otherwise, you may experience connection leaks.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architecture Flow Diagram — Nodemon's Runtime Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    devA[Developer saves file] --> fsA[File System emits change event]
    fsA --> watcherA[Chokidar Watcher detects change]
    watcherA --> filterA{Filter Check: ext match and ignore match}
    filterA -- No match --> silentA[Silently skip event]
    filterA -- Match --> debounceA[Wait for delay period: 1000ms default]
    debounceA --> killA[Send SIGUSR2 to child process]
    killA --> waitA[Wait for graceful exit]
    waitA --> spawnA[Spawn new child process with node app.js]
    spawnA --> attachA[Re-attach watcher to new child]
```
