```mermaid
    attachA --> logA[Log restart event to terminal]
    logA --> devA
```

### 4.2 Configuration Resolution Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Nodemon invoked: nodemon app.js] --> cliA[Parse CLI flags]
    cliA --> checkA{Check for nodemon.json}
    checkA -- Found --> loadA[Load nodemon.json]
    checkA -- Not found --> pkgA[Check package.json nodemonConfig]
    pkgA --> mergeA[Merge all configs by priority]
    loadA --> mergeA
    mergeA --> defaultsA[Apply built-in defaults]
    defaultsA --> finalA[Final merged configuration]
    finalA --> watchA[Initialize file watcher]
    watchA --> runA[Spawn child Node.js process]
    runA --> monitorA[Monitor for file changes loop]
```

### 4.3 Development Workflow Comparison — With vs. Without Nodemon

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WithoutNodemon[Traditional Workflow]
        e1A[Edit code] --> s1A[Save file]
        s1A --> sw1A[Switch to terminal]
        sw1A --> k1A[Press Ctrl + C]
        k1A --> t1A[Type node app.js]
        t1A --> r1A[Press Enter]
        r1A --> t1B[Test in browser]
    end
    subgraph WithNodemon[Nodemon Workflow]
        e2A[Edit code] --> s2A[Save file]
        s2A --> autoA[Nodemon auto-detects]
        autoA --> r2A[Auto-restart in 1 sec]
        r2A --> t2A[Refresh browser]
    end
```

### 4.4 Process Tree Diagram — Nodemon as Parent Supervisor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    shellA[Bash Shell PID 5000] --> nodeA[Nodemon Process PID 5100]
    nodeA --> childA[Node.js App PID 5101]
    nodeA -. SIGUSR2 .-> childA
    childA -. exit .-> nodeA
    nodeA --> spawnA[Spawn new PID 5201]
    nodeA --> watcherA[Chokidar Watcher Thread]
    watcherA -. inotify events .-> nodeA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide for KTU**: Notice the bidirectional arrows in the process tree. The **dashed** arrows represent *signals* (non-data events), while **solid** arrows represent the *supervisory parent-child relationship*. The watcher is a *sibling thread* to the app process — they do not communicate directly; they both report to the Nodemon parent.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`**

**Q1. Define Nodemon. Mention any two advantages of using it in Node.js application development.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Nodemon is a command-line utility that automatically restarts a Node.js application whenever it detects changes in the source files of the project directory.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Eliminates manual restarts**: Developers no longer need to stop and restart the server after every code edit, saving significant development time.
2. **Real-time feedback loop**: Any syntax or runtime error appears immediately after the file is saved, enabling faster debugging cycles.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark per advantage)*

> **`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]`**

**Q2. Differentiate between installing Nodemon locally versus globally. When would you prefer each approach?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Local Install (`--save-dev`) | Global Install (`-g`) |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Project-specific | System-wide |
| Stored in | Project's `node_modules` | Global Node path |
| Listed in | `devDependencies` in `package.json` | Not listed anywhere |
| Use case | Real KTU projects, team collaboration, CI/CD | Quick demos, single-file scripts |

**Preference rule**: Use **local install** for any project tracked by Git or submitted for evaluation. Use **global install** only for throwaway testing.

*(2 marks for the table, 1 mark for the preference rule)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 3, Expected]`**

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the architecture of Nodemon with a neat diagram. Describe how it detects file changes and triggers a restart. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write the step-by-step procedure to set up a Node.js Express application with automatic server restart using Nodemon. Include the relevant `package.json` modifications. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(a):

Nodemon operates as a **parent process supervisor** wrapping a child Node.js process. Its architecture consists of **five layers**:

1. **CLI Parser Layer**: Reads the entry script path and command-line flags. For example, `nodemon app.js` sets `app.js` as the spawn target.

2. **Configuration Resolver**: Loads configuration in priority order: CLI flags → `nodemon.json` → `package.json` `nodemonConfig` key → defaults.

3. **File System Watcher**: Uses the `chokidar` library (a wrapper over `fs.watch`/`inotify`) to subscribe to `change`, `add`, `unlink`, `addDir`, and `unlinkDir` events. It filters events based on the configured `ext` and `ignore` patterns.

4. **Process Supervisor**: When a qualifying event fires, it:
   - Sends `SIGUSR2` to the child process.
   - Waits for graceful termination.
   - Spawns a new child via `child_process.spawn()`.
   - Re-attaches the watcher to the new process.

5. **Event Emitter**: Emits lifecycle events (`start`, `restart`, `crash`, `exit`) that can be observed by attached debugging tools.

**Detection-restart flow**:
- File save → `inotify` kernel event → `chokidar` callback → debounce timer (default 1000ms) → kill child → spawn new child → log restart.

*(3 marks for layer explanation, 2 marks for diagram, 2 marks for flow description)*

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Procedure to set up Express + Nodemon:**

1. **Create project folder**:
   `mkdir ktu-express-app && cd ktu-express-app`

2. **Initialize npm**:
   `npm init -y`
   *(Creates `package.json` with default fields.)*

3. **Install Express**:
   `npm install express`

4. **Install Nodemon as dev dependency**:
   `npm install --save-dev nodemon`

5. **Create `app.js`** with a minimal Express server:
   ```javascript
   const express = require('express');
   const app = express();
   app.get('/', (req, res) => res.send('KTU 2024'));
   app.listen(3000, () => console.log('Running on 3000'));
   ```

6. **Modify `package.json` scripts block**:
   ```json
   "scripts": { "start": "nodemon app.js" }
   ```

7. **Run the application**:
   `npm start`

8. **Verify auto-restart**: Edit `app.js` (e.g., change `'KTU 2024'` to `'KTU Web App'`), save, and observe the terminal logs indicating `[nodemon] restarting due to changes...`.

9. **Optional**: Create `nodemon.json` for advanced configuration with custom `watch`, `ext`, `ignore`, and `delay` properties.

*(1 mark per step × 6 steps = 6 marks, 1 mark for diagram/justification)*

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** What is the default configuration cascade of Nodemon? List any four Nodemon CLI flags with their purpose. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the difference between Nodemon, `node --watch` (built-in), and `pm2`. When would you choose each? **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Default Configuration Cascade** (priority high → low):
1. **CLI flags** (e.g., `--delay 500`)
2. **`nodemon.json`** in project root
3. **`package.json` → `nodemonConfig`** key
4. **Built-in defaults**

**Built-in defaults include**:
- Watched extensions: `js, mjs, json, coffee, litcoffee`
- Ignored: `node_modules, .git, .nyc_output`
- Restart delay: 1000 ms
- Default executable: `node`

**Four Nodemon CLI flags:**

| Flag | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `--watch <path>` | Adds an extra directory to the watch list beyond the current working directory |
| `--ext <exts>` | Specifies additional file extensions to watch (comma-separated) |
| `--delay <seconds>` | Sets a debounce delay to avoid restart loops during bulk saves |
| `--ignore <pattern>` | Excludes files matching the pattern from triggering restarts |

*(3 marks for cascade + defaults, 4 marks for flag table)*

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(b):

| Tool | Type | Use Case | Stop/Restart | Config File |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Nodemon** | Dev-only CLI wrapper | Local development with hot reload | Auto on file change | `nodemon.json` or `package.json` |
| **`node --watch`** | Built-in Node.js flag (v18+) | Minimalist projects, no extra dependency | Auto on file change | None (CLI only) |
| **PM2** | Production process manager | Server deployment, clustering, load balancing | Auto on crash, manual via `pm2 restart` | `ecosystem.config.js` |

**Selection criteria**:
- Use **Nodemon** when developing an Express/Node.js app locally and need rich features like custom ignore patterns, multi-directory watching, and signal control.
- Use **`node --watch`** for simple scripts, learning exercises, or when you want zero dependencies.
- Use **PM2** in **production environments** for zero-downtime reloads, log management, and cluster-mode scaling.

*(3 marks for table, 4 marks for selection reasoning)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing `--save-dev` with `--save`**: Many students write `npm install nodemon` without the `--save-dev` flag. This puts Nodemon in `dependencies` (production), which is **wrong** and will cost 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the `scripts` block edit**: A correct installation without modifying the `scripts` block to use `nodemon app.js` is incomplete. Examiners expect `npm start` to invoke Nodemon.
> 3. **Saying "Nodemon reloads the page"**: Nodemon restarts the **server**, not the **browser page**. The browser must still be refreshed manually. Examiners penalize this misconception.
> 4. **Recommending Nodemon for production**: Nodemon is **development-only**. Never suggest it for production. This is an instant 2-mark deduction in viva questions.
> 5. **Writing `nodemon --save app.js`**: This is syntactically wrong. The `--save` flag is a `npm install` flag, not a `nodemon` flag. Mixing the two is a common error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Nodemon = Dev-only auto-restart utility** for Node.js applications.
- ✅ **Install locally** with `npm install --save-dev nodemon` — this is the KTU-recommended approach.
- ✅ **Modify the `scripts` block** in `package.json` to `"start": "nodemon app.js"`.
- ✅ **Run** with `npm start`, not with `node app.js` directly.
- ✅ **Default watched extensions**: `js, mjs, json, coffee, litcoffee`.
- ✅ **Default ignored folders**: `node_modules`, `.git`.
- ✅ **Default restart delay**: **1000 ms** (1 second).
- ✅ **Manual restart trigger**: type `rs` in the terminal.
- ✅ **Nodemon uses SIGUSR2** to gracefully stop the child process before restart.
- ✅ **Configuration priority**: CLI flags > `nodemon.json` > `package.json` (`nodemonConfig`) > defaults.
- ✅ **Use `--legacy-watch`** when developing inside Docker or on network drives (forces polling).
- ✅ **Nodemon is NOT for production** — use `pm2`, `forever`, or `systemd` instead.
- ✅ **Alternative for simple cases**: `node --watch app.js` (built-in since Node.js v18+).
- ✅ **Project layout convention**: Place configuration in a `nodemon.json` file at the project root for cleaner version control.
- ✅ **Watch the `src/` folder explicitly** if you follow an MVC folder structure where `app.js` is at the root.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Public Folder for Serving Static Files
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Public Folder for Serving Static Files ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of server-side web application development (particularly with **Node.js / Express.js** frameworks covered under KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3), the **Public Folder** is a designated directory in the project root that is configured to host **static assets** — files that are delivered to the client *exactly as they are stored on the server*, without any dynamic processing, template rendering, or server-side logic. $$ \text{Static Resource} = \begin{Bmatrix} \text{HTML files} \\ \text{CSS stylesheets} \\ \text{JavaScript files} \\ \text{Images (PNG, JPG, SVG, GIF)} \\ \text{Fonts (WOFF, TTF, OTF)} \\ \text{Media (MP4, MP3, PDF)} \\ \text{Favicons and manifest files} \end{Bmatrix} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A static file is one whose content is **fixed at the time of deployment** and is served verbatim. The server does *not* execute, compile, or modify the file before transmission. This is the cornerstone contrast with **dynamic content**, which is generated on-the-fly via templating engines (EJS, Pug, Handlebars) covered in earlier sub-modules of Module 3. > [!NOTE] > **The Public Directory Convention:** In an Express.js project, the folder is conventionally named `public/` (or `static/`). Any file placed inside this directory becomes *automatically accessible* by clients via a URL path that mirrors the file's relative location inside the folder. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Shop Window Display Imagine a **clothing store** on a busy street: | Store Concept | Web Application Equivalent | |---|---| | **Shop window display** (mannequins, price tags) | The **Public Folder** — items on display for everyone | | **Storage room in the back** (inventory, accounts) | **Server-side logic, databases, private routes** | | **Window shopper** | A **browser** requesting a resource | | **Manager choosing what to display** | The **developer** deciding which assets are public | | **Items shown as-is** (no alterations) | **Static files served verbatim** | Just as a store manager only puts finished, ready-to-show products in the window (and keeps ledgers and sensitive files locked in the back), a web developer only places **finished, non-sensitive assets** in the `public/` folder. The cashier's records, supplier contracts, and internal memos **must never** be placed in the window — and sensitive server files must never be placed in `public/`. > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:** If opening the file in any text editor reveals *no server-side code that needs to be executed* (no `<%= %>`, no ``, no `{{ }}` template tags), then it is a **static file** and *can* be hosted from the public folder. ### 1.3 What the Public Folder Is NOT It is equally important to understand what the public folder excludes: - ❌ **Server-side JavaScript files** (e.g., `app.js`, `routes/`) — these contain executable code with database credentials. - ❌ **`node_modules/`** — third-party packages; serving these would expose source code and bloat bandwidth. - ❌ **`.env` files** — environment variables, API keys, secrets. - ❌ **Configuration files** (`package.json`, `package-lock.json`) — may reveal dependencies and vulnerabilities. - ❌ **Dynamic template files** (`.ejs`, `.pug`) — these need server rendering, not direct delivery. - ❌ **`.git/` directory** — version control metadata. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Configuration Sheet ### 2.1 The Operational Model — How Static Serving Works The lifecycle of a static file request is a **linear, three-stage pipeline**. Understanding each stage is critical for KTU exam questions on request flow: #### Stage 1: Client Request Initiation The browser constructs a URL based on the document's relative references. For example, when an HTML file contains: $$ \text{HTML Reference} \rightarrow \texttt{} $$ The browser issues a `GET` HTTP request to the server at the path `/css/style.css`. #### Stage 2: Middleware Interception The Express server processes the incoming request through a **chain of middleware functions** in the order they were registered using `app.use()`. The static-serving middleware is *typically* the *first* middleware registered (for performance reasons). #### Stage 3: File Resolution and Delivery The middleware uses Node.js's built-in `fs` (file system) module to: 1. **Resolve** the URL path to a physical file system path. 2. **Verify** the file exists. 3. **Determine** the MIME type via the `mime-types` package. 4. **Stream** the file to the response with appropriate HTTP headers. 5. **Terminate** the request-response cycle (via `next()` is *not* called for matched static files). ### 2.2 Configuration Cheat Sheet — The Express `express.static()` Built-in The `express.static()` function is a **built-in middleware** shipped with Express. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects familiarity with its signature: ```javascript express.static(root, [options]) ``` | Parameter | Type | Default | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | `root` | String | *Required* | The root directory from which to serve static assets | | `options.dotfiles` | String | `'ignore'` | How to handle dotfiles (`allow`, `deny`, `ignore`) | | `options.etag` | Boolean | `true` | Enable/disable ETag generation for caching | | `options.index` | Boolean/String | `'index.html'` | Specify the default file for directory requests | | `options.maxAge` | Number/String | `0` | Set the `Cache-Control: max-age` in milliseconds | | `options.redirect` | Boolean | `true` | Redirect to trailing `/` for directory access | | `options.fallthrough` | Boolean | `true` | Pass to next middleware if file not found | ### 2.3 The High-Yield URL-to-File Mapping Rule The most important KTU exam concept is the **mount path vs. serve path** distinction. The relationship is governed by: $$ \text{Requested URL} = \text{Mount Path} + \text{Relative File Path inside Root} $$ | `app.use()` Configuration | Physical File Location | Accessible URL | |---|---|---| | `app.use(express.static('public'))` | `public/index.html` | `http://host/index.html` | | `app.use(express.static('public'))` | `public/css/style.css` | `http://host/css/style.css` | | `app.use('/static', express.static('public'))` | `public/css/style.css` | `http://host/static/css/style.css` | | `app.use(express.static('files'))` | `files/images/logo.png` | `http://host/images/logo.png` | ### 2.4 Why the Public Folder Matters in Engineering In real-world web engineering, serving static files efficiently is not a trivial concern. Consider the following production realities: > [!NOTE] > **Performance Engineering Context:** A modern web page makes an average of **30–50 requests** for static assets (CSS, JS, images, fonts). Efficient static serving can reduce server CPU load by **40–60%** compared to dynamic rendering for the same content. This is why **CDNs (Content Delivery Networks)** exist — they are essentially *distributed, highly optimized public folders*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Security Engineering Context:** Misconfigured static serving is one of the **OWASP Top 10** adjacent vulnerabilities. Serving `.env` files, `.git/HEAD`, or `package.json` can leak database credentials, source code, and dependency versions to attackers. Always explicitly define what is in `public/`. ### 2.5 The Static Serving Pipeline — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Browser GET /css/style.css] --> B[Express Server] B --> C{Middleware Chain} C --> D[express.static middleware] D --> E{File exists in public/?} E -- Yes --> F[Determine MIME type] F --> G[Set Cache-Control, ETag headers] G --> H[Stream file to response] H --> I[Client receives 200 OK] E -- No --> J[Call next middleware] J --> K[Route handler or 404] ``` ## 3. Step-by-Step Implementation & Analytical Walkthrough ### 3.1 Setting Up the Public Folder — Exhaustive Procedure The KTU 2024 lab exam frequently requires a complete project setup. Below is the **full, step-by-step implementation** that would earn full marks. #### Step 1: Project Initialization ```bash mkdir my-web-app cd my-web-app npm init -y npm install express ``` > **Logic:** The `npm init -y` command creates a `package.json` file with default metadata. The `npm install express` command downloads the Express framework into `node_modules/` and adds it as a dependency in `package.json`. #### Step 2: Directory Structure Creation ```text my-web-app/ ├── node_modules/ ├── public/ │ ├── index.html │ ├── css/ │ │ └── style.css │ ├── js/ │ │ └── app.js │ └── images/ │ └── logo.png ├── app.js ├── package.json └── package-lock.json ``` > **Logic:** The `public/` directory is created at the project root. Sub-directories (`css/`, `js/`, `images/`) organize assets by type — this is a best practice that improves maintainability and cache-busting strategies. #### Step 3: Express Application Setup — `app.js` ```javascript // app.js — Main server file const express = require('express'); const path = require('path'); const app = express(); const PORT = 3000; // Step 3a: Configure the static middleware // The string 'public' tells Express to look in the ./public directory app.use(express.static(path.join(__dirname, 'public'))); // Step 3b: Define dynamic routes (these are NOT static) app.get('/', (req, res) => { res.send('Welcome to the Home Page'); }); app.get('/about', (req, res) => { res.send('About Us Page'); }); // Step 3c: Start the server app.listen(PORT, () => { console.log(`Server is running on http://localhost:${PORT}`); }); ``` > **Logic Walkthrough:** > - `path.join(__dirname, 'public')` constructs an **absolute path** to the `public` directory. Using `__dirname` ensures the path is correct regardless of the process's current working directory (CWD). > - `app.use(express.static(...))` registers the static middleware *before* route handlers. This is critical: static files are served *first*, and only unmatched requests fall through to dynamic routes. > - If a request to `/` arrives, the static middleware first checks for `public/index.html`. If it exists, it is served (200 OK). If not, the request falls through to the route handler. #### Step 4: Sample Static HTML File — `public/index.html` ```html My Web App

Hello from the Public Folder!

``` > **Logic Walkthrough:** > - The `href` and `src` attributes use **absolute paths** (starting with `/`). These are resolved relative to the **server root**, not the document URL. > - The browser will issue separate `GET` requests for `style.css`, `logo.png`, and `app.js` once it parses the HTML. > - The server's static middleware will resolve these to `public/css/style.css`, `public/images/logo.png`, and `public/js/app.js` respectively. #### Step 5: Virtual Path Prefix (Advanced) For production environments, it is common to mount static files under a **virtual path prefix**: ```javascript // Mount public/ under the URL prefix /assets app.use('/assets', express.static(path.join(__dirname, 'public'))); ``` > **Logic:** With this configuration: > - `public/css/style.css` is now accessible at `http://localhost:3000/assets/css/style.css` > - `public/images/logo.png` is accessible at `http://localhost:3000/assets/images/logo.png` > - The physical file structure remains unchanged; only the URL path is prefixed. The mapping formula becomes: $$ \text{Accessible URL} = \underbrace{\text{Mount Path}}_{\text{/assets}} \; + \; \underbrace{\text{Relative Path in Root}}_{\text{/css/style.css}} $$ $$ \text{Accessible URL} = \text{/assets/css/style.css} $$ ### 3.2 Multiple Static Directories — Analytical Scenario Express allows multiple static directories to be registered. The order of registration determines **priority**: ```javascript // Higher priority — checked first app.use(express.static(path.join(__dirname, 'public'))); // Lower priority — checked only if file not found in 'public' app.use(express.static(path.join(__dirname, 'node_modules/bootstrap/dist'))); ``` > **Logic Walkthrough:** When a request arrives, Express checks the `public/` directory first. If the file is found, it is served. If not, Express checks the Bootstrap `dist/` directory. This pattern is useful for serving framework assets (like Bootstrap CSS) directly from `node_modules` without copying them into your project. ### 3.3 Security Configuration — Blocking Dotfiles By default, Express's static middleware **ignores dotfiles** (files starting with `.`). This is a security feature: ```javascript // OPTION 1: Ignore dotfiles (default behavior) app.use(express.static('public', { dotfiles: 'ignore' })); // OPTION 2: Deny dotfiles (send 403 Forbidden) app.use(express.static('public', { dotfiles: 'deny' })); // OPTION 3: Allow dotfiles (DANGEROUS — not recommended) app.use(express.static('public', { dotfiles: 'allow' })); ``` > **Logic:** The `dotfiles` option prevents accidental exposure of files like `.htaccess`, `.env`, or `.gitignore` that may inadvertently be placed in the public directory. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Project Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow ```mermaid graph TD Client[Client Browser] Server[Express Server] Middleware[express.static Middleware] FSPublic[File System: public/] FSRoutes[Route Handlers] FS404[404 Handler] Client -->|HTTP GET request| Server Server -->|Pass to middleware chain| Middleware Middleware -->|Resolve URL to file path| FSPublic FSPublic -->|File found: stream + 200 OK| Client FSPublic -->|File not found: next| FSRoutes FSRoutes -->|Route matched: send response| Client FSRoutes -->|No route matched| FS404 FS404 -->|404 Not Found| Client ``` ### 4.2 URL Resolution Algorithm — Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Incoming GET request: /css/style.css]) --> Strip[Strip mount path: css/style.css] Strip --> Join[Join with root directory: public/css/style.css] Join --> Check{File exists on disk?} Check -- Yes --> MIME[Determine MIME type: text/css] MIME --> Headers[Set response headers: Content-Type, ETag, Cache-Control] Headers --> Stream[Stream file to response] Stream --> End200([Response: 200 OK]) Check -- No --> Next[Call next middleware] Next --> RouteCheck{Route handler exists?} RouteCheck -- Yes --> DynamicResponse[Dynamic response generated] RouteCheck -- No --> End404([Response: 404 Not Found]) DynamicResponse --> EndDyn([Response: 200 OK with dynamic content]) ``` ### 4.3 Static Folder Hierarchy — Tree Structure ```mermaid graph TD Root[my-web-app/] Pub[public/] Css[css/] Js[js/] Img[images/] Fonts[fonts/] Html1[index.html] Style[style.css] Script[app.js] Logo[logo.png] Roboto[roboto.woff2] Root --> Pub Root --> AppJs[app.js] Root --> Pkg[package.json] Pub --> Html1 Pub --> Css Pub --> Js Pub --> Img Pub --> Fonts Css --> Style Js --> Script Img --> Logo Fonts --> Roboto ``` ### 4.4 Request-Response Sequence Diagram ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant B as Browser participant S as Express Server participant M as Static Middleware participant FS as File System B->>S: GET /css/style.css S->>M: Forward request to middleware chain M->>FS: Check if public/css/style.css exists alt File Found FS-->>M: File stream (data chunks) M->>M: Determine MIME type (text/css) M->>M: Set Cache-Control and ETag headers M-->>B: HTTP 200 OK + file content else File Not Found FS-->>M: ENOENT error M->>S: next() to next middleware S->>S: Check route handlers alt Route Matched S-->>B: HTTP 200 OK + dynamic content else No Route Matched S-->>B: HTTP 404 Not Found end end ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define the term "static file" in the context of web application development. List any four examples of static files typically served from a public folder.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **static file** is a file whose content is **fixed at deployment time** and is delivered to the client **without any server-side processing, compilation, or modification**. The server transmits the file verbatim via HTTP. **Four examples:** 1. **HTML files** (e.g., `index.html`) — though often with dynamic links, the file itself is not processed. 2. **CSS stylesheets** (e.g., `style.css`) — pure styling rules, no server logic. 3. **Images** (e.g., `logo.png`, `banner.jpg`) — binary data served as-is. 4. **Client-side JavaScript files** (e.g., `app.js`) — executed by the browser, not the server. *(Award 1 mark for the definition, 0.5 marks each for the four examples = total 3 marks.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Explain the purpose of the `express.static()` middleware in Node.js. Write the syntax to serve files from a directory named `assets` mounted under the URL path `/resources`.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Purpose:** The `express.static()` is a **built-in middleware function** in Express.js that serves static files (HTML, CSS, JS, images, etc.) from a specified root directory. It handles file resolution, MIME type detection, caching headers, and streaming automatically, eliminating the need for manual file system operations in route handlers. *(2 marks)* **Syntax:** ```javascript app.use('/resources', express.static('assets')); ``` *(1 mark for the correct syntax. Note the order: mount path is the first argument to `app.use()`, and the root directory is passed to `express.static()`.)* --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** Explain the concept of a public folder in Express.js applications. Discuss the directory structure best practices for organizing static assets, with a suitable example. *(7 marks)* **(b)** With the help of a complete Express.js code example, demonstrate how to: - Serve static files from a `public` directory - Use a virtual path prefix `/static` for all static assets - Configure caching with a `maxAge` of 1 day - Handle the case when a requested static file is not found *(7 marks)* --- **Model Answer — Question A:** **(a) Public Folder Concept & Best Practices (7 Marks):** The **public folder** in an Express.js application is a designated directory that hosts **static assets** — files delivered to clients without server-side modification. The `express.static()` middleware maps this directory to a URL space, making files accessible via HTTP. **Key Concepts:** - Static files are served **as-is** (verbatim). - The folder is conventionally named `public/` or `static/`. - Assets are organized into **sub-directories by type** (CSS, JS, images, fonts). - **Sensitive files** (`.env`, server code, `node_modules`) must **never** be placed in the public folder. **Recommended Directory Structure:** ```text project-root/ ├── public/ │ ├── index.html │ ├── about.html │ ├── css/ │ │ ├── main.css │ │ └── responsive.css │ ├── js/ │ │ ├── main.js │ │ └── utils.js │ ├── images/ │ │ ├── logo.png │ │ └── hero-banner.jpg │ └── fonts/ │ └── custom-font.woff2 ├── routes/ │ └── api.js ├── app.js ├── package.json └── .env ``` *(2 marks for concept, 2 marks for structure explanation, 3 marks for the example tree.)* --- **(b) Complete Code Example (7 Marks):** ```javascript // app.js const express = require('express'); const path = require('path'); const app = express(); const PORT = process.env.PORT || 3000; // (i) Serve static files from 'public' directory // (ii) Mount under virtual path prefix '/static' // (iii) Configure caching: maxAge = 1 day = 86400000 ms app.use('/static', express.static(path.join(__dirname, 'public'), { maxAge: '1d', // Cache-Control: max-age=86400 etag: true, // Enable ETag for cache validation dotfiles: 'ignore', // Security: ignore dotfiles index: 'index.html' // Default file for directory requests })); // Route to demonstrate what happens when a static file is NOT found app.get('/static/*', (req, res) => { res.status(404).send(`

404 - Static File Not Found

The requested file ${req.originalUrl} does not exist in the public directory.

Go back to static assets `); }); // Dynamic route (not static) app.get('/', (req, res) => { res.send(`

Dynamic Home Page

`); }); app.listen(PORT, () => { console.log(`Server running at http://localhost:${PORT}`); console.log(`Static assets available at http://localhost:${PORT}/static/`); }); ``` **Explanation of Key Lines:** | Line | Explanation | Marks | |---|---|---| | `path.join(__dirname, 'public')` | Constructs an **absolute path** to the `public` directory, ensuring correctness regardless of CWD. | 1 | | `'/static'` (mount path) | All static URLs will be prefixed with `/static`. E.g., `public/css/main.css` → `http://host/static/css/main.css`. | 1.5 | | `maxAge: '1d'` | Sets the `Cache-Control: max-age=86400` header, instructing browsers to cache assets for 24 hours. | 1 | | `dotfiles: 'ignore'` | Prevents accidental exposure of `.env`, `.gitignore`, etc. | 1 | | `app.get('/static/*', ...)` | Catch-all route to handle 404 cases for missing static files gracefully. | 1.5 | | `app.listen(PORT, ...)` | Starts the server on the specified port. | 1 | --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **(a)** Differentiate between **static file serving** and **dynamic content generation** in Express.js. Explain when each approach is appropriate with suitable examples. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Design and implement an Express.js application that: - Serves a homepage (`index.html`) from the public folder - Serves CSS and JS files from appropriate sub-directories - Implements a dynamic `/api/time` route that returns the current server time - Logs each request with a timestamp using custom middleware *(7 marks)* --- **Model Answer — Question B:** **(a) Static vs. Dynamic Content (7 Marks):** | Aspect | Static File Serving | Dynamic Content Generation | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Files delivered **as-is** from disk. | Content **generated on-the-fly** per request. | | **Processing** | **No** server-side execution. | Server executes code, queries DB, renders templates. | | **Performance** | **Extremely fast** (direct file I/O, can be CDN-cached). | **Slower** (CPU, DB, rendering overhead). | | **Personalization** | **None** — same file for all users. | **High** — content varies per user/request. | | **Caching** | Browser, CDN, proxy caching. | Usually not cacheable. | | **Example** | `app.use(express.static('public'))` | `app.get('/user/:id', (req, res) => { ... })` | | **Use Case** | Logos, CSS, JS libraries, documentation PDFs. | User dashboards, real-time data, personalized feeds. | | **MIME Type** | Determined by file extension. | Set explicitly via `res.type()` or `res.json()`. | *(2 marks for the table, 2 marks for when each is appropriate, 3 marks for code examples.)* **When Static is Appropriate:** - Marketing landing pages - Product images and videos - Framework assets (Bootstrap, jQuery) - Downloadable files (PDFs, ZIPs) **When Dynamic is Appropriate:** - User authentication dashboards - Real-time stock prices - Search results with personalization - E-commerce shopping carts --- **(b) Complete Application Design (7 Marks):** **File Structure:** ```text ktu-webapp/ ├── public/ │ ├── index.html │ ├── css/ │ │ └── styles.css │ └── js/ │ └── main.js ├── middleware/ │ └── logger.js ├── app.js └── package.json ``` **`middleware/logger.js` — Custom Request Logger:** ```javascript // Custom middleware to log every request with timestamp const requestLogger = (req, res, next) => { const timestamp = new Date().toISOString(); console.log(`[${timestamp}] ${req.method} ${req.originalUrl} - IP: ${req.ip}`); next(); // Pass control to the next middleware }; module.exports = requestLogger; ``` **`app.js` — Main Application:** ```javascript const express = require('express'); const path = require('path'); const requestLogger = require('./middleware/logger'); const app = express(); const PORT = 3000; // (i) Register custom logging middleware FIRST app.use(requestLogger); // (ii) Serve static files from 'public' directory app.use(express.static(path.join(__dirname, 'public'), { extensions: ['html', 'htm'] // Allows /about to serve /about.html })); // (iii) Dynamic route: /api/time returns current server time app.get('/api/time', (req, res) => { const now = new Date(); res.json({ iso: now.toISOString(), unix: now.getTime(), readable: now.toString(), timezone: Intl.DateTimeFormat().resolvedOptions().timeZone }); }); // (iv) 404 handler for unmatched routes app.use((req, res) => { res.status(404).send('

404 - Page Not Found

'); }); app.listen(PORT, () => { console.log(`Server running at http://localhost:${PORT}`); }); ``` **`public/index.html` — Static Homepage:** ```html KTU Web App

Welcome to KTU Web Application

Current server time: Loading...

``` **`public/js/main.js` — Client-Side Fetch:** ```javascript document.getElementById('fetchTime').addEventListener('click', async () => { const response = await fetch('/api/time'); const data = await response.json(); document.getElementById('time').textContent = data.readable; }); ``` **Explanation of Marks Distribution:** | Component | Description | Marks | |---|---|---| | Custom logger middleware | Modular logging with timestamp, method, URL, IP. | 1.5 | | Static file serving with `extensions` | Serves `.html` files without extension in URL. | 1.5 | | Dynamic `/api/time` route | Returns JSON with multiple time formats. | 2 | | Correct middleware ordering | Logger → static → dynamic routes → 404. | 1 | | HTML + CSS + JS integration | Demonstrates end-to-end static-to-dynamic flow. | 1 | --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Path Argument Confusion:** Students often write `app.use(express.static('/public'))` with a leading slash. This creates an **absolute filesystem path** at the system root (`/public`), not a relative path to the project. Always use `path.join(__dirname, 'public')` or the relative string `'public'`. > 2. **Middleware Order Errors:** Placing route handlers *before* `express.static()` means static files are never served (routes intercept first). Always register static middleware **first**. > 3. **Mount Path Misunderstanding:** Writing `app.use('/static', express.static('public'))` does NOT make files accessible at `/style.css` — they are at `/static/style.css`. Students forget the prefix. > 4. **Serving Sensitive Files:** Students sometimes place `app.js` or `.env` in the public folder "for testing." This is a **critical security vulnerability** and will result in **zero marks** for security-related questions. > 5. **Confusing `res.sendFile()` with `express.static()`:** `res.sendFile()` serves a **single file** in a route handler. `express.static()` is a **middleware** that serves an entire directory. Do not interchange them. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ The **public folder** is a directory for **static assets** (HTML, CSS, JS, images, fonts) served verbatim without server-side processing. - ✅ `express.static(root)` is the **built-in middleware** for serving static files in Express.js. - ✅ The **URL-to-file mapping** follows: `Requested URL = Mount Path + Relative Path in Root`. - ✅ **Virtual path prefix** is added as the first argument to `app.use()`: `app.use('/assets', express.static('public'))`. - ✅ Static middleware should be registered **first** (before route handlers) for performance and correctness. - ✅ Use `path.join(__dirname, 'public')` for **absolute, CWD-independent** paths. - ✅ The `dotfiles` option (default: `'ignore'`) prevents accidental exposure of sensitive dotfiles. - ✅ The `maxAge` option controls browser caching via the `Cache-Control` header. - ✅ **Security rule:** Never place `.env`, server code, `node_modules/`, or `.git/` in the public folder. - ✅ **Performance tip:** In production, use a reverse proxy (Nginx) or CDN to serve static files, offloading the Node.js process. - ✅ Multiple static directories can be chained: Express checks them **in registration order** (first match wins).

topics.title: Creating Page Routes
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Creating Page Routes

# 🌐 Creating Page Routes in Web Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Essence):**
> *Page routing* is the mechanism by which a web application maps a specific URL (Uniform Resource Locator) path to a corresponding view, component, or handler function, enabling navigation between different logical pages or screens — without requiring a full page reload from the server in the case of client-side routing.

---

## 🧠 Core Technical Definition

In modern web application development, a **route** is a declarative mapping between a **URL pattern** and the **UI component (or page)** that should be rendered when a user visits that URL. When a web framework sets up routing infrastructure, it intercepts URL changes and dynamically renders the appropriate view, making the application feel like a traditional multi-page website while actually being a **Single Page Application (SPA)**.

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hotel Floor Directory"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Hotel Floor Directory**
>
> Imagine you walk into a **5-star hotel** and want to go to **Room 304**.
> - The **URL** is like the **room number** you type at the lift panel (`/room/304`).
> - The **Router** is like the **floor directory** — it looks at your room number and tells you *which floor* to go to and *which door* to knock on.
> - The **Component/Page** is the **room itself** — what you actually experience when you arrive.
> - The **History API** is the **building's internal memory** — it remembers that you came from the lobby, so the "Back" button takes you there instantly.
>
> You never rebuild the whole hotel each time you change rooms. The hotel (your app) stays standing, and only the **view** changes. This is exactly what client-side routing does in an SPA.

### 📌 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Route** | A URL-to-component mapping rule |
| **Router** | The engine that matches URLs to routes |
| **Route Path** | The URL pattern (e.g., `/users/:id`) |
| **Route Parameter** | Dynamic segment in a URL (e.g., `:id` in `/users/:id`) |
| **Navigation** | The act of moving from one route to another |
| **SPA** | Single Page Application — a web app that loads once and updates dynamically |
| **MPA** | Multi-Page Application — a traditional site where every URL is a full server request |
| **History API** | Browser API (`pushState`, `replaceState`) that enables URL manipulation without reload |
| **Nested Route** | A route rendered inside a parent route's component |
| **Route Guard** | Logic that blocks or allows navigation based on conditions (e.g., authentication) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme places strong emphasis on understanding the **difference between client-side and server-side routing** because it determines whether your application is classified as an SPA or MPA. Expect a 3-mark question on this distinction.

---

## 🆚 Client-Side vs Server-Side Routing

| Feature | Server-Side Routing (MPA) | Client-Side Routing (SPA) |
|---------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Page Reload** | Full reload on every navigation | No reload — only view swap |
| **URL Handling** | Browser sends request to server | JavaScript intercepts URL change |
| **Initial Load** | Fast (only one page) | Slower (entire app bundle) |
| **Subsequent Loads** | Slower (full HTML each time) | Near-instant (view swap) |
| **SEO** | Naturally strong | Needs extra SSR/SSG setup |
| **API Used** | HTTP requests | History API (`pushState`) or Hash (`#/`) |
| **Examples** | PHP, Django, traditional JSP | React Router, Vue Router, Angular Router |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📚 Deep Theoretical Analysis

## 🔧 How Page Routing Works — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The operational flow below is the **core mental model** KTU examiners test. Memorize the sequence — it is asked almost every year.

1. **User Action Initiation** — The user clicks a `<Link>`, types a URL, or triggers programmatic navigation (`router.push('/home')`).
2. **URL Change Event** — The browser's URL bar updates. The router intercepts this change via the **History API** (`popstate` event) or by overriding the default link behavior.
3. **Route Matching Algorithm** — The router compares the new URL against all registered route patterns, using pattern-matching (often regex or path-to-regexp style).
4. **Component Resolution** — The matched route's associated component (or render function) is identified.
5. **Conditional Rendering** — The previously rendered component is **unmounted** (or hidden), and the new component is **mounted** in the router's outlet/placeholder.
6. **Lifecycle Hooks Fire** — Route guards (`onBeforeNavigate`, `loader`, etc.) execute. Data fetching begins.
7. **DOM Update** — The virtual DOM diffs and the browser repaints only the changed region — **no full reload**.

---

## 🧩 Anatomy of a Route Definition

A typical route object contains **five high-yield fields** that KTU expects you to identify:

$$\text{Route} = \{ \text{path}, \text{element}, \text{children}, \text{loader}, \text{guard} \}$$

| Field | Purpose | Example Value |
|-------|---------|---------------|
| `path` | URL pattern to match | `/products/:productId` |
| `element` | Component to render | `<ProductDetail />` |
| `children` | Nested routes (relative paths) | `[{ path: 'reviews' }]` |
| `loader` | Async data fetcher before render | `async () => fetch(...)` |
| `guard` | Access control predicate | `() => isAuthenticated` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `:productId` instead of `productId`?**
> The colon-prefixed syntax is a **parameter placeholder** — when the URL is `/products/42`, the router extracts `42` and makes it available as `params.productId` inside the component. This is **dynamic routing** at its finest.

---

## 📊 KTU Formula Sheet — Routing Patterns & URL Structures

| Concept | Pattern Syntax | Example URL | Captured Params |
|---------|---------------|-------------|-----------------|
| Static Path | `/about` | `/about` | None |
| Dynamic Segment | `/users/:id` | `/users/101` | `id = "101"` |
| Optional Segment | `/posts/:slug?` | `/posts` or `/posts/hello` | `slug` may be `undefined` |
| Wildcard / Catch-All | `/files/*` | `/files/a/b/c.txt` | `*` captures full remainder |
| Nested Relative | `/dashboard` + `settings` | `/dashboard/settings` | Inherits parent context |
| Query String | `/search?q=react` | `/search?q=react` | Parsed into `searchParams` |
| Hash Route (legacy) | `/#/home` | `/#/home` | No server read |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Dynamic routing with parameters is the backbone of **e-commerce product pages**, **user profile pages**, **blog post URLs**, and **admin dashboards** — all of which are **high-frequency KTU case study topics**. Mastering `useParams()` style APIs is non-negotiable.

---

## 🛠️ Routing Approaches in Modern Web Apps

| Approach | Mechanism | Best Use Case |
|----------|-----------|---------------|
| **Hash-based Routing** | Uses `#/path` in URL; no server config needed | Legacy apps, static hosting without rewrites |
| **History API (pushState)** | Clean URLs (`/path`); requires server fallback config | Production SPAs on Node/NGINX |
| **Memory Routing** | Routing held in JS memory, URL never changes | React Native, testing environments |
| **File-based Routing** | Folders become routes (Next.js, Nuxt) | SSR frameworks, content sites |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse the **Hash Router** with the **Browser/History Router**. The key difference: `/#/about` (Hash) sends only `/` to the server, while `/about` (History) sends the full path. KTU frequently tests this with a one-liner: *"What is the role of the server in History API routing?"* The answer: **It must serve `index.html` for all unknown paths** (catch-all route) to prevent 404 errors on hard refresh.

---

## 🧭 Programmatic vs Declarative Navigation

- **Declarative Navigation:** Using `<Link to="/home">Home</Link>` — the router handles the click.
- **Programmatic Navigation:** Calling `router.push('/home')` or `navigate('/home')` inside a function (e.g., after form submission or login).

$$\text{Navigation Type} = \begin{cases} \text{Declarative} & \text{if user-driven (click)} \\ \text{Programmatic} & \text{if logic-driven (post-auth, redirect)} \end{cases}$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 🧪 Worked Example 1 — URL-to-Component Resolution

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Typical):** Given the route definition `/users/:userId/posts/:postId` and a user visiting `/users/7/posts/42`, extract the route parameters and identify the rendered component.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify the route pattern.**

$$\text{Pattern} = \texttt{/users/:userId/posts/:postId}$$

**Step 2 — Tokenize the pattern by `/`.**

$$\text{Pattern Tokens} = [\texttt{users}, \texttt{:userId}, \texttt{posts}, \texttt{:postId}]$$

**Step 3 — Tokenize the visited URL by `/`.**

$$\text{URL Tokens} = [\texttt{users}, \texttt{7}, \texttt{posts}, \texttt{42}]$$

**Step 4 — Apply positional matching.** Each token at position $i$ in the URL is matched against the token at position $i$ in the pattern.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\texttt{users} \equiv \texttt{users} \quad \checkmark \\
&\texttt{:userId} \leftarrow \texttt{7} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{params.userId} = \texttt{"7"} \\
&\texttt{posts} \equiv \texttt{posts} \quad \checkmark \\
&\texttt{:postId} \leftarrow \texttt{42} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{params.postId} = \texttt{"42"}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final result object.**

$$\text{params} = \{ \text{userId}: \texttt{"7"},\ \text{postId}: \texttt{"42"} \}$$

**Step 6 — Component resolution.** The route's `element` property points to `<UserPostDetail />`, which receives `params` as a prop (or via the `useParams()` hook).

---

## 🧪 Worked Example 2 — Nested Route Tree Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Construct the route tree for an e-commerce app with `Home`, `Products` (parent), `ProductDetail` (child of Products), and `Cart` (standalone). Determine the final URL paths.

### Step-by-Step Construction:

**Step 1 — Define the parent route.**

$$\text{parent} = \{ \text{path}: \texttt{/products},\ \text{element}: \langle \text{ProductsLayout} \rangle \}$$

**Step 2 — Add a child route using a *relative* path (no leading `/`).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{child} &= \{ \text{path}: \texttt{:productId},\ \text{element}: \langle \text{ProductDetail} \rangle \} \\
\Rightarrow \text{Full URL} &= \texttt{/products} + \texttt{/} + \texttt{:productId} = \texttt{/products/:productId}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Add standalone routes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{home} &= \{ \text{path}: \texttt{/},\ \text{element}: \langle \text{Home} \rangle \} \\
\text{cart} &= \{ \text{path}: \texttt{/cart},\ \text{element}: \langle \text{Cart} \rangle \}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine into the final routes array.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Routes} = [ &\text{home},\ \text{parent}, \text{cart} ] \\
\text{where parent.children} = [ &\text{child} ]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Render logic.** When a user visits `/products/42`:
- `ProductsLayout` renders.
- Inside its `<Outlet />` placeholder, `ProductDetail` mounts with `params = { productId: "42" }`.
- Visiting `/products` alone renders `ProductsLayout` with an empty `<Outlet />` (or an index route if defined).

---

## 🧪 Worked Example 3 — Route Guard Decision Tree

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Implement a `requireAuth` guard that redirects unauthenticated users to `/login` when they try to access `/dashboard`.

### Step-by-Step Logic:

**Step 1 — Define the guard function.**

$$\text{guard}(\text{ctx}) = \begin{cases} \text{true} & \text{if } \text{ctx.isAuthenticated} = \text{true} \\ \text{redirect to } \texttt{/login} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Attach the guard to the protected route.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{dashboardRoute} = \{ &\text{path}: \texttt{/dashboard}, \\
&\text{element}: \langle \text{Dashboard} \rangle, \\
&\text{beforeEnter}: \text{requireAuth} \;\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Execution trace for an unauthenticated user.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{User visits } &\texttt{/dashboard} \\
\Rightarrow \text{Router invokes } &\text{requireAuth(ctx)} \\
\Rightarrow \text{guard returns } &\text{redirect(/login)} \\
\Rightarrow \text{Router replaces } &\text{URL with /login} \\
\Rightarrow \text{Component rendered: } &\langle \text{Login} \rangle
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Execution trace for an authenticated user.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{User visits } &\texttt{/dashboard} \\
\Rightarrow \text{guard returns } &\text{true} \\
\Rightarrow \text{Router proceeds to } &\text{render } \langle \text{Dashboard} \rangle
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 📊 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Scenarios

| Scenario | Routing Strategy | Key Function/Hook |
|----------|------------------|-------------------|
| User profile page | Dynamic param `:id` | `useParams()` |
| Search results page | Query string `?q=` | `useSearchParams()` |
| Post-login redirect | Programmatic | `navigate('/dashboard')` |
| Protected admin area | Guard | `beforeEnter` / `loader` |
| Multi-step form | Nested routes | `<Outlet />` with shared parent state |
| 404 / not found | Wildcard `*` | Catch-all route |
| Scroll position on back nav | Scroll restoration API | Browser History API |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 📐 Diagram 1 — Routing Architecture in an SPA

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    user1["User Clicks Link or Types URL"]
    histapi["Browser History API pushState popstate"]
    router1["Router Intercepts URL Change"]
    matcher{"URL Matches Which Route Pattern"}
    r1["Route: / Static Home Component"]
    r2["Route: /about Static About Component"]
    r3["Route: /users:userId Dynamic UserProfile Component"]
    r4["Route: * Catch-All NotFound Component"]
    guard1{"Route Guard Check Auth or Role"}
    render1["Render New Component in Outlet"]
    unmount1["Unmount Previous Component"]
    scrollapi["Scroll Restoration Optional"]
    user1 --> histapi
    histapi --> router1
    router1 --> matcher
    matcher --> r1
    matcher --> r2
    matcher --> r3
    matcher --> r4
    r1 --> guard1
    r2 --> guard1
    r3 --> guard1
    r4 --> render1
    guard1 -->|Pass| render1
    guard1 -->|Fail| redir["Redirect to Login"]
    redir --> render1
    render1 --> unmount1
    unmount1 --> scrollapi
```

```mermaid
    r_productdetail["path: :productId  element: ProductDetail"]
    r_cart["path: /cart  element: Cart"]

    root --> r_home
    root --> r_products
    root --> r_cart
    root --> r_404
    r_products --> r_productlist
    r_products --> r_productdetail
```

## 📐 Diagram 3 — Navigation Flow (Declarative vs Programmatic)

```mermaid
    participant L as Link Component
    participant P as Page Logic Code
    participant R as Router
    participant V as View Outlet
    U->>L: Click Home Button
    L->>R: Declarative navigate to home
    R->>R: Match path slash
    R->>V: Render Home Component
    Note over U,V: Declarative path done
    U->>P: Submit Login Form
    P->>R: Programmatic navigate to dashboard
    R->>R: Match path dashboard
    R->>V: Render Dashboard Component
```

## 📐 Diagram 4 — URL Parsing Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    url["Input URL /users/7/posts/42"]
    split["Split by forward slash"]
    tokens["Tokens users 7 posts 42"]
    pattern["Pattern /users/:userId/posts/:postId"]
    ptokens["Pattern Tokens users userId posts postId"]
    match["Positional Token Comparison"]
    param1["params userId equals 7"]
    param2["params postId equals 42"]
    result["Final params object ready for component"]
    url --> split --> tokens
    pattern --> ptokens
    tokens --> match
    ptokens --> match
    match --> param1 --> result
    match --> param2 --> result
```
