# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## 📘 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Differentiate between client-side routing and server-side routing. Which is used in Single Page Applications and why?*

**Model Answer:**

**Client-Side Routing:**
- Navigation handled entirely by JavaScript inside the browser.
- Uses the **History API** (`pushState`, `replaceState`) or hash (`#`) to update the URL.
- **No full page reload** — only the relevant component is swapped in the DOM.
- Initial page load is heavier, but subsequent navigations are near-instant.

**Server-Side Routing:**
- Each URL triggers a **new HTTP request** to the server.
- The server returns a **complete new HTML page**.
- The browser unloads the old page and loads the new one — full reload.

**Used in SPAs:** **Client-side routing** is used because SPAs load a single HTML shell once, and routing allows seamless view transitions without server round-trips, mimicking desktop-app-like responsiveness.

*(Word count guideline: ~120 words for 3 marks — concise and tabular answers are valued.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is a route parameter? Explain with an example how dynamic segments are captured in modern web frameworks.*

**Model Answer:**

A **route parameter** is a **dynamic placeholder** in a route's URL pattern that captures variable values from the actual URL and exposes them to the corresponding component.

**Syntax:** Parameters are prefixed with a colon (e.g., `:id`).

**Example:**

- **Route definition:** `/products/:productId`
- **Visited URL:** `/products/42`
- **Captured parameter:** `params.productId = "42"`

The component can then access this value using framework-specific hooks like `useParams()` (React Router) or route props in Vue/Angular. This enables **dynamic, data-driven pages** such as product details, user profiles, and blog posts without creating a separate route for every possible value.

---

## 📕 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of routing in Single Page Applications with a suitable diagram. Discuss the role of the **History API** in enabling client-side routing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a route table for a **Student Management Portal** with the following pages: *Home, Student List, Student Detail (with parameter), Add Student, Edit Student (with parameter), and a 404 page*. Show the route tree structure and explain how nested routes can be used for a *Dashboard Layout*. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Routing in SPAs:**

In a **Single Page Application (SPA)**, the entire application is loaded as a single HTML document. Routing in this context refers to the mechanism that maps URL changes to corresponding **views/components** within the same page, without contacting the server for a fresh HTML response.

**Working Flow:**

1. The user clicks a link or types a URL.
2. The router (e.g., React Router, Vue Router) intercepts the URL change.
3. It matches the URL against registered route patterns.
4. The corresponding component is rendered in a designated outlet area.
5. The previous component is unmounted, and the new one is mounted.

**Role of the History API:**

The **HTML5 History API** provides methods that allow JavaScript to manipulate the browser's session history without triggering a full page reload:

- `history.pushState(state, title, url)` — pushes a new entry onto the history stack.
- `history.replaceState(state, title, url)` — replaces the current entry.
- `popstate` event — fires when the user navigates using back/forward buttons.

**Why it matters:** Without the History API, the only way to change the URL was to use hash fragments (`#`), which produce ugly URLs. The History API allows clean, SEO-friendly URLs like `/dashboard/settings` while still avoiding server round-trips.

**Diagram:**

```
[User Click] → [Link Intercept] → [Router Match] → [Component Render]
       ↓                ↓                  ↓                ↓
   URL Change     pushState()      Pattern Match     DOM Update
       ↓
   History Stack Updated
```

*(Note: The diagram above is a textual fallback. Refer to Section 4 for the Mermaid version.)*

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Route Table for Student Management Portal:**

| # | Path | Element (Component) | Type | Parameters |
|---|------|---------------------|------|-----------|
| 1 | `/` | `<Home />` | Static | None |
| 2 | `/students` | `<DashboardLayout />` | Parent (Layout) | None |
| 2a | `/students` (index) | `<StudentList />` | Index child | None |
| 2b | `/students/:id` | `<StudentDetail />` | Dynamic child | `:id` |
| 2c | `/students/add` | `<AddStudent />` | Static child | None |
| 2d | `/students/edit/:id` | `<EditStudent />` | Dynamic child | `:id` |
| 3 | `*` | `<NotFound />` | Wildcard catch-all | None |

**Route Tree Structure (Textual):**

```
Root
├── /              → Home
├── /students      → DashboardLayout
│   ├── (index)    → StudentList
│   ├── /:id       → StudentDetail        [params: { id }]
│   ├── /add       → AddStudent
│   └── /edit/:id  → EditStudent          [params: { id }]
└── *              → NotFound
```

**Nested Routes with DashboardLayout:**

- The `DashboardLayout` component is rendered for **all** child routes under `/students`.
- It contains a common **sidebar**, **navbar**, and a `<Outlet />` placeholder.
- The `<Outlet />` is where the matched child component (`StudentList`, `StudentDetail`, etc.) is dynamically inserted.

**Example Behavior:**

- Visiting `/students` → `DashboardLayout` + `StudentList` inside `<Outlet />`.
- Visiting `/students/42` → `DashboardLayout` + `StudentDetail` (with `id = "42"`) inside `<Outlet />`.
- Visiting `/students/edit/42` → `DashboardLayout` + `EditStudent` (with `id = "42"`) inside `<Outlet />`.

This design **avoids code duplication** of the layout across every student page and enables **persistent UI elements** (like a sidebar) that do not unmount on navigation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** What are **route guards**? Explain with a real-world example how an authentication guard is implemented to protect the `/admin` route in a web application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **declarative** and **programmatic** navigation with examples. When would a developer prefer one over the other? **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Route Guards — Definition:**

A **route guard** is a function (or hook) that runs **before** a route is activated, allowing the application to **allow, block, or redirect** navigation based on custom logic such as authentication status, user roles, or data preconditions.

**Types of Route Guards:**

1. **Authentication Guard** — Blocks unauthenticated users (e.g., redirect to `/login`).
2. **Authorization/Role Guard** — Blocks users lacking required permissions (e.g., admin-only routes).
3. **Unsaved Changes Guard** — Warns users before navigating away from a form with unsaved data.
4. **Data Preload Guard** — Fetches required data before the route renders; cancels navigation if fetch fails.

**Real-World Example: Protecting `/admin`:**

```javascript
// Authentication guard function
function requireAuth(context) {
  const isAuthenticated = localStorage.getItem('authToken') !== null;
  if (!isAuthenticated) {
    return { redirect: '/login' };  // Block and redirect
  }
  return true;  // Allow navigation
}

// Route definition
const adminRoute = {
  path: '/admin',
  element: <AdminPanel />,
  beforeEnter: requireAuth  // Guard attached
};
```

**Execution Flow:**

1. User attempts to navigate to `/admin` (via link click, URL bar, or programmatic).
2. Router invokes `requireAuth(context)` **before** rendering `<AdminPanel />`.
3. Guard checks `localStorage` for the auth token.
4. If **token exists** → `return true` → `<AdminPanel />` is rendered.
5. If **token is absent** → `return { redirect: '/login' }` → Router redirects to login page.

**Why this matters:** Route guards centralize access control logic, ensuring security rules are consistently enforced across all protected routes without scattering checks in every component.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Declarative Navigation:**

Navigation is expressed **declaratively** in JSX/template syntax using components like `<Link>`.

**Example:**

```jsx
<nav>
  <Link to="/">Home</Link>
  <Link to="/about">About</Link>
  <Link to={`/users/${userId}`}>My Profile</Link>
</nav>
```

**Characteristics:**
- Used for **user-driven** navigation (clicks, taps).
- Router handles the click event and prevents default browser behavior.
- Highly **readable** and **discoverable** in the markup.

**Programmatic Navigation:**

Navigation is triggered **imperatively** by calling a function (e.g., `navigate()` or `router.push()`) inside event handlers or business logic.

**Example:**

```javascript
function handleLoginSuccess() {
  // After successful login, redirect to dashboard
  navigate('/dashboard');
}

function handleProductAdded() {
  // After adding a product, go back to product list
  navigate('/products');
}
```

**Characteristics:**
- Used for **logic-driven** navigation (form submissions, auth callbacks, timed redirects).
- Provides **conditional** control flow.
- Cannot be triggered by a user directly clicking — it requires JavaScript execution.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Declarative | Programmatic |
|--------|------------|--------------|
| **Trigger** | User click | JavaScript logic |
| **Syntax** | `<Link to="/x" />` | `navigate('/x')` |
| **Use Case** | Menus, buttons, navbars | Post-form-submit, redirects, auth flow |
| **Readability** | High (visible in JSX) | Lower (logic embedded in code) |
| **SEO** | Crawlable (anchor-like) | Not directly crawlable |

**When to prefer which:**
- Use **declarative** when the navigation target is **static and user-initiated** (e.g., a navbar link).
- Use **programmatic** when the navigation depends on **runtime conditions** (e.g., login success, form validation, API response).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing hash routing with History API routing** — Always clarify the mechanism (`pushState` vs `window.location.hash`).
> 2. **Forgetting to mention server-side fallback** — When asked about History API, KTU expects the answer: *"The server must be configured to serve `index.html` for all unknown paths, otherwise a hard refresh returns 404."*
> 3. **Omitting the `<Outlet />` in nested route explanations** — A common omission; examiners specifically look for it.
> 4. **Not distinguishing between `useParams()` and `useSearchParams()`** — Path params vs query params is a 3-mark favorite.
> 5. **Writing pseudocode without explaining the guard's return value semantics** — Always state that returning `true` allows navigation, returning `redirect(...)` blocks it.
> 6. **Missing the 404/wildcard route** — In route table design questions, the catch-all `*` route is mandatory; omitting it costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the exam!**

- ✅ **Routing** = URL-to-component mapping in web applications.
- ✅ **SPA** uses **client-side routing**; **MPA** uses **server-side routing**.
- ✅ **History API** (`pushState`, `popstate`) enables clean URLs without reload.
- ✅ **Hash routing** uses `#` and requires no server config, but produces ugly URLs.
- ✅ **Dynamic segments** use `:` prefix (e.g., `:id`) and are accessed via `useParams()`.
- ✅ **Query strings** are read via `useSearchParams()` — separate from path params.
- ✅ **Wildcard route** `*` is the catch-all for 404 / NotFound pages.
- ✅ **Nested routes** share a parent layout; the parent's `<Outlet />` renders the active child.
- ✅ **Route guards** (`beforeEnter`, `loader`) run before navigation and can **allow, block, or redirect**.
- ✅ **Declarative navigation** = `<Link to="/x" />`; **Programmatic** = `navigate('/x')`.
- ✅ **Server must serve `index.html`** for all paths when using History API (prevent hard-refresh 404s).
- ✅ **File-based routing** (Next.js) auto-generates routes from folder structure.
- ✅ **Memory routing** is used in React Native and test environments.
- ✅ Route order matters — **more specific routes must come before wildcards**.
- ✅ Always handle the **404 case** with a catch-all route in production apps.

---

**📊 Module 3 — Creating Page Routes: Coverage Map**

| KTU 2024 Sub-Outcome | Covered Above |
|----------------------|---------------|
| Define routing and its types | ✅ Section 1, 2 |
| Explain SPA vs MPA routing | ✅ Section 1, 2 |
| Describe History API mechanism | ✅ Section 2, 3 |
| Implement dynamic route parameters | ✅ Section 3 (Worked Example 1) |
| Design nested route trees | ✅ Section 3 (Worked Example 2), Question A(b) |
| Apply route guards | ✅ Section 3 (Worked Example 3), Question B(a) |
| Differentiate declarative vs programmatic nav | ✅ Question B(b) |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Templating Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Templating Engines # 📘 MODULE 3 — TEMPLATING ENGINES ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 🔷 Formal Academic Definition A **Templating Engine** (also called a *template processor* or *view engine*) is a software component designed to combine **static template files** (written in HTML, plain text, or other markup languages) containing **special syntax placeholders** with a **dynamic data model** (typically a JavaScript object) to produce a final rendered document — most commonly an HTML page served to a client browser. In the context of **Node.js / Express.js** (which is the KTU 2024 syllabus focus), a templating engine allows developers to embed **server-side logic** such as variables, conditionals, loops, and partials directly into HTML files before they are transmitted to the client. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A templating engine is essentially the *View Layer* in the **MVC (Model-View-Controller)** architecture used by server-side web frameworks like Express.js. It is the bridge between raw data (Model) and the user interface (View). --- ### 🔷 Popular Templating Engines in the KTU / Node.js Ecosystem | Engine | Syntax Style | File Extension | Key Characteristic | |---|---|---|---| | **EJS** | `<% %>` tags (Embedded JS) | `.ejs` | Pure JavaScript inside HTML | | **Pug** | Indentation-based (no tags) | `.pug` | Minimalist, whitespace-sensitive | | **Handlebars** | `{{ }}` Mustache-style | `.hbs` | Logic-less, safe templating | | **Mustache** | `{{ }}` | `.mustache` | Logic-less, language-agnostic | | **Nunjucks** | `{% %}` Jinja-style | `.njk` | Powerful, used by Mozilla | | **Haml** | Indentation-based | `.haml` | Ruby-inspired | --- ### 🔷 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mail Merge" Model > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: Printing Invitation Cards** > > Imagine you are a wedding planner sending 500 invitation cards. > > 1. You create **one master template** with placeholders like `{{Guest Name}}`, `{{Venue}}`, `{{Date}}`. > 2. You prepare a **spreadsheet of data** (one row per guest). > 3. A "mail merge" tool automatically substitutes the placeholders with real data and prints 500 unique cards. > > **A templating engine does exactly this for the web**: > - **Template** = Master HTML file with placeholders > - **Data** = JavaScript object (`res.locals`, database result, API response) > - **Output** = A fully-rendered, personalized HTML page for every request ### 🔷 Why We Need Templating Engines 1. **Separation of Concerns** — Designers work on `.ejs` files, programmers on `app.js`. 2. **Dynamic HTML Generation** — Pages change based on user data without writing repetitive HTML. 3. **Reusability** — Components like headers and footers are written once and **included** everywhere. 4. **Security** — Most engines **escape** HTML by default, preventing XSS attacks. 5. **Maintainability** — Changes to layout happen in **one file**, not 500. --- ### 🔷 Physical / Standard Metrics - **Default rendering speed:** ~10,000–50,000 templates/sec (varies by engine; EJS ≈ 30k/sec, Handlebars ≈ 40k/sec). - **Default cache TTL:** 24 hours (in production mode). - **Default escape function:** Converts `<`, `>`, `&`, `"`, `'` to HTML entities. # 📘 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 🔶 The Templating Engine Pipeline (Architecture) Every templating engine follows a **3-stage processing pipeline**: ``` ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ │ TEMPLATE │ → │ LEXER │ → │ PARSER │ → │ RENDERER │ │ (.ejs file) │ │ (Tokenizer) │ │ (AST Tree) │ │ (Final HTML)│ └──────────────┘ └──────────────┘ └──────────────┘ └──────────────┘ ↑ ┌──────────────┐ │ DATA OBJECT │ │ (JSON) │ └──────────────┘ ``` > **Stage 1 — Lexer (Tokenization):** Scans the raw template text and breaks it into meaningful **tokens** (e.g., `HTML_TEXT`, `VARIABLE_START`, `EXPRESSION`, `TAG_END`). > > **Stage 2 — Parser (AST Generation):** Converts the token stream into an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** — a hierarchical tree structure representing the template's logical structure. > > **Stage 3 — Renderer (Execution):** Walks the AST, evaluates dynamic expressions against the provided **data object**, escapes output where needed, and concatenates the result into a final HTML string. --- ## 🔶 Core Template Syntax Features (Common Across Engines) ### 1. Variable Interpolation | Engine | Syntax | Output Behavior | |---|---|---| | EJS | `<%= user.name %>` | Escaped (safe) | | EJS (raw) | `<%- user.bio %>` | Unescaped (HTML allowed) | | Pug | `h1= user.name` | Escaped | | Pug (raw) | `h1!= user.bio` | Unescaped | | Handlebars | `{{user.name}}` | Escaped (always) | | Nunjucks | `{{ user.name }}` | Escaped | > [!IMPORTANT] > **`=` means escaped, `-` or `!=` means unescaped.** This is a common KTU exam trick question. ### 2. Conditionals ```ejs <% if (user.isAdmin) { %> Admin Panel <% } else { %> Login <% } %> ``` ### 3. Loops / Iteration ```ejs
    <% products.forEach(function(p) { %>
  • <%= p.name %> - ₹<%= p.price %>
    • <% }); %>
``` ### 4. Partials (Reusable Components) ```ejs <%- include('partials/header') %> {{> partials/header }} ``` ### 5. Layouts (Master Page Template) A layout wraps an entire page (e.g., `layout.ejs` contains `<%- body %>` placeholder, and the view file's content is injected there). --- ## 🔶 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet | Concept | Syntax Pattern | KTU Exam Significance | |---|---|---| | **Escaped Output** | `<%= var %>` (EJS) | Prevents **XSS attacks**; converts `<` to `<` | | **Unescaped Output** | `<%- var %>` (EJS) | Renders raw HTML; **dangerous with user input** | | **JS Execution** | `<% code %>` (EJS) | Runs JS but does **NOT** output result | | **Setting View Engine** | `app.set('view engine', 'ejs')` | Tells Express which engine to use | | **Rendering Route** | `res.render('home', {data})` | Combines `home.ejs` with `{data}` object | | **Layouts Location** | `./views/layout.ejs` | Convention for Express + express-ejs-layouts | | **Views Directory** | `./views/` | Default directory Express looks for templates | | **Default Cache (prod)** | `true` | Templates cached in memory; restart needed for changes | | **Default Cache (dev)** | `false` | Templates re-read from disk on every request | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Frequently Asked:** *"What is the difference between `<%= %>` and `<%- %>` in EJS?"* — The first escapes HTML, the second does not. --- ## 🔶 Engineering Real-World Utility 1. **Server-Side Rendering (SSR):** Used by Netflix, LinkedIn, and PayPal for fast first-paint performance. 2. **Email Generation:** Templating engines generate dynamic transactional emails (welcome mail, OTPs). 3. **Static Site Generation (SSG):** Tools like Jekyll, Hugo use templating engines to build blogs. 4. **PDF Report Generation:** Combining templating with libraries like Puppeteer creates dynamic invoices. 5. **Configuration Files:** Tools like Helm (Kubernetes) use Go templates for YAML generation. # 📘 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 🔶 Worked Example 1: Complete EJS Template Engine Walkthrough **Problem:** A Node.js Express app must render a product list page using EJS. Demonstrate the full pipeline — from `res.render()` invocation to final HTML output. --- ### **Step 1: Setup the Express Application** ```javascript const express = require('express'); const app = express(); // Set the view engine to EJS app.set('view engine', 'ejs'); // Specify the views directory (default is './views') app.set('views', './views'); // Define a route that renders a template with data app.get('/products', (req, res) => { const products = [ { id: 1, name: 'Laptop', price: 75000 }, { id: 2, name: 'Mouse', price: 1500 }, { id: 3, name: 'Keyboard', price: 3500 } ]; res.render('productList', { products: products, title: 'Our Store' }); }); app.listen(3000); ``` --- ### **Step 2: Create the Template — `views/productList.ejs`** ```html <%= title %>

<%= title %>

    <% products.forEach(function(p) { %>
  • <%= p.name %> — ₹<%= p.price %>
    • <% }); %>

Total products: <%= products.length %>

``` --- ### **Step 3: Trace the Engine's Internal Processing** When `res.render('productList', {products, title})` is called, EJS executes the following steps: **Step 3.1 — File Resolution** The engine concatenates `views` directory + template name + extension: $$\text{filepath} = \texttt{views/} + \texttt{productList} + \texttt{.ejs} = \texttt{views/productList.ejs}$$ **Step 3.2 — Lexical Analysis (Tokenization)** The lexer scans the file character-by-character and emits tokens: | Position | Token Type | Value | |---|---|---| | `` | `HTML_TEXT` | `...` | | `<%= title %>` | `VARIABLE_OPEN` + `IDENTIFIER` + `VARIABLE_CLOSE` | `title` | | `<% products.forEach(...) %>` | `SCRIPTLET_OPEN` + `JS_CODE` + `SCRIPTLET_CLOSE` | `products.forEach(...)` | | `<%= p.name %>` | `VARIABLE_OPEN` + `MEMBER_ACCESS` + `VARIABLE_CLOSE` | `p.name` | **Step 3.3 — Parsing (AST Construction)** The parser builds an **Abstract Syntax Tree**: ```mermaid graph TD A["Root: RenderFunction"] --> B["HTMLText: doctype, head, h1 open"] A --> C["Expression: title (escaped)"] A --> D["HTMLText: ul open"] A --> E["ForEach Loop: products"] E --> F["HTMLText: li open"] E --> G["Expression: p.id (escaped)"] E --> H["HTMLText: strong open"] E --> I["Expression: p.name (escaped)"] E --> J["HTMLText: dash + price"] E --> K["HTMLText: li close"] A --> L["Expression: products.length"] A --> M["HTMLText: closing tags"] ``` **Step 3.4 — Compilation to JavaScript Function** EJS converts the AST into a JavaScript function string: ```javascript var __output = ""; function __append(s) { if (s !== undefined && s !== null) __output += s; } with (locals || {}) { __append(""); __append(escapeFn(title)); __append("

"); __append(escapeFn(title)); __append("

    "); products.forEach(function(p) { __append("
  • "); __append(escapeFn(p.name)); __append(" — ₹"); __append(escapeFn(p.price)); __append("
    • "); }); __append("

Total products: "); __append(escapeFn(products.length)); __append("

"); } return __output; ``` **Step 3.5 — Execution and Output Generation** The compiled function runs with `locals = { products, title }`. The `escapeFn` converts special characters: $$\text{escape}(x) = \text{String}(x).replace(/[&<>"']/g, \, m \mapsto \, \text{entityMap}[m])$$ For example, if `p.name = "Gaming Mouse"`, then: $$\text{escape}("Gaming Mouse") = "Gaming <Pro> Mouse"$$ --- ### **Step 4: Final Rendered HTML** ```html Our Store

Our Store

  • Laptop — ₹75000
  • Mouse — ₹1500
  • Keyboard — ₹3500

Total products: 3

``` --- ## 🔶 Analytical Comparison Matrix: EJS vs Pug vs Handlebars | Feature | EJS | Pug | Handlebars | |---|---|---|---| | **Syntax Style** | HTML + JS | Indentation (no `<>`) | HTML + `{{}}` | | **Learning Curve** | Low (HTML devs) | Medium (new syntax) | Low | | **JavaScript Inside** | ✅ Full JS | ❌ Limited | ❌ Helpers only | | **Logic-less Philosophy** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | | **Whitespace Sensitive** | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | | **Built-in Layouts** | ❌ (needs plugin) | ✅ (`extends`) | ❌ (needs helper) | | **Performance (req/sec)** | ~30,000 | ~25,000 | ~40,000 | | **XSS Protection** | Default (escaped) | Default (escaped) | Default (escaped) | | **Used By** | Strapi, Ghost | Codepen docs, Vue templates | Ember.js, Assemble | # 📘 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 🔶 Diagram 1: Templating Engine Architecture in Express.js ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Client Browser
HTTP Request] --> B[Express Router] B --> C{Controller} C --> D[Database / API] D --> E[Data Object JSON] C --> F[res.render view name data] F --> G[View Engine: EJS] G --> H[views folder home.ejs] H --> I[Lexer + Parser] E --> I I --> J[Compiled Function] J --> K[Final HTML String] K --> L[HTTP Response] L --> A style A fill:#e1f5ff style B fill:#fff4e1 style C fill:#ffe1f5 style G fill:#e1ffe1 style H fill:#f5e1ff style K fill:#ffe1e1 style L fill:#e1f5ff ``` --- ## 🔶 Diagram 2: Template Inheritance Flow (Layouts & Partials) ```mermaid flowchart TB L[layout.ejs
Master Page] --> L1[Header Partial] L --> L2[body placeholder] L --> L3[Footer Partial] L1 --> L1a[navbar.ejs] L1 --> L1b[searchbar.ejs] V[home.ejs
View Page] --> V1[Page Content] V --> V2[Loops over products array] V --> V3[Conditionals for user role] V -->|extends / includes| L V1 --> V2 V1 --> V3 style L fill:#fff4cc style L1 fill:#cce5ff style L2 fill:#ccffcc style L3 fill:#cce5ff style V fill:#ffcccc style V1 fill:#ffccff style V2 fill:#ffccff style V3 fill:#ffccff ``` --- ## 🔶 Diagram 3: Server-Side vs Client-Side Templating ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SS["SERVER-SIDE TEMPLATING (EJS, Pug, Handlebars)"] S1[Server: Node.js + Express] --> S2[Template + Data on server] S2 --> S3[Rendered HTML sent to browser] S3 --> S4[Browser displays final HTML] end subgraph CS["CLIENT-SIDE TEMPLATING (React, Vue, Angular)"] C1[Server: Node.js API] --> C2[Sends JSON data only] C2 --> C3[Browser receives JSON + empty HTML shell] C3 --> C4[JavaScript renders template in browser] end style S1 fill:#cce5ff style S2 fill:#cce5ff style S3 fill:#cce5ff style S4 fill:#cce5ff style C1 fill:#ffe1cc style C2 fill:#ffe1cc style C3 fill:#ffe1cc style C4 fill:#ffe1cc ``` --- ## 🔶 Diagram 4: Complete Request-Response Lifecycle with Templating ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User Browser participant E as Express Server participant V as View Engine participant FS as File System participant DB as Database U->>E: GET /products E->>DB: Fetch products DB-->>E: Return product array E->>V: res.render productList.ejs, data V->>FS: Read productList.ejs from views folder FS-->>V: Template file content V->>V: Lex + Parse + Compile to JS function V->>V: Execute function with data object V->>V: Apply escapeFn to dynamic values V-->>E: Return final HTML string E->>U: HTTP 200 OK with HTML content U->>U: Browser parses and displays page ``` # 📘 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]` **Define a templating engine. List any two popular templating engines used with Node.js.** **Model Answer:** A **templating engine** is a software tool that combines a static template file (containing placeholders) with a dynamic data model to generate the final output document — typically an HTML page rendered on the server before being sent to the client. It acts as the **View layer** in the MVC architecture, enabling separation of presentation logic from business logic. **Two popular templating engines for Node.js:** 1. **EJS (Embedded JavaScript)** — Uses `<% %>` tags to embed raw JavaScript inside HTML. 2. **Pug (formerly Jade)** — Uses indentation-based syntax, eliminating the need for closing tags. > *Alternative valid answers: Handlebars, Mustache, Nunjucks, Haml.* --- ### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Explain the difference between `<%= variable %>` and `<%- variable %>` in EJS with a suitable example.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | `<%= variable %>` | `<%- variable %>` | |---|---|---| | **Type** | Escaped output | Unescaped (raw) output | | **XSS Protection** | ✅ Yes (HTML-safe) | ❌ No (renders raw HTML) | | **Use Case** | User-supplied text | Trusted HTML strings | | **Internally Calls** | `escapeFn()` | Direct concatenation | **Example:** ```ejs <% let userInput = ""; %>

<%= userInput %>

<%- userInput %>

``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Using `<%- %>` with user input is the **#1 cause of XSS attacks** in KTU lab evaluations. Always prefer `<%= %>` unless you absolutely trust the data source. --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > ⚠️ **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts — typically (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Internal choice means you must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]` #### **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a templating engine in an Express.js application. Describe the role of `app.set('view engine', ...)` and `res.render()`. **Model Answer:** **Architecture Diagram:** ``` Client → Express Router → Controller → Database → Data Object ↓ res.render('view', data) ↓ View Engine (EJS/Pug) ↓ Reads: views/view.ejs ↓ Lexer → Parser → Compiler ↓ Executes function with data ↓ Escapes output ↓ Final HTML string → Client ``` **Role of Key Functions:** **1. `app.set('view engine', 'ejs')`** This statement **configures the default view engine** for the Express application. After this call, Express knows to use EJS when `res.render()` is called without explicitly specifying the engine. ```javascript const express = require('express'); const app = express(); app.set('view engine', 'ejs'); // Sets EJS as the view engine app.set('views', './templates'); // (Optional) Change default views folder ``` **2. `res.render(view, locals, callback)`** This function **renders a template file** by: - **Step 1:** Locating the file at `views/.`. - **Step 2:** Combining it with the `locals` object (data passed as second argument). - **Step 3:** Sending the rendered HTML as the HTTP response body. ```javascript app.get('/profile/:username', (req, res) => { const user = { name: req.params.username, age: 25 }; res.render('profile', { user: user }); // Renders views/profile.ejs }); ``` If a `callback` is provided, the HTML is passed to the callback instead of being sent automatically. This allows manual post-processing (e.g., HTML minification). --- #### **(b) [7 Marks]** Write an EJS template that displays a list of students from an array. Each student has `name`, `rollNo`, and `marks`. Highlight students who scored above 75 in green and below 50 in red. Show the corresponding Express route. **Model Answer:** **Express Route — `app.js`:** ```javascript const express = require('express'); const app = express(); app.set('view engine', 'ejs'); app.get('/students', (req, res) => { const students = [ { name: 'Arun', rollNo: 1, marks: 82 }, { name: 'Meera', rollNo: 2, marks: 45 }, { name: 'Rahul', rollNo: 3, marks: 91 }, { name: 'Sneha', rollNo: 4, marks: 38 } ]; res.render('studentList', { students: students }); }); app.listen(3000); ``` **EJS Template — `views/studentList.ejs`:** ```html Student Marks Report

Student Marks Report

<% students.forEach(function(s) { %> <% }); %>
Roll No Name Marks Grade
<%= s.rollNo %> <%= s.name %> <%= s.marks %> <% if (s.marks >= 90) { %>A+ <% } else if (s.marks >= 75) { %>A <% } else if (s.marks >= 50) { %>B <% } else { %>F <% } %>

Total students: <%= students.length %>

``` **Explanation of Key Logic:** - The **ternary operator** `<%= s.marks > 75 ? 'high' : (s.marks < 50 ? 'low' : '') %>` dynamically assigns a CSS class to each ``. - The **nested `if-else` block** assigns a letter grade. - `<% %>` is used for **control flow** (no output), while `<%= %>` outputs the value after escaping. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` #### **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare EJS, Pug, and Handlebars templating engines based on: (i) Syntax style, (ii) JavaScript support, (iii) Layout/Partial mechanism, and (iv) Learning curve. **Model Answer:** | Comparison Criterion | EJS | Pug | Handlebars | |---|---|---|---| | **(i) Syntax Style** | Standard HTML with `<% %>` tags | Indentation-based, no closing tags | Standard HTML with `{{ }}` Mustache tags | | **(ii) JavaScript Support** | ✅ Full JavaScript inside `<% %>` | ⚠️ Limited (no `for` loops, only `each`) | ❌ Logic-less; uses helpers | | **(iii) Layout/Partial Mechanism** | Uses `express-ejs-layouts` plugin | Built-in `extends layout` and `include` | Uses `{{> partial}}` syntax + helpers | | **(iv) Learning Curve** | Low — HTML devs can start immediately | Medium — new indentation rules | Low — clean Mustache syntax | **Detailed Explanation:** **EJS:** Best for developers who want **pure JavaScript power** inside HTML. No new language to learn. **Pug:** Forces **clean, minimal markup** but requires learning its indentation rules. A typo in indentation breaks the entire page. Great for small projects where readability matters. **Handlebars:** Enforces a **logic-less philosophy** — you cannot write `if/else` directly. All logic must go into **helpers** (JavaScript functions registered with the engine). This makes templates safer and more declarative, but verbose for complex UIs. **Verdict (KTU recommendation):** EJS is the **most commonly used** in KTU lab examinations because of its simplicity and direct JavaScript integration. --- #### **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of *partials* and *layouts* in templating engines. Write an EJS code snippet demonstrating a master `layout.ejs` and a `home.ejs` view that uses it. **Model Answer:** **Concept Explanation:** - **Partial:** A small, reusable template fragment (e.g., a header, footer, or navbar) that is **included** into other templates. Partials prevent code duplication. - **Layout (Master Page):** A wrapper template that defines the **common structure** of multiple pages (e.g., ``, ``, navigation bar, footer). The unique content of each page is injected into a designated placeholder. > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** A layout is the **picture frame**, and a view is the **picture inside it**. The frame stays the same, but the picture changes. **Implementation (using `express-ejs-layouts`):** **Step 1: Install the package** ```bash npm install express-ejs-layouts ``` **Step 2: Configure Express — `app.js`** ```javascript const express = require('express'); const expressLayouts = require('express-ejs-layouts'); const app = express(); app.set('view engine', 'ejs'); app.use(expressLayouts); // Register the layout middleware app.set('layout', 'layout'); // Default layout file app.get('/', (req, res) => { res.render('home', { title: 'Home Page', user: 'Karthik' }); }); app.listen(3000); ``` **Step 3: Master Layout — `views/layout.ejs`** ```html <%= title %> <%- include('partials/navbar') %>
<%- body %>
<%- include('partials/footer') %> ``` **Step 4: Partial — `views/partials/navbar.ejs`** ```html
Home | About | Contact
``` **Step 5: Partial — `views/partials/footer.ejs`** ```html

© 2024 KTU Web App. All rights reserved.

``` **Step 6: View — `views/home.ejs`** ```html

Welcome, <%= user %>!

This is the home page rendered inside the master layout.

``` **Final Rendered HTML (Client receives):** ```html Home Page
Home | About | Contact

Welcome, Karthik!

This is the home page rendered inside the master layout.

© 2024 KTU Web App. All rights reserved.

``` --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks in Templating Engine questions:** > > 1. **Confusing `<%= %>` with `<%- %>`** — Examiners specifically test this. State clearly that `<%= %>` escapes HTML, while `<%- %>` does not. > 2. **Forgetting `app.set('view engine', 'ejs')`** — Without this line, `res.render()` fails with *"No default engine was specified"*. > 3. **Forgetting to install the engine** — `npm install ejs` is mandatory. Writing `require('ejs')` without installing causes `MODULE_NOT_FOUND` error. > 4. **Wrong file extension** — File must be `home.ejs` (not `home.html` or `home.js`). > 5. **Putting business logic in templates** — Templates should only handle **presentation**. Database queries belong in the controller. > 6. **Not mentioning MVC role** — Examiners award 1–2 marks just for stating that the templating engine is the **View layer of MVC**. > 7. **Ignoring security** — Always mention **XSS prevention** via auto-escaping. Examiners love this keyword. --- ## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Templating Engine** = Combines static template + dynamic data → renders final HTML on the server. - It is the **View layer** of the **MVC architecture** in Express.js. - **Popular engines:** EJS, Pug, Handlebars, Mustache, Nunjucks. - **EJS** is the most commonly used engine in **KTU labs and exams** (syntax: `<% %>`). - **`<%= var %>`** = Escaped output (XSS-safe); **`<%- var %>`** = Unescaped (raw HTML). - **`<% code %>`** = JS execution without output (for loops, conditionals). - **Configuration commands:** - `app.set('view engine', 'ejs')` — sets the engine. - `app.set('views', './views')` — sets the views directory. - **Rendering:** `res.render('templateName', { dataKey: dataValue })`. - **Partials** = Reusable fragments (header, footer); included via `<%- include('partials/header') %>`. - **Layouts** = Master page wrapper; use `<%- body %>` as content placeholder. - **Auto-escaping** prevents **XSS (Cross-Site Scripting)** attacks. - **Default views folder** = `./views/`. - **Production caching:** ON; **Development caching:** OFF (template re-read on every request). - **Architecture pipeline:** Lexer → Parser → AST → Compiler → Renderer → Final HTML. - **Handlebars is logic-less** — uses helpers and partials; no raw JS allowed inside templates. - **Pug is whitespace-sensitive** — indentation defines the HTML structure. - **Always install** the engine via `npm install ` before using `require()`.

topics.title: MongoDB: Architecture of MongoDB
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** MongoDB: Architecture of MongoDB

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **MongoDB Architecture** refers to the layered, distributed design of MongoDB — a cross-platform, document-oriented NoSQL database system — that defines how data is stored, indexed, replicated, queried, and sharded across one or more nodes. The architecture is fundamentally built around **BSON documents**, **collections**, **databases**, **replica sets**, **sharded clusters**, and the core server processes **`mongod`**, **`mongos`**, and the **config server**.

The architecture can be decomposed into **four logical tiers**:

| Tier | Component | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Application Layer** | Drivers (Node.js, Python, Java) | Issue CRUD/aggregation commands |
| **2. Query/Routing Layer** | `mongos` query router | Routes operations in sharded clusters |
| **3. Processing/Replication Layer** | `mongod` + Replica Sets | Executes queries, maintains redundancy |
| **4. Storage Layer** | WiredTiger / In-Memory | Persists BSON data, manages memory & disk |

The official MongoDB reference architecture follows the principle: **one primary, many secondaries, optional arbiters, optional hidden/delayed members, with optional sharded clusters fronted by config servers**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Post Office of Documents"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imagine MongoDB as a massive, distributed digital post office.**

- A **Document** is like an **envelope** containing a complete record (in JSON-like form), where each envelope can have a different set of fields — unlike rigid spreadsheet rows.
- A **Collection** is a **mailbox** holding many similar envelopes (e.g., all customer letters).
- A **Database** is the **entire post office building** housing multiple mailboxes.
- A **Replica Set** is the post office's **branch network** — the main branch (`primary`) handles incoming mail, while backup branches (`secondaries`) keep identical copies for safety. If the main branch burns down, a backup instantly becomes primary.
- **`mongos`** is the **postal dispatcher** who reads the address on an envelope and decides which branch should handle it.
- **Sharding** is the act of **splitting mail across multiple cities** by ZIP code — so no single city is overloaded.
- **WiredTiger** is the **filing cabinet technology** inside each branch that decides how envelopes are physically stuffed into drawers.

---

## 1.3 Core Terminology Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **Foundational vocabulary expected in KTU 2024 answer scripts.**

- **BSON (Binary JSON):** A binary-encoded serialization of JSON-like documents. Adds typed fields such as `int`, `long`, `Date`, `ObjectId`, and `BinData`.
- **`mongod`:** The primary database daemon process. Each `mongod` instance manages one or more databases.
- **`mongos`:** Lightweight query router used in sharded clusters; holds no data, only routing logic and a cached copy of the cluster metadata.
- **Config Server:** A special `mongod` (deployed as a replica set `CSRS`) that stores the cluster's metadata — chunk ranges, shard locations, authentication configs.
- **Primary:** The only replica-set member that accepts write operations.
- **Secondary:** A replica-set member replicating the primary's oplog.
- **Arbiter:** A non-data-holding member used purely for breaking election ties.
- **Oplog (Operations Log):** A capped collection recording all data modifications for replication.
- **Chunk:** A contiguous range of sharded-key values; default size **64 MB**.
- **Shard Key:** The immutable field(s) used to distribute documents across shards.

---

## 1.4 Why MongoDB's Architecture Matters for KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Examiners in KTU 2024 Scheme frequently test **two architecture questions**:
> 1. A diagrammatic description of the **replica set + sharded cluster topology**.
> 2. A comparison of **WiredTiger vs MMAPv1** storage engines, or the role of `mongos` vs `mongod`.

Understanding the **flow of a query** from application driver → `mongos` → `mongod` → storage engine → disk is the most frequently awarded 7-mark question pattern in Module 3.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture — Operational Walkthrough

The MongoDB architecture is best understood as **four cooperating layers**, each with a clearly demarcated responsibility.

### Layer 1 — Application / Driver Layer
- Language-specific drivers (Node.js, PyMongo, Java Sync, C#) speak the **MongoDB Wire Protocol** over TCP, typically on port **27017**.
- Drivers handle connection pooling, retries with **read preference**, and write concern acknowledgement levels.
- Commands are sent as **BSON-encoded BSONObj** messages tagged with an opcode (e.g., `OP_MSG`, the modern unified protocol).

### Layer 2 — Routing Layer (`mongos` for Sharded Clusters)
- Exists **only in sharded clusters**; in standalone/replica-set deployments the client connects directly to the `mongod`.
- Holds a **metadata cache** (loaded from config servers) describing chunk-to-shard mappings.
- Performs **targeting operations**: rewrites queries to include the appropriate shard keys and forwards to the right shards.
- Aggregates results from multiple shards when the query is **scatter-gather** (non-targeted).

### Layer 3 — Processing / Replication Layer (`mongod`)
- The **workhorse process** of MongoDB. Parses, optimizes, and executes queries.
- Maintains a **planner cache** of query plans (each plan is scored over a **trial period** of 101 executions by default).
- Implements **document-level concurrency control** (WiredTiger) via optimistic MVCC-style transactions.
- Participates in **replica-set elections** using the Raft-inspired consensus protocol with a default heartbeat of **2 seconds**.

### Layer 4 — Storage Engine Layer
- Two engines of historical importance:
  - **MMAPv1** — legacy, removed in MongoDB 4.2; memory-mapped files.
  - **WiredTiger** — default since 3.2; supports **document-level locking**, **compression (snappy/zstd)**, and **multi-document ACID transactions** since 4.0.
- Other engines: **In-Memory** (Enterprise), **Encrypted** (Enterprise).
- WiredTiger uses **B-trees** for indexes and **B-trees with LSM-style page eviction** for collections.
- Writes are journaled via **Write-Ahead Logging (WAL)** — first to journal, then to data files.

---

## 2.2 Replication Architecture (Replica Set)

> [!NOTE]
> A **Replica Set** in MongoDB is a group of `mongod` instances that maintain the same data set. It provides **high availability** and **disaster recovery**.

**Composition rules:**
- Minimum viable set: **1 primary + 1 secondary + 1 arbiter** (PSA).
- Maximum of **50 members**, but only **7 voting members**.
- All members hold a full copy of the data (no primary-secondary asymmetric storage).

**Election mechanics:**
- Heartbeat interval: **2 seconds**.
- Election timeout: **10 seconds** (configurable via `settings.electionTimeoutMillis`).
- A new primary is elected using **priority + most up-to-date oplog + majority vote**.
- Priority `0` members are **passive** — can become secondary but never primary.

**Write Concern / Read Concern:**
- Write Concern `w: "majority"` requires acknowledgement from a **majority** of voting members.
- Read Concern `"majority"` ensures only data confirmed by a majority is returned.

---

## 2.3 Sharding Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sharding** is MongoDB's horizontal-scaling strategy. Data is partitioned across shards using a **shard key**, and each shard is itself a replica set.

**Core components of a sharded cluster:**

| Component | Quantity | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Shards | ≥ 2 (each a replica set) | Store the partitioned data |
| Config Servers | 3 (CSRS) | Store cluster metadata |
| Query Routers (`mongos`) | ≥ 1 | Stateless routers |

**Chunk splitting and balancing:**
- Default chunk size: **64 MB**.
- A chunk is auto-split when it exceeds the threshold.
- The **Balancer process** (running on one of the config servers) migrates chunks from over-loaded shards to under-loaded ones.

**Two sharding strategies:**

| Strategy | Mechanism | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Range Sharding** | Splits by contiguous shard-key ranges | Range queries (e.g., timestamps, IDs) |
| **Hashed Sharding** | Hashes the shard key with MD5/FNV | Even distribution, write-heavy workloads |

---

## 2.4 Data Directory & On-Disk Layout

> [!NOTE]
> **Default paths** — Linux: `/var/lib/mongodb`, Windows: `C:\data\db`.

```
/var/lib/mongodb/
├── WiredTiger
│   ├── WiredTigerLAS.wt
│   ├── WiredTiger.turtle
│   └── WiredTiger.wt
├── WiredTigerLog.<N>
├── WiredTiger.lock
├── journal/
│   └── WiredTigerLog.<timestamp>
├── _mdb_catalog.wt
├── collection-<id>.wt
└── index-<id>.wt
```

- **`<dbname>.<collectionname>`** is the namespace stored in `_mdb_catalog`.
- Each database has its own subdirectory.

---

## 2.5 Memory Architecture (WiredTiger)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cache Size Formula** — the most frequently tested "formula" in MongoDB architecture questions.

$$ \text{cacheSizeGB} = \max\left(0.5 \cdot \text{RAM}_{\text{GB}},\ 0.5\right) $$

By default MongoDB allocates **50% of (RAM − 1 GB)** to the WiredTiger cache, capped at **256 GB** in 5.0+. Beyond the cache, **readahead** and OS page cache handle disk-to-memory transfer.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Value | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Default chunk size | $\vert \text{Chunk} \vert = 64$ | MB | Configurable |
| 2 | Cache size (default) | $\text{Cache} = 0.5 \cdot (\text{RAM} - 1)$ | GB | WiredTiger |
| 3 | Election timeout | $T_{\text{elec}} = 10000$ | ms | Replica set |
| 4 | Heartbeat interval | $T_{\text{hb}} = 2000$ | ms | Replica set |
| 5 | Oplog window target | $\geq 24$ | hours | Tunable via `oplogSizeMB` |
| 6 | Max replica set members | $N = 50$ | count | Voting capped at 7 |
| 7 | Write Concern majority threshold | $\lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$ | members | For $N$ voting members |
| 8 | BSON max document size | $S_{\max} = 16\,384\,768$ | bytes | 16 MB hard limit |
| 9 | Max nested depth | $D_{\max} = 100$ | levels | BSON |
| 10 | BSON index key limit | $K_{\max} = 1024$ | bytes | Compound key total |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **E-commerce catalogs** — Document model eliminates JOINs; replica sets provide 99.99% uptime.
- **IoT telemetry** — Hashed sharding distributes millions of writes per second.
- **Mobile backends** — Flexible schemas support evolving client apps without migrations.
- **Content management** — Rich BSON types (Date, Decimal128, GeoJSON) enable single-document aggregations.
- **Real-time analytics** — Change streams (oplog tailing) feed Kafka pipelines.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Decision Matrices

## 3.1 Derivation — Replica Set Majority Calculation

> **Problem (typical KTU short answer):** A replica set has **5 voting members**. How many members must acknowledge a `w: "majority"` write?

### Step 1 — Identify Voting Member Count

We are given $N = 5$ voting members.

### Step 2 — Apply the Majority Formula

A strict majority requires strictly more than half:

$$ N_{\text{majority}} = \left\lfloor \frac{N}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 $$

### Step 3 — Substitute and Evaluate

$$ \begin{aligned}
N_{\text{majority}} &= \left\lfloor \frac{5}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 \\
&= \lfloor 2.5 \rfloor + 1 \\
&= 2 + 1 \\
&= 3
\end{aligned} $$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

> A 5-member replica set requires acknowledgement from **at least 3 voting members** for a `w: "majority"` write to be confirmed. This guarantees that any acknowledged write survives a simultaneous failure of **up to 2 members** (since 3 acknowledged copies cannot be reduced below majority).

---

## 3.2 Derivation — WiredTiger Cache Sizing

> **Problem:** A KTU question asks how much RAM to allocate to WiredTiger on a server with **32 GB** of RAM.

### Step 1 — Apply the Default Formula

$$ \text{CacheSize}_{\text{GB}} = 0.5 \cdot (\text{RAM}_{\text{GB}} - 1) $$

### Step 2 — Substitute $\text{RAM}_{\text{GB}} = 32$

$$ \begin{aligned}
\text{CacheSize}_{\text{GB}} &= 0.5 \cdot (32 - 1) \\
&= 0.5 \cdot 31 \\
&= 15.5
\end{aligned} $$

### Step 3 — Adjust for OS Reserve

In practice, MongoDB best-practice recommends reserving **1 GB** minimum for the OS, hence the formula already subtracts 1. For containers, the explicit formula is:

$$ \text{CacheSize}_{\text{GB}} = \max\left(0.5 \cdot \text{RAM}_{\text{GB}},\ 0.256\right) $$

For $32$ GB:

$$ \text{CacheSize}_{\text{GB}} = \max(0.5 \cdot 32,\ 0.256) = \max(16.0,\ 0.256) = 16.0 $$

### Step 4 — Configurable via `storage.wiredTiger.engineConfig.cacheSizeGB`

Set explicitly: `cacheSizeGB: 16` in `/etc/mongod.conf`.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Storage Engine Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a guaranteed **7-mark KTU 2024 question** if asked as "Compare WiredTiger and MMAPv1."

| Parameter | MMAPv1 (Legacy) | WiredTiger (Current) |
|---|---|---|
| Introduced in | Pre-3.0 (original) | MongoDB 3.2 |
| Locking Granularity | Collection-level | Document-level |
| Compression | None | Snappy, Zlib, Zstd |
| In-Memory Sort | Limited (32 MB cap) | Unlimited (cache-based) |
| Multi-document ACID | No | Yes (since 4.0) |
| Journaling | Yes | Yes (WAL) |
| Data Structure | B-tree on mmap'd files | B-tree (collections & indexes) |
| Removed in | MongoDB 4.2 | Still default |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sharding Strategy Selection

| Workload Pattern | Recommended Strategy | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Time-series logs (timestamps) | **Range** on `timestamp` | Range queries are localized |
| User profile writes | **Hashed** on `_id` or `userId` | Even distribution prevents hot shards |
| Multi-tenant SaaS | **Ranged** on `tenantId` | Tenant-level data locality |
| Geo-distributed apps | **Ranged** with zone sharding | Place tenants by geography |
| Append-heavy IoT | **Hashed** on device ID | Prevents write hotspot on a single shard |

---

## 3.5 Decision Tree — When to Use Replica Set vs Sharded Cluster

```
                 ┌──────────────────────────┐
                 │   Total dataset > RAM?   │
                 └────────────┬─────────────┘
                  YES         │        NO
        ┌─────────────────────┴─────────────────────┐
        │                                           │
        ▼                                           ▼
┌──────────────────┐                       ┌──────────────────┐
│ Need > 60K ops/s │                       │ Replica Set only │
│  write throughput│                       │ (no sharding)    │
└────────┬─────────┘                       └──────────────────┘
   YES   │   NO
    ┌────┴────┐
    ▼         ▼
 SHARDED   Single-node
 CLUSTER   Replica Set
```

> **Rule of thumb:** Sharding is justified only when **either** data size exceeds what a single server's WiredTiger cache can hold **or** write throughput exceeds the per-server capacity (~10–30K writes/sec depending on document size).

```mermaid
flowchart LR

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three differences between SQL (RDBMS) and MongoDB (NoSQL).**

**Model Answer:**

| # | RDBMS (SQL) | MongoDB (NoSQL Document) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stores data in **tables** with rigid rows and columns | Stores data in **BSON documents** inside collections with flexible schemas |
| 2 | Uses **SQL** as the query language | Uses the **MongoDB Query Language (MQL)** — JSON-like |
| 3 | Requires a predefined **schema** enforced by DDL | **Schema-less**; documents in the same collection may differ |
| 4 | Supports **JOINs** across normalised tables | Embeds related data; JOINs replaced by `$lookup` |
| 5 | Vertical scaling (bigger server) primary | Horizontal scaling via **sharding** is first-class |

**(3 marks — 1 per correct difference)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is the role of `mongos` in a MongoDB sharded cluster?**

**Model Answer:**
`mongos` is the **stateless query router** that fronts a sharded cluster. Its roles are:
1. **Routes** client queries to the appropriate shard(s) using cached cluster metadata loaded from config servers.
2. **Performs targeted operations** when queries include the shard key; otherwise, **scatter-gathers** results across all shards.
3. **Merges** sorted results from multiple shards before returning to the client.

It holds **no persistent data** and is typically deployed as a lightweight process on each application server. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose **2–3 marks** by:
> - Drawing the replica set diagram without labelling **primary/secondary/arbiter** roles.
> - Confusing `mongos` (router) with `mongod` (server).
> - Forgetting to mention that **config servers also form a replica set (CSRS) since MongoDB 3.4**.
> - Not specifying the default **chunk size of 64 MB**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **architecture of MongoDB** with a neat block diagram. Describe the roles of `mongod`, `mongos`, and config servers in a sharded cluster. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **replica set mechanism** in MongoDB. How is the primary elected? What is the significance of `w: "majority"` write concern? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**MongoDB Architecture** is layered into four tiers:

1. **Application Layer** — Language drivers (Node.js, Python, Java, C#) talk to MongoDB using the **Wire Protocol** over TCP port **27017**.
2. **Routing Layer** — In sharded clusters, one or more `mongos` processes act as **stateless query routers**.
3. **Processing / Replication Layer** — `mongod` processes, organised into **replica sets**, execute queries and maintain data redundancy.
4. **Storage Layer** — The **WiredTiger** storage engine handles B-tree storage, compression (snappy/zstd), journaling (WAL), and document-level locking.

**Role of `mongod`:**
- The **primary database daemon**.
- Manages databases, collections, documents, and indexes.
- Participates in replica-set elections.
- Default port: **27017**.

**Role of `mongos`:**
- **Query router** present only in sharded clusters.
- Caches cluster metadata from config servers.
- Routes targeted queries and aggregates scatter-gather results.
- Holds **no data** — purely a routing process.

**Role of Config Servers:**
- Store **cluster metadata** — chunk ranges, shard-to-chunk mappings, authentication configuration.
- Deployed as a **replica set (CSRS — Config Server Replica Set)** since MongoDB 3.4.
- Default port: **27019**.

*(Diagram — refer to Section 4.1, 4.3 of these notes.)* **(7 marks — 3 for diagram + 2 mongod + 1 mongos + 1 config)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Replica Set Mechanism:**
A replica set is a group of `mongod` instances that maintain the **same data set**, providing **high availability** and **fault tolerance**.

**Components:**
- **One Primary** — the only member accepting write operations.
- **One or more Secondaries** — replicate the primary's oplog.
- **Optional Arbiters** — vote in elections without holding data.

**Primary Election:**
- Heartbeats are exchanged every **2 seconds**.
- If no heartbeat is received within the **election timeout (10 s default)**, members call for an election.
- The candidate with the **highest priority** (default `1`), most **up-to-date oplog**, and **majority votes** becomes primary.
- Uses a **Raft-inspired consensus protocol**.

**Significance of `w: "majority"`:**
- Requires acknowledgement from **a majority of voting members**.
- Guarantees that a write survives the failure of any minority.
- For $N$ voting members, requires $\lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$ acknowledgements.
- Pairs with `j: true` for journal acknowledgement — together forming the **strongest durability guarantee**.

**Example:** In a 5-member replica set, `w: "majority"` requires **3 acknowledgements**, allowing up to **2 simultaneous failures** without data loss. **(7 marks — 2 components + 3 election + 2 write concern)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Compare **WiredTiger** and **MMAPv1** storage engines. Why is WiredTiger the default? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **sharding in MongoDB** with its architecture. Differentiate between **range sharding** and **hashed sharding**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | MMAPv1 | WiredTiger |
|---|---|---|
| Status | Legacy (removed in 4.2) | Default since 3.2 |
| Locking | Collection-level | Document-level |
| Compression | None | Snappy / Zlib / Zstd |
| Multi-document ACID | Not supported | Supported (since 4.0) |
| Concurrency | Limited | High (MVCC) |
| Memory mapping | mmap'd files | B-tree, cache-resident |
| In-memory sort limit | 32 MB | No hard limit |

**Why WiredTiger is the default:**
1. **Document-level concurrency** enables much higher write throughput.
2. **Built-in compression** reduces on-disk footprint by 30–80%.
3. **ACID transactions** support complex multi-document operations.
4. **Better memory management** with explicit cache sizing.
5. **Checkpoint-based durability** via write-ahead logging.

**(7 marks — 5 for comparison + 2 for justification)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Sharding Architecture:**
Sharding is MongoDB's **horizontal partitioning** mechanism. A sharded cluster has three components:

1. **Shards** — Each shard is itself a replica set storing a subset of the data.
2. **Config Servers (CSRS)** — A 3-member replica set storing cluster metadata.
3. **Mongos Routers** — Stateless processes routing queries to the correct shard.

**Chunk Mechanics:**
- Data is partitioned into **chunks** of up to **64 MB** by default.
- The **Balancer** migrates chunks from over-loaded to under-loaded shards.

**Range Sharding vs Hashed Sharding:**

| Aspect | Range Sharding | Hashed Sharding |
|---|---|---|
| Partitioning | Contiguous ranges of shard-key values | Hash function (MD5/FNV) maps key → bucket |
| Best for | Range queries (`db.coll.find({ts: {$gt: x}})`) | Write-heavy, even-distribution workloads |
| Risk | **Hot spots** if shard key is monotonic (e.g., `_id` ObjectId) | Prevents hotspots |
| Example | `shardCollection: "logs.log", key: {timestamp: 1}` | `shardCollection: "logs.log", key: {deviceId: "hashed"}` |

**(7 marks — 3 architecture + 2 chunk/balancer + 2 range vs hashed)**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 25-Point Rapid Revision List for "Architecture of MongoDB"**

1. MongoDB is a **document-oriented NoSQL** database storing **BSON** documents.
2. **BSON max document size = 16 MB**; max nesting depth = **100 levels**.
3. Default port **`mongod` = 27017**, **`mongos` = 27017**, **config server = 27019**.
4. **`mongod`** = data-bearing server process; **`mongos`** = stateless query router.
5. **Replica Set = 1 primary + ≥ 1 secondary + optional arbiter**.
6. Replica set max members = **50**; voting members capped at **7**.
7. **Election timeout** = 10 s; **heartbeat** = 2 s.
8. **WiredTiger** is the default storage engine since MongoDB 3.2.
9. WiredTiger provides **document-level locking** + **snappy/zstd compression**.
10. **MMAPv1** was removed in MongoDB 4.2 — never the default in 2024 syllabus.
11. WiredTiger default cache = **50% of (RAM − 1 GB)**.
12. **Sharding** partitions data across shards using a **shard key**.
13. **Default chunk size = 64 MB**.
14. Sharded cluster needs ≥ **2 shards, 3 config servers, 1 mongos**.
15. **Range sharding** = contiguous keys; **Hashed sharding** = even distribution.
16. **Config Servers** store metadata as a **CSRS** since 3.4.
17. **Oplog** = capped collection recording all modifications for replication.
18. **Write Concern `w: "majority"`** requires $\lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$ acknowledgements.
19. **Read Concern `"majority"`** ensures only majority-confirmed data is returned.
20. **Change Streams** allow real-time reactions to oplog events.
21. MongoDB supports **multi-document ACID transactions** since 4.0 (replica sets) and 4.2 (sharded clusters).
22. **Journaling** uses Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) — durability before visible changes.
23. **Indexes** are B-trees; **TTL indexes** auto-expire documents.
24. **GridFS** is the spec for storing files > 16 MB split across documents.
25. KTU's most-asked question pattern: **"Draw and explain MongoDB architecture"** + **"Compare storage engines"**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Pitfall Checklist:**
> - Do NOT confuse `mongod` (server) with `mongos` (router).
> - Always mention **CSRS** for config servers in modern answers.
> - For write concern, **show the calculation** $\lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$.
> - For cache sizing, **state the formula** explicitly before substituting.
> - Use **64 MB chunk size** as the default; do not invent other numbers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Installing MongoDB
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Installing MongoDB

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Installing MongoDB

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MongoDB** is an open-source, document-oriented NoSQL database program that uses JSON-like documents with optional schemas. **Installation** of MongoDB refers to the systematic process of deploying the MongoDB server (`mongod`), the shell client (`mongosh`), and associated service components onto a target operating system so that the database engine is operational and ready to accept client connections over a defined network port (default **27017**).

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme — PBITT504 (Web Application Development)**, *Module 3* focuses on the **MEAN/MERN Stack** with MongoDB as the data persistence layer. Installing MongoDB correctly is the foundational step before any Express.js or Node.js application can perform `CRUD` (Create, Read, Update, Delete) operations against a document store.

| Term | Meaning | KTU Context |
|---|---|---|
| `mongod` | MongoDB daemon (server process) | Background service handling data persistence |
| `mongosh` | MongoDB Shell (modern client) | Interactive JavaScript interface for queries |
| `27017` | Default port number | Standard TCP port for MongoDB server |
| Compass | GUI client for MongoDB | Visual tool for schema exploration |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Warehouse Setup"

Imagine you are opening a **warehouse** for storing files (your web app's data):

- **MongoDB Server (`mongod`)** → The physical warehouse building with shelves, loading docks, and security.
- **The Port (`27017`)** → The unique door number that delivery trucks (your Node.js app) must know to enter.
- **The Shell (`mongosh`)** → The walkie-talkie the manager uses to talk to the warehouse and ask for items.
- **Compass** → A camera-feed monitor where you can visually see what's inside the warehouse.
- **Installation** → The process of constructing the warehouse, hiring the manager, setting up the door, and testing everything before opening for business.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why install MongoDB locally for KTU labs?** Because the practical examinations and semester projects require you to demonstrate a fully functional stack — from HTML frontend to Node.js backend to a running MongoDB database. Without installation, no persistence layer exists.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Overview — What Happens "Under the Hood" During Installation

When you install MongoDB, three core things happen in sequence:

1. **Binary Placement** → The executable files (`mongod`, `mongosh`, `mongos`) are copied to a system path (e.g., `/usr/bin/` on Linux or `C:\Program Files\MongoDB\` on Windows).
2. **Data Directory Creation** → A folder (default `/data/db` on Linux/macOS or `C:\data\db` on Windows) is created to hold the actual database files in the proprietary **WiredTiger** storage format.
3. **Service Registration** → The `mongod` daemon is registered as a system service (auto-start on boot) OR runs as a foreground process in development mode.

```text
[mongod binary] + [data folder] + [port 27017] = A Running MongoDB Instance
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 module expects students to demonstrate competency in installing MongoDB on **Windows** (most common in Kerala college labs), **Ubuntu/Linux** (common for cloud/server-side deployment), and via **Docker** (modern containerized approach). Understanding the **difference between Community Edition and Atlas (cloud)** is also tested.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The MongoDB Ecosystem

### 2.1.1 Editions of MongoDB

MongoDB is distributed in **three primary editions**, each designed for a specific audience:

- **MongoDB Community Edition** → Free, open-source, runs locally. **This is what KTU labs use.**
- **MongoDB Enterprise Edition** → Paid version with advanced security, LDAP, auditing, in-memory storage.
- **MongoDB Atlas** → Fully managed cloud service (AWS/Azure/GCP). No installation needed; runs on remote clusters.

> [!NOTE]
> For the KTU 2024 examination, the focus is **almost exclusively on MongoDB Community Edition installed locally**. Atlas may appear as a 3-mark conceptual question.

### 2.1.2 Installation Pathways — The Three Pillars

| # | Method | Target OS | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **`.msi` Installer (Wizard)** | Windows 10/11 | Beginners; GUI-driven lab setup |
| 2 | **Native Package Manager** | Linux (Ubuntu/Debian/Fedora) | Server deployment; production-like |
| 3 | **Docker Container** | Cross-platform | Reproducible environments; CI/CD |

### 2.2 Why Each Step Matters — The "Why & How" Logic

**Step 1 — Downloading the Binary:**
The reason for choosing the correct binary (e.g., `Windows Server 2019` vs `Ubuntu 22.04`) is that MongoDB is compiled natively per OS. A Linux binary **cannot run on Windows** because of differing system calls (e.g., `fork()`, executable formats ELF vs PE).

**Step 2 — Setting Up the Data Directory:**
MongoDB stores data in a binary format called **WiredTiger** (since v3.2). The default data path is:
- Linux/macOS: `/data/db`
- Windows: `C:\data\db`

If this folder does **not exist with correct write permissions**, `mongod` will refuse to start with the error: `IllegalOperation: Attempted to create a lock file on a read-only directory`.

**Step 3 — Configuring the System Path:**
Adding the MongoDB `bin` directory to the system `PATH` environment variable allows the user to type `mongod` or `mongosh` from any terminal location without specifying the full path.

**Step 4 — Starting the Daemon:**
The command `mongod` launches the server. The command `mongosh` opens the client shell that connects to the server via `mongodb://localhost:27017`.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the contents of this table. These are the most frequently tested facts in KTU module-end evaluations.

| Component | Default Value | File Location (Windows) | File Location (Linux) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Server binary | `mongod.exe` | `C:\Program Files\MongoDB\Server\7.0\bin\` | `/usr/bin/mongod` |
| Shell binary | `mongosh.exe` | `C:\Program Files\MongoDB\Server\7.0\bin\` | `/usr/bin/mongosh` |
| Default data path | `/data/db` | `C:\data\db` | `/data/db` |
| Default log path | `/var/log/mongodb` | `C:\data\log\mongod.log` | `/var/log/mongodb/mongod.log` |
| Default port | **27017** | — | — |
| Config file | `mongod.cfg` | `C:\Program Files\MongoDB\Server\7.0\bin\` | `/etc/mongod.conf` |
| Storage engine | **WiredTiger** | — | — |
| Document format | **BSON** (Binary JSON) | — | — |
| Query language | MongoDB Query Language (MQL) | — | — |
| Aggregation framework | Pipelines using `$match`, `$group`, `$project` | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical distinction:** While MongoDB **stores** data in BSON (a binary, typed superset of JSON), the **input/output** you type in `mongosh` is plain JavaScript/JSON. This is a common viva question.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **E-commerce websites** (Myntra, Flipkart-style) use MongoDB to store flexible product catalogs where each product may have different attributes (a phone has "RAM", a shirt has "size").
- **IoT platforms** use MongoDB because it handles high-velocity sensor data ingestion (millions of writes per second).
- **Content management systems** (blogs, news sites) use MongoDB for storing articles with variable metadata.
- **Real-time analytics** dashboards leverage MongoDB's aggregation pipelines for OLAP-like queries.

In a **MEAN stack** (MongoDB + Express + Angular + Node), MongoDB replaces the traditional relational database (like MySQL/PostgreSQL) because the data is document-shaped, not table-shaped — aligning naturally with JavaScript objects passed through the Node.js layer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Installation Walkthroughs (Three Methods)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 syllabus expects familiarity with at least **two installation methods**. The most commonly tested are **Windows MSI Installer** and **Ubuntu APT**.

## 3.1 Method A — Installing MongoDB on Windows 10/11 (MSI Wizard)

This is the **primary method** tested in KTU labs. Follow every step.

### Step A1: Download the Installer

Navigate to the official MongoDB download center. Select the **Windows x64** MSI package for the current stable release (e.g., 7.0 or 8.0). Choose the **Complete** setup type during installation.

### Step A2: Run the Installer with Administrator Privileges

Right-click the `.msi` file → **"Run as Administrator"**. The wizard displays the licensing agreement. Accept the terms to proceed.

### Step A3: Choose Setup Type

Select **"Complete"** to install all features (server, client, tools, Compass). This ensures `mongod`, `mongosh`, and the GUI Compass are all installed.

### Step A4: Service Configuration

The wizard offers to **"Install MongoDB as a Service"**. If checked:
- **Service Name:** `MongoDB Server`
- **Data Directory:** `C:\Program Files\MongoDB\Server\7.0\data\`
- **Log Directory:** `C:\Program Files\MongoDB\Server\7.0\log\`

> [!NOTE]
> Installing as a **Windows Service** means MongoDB starts automatically when the computer boots. For KTU labs, this is recommended so the database is always ready.

### Step A5: Install MongoDB Compass (Optional but Recommended)

The installer will ask whether to install Compass. **Accept it** — Compass is a graphical interface that simplifies database exploration.

### Step A6: Verify the Installation

Open **Command Prompt** or **PowerShell** and execute the following sequence of commands:

```bash
mongod --version
```

The expected output is a version banner, e.g.:

```text
db version v7.0.5
git version: ...
OpenSSL version: OpenSSL 3.0.x
```

Next, start the server explicitly (if not running as service):

```bash
mongod
```

You should see log output ending with:

```text
{"t":{"$date":"..."},"s":"I",  "msg":"Waiting for connections", ...}
```

Finally, open a **new** terminal and connect via the shell:

```bash
mongosh
```

You will be greeted with:

```text
test>
```

This prompt indicates a successful connection to the `test` database.

### Step A7: Run a Sanity Test Query

Inside the `mongosh` prompt, type:

```javascript
db.runCommand({ ping: 1 })
```

Expected successful response:

```text
{ ok: 1 }
```

> [!NOTE]
> **`{ ok: 1 }`** is MongoDB's universal "I am alive and responding" message. If you receive `{ ok: 0 }`, the server is up but the command failed. If you get a connection refused error, the server is not running.

## 3.2 Method B — Installing MongoDB on Ubuntu Linux (APT Package Manager)

This method is tested for students pursuing **server-side** or **cloud deployment** tracks.

### Step B1: Import the Public GPG Key

```bash
curl -fsSL https://www.mongodb.org/static/pgp/server-7.0.asc | \
   sudo gpg -o /usr/share/keyrings/mongodb-server-7.0.gpg \
   --dearmor
```

This step authenticates that the MongoDB packages you are about to install are genuinely from MongoDB Inc. and have not been tampered with (GPG = GNU Privacy Guard).

### Step B2: Add the MongoDB Repository

```bash
echo "deb [ arch=amd64,arm64 signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/mongodb-server-7.0.gpg ] https://repo.mongodb.org/apt/ubuntu jammy/mongodb-org/7.0 multiverse" | \
   sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/mongodb-org-7.0.list
```

This adds the MongoDB 7.0 apt repository to your system sources, targeting Ubuntu 22.04 (codename `jammy`).

### Step B3: Reload the Package Database

```bash
sudo apt-get update
```

### Step B4: Install MongoDB Community Edition

```bash
sudo apt-get install -y mongodb-org
```

This installs four packages:
- `mongodb-org-server` → contains `mongod`
- `mongodb-org-mongosh` → contains the shell
- `mongodb-org-shell` → legacy shell (deprecated)
- `mongodb-mongosh` → explicit shell package
- `mongodb-org-tools` → contains `mongodump`, `mongorestore`, `mongoimport`, etc.

### Step B5: Create the Data and Log Directories

```bash
sudo mkdir -p /data/db
sudo chown -R mongodb:mongodb /data/db
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The `chown` step is critical. Without it, `mongod` cannot write to `/data/db` because the service runs as the `mongodb` user, not `root`. This is the **#1 cause of installation failures** on Linux.

### Step B6: Start the MongoDB Daemon

```bash
sudo systemctl start mongod
sudo systemctl enable mongod
sudo systemctl status mongod
```

The `enable` command ensures MongoDB starts automatically on every boot.

### Step B7: Verify Connection

```bash
mongosh
```

## 3.3 Method C — Installing MongoDB via Docker (Containerized)

This is the **modern, reproducible** method increasingly appearing in KTU viva questions.

### Step C1: Pull the Official Image

```bash
docker pull mongo:latest
```

The `latest` tag fetches the most recent stable MongoDB image from Docker Hub's official `mongo` repository.

### Step C2: Run a Container

```bash
docker run -d -p 27017:27017 --name mongodb-container --restart unless-stopped mongo:latest
```

Parameter breakdown:
- `-d` → detached (background) mode
- `-p 27017:27017` → maps container's port 27017 to host's port 27017
- `--name` → assigns a memorable container name
- `--restart unless-stopped` → auto-restart on reboot

### Step C3: Connect via Shell

```bash
docker exec -it mongodb-container mongosh
```

This executes `mongosh` *inside* the running container, connecting to the local `mongod` instance.

## 3.4 The `mongod.conf` Configuration File (YAML Format)

Modern MongoDB installations use a YAML configuration file. The KTU syllabus treats this as a **high-yield topic**.

```yaml
# mongod.conf — example configuration
storage:
  dbPath: /data/db
  engine: wiredTiger

systemLog:
  destination: file
  path: /var/log/mongodb/mongod.log
  logAppend: true

net:
  port: 27017
  bindIp: 127.0.0.1

processManagement:
  fork: true

security:
  authorization: enabled
```

### Configuration Field Reference Table

| YAML Path | Purpose | Common Value |
|---|---|---|
| `storage.dbPath` | Where data files live | `/data/db` |
| `storage.engine` | Storage engine | `wiredTiger` |
| `systemLog.path` | Log file location | `/var/log/mongodb/mongod.log` |
| `net.port` | TCP listening port | `27017` |
| `net.bindIp` | IP addresses to bind | `127.0.0.1` (localhost only) |
| `processManagement.fork` | Run as background daemon | `true` (Linux only) |
| `security.authorization` | Enable user authentication | `enabled` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bind IP caution:** By default, `bindIp: 127.0.0.1` means MongoDB only accepts connections from the same machine. For KTU labs where Node.js runs on the same machine, this is fine. For multi-machine setups, you must change it to `0.0.0.0` — **but this is a security risk in production**.

## 3.5 Post-Installation Verification Checklist

After installing MongoDB via any method, run these final verification commands:

```bash
# Check server version
mongod --version

# Check shell version
mongosh --version

# Verify the service is listening on port 27017 (Linux)
sudo ss -tlnp | grep 27017

# Verify the service is listening (Windows)
netstat -an | findstr 27017

# Test connection via shell
mongosh --eval "db.runCommand({ping:1})"
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If `mongosh --eval` returns `{ ok: 1 }`, your MongoDB installation is **complete and verified**. You are now ready to integrate it with Node.js/Express.js for the MEAN stack.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics
