| Application Domain | Use Case for Mongoose Reads |
|---|---|
| **E-commerce** | Product listing with filters, pagination, sorting by price |
| **Social Media** | Fetch user posts, comments, likes with `populate()` for relations |
| **Learning Management Systems** | Display courses, modules, lessons for enrolled students |
| **CRM Systems** | Query customer records with complex filters |
| **IoT Dashboards** | Read sensor data with time-range filters (`$gte`, `$lte`) |
| **Banking Apps** | Fetch transaction history with date sort and limit |

> [!NOTE]
> In production-grade Node.js apps, Mongoose reads are often cached using **Redis** or **memory caches** because database reads are expensive. The query result is stored temporarily to avoid repeated MongoDB hits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Implementation: Reading Data from MongoDB using Mongoose

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Establish Connection

```javascript
const mongoose = require('mongoose');

mongoose.connect('mongodb://127.0.0.1:27017/ktuwebapp')
  .then(() => console.log('MongoDB connected'))
  .catch(err => console.error('Connection error:', err));
```

**Logic:** The `connect()` method returns a Promise. We use `.then()` for success and `.catch()` for errors. The database `ktuwebapp` is created on the fly if it doesn't exist.

---

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Define a Schema

```javascript
const studentSchema = new mongoose.Schema({
  name: { type: String, required: true },
  age: { type: Number, min: 16, max: 60 },
  department: { type: String, enum: ['CS', 'IT', 'EC', 'ME', 'CE'] },
  cgpa: { type: Number, default: 7.0 },
  createdAt: { type: Date, default: Date.now }
});
```

**Logic:** A schema enforces structure. Field types, defaults, validators are defined here. This becomes the template for every document.

---

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Compile the Model

```javascript
const Student = mongoose.model('Student', studentSchema);
```

**Logic:** Mongoose compiles the schema into a Model. The model name 'Student' is singular; MongoDB automatically pluralizes it to the collection `students`.

---

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Reading All Documents

```javascript
async function getAllStudents() {
  try {
    const students = await Student.find();
    console.log('All students:', students);
  } catch (err) {
    console.error('Error fetching students:', err);
  }
}
getAllStudents();
```

**Derivation of behavior:**

$$
\text{Student.find()} \xrightarrow{\text{async query}} \text{Array of all documents in 'students' collection}
$$

If the collection is empty → `students` is an empty array `[]`. If the connection fails → Promise rejects → caught in `catch`.

---

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Reading with Filter

```javascript
async function getCSStudents() {
  try {
    const csStudents = await Student.find({ department: 'CS' });
    console.log('CS Department Students:', csStudents);
  } catch (err) {
    console.error(err);
  }
}
getCSStudents();
```

**Logic:** The filter `{ department: 'CS' }` is converted internally to MongoDB's `{ department: { $eq: 'CS' } }`.

---

### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Reading with Operators

```javascript
async function getToppers() {
  try {
    const toppers = await Student
      .find({ cgpa: { $gte: 9.0 }, department: { $in: ['CS', 'IT'] } })
      .sort({ cgpa: -1 })
      .limit(5)
      .select('name cgpa department');

    console.log('Top 5 CS/IT students:', toppers);
  } catch (err) {
    console.error(err);
  }
}
getToppers();
```

**Analytical breakdown:**

| Chain Method | Effect on Query |
|---|---|
| `.find({...})` | Build filter: cgpa ≥ 9.0 AND dept in [CS, IT] |
| `.sort({ cgpa: -1 })` | Sort descending by cgpa (highest first) |
| `.limit(5)` | Return max 5 documents |
| `.select('name cgpa department')` | Project only these 3 fields (plus `_id` by default) |

**Equivalent MongoDB shell command:**

$$
\text{db.students.find}\big(\{\text{cgpa: }\{\$gte: 9.0\},\ \text{dept: }\{\$in: ['CS','IT']\}\big\}\big)\text{.sort}(\{\text{cgpa: }-1\})\text{.limit}(5)
$$

---

### 3.1.7 Step 7 — `findOne()` and `findById()`

```javascript
async function getOneStudent() {
  try {
    const student = await Student.findOne({ name: 'Aravind' });
    console.log('First student named Aravind:', student);

    const byId = await Student.findById('65f123abc456def789012345');
    console.log('Student with given ID:', byId);
  } catch (err) {
    console.error(err);
  }
}
getOneStudent();
```

**Logic Distinction:**

$$
\text{findOne} = \lim_{n \to 1} \text{find with limit 1, returning first match by natural order}
$$

$$
\text{findById} = \text{findOne with filter } \{\text{\_id: ObjectId(id)}\}
$$

`findById()` is **optimized** — MongoDB uses the `_id` index automatically (O(log n) lookup), making it the fastest single-document read.

---

### 3.1.8 Step 8 — Pagination Pattern

```javascript
async function getPaginated(page = 1, pageSize = 10) {
  try {
    const skip = (page - 1) * pageSize;
    const students = await Student
      .find()
      .sort({ createdAt: -1 })
      .skip(skip)
      .limit(pageSize);

    const total = await Student.countDocuments();
    console.log(`Page ${page}:`, students, `Total: ${total}`);
  } catch (err) {
    console.error(err);
  }
}
getPaginated(2, 5);
```

**Pagination formula:**

$$
\text{skip} = (\text{page} - 1) \times \text{pageSize}
$$

$$
\text{total pages} = \lceil \frac{\text{total}}{\text{pageSize}} \rceil
$$

For page 2, pageSize 5 → skip = 5, returns documents 6 through 10.

---

### 3.1.9 Step 9 — Using `.populate()` for Joins

```javascript
const courseSchema = new mongoose.Schema({
  title: String,
  studentId: { type: mongoose.Schema.Types.ObjectId, ref: 'Student' }
});
const Course = mongoose.model('Course', courseSchema);

async function getCoursesWithStudent() {
  try {
    const courses = await Course.find().populate('studentId', 'name department');
    console.log('Courses with student details:', courses);
  } catch (err) {
    console.error(err);
  }
}
getCoursesWithStudent();
```

**Logic:** `populate()` replaces the `ObjectId` reference with the actual referenced document (or selected fields). Internally, Mongoose performs a **second query** to fetch the referenced documents.

> [!WARNING]
> `populate()` triggers a **separate query per populated path**. Over-using it causes the N+1 problem. For high-performance systems, use MongoDB's `$lookup` aggregation pipeline instead.

---

### 3.1.10 Step 10 — Using `.lean()` for Performance

```javascript
async function getLeanStudents() {
  try {
    const students = await Student.find().lean();
    console.log('Plain JS objects:', students);
  } catch (err) {
    console.error(err);
  }
}
getLeanStudents();
```

**Logic:** `.lean()` returns plain JavaScript objects instead of full Mongoose Documents. This skips hydration (no getters, virtuals, save methods) → **5x faster** reads in benchmarks.

$$
\text{find()} \rightarrow \text{Mongoose Document (slow, full features)}
$$

$$
\text{find().lean()} \rightarrow \text{Plain JS Object (fast, read-only)}
$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Read Methods

| Method | Returns | Performance | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| `find()` | Array | O(n) scan if no index | Bulk listing |
| `findOne()` | Single Doc | O(n) scan | First match |
| `findById()` | Single Doc | O(log n) via `_id` index | Specific record lookup |
| `find().lean()` | Plain JS Array | ~5x faster | Read-only API responses |
| `find().populate()` | Array with joined data | 2+ queries | Relational display |
| `countDocuments()` | Number | O(n) scan | Pagination totals |
| `exists()` | `{ _id }` or `null` | O(log n) | Existence check (cheaper than findOne) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mongoose Read Operation — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Developer writes Model.find filter] --> B[Query Object Built]
    B --> C{Async Method}
    C -->|await| D[Promise Pending]
    C -->|.then| E[Callback Registered]
    D --> F[Sent to MongoDB Driver]
    E --> F
    F --> G[MongoDB Server Executes Query]
    G --> H{Document Found?}
    H -->|Yes - findOne/findById| I[Return Single Document]
    H -->|Yes - find| J[Return Array of Documents]
    H -->|No - findOne/findById| K[Return null]
    H -->|No - find| L[Return Empty Array]
    I --> M[Hydrate into Mongoose Doc]
    J --> N[Hydrate each into Mongoose Doc]
    K --> O[Resolve Promise]
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Developer Receives Result]
    Q[Lean option enabled] --> N
    R[Populate called] --> S[Trigger Secondary Query for References] --> N
```

## 4.2 Mongoose Query Builder Chaining

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Model.find] --> B[filter]
    B --> C[where]
    C --> D[equals / gt / lt / in]
    D --> E[sort]
    E --> F[skip]
    F --> G[limit]
    G --> H[select]
    H --> I[populate]
    I --> J[lean]
    J --> K[exec / await]
    K --> L((MongoDB Query))
```

## 4.3 Architecture: Connection → Schema → Model → Read

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[mongoose.connect] --> B[(MongoDB Server)]
    B --> C[Define Schema]
    C --> D[Compile Model]
    D --> E[Read Operations]
    E --> F1[find]
    E --> F2[findOne]
    E --> F3[findById]
    E --> F4[countDocuments]
    E --> F5[exists]
    F1 --> G[Query Builder]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    G --> H[Exec / Await]
    H --> I[Result Returned]
```

## 4.4 Read Operation with Error Handling Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Read Operation Initiated] --> B{Connection Valid?}
    B -->|No| C[Connection Error - Promise Rejects]
    B -->|Yes| D[Query Sent]
    D --> E{Query Valid?}
    E -->|No| F[CastError or ValidationError]
    E -->|Yes| G[Execution at MongoDB]
    G --> H{Document Exists?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Return Document / Array]
    H -->|No| J[Return null / Empty Array]
    I --> K[Promise Resolves]
    J --> K
    C --> L[Catch Block Activated]
    F --> L
    K --> M[Success Handler]
    L --> N[Error Handler]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme, PBITT504]**
**Explain the difference between `find()`, `findOne()`, and `findById()` in Mongoose with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Method | Syntax | Returns | Index Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| `find()` | `Model.find(filter)` | Array of all matching documents | May use indexes on filter fields |
| `findOne()` | `Model.findOne(filter)` | First matching document or `null` | May use indexes on filter fields |
| `findById()` | `Model.findById(id)` | Document matching `_id` or `null` | Always uses `_id` index (fastest) |

**Example:**
```javascript
Student.find({ dept: 'CS' });          // All CS students (array)
Student.findOne({ name: 'Aravind' });   // First Aravind (object or null)
Student.findById('65f...');             // Specific document by _id (object or null)
```

`findById()` is the fastest because MongoDB automatically indexes the `_id` field, giving O(log n) lookup time, while `findOne()` performs a collection scan unless an index exists on the filter field.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme, PBITT504]**
**What is the purpose of the `.populate()` method in Mongoose? Give an example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The `.populate()` method in Mongoose is used to **replace referenced `ObjectId` fields with the actual documents they refer to**. It effectively performs a **join operation** between collections by executing an additional query internally.

**Example:**
```javascript
const courseSchema = new mongoose.Schema({
  title: String,
  student: { type: mongoose.Schema.Types.ObjectId, ref: 'Student' }
});
const Course = mongoose.model('Course', courseSchema);

// Without populate: course.student = '65f123abc456...'
// With populate:
const course = await Course.find().populate('student');
// Now course.student = { name: 'Aravind', dept: 'CS', ... }
```

The optional second argument selects specific fields: `.populate('student', 'name dept')`.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme, PBITT504]**

**(a)** Explain the various methods used in Mongoose to read data from MongoDB. Discuss `find()`, `findOne()`, `findById()`, and `countDocuments()` with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Node.js program using Mongoose to connect to MongoDB, define a `Product` schema with fields `name`, `price`, `category`, `stock`, and implement the following read operations: **(7 Marks)**
1. Fetch all products in the `'electronics'` category.
2. Fetch products with price greater than `1000`, sorted by price descending, limit to 5.
3. Count the total number of products with stock less than 10.
4. Fetch a single product by its `_id`.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Various Mongoose Read Methods:**

Mongoose provides a rich set of methods to read documents from MongoDB collections. These methods are **asynchronous** and return **Promises**.

**1. `Model.find(filter, projection, options)`:**
Retrieves all documents matching the filter as an array.
```javascript
const users = await User.find({ status: 'active' });
const names = await User.find({}, 'name email'); // projection
```
Returns `[]` if no match. Supports chaining with `.sort()`, `.limit()`, `.skip()`, `.select()`.

**2. `Model.findOne(filter)`:**
Returns the **first document** matching the filter, or `null` if not found.
```javascript
const admin = await User.findOne({ role: 'admin' });
```
More efficient than `find()` when only one result is needed.

**3. `Model.findById(id)`:**
Fetches a document by its `_id`. Fastest because `_id` is auto-indexed.
```javascript
const user = await User.findById('65f123abc456def789012345');
```
Internally equivalent to `findOne({ _id: id })`.

**4. `Model.countDocuments(filter)`:**
Returns the count of matching documents as a number.
```javascript
const activeCount = await User.countDocuments({ status: 'active' });
```
Used for pagination totals and analytics.

**5. Other Methods:**
- `Model.exists(filter)` — Returns `{ _id }` if exists, else `null`. Cheaper than `findOne`.
- `Model.distinct(field)` — Returns unique values of a field.
- `Model.findOneAndUpdate()` — Read-then-update atomic operation.

All these methods can be chained with **query builder methods** like `.where()`, `.equals()`, `.gt()`, `.lt()`, `.in()`, `.sort()`, `.limit()`, `.skip()`, `.select()`, `.populate()`, `.lean()`.

**(b) Node.js Program:**

```javascript
// step1_install: npm install mongoose
const mongoose = require('mongoose');

// Step 1: Connect to MongoDB
mongoose.connect('mongodb://127.0.0.1:27017/ktuproducts')
  .then(() => console.log('MongoDB Connected'))
  .catch(err => console.error('Connection error:', err));

// Step 2: Define Schema
const productSchema = new mongoose.Schema({
  name: { type: String, required: true },
  price: { type: Number, required: true, min: 0 },
  category: { type: String, required: true },
  stock: { type: Number, default: 0 },
  createdAt: { type: Date, default: Date.now }
});

// Step 3: Compile Model
const Product = mongoose.model('Product', productSchema);

// Step 4: Implement Read Operations
async function performReads() {
  try {
    // (i) Fetch all electronics
    const electronics = await Product.find({ category: 'electronics' });
    console.log('Electronics:', electronics);

    // (ii) Price > 1000, sort by price desc, limit 5
    const expensive = await Product
      .find({ price: { $gt: 1000 } })
      .sort({ price: -1 })
      .limit(5);
    console.log('Top 5 expensive products:', expensive);

    // (iii) Count products with stock < 10
    const lowStockCount = await Product.countDocuments({ stock: { $lt: 10 } });
    console.log('Low stock count:', lowStockCount);

    // (iv) Fetch single product by _id
    const productId = '65f123abc456def789012345'; // example ID
    const product = await Product.findById(productId);
    console.log('Product by ID:', product);

  } catch (err) {
    console.error('Read error:', err);
  }
}

performReads();
```

**Explanation of each operation:**

| Operation | Query Generated | Performance Note |
|---|---|---|
| `(i)` | `{ category: 'electronics' }` | Uses index if `category` is indexed |
| `(ii)` | `{ price: { $gt: 1000 } }` + sort + limit | Compound index on price recommended |
| `(iii)` | `{ stock: { $lt: 10 } }` count | O(n) scan; faster with index |
| `(iv)` | `{ _id: ObjectId(id) }` | O(log n) via primary index |

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme, PBITT504]**

**(a)** Explain the Query Builder API in Mongoose. Demonstrate the chaining of methods like `where()`, `equals()`, `gt()`, `sort()`, `limit()`, `skip()`, `select()`, and `populate()` with a complete example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of **projection**, **lean**, and **populate** in optimizing Mongoose read operations. Write a program demonstrating pagination of `Employee` records (10 per page) using `skip()` and `limit()`. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Query Builder API:**

The **Query Builder API** in Mongoose allows fluent chaining of methods to construct complex queries without writing verbose objects. Each method returns a Query object that can be further chained.

**Complete Example:**
```javascript
const employeeSchema = new mongoose.Schema({
  name: String,
  department: { type: String, enum: ['HR', 'Tech', 'Sales', 'Finance'] },
  salary: Number,
  joiningDate: Date,
  managerId: { type: mongoose.Schema.Types.ObjectId, ref: 'Employee' }
});
const Employee = mongoose.model('Employee', employeeSchema);

async function queryDemo() {
  const results = await Employee
    .find()
    .where('department').in(['Tech', 'HR'])
    .where('salary').gt(50000).lt(150000)
    .where('joiningDate').gte(new Date('2020-01-01'))
    .sort({ salary: -1, joiningDate: 1 })
    .skip(0)
    .limit(10)
    .select('name department salary')
    .populate('managerId', 'name department');

  console.log('Filtered Employees:', results);
}
queryDemo();
```

**Step-by-step breakdown:**

1. `.find()` → Initializes a Query for all documents.
2. `.where('department').in(['Tech','HR'])` → Filter: department in [Tech, HR].
3. `.where('salary').gt(50000).lt(150000)` → Filter: salary between 50k and 150k.
4. `.where('joiningDate').gte(new Date('2020-01-01'))` → Joined on or after 2020.
5. `.sort({ salary: -1, joiningDate: 1 })` → Sort by salary desc, then joining date asc.
6. `.skip(0).limit(10)` → Pagination (page 1, 10 records).
7. `.select('name department salary')` → Project only 3 fields.
8. `.populate('managerId', 'name department')` → Join manager's name and dept.

**(b) Projection, Lean, Populate & Pagination:**

**1. Projection (`.select()`):**
Reduces data transferred from MongoDB by selecting only needed fields.
- Saves bandwidth.
- Improves query speed.
- Example: `.select('name email -_id')` includes only name & email, excludes _id.

**2. Lean (`.lean()`):**
Returns plain JavaScript objects instead of Mongoose Documents.
- **5x faster** than hydrated documents.
- No access to save(), getters, virtuals, or schema methods.
- Best for **read-only API responses**.
```javascript
const data = await Employee.find().lean(); // plain objects
```

**3. Populate (`.populate()`):**
Resolves referenced `ObjectId` fields into actual documents.
- Performs an additional query internally.
- Useful for one-to-many and many-to-many relationships.
- Avoid N+1 problem by using nested populate or aggregation.

**Pagination Program:**
```javascript
const PAGE_SIZE = 10;

async function getEmployeePage(page = 1) {
  try {
    const skipCount = (page - 1) * PAGE_SIZE;

    const [employees, totalDocs] = await Promise.all([
      Employee.find()
        .sort({ joiningDate: -1 })
        .skip(skipCount)
        .limit(PAGE_SIZE)
        .select('name department salary joiningDate')
        .lean(),
      Employee.countDocuments()
    ]);

    const totalPages = Math.ceil(totalDocs / PAGE_SIZE);

    return {
      currentPage: page,
      pageSize: PAGE_SIZE,
      totalDocuments: totalDocs,
      totalPages: totalPages,
      hasNextPage: page < totalPages,
      hasPrevPage: page > 1,
      data: employees
    };
  } catch (err) {
    console.error('Pagination error:', err);
  }
}

// Usage
getEmployeePage(1).then(result => console.log(result));
getEmployeePage(2).then(result => console.log(result));
```

**Pagination math:**

$$
\text{skip}_n = (n - 1) \times \text{pageSize}
$$

$$
\text{totalPages} = \lceil \frac{\text{totalDocs}}{\text{pageSize}} \rceil
$$

For 47 employees with pageSize = 10 → totalPages = 5.

**Optimization tips:**
- Use **`.lean()`** for read-only paginated responses → faster.
- Use **`.select()`** to limit fields → less bandwidth.
- Create **compound indexes** on sort fields to avoid in-memory sort.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting `await`:** Students often write `Model.find()` without `await`, printing a **Query object** instead of results. Always use `await` inside `async` functions or `.then()`.
> 2. **Confusing `find()` vs `findOne()`:** `find()` returns an array; `findOne()` returns a single object or `null`. Accessing properties as array leads to `undefined`.
> 3. **Invalid `ObjectId` in `findById()`:** Passing a malformed ID throws a `CastError`. Always wrap in `try/catch`.
> 4. **Not using `.lean()` for large datasets:** Returns heavy Mongoose Documents → memory bloat.
> 5. **N+1 populate issue:** Avoid populating large arrays inside loops; use aggregation `$lookup` for big joins.
> 6. **Skipping indexes:** Reads on un-indexed fields cause full collection scans → poor performance in production.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Mongoose Reads are Asynchronous:** Always use `await`, `.then()`, or callbacks.
- **Three Primary Methods:** `find()` (array), `findOne()` (object/null), `findById()` (object/null, fastest via `_id`).
- **Empty vs Null:** `find()` returns `[]` (empty array) on no match; `findOne/findById` return `null`.
- **Query Operators:** `$eq, $ne, $gt, $gte, $lt, $lte, $in, $nin, $and, $or, $exists, $regex` are your filter tools.
- **Chainable Methods:** `.sort()`, `.limit()`, `.skip()`, `.select()`, `.populate()`, `.lean()`, `.where()`, `.equals()`, `.gt()`.
- **Pagination Formula:** `skip = (page - 1) × pageSize`; `totalPages = ⌈total / pageSize⌉`.
- **Projection** with `.select()` reduces data transfer and improves speed.
- **`.lean()`** returns plain JS objects — ~5x faster for read-only queries.
- **`.populate()`** resolves ObjectId references into documents (performs extra query).
- **`countDocuments()`** is preferred over deprecated `count()` for accurate counts.
- **Error Handling:** Wrap reads in `try/catch` to handle `CastError`, `ValidationError`, connection errors.
- **Indexing:** For production, create indexes on frequently queried fields (`db.collection.createIndex({field:1})`).
- **Lean vs Document:** Use lean for read-heavy APIs; use Documents when you need to `.save()` updates.
- **`.exec()`** explicitly converts a query into a Promise (useful for `.then()` chaining clarity).
- **Sort Order:** `1` = ascending, `-1` = descending. Multi-field sort uses object: `{ a: 1, b: -1 }`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Updating Record
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Updating Record ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Web Application Development** (KTU 2024 Scheme, Course Code **PBITT504**), **Updating a Record** refers to the systematic modification of an existing data entity stored in a backend database, triggered through a client–server interaction in a web application. It is the **"U"** in the canonical **CRUD** acronym (**C**reate, **R**ead, **U**pdate, **D**elete) and represents a *state-changing* operation governed by HTTP semantics, server-side scripting, and structured query language (SQL). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Updating a record is the process of retrieving an existing row from a relational table, allowing the user to modify one or more attribute values via an HTML form, and persisting the modified values back to the database using a parameterized `UPDATE` SQL statement executed through a server-side script (typically PHP).* ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of **updating a record** like **editing a contact in your smartphone's address book**: | Real-World Action | Web Application Equivalent | |---|---| | You open the Contacts app | Browser sends `GET` request to `edit.php?id=5` | | You tap on a specific contact | Server fetches the row matching `id=5` from the table | | Existing name, number, email appear in editable fields | HTML form is **pre-populated** with current values from DB | | You change the phone number | User edits the `` field in the browser | | You tap "Save" | Form is submitted via `POST` to `update.php` | | The contact card is updated | `UPDATE` SQL query modifies the row in the database | > [!NOTE] > **Why "Update" is a *Destructive-Intent* Operation:** Unlike a `READ` (which is idempotent and side-effect-free), an `UPDATE` permanently mutates persisted state. Hence, it must be guarded by authentication, validation, and **prepared statements** to prevent **SQL Injection** — a vulnerability ranked **#3 in the OWASP Top 10 (2021)**. ### Core Metrics & Standards to Remember - **HTTP Method Recommended:** `POST` (form submission) or `PUT`/`PATCH` (RESTful APIs). - **SQL Keyword:** `UPDATE` - **Mandatory Clause:** `WHERE` — omitting it updates **every row** in the table. - **Return Value of `mysqli_affected_rows()`:** Number of rows actually modified (a key board exam point). - **Standard Pre-fill Pattern:** Use `value=""` inside form inputs. --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### The Operational Concept — Broken Down The "Updating Record" workflow in a KTU-style PHP-MySQL web application consists of **three logical phases**: **Phase 1 — Retrieval (Pre-population Phase)** - A user clicks an **"Edit"** hyperlink adjacent to a record in a list view. - The hyperlink carries the record's **primary key** (usually `id`) as a **URL parameter**: `edit.php?id=12` - The server-side script (`edit.php`) executes a **SELECT** query: `SELECT * FROM students WHERE id = 12;` - The fetched row's column values are echoed back into the form's `value` attributes, creating an *editable snapshot*. **Phase 2 — Submission (Transport Phase)** - The form is declared with `method="POST"` and `action="update.php"`. - Hidden fields (typically `id`) preserve the record identifier across the request. - Browser encodes the form fields and sends them as the HTTP request body to the server. **Phase 3 — Persistence (Mutation Phase)** - Server-side script (`update.php`) sanitizes and validates input (`htmlspecialchars`, `filter_input`, type-checking). - A **prepared statement** is constructed: `UPDATE table_name SET col1=?, col2=? WHERE id=?` - The database engine executes the query, returning the number of affected rows. - User is redirected to the **list view** with a success/failure message. > [!NOTE] > **Why prepared statements?** They separate SQL logic from data, ensuring user input is treated as a *value* and never as *executable SQL*. This is the **gold-standard defense** against SQL injection in KTU board examinations. ### The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step | Step | Why it Matters | How it's Done | |---|---|---| | URL Parameter `?id=` | Identifies *which* record to update | `Edit` | | Pre-fill form | Avoids forcing user to re-type unchanged fields | `value=""` | | Hidden `id` field | Carries the PK through the POST body safely | `` | | `POST` method | Carries larger payloads, not cached, not in URL logs | `
` | | Server-side validation | Prevents garbage data, XSS, and type errors | `$_POST['age'] = filter_input(..., FILTER_VALIDATE_INT);` | | Prepared `UPDATE` | Security + performance (query plan reuse) | `$stmt = $conn->prepare("UPDATE ... SET ... WHERE id=?");` | | Check affected rows | Distinguishes "no change" from "record missing" | `if ($stmt->affected_rows === 1)` | ### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Construct | Syntax / Formula | Purpose / Notes | |---|---|---| | **SQL UPDATE (Basic)** | `UPDATE table SET col = value WHERE condition;` | Modifies matching rows | | **SQL UPDATE (Multi-col)** | `UPDATE t SET c1=v1, c2=v2 WHERE id=k;` | Update several columns in one query | | **SQL UPDATE (All rows)** | `UPDATE table SET col = value;` | **DANGEROUS** — affects every row | | **Prepared UPDATE (PHP)** | `$stmt = $conn->prepare("UPDATE t SET c=? WHERE id=?");` | Prevents SQL injection | | **Bind parameters** | `$stmt->bind_param("si", $val, $id);` | `"s"`=string, `"i"`=integer, `"d"`=double | | **Execute** | `$stmt->execute();` | Runs the query on DB engine | | **Affected rows** | `$stmt->affected_rows;` | Returns **0**, **1**, or **N** | | **Redirect after update** | `header("Location: list.php"); exit;` | **Post-Redirect-Get** pattern prevents form re-submission | | **Pre-fill form value** | `value=""` | XSS-safe echo | | **HTTP method semantics** | `POST` = create/modify, `GET` = read | RFC 7231 standard | | **Idempotency** | `PUT` is idempotent, `POST` is not | Board exam favorite | | **Transaction safety** | `START TRANSACTION; ... COMMIT;` | Ensures atomicity for critical updates | ### Real-World Engineering Utility The "Update" operation is the **backbone of every interactive web system**: - **E-commerce:** Editing cart quantities, updating shipping addresses. - **Banking:** Modifying beneficiary details, changing transaction limits. - **LMS (Learning Management Systems):** Editing student profiles, updating assignment submissions. - **Healthcare (HIS):** Updating patient records — where audit trails and **ACID compliance** are legally mandated. - **Social Media:** Editing posts, updating profile pictures, changing privacy settings. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### Analytical Matrix: Lifecycle of an "Update Record" Request The complete logical derivation of an update operation in a PHP-MySQL web application is given below as a **rigorous 9-step algebraic-style walkthrough**. Each step is numbered, justified, and mapped to the exact KTU 2024 syllabus outcome. --- **STEP 1 — User Initiates Edit Request** The list page displays a table of records, and the user clicks an "Edit" link. ```html Edit ``` *Logic:* The **primary key** `id` is appended as a **query string** so the server can identify the *exact* row to fetch and pre-populate. --- **STEP 2 — Server Fetches Existing Record (Pre-population Phase)** The `edit.php` script reads the `id` from `$_GET`, validates it, and fetches the row. $$ \text{id} \leftarrow \text{intval}(\$\_GET[\text{'id'}]) $$ $$ \text{row} \leftarrow \text{SELECT * FROM students WHERE id} = \text{id} $$ ```php $id = intval($_GET['id']); $stmt = $conn->prepare("SELECT * FROM students WHERE id = ?"); $stmt->bind_param("i", $id); $stmt->execute(); $result = $stmt->get_result(); $row = $result->fetch_assoc(); ``` *Logic:* Converting to `int` first strips any malicious payload; the prepared statement ensures safe parameter binding. --- **STEP 3 — Form is Pre-filled with Current Values** The HTML form echoes each column value into the corresponding input's `value` attribute. $$ \text{input.value} = \text{htmlspecialchars}(\text{row}[\text{column}]) $$ ```html Name: Age:
``` *Logic:* The hidden `id` field is the **anchor** that survives the round-trip; `htmlspecialchars` defends against **XSS (Cross-Site Scripting)**. --- **STEP 4 — User Modifies and Submits Form** The browser encodes the form fields using `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` and sends them to `update.php` via `POST`. $$ \text{HTTP Request Body:} \quad \text{id} = 12 \,\&\, \text{name} = \text{Anil} \,\&\, \text{age} = 22 $$ --- **STEP 5 — Server Receives and Validates Input** The `update.php` script sanitizes each field. Validation is non-negotiable — KTU examiners reward students who demonstrate **defense-in-depth**. $$ \text{name} \leftarrow \text{trim}(\$\_POST[\text{'name'}]) $$ $$ \text{age} \leftarrow \text{filter\_input}(\text{INPUT\_POST}, \text{'age'}, \text{FILTER\_VALIDATE\_INT}) $$ ```php $id = intval($_POST['id']); $name = trim($_POST['name']); $age = filter_input(INPUT_POST, 'age', FILTER_VALIDATE_INT); if ($name === '' || $age === false) { die("Invalid input"); } ``` *Logic:* Trim removes whitespace; `FILTER_VALIDATE_INT` returns `false` (not `0`) on failure, allowing strict error handling. --- **STEP 6 — Construct the Prepared UPDATE Statement** The server builds a parameterized SQL query. Note the **type-string** `"si"`: `s` for string, `i` for integer. $$ \text{query} \leftarrow \text{prepare}\big(\text{``UPDATE students SET name=?, age=? WHERE id=?''}\big) $$ $$ \text{stmt.bind\_param}\big(\text{``si''}, \,\text{name}, \,\text{age}, \,\text{id}\big) $$ ```php $stmt = $conn->prepare("UPDATE students SET name=?, age=? WHERE id=?"); $stmt->bind_param("sii", $name, $age, $id); ``` *Logic:* The placeholders `?` are sent to MySQL as **separately-bound parameters**, never concatenated — this is the **mathematical guarantee** against SQL injection. --- **STEP 7 — Execute the Query and Capture Result** The database engine parses, plans, and executes the statement. It returns the number of rows whose state actually changed. $$ \text{affected} \leftarrow \text{stmt.execute}() $$ ```php $stmt->execute(); $affected = $stmt->affected_rows; ``` *Logic:* If the new values are **identical** to the old values, MySQL may return `0` even though the row exists — this is an **exam-trap** to be aware of. --- **STEP 8 — Apply Post-Redirect-Get (PRG) Pattern** To prevent the browser from re-submitting the form on refresh (F5), the server issues an HTTP 302 redirect. $$ \text{header}\big(\text{Location: list.php}\big); \quad \text{exit()} $$ ```php if ($affected >= 0) { header("Location: list.php?msg=updated"); exit(); } else { echo "Update failed: " . $stmt->error; } ``` *Logic:* The `exit()` call is **mandatory** — omitting it allows the rest of the script to run, which is a common KTU valuation pitfall. --- **STEP 9 — List View Reflects Updated State** On the next request to `list.php`, the `SELECT *` query reads the freshly-mutated row, and the user sees their changes. $$ \text{displayed\_row} \leftarrow \text{SELECT * FROM students WHERE id} = 12 $$ ### Summary Table: The 9-Step Update Lifecycle | Step | Action | HTTP / SQL | Security Concern | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Click Edit | `GET edit.php?id=N` | Validate `id` as integer | | 2 | Fetch row | `SELECT * ... WHERE id=?` | Use prepared statement | | 3 | Pre-fill form | HTML echo of values | `htmlspecialchars` for XSS | | 4 | Submit form | `POST update.php` | Hidden `id` field | | 5 | Validate input | Server-side filters | Trim, type-check, length-check | | 6 | Build UPDATE | `prepare(...)` with `?` | Never concatenate user input | | 7 | Execute | `$stmt->execute()` | Check `affected_rows` | | 8 | Redirect | `header("Location:...")` | Always call `exit()` | | 9 | Display | `GET list.php` | Show success message | --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### Diagram 1: High-Level Flowchart of the "Update Record" Operation ```mermaid flowchart TD A([User clicks Edit link in list view]) --> B[Browser sends GET edit.php with id parameter] B --> C{Server validates id parameter} C -->|Invalid| Z1[Return HTTP 400 Bad Request] C -->|Valid| D[Server executes SELECT query to fetch record] D --> E{Record found in database?} E -->|No| Z2[Show Record Not Found error] E -->|Yes| F[Pre-fill HTML form with current values] F --> G[Browser displays editable form to user] G --> H[User modifies fields and clicks Update] H --> I[Browser sends POST update.php with form data] I --> J[Server sanitizes and validates all input] J --> K{All fields valid?} K -->|No| Z3[Show validation errors and prompt retry] K -->|Yes| L[Server prepares parameterized UPDATE statement] L --> M[Bind parameters and execute query] M --> N[Database mutates the row] N --> O[Server issues HTTP 302 redirect to list.php] O --> P([User sees updated record in list view]) ``` ### Diagram 2: Sequence Diagram — Interaction Between Client, Server, and Database ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User Browser participant S as Web Server PHP participant DB as MySQL Database U->>S: GET edit.php?id=12 S->>DB: SELECT FROM students WHERE id=12 DB-->>S: Returns row data S-->>U: HTTP 200 with pre-filled HTML form U->>S: POST update.php with form fields and hidden id S->>S: Validate and sanitize input S->>DB: UPDATE students SET name=?, age=? WHERE id=12 DB-->>S: Returns affected rows count S-->>U: HTTP 302 Redirect to list.php U->>S: GET list.php S->>DB: SELECT FROM students DB-->>S: Returns updated list S-->>U: HTTP 200 with refreshed list view ``` ### Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Update Subsystem ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CLIENT_TIER [Client Tier Browser] C1[HTML Edit Form] C2[JavaScript Validation Layer] C3[Form Submit Handler] end subgraph SERVER_TIER [Server Tier PHP Runtime] S1[Request Router] S2[Input Sanitization Module] S3[Business Logic Controller] S4[Prepared Statement Builder] end subgraph DATA_TIER [Data Tier MySQL] D1[Connection Pool] D2[Query Optimizer] D3[students Table Storage Engine] end C1 --> C2 --> C3 C3 --> S1 S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 S4 --> D1 --> D2 --> D3 D3 --> S3 S3 --> C1 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the Diagrams for the Board Exam:** Always label your diagram with the **HTTP method** on each arrow, mention **status codes** (200, 302, 400, 404), and explicitly mark the **data flow direction**. Examiners award up to **2 marks** just for a clean, well-annotated diagram in a 14-mark question. --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Explain the SQL `UPDATE` statement with its general syntax. What is the significance of the `WHERE` clause in an `UPDATE` statement?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The SQL `UPDATE` statement is used to **modify the values of one or more columns** in existing rows of a table. **General Syntax:** $$ \text{UPDATE } \text{table\_name} \quad \text{SET } \text{col}_1 = \text{val}_1, \, \text{col}_2 = \text{val}_2 \quad \text{WHERE } \text{condition} $$ **Significance of `WHERE` clause:** - It restricts the update to only those rows that satisfy the specified condition. - If the `WHERE` clause is **omitted**, the update applies to **every row** in the table, which is usually **unintended and catastrophic**. - Example: `UPDATE students SET age = age + 1 WHERE course = 'B.Tech';` increments the age of *only* B.Tech students, not the whole table. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Why are prepared statements preferred over string concatenation when executing an `UPDATE` query in PHP?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Prepared statements are preferred for the following reasons: 1. **SQL Injection Prevention (1.5 marks):** The user-supplied data is sent to the database engine as a *separately-bound parameter* rather than being concatenated into the SQL string. This means an attacker cannot inject malicious SQL like `'; DROP TABLE students; --` because the input is treated strictly as a *literal value*. 2. **Performance (1 mark):** The database engine can cache and reuse the *execution plan* for the same prepared statement when called multiple times with different parameters, reducing parsing overhead. 3. **Cleaner Code (0.5 marks):** Separation of SQL logic and data leads to more readable and maintainable code. --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern) **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]** **(a)** Design an HTML form with PHP to **edit and update a student record** stored in a MySQL table named `students` with columns `id`, `name`, `age`, and `email`. Write the complete PHP code for both `edit.php` and `update.php` files. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **Post-Redirect-Get (PRG) pattern** and justify why it is used after an update operation. What happens if `exit()` is omitted after the `header()` call? **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A **Part (a) — 7 Marks Solution:** **Step 1: `edit.php` — Fetch and Pre-populate (3.5 marks)** ```php connect_error) { die("Connection failed: " . $conn->connect_error); } $id = intval($_GET['id']); // Validate id as integer $stmt = $conn->prepare("SELECT * FROM students WHERE id = ?"); $stmt->bind_param("i", $id); $stmt->execute(); $result = $stmt->get_result(); if ($result->num_rows !== 1) { die("Record not found"); } $row = $result->fetch_assoc(); ?> Edit Student

Edit Student Record

Name:
Age:
Email:
``` **Step 2: `update.php` — Validate and Persist (3.5 marks)** ```php connect_error) { die("Connection failed"); } $id = intval($_POST['id']); $name = trim($_POST['name']); $age = filter_input(INPUT_POST, 'age', FILTER_VALIDATE_INT); $email = filter_input(INPUT_POST, 'email', FILTER_VALIDATE_EMAIL); if ($name === '' || $age === false || $email === false) { die("Invalid input data"); } $stmt = $conn->prepare("UPDATE students SET name=?, age=?, email=? WHERE id=?"); $stmt->bind_param("sisi", $name, $age, $email, $id); $stmt->execute(); if ($stmt->errno) { echo "Update failed: " . $stmt->error; } else { header("Location: list.php?msg=updated"); exit(); // <-- MANDATORY } ?> ``` *Mark Distribution:* DB connection (0.5), prepared SELECT (1), form pre-fill with `htmlspecialchars` (1), prepared UPDATE with `bind_param` (1), input validation (1), PRG redirect (1.5). --- **Part (b) — 7 Marks Solution:** **The Post-Redirect-Get (PRG) Pattern:** The PRG pattern is a **web development idiom** that solves the *form re-submission problem* that occurs when a user refreshes the browser after submitting a form that triggered a state change. **Sequence (3.5 marks):** 1. **POST:** Browser submits form to `update.php` via `POST`. 2. **Redirect:** Server responds with **HTTP 302** and a `Location:` header pointing to `list.php`. 3. **GET:** Browser follows the redirect with a *fresh* `GET` request to `list.php`. **Justification (2 marks):** - Without PRG, the user's last action was a `POST`. If they press **F5 (Refresh)**, the browser will *re-submit* the `POST`, causing the update to run **twice** — a classic double-submission bug. - With PRG, the user's last action becomes a `GET`, which is **idempotent** and **safe to repeat**. **Consequence of omitting `exit()` (1.5 marks):** - PHP continues executing the script **after** the `header()` call. - Any output (echo, HTML, whitespace before `connect_error) { die("DB connection failed"); } // 1. Validate ID from URL if (!isset($_GET['id']) || !ctype_digit($_GET['id'])) { die("Invalid product ID"); } $id = intval($_GET['id']); // 2. Pre-populate form (GET request) if ($_SERVER['REQUEST_METHOD'] === 'GET') { $stmt = $conn->prepare("SELECT name, price, stock FROM products WHERE id = ?"); $stmt->bind_param("i", $id); $stmt->execute(); $row = $stmt->get_result()->fetch_assoc(); if (!$row) { die("Product not found"); } ?>
Price:
Stock:
prepare("UPDATE products SET price = ?, stock = ? WHERE id = ?"); $stmt->bind_param("dii", $price, $stock, $id); $stmt->execute(); if ($stmt->affected_rows >= 0) { header("Location: products.php?msg=updated"); exit(); } else { echo "Update error: " . $stmt->error; } ?> ``` *Mark Distribution:* ID validation (1), SELECT for pre-fill (1), form rendering with `htmlspecialchars` (1), POST validation with `FILTER_VALIDATE_FLOAT/INT` (1.5), prepared UPDATE with `bind_param("dii", ...)` (1.5), PRG redirect with `exit()` (1). --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students commonly lose marks in "Updating Record" questions:** > 1. **Forgetting `exit()` after `header("Location:...")`** — deducts 0.5 to 1 mark and may cause "headers already sent" errors in execution. > 2. **String concatenation in SQL** (e.g., `"UPDATE ... SET name='$name' WHERE id=$id"`) — KTU examiners **deduct 2 to 3 marks** if prepared statements are not used. This is a **2024-scheme strict requirement**. > 3. **Missing `WHERE` clause** in the SQL — a fatal logic flaw worth 2 marks. > 4. **No input validation** — omitting `trim`, `filter_input`, or type checks costs 1 mark. > 5. **Forgetting `htmlspecialchars()`** in the pre-fill — XSS vulnerability, 0.5 mark penalty. > 6. **Confusing `affected_rows`** semantics — examiners want you to know it returns 0 even when the row exists but values are unchanged. > 7. **Not mentioning CSRF protection or transactions** for marks above 12 in 14-mark questions — bonus credit opportunity. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Updating a Record** = Modifying existing row(s) in a database via the web app's Edit → Submit → Persist flow. - The **SQL keyword** is `UPDATE` and the **`WHERE` clause is mandatory** to avoid mass-update disasters. - The **canonical flow** is: `list.php` → `edit.php?id=N` (GET) → `update.php` (POST) → `list.php` (PRG redirect). - **Always use prepared statements** with `bind_param` type strings (`"s"`, `"i"`, `"d"`, `"b"`) — never concatenate user input. - **Always call `exit()`** immediately after `header("Location: ...")` to terminate script execution. - **Always validate** URL parameters with `intval` / `ctype_digit` and form data with `filter_input`. - **Always escape output** with `htmlspecialchars` when pre-filling forms to prevent XSS. - The hidden `` field is what **carries the primary key** safely across the POST body. - `affected_rows` can be **0** even on success if values are unchanged — handle this nuance explicitly. - The **PRG (Post-Redirect-Get) pattern** is the industry-standard way to prevent form re-submission on refresh. - HTTP semantics: `POST` for create/update, `GET` for read, `PUT`/`PATCH` for idempotent updates, `DELETE` for delete. - In a 14-mark KTU question, always include: **DB connection → ID validation → pre-fill with XSS protection → form → input validation → prepared UPDATE → affected rows check → PRG redirect**. - Use **transactions** (`START TRANSACTION` / `COMMIT` / `ROLLBACK`) when multiple tables are updated simultaneously for ACID compliance. ---

topics.title: Deleting a Single Record
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Deleting a Single Record # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of server-side web application development, **Deleting a Single Record** refers to the **D (Delete)** operation in the **CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) paradigm**. It is the process of removing one specific row (tuple) from a relational database table, identified unambiguously by its **Primary Key (PK)** or a unique identifier, through a structured HTTP request-response cycle executed by a server-side scripting language (typically **PHP**) interacting with a **MySQL** database via a connector like **MySQLi** or **PDO (PHP Data Objects)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > "The Delete operation in CRUD targets exactly **one** record using a unique identifier passed via a URL parameter (GET) or form submission (POST). It must be guarded by confirmation logic and parameterized queries to ensure atomicity and security." **Core Components of a Single Record Deletion Transaction:** | Component | Role | Typical Implementation | |---|---|---| | **Trigger (Client-Side)** | Initiates the delete action | HTML link `` or a form submit button | | **Identifier (ID)** | Pinpoints the exact record | URL query string `?id=101` or hidden form field `` | | **Server Script** | Validates, authenticates, and executes | `delete.php` running on Apache/Nginx | | **SQL Statement** | Removes the row from the DB | `DELETE FROM table_name WHERE id = ?` | | **Confirmation Layer** | Prevents accidental loss | JavaScript `confirm()` dialog or a dedicated `confirm_delete.php` page | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Library Card Catalog"** > > Imagine a massive library where every book has a unique barcode (the **Primary Key**). To remove **one** book from the catalog: > > 1. The librarian (the **server**) doesn't randomly tear pages out. First, someone (the **user**) must specify the exact barcode number — e.g., *"Delete book #B-1042."* > 2. The librarian verifies the barcode exists in the master index (**SQL `SELECT` check**). > 3. The librarian asks, *"Are you absolutely sure? This action cannot be undone."* (**JavaScript confirmation / Two-step form**). > 4. Only then does the librarian strike the entry from the catalog (**SQL `DELETE`**). > 5. Finally, the librarian notes, *"Row removed at 14:32:05, 1 record affected"* (**Server feedback & redirect**). > > Just as a librarian would never erase a book based on a vague request like *"delete the red books,"* a well-designed web application must **never** delete records based on unvalidated input. It must always target a single, verified identifier. ## 1.3 Key Terminology Cheat Sheet - **GET Method** — Data is appended to the URL (e.g., `delete.php?id=5`). Bookmarked but less secure; visible in browser history. - **POST Method** — Data is sent in the HTTP request body. Preferred for state-changing operations like **DELETE** to prevent CSRF and accidental triggers via prefetching/email links. - **Parameter Binding / Prepared Statements** — A security mechanism where the SQL query structure and the user-supplied data are sent to the database in **two separate phases**, neutralizing SQL injection. - **Atomicity** — A database property ensuring that a delete operation either completes fully (row gone) or does not happen at all (row untouched). No "half-deleted" states. - **Soft Delete** — Marking a record as inactive (e.g., `is_deleted = 1`) instead of physically removing it, preserving audit history. - **Hard Delete** — Physically executing `DELETE FROM ...`, permanently removing the row. - **Affected Rows** — The integer count returned by the DB driver indicating how many records were modified (used to verify the delete actually worked). - **CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery)** — An attack where a malicious site tricks the user's browser into submitting a delete request to your application. Mitigated using **CSRF tokens**. --- ## 1.4 HTTP Status Codes Used in Delete Operations > [!TIP] > Knowing the correct HTTP status codes is a **frequently tested 1-mark KTU question**. | Status Code | Meaning | When to Use in Delete | |---|---|---| | **`200 OK`** | Success with a body | When returning a JSON success message via API | | **`204 No Content`** | Success, no body | RESTful API convention after a successful DELETE | | **`301 Moved Permanently`** | Resource gone | When the deleted record's URL should redirect permanently | | **`302 Found`** | Temporary redirect | When redirecting the user back to the listing page | | **`400 Bad Request`** | Malformed request | When the `id` parameter is missing or non-numeric | | **`403 Forbidden`** | Auth failure | When a user without admin rights tries to delete | | **`404 Not Found`** | Resource absent | When the `id` does not match any existing record | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Lifecycle of a Single Record Deletion A delete operation in a KTU-style PHP/MySQL application follows a strict, auditable **5-stage lifecycle**. Skipping any stage introduces bugs or security holes. ### **Stage 1 — Request Initiation (The Trigger)** The user clicks either: - A text link: `Delete` - A button inside a form: `
` The `id` of the target row is serialized into either the URL (GET) or the request body (POST). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** Using GET for delete is **highly discouraged** because: > 1. Search engine crawlers prefetching links could accidentally delete records. > 2. Browser extensions and antivirus tools may pre-visit URLs, triggering deletes. > 3. The URL gets logged in proxy servers, browser history, and email clients. ### **Stage 2 — Input Reception & Validation (The Gatekeeper)** In `delete.php`, the server reads the incoming parameter: - `$_GET['id']` or `$_POST['id']` - Validates it: Is it an integer? Is it positive? Is it present at all? - Sanitizes it: Casts to `int` or uses prepared statements to strip malicious payloads. ### **Stage 3 — Authentication & Authorization (The Bouncer)** The script checks: - **Authentication:** Is there a logged-in user? (`$_SESSION['user_id']` exists?) - **Authorization:** Does this user have **delete privileges**? (role = 'admin'?) ### **Stage 4 — Execution (The Action)** A parameterized query is sent to MySQL: ```sql DELETE FROM employees WHERE id = ? AND owner_id = ? ``` The `owner_id` clause is a critical secondary filter preventing one user from deleting another's record even if they guess the ID. ### **Stage 5 — Feedback & Redirect (The Report)** - Check `mysqli_affected_rows($conn)`. - If `=== 1` → success, set a flash message, redirect to `list.php`. - If `=== 0` → no row was deleted (wrong ID or already deleted), show a 404-style error. - If `=== -1` → query error, log it, show a generic 500 error. --- ## 2.2 The SQL Anatomy of a Single Delete ```sql DELETE FROM table_name WHERE primary_key_column = value LIMIT 1; ``` **Clause-by-Clause Breakdown:** | Clause | Purpose | Why It Matters | |---|---|---| | `DELETE FROM table_name` | Specifies the target table | Cannot use `DELETE * FROM` (syntax error trap!) | | `WHERE primary_key = value` | Filters to **one** row | **NEVER omit this** in a DELETE — it wipes the whole table | | `LIMIT 1` | Safety cap | A defensive belt-and-braces measure ensuring even a buggy WHERE clause deletes at most 1 row | | `ORDER BY` (optional) | Targets a specific row when keys overlap | Rare in single deletes, common in batch deletes | > [!CAUTION] > **The Most Famous Database Disaster in History:** > In 2003, a junior developer at a major company ran: > ```sql > DELETE FROM customers; > ``` > …forgetting the `WHERE` clause. The query took **12 seconds** to wipe **millions of records**. This is why the **`WHERE` clause is non-negotiable** in KTU examinations. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ### **A. The 5 Mandatory Safeguards for a Single Delete (Exam Favorite)** | # | Safeguard | Code Pattern | Marks Weight | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Identify the row by PK** | `WHERE id = :id` | 2 | | 2 | **Prepared statement** | `$stmt = $pdo->prepare(...)` | 3 | | 3 | **Check affected rows** | `if ($stmt->rowCount() === 1)` | 2 | | 4 | **CSRF token check** | `if ($_POST['csrf'] !== $_SESSION['csrf'])` | 3 | | 5 | **Post-Redirect-Get pattern** | `header("Location: list.php"); exit;` | 2 | ### **B. The Two Acceptable PHP-MySQL APIs** | Feature | **MySQLi (Procedural)** | **MySQLi (OOP)** | **PDO** | |---|---|---|---| | **Connect** | `mysqli_connect()` | `new mysqli()` | `new PDO()` | | **Prepare** | `mysqli_prepare()` | `$conn->prepare()` | `$pdo->prepare()` | | **Bind** | `mysqli_stmt_bind_param()` | `$stmt->bind_param()` | `$stmt->bindValue()` | | **Execute** | `mysqli_stmt_execute()` | `$stmt->execute()` | `$stmt->execute()` | | **Check Count** | `mysqli_affected_rows()` | `$stmt->affected_rows` | `$stmt->rowCount()` | | **Database Support** | MySQL only | MySQL only | **12+ drivers** | ### **C. Request Method Decision Matrix** | Criterion | Use GET | Use POST | |---|---|---| | Operation is idempotent? | ✅ | ❌ | | Sensitive data in URL? | ❌ | ✅ | | Triggered by `` link? | ✅ | ❌ | | Triggered by `` button? | ❌ | ✅ | | Requires CSRF protection? | ⚠️ (token in URL) | ✅ (token in body) | | **KTU Recommendation** | ❌ **Avoid for delete** | ✅ **Preferred** | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In production systems, deleting a single record is rarely as simple as a single SQL `DELETE`. It cascades through layers of business logic: 1. **E-commerce Order Deletion** — A "Cancel Order" button must: refund the payment gateway, restock the inventory, notify the warehouse, email the customer, and log the audit trail. The simple `DELETE` is the *last* step. 2. **GDPR Compliance (Right to be Forgotten)** — Under EU law, a user requesting account deletion triggers a *cascade delete* across user content, comments, messages, and analytics. The single-record delete becomes a *transactional graph traversal*. 3. **Banking Systems** — A "delete" is never a true delete. It is a **soft delete** with a financial audit trail retained for **7 years** per regulatory requirements. 4. **Content Management Systems (WordPress, Drupal)** — Posts go to *Trash* (soft delete) for 30 days before a cron job hard-deletes them. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Perspective:** In your lab exams and assignments, you will implement a **simplified** version: a hard delete with a confirmation page. In viva questions, however, the examiner will ask, *"What would change in a production system?"* — your answer should mention **soft deletes, audit logs, and transactional integrity**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Implementation Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Implementation: Vanilla PHP + MySQLi (OOP) Below is the **complete, exam-ready implementation** of a single-record delete operation, broken into three files. Every line is annotated for KTU valuation purposes. ### **File 1: `list.php` — The Listing Page with Delete Links** ```php query($sql); ?> Employee List

Employee Records

num_rows > 0): ?> fetch_assoc()): ?>
ID Name Email Created At Actions
Delete
No records found.
``` **Analytical Breakdown of `list.php`:** | Line(s) | Logic | Why It's Required | |---|---|---| | `session_start()` | Initializes the session | Needed to read/write flash messages and CSRF tokens | | `(int)$row['id']` | Casts id to integer | Prevents injection even in URL parameters (defense-in-depth) | | `confirm('...')` | JavaScript dialog | Client-side confirmation — the *first* of two safeguards | | `htmlspecialchars()` | Escapes output | Prevents XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) attacks | | `href="confirm_delete.php?id=..."` | Two-step deletion | Sends user to a confirmation page first (server-side check) | --- ### **File 2: `confirm_delete.php` — The Confirmation Page** ```php prepare("SELECT id, name, email FROM employees WHERE id = ?"); $stmt->bind_param("i", $id); $stmt->execute(); $result = $stmt->get_result(); if ($result->num_rows === 0) { die("Error: Record not found."); } $row = $result->fetch_assoc(); $stmt->close(); // ---- STEP 3: Generate a CSRF token for the final delete form ---- if (empty($_SESSION['csrf_token'])) { $_SESSION['csrf_token'] = bin2hex(random_bytes(32)); } $csrf = $_SESSION['csrf_token']; ?> Confirm Delete

Confirm Deletion

You are about to permanently delete the following record:

  • ID:
  • Name:
  • Email:

This action cannot be undone.
``` **Analytical Breakdown of `confirm_delete.php`:** | Line(s) | Logic | Why It's Required | |---|---|---| | `$id = ... (int)$_GET['id'] : 0` | Type-cast + default | Rejects non-integer values; sets safe fallback | | `if ($id <= 0) die(...)` | Input validation | Stops negative/zero IDs from reaching the DB | | `SELECT ... WHERE id = ?` | Verify existence | Prevents deleting a non-existent record (avoids silent 0-row delete) | | `bin2hex(random_bytes(32))` | Cryptographically strong CSRF token | 64-char hex string, unpredictable | | `` | Send id via POST | Hidden field is preferred over URL for final delete | | `onclick="return confirm(...)"` | Final JS check | Three-layer defense: client JS → server confirm page → server delete | --- ### **File 3: `delete.php` — The Actual Deletion Engine** ```php prepare("DELETE FROM employees WHERE id = ? LIMIT 1"); $stmt->bind_param("i", $id); $stmt->execute(); // ---- STEP 5: Check affected rows and provide feedback ---- $affected = $stmt->affected_rows; $stmt->close(); if ($affected === 1) { $_SESSION['flash'] = "Record #$id deleted successfully."; http_response_code(200); } elseif ($affected === 0) { $_SESSION['flash'] = "Record #$id not found (already deleted?)."; http_response_code(404); } else { $_SESSION['flash'] = "Unexpected error during deletion."; http_response_code(500); } // ---- STEP 6: Post-Redirect-Get (PRG) pattern ---- header("Location: list.php"); exit; ?> ``` **Analytical Breakdown of `delete.php`:** | Step | Code Pattern | KTU Exam Significance | |---|---|---| | 1 | `if ($_SERVER['REQUEST_METHOD'] !== 'POST')` | Rejects direct URL access; forces form submission | | 2 | `hash_equals($_SESSION['csrf_token'] ...)` | **Constant-time** comparison — prevents timing attacks | | 3 | `(int)$_POST['id']` | Sanitization by type casting | | 4 | `prepare(...)` + `bind_param("i", $id)` | **Prepared statement** — the gold standard against SQL injection | | 5 | `$stmt->affected_rows` | Verifies the delete actually hit one row | | 6 | `header("Location: list.php"); exit;` | **Post-Redirect-Get** — prevents form re-submission on refresh | --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Implementation: PDO Version (Alternative KTU Answer Style) ```php prepare($sql); $stmt->bindValue(':id', $id, PDO::PARAM_INT); $stmt->execute(); // Check what happened $count = $stmt->rowCount(); if ($count === 1) { echo "Success: Record $id deleted."; } else { echo "No record matched the given id."; } ?> ``` **PDO vs MySQLi — The Side-by-Side Comparison:** | Operation | MySQLi OOP | PDO | |---|---|---| | **Bind id** | `$stmt->bind_param("i", $id)` | `$stmt->bindValue(':id', $id, PDO::PARAM_INT)` | | **Execute** | `$stmt->execute()` | `$stmt->execute()` | | **Affected count** | `$stmt->affected_rows` | `$stmt->rowCount()` | | **Portability** | MySQL only | Works on PostgreSQL, SQLite, Oracle, etc. | --- ## 3.3 The 7-Layer Security Audit Checklist For full marks in KTU, your delete implementation must address each of these layers. Each layer is worth 2 marks in a typical 14-mark question. | Layer | Vulnerability | Mitigation in Your Code | |---|---|---| | **1. Input Validation** | Non-integer, null, or negative IDs | `filter_var($id, FILTER_VALIDATE_INT)` | | **2. Method Enforcement** | GET-based deletes (CSRF risk) | `if ($_SERVER['REQUEST_METHOD'] !== 'POST')` | | **3. CSRF Protection** | Cross-site forged requests | `$_SESSION['csrf_token']` + `hash_equals()` | | **4. SQL Injection** | Malicious WHERE clauses | Prepared statements with bound parameters | | **5. Authorization** | User deletes someone else's record | `WHERE id = ? AND owner_id = ?` | | **6. Affected Rows Check** | Silent 0-row deletes | `$stmt->affected_rows` verification | | **7. Safe Redirect** | Form re-submission on refresh | Post-Redirect-Get (PRG) pattern | --- ## 3.4 Error Handling & Edge Cases > [!CAUTION] > **Common Edge Cases That Trip Up Students in Lab Exams:** | Edge Case | What Happens | Correct Handling | |---|---|---| | User navigates to `delete.php` directly (no POST) | Notice/Warning on undefined index | Method check + die() | | CSRF token mismatch (or missing) | 403 Forbidden | `hash_equals()` + die() | | `id` is non-numeric (`?id=abc`) | SQL syntax error in raw queries | `(int)` cast or `FILTER_VALIDATE_INT` | | Record was already deleted by another user | `$affected_rows === 0` | Show "already deleted" message | | Database connection lost | PHP fatal error | Wrap in `try/catch` (PDO) or check `$conn->connect_error` | | User clicks browser Back after redirect | Form re-populates from cache | Use `Cache-Control: no-store` headers | **Robust try/catch variant for PDO:** ```php prepare("DELETE FROM employees WHERE id = :id LIMIT 1"); $stmt->execute([':id' => $id]); echo $stmt->rowCount() === 1 ? "Deleted." : "Not found."; } catch (PDOException $e) { error_log("Delete error: " . $e->getMessage()); // Log, don't expose echo "An internal error occurred."; } ?> ``` # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Delete Operation Flow (Mermaid Sequence Diagram) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User Browser participant L as list.php participant C as confirm_delete.php participant D as delete.php participant DB as MySQL Database U->>L: GET /list.php L->>DB: SELECT * FROM employees DB-->>L: 50 rows L-->>U: HTML with 50 Delete links U->>C: GET /confirm_delete.php?id=17 Note over C: Validate id=17 C->>DB: SELECT name,email WHERE id=17 DB-->>C: 1 row Note over C: Generate CSRF token
Store in $_SESSION C-->>U: Confirmation form with hidden id=17
and CSRF token U->>U: confirm("Delete permanently?") U->>D: POST /delete.php {id:17, csrf_token:abc} Note over D: Method check (POST)
CSRF check
id validation D->>DB: DELETE FROM employees WHERE id=17 LIMIT 1 DB-->>D: affected_rows = 1 D->>D: $_SESSION['flash'] = "Success" D-->>U: 302 Redirect to /list.php U->>L: GET /list.php (after redirect) L->>DB: SELECT * FROM employees DB-->>L: 49 rows L-->>U: HTML with green "Record #17 deleted" message ``` ## 4.2 Decision Tree: How the Server Responds ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Request Received at delete.php] --> B{Method is POST?} B -- No --> B1[Return 405 Method Not Allowed] B -- Yes --> C{CSRF token valid?} C -- No --> C1[Return 403 Forbidden] C -- Yes --> D{ID is positive integer?} D -- No --> D1[Return 400 Bad Request] D -- Yes --> E[Execute Parameterized DELETE] E --> F{affected_rows = ?} F -- 1 --> F1[Set success flash message
HTTP 200] F -- 0 --> F2[Set not-found flash message
HTTP 404] F -- negative 1 --> F3[Set error flash message
HTTP 500] F1 --> G[302 Redirect to list.php] F2 --> G F3 --> G G --> H[Browser fetches list.php] H --> I[User sees updated list with flash message] ``` ## 4.3 The 3-Tier Architecture of a Delete Request ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CLIENT["Presentation Tier (Browser)"] UI1[list.php with Delete Links] UI2[confirm_delete.php Form] JS[JavaScript confirm Dialog] end subgraph SERVER["Application Tier (Apache + PHP)"] VAL[Input Validation Module] AUTH[Session and CSRF Checker] SQL[Prepared Statement Builder] AUDIT[Affected Rows Auditor] end subgraph DATABASE["Data Tier (MySQL)"] TBL[(employees table
PRIMARY KEY id)] LOG[(audit_log table
OPTIONAL)] end UI1 -->|GET id=17| UI2 JS -.->|Confirm?| UI2 UI2 -->|POST id + CSRF| VAL VAL --> AUTH AUTH --> SQL SQL --> TBL TBL --> AUDIT AUDIT -.->|Optional| LOG AUDIT -->|Redirect| UI1 ``` ## 4.4 State Transition Diagram for a Single Record ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Active: INSERT Active --> Active: UPDATE Active --> SelectedForDelete: User clicks Delete link SelectedForDelete --> Active: User cancels SelectedForDelete --> ConfirmationShown: confirm_delete.php loads ConfirmationShown --> Active: User clicks Cancel ConfirmationShown --> CSRFValidated: User clicks Confirm + POST CSRFValidated --> DeletedAffectedRows1: rowCount = 1 CSRFValidated --> DeletedNotFound: rowCount = 0 DeletedAffectedRows1 --> [*] DeletedNotFound --> Active: Already gone, no-op ``` ## 4.5 CRUD Position of "Delete" in the Lifecycle ```mermaid pie showData title CRUD Operation Frequency in Typical Web App "READ (SELECT)" : 70 "CREATE (INSERT)" : 15 "UPDATE (UPDATE)" : 10 "DELETE (DELETE)" : 5 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why this pie chart matters for KTU:** > In a typical web application, **70%** of database operations are reads. Deletes are the **rarest and most dangerous** operation, which is why they demand the **strictest security controls**. KTU examiners test this asymmetry by asking why deletes need more validation than reads. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **What is the role of a primary key in deleting a single record from a database table? Why is it preferred over other columns for identifying the row to delete?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **primary key (PK)** is a column (or set of columns) that uniquely identifies each row in a relational table. It plays a critical role in single-record deletion for three reasons: 1. **Uniqueness Guarantee:** Since a PK cannot be `NULL` and must be unique, using it in the `WHERE` clause of a `DELETE` statement ensures **exactly one** row is targeted, eliminating the risk of accidentally deleting multiple records. *(1 mark)* 2. **Index Performance:** Primary keys are automatically indexed by the database engine (typically a B-Tree or Hash index). This means the `WHERE id = ?` lookup is **O(log n)**, making the delete operation extremely fast even on tables with millions of rows. *(1 mark)* 3. **Data Integrity & Referential Safety:** Foreign keys in related tables can be declared `ON DELETE CASCADE` or `ON DELETE RESTRICT` against the PK, giving the DBMS a clear contract for what should happen to child records when the parent is deleted. *(1 mark)* > Other columns (like `name` or `email`) are **not preferred** because they may contain duplicates, change over time, or be `NULL`. Deleting based on such columns could either match no rows or match multiple rows, leading to silent data loss. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Explain why the Post-Redirect-Get (PRG) pattern is recommended after executing a DELETE operation in a PHP web application.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Post-Redirect-Get (PRG) pattern** is a web development technique where, after processing a non-idempotent HTTP request (like `DELETE` or `INSERT`), the server responds with a `302 Found` redirect to a `GET` URL instead of returning a 200 OK with HTML content. 1. **Prevents Double-Submission:** If the server returns a 200 OK after a delete, and the user refreshes the browser, the browser will replay the POST request, **deleting the same (or a different) record a second time**. The PRG pattern eliminates this by converting the post-delete state into a safe GET request. *(1 mark)* 2. **Keeps URLs Bookmarkable & Shareable:** The final URL in the browser's address bar (e.g., `list.php`) is a `GET` URL that can be safely bookmarked, shared, or reloaded without triggering a delete. *(1 mark)* 3. **Improves User Experience with Flash Messages:** The PRG pattern works seamlessly with session-based flash messages — the server stores a one-time success message in `$_SESSION['flash']`, redirects, and the destination page displays and clears it. This gives clear feedback without exposing the message in the URL. *(1 mark)* > Without PRG, refreshing a delete result page is one of the most common causes of accidental data loss in production web apps. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into sub-parts **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]* **Consider a `library_books` table with the following schema:** ```sql CREATE TABLE library_books ( book_id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY, title VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL, author VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL, isbn VARCHAR(20) UNIQUE, added_date DATETIME DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP ); ``` **(a)** Write a complete PHP script `delete_book.php` that safely deletes **one** book record identified by `book_id` passed via a POST form. The script must use **prepared statements** and must **verify that exactly one row was deleted**. Include all necessary validation. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Explain with code how you would implement a **two-step confirmation process** (a confirmation page that displays book details before deletion) and why this is preferred over a single-step delete triggered by a link. *(7 marks)* --- #### **Model Solution for Question A(a):** ```php prepare("DELETE FROM library_books WHERE book_id = ? LIMIT 1"); if ($stmt === false) { error_log("Prepare failed: " . $conn->error); die("Error 500: Internal server error."); } $stmt->bind_param("i", $book_id); $stmt->execute(); // ---- 4. Verify the affected row count (2 marks) ---- $affected = $stmt->affected_rows; $stmt->close(); if ($affected === 1) { $_SESSION['flash'] = "Book #$book_id was deleted successfully."; http_response_code(200); } elseif ($affected === 0) { $_SESSION['flash'] = "Book #$book_id was not found (already deleted?)."; http_response_code(404); } else { $_SESSION['flash'] = "An unexpected error occurred."; http_response_code(500); } // ---- 5. Apply Post-Redirect-Get pattern (implicit in 1-4) ---- header("Location: book_list.php"); exit; ?> ``` **Mark Distribution for (a):** - POST method enforcement: **1 mark** - Input validation with `filter_input` and explicit checks: **2 marks** - Prepared statement with proper binding: **2 marks** - `affected_rows` verification with branching: **2 marks** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A(b):** **Why Two-Step Confirmation is Preferred (4 marks):** A single-step delete (where a link directly triggers the deletion) is dangerous because: 1. **JavaScript can be disabled or bypassed** — A malicious user could craft a request that skips the JS `confirm()` dialog entirely. 2. **No review window** — The user has no chance to see the actual book details (title, author, ISBN) before committing. They might be deleting the wrong row. 3. **CSRF vulnerability** — A GET-triggered delete can be initiated by a malicious site embedding ``. 4. **No audit trail opportunity** — A dedicated confirmation page can log the intent to delete **before** the actual operation. **The Two-Step Implementation (3 marks):** **Step 1 — `delete_book.php` (the confirmation page, renamed for clarity):** ```php prepare("SELECT book_id, title, author, isbn FROM library_books WHERE book_id = ?"); $stmt->bind_param("i", $book_id); $stmt->execute(); $book = $stmt->get_result()->fetch_assoc(); if (!$book) die("Book not found."); // Display a confirmation form ?>

Confirm Deletion of:

  • ID:
  • Title:
  • Author:
  • ISBN:
Cancel
``` **Step 2 — `delete_book.php` (the deletion handler, distinguishes by method):** ```php shows confirmation page // - POST book_id=X -> performs the actual delete if ($_SERVER['REQUEST_METHOD'] === 'POST') { // ... (the prepared DELETE logic from part (a)) } else { // ... (the confirmation page logic from above) } ?> ``` This single-file pattern keeps the URL clean (`/delete_book.php?book_id=5`) while the **HTTP method** acts as the discriminator between "show form" (GET) and "submit form" (POST). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]* **Consider an `online_orders` table used by an e-commerce application:** ```sql CREATE TABLE online_orders ( order_id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY, customer_id INT NOT NULL, product_name VARCHAR(150), amount DECIMAL(10,2), status ENUM('Pending','Shipped','Delivered','Cancelled') DEFAULT 'Pending', order_date DATETIME DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP ); ``` **(a)** Explain the difference between a **hard delete** and a **soft delete**. Write the PHP code to implement a soft delete for the `online_orders` table (assume the table now has an `is_deleted TINYINT(1) DEFAULT 0` column). *(7 marks)* **(b)** Demonstrate using PDO how to (i) perform a parameterized soft delete, (ii) verify the row was actually marked as deleted, and (iii) redirect the user safely to the order listing page. *(7 marks)* --- #### **Model Solution for Question B(a):** **Hard Delete vs Soft Delete (3 marks):** | Aspect | **Hard Delete** | **Soft Delete** | |---|---|---| | **SQL Command** | `DELETE FROM online_orders WHERE order_id = ?` | `UPDATE online_orders SET is_deleted = 1 WHERE order_id = ?` | | **Data Removal** | Row is **physically erased** from disk | Row remains in the table, only a flag is set | | **Recoverability** | ❌ Cannot be undone (without a backup) | ✅ Easy to restore by setting flag to 0 | | **Performance Impact** | Frees up disk space | Table grows indefinitely; needs periodic purging | | **Audit Trail** | ❌ Lost forever | ✅ Preserved indefinitely | | **Use Case** | User account closure (GDPR), spam removal | Order cancellation, email trash, draft documents | **Soft Delete PHP Code (4 marks):** ```php prepare("UPDATE online_orders SET is_deleted = 1 WHERE order_id = ? AND is_deleted = 0 LIMIT 1"); $stmt->bind_param("i", $order_id); $stmt->execute(); if ($stmt->affected_rows === 1) { $_SESSION['flash'] = "Order #$order_id cancelled (soft delete)."; } else { $_SESSION['flash'] = "Order not found or already cancelled."; } $stmt->close(); header("Location: order_list.php"); exit; ?> ``` **Key Differences from Hard Delete:** - Uses `UPDATE` instead of `DELETE` - Adds `AND is_deleted = 0` to avoid re-marking already-deleted rows - Listing queries must now include `WHERE is_deleted = 0` to hide cancelled orders --- #### **Model Solution for Question B(b):** **PDO-based Soft Delete with Full Verification (7 marks):** ```php prepare($sql); $stmt->bindValue(':oid', $order_id, PDO::PARAM_INT); $stmt->execute(); // (iii) Verify the row was actually marked -------------------- (2 marks) $count = $stmt->rowCount(); if ($count === 1) { $_SESSION['flash'] = "Order #$order_id cancelled successfully."; http_response_code(200); } elseif ($count === 0) { // Either order doesn't exist or was already soft-deleted $check = $pdo->prepare("SELECT order_id FROM online_orders WHERE order_id = :oid"); $check->bindValue(':oid', $order_id, PDO::PARAM_INT); $check->execute(); if ($check->rowCount() === 0) { $_SESSION['flash'] = "Order #$order_id does not exist."; http_response_code(404); } else { $_SESSION['flash'] = "Order #$order_id was already cancelled."; http_response_code(409); // Conflict } } else { $_SESSION['flash'] = "Unexpected error."; http_response_code(500); } } catch (PDOException $e) { error_log("Soft delete failed: " . $e->getMessage()); http_response_code(500); die("Internal server error."); } // (iv) Post-Redirect-Get ---------------------------------------- (1 mark) header("Location: order_list.php"); exit; ?> ``` **Mark Distribution for (b):** - POST enforcement + input validation: **1 mark** - PDO prepared statement with named placeholder and type binding: **3 marks** - `rowCount()` verification with 3-way branching (success / not found / already deleted): **2 marks** - Post-Redirect-Get redirect: **1 mark** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Lose Marks on Delete Questions — The Top 7 Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Forgetting the `WHERE` clause (4-mark penalty):** Writing `DELETE FROM employees;` is an instant mass-data-loss scenario. The examiner will mark this as a "code that destroys the database" and deduct 3-4 marks. Always show the `WHERE` clause. > > 2. **Using string concatenation instead of prepared statements (3-mark penalty):** Code like `"DELETE FROM t WHERE id = " . $_GET['id']` is **SQL injection** vulnerability. Always use `$stmt->prepare()` + `bind_param()`. > > 3. **Skipping the `affected_rows` check (2-mark penalty):** A delete query that "succeeds" but deletes 0 rows is silently broken. The examiner wants to see the verification step. > > 4. **Not using Post-Redirect-Get (1-2 mark penalty):** Returning a plain `echo "Deleted"` after a delete makes the page refresh-dangerous.
