Always `header("Location: ...")`. > > 5. **Using GET for delete (2-mark penalty):** Modern KTU rubrics penalize GET-triggered deletes because of CSRF risk. Use POST. > > 6. **No CSRF protection in advanced questions (3-mark penalty):** For 14-mark questions, the absence of a CSRF token mechanism is a significant deduction. > > 7. **Wrong `rowCount()` vs `num_rows()` (1-mark penalty):** For write operations (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE), use `$stmt->affected_rows` (MySQLi) or `$stmt->rowCount()` (PDO). `num_rows()` is **only** for SELECT result sets. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print This Before Your Exam!** ### **🔑 Core Concepts** - [x] A single-record delete targets **one** row using its **primary key** in a `WHERE` clause. - [x] The minimum SQL is `DELETE FROM table WHERE pk = ? LIMIT 1;`. - [x] The `LIMIT 1` clause is a **defensive safety net** against buggy WHERE clauses. - [x] Omitting the `WHERE` clause in a `DELETE` is a **catastrophic** mistake. ### **🔐 Security Essentials** - [x] Always use **prepared statements** (MySQLi `prepare()` + `bind_param()` or PDO `prepare()` + `bindValue()`). - [x] Always validate that the request method is **POST** for state-changing operations. - [x] Always implement a **CSRF token** for 14-mark questions. - [x] Always **type-cast** the incoming id to an integer. - [x] Never expose raw SQL error messages to the browser — use `error_log()`. ### **⚙️ Implementation Best Practices** - [x] Use a **two-step confirmation**: `confirm_delete.php` (GET) → `delete.php` (POST). - [x] Check **`affected_rows`** after every delete to verify success. - [x] Apply the **Post-Redirect-Get (PRG)** pattern with `header("Location: ...")` + `exit`. - [x] Use **flash messages** stored in `$_SESSION` for user feedback. - [x] Apply `htmlspecialchars()` on **all** output to prevent XSS. ### **🔄 Soft vs Hard Delete** - [x] **Hard delete** = `DELETE FROM` (permanent, irrecoverable). - [x] **Soft delete** = `UPDATE ... SET is_deleted = 1` (recoverable, audit-friendly). - [x] Production systems **prefer soft delete** for user-generated content. - [x] Soft-deleted records must be excluded from listing queries with `WHERE is_deleted = 0`. ### **📊 MySQLi vs PDO** - [x] **MySQLi** = MySQL only; uses `bind_param("i", $id)`; `affected_rows`. - [x] **PDO** = Database-agnostic; uses `bindValue(':id', $id, PDO::PARAM_INT)`; `rowCount()`. - [x] KTU accepts **either** API; consistency in the rest of your project matters more. ### **🌐 HTTP Status Codes for Delete** - [x] `200` = success with body - [x] `204` = success, no body (RESTful APIs) - [x] `302` = redirect (used in PRG) - [x] `400` = bad request (invalid id) - [x] `403` = forbidden (CSRF / auth fail) - [x] `404` = not found (no matching row) - [x] `405` = method not allowed (GET used instead of POST) - [x] `500` = server error (DB connection lost) ### **🧠 Mnemonics to Memorize** - **"I-C-A-R-F"** = the 5 stages: **I**nitiation → **C**onfirmation → **A**uthorization → **R**emoval → **F**eedback - **"P-PRG"** = **P**repared statement + **P**ost-Redirect-Get - **"1-2-3"** = 1 row targeted, 2-step confirmation, 3 layers of defense (JS / server confirm page / final POST) > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Tip:** When the question says *"write a PHP script to delete a record"*, **always default to the most secure version** you've learned — prepared statement, POST method, affected_rows check, and PRG redirect. Examiners reward defensive code, not the shortest possible code.

topics.title: Saving to the Database
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Saving to the Database

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Web Application Development** (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3), **"Saving to the Database"** refers to the systematic process of **persisting user-submitted or server-generated data into a persistent storage layer (typically a Relational Database Management System like MySQL, PostgreSQL, or SQLite, or a NoSQL store like MongoDB)** through a web application stack.

The operation generally follows the **ACID** properties:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ACID Properties (Mandatory KTU Definition)**
> - **A**tomicity: The transaction is all-or-nothing.
> - **C**onsistency: The database moves from one valid state to another.
> - **I**solation: Concurrent transactions do not interfere with each other.
> - **D**urability: Once committed, the data survives system failures.

The standard flow in a web application is:
$$\text{Client (Browser)} \xrightarrow{\text{HTTP POST}} \text{Server (Backend)} \xrightarrow{\text{SQL/ORM}} \text{Database (Persistent Storage)}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of saving to a database like a **Library Card Catalog System**:

1. A user fills out a registration form (analogous to a paper form).
2. The form is handed to the librarian (the **server-side script**, e.g., PHP, Node.js, Django).
3. The librarian verifies the details (validation), writes them in a standard register (database table), and files it on a shelf (the database file).
4. If you skip the librarian (and inject raw text), the library can be set on fire (the **SQL Injection** attack).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU Module 3, students are expected to understand how data flows from a **client-side form** → **server-side controller** → **database**, using proper validation and **parameterized queries** to avoid security pitfalls.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Meaning | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **CRUD** | Create, Read, Update, Delete | The four fundamental data operations |
| **Prepared Statement** | A pre-compiled SQL query with placeholders | Prevents SQL Injection |
| **ORM** | Object-Relational Mapping (e.g., Sequelize, Mongoose) | Maps DB tables to programming objects |
| **Connection Pool** | A cache of database connections | Improves performance |
| **Transaction** | A unit of work that satisfies ACID properties | Ensures data integrity |
| **PDO** | PHP Data Objects (DB abstraction layer) | Standard PHP DB access method |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Metric:** A well-designed "save" operation in production should complete in **< 200 ms** for a single row insert, with a target throughput of **> 500 transactions/second** on commodity hardware.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Save Operation: 7 Logical Steps

The act of saving data to a database in a web application is broken down into **seven rigorously ordered logical steps**:

1. **Form Submission (Client-Side)**: The browser sends a `POST` (or `PUT`) request with form-encoded or JSON data.
2. **Request Reception (Server-Side)**: The web server (Apache, Nginx) forwards the request to the application layer (PHP, Express.js, Django).
3. **Input Sanitization & Validation**: Strip whitespace, escape HTML, validate email format, enforce length limits, and check data types.
4. **Database Connection Establishment**: Open a connection using credentials (host, username, password, database name).
5. **Query Construction (Parameterized)**: Build a SQL `INSERT` (or `UPDATE`) statement with `?` placeholders (MySQL) or named parameters.
6. **Execution & Transaction Handling**: Execute the query inside a transaction (`BEGIN ... COMMIT` or `ROLLBACK` on error).
7. **Response Generation**: Return success/failure status (HTTP 200, 201, 400, 500) and close the connection.

## 2.2 The High-Yield Formula & Syntax Sheet

| Concept | Standard Syntax / Formula | Unit / Format |
|---|---|---|
| HTTP POST Request | `POST /api/users HTTP/1.1` with body | Text/JSON |
| Connection String (MySQL) | `mysql:host=localhost;dbname=ktu_students;charset=utf8mb4` | String |
| Parameterized INSERT | `INSERT INTO t (c1, c2) VALUES (?, ?)` | SQL |
| Placeholder Binding Types | `?` (positional) or `:name` (named) | Symbol |
| HTTP Success Code | **201 Created** | Numeric |
| HTTP Validation Error | **400 Bad Request** | Numeric |
| HTTP Server Error | **500 Internal Server Error** | Numeric |
| Time Complexity (Insert) | $O(\log n)$ with B-Tree index on PK | Big-O |
| Space Complexity | $O(1)$ for a single row write | Big-O |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Parameterized Queries Matter:** Using string concatenation `query = "INSERT ... " + userInput` is the **#1 cause of SQL Injection vulnerabilities** (OWASP Top 10, A03:2021).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In production engineering, the "save" operation is the heart of:

- **E-Commerce Checkouts**: Persisting cart, order, and payment records.
- **Healthcare Systems**: Storing Electronic Health Records (EHR) with audit trails.
- **Banking**: Logging every transaction in a **double-entry ledger**.
- **Social Media**: Saving posts, comments, likes — often at **millions of writes per second** (handled via sharding and eventual consistency).

The theoretical foundation relies on **Codd's 12 Rules for Relational Databases** (1970), particularly **Rule 6: The Data Sublanguage Rule**, which mandates the use of SQL as a comprehensive data manipulation language.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Saving a Student Record (PHP + MySQL via PDO)

Below is an **exhaustive, line-by-line derivation** of a complete save operation. Every step is explained, satisfying the "no truncation" mandate.

### Step 1: Define the Database Schema

We first establish the target table. The schema is the **destination** of the save operation.

```sql
CREATE TABLE students (
    id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
    reg_no VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    email VARCHAR(120) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    cgpa DECIMAL(3,2) DEFAULT 0.00,
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);
```

> **Logic:** `id` is the surrogate primary key. `reg_no` and `email` carry **UNIQUE** constraints to prevent duplicate enrollments — a hard validation enforced at the database level (defense in depth).

### Step 2: Receive and Sanitize the Form Data (Server-Side)

```php
// sanitize.php logic derivation
$regNo   = trim($_POST['reg_no']  ?? '');
$name    = trim($_POST['name']    ?? '');
$email   = trim($_POST['email']   ?? '');
$cgpa    = trim($_POST['cgpa']    ?? '');

$regNo   = htmlspecialchars($regNo, ENT_QUOTES, 'UTF-8');
$name    = htmlspecialchars($name,  ENT_QUOTES, 'UTF-8');
$email   = filter_var($email, FILTER_SANITIZE_EMAIL);
$cgpa    = filter_var($cgpa,  FILTER_VALIDATE_FLOAT);
```

> **Derivation Logic:**
> - `trim()` removes accidental whitespace.
> - `htmlspecialchars()` neutralizes Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) payloads.
> - `filter_var()` applies RFC-compliant validators (email, float).
> - The null-coalescing operator `??` prevents undefined-index notices.

### Step 3: Validate the Inputs

```php
$errors = [];

if ($regNo === '' || strlen($regNo) > 20) { $errors[] = 'Invalid Reg No'; }

if ($name === '' || strlen($name) > 100) { $errors[] = 'Invalid Name'; }

if (!filter_var($email, FILTER_VALIDATE_EMAIL)) { $errors[] = 'Invalid Email'; }

if ($cgpa === false || $cgpa < 0 || $cgpa > 10) { $errors[] = 'Invalid CGPA'; }

if (!empty($errors)) {
    http_response_code(400);
    echo json_encode(['status' => 'error', 'messages' => $errors]);
    exit;
}
```

> **Derivation Logic:** We collect *all* errors before responding, which is superior UX to halting on the first failure. The HTTP **400** status code is the standardized KTU-recommended response for client-side validation failures.

### Step 4: Establish the Database Connection (PDO)

```php
$dsn = 'mysql:host=localhost;dbname=ktu_db;charset=utf8mb4';

$dbUser = 'ktu_app';
$dbPass = 'S3cur3P@ss!';

$options = [
    PDO::ATTR_ERRMODE            => PDO::ERRMODE_EXCEPTION,
    PDO::ATTR_DEFAULT_FETCH_MODE => PDO::FETCH_ASSOC,
    PDO::ATTR_EMULATE_PREPARES   => false,
];

try {
    $pdo = new PDO($dsn, $dbUser, $dbPass, $options);
} catch (PDOException $e) {
    http_response_code(500);
    echo json_encode(['status' => 'error', 'message' => 'DB connection failed']);
    exit;
}
```

> **Derivation Logic:**
> - The DSN (Data Source Name) is a URI-like connection string.
> - `PDO::ERRMODE_EXCEPTION` forces errors to throw exceptions (modern best practice).
> - `PDO::ATTR_EMULATE_PREPARES => false` sends the query and data **separately** to MySQL — the true defense against SQL injection.
> - Wrapping connection logic in a `try-catch` prevents leaking credentials in stack traces.

### Step 5: Build the Parameterized INSERT Statement

```php
$sql = 'INSERT INTO students (reg_no, name, email, cgpa)
        VALUES (:reg_no, :name, :email, :cgpa)';

$stmt = $pdo->prepare($sql);
```

> **Derivation Logic:** We use **named placeholders** (`:reg_no`, etc.) for readability. The `prepare()` method sends the SQL template to the DBMS, which parses, validates, and generates an execution plan — **before** any user data is bound.

### Step 6: Bind the Parameters

```php
$stmt->bindValue(':reg_no', $regNo, PDO::PARAM_STR);
$stmt->bindValue(':name',   $name,  PDO::PARAM_STR);
$stmt->bindValue(':email',  $email, PDO::PARAM_STR);
$stmt->bindValue(':cgpa',   $cgpa,  PDO::PARAM_STR);
```

> **Derivation Logic:** Each `bindValue()` call explicitly declares the **data type** (`PARAM_STR`). The database engine treats the value as a literal string — never as executable SQL — even if the user submitted `'; DROP TABLE students; --`.

### Step 7: Execute Inside a Transaction

```php
try {
    $pdo->beginTransaction();
    $stmt->execute();
    $newId = $pdo->lastInsertId();
    $pdo->commit();

    http_response_code(201);
    echo json_encode([
        'status' => 'success',
        'id'     => $newId,
        'message'=> 'Student record saved'
    ]);
} catch (PDOException $e) {
    $pdo->rollBack();
    http_response_code(500);
    echo json_encode([
        'status' => 'error',
        'message'=> 'Could not save record'
    ]);
}
```

> **Derivation Logic:**
> - `beginTransaction()` opens a logical work unit.
> - `lastInsertId()` retrieves the auto-generated `id` — useful for the client's confirmation UI.
> - `commit()` finalizes the write (makes it visible to other connections).
> - `rollBack()` on exception guarantees **Atomicity** — partial writes are never persisted.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Save Approaches

| Approach | Security | Performance | Maintainability | KTU Recommendation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| String Concatenation | **Very Low** (SQL Injection) | Fast (no prepare) | Poor | **Avoid** |
| `mysqli_real_escape_string` | Medium | Fast | Medium | Legacy only |
| PDO Parameterized | **High** | Good (1 round-trip) | Excellent | **Recommended** |
| ORM (Eloquent, Sequelize) | High | Slight overhead | Excellent | **Recommended for large apps** |
| Stored Procedure Call | High | Excellent | Low portability | Use in legacy banking systems |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow: The Save Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Browser Form Submit] -->|HTTP POST| B[Web Server]
    B --> C[Application Controller]
    C --> D{Input Valid?}
    D -->|No| E[Return HTTP 400 Error]
    D -->|Yes| F[Open PDO Connection]
    F --> G[Prepare SQL Template]
    G --> H[Bind User Parameters]
    H --> I[Begin Transaction]
    I --> J[Execute INSERT Query]
    J -->|Success| K[Commit Transaction]
    K --> L[Return HTTP 201 Created]
    J -->|Failure| M[Rollback Transaction]
    M --> N[Return HTTP 500 Error]
    L --> O[Close Connection]
    N --> O
    O --> P[End of Operation]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence: Multi-Tier Save Operation

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Browser
    participant W as Web Server
    participant A as App Controller
    participant D as Database
    U->>W: POST /students (form data)
    W->>A: Forward request
    A->>A: Validate and Sanitize
    A->>D: prepare INSERT
    D-->>A: Statement ready
    A->>D: bind values + execute
    D-->>A: lastInsertId = 42
    A->>D: COMMIT
    A-->>U: HTTP 201 + JSON
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CL[Client Tier]
        CL1[HTML Form]
        CL2[JavaScript Validation]
    end
    subgraph SR[Server Tier]
        SR1[Routing Layer]
        SR2[Validation Layer]
        SR3[Business Logic Layer]
        SR4[Data Access Layer - DAO]
    end
    subgraph DB[Persistence Tier]
        DB1[Connection Pool]
        DB2[Query Engine]
        DB3[Storage Engine InnoDB]
    end
    CL --> SR
    SR --> DB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This 3-tier separation is the **MVC + DAO** pattern mandated in KTU's professional web development courses. Each tier has a single responsibility, enabling unit testing and horizontal scaling.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define SQL Injection. How do parameterized queries prevent it? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

SQL Injection is a code-injection technique where an attacker inserts malicious SQL statements into an input field that is later passed to a backend database for execution. For example, entering `'; DROP TABLE users; --` in a login field can destroy an entire table.

**How Parameterized Queries Prevent It:**

Parameterized queries (also called prepared statements) separate the SQL logic from the data. The SQL template is sent to the database first, parsed, and compiled. The user input is then bound as a **literal value**, never as executable SQL. The database engine treats the input as data, not as part of the command, so injection attempts are neutralized syntactically.

In PHP PDO, this is achieved via:

```php
$stmt = $pdo->prepare('SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = :email');
$stmt->execute([':email' => $userInput]);
```

This guarantees that even malicious input is stored as plain text.

### Question 2: List the ACID properties of a database transaction. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

1. **Atomicity** — A transaction is treated as a single, indivisible unit. Either all operations succeed or none do.
2. **Consistency** — The database transitions from one valid state to another, preserving all defined rules and constraints.
3. **Isolation** — Concurrent transactions execute independently; intermediate states of one transaction are invisible to others.
4. **Durability** — Once a transaction is committed, its changes are permanent, surviving crashes, power failures, and system errors.

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> > [!WARNING]
> > Students typically **lose 3–4 marks** by: (1) forgetting to mention the importance of `PDO::ATTR_EMULATE_PREPARES => false` for true prepared statements, (2) skipping the `try-catch` block around transactions, and (3) using the wrong HTTP status code (using 200 instead of 201 for successful resource creation). Always include validation, security, and proper status codes.

---

### **Question A: 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the complete lifecycle of saving a form-submitted record into a MySQL database using PHP PDO. Include all the layers: client-side submission, server-side validation, parameterized query construction, and transaction handling.

**Model Answer:**

The complete lifecycle consists of **seven steps** that must be performed in strict order:

**Step 1 — Client-Side Submission:**
The user fills out an HTML form (e.g., `register.html`) and clicks Submit. The browser issues an `HTTP POST` request to the server, encoding the form fields in the body as `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` or `multipart/form-data`.

**Step 2 — Server-Side Reception:**
The web server (Apache) hands the request to the PHP interpreter. The form data is available in the superglobal `$_POST` array.

**Step 3 — Validation and Sanitization:**
The server-side script applies:
- `trim()` to remove whitespace.
- `htmlspecialchars()` to neutralize XSS.
- `filter_var()` to validate email and numeric formats.
- Custom length and range checks.

Any failure results in an HTTP **400 Bad Request** response and the script terminates via `exit`.

**Step 4 — Database Connection:**
A `PDO` object is instantiated with a DSN (Data Source Name), username, password, and an options array. Crucially, the options include `PDO::ATTR_ERRMODE => PDO::ERRMODE_EXCEPTION` and `PDO::ATTR_EMULATE_PREPARES => false` to ensure true server-side prepared statements.

**Step 5 — Query Preparation:**
The SQL string `'INSERT INTO students (reg_no, name, email) VALUES (:reg, :name, :email)'` is passed to `$pdo->prepare()`. The DBMS parses, validates, and creates an execution plan — without any user data.

**Step 6 — Parameter Binding and Execution:**
User values are bound via `$stmt->bindValue()` with explicit `PDO::PARAM_STR` types. The query is then executed. The `lastInsertId()` method retrieves the auto-generated primary key.

**Step 7 — Transactional Commit:**
The execution is wrapped between `$pdo->beginTransaction()` and `$pdo->commit()`. If any exception occurs, `$pdo->rollBack()` ensures no partial writes are persisted. A successful operation returns HTTP **201 Created** with a JSON payload.

This lifecycle ensures **security, data integrity, and proper error handling** — the three pillars of professional web data persistence.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between `mysqli` procedural, `mysqli` object-oriented, and `PDO` approaches for database operations in PHP. Which is preferred for modern applications and why?

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | `mysqli` Procedural | `mysqli` OOP | `PDO` |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Database Support** | MySQL only | MySQL only | MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite, Oracle, MS SQL |
| **API Style** | Functions | Objects | Objects |
| **Prepared Statements** | Yes | Yes | Yes (with `ATTR_EMULATE_PREPARES` control) |
| **Named Placeholders** | No | No | Yes (`:name` syntax) |
| **Exception Handling** | Manual | Manual | Built-in via `ERRMODE_EXCEPTION` |
| **Portability** | Low | Low | **Very High** |
| **Learning Curve** | Easiest | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Modern Recommendation** | Legacy | Acceptable | **Preferred** |

**Conclusion:** For modern web applications, **PDO is the preferred approach** because it offers: (1) database portability — a single codebase can target multiple DBMS; (2) cleaner exception-based error handling; (3) named placeholders for readability; and (4) better support for advanced features like transactions. The procedural `mysqli` approach is reserved for legacy codebases only.

---

### **Question B: 14 Marks** (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is an ORM? Explain the advantages and disadvantages of using an ORM for database operations compared to raw SQL queries.

**Model Answer:**

**ORM (Object-Relational Mapping)** is a programming technique that converts data between incompatible type systems — specifically, between an object-oriented programming language (PHP, Python, JavaScript) and a relational database.

In an ORM, each database table is represented as a **class**, each row as an **object**, and each column as an **attribute**. For example, in **Eloquent (Laravel)**:

```php
$student = new Student();
$student->name  = 'Anand';
$student->email = 'anand@ktu.in';
$student->save();
```

This single `save()` call generates and executes a parameterized `INSERT INTO students ...` statement.

**Advantages of ORM:**

1. **Productivity**: Developers write less code, reducing development time.
2. **Database Portability**: The same code works on MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQLite (subject to dialect differences).
3. **Security**: ORMs generate parameterized queries by default, preventing SQL injection.
4. **Maintainability**: Business logic lives in the model, not scattered in SQL strings.
5. **Type Safety**: Attribute access is type-checked at the language level.

**Disadvantages of ORM:**

1. **Performance Overhead**: ORMs generate sub-optimal SQL for complex queries (the "N+1 query problem").
2. **Learning Curve**: Heavy ORMs (Hibernate, Eloquent) have steep learning curves.
3. **Abstraction Leak**: Complex joins, window functions, and CTEs are often poorly supported.
4. **Memory Footprint**: Hydrating large result sets into objects consumes significant RAM.

**Conclusion:** ORMs are ideal for **CRUD-heavy applications** (admin panels, SaaS dashboards). For **analytics, reporting, or data warehousing**, raw SQL remains superior.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a complete Node.js + Express + MySQL example for saving a user record, including input validation, parameterized queries, and proper error handling. Explain each section.

**Model Answer:**

```javascript
// saveUser.js
const express = require('express');
const mysql   = require('mysql2/promise');
const { body, validationResult } = require('express-validator');

const app  = express();
app.use(express.json());

// 1. Connection pool (better than single connection)
const pool = mysql.createPool({
    host: 'localhost',
    user: 'ktu_app',
    password: 'S3cur3P@ss!',
    database: 'ktu_db',
    waitForConnections: true,
    connectionLimit: 10,
    queueLimit: 0
});

// 2. Validation middleware
const validateUser = [
    body('name').isLength({ min: 2, max: 100 }).trim().escape(),
    body('email').isEmail().normalizeEmail(),
    body('age').isInt({ min: 16, max: 100 })
];

// 3. The SAVE endpoint
app.post('/api/users', validateUser, async (req, res) => {
    // 3a. Check validation results
    const errors = validationResult(req);
    if (!errors.isEmpty()) {
        return res.status(400).json({ errors: errors.array() });
    }

    const { name, email, age } = req.body;

    // 3b. Parameterized INSERT
    const sql = 'INSERT INTO users (name, email, age) VALUES (?, ?, ?)';

    try {
        const [result] = await pool.execute(sql, [name, email, age]);
        return res.status(201).json({
            status: 'success',
            id: result.insertId
        });
    } catch (err) {
        // 3c. Handle unique constraint violation
        if (err.code === 'ER_DUP_ENTRY') {
            return res.status(409).json({ status: 'error', message: 'Email already exists' });
        }
        return res.status(500).json({ status: 'error', message: 'Server error' });
    }
});

app.listen(3000, () => console.log('Server running on port 3000'));
```

**Explanation of Each Section:**

1. **Connection Pool**: `mysql.createPool()` creates up to 10 reusable connections. This avoids the latency of opening/closing a connection per request.
2. **Validation Middleware**: `express-validator` checks input **before** the controller runs. The `.escape()` and `.normalizeEmail()` calls sanitize the data.
3. **The Save Endpoint**:
   - 3a: `validationResult` aggregates all errors; the request is rejected with **400** if any exist.
   - 3b: The `?` placeholders are bound by `pool.execute()`, which uses **true** MySQL prepared statements (binary protocol).
   - 3c: The catch block distinguishes between a duplicate-email error (HTTP **409 Conflict**) and a generic server error (HTTP **500**), giving the client actionable feedback.

This pattern satisfies the KTU-recommended best practices: validation, security, transaction-safety, and proper HTTP semantics.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Exam Day Saver)**

- [x] **Always use parameterized queries** (PDO, `?` placeholders, or named `:placeholders`) — never concatenate user input into SQL.
- [x] **ACID** = Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability — must satisfy all four for reliable transactions.
- [x] **Use `beginTransaction()` + `commit()` + `rollBack()`** in PDO for multi-step writes.
- [x] **HTTP 201** is the correct status for successful resource creation (not 200).
- [x] **HTTP 400** for client validation errors; **HTTP 500** for server errors; **HTTP 409** for conflicts.
- [x] **Validate on the server** even if you validate on the client (defense in depth).
- [x] **PDO is preferred** over `mysqli` in modern PHP for portability and exception support.
- [x] **ORMs** (Eloquent, Sequelize, Mongoose) boost productivity but can be slower for complex queries.
- [x] **Connection pools** improve throughput by reusing connections.
- [x] **Data types matter**: bind values with `PARAM_STR`, `PARAM_INT`, etc., in PDO.
- [x] **Database-level constraints** (UNIQUE, NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY) are your last line of defense.
- [x] **Always close the cursor / connection** to avoid resource leaks (PDO does this automatically on script end).

> **Closing Note (KTU 2024 Scheme Focus):** Module 3 emphasizes the **practical implementation** of database operations. Examiners look for code that demonstrates security awareness, proper error handling, and correct HTTP semantics. Memorize the 7-step save lifecycle and the ACID properties — they appear in nearly every question paper.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Data with Templating Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Dynamic Data with Templating Engines ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Templating Engine (Definition):** A **templating engine** (also called a *template processor* or *view engine*) is a software component designed to combine **static template files** (containing the HTML markup structure and special placeholder/tag syntax) with a **dynamic data model** (a JavaScript object, dictionary, or context) at runtime to produce a final rendered document — typically an **HTML string** sent to the client's browser. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, templating engines are studied under the **MVC (Model–View–Controller)** pattern where they fulfil the role of the **View** layer. The engine performs **token substitution**, **control-flow evaluation** (loops, conditionals), and **data interpolation** on the template. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Form Letter" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine you are a bank manager who sends 10,000 welcome letters to new account holders. The letter is **95% identical** — the bank name, the rules, the footer. But the **name, account number, and balance** are unique. So you take a **master template (form letter)** with blanks like `{{customer_name}}` and `{{account_no}}`, then the secretary fills in the blanks using a **data sheet (JSON/object)** for each customer. The templating engine is that "smart secretary" — it reads the template, finds the blanks, and substitutes the live data. ### 1.3 Why Are Templating Engines Required? > [!NOTE] > **The Core Problem:** Without templates, you would have to build HTML strings in your server code by **string concatenation**, which leads to: > - **XSS vulnerabilities** (un-escaped user data) > - **Unmaintainable spaghetti code** (logic + markup mixed) > - **No designer-developer separation** (frontend devs can't touch server files) > - **Inconsistency** (different pages look slightly different) A templating engine enforces **separation of concerns**: developers handle **data & logic** (Controller/Model), while designers handle **presentation** (View template). The **data binding** happens in one well-defined place. ### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Terminology > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU Terminology (Must Memorize):** > - **Template File** — A static file with placeholders (e.g., `index.ejs`, `home.pug`) > - **Context / Data Model** — The JS object passed to the template (e.g., `{ user: { name: "Anu" } }`) > - **Rendering** — The act of merging template + context into final HTML > - **Escaping** — Automatically converting special characters (`<`, `>`, `&`) into HTML entities to prevent XSS > - **Partial** — A reusable sub-template (e.g., a header or footer) included in other templates > - **Layout / Master Page** — A wrapping template that defines a common shell (header + footer) into which child templates inject content > - **Helper / Filter** — A reusable function callable inside the template (e.g., `{{ price | currency }}`) --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Operational Workflow (How a Template Engine Works) The execution flow of any templating engine follows a **3-stage pipeline**: **Stage 1 — Parse (Compile Phase):** The engine reads the `.ejs` / `.pug` / `.hbs` file and converts the custom syntax (e.g., `<%= user.name %>`) into executable JavaScript code (a function). This step happens **once per template** and the result is **cached** for performance. **Stage 2 — Inject (Data Binding):** At request time, the Controller calls `res.render("home", { user: { name: "Anu" } })`. The engine takes the **compiled function** and the **data context** as input arguments. **Stage 3 — Output (Render to String):** The compiled function executes, performs all interpolations, loops, and conditionals, and returns a **fully resolved HTML string**, which the framework sends as the HTTP response body. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Compile then Render"?** Compiling once and rendering many times is the same performance principle as JIT compilation in Java — it makes the engine **O(n) per request** instead of **O(n × re-parsing)**. ### 2.2 The Core Template Processing Equation $$ \text{RenderedHTML} = T(\text{TemplateSource}, \text{DataContext}) $$ Where: - $T$ = the templating engine function - $\text{TemplateSource}$ = the raw template string/file - $\text{DataContext}$ = the JSON object containing dynamic data **Extended form with options:** $$ \text{RenderedHTML} = T(\text{TemplateSource},\ \text{DataContext},\ \text{Options}) $$ Where $\text{Options}$ may include cache settings, custom helpers, file paths, etc. ### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Engine Feature | Feature | Why It Exists | Real Engineering Benefit | |---|---|---| | **Variables / Interpolation** | Insert dynamic values into HTML | Personalization (e.g., `Hello, {{user.name}}`) | | **Conditionals** (`if/else`) | Show/hide UI based on data | Role-based menus (admin vs guest) | | **Loops** (`for/each`) | Render lists/tables from arrays | Product listings, comment threads | | **Partials** | Reuse a component across pages | Shared header/footer/sidebar | | **Layouts** | Inherit a master page structure | DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) principle | | **Filters / Helpers** | Format data at output time | `{{ date | format('YYYY-MM-DD') }}` | | **Auto-Escaping** | Convert `<` to `<` etc. | Prevents **XSS attacks** | | **Template Caching** | Skip re-parsing unchanged files | 10x–100x faster responses | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Reference Table for KTU Exams:** | **Concept** | **Syntax (Generic)** | **Example** | **Notes** | |---|---|---|---| | Variable Output | `{{ var }}` or `<%= var %>` | `{{ username }}` | Auto-escaped in most engines | | Raw HTML Output | `{{{ var }}}` or `<%- var %>` | `{{{ htmlContent }}}` | **NEVER** use with untrusted data | | If Conditional | `{% if cond %}...{% endif %}` | `{% if user %}

Hi

{% endif %}` | Truthy/falsy check | | For / Each Loop | `{% for x in arr %}...{% endfor %}` | `{% for p in products %}` | Iterates arrays | | Partial Include | `{% include "name" %}` | `{% include "header" %}` | Reusable sub-template | | Layout Inheritance | `{% extends "base" %}` | `{% extends "layout" %}` | Child fills parent's `{% block %}` | | Comment | `{# comment #}` or `` | `{# TODO: fix #}` | Not sent to client | | Filter / Helper | `{{ var \| filter }}` | `{{ price \| currency }}` | Pipe syntax common in Liquid/Jinja | ### 2.5 The Two Major Families of Templating Engines > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Must Know Classification:** **Family A — Logic-Less / Minimal Engines:** - **Examples:** Mustache, Handlebars - **Philosophy:** No actual code in templates — only tags like `{{var}}` and `{{#each}}`. Logic stays in the Controller. - **Pro:** Maximum separation, designers love it. - **Con:** Cannot do complex inline logic. **Family B — Full-Power / Embedded Engines:** - **Examples:** EJS, Pug (formerly Jade), Jinja2, Twig, Liquid - **Philosophy:** Allow raw JavaScript/Python/Ruby code inside templates. - **Pro:** Maximum flexibility. - **Con:** Can become spaghetti if abused (the "PHP problem"). ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Templating engines power nearly every modern web framework: - **Express.js (Node)** → uses `res.render()` with EJS, Pug, or Handlebars - **Django (Python)** → uses **Jinja2** natively - **Flask (Python)** → uses **Jinja2** - **Ruby on Rails** → uses **ERB** (Embedded Ruby) - **Laravel (PHP)** → uses **Blade** - **Spring (Java)** → uses **Thymeleaf** - **ASP.NET (C#)** → uses **Razor (.cshtml)** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Industry Stat:** Roughly **95% of server-rendered web applications** in 2024 use a templating engine for the View layer. Static HTML files alone account for less than 5% of dynamic web traffic. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Building a Product Card with EJS > [!NOTE] > **Scenario (Common KTU Lab Question):** You are given a Node.js Express app and an array of products. Render a Bootstrap-styled card for **each** product using **EJS**. **Step 1 — The Data Context (sent from Controller):** ```javascript // server.js const products = [ { id: 1, name: "Laptop", price: 75000, inStock: true }, { id: 2, name: "Mouse", price: 500, inStock: true }, { id: 3, name: "Keyboard",price: 1500, inStock: false } ]; app.get("/shop", (req, res) => { res.render("shop", { products: products, user: "Anu" }); }); ``` **Step 2 — The Template File (`views/shop.ejs`):** ```html Shop

Welcome, <%= user %>

<% products.forEach(function(p){ %>

<%= p.name %>

₹<%= p.price %>

<% if (p.inStock) { %> <% } else { %> Out of Stock <% } %>
<% }); %>
``` **Step 3 — The Compilation (what EJS does internally):** The EJS engine converts the template into a JavaScript function like this: ```javascript function template(locals) { var __output = ""; __output += "...

Welcome, "; __output += escapeFn(user); // <%= user %> __output += "

"; products.forEach(function(p) { // <% forEach %> __output += "
..."; __output += escapeFn(p.name); // <%= p.name %> if (p.inStock) { // <% if %> __output += ""; } else { __output += "Out of Stock"; } __output += "
"; }); __output += "
"; return __output; } ``` **Step 4 — The Final Rendered HTML (sent to browser):** ```html

Welcome, Anu

Laptop

₹75000

Mouse

₹500

Keyboard

₹1500

Out of Stock
``` ### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — Layout Inheritance with Pug > [!NOTE] > **Scenario:** Create a base layout with a navigation bar and a content block, then a child page that fills the block. **Step 1 — The Layout (`views/layout.pug`):** ```pug doctype html html head title My Site body nav a(href="/") Home a(href="/about") About block content footer © 2024 KTU ``` **Step 2 — The Child Page (`views/about.pug`):** ```pug extends layout block content h1 About Us p We teach web development. ``` **Step 3 — The Render Call:** ```javascript app.get("/about", (req, res) => { res.render("about"); }); ``` **Step 4 — The Resulting HTML:** ```html My Site
HomeAbout

About Us

We teach web development.

© 2024 KTU
``` ### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Major Templating Engines > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the highest-yield table for KTU 2024 Scheme Part A questions:** | **Engine** | **Language** | **Syntax Style** | **Logic Allowed?** | **Auto-Escape** | **Used By** | **File Ext** | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **EJS** | JavaScript | HTML + `<% %>` tags | ✅ Full JS | ✅ Yes (default) | Express.js | `.ejs` | | **Pug** | JavaScript | Indented whitespace | ✅ Full JS | ✅ Yes | Express.js | `.pug` | | **Handlebars** | JavaScript | `{{ }}` + `{{#each}}` | ❌ Logic-less | ✅ Yes | Express, Ember | `.hbs` / `.handlebars` | | **Mustache** | Multi-lang | `{{ }}` minimal | ❌ Logic-less | ✅ Yes | Language-agnostic | `.mustache` | | **Nunjucks** | JavaScript | Jinja-style `{% %}` | ✅ Limited | ✅ Yes | Mozilla, Express | `.njk` | | **Jinja2** | Python | `{{ }}` + `{% %}` | ✅ Full Python | ✅ Yes | Django, Flask | `.jinja2` | | **Twig** | PHP | `{{ }}` + `{% %}` | ✅ Limited | ✅ Yes | Symfony, Drupal | `.twig` | | **Blade** | PHP | `{{ }}` + `@directive` | ✅ Full PHP | ✅ Yes | Laravel | `.blade.php` | | **Liquid** | Ruby | `{{ }}` + `{% %}` | ❌ Logic-less | ✅ Yes | Shopify, Jekyll | `.liquid` | | **Thymeleaf** | Java | HTML + `th:attr` | ✅ Limited | ✅ Yes | Spring Boot | `.html` | | **ERB** | Ruby | `<%= %>` tags | ✅ Full Ruby | ❌ Manual | Ruby on Rails | `.erb` | | **Razor** | C# | `@{ }` blocks | ✅ Full C# | ✅ Yes | ASP.NET Core | `.cshtml` | ### 3.4 The Escaping Derivation (Why `<%=` vs `<%-` Matters in EJS) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Security Question Frequently Asked!** Suppose the user submits a comment: `` **With `<%= comment %>` (escaped — SAFE):** $$ \text{HTML Output} = \text{escapeFn}(\text{comment}) $$ $$ = \text{escapeFn}(\text{``}) $$ $$ = \text{`<script>alert('hacked')</script>`} $$ The browser **displays the text as-is** but does **NOT execute** the script. ✅ Safe. **With `<%- comment %>` (raw — DANGEROUS):** $$ \text{HTML Output} = \text{rawFn}(\text{comment}) = \text{``} $$ The browser **executes the script** → XSS attack. ❌ Unsafe. > [!WARNING] > **Rule of thumb:** Use `<%-` **only** for content **you** generated (e.g., sanitized Markdown output). **Never** for user input. --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Templating Engine Pipeline (Mermaid Flow Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Template File .ejs .pug .hbs] --> B[Parser Compiler Stage] C[Data Context JSON Object] --> D[Runtime Injector] B --> D D --> E[Token Substitution Engine] E --> F[Loop and Conditional Evaluator] F --> G[Escape and Sanitize Module] G --> H[Final HTML String] H --> I[HTTP Response to Client Browser] style A fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309 style C fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8 style H fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d style I fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d ``` ### 4.2 The MVC Position of a Templating Engine ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CLIENT[Client Browser] U[User sends HTTP Request] R[Browser receives HTML Response] end subgraph SERVER[Node Express Server] M[Model: Database Query] C[Controller: Route Handler] T[Templating Engine: View Layer] end U --> C C --> M M --> C C -->|res.render view data| T T -->|HTML string| C C --> R R -.-> U style M fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#c2410c style C fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1d4ed8 style T fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d style U fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#be185d style R fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#be185d ``` ### 4.3 The Two-Family Classification Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB TE[Templating Engines Universe] TE --> FL1[Family A: Logic-Less] TE --> FL2[Family B: Full Power] FL1 --> M1[Mustache] FL1 --> M2[Handlebars] FL1 --> M3[Liquid] FL1 --> M4[Twig with strict mode] FL2 --> P1[EJS] FL2 --> P2[Pug Jade] FL2 --> P3[Jinja2] FL2 --> P4[Blade] FL2 --> P5[Razor] FL2 --> P6[Thymeleaf] style TE fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#854d0e,stroke-width:3px style FL1 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b style FL2 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534 style M1 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 style M2 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 style M3 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 style M4 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 style P1 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 style P2 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 style P3 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 style P4 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 style P5 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 style P6 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 ``` ### 4.4 Template Compilation vs Runtime Rendering Cycle ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Dev as Developer participant FS as File System participant Cache as Template Cache participant Req as HTTP Request participant Engine as Templating Engine participant Browser as Client Browser Note over Dev,Cache: COMPILE PHASE (once per template change) Dev->>FS: Save template.ejs FS->>Engine: Read raw template Engine->>Engine: Parse tokens and syntax Engine->>Cache: Store compiled function Note over Req,Browser: RENDER PHASE (every request) Req->>Engine: GET /page with data context Engine->>Cache: Fetch compiled function Cache-->>Engine: Return compiled function Engine->>Engine: Execute with data context Engine->>Browser: Send rendered HTML ``` --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` Define a templating engine. List any two popular templating engines used with Node.js Express.** > [!NOTE] > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > **Definition (2 marks):** A templating engine is a software tool that combines a **static template file** (containing HTML markup and special placeholder syntax) with a **dynamic data model** (a JavaScript object) to produce a fully rendered HTML page at runtime. It enables the **separation of presentation (View) from logic (Controller)** in the MVC pattern. > > **Two examples (1 mark):** **EJS (Embedded JavaScript)** and **Pug** (formerly Jade). Other valid: Handlebars, Nunjucks, Mustache. > > **Mark Distribution:** Definition — 2 marks | Examples — 1 mark --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` Differentiate between `<%= variable %>` and `<%- variable %>` syntax in EJS with a security example.** > [!NOTE] > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > **Difference (2 marks):** > - **`<%= variable %>`** — Outputs the value with **HTML escaping**. Special characters like `<`, `>`, `&` are converted into entities (`<`, `>`, `&`). **Safe** for user input. > - **`<%- variable %>`** — Outputs the value **as raw HTML** without escaping. **Unsafe** for untrusted user input — can lead to XSS. > > **Security Example (1 mark):** If `comment = ""`, then `<%= comment %>` displays the text harmlessly, but `<%- comment %>` executes the JavaScript in the user's browser, causing an XSS attack. > > **Mark Distribution:** Comparison — 2 marks | Example — 1 mark --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Q. (a)** Explain the architecture of a templating engine in a Node.js Express application with a suitable block diagram. Describe the role of `res.render()` and partials. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write an EJS template that takes an array of students (each with `name`, `rollNo`, `marks`) from the controller and displays them as a styled HTML table, highlighting students with marks ≥ 90 in a different background color using a conditional. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Answer for (a) — 7 marks:** > **[2 marks] Architecture:** In an Express application, the templating engine sits in the **View layer** of the MVC pattern. When a request hits a route, the **Controller** fetches data from the **Model** (database/API), then invokes `res.render("templateName", dataObject)`. The engine performs three steps: > 1. **Parse** the `.ejs` / `.pug` / `.hbs` file into a JavaScript function (compiled once and cached). > 2. **Inject** the data object into the compiled function. > 3. **Output** the resulting HTML string as the HTTP response body. > > **[2 marks] Role of `res.render()`:** `res.render(view, locals, callback)` is the Express API call that triggers the templating engine. Arguments: > - `view` — the template file name (without extension) located in the `views/` directory. > - `locals` — an object whose properties become accessible as variables inside the template. > - `callback` — optional; if provided, the rendered HTML is passed to the callback instead of being sent automatically. > > **[2 marks] Partials:** A partial is a **reusable sub-template** (e.g., `_header.ejs`, `_footer.ejs`) included in other templates using `<%- include('partialName', { data }) %>` in EJS or `{% include 'partialName' %}` in Jinja2/Handlebars. Partials enable **DRY** (Don't Repeat Yourself) — the same navigation bar or footer is defined once and reused across hundreds of pages. Changing the partial updates all pages. > > **[1 mark] Block Diagram:** Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2 (MVC Position of Templating Engine). --- **Model Answer for (b) — 7 marks:** **Controller (`app.js`):** ```javascript const express = require("express"); const app = express(); app.set("view engine", "ejs"); const students = [ { name: "Anu", rollNo: 1, marks: 95 }, { name: "Rahul", rollNo: 2, marks: 78 }, { name: "Meera", rollNo: 3, marks: 92 }, { name: "Vijay", rollNo: 4, marks: 65 } ]; app.get("/students", (req, res) => { res.render("students", { students }); }); ``` **Template (`views/students.ejs`):** ```html Student List

Student Result

<% students.forEach(function(s) { %> <% }); %>
Roll No Name Marks Grade
<%= s.rollNo %> <%= s.name %> <%= s.marks %> <% if (s.marks >= 90) { %> A+ <% } else if (s.marks >= 75) { %> A <% } else { %> B <% } %>
``` > **Mark Distribution for (b):** > - Correct controller with `res.render()` call — **1 mark** > - HTML table structure with ``/`` — **1 mark** > - EJS `forEach` loop — **2 marks** > - Conditional for topper class (using ternary or `if`) — **2 marks** > - Grade `if/else if/else` block — **1 mark** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **Q. (a)** Compare logic-less templating engines (Mustache, Handlebars) with full-power engines (EJS, Pug). Discuss the security implications of each approach. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design a **layout system** using Pug with a base layout (`base.pug`) and a child page (`profile.pug`) that displays a logged-in user's profile (name, email, role) passed from the controller. Show the complete code. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Answer for (a) — 7 marks:** > **[3 marks] Logic-Less Engines (Mustache, Handlebars):** > - **Syntax:** Uses minimal tags like `{{var}}`, `{{#each items}}...{{/each}}`, `{{#if loggedIn}}...{{/if}}`. > - **Philosophy:** Templates contain **zero executable code**. All logic lives in the Controller. Designers can edit templates without breaking the application. > - **Pros:** Maximum separation of concerns, highly readable, easier to onboard frontend designers, naturally prevents spaghetti. > - **Cons:** Cannot perform complex inline calculations; must pre-process data in the controller. > - **Examples:** Mustache (language-agnostic), Handlebars (extends Mustache with helpers). > > **[2 marks] Full-Power Engines (EJS, Pug):** > - **Syntax:** EJS uses `<% %>` for raw JS; Pug uses indented whitespace-based syntax. > - **Philosophy:** Allows any JavaScript/Python/Ruby code inside templates. > - **Pros:** Maximum flexibility, fewer round-trips between template and controller. > - **Cons:** Templates can become **unmaintainable spaghetti** ("PHP anti-pattern"), logic leaks into the view layer. > > **[2 marks] Security Implications:** > - **Logic-less** engines tend to **auto-escape everything** by design, reducing XSS surface area. They also force logic into the controller, where it can be properly validated. > - **Full-power** engines **default to escaping** in EJS (`<%= %>`) but offer **raw output** tags (`<%- %>` in EJS, `!{}` in Pug). Misuse of raw output with untrusted data causes **XSS attacks**. The responsibility is on the developer. > - Pug additionally sanitizes variable names and has built-in **whitespace control**, but it must be **configured with `app.locals.cache = true`** safely in production. --- **Model Answer for (b) — 7 marks:** **Controller (`app.js`):** ```javascript const express = require("express"); const app = express(); app.set("view engine", "pug"); app.get("/profile", (req, res) => { const user = { name: "Anu Krishnan", email: "anu@ktu.ac.in", role: "Administrator" }; res.render("profile", { user: user }); }); ``` **Base Layout (`views/base.pug`):** ```pug doctype html html head title My KTU App link(rel="stylesheet", href="/css/style.css") body header h1 KTU Portal nav a(href="/") Home a(href="/profile") Profile a(href="/logout") Logout main block content footer p © 2024 APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University ``` **Child Page (`views/profile.pug`):** ```pug extends base block content .profile-card h2 User Profile table tr th Name td= user.name tr th Email td= user.email tr th Role td= user.role if user.role === "Administrator" a.button(href="/admin") Go to Admin Panel ``` > **Mark Distribution for (b):** > - Correct `app.set("view engine", "pug")` and `res.render` — **1 mark** > - `base.pug` with `doctype`, `head`, `block content` — **2 marks** > - `profile.pug` with `extends base` — **1 mark** > - Block content with user data — **2 marks** > - Conditional for admin role — **1 mark** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake #1:** Students write `<%- user.name %>` instead of `<%= user.name %>` in EJS when the data is user-provided. **Penalty: 1–2 marks** for security vulnerability. > - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to set the view engine with `app.set("view engine", "ejs")`. The exam often tests if you remember to **configure** before rendering. > - **Common Mistake #3:** Using `res.sendFile()` instead of `res.render()` — the former sends a static file, the latter triggers the templating engine. **Penalty: 2 marks** if the question clearly asks about templating. > - **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing partials (`include`) with layouts (`extends`). They are **different mechanisms**. Partials are pasted in; layouts define an inheritance hierarchy. > - **Common Mistake #5:** Not mentioning **auto-escaping** as a security feature — KTU 2024 Scheme places heavy weight on web security. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam Day:** - ✅ A **templating engine** combines a static template with dynamic data to produce HTML. - ✅ It is the **View layer** in the **MVC pattern**. - ✅ The 3-stage pipeline is: **Parse → Inject → Output**. - ✅ Compilation happens **once per template**; rendering happens **per request**. - ✅ **`res.render("viewName", dataObject)`** is the Express call that triggers the engine. - ✅ `<%= var %>` = **escaped (safe)**; `<%- var %>` = **raw HTML (unsafe)**. - ✅ Templates must be placed in the `views/` directory by default. - ✅ **Partials** are reusable sub-templates (header, footer, navbar). - ✅ **Layouts** (Pug's `extends`, EJS partials with `include`) enable **DRY** design. - ✅ **Auto-escaping** prevents **XSS attacks** — never disable it for user data. - ✅ **Logic-less engines** (Mustache, Handlebars) = safer, more readable, less flexible. - ✅ **Full-power engines** (EJS, Pug, Jinja2) = flexible, but risk spaghetti code. - ✅ **Common engines per language:** JS → EJS/Pug, Python → Jinja2, PHP → Blade/Twig, Java → Thymeleaf, C# → Razor, Ruby → ERB/Liquid. - ✅ **Caching** compiled templates is a major performance optimization. - ✅ **Filters/helpers** (`{{ var | filter }}`) format data at output time (e.g., currency, date). - ✅ **Configuration line:** `app.set("view engine", "ejs")` must precede `res.render()` calls. > [!NOTE] > **📌 One-Line Memory Aid:** *"Template + Data = Rendered HTML — safely escaped, fully dynamic, beautifully separated."* ---

topics.title: Adding Fields to the Schema
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Adding Fields to the Schema

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Mongoose ODM (Object Data Modeling)** for **Node.js** with **MongoDB**, a **Schema** is a structural blueprint that defines the shape, data types, validation rules, and default behaviors of documents stored within a MongoDB collection. **Adding Fields to the Schema** refers to the process of extending or modifying this blueprint by introducing **new attributes (fields/keys)**, either during initial schema definition or via **schema evolution** (also called **schema migration**) for an existing collection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In **PBITT504 – Web Application Development**, *Module 3* focuses on **Database Integration (MongoDB + Mongoose)**. The term "Schema" here strictly refers to the **Mongoose Schema definition** at the application layer, **not** the dynamic, schemaless nature of raw MongoDB collections.

Mathematically, a Mongoose Schema can be expressed as a typed function:

$$S = \{ (f_i, \tau_i, V_i, O_i) \mid i = 1, 2, \dots, n \}$$

Where:
- $S$ = the Schema definition
- $f_i$ = the $i^{th}$ **field name** (key)
- $\tau_i$ = the **type** of the field (e.g., `String`, `Number`, `Date`, `Buffer`, `ObjectId`, custom Schema)
- $V_i$ = the **set of validators** applied to the field (e.g., `required`, `min`, `max`, `enum`, `match`)
- $O_i$ = additional **options** (e.g., `default`, `unique`, `index`, `lowercase`, `trim`)

When we "**add a field**", we are increasing the cardinality of $S$ from $n$ to $n + 1$, introducing a new tuple $(f_{n+1}, \tau_{n+1}, V_{n+1}, O_{n+1})$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Card Catalog

Imagine a **library** where every book must be registered on a **catalog card**:

| Analogy Element | Real-World Library | Mongoose Schema |
|---|---|---|
| The Catalog System | Master register of all books | `mongoose.Schema({...})` |
| An Empty Catalog Card | Blank form | A new document (MongoDB record) |
| Pre-printed Labels on Card | "Title", "Author", "Year" | Existing fields |
| Adding a New Label "Genre" | New column/label printed on the card | **Adding a new field to the Schema** |
| Marking a Label "Mandatory" | Red star on the label | `{ required: true }` validator |
| Pre-filling a Label | Default genre if unknown | `{ default: "Uncategorized" }` |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Schema Design:** MongoDB is *schemaless* at the database level — it will happily store whatever JSON you send it. **Mongoose** enforces schema discipline at the **application layer**, so adding fields explicitly gives you **type safety, validation, and predictable document structure** across your entire Node.js application.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology for KTU 2024

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Vocabulary for Board Exams:**
> - **Field** — A single named attribute within a document (e.g., `email`, `age`).
> - **Schema** — The Mongoose-level blueprint defining all fields and rules.
> - **Model** — A compiled, ready-to-use constructor created from a Schema (`mongoose.model('User', userSchema)`).
> - **Type** — The BSON/JavaScript data type a field must conform to (`String`, `Number`, `Boolean`, `Date`, `Array`, `ObjectId`, `Buffer`, `Mixed`, etc.).
> - **Validator** — A constraint function (e.g., `required`, `minlength`, `enum`).
> - **Default Value** — A value automatically assigned if none is provided at document creation.
> - **Strict Mode** — A Mongoose option (default `true`) that prevents saving fields *not* defined in the schema.

---

## 1.4 Why Add Fields? (Engineering Motivation)

In real-world applications, requirements evolve. A User model might originally need only `name` and `email`. Later, the product team demands:
- A `profilePicture` (String URL).
- A `role` for authorization (enum: `["admin", "user", "editor"]`).
- A `createdAt` timestamp for analytics.
- A `lastLogin` timestamp (Date).

Each of these requires **adding a new field to the schema**, often with appropriate validation, default values, and indexing for performance. The process must be **controlled, reversible, and consistent** across development, staging, and production environments.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Mongoose Field Declaration

A single field declaration in a Mongoose Schema is a key-value pair with the following layered structure:

```javascript
fieldName: {
    type: <DataType>,
    required: <Boolean|Function|Array>,
    default: <Value|Function>,
    unique: <Boolean>,
    index: <Boolean>,
    validate: <Function|Object|Array>,
    min: <Number|Date>,
    max: <Number|Date>,
    minlength: <Number>,
    maxlength: <Number>,
    match: <RegExp>,
    enum: <Array>,
    lowercase: <Boolean>,
    uppercase: <Boolean>,
    trim: <Boolean>,
    select: <Boolean>,
    immutable: <Boolean>
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The `type` property is the ONLY mandatory property for every field.** All other properties are optional enhancements.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: How a Field Is Processed

When you add a field and try to save a document, Mongoose executes the following internal pipeline:

1. **Type Coercion** — Mongoose attempts to cast the incoming value into the declared `type`. Example: incoming `"25"` is cast to `Number 25` if type is `Number`.
2. **Default Application** — If no value is provided, the `default` value (or a function returning a value) is applied.
3. **Required Check** — If `required: true` and value is still `undefined`, Mongoose throws a `ValidationError`.
4. **Validator Execution** — Custom `validate` functions, `enum` membership, `min/max`, `match` (regex), and built-in validators run in parallel.
5. **Pre-Save Hooks** — Any registered `pre('save', ...)` middleware runs (e.g., password hashing).
6. **Indexing** — If `index: true` or `unique: true`, the field is indexed in MongoDB.
7. **Persistence** — The validated, typed document is sent to MongoDB.

---

## 2.3 Field Type Reference Table

| Mongoose Type | JavaScript/BSON Equivalent | Typical Use Case | Example Declaration |
|---|---|---|---|
| `String` | `string` | Names, emails, descriptions | `name: { type: String, required: true }` |
| `Number` | `number` (double) | Age, price, quantity | `price: { type: Number, min: 0 }` |
| `Date` | `Date` object | Timestamps, deadlines | `createdAt: { type: Date, default: Date.now }` |
| `Boolean` | `boolean` | Flags, toggles | `isActive: { type: Boolean, default: true }` |
| `ObjectId` | `ObjectId` | Foreign key references | `author: { type: Schema.Types.ObjectId, ref: 'User' }` |
| `Array` | `array` | Lists, tags | `tags: [String]` |
| `Buffer` | `Buffer` | Binary data (files) | `avatar: Buffer` |
| `Mixed` | `object` (any) | Arbitrary JSON | `metadata: Schema.Types.Mixed` |
| `Decimal128` | high-precision decimal | Currency, finance | `balance: Schema.Types.Decimal128` |
| `Map` | ES6 Map | Key-value collections | `socialLinks: { type: Map, of: String }` |

---

## 2.4 Validators and Constraints — The Engineer's Quick Reference

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Validator:** Validators protect data integrity at the application layer before the data ever reaches MongoDB, drastically reducing bad data and downstream bugs.

| Validator | Purpose | Example Value | Applies To |
|---|---|---|---|
| `required` | Field must be present | `true` | All types |
| `default` | Auto-fill value | `"guest"`, `Date.now` | All types |
| `unique` | No duplicates in collection | `true` | All types |
| `enum` | Value must be in allowed list | `["admin","user"]` | String, Number |
| `min` / `max` | Numeric / date range | `0`, `120` | Number, Date |
| `minlength` / `maxlength` | String length range | `3`, `50` | String |
| `match` | Regex pattern check | `/^[\w-\.]+@([\w-]+\.)+[\w-]{2,4}$/` | String |
| `validate` | Custom async/sync logic | `function(v){ return v.length > 0 }` | All types |

---

## 2.5 Methods to Add a Field — Three Engineering Strategies

### Strategy 1: Direct Schema Modification (Development Phase)
Add the field directly to the schema file. Restart the server. New documents will have the field; existing documents will **not** automatically gain the field.

### Strategy 2: Default Value Backfilling (Safe for Production)
Combine a `default` value with a **migration script** that loops through all existing documents and assigns the field if missing:

```javascript
await Model.updateMany(
    { newField: { $exists: false } },
    { $set: { newField: "defaultValue" } }
);
```

### Strategy 3: Schema Versioning (Enterprise Pattern)
Add a `schemaVersion` field to track evolution. Old documents are processed by version-aware middleware to ensure compatibility.

---

## 2.6 The Strict Mode Trap

By default, Mongoose operates in **strict mode**. This means if you attempt to save a document containing a field **not defined in the schema**, the unknown field is **silently dropped** — it is not stored in MongoDB.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often expect MongoDB's "schemaless" nature to mean any field is saved. This is **false** when using Mongoose. Mongoose enforces schema discipline. To disable, set `{ strict: false }` in the schema options — but this is strongly discouraged in production.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Adding schema fields correctly is foundational for:
- **Authentication Systems** (adding `password`, `resetToken`, `otpExpiry`).
- **E-Commerce Platforms** (adding `discount`, `stockCount`, `ratings`).
- **Social Media Apps** (adding `followers`, `likes`, `bio`).
- **Analytics Dashboards** (adding `viewCount`, `engagementScore`).
- **Authorization Layers** (adding `role`, `permissions`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Exam Tip:** Always mention that Mongoose operates at the **application layer**, while MongoDB's schema flexibility exists at the **database layer**. The two layers have *different* rules.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough #1: Adding a Simple `String` Field

**Scenario:** Extend a `User` schema to include a `phone` field that is **optional** but **must be exactly 10 digits** when provided.

### Step 1 — The Original Schema (Before)

```javascript
const mongoose = require('mongoose');
const { Schema } = mongoose;

const userSchema = new Schema({
    name:  { type: String, required: true },
    email: { type: String, required: true, unique: true }
});

module.exports = mongoose.model('User', userSchema);
```

### Step 2 — The Modified Schema (After)

```javascript
const userSchema = new Schema({
    name:  { type: String, required: true },
    email: { type: String, required: true, unique: true },

    // NEW FIELD ADDED BELOW
    phone: {
        type: String,
        required: false,             // Optional
        match: /^[0-9]{10}$/,        // Regex: exactly 10 digits
        trim: true                   // Strip whitespace
    }
});
```

### Step 3 — Logical Derivation of Each Property

| Property | Logical Reasoning |
|---|---|
| `type: String` | Phone numbers are textual (preserve leading zeros, no arithmetic) |
| `required: false` | The user may not provide a phone; field is optional |
| `match: /^[0-9]{10}$/` | Business rule: 10-digit numeric phone |
| `trim: true` | User may accidentally type spaces — strip them before validation |

### Step 4 — Validation Test Cases

```javascript
// CASE A: Valid phone → saved
const u1 = new User({ name: "Anu", email: "a@x.com", phone: "9876543210" });
await u1.save();  // SUCCESS

// CASE B: Invalid phone (9 digits) → rejected
const u2 = new User({ name: "Anu", email: "b@x.com", phone: "987654321" });
await u2.save();  // THROWS ValidationError: Path `phone` is invalid

// CASE C: Phone omitted → saved (field is optional)
const u3 = new User({ name: "Anu", email: "c@x.com" });
await u3.save();  // SUCCESS, phone is undefined
```

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Walkthrough #2: Adding a Field with `default`, `unique`, and Custom Validator

**Scenario:** Add an `employeeId` (auto-generated, unique), `role` (enum), and `isActive` (default `true`).

```javascript
const employeeSchema = new Schema({
    name:       { type: String, required: true },
    department: { type: String, required: true },

    // NEW FIELDS BELOW
    employeeId: {
        type: String,
        unique: true,
        default: function() {
            return 'EMP-' + Date.now() + '-' + Math.floor(Math.random() * 1000);
        }
    },
    role: {
        type: String,
        enum: ['admin', 'manager', 'employee', 'intern'],
        default: 'employee',
        required: true
    },
    isActive: {
        type: Boolean,
        default: true
    }
});
```

### Step 3 — The Validation Pipeline (Analytical Trace)

For a new document `{ name: "Ravi", department: "IT" }`:

1. `name` → passed (provided).
2. `department` → passed (provided).
3. `employeeId` → no value → `default` function executes → returns `"EMP-1718000000-456"`.
4. `role` → no value → `default: 'employee'` applied → passes `enum` check.
5. `isActive` → no value → `default: true` applied.
6. Document saved to MongoDB.

For `{ name: "Meera", department: "HR", role: "CEO" }`:

1. `role` → value `"CEO"` → fails `enum` check → **ValidationError**: `Path 'role' has a value 'CEO' that is not in the allowed list`.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Walkthrough #3: Migration Script for Existing Documents

**Scenario:** The `Product` schema gains a new field `discountPercentage`. Existing documents must be backfilled with a default of `0`.

### Step 1 — Update the Schema

```javascript
const productSchema = new Schema({
    name:     { type: String, required: true },
    price:    { type: Number, required: true, min: 0 },

    // NEW FIELD
    discountPercentage: {
        type: Number,
        default: 0,
        min: 0,
        max: 100
    }
});
```

### Step 2 — Migration Script (Run Once)

```javascript
const mongoose = require('mongoose');
const Product = require('./models/Product');

async function migrateDiscountField() {
    await mongoose.connect('mongodb://localhost:27017/shopDB');

    const result = await Product.updateMany(
        { discountPercentage: { $exists: false } },
        { $set: { discountPercentage: 0 } }
    );

    console.log(`Migrated ${result.modifiedCount} documents.`);
    await mongoose.disconnect();
}

migrateDiscountField().catch(console.error);
```

### Step 3 — Analytical Derivation of the Query

| Operator | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `updateMany` | Update ALL documents matching the filter |
| `{ discountPercentage: { $exists: false } }` | Filter: only documents where the field is missing |
| `{ $set: { discountPercentage: 0 } }` | Action: set the field to `0` |
| `result.modifiedCount` | Returns the count of updated documents |

The query is **idempotent** — running it multiple times is safe because documents that already have the field are filtered out by `$exists: false`.

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Walkthrough #4: Adding a Nested Schema Field (Array of Subdocuments)

```javascript
const orderItemSchema = new Schema({
    productId: { type: Schema.Types.ObjectId, ref: 'Product', required: true },
    quantity:  { type: Number, required: true, min: 1 },
    price:     { type: Number, required: true, min: 0 }
});

const orderSchema = new Schema({
    userId: { type: Schema.Types.ObjectId, ref: 'User', required: true },
    orderDate: { type: Date, default: Date.now },

    // NEW FIELD: Array of subdocuments
    items: [orderItemSchema],

    // NEW FIELD: Calculated total
    totalAmount: { type: Number, min: 0 }
});
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Subdocument arrays** are validated at the parent level. Each element in the `items` array is checked against `orderItemSchema` independently.

---

## 3.5 The Strict-Mode Mathematical Behavior

In strict mode, Mongoose filters out unknown fields via the following logical equivalence:

$$
\text{saved}(d) = \{ (k, v) \in d \mid k \in \text{SchemaKeys} \} \cup \text{default-applied keys}
$$

Where $d$ is the input document and $\text{SchemaKeys}$ is the set of declared field names. Any key $k \notin \text{SchemaKeys}$ is **discarded** (unless `strict: false`).

**Example:**

```javascript
const strictSchema = new Schema({ name: String });
const M = mongoose.model('M', strictSchema);

const doc = new M({ name: "Anu", rogueField: "hack-attempt" });
await doc.save();
// In MongoDB: { name: "Anu" }   ← "rogueField" is SILENTLY DROPPED
```

---

## 3.6 The Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Adding a Field

```
Q1: Is the field mandatory?
    ├── YES → set required: true
    └── NO  → set required: false (or omit)

Q2: Does the field have a sensible default?
    ├── YES → set default: <value>
    └── NO  → leave undefined

Q3: Are there only specific allowed values?
    ├── YES → use enum: [...]
    └── NO  → skip

Q4: Is the field a foreign key?
    ├── YES → use Schema.Types.ObjectId + ref: 'ModelName'
    └── NO  → use primitive type

Q5: Does the field need to be unique?
    ├── YES → set unique: true (creates a unique index)
    └── NO  → skip

Q6: Is this a production system with existing data?
    ├── YES → write a migration script (updateMany with $exists: false)
    └── NO  → restart server, new schema applies to new documents
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mongoose Field-Addition Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Developer Edits Schema File] --> B[Define New Field with type and validators]
    B --> C{Mongoose Schema Compiled into Model}
    C --> D[Application Receives Save Request]
    D --> E[Type Coercion Layer]
    E --> F{Value Provided?}
    F -- No --> G[Apply default Value]
    F -- Yes --> H[Cast to Declared Type]
    G --> H
    H --> I{Required Check}
    I -- Fails --> J[Throw ValidationError]
    I -- Passes --> K[Run All Validators]
    K --> L{All Validations Pass?}
    L -- No --> J
    L -- Yes --> M[Pre-save Middleware Hooks]
    M --> N{Strict Mode Active?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Filter Out Unknown Fields]
    N -- No --> P[Preserve All Fields]
    O --> Q[Send Document to MongoDB]
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Persist in BSON Format]
    R --> S[Return Saved Document to Application]
```

---

## 4.2 Entity-Field Relationship Diagram for a User Document

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USR[User Document Structure]
        U1[userId: ObjectId]
        U2[name: String]
        U3[email: String]
        U4[phone: String]
        U5[role: String]
        U6[createdAt: Date]
        U7[isActive: Boolean]
    end

    U1 --- U2
    U2 --- U3
    U3 --- U4
    U4 --- U5
    U5 --- U6
    U6 --- U7

    U3 -.-> V1[Validator: required, unique, match]
    U4 -.-> V2[Validator: match, 10 digits]
    U5 -.-> V3[Validator: enum, default]
    U6 -.-> V4[Validator: default Date.now]
    U7 -.-> V5[Validator: default true]
```

---

## 4.3 Migration Strategy Flow (Production-Safe Schema Evolution)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Identify Need to Add Field] --> S2[Update Mongoose Schema with new field and default]
    S2 --> S3[Deploy New Application Code]
    S3 --> S4[Run Migration Script updateMany with exists false]
    S4 --> S5{All Documents Updated?}
    S5 -- No --> S6[Investigate Errors and Re-run]
    S5 -- Yes --> S7[Verify Sample Documents in MongoDB]
    S7 --> S8[Application Reads and Writes New Field Normally]
    S6 --> S4
```

---

## 4.4 Validator Execution Order Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X1[Incoming Document] --> X2[Apply Defaults]
    X2 --> X3[Cast Types]
    X3 --> X4[Check Required Flag]
    X4 -- Missing --> X5[Reject ValidationError]
    X4 -- Present --> X6[Run Built-in Validators min, max, enum, match]
    X6 -- Fail --> X5
    X6 -- Pass --> X7[Run Custom Validate Functions]
    X7 -- Fail --> X5
    X7 -- Pass --> X8[Document Is Valid and Ready to Persist]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a Mongoose Schema. Explain how you would add a new required `email` field to an existing `User` schema with proper validation.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Mongoose Schema** is a blueprint that defines the structure, data types, and validation rules for documents stored in a MongoDB collection. It operates at the application layer of a Node.js application, enforcing data integrity before documents are persisted.

To add a new required `email` field with validation:

```javascript
const mongoose = require('mongoose');
const { Schema } = mongoose;

const userSchema = new Schema({
    name: { type: String, required: true },

    // NEW FIELD ADDED
    email: {
        type: String,
        required: true,
        unique: true,
        lowercase: true,
        trim: true,
        match: /^[\w-\.]+@([\w-]+\.)+[\w-]{2,4}$/
    }
});

module.exports = mongoose.model('User', userSchema);
```

- `required: true` — field must be present.
- `unique: true` — no two users can share the same email.
- `match` — enforces a valid email pattern via regex.
- `lowercase: true` and `trim: true` — normalize input.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the role of the `default` property in a Mongoose schema field? Provide an example.**

**Model Answer:**

The `default` property specifies a value that is **automatically assigned to a field** if no value is provided when creating a document. It ensures the field always has a meaningful value, reducing the risk of `undefined` entries in the database.

**Example:**

```javascript
const postSchema = new Schema({
    title:   { type: String, required: true },
    content: { type: String, required: true },
    views:   { type: Number, default: 0 },
    createdAt: { type: Date, default: Date.now }
});
```

If a new post is created without specifying `views`, Mongoose automatically assigns `0`. Similarly, `createdAt` is set to the current timestamp at the moment of document creation.

The `default` value can also be a **function** for dynamic values:

```javascript
createdAt: { type: Date, default: () => new Date() }
```

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of a Mongoose Schema with a neat diagram. Describe the process of **adding a new field** to an existing schema, including type, validators, and default values. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain how you would perform a **schema migration** to add a new `discountPercentage` field to an existing `Product` collection in MongoDB. Discuss the importance of idempotency. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

A **Mongoose Schema** is a structural definition for documents in a MongoDB collection. It defines:
- **Field names** (keys).
- **Data types** (String, Number, Date, etc.).
- **Validators** (required, enum, match, min/max).
- **Options** (default, unique, index, lowercase).

**Diagram of Schema-Field Relationship:**

```
+-----------------------------+
|         UserSchema          |
+-----------------------------+
|  name : String (required)   |
|  email: String (unique)     |
|  age  : Number (min: 0)     |
+-----------------------------+
            |
            v
+-----------------------------+
|     Mongoose Model          |
+-----------------------------+
|  new User({...})            |
|  User.find()                |
|  User.updateMany()          |
+-----------------------------+
            |
            v
+-----------------------------+
|     MongoDB Collection      |
+-----------------------------+
|  { name, email, age, ...}   |
+-----------------------------+
```
