In production-grade engineering, validation middleware is the **first line of defense** against:

- **SQL Injection** — Malformed input is rejected before reaching the ORM.
- **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** — Sanitization removes embedded script tags.
- **Data Corruption** — Type mismatches are caught before database writes.
- **Server Overload** — Oversized payloads are rejected at the boundary.
- **API Contract Violations** — Clients sending unexpected fields get clear 400 errors.

> [!NOTE]
> Industry-standard frameworks like **express-validator**, **Joi (by Hapi)**, **Zod**, and **class-validator** are the most common validation libraries referenced in KTU 2024 Scheme lab and exam questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Registration Patterns & Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 Pattern 1: Custom Validation Middleware (Manual Approach)

The most fundamental pattern involves writing a custom validation function. Below is the **exhaustive step-by-step construction**.

### Step 1: Define the Validator Function

```javascript
// validators/userValidator.js
const validateUser = (req, res, next) => {
  const { name, email, age } = req.body;
  const errors = [];

  // Step 1.1: Check for required field 'name'
  if (!name || typeof name !== 'string') {
    errors.push({ field: 'name', message: 'Name is required and must be a string' });
  }

  // Step 1.2: Validate email format using regex
  const emailRegex = /^[^\s@]+@[^\s@]+\.[^\s@]+$/;
  if (!email || !emailRegex.test(email)) {
    errors.push({ field: 'email', message: 'A valid email is required' });
  }

  // Step 1.3: Validate age is a positive integer
  if (age === undefined || !Number.isInteger(age) || age < 0 || age > 150) {
    errors.push({ field: 'age', message: 'Age must be an integer between 0 and 150' });
  }

  // Step 1.4: Decision Branch
  if (errors.length > 0) {
    return res.status(400).json({ status: 'fail', errors });
  }

  // Step 1.5: Sanitize (trim whitespace)
  req.body.name = name.trim();
  req.body.email = email.trim().toLowerCase();

  next();  // Pass control to next middleware
};

module.exports = { validateUser };
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reasoning Behind Each Step:**
> - **Step 1.1** ensures the **presence** of a mandatory field.
> - **Step 1.2** enforces the **format** contract (valid email structure).
> - **Step 1.3** enforces **range** and **type** constraints.
> - **Step 1.4** is the **decision node**: if any rule fails, terminate with `400`; otherwise proceed.
> - **Step 1.5** performs **sanitization**, mutating `req.body` for downstream controllers.

### Step 2: Register in the Route Definition

```javascript
// routes/userRoutes.js
const express = require('express');
const router = express.Router();
const { validateUser } = require('../validators/userValidator');
const userController = require('../controllers/userController');

// Inline registration of validation middleware
router.post('/users', validateUser, userController.createUser);
router.put('/users/:id', validateUser, userController.updateUser);

module.exports = router;
```

### Step 3: Mount the Router in the Main Application

```javascript
// app.js
const express = require('express');
const app = express();
const userRoutes = require('./routes/userRoutes');

// Step 3.1: Built-in middleware to parse JSON body
app.use(express.json());

// Step 3.2: Mount the router (contains validation middleware)
app.use('/api', userRoutes);

app.listen(3000, () => console.log('Server running on port 3000'));
```

## 3.2 Pattern 2: Using `express-validator` (Declarative Approach)

The `express-validator` library provides a chainable, declarative API. This is the **most frequently tested** pattern in KTU exams.

### Step 1: Install the Library

```bash
npm install express-validator
```

### Step 2: Define Validation Rules as an Array

```javascript
// validators/authValidator.js
const { body, validationResult } = require('express-validator');

const registerValidationRules = () => {
  return [
    body('username')
      .trim()
      .notEmpty().withMessage('Username is required')
      .isLength({ min: 3, max: 20 }).withMessage('Username must be 3-20 chars'),

    body('email')
      .trim()
      .isEmail().withMessage('Must be a valid email')
      .normalizeEmail(),

    body('password')
      .isLength({ min: 8 }).withMessage('Password must be at least 8 chars')
      .matches(/[A-Z]/).withMessage('Password must contain an uppercase letter')
      .matches(/\d/).withMessage('Password must contain a number')
  ];
};
```

### Step 3: Create the Validation Result-Checking Middleware

```javascript
const validate = (req, res, next) => {
  const errors = validationResult(req);
  if (!errors.isEmpty()) {
    return res.status(400).json({ 
      status: 'fail', 
      errors: errors.array() 
    });
  }
  next();
};
```

### Step 4: Register Both at the Route Level

```javascript
const { registerValidationRules, validate } = require('../validators/authValidator');

router.post(
  '/register',
  registerValidationRules(),   // Array of validation middlewares
  validate,                    // Single result-checking middleware
  authController.register      // Controller (only reached if valid)
);
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two separate registrations?** The `registerValidationRules()` function returns an **array** of middleware functions. Express automatically iterates over the array. The separate `validate` middleware then aggregates the results and decides whether to proceed.

## 3.3 Pattern 3: Schema-Based Validation with Joi

```javascript
const Joi = require('joi');

const userSchema = Joi.object({
  name: Joi.string().min(2).max(50).required(),
  email: Joi.string().email().required(),
  age: Joi.number().integer().min(0).max(150)
});

const validateWithJoi = (req, res, next) => {
  const { error, value } = userSchema.validate(req.body, { abortEarly: false });
  if (error) {
    return res.status(400).json({ 
      status: 'fail', 
      errors: error.details.map(d => d.message) 
    });
  }
  req.body = value;  // Replace with sanitized value
  next();
};
```

## 3.4 Registration Order Decision Matrix

The **order** in which validation middleware is registered determines the **flow of control**. Use this decision matrix:

| Scenario | Correct Order |
|---|---|
| Login form with username + password | `[validateUsername, validatePassword, loginHandler]` |
| File upload with metadata | `[authMiddleware, fileTypeCheck, fileSizeCheck, uploadHandler]` |
| Public API with rate limiting | `[rateLimiter, validatePayload, publicHandler]` |
| Nested resource update | `[authMiddleware, validateParentId, validateChild, updateHandler]` |

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU exam pitfall is placing the **controller** before the **validator** in the registration list. This renders the validator completely inert, as the controller will execute first and the validator will never run.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Request Lifecycle Through Validation Middleware

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client Sends HTTP Request] --> B[Application-Level Middleware: express.json]
    B --> C[Router-Level Middleware: authCheck]
    C --> D[Route-Level Validation Middleware 1: validateName]
    D --> E{Is Name Valid?}
    E -- No --> F[Send 400 Bad Request with Errors]
    E -- Yes --> G[Route-Level Validation Middleware 2: validateEmail]
    G --> H{Is Email Valid?}
    H -- No --> F
    H -- Yes --> I[Route-Level Validation Middleware 3: validatePassword]
    I --> J{Is Password Valid?}
    J -- No --> F
    J -- Yes --> K[Sanitization Middleware: trim and normalize]
    K --> L[Controller Function: processRequest]
    L --> M[Send 200 OK Response]
    F --> N[End: Client receives error]
    M --> N
```

> [!NOTE]
> This flowchart illustrates the **canonical validation pipeline**. Each node represents an executable function in the Express middleware chain. The diamond shapes (`{}`) are **decision points** that branch the flow between success (`Yes`) and failure (`No`) paths.

## 4.2 Middleware Registration Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph AppLayer["Application Layer: app.js"]
        A1[app.use express.json] --> A2[app.use apiRouter]
    end

    subgraph RouterLayer["Router Layer: userRoutes.js"]
        R1[router.use authMiddleware] --> R2[router.post users]
    end

    subgraph RouteLayer["Route Layer: Inline Registration"]
        V1[validateName] --> V2[validateEmail]
        V2 --> V3[sanitizeInput]
        V3 --> C1[userController.createUser]
    end

    A2 --> R1
    R2 --> V1
```

## 4.3 Three-Tier Registration Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TB
    L1["TIER 1: Application-Level\nScope: ENTIRE APP\nExample: app.use express.json"]
    L2["TIER 2: Router-Level\nScope: ALL ROUTES IN ROUTER\nExample: router.use authCheck"]
    L3["TIER 3: Route-Level\nScope: SINGLE ROUTE\nExample: router.post path validate handler"]

    L1 -->|mounts| L2
    L2 -->|mounts| L3

    L3 -.terminates.-> L4["Controller OR 400 Error Response"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diagram demonstrates the **narrowing scope** principle: Tier 1 sees all requests, Tier 2 sees only requests for its router, and Tier 3 sees only requests matching its specific HTTP method and path. Validation middleware should be placed at **Tier 3** for maximum precision and minimum performance overhead.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Q: Define Validation Middleware. List the three ways it can be registered in an Express.js application.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Validation Middleware is a function in the Express.js request-response pipeline that inspects, sanitizes, and verifies the correctness of incoming request data (`req.body`, `req.query`, `req.params`) before it reaches the controller.

**Three Registration Methods (2 Marks):**
1. **Application-level:** `app.use(validationMiddleware)` — applies to all routes.
2. **Router-level:** `router.use(validationMiddleware)` — applies to all routes in a router.
3. **Route-level (inline):** `router.post('/path', validationMiddleware, handler)` — applies only to a specific route.

---

### Question 2: Short Conceptual
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Q: What is the difference between `next()` and `next(err)` in the context of validation middleware? Why is this distinction important?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Difference (2 Marks):**
- `next()`: Passes control to the **next** middleware in the chain. Used when validation **passes**.
- `next(err)`: Skips the remaining non-error middlewares and jumps directly to the **error-handling middleware**. Used when validation **fails catastrophically** (e.g., database unreachable).

**Importance (1 Mark):** Calling `next()` incorrectly when validation fails allows invalid data to reach the controller. Conversely, calling `res.send()` inside validation means no further middlewares (including error handlers) will execute. The distinction enables **centralized error management**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Q: A student portal requires a registration endpoint. Design and implement a complete Express.js solution that uses `express-validator` to register validation middleware for the following fields:**
- `username` (required, 4-15 alphanumeric characters)
- `email` (required, valid email format, normalized to lowercase)
- `password` (required, minimum 8 characters, must contain at least one digit)
- `role` (optional, must be either "student" or "admin")

**(a) [7 Marks] Write the complete Express.js code that defines and registers all validation middlewares for the registration endpoint.**

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain the execution flow when a request with an invalid email and a missing password is received. What HTTP status code is returned, and what does the response body contain?**

---

#### Solution to Question A

**(a) Complete Code [7 Marks]:**

```javascript
// app.js
const express = require('express');
const app = express();
const { body, validationResult } = require('express-validator');
const authRoutes = require('./routes/authRoutes');

app.use(express.json());
app.use('/api/auth', authRoutes);

app.listen(3000);

// routes/authRoutes.js
const router = express.Router();
const authController = require('../controllers/authController');

// Validation rules array
const registerRules = [
  body('username')
    .trim()
    .notEmpty().withMessage('Username is required')
    .isLength({ min: 4, max: 15 }).withMessage('Username must be 4-15 chars')
    .isAlphanumeric().withMessage('Username must be alphanumeric'),

  body('email')
    .trim()
    .notEmpty().withMessage('Email is required')
    .isEmail().withMessage('Invalid email format')
    .normalizeEmail(),

  body('password')
    .notEmpty().withMessage('Password is required')
    .isLength({ min: 8 }).withMessage('Password must be at least 8 chars')
    .matches(/\d/).withMessage('Password must contain a digit'),

  body('role')
    .optional()
    .isIn(['student', 'admin']).withMessage('Role must be student or admin')
];

// Validation result middleware
const validate = (req, res, next) => {
  const errors = validationResult(req);
  if (!errors.isEmpty()) {
    return res.status(400).json({ status: 'fail', errors: errors.array() });
  }
  next();
};

// Inline registration
router.post('/register', registerRules, validate, authController.register);

module.exports = router;
```

**Explanation of Registration Strategy [embedded in code above]:**
- The `registerRules` array is passed as an **inline argument** to `router.post()`.
- Express iterates the array, executing each rule as a separate middleware.
- The `validate` function aggregates all errors via `validationResult(req)`.
- The `authController.register` is the **final handler**, reached only on full success.

---

**(b) Execution Flow Analysis [7 Marks]:**

**Step-by-Step Flow for an Invalid Request:**

1. **Request arrives:** `POST /api/auth/register` with body `{ "username": "abhi", "email": "invalid-email", "password": "" }`.

2. **Tier 1 — Application-level middleware:** `express.json()` parses the JSON body. `req.body` is now populated.

3. **Tier 2 — Router dispatch:** Express matches the request to `router.post('/register', ...)`.

4. **Tier 3.1 — `body('username')` validator:** Checks that `"abhi"` is alphanumeric and 4-15 chars. **Passes.**

5. **Tier 3.2 — `body('email')` validator:** Checks `isEmail()`. The value `"invalid-email"` **fails** the regex. The error is stored on `req` but execution **continues** (express-validator does not terminate early by default).

6. **Tier 3.3 — `body('password')` validator:** Checks `notEmpty()`. The empty string `""` **fails**. Error stored.

7. **Tier 3.4 — `body('role')` validator:** Field is missing, but it's `optional()`. **Passes** (no error).

8. **`validate` middleware:** Calls `validationResult(req)`, which returns an object with `isEmpty() === false`. Therefore, the middleware sends:

   **HTTP Status Code:** `400 Bad Request`

   **Response Body:**
   ```json
   {
     "status": "fail",
     "errors": [
       { "path": "email", "msg": "Invalid email format" },
       { "path": "password", "msg": "Password is required" }
     ]
   }
   ```

9. **Termination:** The response is sent. The `authController.register` is **never executed**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Q: Write a custom validation middleware in Express.js (without using third-party libraries) for a `POST /api/products` endpoint. The valid product object must satisfy:**
- `name`: non-empty string, max 100 characters
- `price`: positive number, up to 2 decimal places
- `category`: must be one of "electronics", "clothing", "books"
- `stock`: non-negative integer

**(a) [7 Marks] Implement the custom validation middleware and register it in the route definition.**

**(b) [7 Marks] Write the corresponding error-handling middleware. Demonstrate how `next(err)` is used to forward a database connection failure from a controller to the error handler.**

---

#### Solution to Question B

**(a) Custom Validation Middleware [7 Marks]:**

```javascript
// middleware/validateProduct.js

const VALID_CATEGORIES = ['electronics', 'clothing', 'books'];

const validateProduct = (req, res, next) => {
  const { name, price, category, stock } = req.body;
  const errors = [];

  // Validate name
  if (!name || typeof name !== 'string' || name.trim().length === 0) {
    errors.push('Name is required and must be a non-empty string');
  } else if (name.length > 100) {
    errors.push('Name must not exceed 100 characters');
  }

  // Validate price
  if (typeof price !== 'number' || price <= 0) {
    errors.push('Price must be a positive number');
  } else if (!/^\d+(\.\d{1,2})?$/.test(price.toString())) {
    errors.push('Price must have at most 2 decimal places');
  }

  // Validate category
  if (!VALID_CATEGORIES.includes(category)) {
    errors.push(`Category must be one of: ${VALID_CATEGORIES.join(', ')}`);
  }

  // Validate stock
  if (!Number.isInteger(stock) || stock < 0) {
    errors.push('Stock must be a non-negative integer');
  }

  if (errors.length > 0) {
    return res.status(400).json({ status: 'fail', errors });
  }

  // Sanitize: trim name
  req.body.name = name.trim();
  next();
};

module.exports = validateProduct;
```

**Route Registration:**

```javascript
const express = require('express');
const router = express.Router();
const validateProduct = require('../middleware/validateProduct');
const productController = require('../controllers/productController');

router.post('/products', validateProduct, productController.create);

module.exports = router;
```

---

**(b) Error-Handling Middleware and `next(err)` Demonstration [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1: Error-Handling Middleware (Defined Last in `app.js`):**

```javascript
// app.js
const express = require('express');
const app = express();
const productRoutes = require('./routes/productRoutes');

app.use(express.json());
app.use('/api', productRoutes);

// Error-handling middleware — MUST have 4 parameters
app.use((err, req, res, next) => {
  console.error('Error Stack:', err.stack);
  
  res.status(err.statusCode || 500).json({
    status: 'error',
    message: err.message || 'Internal Server Error'
  });
});

app.listen(3000);
```

**Step 2: Controller That Triggers `next(err)`:**

```javascript
// controllers/productController.js
const db = require('../config/database');

exports.create = async (req, res, next) => {
  try {
    // Attempt to connect to database
    const connection = await db.connect();
    if (!connection) {
      const error = new Error('Database connection failed');
      error.statusCode = 503;
      return next(error);  // Forwards to error-handling middleware
    }
    
    await db.query('INSERT INTO products SET ?', req.body);
    res.status(201).json({ status: 'success', data: req.body });
  } catch (err) {
    next(err);  // Catches any thrown error
  }
};
```

**Flow Explanation:**

- The validation middleware runs first. If valid, it calls `next()` and the controller executes.
- The controller attempts a database operation. If the connection fails, it constructs an `Error` object with `statusCode: 503` and calls `next(err)`.
- Calling `next(err)` **bypasses** all subsequent route handlers and triggers the **error-handling middleware** (identified by its 4-parameter signature).
- The error middleware logs the stack and returns a structured JSON `500` or `503` response.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **The error-handling middleware MUST have exactly 4 parameters** `(err, req, res, next)`. Express identifies error handlers by the arity of the function. A 3-parameter function will be treated as a regular middleware, and `next(err)` will never reach it.
> 2. **Forgetting to register the error middleware LAST** in `app.js` is a common mistake. Express executes middlewares in registration order, so an error handler registered before the routes will never see errors from those routes.
> 3. **Calling `next()` without parentheses in a callback** (e.g., inside a `try/catch`) is a frequent typo that silently breaks the flow. Always use `next()` or `next(err)` as function calls.
> 4. **Sending a response after `next()` is called** results in the `ERR_HTTP_HEADERS_SENT` runtime error. Once `res.json()` is called, the chain must terminate.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- [ ] Validation Middleware is a function with signature `(req, res, next)` that checks request data.
- [ ] Three registration tiers: **Application-level** (`app.use`), **Router-level** (`router.use`), **Route-level inline** (`router.post(path, mw, handler)`).
- [ ] Inline registration is the **most common** pattern for validation; arguments are executed **left-to-right**.
- [ ] `next()` continues the chain; `next(err)` jumps to the **error-handling middleware** (must have 4 parameters).
- [ ] `express-validator` provides chainable validators like `body('field').isEmail().normalizeEmail()`.
- [ ] Joi is used for **schema-based** validation with `Joi.object({...})`.
- [ ] The validation result-checking middleware (e.g., `validationResult(req)`) must be placed **after** the rule array.
- [ ] The `validate` middleware sends `400 Bad Request` on failure and `req.body` is sanitized **before** the controller runs.
- [ ] The order of middleware matters: **parse body first, then validate, then handle**.
- [ ] Sanitization (trim, normalize) modifies `req.body` for downstream use.
- [ ] Built-in middleware `express.json()` must be registered **before** any body validation middleware.
- [ ] Error-handling middleware is recognized by Express only if it has **exactly 4 parameters**.
- [ ] Multiple validations can be chained: `[validateName, validateEmail, validatePassword, handler]`.
- [ ] `optional()` modifier in `express-validator` skips validation if the field is absent.
- [ ] **Common pitfall:** Placing the controller **before** the validator in the route definition list — this disables validation entirely.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Refactoring to MVC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Refactoring to MVC # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Refactoring to MVC** is the disciplined, behavior-preserving process of restructuring an existing web application's source code so that its concerns are cleanly separated into three interconnected components: the **Model** (data and business logic), the **View** (presentation/user interface), and the **Controller** (input handling and application flow). The objective is to transform tangled, monolithic code into a modular architecture without altering the application's external functionality. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, refactoring to MVC is treated as a **software engineering design activity** that improves **internal structure** (readability, maintainability, extensibility) while preserving **external behavior** (functional outputs remain identical). It is a specific application of the broader *Refactoring* concept introduced by Martin Fowler, applied within the context of the **Model-View-Controller architectural pattern** (Trygve Reenskaug, 1979). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4)**: Refactoring to MVC is the bridge between ad-hoc scripting and proper software architecture. It directly addresses Course Outcomes related to *designing modular web applications* and *applying architectural patterns*. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen Imagine a small roadside food stall where **one person** simultaneously: - Takes the customer's order, - Cooks the food, - Hands it over and collects the money. This works for a small crowd but collapses under load. Now imagine a **structured restaurant**: | Role in Restaurant | Equivalent in MVC | Responsibility | |---|---|---| | **Waiter** (takes order, delivers food) | **Controller** | Receives user input, coordinates response | | **Chef** (cooks using ingredients/recipes) | **Model** | Business logic, data access, rules | | **Plate & Presentation** (how food looks) | **View** | UI rendering, output formatting | | **Customer** | **User / Browser** | Initiates requests, consumes output | > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Takeaway**: The waiter never cooks. The chef never decorates the plate. The plate never decides what's inside it. This is **separation of concerns (SoC)** — the soul of MVC. --- ## 1.3 What is "Refactoring" (Pre-Requisite Concept)? > [!NOTE] > **Refactoring** (Martin Fowler, 1999) = *Improving the design of existing code without changing its external behavior.* Refactoring to MVC specifically means restructuring code so it conforms to the MVC architectural pattern. **Key properties of any refactoring**: - **Behavior-preserving**: Inputs and outputs remain identical. - **Structure-improving**: Code becomes cleaner, modular, testable. - **Incrementally applied**: Performed in small, verifiable steps. ## 1.4 Why Refactor to MVC? (The Motivation) A typical *non-MVC* web application (e.g., a single PHP file mixing HTML, SQL, and business logic) suffers from: - **Tight coupling**: UI changes ripple into database code. - **Low cohesion**: One file does many unrelated things. - **Difficult testing**: Logic is embedded inside HTML. - **Poor reusability**: Business rules cannot be reused across pages. > [!IMPORTANT] > The **MVC refactoring goal** is to **decouple** these concerns so the application becomes **modular, testable, maintainable, and scalable** — properties that KTU examiners explicitly reward in design questions. --- ## 1.5 Core Vocabulary (Mandatory KTU Terminology) | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **Model** | Encapsulates application data, state, and business rules; independent of UI | | **View** | Renders the Model's state into a form suitable for interaction (HTML, JSON, etc.) | | **Controller** | Handles user input, manipulates the Model, and selects an appropriate View | | **Separation of Concerns (SoC)** | Each component has one well-defined responsibility | | **Coupling** | Degree of interdependence between modules; **low coupling** is a goal | | **Cohesion** | Degree to which elements inside a module belong together; **high cohesion** is a goal | | **Tight Coupling (Anti-pattern)** | When changing one component forces changes in another | | **Spaghetti Code (Anti-pattern)** | Control flow tangled across HTML, SQL, and logic — the prime refactoring target | --- ## 1.6 Physical / Conceptual Constants & Standards > [!NOTE] > Refactoring itself has no physical constants, but the following are **standard KTU-recognized metrics** used to evaluate refactoring quality: > - **Cyclomatic Complexity (McCabe, 1976)**: Number of linearly independent paths through a program; **target $\leq 10$** per module. > - **Lines of Code (LOC) per function**: Recommended **$\leq 50$** for maintainability. > - **Cohesion Metric (LCOM - Lack of Cohesion of Methods)**: **Lower is better**. > - **Coupling Metric (CBO - Coupling Between Objects)**: **Lower is better**. $$\text{Goal of MVC Refactoring} = \min(\text{Coupling}) \;\wedge\; \max(\text{Cohesion})$$ The arrow $\wedge$ here is read as "and" (logical conjunction) — the refactorer aims to **simultaneously minimize coupling and maximize cohesion**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of MVC — Operational Breakdown ### 2.1.1 The Model The Model is the **heart** of the application. It represents: - **Data structures** (entities, DTOs, domain objects) - **Business rules** (validation, calculations, workflows) - **Persistence** (database access, file I/O, API calls) > [!IMPORTANT] > The Model **must not** know that a View exists. It exposes a public API (getters/setters/methods) and broadcasts state-change **events** to interested parties (typically the Controller or a registered observer). **Characteristics of a "good" Model**: - Framework-agnostic - Independently unit-testable - Reusable across multiple Views and Controllers --- ### 2.1.2 The View The View is the **presentation layer**. It: - Receives data from the Model (directly or via Controller) - Renders output (HTML, JSON, XML, PDF, console text) - Forwards user input back to the Controller (via events/forms) > [!NOTE] > A View should contain **minimal logic** — only formatting, looping, and conditional display of data. **No business rules** belong here. **Common View technologies in KTU syllabus context**: - Server-rendered: JSP, Thymeleaf, PHP templates, EJS - Client-rendered: React, Vue, Angular (for REST-based MVC) - Data formats: JSON, XML (for API endpoints) --- ### 2.1.3 The Controller The Controller is the **mediator**. It: - Receives HTTP requests (or user events) - Interprets and validates input - Invokes the appropriate Model methods - Selects the next View to render > [!TIP] > The Controller is **thin** — it should not contain business logic, only orchestration. Heavy logic in a Controller is a code smell called "**Fat Controller**" and is itself a refactoring target. --- ## 2.2 The Refactoring Process — Step-by-Step Operational Logic Refactoring to MVC is **not a single leap**; it is a **sequence of disciplined steps**. The KTU-recommended workflow: 1. **Identify the existing code smells** — locate mixed concerns (SQL in HTML, logic in views). 2. **Establish a safety net** — write characterization tests that capture current behavior. 3. **Extract the Model layer** — pull out data structures, business rules, and DB code into dedicated classes/modules. 4. **Extract the View layer** — isolate presentation markup from logic; create templates with placeholders. 5. **Extract the Controller layer** — create request handlers that coordinate Model and View. 6. **Introduce a routing/dispatch mechanism** — front controller pattern to map URLs to controllers. 7. **Apply design patterns** — Observer (Model→View notification), Strategy (View selection), Factory (controller creation). 8. **Run tests after every step** — ensure behavior is preserved. 9. **Measure improvement** — compute cyclomatic complexity, coupling, cohesion metrics. 10. **Document the new architecture** — class diagrams, sequence diagrams. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake (KTU valuation)**: Refactoring without a test suite is **dangerous** — students often claim "behavior preserved" but cannot prove it. Always mention **characterization tests** as a prerequisite. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table summarizes every formula, metric, and design principle a KTU 2024 student must know for this topic. | Concept | Formula / Definition | Symbol / Variable | Target / Unit | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---|---| | Cyclomatic Complexity | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | $E$ = edges, $N$ = nodes, $P$ = connected components | $V(G) \leq 10$ | Measures control-flow complexity | | Alternative V(G) | $V(G) = \pi + 1$ | $\pi$ = number of predicate nodes | $V(G) \leq 10$ | Simpler computation for exams | | Coupling Between Objects | $\text{CBO}(C) = \vert \text{Classes coupled to } C \vert$ | — | Minimize | Measures inter-module dependency | | Lack of Cohesion (LCOM) | $LCOM = P - Q$ if $P > Q$, else $0$ | $P$ = method pairs not sharing fields, $Q$ = pairs sharing fields | $\rightarrow 0$ | Measures intra-module focus | | Lines of Code (per function) | $LOC_{fn}$ | — | $\leq 50$ | Maintainability guideline | | Maintainability Index | $MI = 171 - 5.2 \ln V - 0.23 V - 16.2 \ln L + 50 \sin \sqrt{2.46 C}$ | $V$ = Halstead volume, $L$ = LOC, $C$ = cyclomatic complexity | $MI \geq 65$ (good) | Composite quality score | | SoC Principle | $\text{Responsibility}(M) \cap \text{Responsibility}(V) = \emptyset$ | $M$ = Model, $V$ = View | Disjoint sets | No overlap in duties | | MVC Invariant | $\text{Controller} = f(\text{Request}) \rightarrow (\text{Model update}, \text{View selection})$ | — | Deterministic | Defines controller behavior | | Observer Pattern Notification | $\text{Model} \xrightarrow{\text{notify}} \text{Registered Observers (Views)}$ | — | Loose coupling | Decouples Model from View | --- ## 2.4 Design Patterns Associated with MVC Refactoring > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU Module 4 frequently tests **pattern identification** in refactored code. Memorize the following: | Pattern | Role in MVC | Intent | |---|---|---| | **Front Controller** | Single entry point for all requests | Centralizes routing & security | | **Observer** | Model notifies Views of changes | Decouples Model from View | | **Strategy** | Controller selects View dynamically | Pluggable views | | **Factory** | Creates Controller/Model instances | Centralized object creation | | **Composite View** | Builds views from sub-views | Reusable UI components | | **Data Transfer Object (DTO)** | Carries data between layers | Reduces remote calls | | **Active Record / DAO** | Model persistence abstraction | Separates DB from logic | --- ## 2.5 Before vs After Refactoring — A High-Yield Comparison | Dimension | Before (Spaghetti) | After (MVC) | |---|---|---| | **File count** | 1–2 monolithic files | Many small, focused files | | **Coupling** | High (everything depends on everything) | Low (one-way dependencies) | | **Cohesion** | Low (mixed concerns) | High (one responsibility) | | **Testability** | Very hard | Easy (Model tested in isolation) | | **Reusability** | Near zero | High (Model reused across views) | | **Team productivity** | Merge conflicts, slow | Parallel development possible | | **Cyclomatic Complexity** | Often $> 20$ | Typically $\leq 10$ | | **Bug surface** | Large | Localized | --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > MVC refactoring is **not academic** — it is the foundation of nearly every modern web framework: > - **Java**: Spring MVC, Struts, JSF > - **Python**: Django (MVT), Flask + extensions > - **JavaScript**: Express.js, NestJS, Next.js > - **PHP**: Laravel, CodeIgniter, Symfony > - **Ruby**: Ruby on Rails > - **C#**: ASP.NET MVC The act of refactoring legacy code into MVC is a **daily professional task** in enterprise software maintenance — making it a high-yield topic for KTU's employment-oriented outcomes (PO5, PO6). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Worked Example ## 3.1 Derivations of Key Metrics Used in MVC Refactoring ### 3.1.1 Derivation of Cyclomatic Complexity $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ Cyclomatic complexity measures the number of **independent paths** through a program's control-flow graph (CFG). It was introduced by Thomas J. McCabe in 1976. $$\begin{aligned} \text{Let } G &= (N, E) \text{ be the control-flow graph} \\ \text{where } N &= \text{number of nodes (statements)} \\ E &= \text{number of edges (control transfers)} \\ P &= \text{number of connected components (usually } P = 1 \text{ for a single function)} \end{aligned}$$ By Euler's theorem for planar graphs applied to a program's CFG: $$\begin{aligned} V(G) &= E - N + 2P \\ \text{For a single function: } P &= 1 \\ \Rightarrow V(G) &= E - N + 2 \end{aligned}$$ **Equivalent practical formula** (using decision points): $$\begin{aligned} V(G) &= \pi + 1 \\ \text{where } \pi &= \text{number of predicate nodes} \\ &\quad (\texttt{if}, \texttt{else if}, \texttt{case}, \texttt{while}, \texttt{for}, \texttt{catch}, \texttt{\&\&}, \texttt{||}) \end{aligned}$$ > [!TIP] > **Intuition**: Every `if` or loop adds a new path the program *could* take. $V(G)$ counts these alternative routes. A function with $V(G) = 1$ has a single straight-line path (no decisions). $V(G) = 10$ means 10 independent test cases are needed to cover all branches — manageable. $V(G) > 10$ is a refactoring signal. --- ### 3.1.2 Worked Numerical Example — Cyclomatic Complexity Consider this **pre-refactor** PHP code (a common anti-pattern): $$\begin{aligned} \texttt{function processOrder(\$order)} \; \{ \\ \quad \texttt{if (\$order->isValid())} \; \{ \\ \quad\quad \texttt{if (\$order->hasStock())} \; \{ \\ \quad\quad\quad \texttt{\$order->charge();} \\ \quad\quad\quad \texttt{if (\$order->isPaid())} \; \{ \\ \quad\quad\quad\quad \texttt{\$order->ship();} \\ \quad\quad\quad \} \; \texttt{else} \; \{ \\ \quad\quad\quad\quad \texttt{throw new PaymentException();} \\ \quad\quad\quad \} \\ \quad\quad \} \; \texttt{else} \; \{ \\ \quad\quad\quad \texttt{throw new OutOfStockException();} \\ \quad\quad \} \\ \quad \} \; \texttt{else} \; \{ \\ \quad\quad \texttt{throw new InvalidOrderException();} \\ \quad \} \\ \texttt{\}} \end{aligned}$$ **Counting predicate nodes** $\pi$: $$\begin{aligned} \pi &= (\texttt{isValid}) + (\texttt{hasStock}) + (\texttt{isPaid}) \\ &= 1 + 1 + 1 = 3 \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} V(G) &= \pi + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4 \end{aligned}$$ This is *technically* acceptable, but if `processOrder` were mixed with **view rendering and SQL**, complexity would skyrocket. --- ### 3.1.3 Analytical Matrix — Code Smell → MVC Refactoring The following matrix is **exam-ready** and directly answers KTU's "explain the refactoring steps" type questions. | # | Code Smell (Before) | Location | Refactoring Action | After (MVC Layer) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | SQL queries embedded in HTML | View | Extract to a data-access class | **Model** (DAO/Repository) | | 2 | Business validation in JSP/PHP template | View | Move to domain object methods | **Model** | | 3 | `$_POST` handling inside HTML form handler | View | Move to dedicated handler class | **Controller** | | 4 | HTML markup mixed with `echo` of data | View | Replace with template placeholders | **View** (cleaned) | | 5 | Global variables carrying state | Everywhere | Encapsulate in Model class | **Model** | | 6 | Direct DB connection in every page | Multiple | Centralize in a single Model layer | **Model** | | 7 | Hard-coded navigation URLs | View | Centralize routing in Front Controller | **Controller** | | 8 | Calculation logic in templates | View | Move to Model method | **Model** | | 9 | Authentication check duplicated | Everywhere | Move to a single controller filter | **Controller** | | 10 | No separation of data + display | View | Introduce a DTO/ViewModel | **Model + View** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip**: When asked "Explain how you would refactor X to MVC," present a **table like the above** for full marks. It demonstrates systematic thinking. --- ## 3.2 Step-by-Step Refactoring Walk-Through (The "Canonical" Example) ### Starting Point — A Single-File Monolith (BEFORE) A typical pre-refactor `login.php`: $$\begin{aligned} &\texttt{} \\ &\texttt{Welcome \$user";} \\ &\texttt{\quad\} else \{ } \\ &\texttt{\quad\quad echo "

Login Failed

";} \\ &\texttt{\quad\}} \\ &\texttt{\} else \{ } \\ &\texttt{\quad echo '
User: Pass:} \\ &\texttt{\quad\quad
';} \\ &\texttt{\}} \\ &\texttt{?>} \end{aligned}$$ **Code smells present**: - DB connection logic in a UI file - SQL query in presentation layer - HTML embedded with branching logic - No separation of input / processing / output - SQL injection vulnerability (also a refactoring target) --- ### Refactoring Step 1 — Extract the Model Create a dedicated `UserModel.php` (or `User.java`, `models/User.js`): $$\begin{aligned} &\texttt{class UserModel \{ } \\ &\texttt{\quad private \$pdo;} \\ &\texttt{\quad public function \_\_construct(\$pdo) \{ \$this->pdo = \$pdo; \}} \\ &\texttt{\quad public function authenticate(\$username, \$password): bool \{ } \\ &\texttt{\quad\quad \$stmt = \$this->pdo->prepare(} \\ &\texttt{\quad\quad\quad "SELECT id FROM users WHERE name = :u AND pass = :p");} \\ &\texttt{\quad\quad \$stmt->execute([':u' => \$username, ':p' => \$password]);} \\ &\texttt{\quad\quad return \$stmt->fetchColumn() !== false;} \\ &\texttt{\quad\}} \\ &\texttt{\}} \end{aligned}$$ **What changed**: - DB code **moved** out of view - Parameterized queries (security improvement as a side effect) - Method `authenticate()` is now **unit-testable** in isolation --- ### Refactoring Step 2 — Extract the Controller Create `LoginController.php`: $$\begin{aligned} &\texttt{class LoginController \{ } \\ &\texttt{\quad private \$model;} \\ &\texttt{\quad public function \_\_construct(UserModel \$model) \{ } \\ &\texttt{\quad\quad \$this->model = \$model;} \\ &\texttt{\quad\}} \\ &\texttt{\quad public function handle(): array \{ } \\ &\texttt{\quad\quad if (\$\_SERVER["REQUEST\_METHOD"] !== "POST") \{ } \\ &\texttt{\quad\quad\quad return ["view" => "login\_form", "data" => []];} \\ &\texttt{\quad\quad\}} \\ &\texttt{\quad\quad \$ok = \$this->model->authenticate(} \\ &\texttt{\quad\quad\quad \$\_POST["user"], \$\_POST["pass"]);} \\ &\texttt{\quad\quad return ["view" => \$ok ? "welcome" : "login\_failed",} \\ &\texttt{\quad\quad\quad "data" => ["user" => \$\_POST["user"]]];} \\ &\texttt{\quad\}} \\ &\texttt{\}} \end{aligned}$$ **What changed**: - All **input handling** is centralized here - Controller returns **data + view name** — no HTML, no SQL - Decision logic stays; business logic does not --- ### Refactoring Step 3 — Extract the View Create `views/login_form.php`, `views/welcome.php`, `views/login_failed.php` — each containing **only HTML with placeholder syntax**. **`views/welcome.php`**: $$\begin{aligned} &\texttt{

Welcome !

} \\ &\texttt{

Go to dashboard

} \end{aligned}$$ **`views/login_form.php`**: $$\begin{aligned} &\texttt{
} \\ &\texttt{\quad } \\ &\texttt{\quad } \\ &\texttt{\quad } \\ &\texttt{
} \end{aligned}$$ --- ### Refactoring Step 4 — Introduce a Front Controller A single `index.php` (the front controller) routes all requests: $$\begin{aligned} &\texttt{// index.php (Front Controller)} \\ &\texttt{\$path = \$\_SERVER["REQUEST\_URI"];} \\ &\texttt{switch (\$path) \{ } \\ &\texttt{\quad case "/login":} \\ &\texttt{\quad\quad \$ctrl = new LoginController(\$model);} \\ &\texttt{\quad\quad \$result = \$ctrl->handle();} \\ &\texttt{\quad\quad include "views/" . \$result["view"] . ".php";} \\ &\texttt{\quad\quad break;} \\ &\texttt{\quad // ... other routes} \\ &\texttt{\}} \end{aligned}$$ --- ### Final State — AFTER (MVC Architecture) $$\begin{aligned} \text{Project structure} &= \begin{cases} \texttt{index.php} & \text{: Front Controller (router)} \\ \texttt{controllers/} & \text{: LoginController, ...} \\ \texttt{models/} & \text{: UserModel, ...} \\ \texttt{views/} & \text{: login\_form.php, welcome.php, ...} \\ \texttt{config/} & \text{: database.php, ...} \end{cases} \end{aligned}$$ **Quantitative improvement** (measurable for KTU): $$\begin{aligned} V(G)_{\text{before}} &= 4 \text{ (in login.php, with hidden DB/HTML coupling)} \\ V(G)_{\text{after}} &= 2 \text{ (in LoginController::handle)} \\ \text{Coupling}_{\text{after}} &= \text{Low (Controller } \leftrightarrow \text{ Model; Controller } \rightarrow \text{ View)} \\ \text{Cohesion}_{\text{after}} &= \text{High (each class has one job)} \end{aligned}$$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Refactoring Verification Checklist A **complete** MVC refactor must satisfy all rows below. Use this as a self-evaluation tool. | # | Verification Question | Expected Answer | If No → Action | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Does the Model know about the View? | **No** | Decouple using Observer pattern | | 2 | Does the View contain business rules? | **No** | Move logic to Model | | 3 | Does the Controller access the DB directly? | **No** | Move to Model | | 4 | Are SQL queries present outside the Model? | **No** | Move to Model/DAO | | 5 | Is HTML generation outside the View? | **No** | Move to View templates | | 6 | Can the Model be unit-tested without a server? | **Yes** | Remove framework dependencies | | 7 | Are there any global variables? | **No** | Encapsulate in Model | | 8 | Is there a single entry point (Front Controller)? | **Yes** | Add router | | 9 | Are there characterization tests? | **Yes** | Write tests first | | 10 | Does cyclomatic complexity of each method $\leq 10$? | **Yes** | Decompose further | > [!WARNING] > **Refactor only when tests pass** — this is Fowler's golden rule. KTU students often skip testing; the verifier checklist above makes it explicit. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level MVC Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) > [!NOTE] > The following diagram shows the **canonical MVC flow** with one-way dependencies, illustrating the result of a successful refactor. ```mermaid flowchart LR user1[User Browser] fc1[Front Controller / Router] ctrl1[Controller] model1[Model] db1[(Database)] view1[View Template] response1[HTTP Response] user1 -- HTTP Request --> fc1 fc1 -- Dispatches to --> ctrl1 ctrl1 -- Invokes business logic --> model1 model1 -- CRUD operations --> db1 model1 -- Returns data --> ctrl1 ctrl1 -- Selects view and passes data --> view1 view1 -- Renders HTML --> response1 response1 -- Sent back --> user1 style user1 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1976d2,color:#000 style fc1 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#f57c00,color:#000 style ctrl1 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#7b1fa2,color:#000 style model1 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#388e3c,color:#000 style db1 fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b,color:#000 style view1 fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#00838f,color:#000 style response1 fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#616161,color:#000 ``` **Reading the diagram**: - Arrows are **one-way** — the hallmark of loose coupling. - The **Model** never points at the View (in the passive MVC variant) or, in active MVC, notifies Views through the Observer pattern (dashed). - The **Controller** is the only component that knows about both Model and View. --- ## 4.2 Refactoring Process Flow (Mermaid State Diagram) > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **exam-friendly** workflow for refactoring to MVC. Memorize the sequence. ```mermaid flowchart TD s1[Step 1: Identify code smells] s2[Step 2: Write characterization tests] s3[Step 3: Extract Model layer] s4[Step 4: Extract View layer] s5[Step 5: Extract Controller layer] s6[Step 6: Introduce Front Controller] s7[Step 7: Apply design patterns] s8[Step 8: Run tests and measure metrics] s9{All tests pass and metrics improved?} s10[Step 9: Document architecture] s11[Refactor complete] s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 --> s6 --> s7 --> s8 --> s9 s9 -- Yes --> s10 --> s11 s9 -- No --> s3 style s1 fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828,color:#000 style s2 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00,color:#000 style s3 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000 style s4 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000 style s5 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000 style s6 fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#ad1457,color:#000 style s7 fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#00695c,color:#000 style s8 fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000 style s9 fill:#eceff1,stroke:#37474f,color:#000 style s10 fill:#f1f8e9,stroke:#558b2f,color:#000 style s11 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 ``` **Note the feedback loop**: If metrics do not improve or tests fail, we **return to Step 3** and re-extract. Refactoring is iterative. --- ## 4.3 Before vs After — Block Architecture Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph before[BEFORE Refactor Monolith] b1[login.php] b2[HTML markup] b3[SQL queries] b4[Business logic] b5[Form handling] b1 --- b2 b1 --- b3 b1 --- b4 b1 --- b5 end subgraph after[AFTER Refactor MVC] a1[index.php Front Controller] a2[LoginController] a3[UserModel] a4[Views templates] a5[(Database)] a1 --> a2 a2 --> a3 a2 --> a4 a3 --> a5 end style b1 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 style b2 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 style b3 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 style b4 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 style b5 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 style a1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style a2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style a3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style a4 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style a5 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 ``` **Visual contrast**: - **Left (red)**: One node, everything tangled. This is *high coupling, low cohesion*. - **Right (green)**: Multiple focused nodes with clear directional flow. This is the *refactoring outcome*. --- ## 4.4 Observer Pattern in MVC (Model → View Notification) ```mermaid flowchart LR obs[Observer Interface] v1[View 1] v2[View 2] v3[View 3] mdl[Model Subject] mdl -. implements .-> obs v1 -. implements .-> obs v2 -. implements .-> obs v3 -. implements .-> obs mdl -- notify state change --> v1 mdl -- notify state change --> v2 mdl -- notify state change --> v3 v1 -- update view --> mdl v2 -- update view --> mdl v3 -- update view --> mdl style obs fill:#ede7f6,stroke:#4527a0,color:#000 style v1 fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000 style v2 fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000 style v3 fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000 style mdl fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 ``` **Reading**: The Model doesn't know *which* Views exist. It just broadcasts via the Observer interface. Each View registers itself and reacts to changes — the **Open/Closed Principle** in action. --- ## 4.5 Class Diagram (UML-Style Block View) of Refactored Application ```mermaid flowchart TB classF[FrontController] classC[LoginController] classM[UserModel] classV[View Interface] classV1[WelcomeView] classV2[LoginFormView] classD[Database] classF --> classC classC --> classM classC --> classV classV <|.. classV1 classV <|.. classV2 classM --> classD style classF fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 style classC fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000 style classM fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000 style classV fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#00695c,color:#000 style classV1 fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#00695c,color:#000 style classV2 fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#00695c,color:#000 style classD fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#ad1457,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **`<|..`** in Mermaid denotes **realization (interface implementation)**. The two View classes realize a common `View` interface, enabling the Strategy pattern. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1. Define refactoring. Why is it important in the context of MVC architecture?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]` **Model Answer (3 marks)**: > **Refactoring** is the process of restructuring existing computer code **without changing its external behavior** to improve its internal structure, readability, and maintainability (Fowler, 1999). > **Importance in MVC context**: > 1. **Enforces separation of concerns** — separates Model, View, and Controller into distinct modules. > 2. **Improves testability** — Models can be unit-tested independently of the UI. > 3. **Reduces coupling** — minimizes inter-dependencies between layers, enabling parallel development. > 4. **Enables extensibility** — new views or controllers can be added without modifying existing code (Open/Closed Principle). > Without refactoring, web applications evolve into **spaghetti code** with high cyclomatic complexity ($V(G) > 20$), making them impossible to maintain. --- ### **Q2. List any three design patterns commonly used when refactoring to MVC and state their roles.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks)**: | # | Pattern | Role in MVC Refactoring | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Front Controller** | Provides a single entry point (e.g., `index.php`) that routes all HTTP requests to appropriate controllers. | | 2 | **Observer** | Allows the Model to notify registered Views of state changes without knowing the Views exist. | | 3 | **Strategy** | Enables the Controller to select a View dynamically at runtime, supporting multiple output formats. | > **Alternative correct patterns**: Factory, Composite View, DAO (Data Access Object), MVC itself, Template Method, Decorator. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > KTU End-Semester Examinations follow an **internal choice** in Part B. The student answers **one** of two alternatives. Both alternatives are provided below with complete sub-parts and full model solutions. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]` #### **Q. (a) Explain the MVC architectural pattern with a neat block diagram. Differentiate between Model, View, and Controller with examples. (7 marks)** **Model Answer**: **Definition (1 mark)**: MVC is an architectural pattern that separates an application into three interconnected components: **Model** (data + business logic), **View** (presentation), and **Controller** (input handling). It enforces **separation of concerns** (SoC). **Block Diagram (2 marks)**: Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes. Arrows should be one-way, showing: $$\text{Request} \rightarrow \text{Controller} \rightarrow \text{Model} \rightarrow \text{Controller} \rightarrow \text{View} \rightarrow \text{Response}$$ **Differences (4 marks)**: | Aspect | Model | View | Controller | |---|---|---|---| | **Purpose** | Manages data and business rules | Renders the user interface | Handles user input and coordinates flow | | **Example** | `User.java` with `authenticate()` | `login.jsp`, HTML template | `LoginController.java` with `handle()` method | | **Depends on** | Nothing (framework-agnostic) | Model data (read-only) | Model + View selection | | **Knows about** | Itself only | Model state | Model and View registry | | **Contains** | Validation, DB queries, calculations | Markup, formatting, loops | Request parsing, dispatch | | **Testable with** | JUnit, plain unit tests | Snapshot tests, browser | Mock objects for Model | > **Key Rule (1 mark)**: The Model **must not** import or reference any View class. The Controller is the **only** component that knows both Model and View. --- #### **Q. (b) Discuss the step-by-step process of refactoring a legacy web application to follow the MVC pattern. Illustrate with an example. (7 marks)** **Model Answer**: **Introduction (1 mark)**: Refactoring to MVC transforms tangled monolithic code into a modular structure with clear responsibility boundaries. The process is **behavior-preserving** and must be backed by **characterization tests** that document current behavior before changes begin. **Steps (5 marks)**: 1. **Identify code smells** — locate SQL in HTML, business logic in templates, global state, fat controllers. (1 mark) 2. **Establish safety net** — write characterization tests capturing current outputs for representative inputs. (1 mark) 3. **Extract the Model** — move DB queries, validation, and business rules into dedicated classes. Introduce a Data Access Object (DAO) for persistence. (1 mark) 4. **Extract the View** — isolate markup into template files; replace inline logic with template placeholders. (1 mark) 5. **Extract the Controller** — create request-handler classes; introduce a Front Controller (router) for centralized URL dispatch. (1 mark) **Example (1 mark)**: A pre-refactor `login.php` (refer Section 3.2 of these notes) is split into `UserModel` (data + auth), `LoginController` (flow), `views/login_form.php` and `views/welcome.php` (presentation), with `index.php` acting as the Front Controller. Behavior is verified identical via tests, but cyclomatic complexity drops from $V(G) = 4$ in a tangled file to $V(G) = 2$ in the focused controller. **Conclusion**: The refactor produces a structure satisfying the **verifier checklist** in Section 3.3 — low coupling, high cohesion, independent testability, and one-way dependencies. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]` #### **Q. (a) What is a "code smell"? List and briefly explain five code smells that motivate refactoring to MVC. (7 marks)** **Model Answer**: **Definition (1 mark)**: A **code smell** (Kent Beck) is a surface indication in source code that usually corresponds to a deeper problem — not a bug, but a symptom of poor design that increases risk of future bugs and slows development. **Five code smells motivating MVC refactoring (1 mark each)**: 1. **Spaghetti Code** — Control flow is tangled across markup, SQL, and logic in a single file. Symptom: a 500-line `process.php` that mixes HTML, queries, and calculations. 2. **Duplicate Code** — The same DB connection or business rule appears in 20 different pages. Symptom: changing the schema requires editing 20 files. → Solution: centralized Model. 3. **Long Method / God Class** — A single function does input parsing, validation, DB access, and HTML rendering. Cyclomatic complexity $V(G) > 15$. → Solution: split into Model, View, Controller. 4. **Feature Envy** — A method in the View accesses the Model's data excessively, indicating misplaced responsibility. → Solution: move logic into the Model. 5. **Shotgun Surgery** — A small change (e.g., renaming a column) requires modifying many files. → Solution: encapsulate DB access in a single Model class. 6. *(Optional 6th)* **Primitive Obsession** — Passing raw arrays/strings instead of structured DTOs between layers. → Solution: introduce domain objects in the Model. **Link to MVC (1 mark)**: Each smell is resolved by relocating the offending code into its proper MVC layer — proving that MVC is not just a structure but a **diagnostic and corrective framework**. --- #### **Q. (b) Explain the Front Controller pattern and the Observer pattern as used in MVC. Draw a sequence diagram showing their interaction. (7 marks)** **Model Answer**: **Front Controller Pattern (2.5 marks)**: The **Front Controller** is a single entry point (e.g., `index.php`, Spring's `DispatcherServlet`) that handles **all** incoming HTTP requests. It: - Receives every request (e.g., `/login`, `/cart`, `/checkout`). - Performs common preprocessing: authentication, logging, locale resolution. - Routes the request to the appropriate Controller using a routing table or configuration. - Centralizes error handling and security. > **Benefit**: Eliminates duplicated boilerplate across pages; aligns with SoC. **Observer Pattern (2.5 marks)**: The **Observer** pattern defines a **one-to-many dependency** between objects. In MVC, the **Model acts as the Subject** and **Views act as Observers**. When Model state changes: 1. The Model calls `notify()` on all registered Observers. 2. Each View receives the notification and refreshes its display. 3. The Model never holds direct references to concrete View classes — only to an `Observer` interface. > **Benefit**: Views can be added/removed at runtime without modifying the Model (Open/Closed Principle). **Sequence Diagram (2 marks)**: ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User participant FC as FrontController participant C as Controller participant M as Model participant V as View U->>FC: GET /login FC->>C: dispatch to LoginController C->>M: authenticate(user, pass) M-->>C: return success C->>V: select WelcomeView V-->>U: render HTML response Note over M,V: In active MVC, Model also notifies View via Observer M-->>V: notify state change V->>M: get updated data M-->>V: return data ``` **Conclusion**: The Front Controller centralizes request handling, while the Observer decouples Model from View — together they make MVC both **centralized** (entry point) and **decoupled** (internal communication). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places where students lose marks on Refactoring-to-MVC questions:** > > 1. **Confusing "refactor" with "rewrite"** — Refactoring preserves external behavior. A student who says "we will rewrite the application" loses the **definition mark**. > 2. **Skipping tests** — Any refactoring answer that does not mention **characterization tests / safety net** loses marks in 2024 Scheme, which emphasizes verification. > 3. **Drawing two-way arrows between Model and View** — In the canonical MVC, dependencies are **one-way**. Two-way arrows in a diagram cost 1–2 marks. > 4. **Putting business logic in the View** — When asked "where does validation go?" the answer is **Model**, not the View. > 5. **Forgetting the Front Controller** — Modern MVC has a router/front controller. Mentioning it explicitly earns the **+1 edge mark**. > 6. **No metrics** — The 2024 scheme values **measurable improvement**. Citing cyclomatic complexity reduction (e.g., from $V(G) = 8$ to $V(G) = 3$) scores higher than vague "it became cleaner" claims. > 7. **Mixing up Observer and Mediator** — Model → View notification is **Observer**, not Mediator. The Controller is closer to a Mediator. > 8. **Confusing MVC with three-tier architecture** — Three-tier is **physical** (Client, App Server, DB Server); MVC is **logical** (Model, View, Controller). Examiners test this distinction. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-density revision checklist for KTU 2024 exam day:** - [x] **Refactoring** = restructuring code **without changing behavior** (Fowler, 1999). - [x] **MVC** = Model + View + Controller, enforcing **separation of concerns**. - [x] **Model**: data + business logic; framework-agnostic; unit-testable; **does NOT know the View**. - [x] **View**: presentation only; minimal logic; renders Model data. - [x] **Controller**: thin mediator; handles input; invokes Model; selects View. - [x] **One-way dependencies**: Request → Controller → Model → Controller → View → Response. - [x] **Refactoring steps**: Identify smells → Write tests → Extract Model → Extract View → Extract Controller → Add Front Controller → Apply patterns → Verify metrics. - [x] **Mandatory safety net**: **characterization tests** must exist before refactoring. - [x] **Key patterns**: Front Controller, Observer, Strategy, Factory, Composite View, DAO. - [x] **Key metrics**: - Cyclomatic complexity: $V(G) = E - N + 2P = \pi + 1$, target $\leq 10$. - Maintainability Index target: $\geq 65$. - Low coupling (CBO), high cohesion (low LCOM). - [x] **Front Controller**: single entry point for all requests (e.g., `index.php`, `DispatcherServlet`). - [x] **Observer pattern**: Model notifies Views; Views register as Observers. - [x] **Difference from 3-tier**: MVC is **logical**; 3-tier is **physical deployment**. - [x] **Frameworks using MVC**: Spring MVC, Django (MVT), Laravel, Express, Rails, ASP.NET MVC. - [x] **Goal formula**: $\min(\text{Coupling}) \wedge \max(\text{Cohesion})$. - [x] **Anti-patterns to recognize**: Spaghetti code, God class, Fat Controller, Feature Envy, Shotgun Surgery, Primitive Obsession. - [x] **Behavior preservation is non-negotiable** — if outputs change, it is not refactoring, it is rewriting. - [x] **Iterative**: refactor → test → measure → re-refactor if metrics do not improve.

topics.title: Refactoring Validation Layer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Refactoring Validation Layer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Refactoring the Validation Layer** is the disciplined, behavior-preserving restructuring of the application's input-validation logic to improve its internal structure, readability, extensibility, and reusability — **without altering its external observable behavior**.

In the context of **Web Application Development**, the **validation layer** is the architectural tier responsible for verifying that incoming data (HTTP requests, form submissions, API payloads, query parameters, file uploads) conforms to predefined syntactic, semantic, and business constraints **before** it reaches the domain/business logic or persistence layer.

| Term | KTU 2024 Definition |
|---|---|
| **Validation** | The process of ensuring that data satisfies correctness, completeness, and security rules. |
| **Layer** | A logical partition in the MVC/MVVM architecture that groups related responsibilities. |
| **Refactoring** | A controlled transformation technique introduced by *Martin Fowler* that improves design **after** the code is written, without changing functionality. |
| **Validation Layer** | The dedicated component(s) that intercept and verify data between the Presentation and Business tiers. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Airport Security Checkpoint**
> 
> Imagine a busy international airport. Before any passenger can board a plane, they must pass through a **security checkpoint**. This checkpoint does ONE thing well: it checks your boarding pass, ID, luggage weight, and visa documents. It does NOT fly the plane, sell tickets, or refuel aircraft.
> 
> Now imagine what happens when the airport grows: there are 15 different check-in counters, each inventing its OWN security rules. One accepts expired passports, another weighs bags incorrectly, a third rejects valid visas. Total chaos.
> 
> **Refactoring the Validation Layer** is like rebuilding that security system into a **single, standardized, automated checkpoint** that:
> - Enforces the same rules everywhere.
> - Is easy to update (when visa laws change, only ONE place is updated).
> - Returns clear error messages.
> - Doesn't slow down legitimate travelers.

In software terms, the **"checkpoint"** is your *centralized validator*, and **"passengers"** are *incoming HTTP requests*.

---

## 1.3 Why Does the Validation Layer Need Refactoring?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 4**
> Validation code often becomes a maintenance nightmare due to:
> 1. **Duplication** — same rules written in HTML, JavaScript, and server-side code.
> 2. **Scattering** — validation logic buried inside Controllers, Models, and Views.
> 3. **Tight Coupling** — validators tied to specific UI frameworks.
> 4. **Inconsistency** — client-side and server-side rules drift apart.
> 5. **Poor Error Reporting** — cryptic messages or generic "Invalid Input" responses.

### The Three Pillars of a Healthy Validation Layer

- **🔒 Correctness** — Rejects malformed/malicious data.
- **📢 Clarity** — Returns precise, actionable error messages.
- **♻️ Reusability** — Same rule applies across REST APIs, forms, batch jobs, and CLI tools.

---

## 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics (KTU Board Standard)

| Metric | Standard Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Cyclomatic Complexity of Validator** | $\leq 10$ | Number of independent paths in validator code. |
| **Validation Latency** | $< \mathbf{50\,ms}$ | Time to validate a typical request. |
| **Rule Coverage** | $\mathbf{100\%}$ | Every input field must have at least one rule. |
| **DRY Ratio** | $\mathbf{1.0}$ | No duplicated validation rule across layers. |
| **Error Message Specificity** | $\geq \mathbf{90\%}$ | % of errors that point to the exact failing field. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Typical (Un-refactored) Validation Layer

Most early-stage web applications follow the **"Fat Controller"** anti-pattern:

```
HTTP Request
     ↓
[Controller]  ← Validation lives here
     ↓
[Model.save()] ← Some validation also here
     ↓
[Database]
```

> [!WARNING]
> This violates the **Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)** from Robert C. Martin's SOLID design principles.

---

## 2.2 Code Smells That Trigger Validation-Layer Refactoring

| # | Code Smell | Symptom | KTU Term |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Duplicated Validation** | Same email regex in 5 controllers | *Shotgun Surgery* |
| 2 | **Long Method** | One method contains 200 lines of `if-else` checks | *Long Method* |
| 3 | **Primitive Obsession** | Phone numbers stored as raw strings | *Primitive Obsession* |
| 4 | **Feature Envy** | Controller reaches deep into Model to validate | *Feature Envy* |
| 5 | **Inappropriate Intimacy** | Validator knows DB schema details | *Coupling* |
| 6 | **Conditional Complexity** | Nested `if (a && (b || c))` chains | *Switch Statements* |
| 7 | **Refused Bequest** | Subclass overrides all parent validators | *Hierarchy Misuse* |

---

## 2.3 The Refactoring Catalog (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **These are the exact refactoring patterns the KTU examiner expects you to name, recognize, and apply.**

### 2.3.1 **Extract Validator Object**
Pulls all validation logic out of the controller into a dedicated class (e.g., `UserRegistrationValidator`, `CreateOrderValidator`).

### 2.3.2 **Replace Conditional Logic with Polymorphism**
Instead of:

$$
\text{if } \text{type} = \text{"email"} \text{ then } R_1 \text{ else if } \text{type} = \text{"phone"} \text{ then } R_2 \dots
$$

Create an abstract `Validator` with concrete subclasses: `EmailValidator`, `PhoneValidator`.

### 2.3.3 **Introduce Specification Pattern**
Encapsulates a business rule as a *composable boolean object*:

$$
S = \big( \text{EmailUnique} \,\wedge\, \text{Age} \geq 18 \,\wedge\, \text{CountryAllowed} \big)
$$

Multiple specifications can be combined using **AND (`∧`)**, **OR (`∨`)**, **NOT (`¬`)** operators.

### 2.3.4 **Introduce Form Object / DTO (Data Transfer Object)**
A Form Object is a transient class that:
- Receives raw request data.
- Performs validation.
- Transforms data into a clean shape before passing to the domain layer.

$$
\text{FormObject} : \text{RawRequest} \xrightarrow{\text{validate + transform}} \text{CleanDTO} \rightarrow \text{DomainModel}
$$

### 2.3.5 **Chain of Responsibility**
Validators are linked in a chain. Each validator either handles the request or passes it to the next:

$$
V_1 \rightarrow V_2 \rightarrow V_3 \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow V_n \rightarrow \text{OK}
$$

If any $V_i$ fails, the chain short-circuits with an error.

### 2.3.6 **Decorator Pattern for Cross-Cutting Concerns**
Wraps a base validator to add behaviour without modifying it:
- `LoggingValidator(Validator)`
- `CachingValidator(Validator)`
- `RateLimitValidator(Validator)`

### 2.3.7 **Move Validation to Domain Model (DDD)**
Pushes validation into the entity itself via `isValid()`, invariants, and factory methods — so an invalid object **cannot exist**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use $\vert$ for absolute value, $\mid$ for divisibility — NEVER use bare pipes inside markdown tables.**

| # | Concept | Mathematical / Logical Notation | Unit / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Validation Function | $V : \text{Input} \rightarrow \{\text{Valid}, \text{Invalid}\}$ | Boolean |
| 2 | Error Set | $E = \{e_1, e_2, \dots, e_n\}$ | Set of error objects |
| 3 | Composite Specification | $S = S_1 \wedge S_2 \wedge \dots \wedge S_n$ | Boolean algebra |
| 4 | Negation | $\neg S$ | Boolean algebra |
| 5 | Disjunction | $S_1 \vee S_2$ | Boolean algebra |
| 6 | Validation Coverage | $C = \dfrac{\mid \text{Fields with rules} \mid}{\mid \text{Total fields} \mid} \times 100\%$ | Percentage |
| 7 | Cyclomatic Complexity | $M = E - N + 2P$ | Integer |
| 8 | Rule Independence | Two rules $R_i, R_j$ are independent iff $R_i \wedge R_j \not\Rightarrow R_i \vee R_j$ | Logical |
| 9 | Refactoring Invariant | $f_{\text{before}}(x) \equiv f_{\text{after}}(x) \;\; \forall x \in \text{Input}$ | Functional |
| 10 | Chain Depth | $\text{depth} = \mid \text{Chain} \mid$ | Integer |

Where:
- $E$ = number of edges (control flow)
- $N$ = number of nodes (statements)
- $P$ = number of connected components (typically $P = 1$)

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application |
|---|---|
| **FinTech** | Validating KYC documents, transaction limits, currency codes. |
| **Healthcare** | Enforcing HIPAA-compliant patient data formats. |
| **E-Commerce** | Cart total sanity checks, inventory availability, coupon validity. |
| **IoT** | Sensor data range checks, timestamp monotonicity. |
| **Government** | Tax form schema validation against published XML schemas (XSD). |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Refactoring Procedure & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Canonical 7-Step Refactoring Procedure (Martin Fowler + KTU Adaptation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the exact procedure expected in KTU 14-mark answers. Memorize the order.**

### **Step 1 — Identify the Validation Code Smell**
Search the codebase for `if`-statements, regex patterns, or manual null-checks inside Controllers or Services. Use static analysis tools (e.g., SonarQube, ESLint) to flag *Long Method* and *Duplicated Code*.

### **Step 2 — Write Characterization Tests (Golden Master)**
Before touching the code, write tests that capture the **current behavior** of the validation. These serve as a safety net:

$$
\forall \, x \in \text{Sample Inputs}: \quad \text{Test}_{\text{before}}(x) \equiv \text{Test}_{\text{after}}(x)
$$

### **Step 3 — Extract Validation into a Dedicated Object**
Move the validation code from the Controller into a new class named after its responsibility:

$$
\text{Controller.validate}() \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{OrderValidator.validate}() \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{OrderValidator}
$$

### **Step 4 — Apply Pattern Selection**
Choose ONE of:
- **Strategy / Polymorphism** (if validation branches on type)
- **Specification Pattern** (if rules need composability)
- **Chain of Responsibility** (if order of checks matters)
- **Form Object / DTO** (if request mapping is also needed)

### **Step 5 — Introduce Centralized Error Reporting**
Replace scattered `throw` statements or inline messages with a unified error-response contract:

$$
\text{ErrorResponse} = \{ \text{field}, \text{code}, \text{message}, \text{rejectedValue} \}
$$

### **Step 6 — Run Tests & Verify Invariant**
Re-run the characterization tests. They MUST pass without modification. This proves behavior preservation:

$$
\text{Test}_{\text{after}}(x) \equiv \text{Test}_{\text{before}}(x) \quad \forall x
$$

### **Step 7 — Remove Duplication & Document**
Delete now-redundant validation from old locations. Add Javadoc/PHPDoc comments explaining the rule's source (e.g., *"RFC 5321 email format"*).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Analytical Walkthrough

**Scenario:** A `UserController` in a Laravel/Spring-style application contains the following anti-pattern (pseudocode):

```
function register(request):
    if request.name is null: error
    if request.email not matches "/^[\w.+-]+@[\w-]+\.[\w.-]+$/": error
    if request.password.length < 8: error
    if userRepository.existsByEmail(request.email): error
    if request.age < 18: error
    if request.country not in ALLOWED_LIST: error
    // ... 5 more checks ...
    user = new User(request.name, request.email, ...)
    userRepository.save(user)
```

### **Refactoring Transformation — Step-by-Step**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Before:} \\
&\text{Controller} \;\supset\; \{\text{Validation}\} \;\cup\; \{\text{Persistence}\} \quad \text{(SRP Violation)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{After:} \\
&\text{Controller} \;\rightarrow\; \text{RegisterUserRequest (Form Object)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Validator Chain} \\
&\phantom{\text{Controller} \;\rightarrow\;} \bigl[ V_1 \,:\, \text{NotNull} \bigr] \rightarrow \bigl[ V_2 \,:\, \text{EmailFormat} \bigr] \\
&\phantom{\text{Controller} \;\rightarrow\;} \rightarrow \bigl[ V_3 \,:\, \text{StrongPassword} \bigr] \rightarrow \bigl[ V_4 \,:\, \text{UniqueEmail} \bigr] \\
&\phantom{\text{Controller} \;\rightarrow\;} \rightarrow \bigl[ V_5 \,:\, \text{MinimumAge(18)} \bigr] \rightarrow \bigl[ V_6 \,:\, \text{AllowedCountry} \bigr] \\
&\phantom{\text{Controller} \;\rightarrow\;} \rightarrow \text{User Entity (Domain Invariants)} \rightarrow \text{Persistence}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Specification Composition (Algebraic Form)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{register}} &= \text{NotNull}(name) \,\wedge\, \text{EmailFormat}(email) \,\wedge\, \text{StrongPwd}(pwd) \\
&\quad \wedge\, \text{Unique}(email, DB) \,\wedge\, \text{MinAge}(age, 18) \,\wedge\, \text{InList}(country, L)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where the allowed country list:

$$
L = \{ \text{"IN"}, \text{"US"}, \text{"UK"}, \text{"AE"}, \text{"SG"} \}
$$

### **Error Aggregation (Set Union)**

$$
E_{\text{total}} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} E_i
$$

If multiple fields fail simultaneously, **all errors are returned at once** (not just the first one). This improves UX drastically.

---

## 3.3 Before-After Comparative Matrix

| Aspect | ❌ Before Refactoring | ✅ After Refactoring |
|---|---|---|
| **Location of Rules** | Scattered (Controller + Model + JS) | Centralized in Validator classes |
| **Reusability** | None — copy-pasted per controller | One Validator per use-case, reusable |
| **Testability** | Hard — requires full HTTP request | Easy — pure unit tests on validator |
| **SRP Compliance** | Violated | Satisfied |
| **Open/Closed Principle** | Modifying rules = changing Controller | Add new validator without touching existing code |
| **Cyclomatic Complexity** | $M \approx 15\text{–}25$ | $M \leq 5$ per validator |
| **Error Reporting** | First-error-only | Aggregated error list |
| **Localization Support** | Hard-coded English strings | Externalized to `messages.properties` / `.json` |
| **Performance** | Repeated DB queries for uniqueness | Cached via Specification pattern |
| **Maintainability Index** | Low | High |

---

## 3.4 Measurement of Refactoring Success

$$
\text{Maintainability Index (MI)} = \max\left(0, \; 171 - 5.2 \ln V - 0.23 M - 16.2 \ln L + 50 \sin\sqrt{2.46}\right)
$$

Where $V$ = Halstead Volume, $M$ = Cyclomatic Complexity, $L$ = Lines of Code. A refactored validation layer typically improves MI from the **40–65 band** (difficult to maintain) to the **85–100 band** (highly maintainable).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
