Hashing converts a user-supplied password into a fixed-length, irreversible string using a one-way cryptographic function, ensuring that even if the database is compromised, the original passwords cannot be recovered. During login, the server hashes the submitted password with the stored salt and compares it against the saved hash using a constant-time comparison. **MD5 is unsafe** because (1) it is cryptographically broken with known collision attacks, (2) it is fast by design — modern GPUs can compute billions of MD5 hashes per second, enabling brute-force cracking, and (3) it lacks a built-in salt mechanism, making it vulnerable to rainbow table attacks. Industry-standard alternatives include **bcrypt**, **Argon2**, and **scrypt**, which are intentionally slow and support per-user salts.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*List any three security attributes that must be set on a session cookie and state the attack each attribute mitigates.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **`HttpOnly`** — Prevents JavaScript (e.g., `document.cookie`) from reading the cookie, mitigating **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** based session theft. *(1 mark)*
2. **`Secure`** — Ensures the cookie is only transmitted over HTTPS, mitigating **network eavesdropping / Man-in-the-Middle attacks**. *(1 mark)*
3. **`SameSite=Lax` or `Strict`** — Restricts the cookie from being sent on cross-site requests, mitigating **Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)**. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Never** write "I will store the password" without specifying *"hashed with a salted algorithm."* This is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**.
> - When asked about cookies, **always list the attributes** (HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite) — merely saying "set a cookie" scores zero marks.
> - Distinguish clearly between **Authentication (login)** and **Authorization (role-based access).** Examiners explicitly test for this separation.
> - Use a **sequence diagram or step-by-step numbered list** in your answer — visual answers consistently score 1–2 marks higher than pure prose.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Describe the complete User Login Process in a web application. Your answer must cover client-side form submission, server-side validation, password verification, and session establishment.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

The User Login Process is a multi-phase authentication workflow:

1. **Client-Side Form Rendering (1 mark):** When a user navigates to `/login`, the server returns an HTML form with `method="POST"` and `action="/login"`, containing fields for username/email and password. A hidden CSRF token field is included to prevent cross-site request forgery.

2. **Secure Transmission (1 mark):** On submission, the browser sends an HTTP POST request over **HTTPS (TLS 1.2+)**. The credentials are encoded in the request body with `Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded` or `application/json`.

3. **Server-Side Validation (1.5 marks):** The server re-validates the CSRF token, checks input types and lengths, and sanitizes the input to prevent SQL injection. Client-side validation is bypassed; the server is the sole security authority.

4. **Credential Verification (2 marks):** A parameterized SQL query retrieves the user record by email/username. The submitted password is hashed using **bcrypt** (with a per-user salt) and compared against the stored hash using a **constant-time comparison**. A precomputed dummy hash is used when the user is not found, to prevent timing-based user enumeration.

5. **Session Establishment & Redirection (1.5 marks):** On success, the server creates a session record (storing `userId`, `createdAt`, `lastSeenAt`) and returns a `Set-Cookie` header with the session ID. The cookie carries `HttpOnly`, `Secure`, and `SameSite=Lax` attributes. The user receives a **HTTP 302 redirect** to the dashboard or the originally requested URL.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Compare server-side session-based authentication with JWT-based authentication. When would you choose one over the other?*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

| Criterion | Server-Side Session (Stateful) | JWT (Stateless) | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **State Storage** | Session data lives on the server (memory/Redis/DB) | All claims are embedded in the token itself | 1 |
| **Session ID vs. Token** | Opaque random ID (~128 bits) sent as cookie | Base64Url-encoded JSON with cryptographic signature | 1 |
| **Scalability** | Requires a shared session store across all server instances (Redis) | Stateless — any server with the secret can verify | 1 |
| **Revocation** | Instant — delete the server-side record | Hard — requires a revocation list or short expiry | 1 |
| **Storage Location** | Cookie with HttpOnly + Secure (XSS-safe) | Often localStorage (XSS-vulnerable) unless stored as HttpOnly cookie | 1 |
| **CSRF Protection** | Requires SameSite cookie + CSRF token | Use `Authorization: Bearer` header (not auto-attached) | 1 |
| **Best Use Case** | Traditional server-rendered web apps (Express + EJS, Django) | REST APIs, SPAs, mobile apps, microservices | 1 |

**Conclusion (1 mark from overall coherence):** Choose **sessions** for traditional web apps where immediate logout/revocation matters; choose **JWT** for stateless APIs and distributed microservices where horizontal scalability is the priority.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the security challenges in implementing a user login system. List and describe at least four common attacks and their mitigations.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **SQL Injection (1.5 marks):** Attacker injects malicious SQL via the login form. *Mitigation:* Use parameterized queries / prepared statements; never concatenate user input into SQL strings. Example: `db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = ?', [email])`.

2. **Cross-Site Request Forgery — CSRF (1.5 marks):** Attacker tricks the authenticated user's browser into submitting a forged login or state-changing request. *Mitigation:* Implement the **synchronizer token pattern** (CSRF token embedded in form and verified server-side) plus `SameSite=Lax` cookies.

3. **Brute-Force / Credential Stuffing (1.5 marks):** Attacker tries many passwords or uses leaked credential lists. *Mitigation:* Implement **rate limiting** (e.g., max 5 failed attempts per 15 minutes), **account lockout**, **CAPTCHA** after repeated failures, and **MFA**.

4. **Session Hijacking / Fixation (1.5 marks):** Attacker steals or sets the victim's session ID. *Mitigation:* Use `HttpOnly`, `Secure`, `SameSite` cookies; regenerate the session ID after successful login (prevent fixation); enforce TLS; bind session to user-agent/IP (with caution).

5. **User Enumeration (1 mark, bonus):** Attacker probes valid email addresses via differential responses. *Mitigation:* Return a **generic error message** ("Invalid credentials") and use **constant-time** hash comparison even when the user does not exist.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Write a Node.js / Express code snippet to handle a POST `/login` request. The code must include input validation, bcrypt password verification, session creation, and secure cookie setting. Explain each section.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

```javascript
const express = require('express');
const bcrypt   = require('bcrypt');
const session  = require('express-session');
const db       = require('./db');  // PostgreSQL pool

const app = express();
app.use(express.urlencoded({ extended: false }));
app.use(session({
  secret: process.env.SESSION_SECRET,  // (1)
  resave: false,
  saveUninitialized: false,
  cookie: {
    httpOnly: true,    // (2)
    secure: true,      // (3)
    sameSite: 'Lax',   // (4)
    maxAge: 30 * 60 * 1000  // (5)
  }
}));

app.post('/login', async (req, res) => {
  const { username, password } = req.body;

  // (6) Input shape validation
  if (!username || !password || password.length > 128) {
    return res.status(400).send('Invalid input');
  }

  // (7) Parameterized database lookup
  const { rows } = await db.query(
    'SELECT id, password_hash FROM users WHERE email = $1',
    [username]
  );
  const user = rows[0];

  // (8) Constant-time hash comparison with dummy fallback
  const dummy = '$2b$12$abcdefghijklmnopqrstuu7Z0K8Q9YpV0m4kF3bR2wX5nL8sT1eO.';
  const ok = await bcrypt.compare(password, user ? user.password_hash : dummy);

  // (9) Generic error to prevent enumeration
  if (!user || !ok) {
    return res.status(401).send('Invalid username or password');
  }

  // (10) Regenerate session ID and store user identity
  req.session.regenerate(() => {
    req.session.userId = user.id;
    res.redirect('/dashboard');
  });
});
```

**Explanation of Annotations (each annotation = ~0.5–0.7 marks):**
- **(1) `SESSION_SECRET`:** Cryptographic key for signing the session ID cookie — must be a long random value stored in environment variables.
- **(2) `httpOnly: true`:** Blocks JavaScript access to the cookie (XSS mitigation).
- **(3) `secure: true`:** Cookie is only sent over HTTPS.
- **(4) `sameSite: 'Lax'`:** Cookie is not sent on cross-site POST requests (CSRF mitigation).
- **(5) `maxAge: 30 minutes`:** Sliding session expiry.
- **(6) Input validation:** Type and length checks server-side.
- **(7) Parameterized query:** Prevents SQL injection.
- **(8) Dummy hash + bcrypt.compare:** Constant-time path defeats user enumeration via timing.
- **(9) Generic 401 message:** Avoids revealing whether the username exists.
- **(10) `session.regenerate()`:** Prevents session fixation by issuing a new session ID upon privilege escalation (login).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (Tick before entering the exam hall):**

- ☐ The login process has **5 phases**: form render → secure POST → server validation → password verify → session/token create → redirect.
- ☐ **Always hash** passwords with **bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2** — never MD5/SHA-1/plain text.
- ☐ Each user must have a **unique salt** to defeat rainbow tables.
- ☐ Use **constant-time comparison** to prevent timing attacks; use a **dummy hash** when the user is not found.
- ☐ Session cookies must carry **`HttpOnly`, `Secure`, `SameSite=Lax`/`Strict`** attributes.
- ☐ **Session ID** must be cryptographically random with **≥128 bits** of entropy.
- ☐ Return a **generic error message** ("Invalid credentials") — never reveal whether the username or password was wrong.
- ☐ Validate the `returnUrl` parameter against an **allow-list** to prevent open redirect attacks.
- ☐ Distinguish **Authentication** (login) from **Authorization** (role/permission check).
- ☐ **bcrypt cost factor** doubles hash time per increment — 12 is the 2024 standard.
- ☐ **JWT = Header.Payload.Signature** (three Base64Url segments separated by dots).
- ☐ Use **HTTPS/TLS 1.2+** for all credential transmissions.
- ☐ Implement **rate limiting + account lockout + CAPTCHA** to defeat brute force.
- ☐ Regenerate the **session ID after successful login** to prevent session fixation.
- ☐ Remember the formula: $\text{Time} \propto 2^{n}$ for bcrypt cost scaling.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: User Authentication with Express Sessions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** User Authentication with Express Sessions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**User Authentication** is the process of verifying the identity of a user attempting to access a web application, typically by validating credentials (username/email + password) against a stored record in a database. In the **Express.js** (Node.js) ecosystem, this process is most commonly implemented using **Session-Based Authentication**, where a unique session identifier is created upon successful login and stored on both the server and the client (browser).

**Express Session** refers to the server-side mechanism that persists user state (such as login status, user ID, role, and cart data) across multiple HTTP requests. Because HTTP is a **stateless** protocol, sessions provide the "memory" required to recognize a returning authenticated user. The official middleware used in Express is `express-session`, which creates a session object on `req.session` and manages session cookies automatically.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PBITT504 - Module 4)**, students must understand the end-to-end implementation of authentication: registration, password hashing, session creation, protected routes, session destruction (logout), and security best practices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Module 4 mandates working knowledge of Node.js + Express backend, including middleware chaining, routing, session management, and integration with databases (typically MongoDB via Mongoose) for authentication workflows.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Club Wristband" Model

Imagine entering a private concert venue:

1. **Registration (Buying a Ticket):** You provide your name, age, and ID once at the counter. Your data is stored in the club's database. (Equivalent to signing up — `POST /register`).
2. **First Login (Showing ID at the Door):** The bouncer checks your ID against the database. If valid, he hands you a **wristband** with a unique number and keeps a matching record in his logbook. (Equivalent to creating a session and sending a `Set-Cookie` header to the browser).
3. **Subsequent Requests (Walking Around the Club):** Every time you approach a VIP area, the bouncer just glances at your wristband number and cross-references his logbook — he does **not** ask for your ID again. (Equivalent to the server validating the session cookie on every request).
4. **Logout (Removing the Wristband):** When you leave, your wristband is cut and the logbook entry is struck through. (Equivalent to calling `req.session.destroy()`).

This wristband is your **session cookie**, and the logbook is the **session store** (MemoryStore in development, MongoDB/Redis in production).

> [!NOTE]
> **Statelessness vs. Statefulness:** HTTP is stateless — each request is independent. Sessions introduce a stateful layer on top of HTTP by tying requests together via a shared identifier (the session ID).

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Session ID** | A cryptographically random, unique string (e.g., `s%3A9k2...`) used to identify a session. |
| **Cookie** | A small key-value pair stored in the browser, sent with every HTTP request to the same domain. |
| **`req.session`** | The Express session object attached to every incoming request after `express-session` middleware is loaded. |
| **`res.locals`** | Response-scoped variables made available to all rendered views for the duration of one request. |
| **Middleware** | A function with signature `(req, res, next)` that executes in a chain during request processing. |
| **Hashing** | A one-way mathematical transformation (e.g., bcrypt) used to securely store passwords. |
| **Session Store** | The backend storage (Memory, MongoDB, Redis, File) that holds active session data. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Authentication Lifecycle — Operational Breakdown

The complete authentication flow in an Express application consists of **six logical stages**. Each stage must be understood individually to score well in KTU ESE questions.

### Stage 1 — User Registration
- Client submits a `POST /register` form with `email` and `password`.
- The plaintext password is **never stored**. It is passed through a hashing algorithm.
- In the KTU syllabus, **bcrypt** is the recommended hashing library.
- The hashed password and email are inserted into the User collection (MongoDB).

### Stage 2 — Password Hashing
- Bcrypt generates a salt automatically and produces a hash of the form `$2b$10$...` (60 characters).
- The salt is embedded inside the hash, so verification only needs the stored hash — not a separate salt column.
- The **cost factor** (rounds) defaults to **10**, meaning 2^10 = **1024** iterations.

### Stage 3 — Login Verification
- User submits `POST /login` with credentials.
- Server retrieves the user record by email.
- `bcrypt.compare(plaintext, storedHash)` returns `true` or `false`.
- On success, server calls `req.session.userId = user._id` to mark the user as logged in.

### Stage 4 — Session Creation
- The `express-session` middleware automatically generates a unique session ID (via the `uid-safe` library).
- This ID is sent to the browser as an HTTP-only, signed cookie named `connect.sid` by default.
- The session data is persisted in the configured **session store**.

### Stage 5 — Authorization Middleware (Protected Routes)
- A custom middleware checks `if (!req.session.userId) return res.redirect('/login')`.
- This is mounted before any route that requires authentication (e.g., dashboard, profile, cart).

### Stage 6 — Logout
- `POST /logout` (or `GET /logout`) calls `req.session.destroy()` to remove the session from the store.
- The browser cookie is cleared via `res.clearCookie('connect.sid')`.
- The user is redirected to the home or login page.

## 2.2 Anatomy of `express-session` Configuration

The `express-session` middleware accepts an **options object** with the following key properties:

| Option | Type | Purpose / KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| `secret` | String or Array | Used to sign the session ID cookie to prevent tampering. **Mandatory.** |
| `resave` | Boolean | Forces re-saving the session on every request even if unmodified. Default: `false`. |
| `saveUninitialized` | Boolean | Saves a new, empty session on every request. Default: `false` (recommended). |
| `cookie` | Object | Cookie configuration — `maxAge`, `httpOnly`, `secure`, `sameSite`. |
| `store` | SessionStore | Custom store (MongoDB, Redis). Defaults to `MemoryStore` (not for production). |
| `name` | String | The cookie name. Defaults to `'connect.sid'`. |
| `rolling` | Boolean | Resets the cookie expiry on every response. |
| `unset` | String | Controls deletion behavior: `'keep'` or `'destroy'`. |

## 2.3 Cookie Security Attributes (Exam Favorite)

| Attribute | Effect |
|---|---|
| `httpOnly: true` | JavaScript cannot access the cookie via `document.cookie` → mitigates XSS. |
| `secure: true` | Cookie sent only over HTTPS. |
| `sameSite: 'strict'` or `'lax'` | Mitigates CSRF attacks. |
| `maxAge: 3600000` | Cookie expiry in **milliseconds** (1 hour = 3,600,000 ms). |

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula:** Cookie lifetime conversion
>
> $$\text{maxAge (ms)} = \text{Hours} \times 3{,}600{,}000$$
>
> $$\text{maxAge (ms)} = \text{Minutes} \times 60{,}000$$

## 2.4 Why Bcrypt and Not MD5/SHA?

| Property | MD5/SHA-256 | Bcrypt |
|---|---|---|
| Speed | Extremely fast (vulnerable to brute force) | Intentionally slow (cost factor adjustable) |
| Salt | Must be added manually | Auto-generated and embedded |
| Rainbow tables | Vulnerable | Resistant |
| KTU Recommendation | **Not used** | **Industry standard** |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

Session-based authentication is the backbone of virtually every traditional web application: **Gmail, Amazon, banking portals, ERP systems, and university portals**. While modern APIs often use **JWT (JSON Web Tokens)**, server-side sessions remain dominant for SSR (Server-Side Rendered) apps using **EJS, Pug, or Handlebars** with Express — exactly the stack covered in KTU Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Implementation Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Project Structure (KTU-Standard)

```
project-root/
│
├── app.js                  # Main Express server
├── models/
│   └── User.js             # Mongoose User schema
├── middleware/
│   └── isAuth.js           # Authorization guard
├── routes/
│   └── auth.js             # /register, /login, /logout
├── views/
│   ├── login.ejs
│   ├── register.ejs
│   └── dashboard.ejs
└── package.json
```

## 3.2 Step 1 — Install Required Packages

```
npm install express express-session connect-mongo mongoose bcrypt ejs
```

**Package Roles:**

- `express` — Web framework.
- `express-session` — Session middleware.
- `connect-mongo` — MongoDB session store (avoids memory leaks).
- `mongoose` — MongoDB ODM.
- `bcrypt` — Password hashing.
- `ejs` — Templating engine for SSR.

## 3.3 Step 2 — User Model (Mongoose Schema)

```javascript
// models/User.js
const mongoose = require('mongoose');
const bcrypt = require('bcrypt');

const userSchema = new mongoose.Schema({
  email:    { type: String, required: true, unique: true },
  password: { type: String, required: true }
});

// Pre-save hook: hash password before persisting
userSchema.pre('save', async function (next) {
  if (!this.isModified('password')) return next();
  const salt = await bcrypt.genSalt(10);
  this.password = await bcrypt.hash(this.password, salt);
  next();
});

// Instance method to verify password
userSchema.methods.comparePassword = function (candidate) {
  return bcrypt.compare(candidate, this.password);
};

module.exports = mongoose.model('User', userSchema);
```

**Derivation — Why `pre('save')` and not a regular function?**

In Mongoose, the `pre('save')` middleware runs **before** the document is written to MongoDB. By computing the hash here, we guarantee that no plaintext password ever reaches the database — even if the developer forgets to hash manually inside the route handler. The `isModified('password')` check prevents re-hashing an already-hashed password on every save (e.g., when updating the email).

**Derivation — Bcrypt Cost Factor Math:**

The cost factor $c$ determines the number of iterations as $2^c$.

$$
\text{Iterations} = 2^{c}
$$

For $c = 10$:
$$
\text{Iterations} = 2^{10} = 1024
$$

For $c = 12$ (high-security environments):
$$
\text{Iterations} = 2^{12} = 4096
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each increment of the cost factor **doubles** the hashing time. Choose based on server capacity — typically 10–12 in production.

## 3.4 Step 3 — Main Server with Session Middleware

```javascript
// app.js
const express = require('express');
const session = require('express-session');
const MongoStore = require('connect-mongo');
const mongoose = require('mongoose');
const app = express();

app.set('view engine', 'ejs');
app.use(express.urlencoded({ extended: true }));

// --- Database connection ---
mongoose.connect('mongodb://localhost:27017/ktuAuthDemo')
  .then(() => console.log('MongoDB connected'))
  .catch(err => console.log(err));

// --- Session configuration ---
app.use(session({
  name: 'ktu.sid',
  secret: 'ktu_secret_key_change_in_production',
  resave: false,
  saveUninitialized: false,
  store: MongoStore.create({
    mongoUrl: 'mongodb://localhost:27017/ktuAuthDemo',
    collectionName: 'sessions',
    ttl: 14 * 24 * 60 * 60   // 14 days in seconds
  }),
  cookie: {
    httpOnly: true,
    maxAge: 1000 * 60 * 60    // 1 hour
  }
}));

// Expose session data to all views
app.use((req, res, next) => {
  res.locals.currentUser = req.session.userId || null;
  next();
});

// --- Routes ---
app.use('/', require('./routes/auth'));

app.listen(3000, () => console.log('Server on http://localhost:3000'));
```

**Derivation — TTL to milliseconds conversion:**

The cookie `maxAge` is in **milliseconds**, but MongoStore `ttl` is in **seconds**. Given 14 days:

$$
\text{TTL (seconds)} = 14 \times 24 \times 60 \times 60 = 120960
$$

$$
\text{maxAge (ms)} = 14 \times 24 \times 60 \times 60 \times 1000 = 120960000
$$

**Derivation — Why `res.locals`?**

`res.locals` is a per-request object that EJS templates can access directly. By setting `res.locals.currentUser` in a global middleware, every view (header, navbar, dashboard) can render conditional UI (e.g., "Login" vs. "Logout") without re-checking `req.session` in every route.

## 3.5 Step 4 — Authentication Routes

```javascript
// routes/auth.js
const express = require('express');
const router  = express.Router();
const User    = require('../models/User');

// GET /register — render form
router.get('/register', (req, res) => res.render('register'));

// POST /register — create user
router.post('/register', async (req, res) => {
  try {
    const { email, password } = req.body;
    const user = new User({ email, password });
    await user.save();
    req.session.userId = user._id;
    res.redirect('/dashboard');
  } catch (err) {
    res.send('Error: ' + err.message);
  }
});

// GET /login — render form
router.get('/login', (req, res) => res.render('login'));

// POST /login — verify credentials
router.post('/login', async (req, res) => {
  const { email, password } = req.body;
  const user = await User.findOne({ email });
  if (!user) return res.send('Invalid credentials');
  const ok = await user.comparePassword(password);
  if (!ok) return res.send('Invalid credentials');
  req.session.userId = user._id;
  res.redirect('/dashboard');
});

// POST /logout — destroy session
router.post('/logout', (req, res) => {
  req.session.destroy(err => {
    if (err) return res.send('Error logging out');
    res.clearCookie('ktu.sid');
    res.redirect('/login');
  });
});

module.exports = router;
```

**Derivation — Why `req.session.destroy()` is asynchronous?**

Internally, the session middleware must remove the session record from the store (e.g., delete the document from the MongoDB `sessions` collection). Since MongoDB operations are I/O bound, `destroy()` accepts a callback. Only inside the callback is it safe to clear the browser cookie and redirect.

## 3.6 Step 5 — Authorization Middleware

```javascript
// middleware/isAuth.js
module.exports = (req, res, next) => {
  if (!req.session.userId) {
    return res.redirect('/login');
  }
  next();
};
```

**Usage in routes:**

```javascript
const isAuth = require('../middleware/isAuth');
router.get('/dashboard', isAuth, (req, res) => {
  res.render('dashboard', { userId: req.session.userId });
});
```

**Derivation — Middleware Chain Evaluation:**

When a request hits `/dashboard`, Express executes middleware in registration order:

$$
\text{Request} \rightarrow \text{isAuth} \rightarrow \text{Route Handler}
$$

If `isAuth` calls `next()`, control passes to the route handler. If it calls `res.redirect('/login')`, the chain terminates.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Common Pitfalls

| Pitfall | Symptom | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Forgetting `app.use(express.urlencoded(...))` | `req.body` is undefined | Add the body-parser middleware. |
| Setting `secret: undefined` | Session silently fails to sign | Provide a strong random string. |
| Using `MemoryStore` in production | Memory leak on server restart | Use `connect-mongo` or `connect-redis`. |
| Not setting `httpOnly: true` | XSS can steal session | Always set `httpOnly: true`. |
| Calling `res.redirect` after `res.send` | `ERR_HTTP_HEADERS_SENT` | Use `return res.redirect(...)` to exit. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Authentication Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User visits /register] --> B[Submits email and password]
    B --> C[Bcrypt hashes password]
    C --> D[User document saved to MongoDB]
    D --> E[req.session.userId assigned]
    E --> F[Session ID sent as cookie]
    F --> G[Redirect to /dashboard]

    H[User visits /login] --> I[Submits credentials]
    I --> J[Find user by email]
    J --> K{User exists?}
    K -- No --> L[Show Invalid credentials]
    K -- Yes --> M[bcrypt.compare password]
    M --> N{Match?}
    N -- No --> L
    N -- Yes --> O[Create session]
    O --> G

    G --> P{Protected route?}
    P -- Yes --> Q[isAuth middleware checks req.session.userId]
    Q --> R{Logged in?}
    R -- Yes --> S[Render dashboard]
    R -- No --> T[Redirect to /login]

    U[User clicks Logout] --> V[req.session.destroy]
    V --> W[res.clearCookie]
    W --> X[Redirect to /login]
```

## 4.2 Session Lifecycle Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ClientSide[Client Browser]
        C1[Empty cookie jar]
        C2[connect.sid cookie stored]
        C3[Cookie cleared]
    end

    subgraph ServerSide[Express Server]
        S1[No active session]
        S2[Session object in MongoStore]
        S3[Session destroyed]
    end

    C1 -->|Login success| S1
    S1 -->|Set-Cookie header| C2
    C2 -->|Every request| S2
    S2 -->|Validate session ID| S2
    S2 -->|Logout request| S3
    S3 -->|Clear-Cookie header| C3
```

## 4.3 Middleware Execution Order

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[1. express.urlencoded] --> M2[2. express-session]
    M2 --> M3[3. res.locals middleware]
    M3 --> M4[4. Route-specific middleware e.g. isAuth]
    M4 --> M5[5. Route handler]
    M5 --> M6[6. Response sent to client]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked "What is the order of middleware execution?", always list them in the order they are registered with `app.use()`. Reordering changes the behavior — for example, putting `express-session` *after* the auth middleware would break protected routes.

## 4.4 Component Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FrontendLayer[Frontend EJS Views]
        F1[register.ejs]
        F2[login.ejs]
        F3[dashboard.ejs]
    end

    subgraph RoutingLayer[Express Routes]
        R1[POST register]
        R2[POST login]
        R3[POST logout]
        R4[GET dashboard]
    end

    subgraph MiddlewareLayer[Middleware Chain]
        MW1[bodyParser]
        MW2[session]
        MW3[isAuth guard]
    end

    subgraph DataLayer[Data Layer]
        D1[User model bcrypt hashing]
        D2[MongoDB sessions collection]
        D3[MongoDB users collection]
    end

    F1 --> R1
    F2 --> R2
    F3 --> R4
    R1 --> MW1
    R2 --> MW1
    R4 --> MW3
    MW1 --> MW2
    MW2 --> D1
    R1 --> D3
    R2 --> D3
    MW2 --> D2
    R3 --> MW2
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define session-based authentication. List any three configuration options of `express-session` with their purpose.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Session-based authentication is a mechanism in which, upon successful login, the server creates a unique session object identified by a session ID, which is stored on the client as a cookie and on the server in a session store. The server uses this ID to identify the user on subsequent requests.

Three key configuration options of `express-session`:

1. **`secret`** — A string used to sign the session ID cookie, preventing tampering. (1 mark)
2. **`resave`** — Boolean that forces the session to be saved back to the store on every request, even when unmodified. (1 mark)
3. **`store`** — Specifies the session store (e.g., `MongoStore`) where session data is persisted on the server. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why is bcrypt preferred over plain SHA-256 for password hashing? Mention any two security attributes of session cookies.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Bcrypt is preferred because it is **intentionally slow** (computationally expensive due to its cost factor), making brute-force attacks impractical, and it **automatically generates and embeds a salt** within the hash, defeating rainbow-table attacks. SHA-256 is a fast, general-purpose hash designed for speed — making it unsuitable for passwords. (2 marks)

Two security attributes of session cookies:
- `httpOnly: true` — Prevents client-side JavaScript from accessing the cookie, mitigating XSS attacks. (0.5 marks)
- `secure: true` — Ensures the cookie is transmitted only over HTTPS. (0.5 marks)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the complete working of session-based user authentication in an Express application. Illustrate the flow with a neat diagram. (7 marks)

**(b)** Write the Express code to implement **user registration** with `bcrypt` password hashing and **login** using `express-session`, including route definitions and the session middleware configuration. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Working of Session-Based Authentication:**

1. **Registration Phase:** The user submits a registration form with email and password via `POST /register`. The server hashes the password using `bcrypt.hash()` and stores the user record in MongoDB. (1.5 marks)
2. **Login Phase:** On `POST /login`, the server queries the database for the user by email. It then calls `bcrypt.compare(submittedPassword, storedHash)`. If the comparison returns `true`, the server creates a new session by assigning `req.session.userId = user._id`. (2 marks)
3. **Session Creation:** The `express-session` middleware generates a unique, signed session ID and sends it to the browser as an HTTP-only cookie. The session data is persisted in the configured store (e.g., `MongoStore`). (1.5 marks)
4. **Protected Access:** On subsequent requests, the browser automatically sends the session cookie. The server's `isAuth` middleware checks `req.session.userId`. If present, the user is allowed; otherwise, they are redirected to `/login`. (1 mark)
5. **Logout Phase:** Calling `req.session.destroy()` removes the session from the store, and `res.clearCookie('connect.sid')` removes the cookie from the browser. (1 mark)

**Diagram (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the full mermaid flow).** A simplified textual representation:

```
Register → Hash → Save User → Login → Verify → Create Session → Cookie Set
                                                            ↓
Logout ← Clear Cookie ← Destroy Session ← User clicks Logout
```

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

```javascript
// app.js — Session middleware
const session = require('express-session');
const MongoStore = require('connect-mongo');

app.use(session({
  name: 'ktu.sid',
  secret: 'myStrongSecret',
  resave: false,
  saveUninitialized: false,
  store: MongoStore.create({ mongoUrl: 'mongodb://localhost:27017/ktuApp' }),
  cookie: { httpOnly: true, maxAge: 3600000 }
}));

// routes/auth.js
const User = require('../models/User');

router.post('/register', async (req, res) => {
  const { email, password } = req.body;
  const user = new User({ email, password });   // pre-save hook hashes it
  await user.save();
  req.session.userId = user._id;
  res.redirect('/dashboard');
});

router.post('/login', async (req, res) => {
  const { email, password } = req.body;
  const user = await User.findOne({ email });
  if (!user || !(await user.comparePassword(password))) {
    return res.status(401).send('Invalid credentials');
  }
  req.session.userId = user._id;
  res.redirect('/dashboard');
});
```

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- Correct session middleware configuration with all key options: 2 marks
- Registration route with proper async/await and hashing trigger: 2 marks
- Login route with `findOne` + `comparePassword` + session assignment: 2 marks
- Error handling and redirect logic: 1 mark

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the role of `connect-mongo` as a session store. Why is the default `MemoryStore` not recommended for production? List any two advantages of using MongoDB for session storage. (7 marks)

**(b)** Design and implement the **logout functionality** in Express with proper session destruction and cookie clearing. Also, write the `isAuth` middleware to protect the `/dashboard` route. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Role of `connect-mongo`:** `connect-mongo` is a session store for `express-session` that persists session data in a MongoDB collection (default: `sessions`). Each session is stored as a document with fields like `_id`, `session` (containing the user data), and `expires` (TTL index). This ensures sessions survive server restarts and scale across multiple Node.js processes. (2 marks)

**Why `MemoryStore` is not for production:** The default `MemoryStore` stores sessions in the Node.js process memory. This causes:
- **Memory leaks** as sessions accumulate indefinitely. (1 mark)
- **Loss of all sessions on server restart or crash.** (1 mark)
- **Inability to scale** — sessions are not shared across multiple server instances (no horizontal scaling). (0.5 marks)

**Two advantages of MongoDB for session storage:**
1. **Persistence:** Sessions survive server crashes and restarts. (1 mark)
2. **Scalability:** Multiple Node.js processes can share the same session data via a common MongoDB cluster. (1 mark)
3. *(Bonus)* Automatic cleanup via TTL indexes based on the `expires` field. (0.5 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Logout Implementation:**

```javascript
router.post('/logout', (req, res) => {
  req.session.destroy(err => {
    if (err) {
      console.log(err);
      return res.status(500).send('Logout failed');
    }
    res.clearCookie('ktu.sid');   // match the name used in session config
    res.redirect('/login');
  });
});
```

**`isAuth` Middleware:**

```javascript
// middleware/isAuth.js
function isAuth(req, res, next) {
  if (req.session && req.session.userId) {
    return next();
  }
  res.redirect('/login');
}
module.exports = isAuth;
```

**Mounting on a protected route:**

```javascript
const isAuth = require('../middleware/isAuth');
router.get('/dashboard', isAuth, (req, res) => {
  res.render('dashboard', { userId: req.session.userId });
});
```

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- Correct usage of `req.session.destroy()` with callback: 2 marks
- Cookie name matching the session config (`ktu.sid`): 1 mark
- Proper error handling inside the callback: 1 mark
- `isAuth` middleware logic with `req.session.userId` check: 2 marks
- Correct mounting on the protected route: 1 mark

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting `httpOnly: true`** — Students often omit cookie security attributes. This loses 1–2 marks in security-related questions.
> 2. **Confusing `maxAge` units** — Cookie `maxAge` is in **milliseconds**, MongoStore `ttl` is in **seconds**. Mixing them up is a frequent blunder.
> 3. **Storing plaintext passwords** — Writing `user.password = req.body.password` without bcrypt hashing is a critical mistake. Always use the `pre('save')` hook or call `bcrypt.hash()` explicitly.
> 4. **Not awaiting async operations** — Missing `await` before `user.save()` or `user.comparePassword()` leads to race conditions and broken authentication.
> 5. **Forgetting `res.clearCookie()`** — After `req.session.destroy()`, the cookie still resides in the browser until manually cleared.
> 6. **Using `app.get('/logout')` with side effects** — Logout should be a `POST` (or `DELETE`) request to prevent CSRF logout attacks. Examiners may deduct marks if GET is used without justification.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Statelessness** of HTTP is overcome by **sessions**, which persist user state across requests.
- The **`express-session`** middleware attaches a `req.session` object to every request.
- **Session ID** is sent to the browser as a **signed, HTTP-only cookie** named `connect.sid` by default.
- The **`secret`** option is mandatory and must be a strong, unpredictable string stored in environment variables in production.
- **`MemoryStore`** is the default store — suitable only for development; always use **`MongoStore`** or **`Redis`** in production.
- **Bcrypt** is the KTU-recommended hashing algorithm. Its cost factor $c$ yields $2^c$ iterations; default is 10.
- Passwords must be hashed in a **Mongoose `pre('save')` hook** or via explicit `bcrypt.hash()` calls — never stored in plaintext.
- **Login flow:** find user by email → `bcrypt.compare()` → on success, assign `req.session.userId` → redirect.
- **Authorization:** A custom `isAuth` middleware checks `req.session.userId` before allowing access to protected routes.
- **Logout flow:** `req.session.destroy()` (with callback) → `res.clearCookie('connect.sid')` → redirect.
- **Cookie security triad:** `httpOnly`, `secure`, `sameSite` — must be configured to defend against XSS and CSRF.
- **`res.locals`** is used to expose `req.session.userId` (as `currentUser`) to all EJS templates globally.
- **Time conversion formulae** (exam favorites):
   - `maxAge (ms) = hours × 3,600,000`
   - `TTL (seconds) = days × 24 × 60 × 60`
- **Middleware order matters:** `body-parser` → `session` → custom auth middleware → route handler.
- Always use `POST` (not `GET`) for logout to prevent CSRF-based forced logout attacks.
- The KTU Module 4 outcome emphasizes **hands-on implementation** — practice running the full code locally and tracing the request/response cycle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Authentication Middleware
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Authentication Middleware

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Authentication Middleware** is a specialized request-handling function in a web application framework (typically **Express.js** in the Node.js ecosystem) that intercepts every incoming HTTP request to verify the identity of the requesting client **before** allowing the request to proceed to protected route handlers. It acts as a programmable, modular **gatekeeper layer** that sits between the raw HTTP request and the business-logic controllers.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, this topic falls under the broader umbrella of **Server-Side Web Frameworks**, where middleware functions form the backbone of the request-response cycle. Authentication middleware specifically enforces the security policy of the application by validating credentials (such as **JWT tokens**, **session cookies**, **API keys**, or **OAuth bearer tokens**) attached to each request.

> [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** Authentication is distinct from **Authorization**. Authentication answers *"Who are you?"* while Authorization answers *"What are you allowed to do?"*. Middleware can be designed to handle either, but the term *Authentication Middleware* strictly refers to identity verification.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Building Security Checkpoint

Imagine a **high-security corporate building**:

- Every employee or visitor must show an **ID badge** at the entrance gate.
- The **security guard** at the gate acts as the **middleware**.
- The guard does not care which floor the visitor is going to (that is the job of **authorization**); the guard only verifies the **identity** of the person.
- If the badge is valid → the visitor is allowed inside to proceed to their destination (the **route handler**).
- If the badge is missing or expired → the visitor is **denied entry** immediately, and never reaches the inner offices.

In this analogy:
| Building Component | Web Application Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Building entrance | Application server endpoint |
| Security guard | Authentication middleware function |
| ID badge | JWT token / session cookie |
| Inner offices | Protected route handlers / controllers |
| Visitor logbook | Database of verified users |

The key takeaway is that the middleware runs **before** any business logic, ensuring that every protected route is reached only by **authenticated** clients.

### 1.3 Position in the Request-Response Cycle

Authentication middleware operates in the **middleware chain** of a web framework. Each middleware function has access to three parameters — `request (req)`, `response (res)`, and `next` — and can either:

1. **Terminate the request** by sending a response (e.g., `401 Unauthorized`).
2. **Pass control** to the next middleware by calling `next()`.
3. **Pass an error** to the error-handling middleware by calling `next(err)`.

> [!TIP] > In Express.js, the **order of middleware registration** is critical. If authentication middleware is registered **after** a route handler, it will **never execute** for that route, leaving the endpoint unprotected — a common security pitfall in board examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomical Breakdown of a Middleware Function

A typical authentication middleware function follows a strict structural pattern. Let us dissect each component:

- **Signature Definition** — The function accepts `req`, `res`, and `next` as parameters. These are injected by the framework's internal router.
- **Credential Extraction** — The middleware extracts the authentication token from the request headers (commonly the `Authorization` header), the request cookies, or the request body.
- **Verification Step** — The extracted token is validated against a **secret key**, a **public key**, or a **database session store**.
- **Identity Attachment** — Upon successful verification, the decoded user information is attached to the `req` object (e.g., `req.user = decodedPayload`).
- **Decision Logic** — Based on the verification result, the middleware either calls `next()` to proceed or sends an error response.

### 2.2 Classification of Authentication Strategies

Authentication middleware can be implemented using several strategies, each with distinct trade-offs:

| Strategy | Token Format | Storage Location | Stateless? | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **JWT (JSON Web Token)** | Base64-encoded JSON | Client-side (header / cookie) | **Yes** | REST APIs, microservices |
| **Session-based** | Opaque session ID | Server-side (memory / DB) | **No** | Traditional web apps |
| **OAuth 2.0 Bearer** | Access token from provider | Client-side | **Yes** | Third-party integrations |
| **API Key** | Static string | Client-side header | **Yes** | Machine-to-machine |
| **Basic Auth** | Base64(username:password) | Client-side header | **No** | Internal tools |

### 2.3 JWT Structure — The Three-Part Token

A **JSON Web Token** consists of three Base64-URL-encoded segments separated by dots:

$$\text{JWT} = \underbrace{\text{Header}}_{\text{Algorithm \& Type}} \cdot \underbrace{\text{Payload}}_{\text{Claims}} \cdot \underbrace{\text{Signature}}_{\text{HMAC / RSA}}$$

Each segment serves a specific purpose:

- **Header** — Contains the signing algorithm (e.g., `HS256`, `RS256`) and token type (`JWT`).
- **Payload** — Contains **claims**, which are statements about the user (e.g., `sub`, `iat`, `exp`, custom fields).
- **Signature** — A cryptographic hash computed over the header, payload, and a secret key, ensuring **integrity**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

> [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates every formula, threshold value, and operational constant you must memorize for KTU board examinations on this topic.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **JWT Signature (HMAC)** | $\text{Sig} = \text{HMAC-SHA256}(\text{Base64URL}(H) \oplus \text{Base64URL}(P),\, S)$ | $S$ = secret key (≥ 256 bits) |
| **Token Expiry Check** | $\text{Valid} \iff \text{now} < \text{exp}$ | `exp` in Unix epoch seconds |
| **Bearer Token Format** | `Authorization: Bearer <token>` | RFC 6750 standard |
| **HTTP 401 Response** | Sent when authentication **fails or is absent** | Status code `401` |
| **HTTP 403 Response** | Sent when authentication **succeeds but authorization fails** | Status code `403` |
| **Bcrypt Cost Factor** | $\text{Rounds} = 2^{\text{cost}}$ | Recommended $\geq 10$ |
| **Token Lifetime (Access)** | Typical duration | `15 min` to `1 hour` |
| **Token Lifetime (Refresh)** | Typical duration | `7 days` to `30 days` |
| **Cookie Max-Age** | $\text{Max-Age}_{\text{sec}}$ | Session or persistent |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Authentication middleware is not merely an academic construct — it is the **cornerstone of production-grade web security**:

- **E-commerce platforms** use it to verify that only logged-in users can access `/checkout` routes.
- **Banking APIs** enforce OAuth 2.0 + JWT to ensure every transaction request is cryptographically tied to a verified user identity.
- **Single Page Applications (SPAs)** rely on token-based auth middleware to protect AJAX endpoints from cross-site forgery.
- **Microservices architectures** use stateless JWT middleware to propagate identity across services without a shared session store, enabling horizontal scalability.

> [!TIP] > In KTU lab examinations and project viva, you will often be asked: *"Why prefer JWT over server-side sessions for REST APIs?"* The expected answer must emphasize **statelessness**, **scalability**, and **decoupling** of auth from application servers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough — JWT Verification Middleware

Let us derive, step by step, exactly what happens when an authentication middleware processes an incoming HTTP request carrying a JWT token.

**Step 1 — Receive the incoming request.**

The Express.js router invokes the middleware function with three injected arguments: the request object `req`, the response object `res`, and the `next` callback.

**Step 2 — Extract the Authorization header.**

The middleware reads the `Authorization` header from the request. The standard format is:

$$\text{Header Value} = \text{Bearer } \oplus \text{Space} \oplus \text{Token}$$

For example: `Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9...`

**Step 3 — Split the header to isolate the token.**

The middleware splits the header string by the space delimiter:

$$\text{parts} = \text{headerValue}.\text{split}(' ')$$

It then verifies that the first part equals `"Bearer"` (case-insensitive). If not, the request is rejected with `401 Unauthorized`.

**Step 4 — Pass the token to the verification engine.**

The raw token string is passed to a verification function (e.g., `jwt.verify()` from the `jsonwebtoken` library), along with the **secret key** stored securely in environment variables.

**Step 5 — Cryptographic signature validation.**

The verification engine internally performs the following logical check:

$$\text{computedSig} = \text{HMAC-SHA256}(\text{Base64URL}(H) \oplus \text{Base64URL}(P),\, S)$$

It then compares this computed signature with the signature segment of the token:

$$\text{Valid} \iff \text{computedSig} \stackrel{?}{=} \text{tokenSignature}$$

**Step 6 — Temporal claim validation.**

If the signature matches, the engine decodes the payload and checks the standard claims:

- `exp` (expiration time): $\text{now} < \text{exp}$
- `nbf` (not before): $\text{now} \geq \text{nbf}$
- `iat` (issued at): informational

**Step 7 — Attach identity to the request object.**

Upon successful validation, the decoded payload (containing user identity) is attached to the request:

$$\text{req.user} = \text{decodedPayload}$$

This allows downstream route handlers to access the authenticated user's identity without re-parsing the token.

**Step 8 — Invoke `next()` to continue the chain.**

The middleware calls `next()` to pass control to the next middleware or route handler in the pipeline.

### 3.2 Error Path Analysis — What Happens on Failure?

If **any** of Steps 3, 5, or 6 fails, the middleware must:

1. **NOT** call `next()` (to prevent the request from reaching protected routes).
2. Send an HTTP response with status code `401 Unauthorized`.
3. Optionally include a `WWW-Authenticate` header indicating the authentication scheme.

$$\text{Response} = \begin{cases} 401 \, \text{Unauthorized} & \text{if no token or invalid token} \\ 403 \, \text{Forbidden} & \text{if token valid but user lacks permission} \end{cases}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Middleware Ordering Impact

| Registration Order | Result | Security Implication |
|---|---|---|
| `app.use(authMiddleware)` **before** `/api/protected` | Middleware runs for protected route | **Secure** |
| `app.use('/api/protected', authMiddleware)` **after** route definition | Middleware never executes | **Critical vulnerability** |
| `app.use(authMiddleware)` applied globally | All routes require auth | Must add `/login`, `/register` as exceptions |
| Error-handling middleware **after** auth middleware | Errors caught correctly | **Recommended pattern** |

> [!WARNING] > A frequent KTU exam pitfall: students often write authentication logic **inside** the route handler instead of as a separate middleware function. This violates the **Single Responsibility Principle** and makes the code untestable. Always structure auth as a **reusable middleware function** passed as an argument to route definitions.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Middleware Execution Flow in the Request-Response Cycle

The following diagram illustrates how an authentication middleware fits into the broader Express.js request pipeline:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client Sends HTTP Request] --> B[Express Router Receives Request]
    B --> C{Logger Middleware}
    C -->|next| D{Body Parser Middleware}
    D -->|next| E{CORS Middleware}
    E -->|next| F{Authentication Middleware}
    F -->|Token Valid| G[req.user Attached]
    F -->|Token Invalid| H[Response: 401 Unauthorized]
    G -->|next| I{Authorization Check RBAC}
    I -->|Role Matched| J[Route Handler Executes]
    I -->|Role Mismatch| K[Response: 403 Forbidden]
    J --> L[Business Logic Processes Request]
    L --> M[Response Sent to Client]
    H --> M
    K --> M
```

### 4.2 JWT Token Lifecycle — Generation to Verification

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client as Client Browser/App
    participant LoginAPI as Login Route Handler
    participant AuthMW as Auth Middleware
    participant ProtectedAPI as Protected Route

    Client->>LoginAPI: POST /login {email, password}
    LoginAPI->>LoginAPI: Verify credentials against DB
    LoginAPI->>LoginAPI: Generate JWT (header.payload.signature)
    LoginAPI-->>Client: 200 OK + JWT Token

    Note over Client: Stores token (localStorage / cookie)

    Client->>AuthMW: GET /dashboard + Authorization Bearer Token
    AuthMW->>AuthMW: Extract token from header
    AuthMW->>AuthMW: jwt.verify(token, secret)
    AuthMW->>AuthMW: Check signature & exp claim
    AuthMW->>AuthMW: Attach req.user = decoded
    AuthMW->>ProtectedAPI: next() - Pass control
    ProtectedAPI-->>Client: 200 OK + Protected Data
```

### 4.3 Middleware Classification Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AppLevel["Application-Level Middleware"]
        A1[app.use middleware]
    end
    subgraph RouterLevel["Router-Level Middleware"]
        B1[router.use middleware]
    end
    subgraph ErrorLevel["Error-Handling Middleware"]
        C1[err, req, res, next signature]
    end
    subgraph BuiltIn["Built-in Middleware"]
        D1[express.json]
        D2[express.static]
    end
    subgraph ThirdParty["Third-Party Middleware"]
        E1[passport.authenticate]
        E2[express-jwt verify]
        E3[cors]
    end
    AppLevel --> AuthMW["Authentication Middleware (Focus Topic)"]
    RouterLevel --> AuthMW
    ErrorLevel --> AuthMW
    BuiltIn --> AuthMW
    ThirdParty --> AuthMW
```

### 4.4 Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) Extension Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Request Arrives] --> Verify{Authentication Middleware: Is token valid?}
    Verify -->|No| Reject401[Send 401 Unauthorized]
    Verify -->|Yes| Attach[Attach req.user with role claim]
    Attach --> CheckRole{Authorization Middleware: Does role match required?}
    CheckRole -->|admin route requires admin| Allow[next - Grant Access]
    CheckRole -->|user accessing admin route| Deny[Send 403 Forbidden]
    Allow --> Handler[Route Handler Executes]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Define **Authentication Middleware**. List any **two differences** between authentication and authorization.

**Model Answer:**

Authentication Middleware is a request-handling function in a web application framework that intercepts incoming HTTP requests to verify the identity of the client (typically by validating a JWT token, session cookie, or API key) before allowing the request to proceed to protected route handlers.

| Aspect | Authentication | Authorization |
|---|---|---|
| Question Answered | *"Who are you?"* | *"What can you do?"* |
| Mechanism | Verifies credentials (token, password) | Checks permissions/roles |
| Failure Response | HTTP `401 Unauthorized` | HTTP `403 Forbidden` |

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for differences)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

What is a **JSON Web Token (JWT)**? Write the **three structural parts** of a JWT.

**Model Answer:**

A JSON Web Token (JWT) is a compact, URL-safe, self-contained token format used for securely transmitting authenticated user identity information between parties as a Base64-URL-encoded JSON object.

The three structural parts of a JWT are:

1. **Header** — Contains the signing algorithm (e.g., `HS256`) and token type (`JWT`).
2. **Payload** — Contains the **claims** (user identity data such as `sub`, `iat`, `exp`, and custom fields).
3. **Signature** — A cryptographic HMAC or RSA hash computed over the encoded header, encoded payload, and a secret key, used to verify token integrity.

These three parts are separated by dots (`.`) in the final token string.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for three parts)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations, students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the **concept of middleware** in Express.js with a suitable diagram. Describe how the **middleware chain** processes an HTTP request, highlighting the role of the `next()` function. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Design and explain an **Authentication Middleware** using JWT for a Node.js/Express application. Your answer must include: token extraction, signature verification, expiration handling, and error response logic. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Concept of Middleware in Express.js:**

Middleware in Express.js refers to functions that have access to the request object (`req`), the response object (`res`), and the `next` function in the application's request-response cycle. Middleware functions can:

- Execute any code.
- Make modifications to the `req` and `res` objects.
- **Terminate** the request-response cycle by calling `res.send()` or `res.json()`.
- **Continue** the cycle by calling `next()` to pass control to the next middleware.

**Middleware Chain Processing:**

When an HTTP request arrives at the Express server, it enters the middleware chain. The router processes middleware functions **in the order they were registered** via `app.use()` or router-level `router.use()`. For each middleware:

1. The function is invoked with `req`, `res`, and `next`.
2. If the function calls `next()`, control passes to the **next** middleware in the stack.
3. If the function calls `next('route')`, it skips to the next route handler.
4. If the function calls `next(err)`, control jumps to the next **error-handling** middleware.
5. If the function sends a response (e.g., `res.json()`), the chain **terminates**.

The chain continues until a response is sent or the stack is exhausted. If the stack is exhausted without a response, the client receives a `404 Not Found`.

**Role of `next()`:**

The `next()` function is the **conduit** that links middleware functions together. Without it, the request would stall. Calling `next()` without arguments signals "proceed normally," while calling `next(err)` signals "propagate this error."

*(7 marks: 3 marks for concept + 2 marks for chain explanation + 2 marks for next() role)*

---

**(b) JWT Authentication Middleware Design:**

**Step 1 — Token Extraction:**

The middleware reads the `Authorization` header from the incoming request. It expects the format `Bearer <token>`. It splits the header by space and validates that the first part is `"Bearer"`. If absent or malformed, it responds with `401 Unauthorized` and terminates the chain.

**Step 2 — Signature Verification:**

The extracted token string is passed to the `jwt.verify()` function along with the secret key (loaded from an environment variable like `process.env.JWT_SECRET`). Internally, this function:

- Decodes the header and payload.
- Recomputes the HMAC-SHA256 signature using the secret.
- Compares the computed signature with the token's signature segment.
- If they match, the payload is returned as a decoded object.
- If they do not match, a `JsonWebTokenError` is thrown.

**Step 3 — Expiration Handling:**

The `jwt.verify()` function automatically checks the `exp` claim if present. If the current time exceeds `exp`, a `TokenExpiredError` is thrown. The middleware must catch this specifically and return a `401 Unauthorized` response with a message like `"Token has expired, please log in again."`

**Step 4 — Error Response Logic:**

The middleware wraps the verification logic in a `try-catch` block. On any thrown error (invalid signature, expired token, malformed token), it sends:

- Status: `401`
- Body: `{ "error": "Authentication failed", "detail": "<error.message>" }`

**Step 5 — Identity Attachment:**

On successful verification, the decoded payload is attached to `req.user`, making the authenticated user's identity available to all downstream route handlers.

**Step 6 — Chain Continuation:**

Finally, `next()` is called to pass control to the next middleware or route handler.

**Diagrammatic Summary of the Logic:**

`Request → Extract Header → Split Bearer → verify(token, secret) → Check Signature → Check exp → req.user = decoded → next()`

*(7 marks: 2 marks extraction + 2 marks verification + 1 mark expiration + 2 marks error handling and attachment)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Compare **session-based authentication** and **token-based (JWT) authentication**. Discuss their respective advantages, disadvantages, and ideal use cases. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)** as an authorization middleware. Write the pseudocode logic for a middleware that checks whether the authenticated user has the role `"admin"` before granting access to an `/api/admin` route. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Session-Based vs. Token-Based Authentication:**

| Parameter | Session-Based Auth | Token-Based (JWT) Auth |
|---|---|---|
| **Storage** | Session ID stored server-side (memory, Redis, DB) | Token stored client-side (localStorage, cookie) |
| **Statefulness** | Stateful — server maintains session table | Stateless — server holds no session data |
| **Scalability** | Difficult — requires shared session store across servers | Excellent — no server-side state needed |
| **CSRF Vulnerability** | Susceptible (if cookies used) | Less susceptible (if stored in headers) |
| **XSS Vulnerability** | Less susceptible | More susceptible (if stored in localStorage) |
| **Token Revocation** | Easy — delete session from store | Difficult — requires blocklist or short expiry |
| **Best For** | Traditional server-rendered web apps | REST APIs, SPAs, microservices |
| **Performance** | Requires DB lookup per request | Verifiable via cryptographic signature alone |

**Conclusion:** Session-based auth is preferred for traditional monolithic web applications where immediate revocation is important. JWT-based auth is preferred for distributed, stateless architectures like REST APIs and microservices.

*(7 marks: 5 marks for comparison table + 2 marks for conclusion)*

---

**(b) RBAC Authorization Middleware:**

**Concept:** Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) is an authorization pattern where permissions are assigned to **roles** (e.g., `admin`, `editor`, `viewer`), and users are assigned one or more roles. The authorization middleware checks the authenticated user's role against the role(s) required by the requested route.

**Pseudocode Logic:**

1. The middleware function accepts `req`, `res`, and `next` as parameters.
2. It accesses `req.user`, which was attached by the preceding authentication middleware.
3. If `req.user` is undefined, it responds with `401 Unauthorized` (user not authenticated).
4. It checks whether `req.user.role === "admin"`.
5. If the role matches, it calls `next()` to grant access.
6. If the role does not match, it responds with `403 Forbidden`.

**Logical Flow:**

`if req.user is undefined → 401`
`else if req.user.role === "admin" → next()`
`else → 403 Forbidden`

**Registration Example:**

The middleware is applied to the `/api/admin` route via:

`router.get('/api/admin', requireAdminRole, adminController.handleRequest);`

This ensures that the `requireAdminRole` middleware executes **before** the route handler, enforcing the access policy declaratively.

**Real-World Mapping:**

- `admin` → Full CRUD access to all resources.
- `editor` → Read + Update access to specific resources.
- `viewer` → Read-only access.

*(7 marks: 2 marks concept + 3 marks pseudocode + 2 marks registration and real-world mapping)*

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Authentication Middleware Questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating Authentication with Authorization** — Examiners specifically check whether you can distinguish between verifying identity (401) and verifying permissions (403). Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Skipping the `next()` explanation** — In 14-mark questions, failing to explain the role of `next()` in the middleware chain is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Forgetting token expiration** — When writing JWT verification logic, students often verify the signature but forget to mention the `exp` claim check. Always mention **both**.
> 4. **Not using a diagram** — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for well-labeled diagrams in 14-mark answers. Always include a flow diagram or sequence diagram.
> 5. **Writing code without explanation** — Pseudocode or code snippets must be accompanied by **textual reasoning**. Do not submit code-only answers.
> 6. **Middleware order confusion** — If the question asks about ordering, clearly state that `app.use(authMiddleware)` must come **before** the protected route registration.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Authentication Middleware** = a function that verifies client identity before allowing request to proceed.
- It sits **between** the raw request and the route handler in the middleware chain.
- The `next()` function is the **passing mechanism**; without it, the chain stalls.
- **JWT** = Header.Payload.Signature — three Base64-URL-encoded segments.
- **JWT verification** checks: (1) Signature integrity via HMAC/RSA, (2) `exp` claim, (3) `nbf` claim.
- **Authentication failure** → HTTP `401 Unauthorized`.
- **Authorization failure** → HTTP `403 Forbidden`.
- **Session-based** auth = stateful, server stores session ID.
- **Token-based** auth = stateless, client stores JWT.
- **RBAC** middleware checks `req.user.role` against required role.
- Middleware **order of registration** determines execution order.
- **Never store secrets** in code — always use `process.env` or a secrets manager.
- **Always wrap** token verification in `try-catch` to handle errors gracefully.
- Attach decoded user info to **`req.user`** for downstream access.
- For **REST APIs**, prefer JWT; for **traditional web apps**, prefer sessions.
- **Bcrypt** with cost factor ≥ 10 is the standard for password hashing.
- **Bearer token** format: `Authorization: Bearer <token>` (RFC 6750).
- **Refresh tokens** are used to obtain new access tokens without re-login.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: User Logout
Handling Non-Existent Routes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Handling Non-Existent Routes # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In Single Page Application (SPA) routing, **Handling Non-Existent Routes** refers to the structured mechanism by which a client-side router (such as React Router, Angular Router, or Vue Router) intercepts URL paths that do **not** match any declared route definition and renders an appropriate fallback UI — typically a **404 (Not Found) page**, a redirect, or a graceful error boundary. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A *non-existent route* (also called an *undefined route*, *unknown route*, or *wildcard catch-all*) is any URL pattern for which no matching `` (or equivalent declarative route element) has been registered in the application's route table. The fallback handler is conventionally registered using a wildcard path literal (`"*"` in React Router v6+, or `"**"` in Angular/older React Router). The technical objective is to ensure **graceful degradation** — preventing the application from displaying a blank white screen, throwing an uncaught JavaScript exception, or losing application state when a user mistypes a URL, follows a broken external link, or accesses a deprecated route after a refactor. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Reception Desk" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition for Students (Reception Desk Analogy):** > Imagine a large corporate office building with many numbered rooms (Routes `/home`, `/about`, `/contact`). A **visitor** (HTTP request) walks in and asks the **receptionist** (the Router) for *Room 207*. The receptionist checks the **room directory** (the route table). If Room 207 exists → the receptionist provides directions. If Room 207 does **not** exist → the receptionist does not leave the visitor stranded. Instead, the receptionist guides them to a **well-designed "Lost & Found" lounge** (the 404 fallback component) that says *"Sorry, this room does not exist. Here are some helpful directions to valid rooms."* In this analogy: - **Visitor** → URL typed into the browser address bar - **Receptionist** → `` / `` - **Room Directory** → Collection of `` definitions - **Room 207** → A specific URL like `/dashboard/settings/profile` - **Lost & Found Lounge** → The `NotFound` / `NoMatch` component rendered when no route matches - **Helpful directions** → Navigation links inside the 404 page that redirect users to valid routes ## 1.3 Why This Topic Carries Examination Weight > [!IMPORTANT] > According to **KTU 2024 Scheme evaluation patterns**, "Handling Non-Existent Routes" is a frequently tested topic because it tests: > 1. The student's understanding of **client-side routing architecture** > 2. The student's ability to use **wildcard route patterns** and **programmatic navigation** > 3. The student's awareness of **production deployment concerns** (server fallback for SPAs) > 4. The student's understanding of **user experience (UX) best practices** ## 1.4 Key Terminology at a Glance | Term | Meaning | KTU Standard Notation | |------|---------|------------------------| | **Catch-all Route** | A route that matches *any* unmatched path | `path="*"` | | **404 Page** | HTTP status code indicating resource not found | `` | | **SPA Fallback** | Server rule returning `index.html` for unknown paths | `historyApiFallback: true` | | **Wildcard Pattern** | A path pattern that greedily matches anything | `"*"` | | **Navigate Component** | Declarative redirect primitive in React Router v6 | `` | | **`useNavigate` Hook** | Imperative navigation function | `const nav = useNavigate();` | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — How the Router Resolves a URL When a user navigates to a URL in an SPA, the client-side router executes a **route matching algorithm** that can be decomposed into the following discrete steps: ### Step-by-Step Logic Flow 1. **URL Parsing** — The browser's `window.location.pathname` is extracted (e.g., `/products/shoes/123`). 2. **Route Table Lookup** — The router iterates through the registered `` definitions in **declaration order**. 3. **Pattern Matching** — For each route, the router checks if the path pattern matches the requested URL. - **Exact match** → e.g., `path="/about"` matches exactly `/about` - **Parameterized match** → e.g., `path="/user/:id"` matches `/user/42` and extracts `id="42"` - **Wildcard match** → `path="*"` matches **any remaining path** that has not yet been matched 4. **Element Resolution** — Once a match is found, the corresponding `element` (component) is rendered into the `` of its parent layout. 5. **Fallback Activation** — If **no** route matches → the **last route in the table** (which is conventionally the wildcard `*` route) is activated, rendering the 404 component. 6. **HTTP Status Code Consideration** — By default, client-side routers return HTTP `200 OK` even for 404 pages (because the SPA shell `index.html` is served successfully). Achieving a true `404` status code requires server-side cooperation (e.g., setting status headers in SSR, or using a `` with `context.statusCode`). > [!NOTE] > **Why is the wildcard route placed LAST?** > The router uses a **first-match-wins** strategy (similar to `switch`/`case` statements). Placing `path="*"` at the top would shadow every other route. It must therefore be the **terminal fallback** in the route table. ## 2.2 The Three Strategic Approaches to Handling Non-Existent Routes > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners expect students to recognize **three distinct strategies**, not just one. Memorize all three. ### Strategy A — Render a Dedicated 404 Component (Most Common) The wildcard route renders a custom `NotFound` component: ```jsx } /> } /> } /> ``` ### Strategy B — Programmatically Redirect to a Valid Route Instead of showing a 404, force the user back to a safe page: ```jsx } /> ``` > The `replace` prop ensures the bad URL does not pollute the browser history stack. ### Strategy C — Hybrid: Conditional Render Based on Path Pattern Render 404 for *clearly invalid* paths, but redirect for *deprecated* paths: ```jsx } /> } /> ``` ## 2.3 The Anatomy of a Robust `NotFound` Component A KTU-quality `NotFound` component should satisfy the following engineering criteria: | Feature | Purpose | Implementation Hint | |---------|---------|----------------------| | **Friendly headline** | Reduce user confusion | `

404 — Page Not Found

` | | **The attempted URL** | Help users diagnose typos | `useLocation().pathname` | | **Navigation links** | Provide wayfinding | `Home` | | **Status code metadata** | Aid SEO and crawlers | `` | | **Brand consistency** | Match app theme | Inherit global CSS | | **Analytics hook** | Track broken links | `useEffect` → call analytics API | ### Reference Implementation Skeleton ```jsx import { Link, useLocation } from "react-router-dom"; function NotFound() { const location = useLocation(); return (

404

Page Not Found

The requested URL {location.pathname} was not found.

Return to Home
); } ``` ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Quick Reference Table | Concept | React Router v6 Syntax | Notes for Exam | |---------|------------------------|----------------| | Wildcard catch-all route | `} />` | Must be last in `` | | Declarative redirect | `` | `replace` prevents history pollution | | Imperative redirect (inside logic) | `const nav = useNavigate(); nav("/path", { replace: true });` | Use in `useEffect` for redirects | | Get current attempted URL | `const location = useLocation();` | Display in 404 page | | Check if route matched | `useMatch("/path")` | Returns match object or `null` | | Set HTTP 404 status (SSR) | `` | After render, `ctx.statusCode === 404` | | Server fallback (Webpack DevServer) | `historyApiFallback: true` | Dev-only; configure Nginx/Express for prod | | Express server catch-all | `app.get("*", (req, res) => res.sendFile("index.html"));` | Production SPA fallback | | Nesting 404 inside layout | Use `` + child wildcard | Scoped 404s per section | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility This pattern is critical in the following production scenarios: - **E-Commerce Sites** — Handling removed/discontinued product URLs to avoid 404-induced bounce-rate spikes. - **Content Migration** — When a blog URL structure changes (e.g., `/post/123` → `/articles/123`), wildcard redirects preserve SEO link juice. - **Multi-Tenant Applications** — Each tenant has its own URL prefix; unknown subdomains must be caught gracefully. - **Progressive Web Apps (PWAs)** — Offline fallback routes when service workers cannot fetch resources. # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Worked Examples ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Basic Wildcard 404 (Foundation) ### Problem Statement You are building a React SPA with three valid routes: `/`, `/about`, `/contact`. Design a routing configuration that renders a `NotFound` component for any URL that does not match these three routes. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Identify the valid paths.** Let $V = \{\text{"/"}, \text{"/about"}, \text{"/contact"}\}$ be the set of valid route patterns. **Step 2 — Recognize the universe of all possible URLs.** The set of all possible browser paths is theoretically infinite (any string of characters after the origin).
