Include HTTP status codes at each transition. #### Model Solution for (a) [7 Marks]: **Step 1 — Define the concept [1 mark]:** Persisting request data on a form means retaining the values a user typed into an HTML form even when the page is re-rendered, typically after a failed validation. In Django, this is achieved by binding the submitted `request.POST` data to a `Form` instance so the template engine outputs the same values back into the input fields. **Step 2 — Role of `request.POST` [2 marks]:** `request.POST` is a `QueryDict` containing all the form fields submitted via an HTTP POST. By passing it as `data=request.POST` to the Form constructor, Django's form framework is informed that the form is *bound* and that subsequent rendering should use these values: ```python form = ContactForm(data=request.POST) ``` **Step 3 — Role of `is_valid()` [2 marks]:** `is_valid()` is a method that triggers the validation pipeline (field validators, `clean_()` hooks, and the form's `clean()` method). It returns `True` only if all fields pass validation. Crucially, it must be called **before** accessing `cleaned_data`: ```python if form.is_valid(): name = form.cleaned_data['name'] ``` **Step 4 — Role of `cleaned_data` [2 marks]:** `cleaned_data` is a dictionary populated by `is_valid()`, containing **sanitized, validated** Python objects (e.g., a `CharField` returns `str`, an `IntegerField` returns `int`). It is the **only safe** way to access user input in Django. Attempting to read `request.POST['name']` directly bypasses validation and is unsafe. #### Model Solution for (b) [7 Marks]: **Flowchart (draw in answer sheet) [5 marks]:** The student must draw the request lifecycle as a flowchart with the following nodes and edges: - **Start** → Browser sends **GET /contact/** (Status: Request) - Django view → checks `request.method` → constructs **unbound form** → `render('contact.html')` → returns **HTTP 200** - Browser → displays form → user fills and clicks Submit - Browser → sends **POST /contact/** with form body (Status: Request, body: `name=...&email=...`) - Django view → checks method → constructs **bound form** with `data=request.POST` → calls `is_valid()` - **Branch A (Valid):** Save to DB → `HttpResponseRedirect('/thanks/')` → **HTTP 302** → Browser sends **GET /thanks/** → Render → **HTTP 200** (End) - **Branch B (Invalid):** Fall through to `render('contact.html', {'form': form})` → **HTTP 200** → Browser displays form **with user's data and error messages** → Loop back to user editing step **Explanation of HTTP codes [2 marks]:** - **200 OK** — Standard successful response (form rendered or thanks page shown). - **302 Found** — Temporary redirect used by `HttpResponseRedirect` to implement the PRG pattern. - **GET vs POST** — GET is idempotent and safe to refresh; POST is not. --- ### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Alternative Choice) **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **bound forms** and **unbound forms** in Django. Include a code example showing how each is instantiated and the appropriate use case for each in a view function. **(b) [7 Marks]** Write a complete Django view function and corresponding template that demonstrates persisting form data when validation fails. The form should collect a user's `username`, `email`, and `age` (age must be ≥ 18), and any validation error should re-display the form **with the user's previously entered data preserved**. #### Model Solution for (a) [7 Marks]: **Comparison Table [4 marks]:** | Aspect | Bound Form | Unbound Form | |---|---|---| | Construction | `Form(data=request.POST)` | `Form()` | | `is_bound` attribute | `True` | `False` | | `is_valid()` outcome | Returns validation result | Always `False` | | Used in | POST request handling | Initial GET request | | Re-renders user input? | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (empty fields) | | Has `cleaned_data`? | After `is_valid()` is True | Never | **Code Example [3 marks]:** ```python def my_view(request): if request.method == 'POST': form = MyForm(data=request.POST) # Bound form if form.is_valid(): return HttpResponseRedirect('/success/') else: form = MyForm() # Unbound form return render(request, 'template.html', {'form': form}) ``` #### Model Solution for (b) [7 Marks]: **Forms.py [2 marks]:** ```python from django import forms class RegistrationForm(forms.Form): username = forms.CharField(max_length=50) email = forms.EmailField() age = forms.IntegerField(min_value=18) ``` **Views.py [3 marks]:** ```python from django.shortcuts import render, HttpResponseRedirect def register(request): if request.method == 'POST': form = RegistrationForm(data=request.POST) # Bound — preserves input if form.is_valid(): # Process the clean data here return HttpResponseRedirect('/welcome/') else: form = RegistrationForm() # Unbound — empty form return render(request, 'register.html', {'form': form}) ``` **register.html [2 marks]:** ```html
{% csrf_token %}

Username: {{ form.username }}

Email: {{ form.email }}

Age: {{ form.age }}

{% if form.errors %}
    {% for field in form %} {% for error in field.errors %}
  • {{ field.label }}: {{ error }}
    • {% endfor %} {% endfor %}
{% endif %}
``` When the user enters `age=15` and clicks Submit, the form re-renders **with the typed values of username and email preserved**, alongside an error message for the age field — this is the **persistence of request data** in action. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting `{% csrf_token %}`** inside the `
` tag → **−1 to −2 marks** (Django rejects the POST with 403 Forbidden). > 2. **Calling `form.cleaned_data['x']` *before* `is_valid()`** → `AttributeError`; in written exams, students lose marks for not mentioning the order. > 3. **Using `form = MyForm(request.POST)`** instead of `form = MyForm(data=request.POST)` → Works, but the *explicit* `data=` keyword is the KTU-expected style and the textbook definition. > 4. **Confusing `initial` (unbound) with `data` (bound)** → Initial is for *defaults* on empty forms; data is for *user-submitted* values. Mixing them is a **2-mark** deduction. > 5. **Returning a direct `render` after a successful POST** instead of `HttpResponseRedirect` → Loses marks for not implementing the **PRG pattern**, which is a Module-4 key concept. > 6. **Not mentioning HTTP status codes (200, 302)** in flowcharts → Examiners specifically look for these in any diagram question. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Persist Request Data on Form** - ⭐ **Statelessness:** HTTP does not remember requests; persistence is the application's job. - ⭐ **Bound Form:** `Form(data=request.POST)` → `is_bound=True` → preserves input. - ⭐ **Unbound Form:** `Form()` → `is_bound=False` → empty fields. - ⭐ **`is_valid()`** must be called **before** accessing `cleaned_data`. - ⭐ **`cleaned_data`** is sanitized, validated, and **type-coerced** (e.g., strings to ints). - ⭐ **`form.errors`** holds field-level and non-field errors; accessible without `is_valid()` first. - ⭐ **Template auto-render:** `{{ form.field }}` outputs the bound value automatically — no extra code needed in the template. - ⭐ **`{% csrf_token %}`** is mandatory in every POST form in Django. - ⭐ **PRG Pattern:** Always `HttpResponseRedirect` after a successful POST to prevent resubmission. - ⭐ **HTTP 200** = render (GET, form display, success page); **HTTP 302** = redirect. - ⭐ **`request.method`** is a string — exact match `'POST'`, not `'post'`. - ⭐ **`initial`** is for unbound forms; **`data`** is for bound forms — **never** use `initial` to "save" user input. - ⭐ **`is_valid()` on an unbound form is always `False`** — so always wrap in `if request.method == 'POST'`. - ⭐ **Use `request.POST.get('key')`** for safe single-value access; **`getlist('key')`** for multi-value fields like checkboxes. - ⭐ **Real-world use:** Contact forms, login forms, registration forms, multi-step wizards, search filters, admin panels.

topics.title: Adding a WYSIWYG Editor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Adding a WYSIWYG Editor ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **WYSIWYG Editor** (pronounced *wiz-ee-wig*) stands for **"What You See Is What You Get"** editor. In the context of web application development, it is a client-side rich-text editing component embedded inside an HTML form that allows end users to create, format, and manipulate textual and visual content using a graphical user interface (GUI) that visually resembles the final rendered output. Formally (per W3C-aligned DOM specifications), a WYSIWYG editor leverages the **HTML5 `contenteditable` attribute** along with the **`document.execCommand()` API** (legacy) or modern custom **Selection / Range APIs** to transform a normal ` {{-- TinyMCE initialization --}} @endsection ``` ### 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation of Each Configuration Block **Step 1 — Selector Binding** ```javascript tinymce.init({ selector: '#body' }); ``` This line tells TinyMCE: *"Find the DOM element with id=`body` and replace it with an editable instance."* The textarea is *not destroyed* — it is hidden and used as a hidden field holding the editor's HTML output on submit. **Step 2 — Plugin Loading** ```javascript plugins: 'advlist autolink lists link image charmap preview anchor' ``` Each plugin is a JS module extending editor capability: - `advlist` → advanced list formatting (nested, custom markers) - `autolink` → auto-detect URLs and convert to links - `image` → image insertion + media dialog - `preview` → side-by-side preview pane **Step 3 — Toolbar Configuration** ```javascript toolbar: 'undo redo | styleselect | bold italic | link image | numlist bullist | preview' ``` Pipes `|` add visual separators. Order = visual order. This is the visible UI ribbon. **Step 4 — Height & Menubar** ```javascript height: 400, menubar: false ``` `menubar: false` removes the top horizontal menu (File/Edit/View) — typical for embedded CMS editors where the toolbar is sufficient. ### 3.4 Controller Logic — Receiving and Persisting HTML In `app/Http/Controllers/PostController.php`: ```php namespace App\Http\Controllers; use Illuminate\Http\Request; use App\Models\Post; class PostController extends Controller { public function store(Request $request) { $request->validate([ 'title' => 'required|max:255', 'body' => 'required', ]); Post::create([ 'title' => $request->title, 'body' => $request->body, // HTML string from TinyMCE 'user_id' => auth()->id(), ]); return redirect()->route('posts.index') ->with('success', 'Post published successfully.'); } } ``` ### 3.5 Security — Sanitization (Critical Step) > [!WARNING] > **XSS Vulnerability Alert:** WYSIWYG editors output raw HTML. If saved *as-is* and rendered without sanitization, attackers can inject ` ``` **Step 2 — Blade View (`resources/views/posts/create.blade.php`):** ```html @extends('layouts.app') @section('content')
@csrf
@endsection ``` **Step 3 — Controller (`PostController.php`):** ```php public function store(Request $request) { $validated = $request->validate([ 'title' => 'required|max:255', 'body' => 'required', ]); Post::create([ 'title' => $validated['title'], 'body' => $validated['body'], 'user_id' => auth()->id(), ]); return redirect()->route('posts.index'); } ``` **Step 4 — Migration:** ```php $table->longText('body'); ``` **Step 5 — Security (Securing Rendered HTML):** Raw HTML from WYSIWYG editors poses **XSS risks**. Use **HTMLPurifier**: ```php use HTMLPurifier; $config = HTMLPurifier_Config::createDefault(); $purifier = new HTMLPurifier($config); $cleanBody = $purifier->purify($post->body); ``` In blade: ```html
{!! $cleanBody !!}
``` **Crucial Distinction:** - `{{ $body }}` → **escapes** HTML (renders tags as text, safe by default). - `{!! $body !!}` → **renders** raw HTML (use only after sanitization). *(Distribution: 1.5 marks CDN + 2 marks blade view + 1.5 marks controller + 1 mark security.)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Option B* **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, ESE Module 4, Set B]** **(a)** Compare the **iframe-based** and **div-based (contenteditable)** approaches to building WYSIWYG editors. Which approach is dominant in modern editors and why? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Illustrate the complete flow of saving and displaying WYSIWYG-generated content in a Laravel application, including database schema, model definition, view rendering, and XSS prevention. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Answer for Question B:** **(a) iframe vs div WYSIWYG Approaches (7 Marks):** | Aspect | iframe-based | div-based (contenteditable) | |--------|--------------|----------------------------| | **Container** | An isolated `

topics.title: Deployment: Setting Up MongoDB Atlas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Deployment: Setting Up MongoDB Atlas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Deployment: Setting Up MongoDB Atlas

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **MongoDB Atlas** is the official **fully-managed cloud database service (DBaaS — Database-as-a-Service)** offered by MongoDB Inc. It automates the provisioning, patching, scaling, backup, and monitoring of MongoDB clusters across leading cloud providers such as **AWS, Google Cloud Platform (GCP), and Microsoft Azure**.

In the context of the **PBITT504 — Web Application Development** course, MongoDB Atlas is the *production deployment target* for the **MERN/MEAN stack** applications that students build. It eliminates the need to manually install, configure, secure, and maintain a MongoDB server on local or on-premise hardware.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rented Fully-Furnished Apartment"

| Concept | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Local MongoDB** | Building your own house — you buy land, lay bricks, fix plumbing, set up electricity, and maintain everything yourself. |
| **MongoDB Atlas** | Renting a **fully-furnished serviced apartment** in any city (cloud region) you want. The landlord (Atlas) handles plumbing, electricity, security, repairs, and even regular cleaning (backups). You just bring your luggage (application code) and move in. |
| **Cluster** | The entire apartment building with multiple floors (nodes) for redundancy. |
| **Database User** | The person who has the **keys** to enter the apartment. |
| **Network Access (IP Whitelist)** | The **security guard at the gate** who only allows listed visitors inside. |
| **Connection String (URI)** | The **full postal address** of your apartment — anyone with it can find and reach you. |

### 1.3 Why MongoDB Atlas is a KTU-Highlighted Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Under the *Deployment* module, KTU expects students to demonstrate the ability to **independently provision a cloud-hosted NoSQL database**, configure **security boundaries (IP whitelisting + database users)**, and **integrate it with a deployed web application** (typically via the `MONGODB_URI` environment variable in services like Render, Vercel, or Heroku).

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (KTU Vocabulary)

- **M0 Tier (Free Forever)** — Shared RAM, **512 MB storage**, ideal for learning and small projects.
- **M10 / M20 / M30 ...** — Dedicated clusters starting at **$0.08/hr** with SSD storage.
- **Region Latency** — Typically **< 50 ms** when application server and Atlas cluster are in the same region.
- **Default Port** — **27017** (standard MongoDB wire protocol port).
- **Replica Set** — Minimum **3 nodes** for production high availability (1 Primary + 2 Secondaries).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Hierarchy of MongoDB Atlas

MongoDB Atlas follows a strict **hierarchical resource model** that students must internalize:

1. **Organization** → The topmost billing and access-control container (e.g., a college or company).
2. **Project** → A logical grouping inside an organization (e.g., "B.Tech Sem 5 Mini-Project").
3. **Cluster** → The actual deployment of one or more MongoDB nodes (the database server group).
4. **Database** → Inside the cluster, you create logical databases (e.g., `studentDB`).
5. **Collection** → A group of MongoDB documents (analogous to a table in SQL).
6. **Document** → A BSON/JSON record (the atomic unit of data, max **16 MB**).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Configuration Step

| Step | Purpose | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Choose Cloud Provider & Region** | Determines physical location of data | Reduces network latency for end-users; required for **data residency compliance**. |
| **Select Cluster Tier (M0, M10...)** | Decides compute, RAM, and storage | Controls cost vs. performance tradeoff. |
| **IP Whitelist (Network Access)** | Restricts which IP addresses can connect | Prevents unauthorized database access from the public internet. |
| **Create Database User** | Authenticates the connecting client | Implements **Authentication, Authorization, and Auditing (AAA)**. |
| **Choose Connection Method** | Defines driver/protocol used by app | Standardizes integration with Node.js, Python, Java, etc. |
| **Enable Backups (M10+)** | Periodic snapshots of data | Enables **Point-in-Time Recovery (PITR)**. |

### 2.3 Connection String Anatomy — The Most Asked KTU Concept

The **Connection String (URI)** is the single most important artifact produced by Atlas. A typical URI looks like:

```
mongodb+srv://<username>:<password>@cluster0.xxxxx.mongodb.net/<dbname>?retryWrites=true&w=majority&appName=Cluster0
```

Breaking it down (this is a frequent **2-mark short-answer question** in KTU):

| Component | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `mongodb+srv://` | The **SRV connection scheme** — Atlas uses DNS SRV records to discover all cluster nodes automatically. |
| `<username>` | The database user created in the *Database Access* tab. |
| `<password>` | The user's password (URL-encoded if it contains special characters like `@` or `:`). |
| `cluster0.xxxxx.mongodb.net` | The **DNS hostname** of your cluster. |
| `?retryWrites=true` | Automatically retries write operations once on transient network failure. |
| `&w=majority` | Write is acknowledged only after it reaches a **majority of replica set members** (durability). |
| `&appName=Cluster0` | A label shown in Atlas monitoring dashboards. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The `+srv` scheme is **DNS-based** and requires the `mongodb` driver to support it. Always URL-encode special characters in the password (e.g., `@` becomes `%40`) — otherwise the connection string parser will fail silently.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

| Parameter | Free Tier (M0) | Dedicated (M10+) |
|---|---|---|
| **Storage** | 512 MB | 10 GB → petabytes |
| **RAM** | Shared | 2 GB → 768 GB |
| **vCPUs** | Shared | 2 → 192 |
| **Replica Set** | 3 nodes (auto) | 3 → 50 nodes |
| **Backups** | Not available | Continuous + PITR (7 days) |
| **Cost** | **$0** | From **$0.08/hr** |
| **SSL/TLS** | Enforced by default | Enforced by default |
| **Default Port** | 27017 | 27017 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility in the Real World:** A KTU student deploying their MERN mini-project on **Render / Vercel / Railway** uses Atlas as the *only* reliable way to provide a **persistent, publicly reachable MongoDB instance** — because most free PaaS tiers do not include a managed database. Atlas M0 + Render free web service is the canonical **$0 deployment stack** taught in universities.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Setup Procedure (Exhaustive Walkthrough)

The following is the **canonical, end-to-end setup procedure** that every KTU 2024 Scheme student must be able to reproduce from memory during the lab viva and university practical examination.

### Phase 1 — Account Creation

**Step 1.1** Navigate to the official portal: `https://www.mongodb.com/cloud/atlas/register`.

**Step 1.2** Sign up using one of:
- Google account (recommended for students)
- GitHub account (very common for MERN developers)
- Email + password

**Step 1.3** Verify your email address by clicking the link sent by MongoDB.

**Step 1.4** Choose the persona **"I'm learning MongoDB"** to receive a free M0 cluster offer.

### Phase 2 — Project & Cluster Provisioning

**Step 2.1** Atlas creates a default organization named after your account. Create a new **Project** (e.g., `KTU-Sem5-MiniProject`).

**Step 2.2** Click **"Build a Database"** or **"Create a Deployment"**.

**Step 2.3** Select the deployment type:
- **Shared** → M0 (FREE, recommended for students)
- **Dedicated** → M10+ (production)

**Step 2.4** Configure the cluster:

| Configuration | Recommended Student Choice | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Cloud Provider** | AWS | Most widely supported, most regions. |
| **Region** | Closest to India → `ap-south-1` (Mumbai) | Lowest latency (~30 ms). |
| **Cluster Tier** | M0 Sandbox | Free forever. |
| **Cluster Name** | `Cluster0` (default) | Standard, easy to remember. |
| **Additional Settings** | MongoDB Version: latest (7.0 or 8.0) | Most recent stable features. |

**Step 2.5** Click **"Create Deployment"** → Atlas provisions the cluster in **1–3 minutes**.

### Phase 3 — Database User Creation (Authentication Layer)

**Step 3.1** In the security quick-start dialog, click **"Create Database User"**.

**Step 3.2** Choose **Password** authentication method.

**Step 3.3** Set a strong username and password.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Use **only alphanumeric characters and underscores** in the password. Special characters like `@`, `:`, `/`, `?`, `#` MUST be URL-encoded inside the connection string or Atlas will refuse the connection. Examiners specifically test this.

**Step 3.4** Assign the **Built-in Role** `Read and Write to any database` (sufficient for student projects). For production, follow the **Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP)**.

### Phase 4 — Network Access Configuration (IP Whitelist)

**Step 4.1** Choose where to connect from:
- **My Local Environment** → Enter your current public IP.
- **Cloud Environment** → Paste the egress IP of your PaaS provider (e.g., Render provides this).
- **Allow Access from Anywhere** → `0.0.0.0/0` (NOT recommended for production, but acceptable for student demos).

**Step 4.2** Click **"Add Entry"** → confirmation appears within seconds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Viva Question:** *"What does 0.0.0.0/0 mean in the IP whitelist?"* → It means **any IPv4 address on the public internet** can attempt to connect. The connection will still be denied if the username/password is wrong, but the attack surface is greatly increased.

### Phase 5 — Connection Method Selection

**Step 5.1** Atlas offers three connection methods:

1. **Drivers** (used by your application code — Node.js, Python, Java, etc.)
2. **MongoDB Shell (`mongosh`)** — used for manual queries during development.
3. **MongoDB Compass** (GUI) — visual query and schema exploration.

**Step 5.2** Select **Drivers** → choose your driver version (e.g., Node.js + 6.0+).

**Step 5.3** Copy the generated **connection string**.

**Step 5.4** Replace `<username>` and `<password>` placeholders with the credentials from Phase 3.

**Step 5.5** Replace `<dbname>` with the name of your application's database (e.g., `studentAppDB`).

### Phase 6 — Application Integration

The connection string is stored in a **`.env` file** in the web application's root directory — this is KTU-recommended best practice for the **12-factor app methodology**:

```
MONGODB_URI=mongodb+srv://appUser:S3cure%40Pass@cluster0.abcde.mongodb.net/studentAppDB?retryWrites=true&w=majority
PORT=3000
```

The Node.js application loads it using `dotenv`:

```
require('dotenv').config();
const mongoose = require('mongoose');

mongoose.connect(process.env.MONGODB_URI)
  .then(() => console.log('Atlas connected successfully'))
  .catch(err => console.error('Atlas connection error:', err));
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Never commit the `.env` file to public GitHub repositories. Add `.env` to `.gitignore` immediately. This is a **frequently asked 3-mark question** in university viva.

### Phase 7 — Verification

**Step 7.1** Run your application locally — check the console for `Atlas connected successfully`.

**Step 7.2** Open Atlas UI → **Database Deployments → Browse Collections** → confirm your collections and documents appear.

**Step 7.3** Use **Atlas Charts** or **Real-Time Performance Panel** to monitor query execution.

### Step-by-Step Summary Checklist

| # | Action | Marks Weight (Practical) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Account created and verified | 1 |
| 2 | Free M0 cluster provisioned in correct region | 2 |
| 3 | Database user created with strong credentials | 2 |
| 4 | Correct IP whitelist entry | 2 |
| 5 | Connection string correctly placed in `.env` | 2 |
| 6 | Application successfully reads/writes to Atlas | 1 |
| **Total** | | **10 marks** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Architecture — How Atlas Fits Into a MERN Deployment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    clientA[User Browser] -->|HTTPS| webA[Render Vercel Web Server]
    clientB[Mobile App] -->|HTTPS| webA
    webA -->|TCP 27017 over TLS| atlasA[MongoDB Atlas Cluster ap-south-1]
    atlasA --> primaryA[Primary Node]
    atlasA --> secondaryA[Secondary Node 1]
    atlasA --> secondaryB[Secondary Node 2]
    primaryA -.Replication.- secondaryA
    primaryA -.Replication.- secondaryB
    devA[Developer Laptop] -->|mongosh or Compass| atlasA
    devA -->|git push| webA
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Atlas Internal Components

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    orgA[Organization KTU Student]
    projA[Project Sem5 Mini]
    clusterA[Cluster Cluster0 M0]
    dbA[Database studentAppDB]
    collA[Collection users]
    collB[Collection courses]
    userA[DB User appUser]
    ipA[IP Whitelist 0.0.0.0 0]
    backupA[Backups M0 Not Available]
    monitorA[Atlas Monitoring Dashboard]

    orgA --> projA
    projA --> clusterA
    clusterA --> dbA
    dbA --> collA
    dbA --> collB
    clusterA -.Authenticates.- userA
    clusterA -.Allows.- ipA
    clusterA -.Monitored by.- monitorA
    clusterA -.Backs up to.- backupA
```

### 4.3 Setup Flow — End-to-End Sequential Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[Step 1 Sign Up at mongodb.com] --> step2[Step 2 Verify Email]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3 Create Project]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4 Build Database M0]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5 Choose AWS ap-south-1]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6 Create Database User]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7 Add IP to Whitelist]
    step7 --> step8[Step 8 Copy Connection String]
    step8 --> step9[Step 9 Paste in env file]
    step9 --> step10[Step 10 Test Connection]
    step10 --> step11{Connection Successful?}
    step11 -->|Yes| step12[Step 11 Browse Collections in Atlas UI]
    step11 -->|No| step13[Troubleshoot IP Password or Driver Version]
    step13 --> step8
```

### 4.4 Request-Response Flow When a Web App Talks to Atlas

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Browser
    participant W as Web App on Render
    participant D as Driver Mongoose
    participant A as Atlas Cluster
    participant P as Primary Node
    participant S as Secondary Node

    U->>W: GET api students
    W->>D: Student.find
    D->>A: TLS connection to cluster0.abcde.mongodb.net 27017
    A->>P: Route query to Primary
    P-->>A: Result set
    A-->>D: BSON documents
    D-->>W: JSON
    W-->>U: 200 OK with student list
    P->>S: Async replication oplog
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**
*What is MongoDB Atlas? List any two of its advantages over a locally installed MongoDB server.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** MongoDB Atlas is a fully-managed cloud database service provided by MongoDB Inc. that deploys, maintains, and scales MongoDB clusters on cloud platforms such as AWS, Google Cloud, and Azure.

**Any two advantages (2 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Zero server administration:** Atlas handles installation, patching, upgrades, and OS-level security automatically, freeing the developer to focus purely on application logic.
2. **Built-in high availability:** Even the free M0 tier provisions a 3-node replica set with automatic failover, which is impractical to configure manually on a laptop.
3. **Global scalability:** Clusters can be scaled vertically (bigger machines) or horizontally (sharding) with a single click.
4. **Integrated monitoring & security:** Atlas ships with real-time performance dashboards, automated backups (M10+), and TLS-encrypted connections by default.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*Explain the purpose of the IP Whitelist in MongoDB Atlas. What happens if you set the entry to `0.0.0.0/0`?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Purpose (2 marks):** The IP Whitelist is a **network access control list (ACL)** that restricts which public IP addresses are allowed to initiate a TCP connection to the Atlas cluster on port 27017. It is the first line of defense against unauthorized access from the public internet, implementing the *network-layer* of the AAA security model.

**Effect of `0.0.0.0/0` (1 mark):** It permits **any IPv4 address on the public internet** to attempt a connection. While authentication (username/password) still protects data, this drastically increases the attack surface and is strongly discouraged in production. It is, however, commonly used by students deploying demo apps.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **hierarchical architecture** of MongoDB Atlas — from *Organization* down to *Document*. Explain the role of the `mongodb+srv://` connection string. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List and explain the **steps to set up a free M0 cluster** in MongoDB Atlas, including cluster configuration, database user creation, and IP whitelisting. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Hierarchical Architecture (5 marks):**

The MongoDB Atlas resource hierarchy is a **strictly nested** structure:

1. **Organization** (1 mark) — The topmost container; it owns billing, projects, and access control lists. A single user can belong to multiple organizations.
2. **Project** (1 mark) — A logical workspace inside an organization, used to group related clusters (e.g., all clusters for one mini-project).
3. **Cluster** (1 mark) — The actual deployment of MongoDB database processes on the chosen cloud provider's virtual machines. A cluster can be a single-node, replica set, or sharded cluster.
4. **Database** (1 mark) — A namespace inside a cluster that contains collections. Each database has its own set of files on disk.
5. **Collection** (1 mark) — A group of MongoDB documents, analogous to a relational table but **schema-less**.
6. **Document** (Bonus point) — The atomic unit of data in BSON format, max 16 MB.

**Connection String (2 marks):**

The URI begins with `mongodb+srv://`. The `+srv` suffix tells the MongoDB driver to perform a **DNS SRV lookup** on the cluster hostname, which returns **all cluster node hostnames and ports** in a single round trip. This eliminates the need to hardcode individual node addresses, enabling **zero-downtime cluster topology changes** (e.g., adding a new replica). The string also includes authentication credentials, a default database, and connection options like `retryWrites=true&w=majority`.

---

##### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Sign Up (1 mark):** Register at `mongodb.com/cloud/atlas/register` using Google, GitHub, or email. Verify the email and select the *"learning"* persona to unlock the M0 free tier.

**Step 2 — Create Project (1 mark):** Click *New Project*, name it (e.g., `KTU-Sem5`), and click *Create Project*.

**Step 3 — Build Database (1 mark):** Click *Build a Database*, choose **Shared → M0 (FREE)**, select **AWS**, choose region **Mumbai (ap-south-1)**, and name the cluster `Cluster0`. Click *Create* (1–3 min provisioning).

**Step 4 — Create Database User (1 mark):** In the security quick-start, set a username and a strong alphanumeric password. Assign the built-in role *Read and Write to any database*. Click *Create User*.

**Step 5 — Add IP to Whitelist (1 mark):** Choose *My Local Environment* (auto-detects IP) or paste `0.0.0.0/0` for anywhere access. Click *Add Entry*.

**Step 6 — Choose Connection Method (1 mark):** Select *Drivers*, choose *Node.js*, and copy the generated URI.

**Step 7 — Integrate with App (1 mark):** Paste the URI into the `.env` file as `MONGODB_URI=...`. URL-encode any special characters in the password. Test the connection by running the app.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]`

**(a)** Compare **Shared (M0)** and **Dedicated (M10+)** cluster tiers in MongoDB Atlas across at least four parameters. When would you recommend M10 over M0? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **security model of MongoDB Atlas** with respect to *Authentication* and *Authorization*. What is the Principle of Least Privilege and how does Atlas support it? **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (4 marks — 1 mark per row):**

| Parameter | Shared (M0) | Dedicated (M10+) |
|---|---|---|
| **Cost** | Free forever | From $0.08/hour (~$57/month) |
| **Storage** | 512 MB | 10 GB → petabytes (auto-scaling) |
| **RAM & vCPU** | Shared, burstable | Dedicated, guaranteed |
| **Backups** | Not available | Continuous + PITR (7 days) |
| **Performance Insights** | Basic | Advanced (slow-query analyzer) |
| **Support** | Community only | 24×7 enterprise support |
| **High Availability** | 3-node replica set (auto) | Multi-region clusters possible |

**When to upgrade to M10 (3 marks):**

1. When the application goes into **production** and downtime is unacceptable.
2. When storage exceeds **400 MB** (90% of 512 MB) — Atlas sends a warning email.
3. When **Point-in-Time Recovery** is required for audit / data-loss protection.
4. When query latency exceeds SLO thresholds (M0 has shared, contended resources).

---

##### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Authentication (3 marks):**
Atlas supports two authentication mechanisms:
1. **SCRAM (Salted Challenge Response Authentication Mechanism)** — the default; username + password hashed with bcrypt and stored in the `admin.system.users` collection.
2. **X.509 Certificates** — for mutual TLS authentication in production.
3. **AWS IAM / Azure AD Federation** — for SSO integration in enterprise setups.

**Authorization (2 marks):**
Atlas uses **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)**. Each user is assigned one or more built-in roles such as:
- `read` — read all non-system collections
- `readWrite` — read and write all non-system collections
- `dbAdmin` — schema-related operations
- `userAdmin` — manage users and roles
- `clusterAdmin` — full cluster-level control

**Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP) (2 marks):**
PoLP states that a user should be granted **only the minimum permissions necessary** to perform their function. Atlas supports PoLP by allowing:
1. **Database-scoped roles** (e.g., read-only on `studentAppDB` only).
2. **Custom roles** with fine-grained action lists (find, insert, update, etc.).
3. **Project-level access** separation so a junior dev can access dev clusters but not production.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing authentication with authorization** — Authentication answers *"Who are you?"*; Authorization answers *"What can you do?"* Examiners deduct 1 mark if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Forgetting to URL-encode special characters** in the Atlas password when embedding it in the connection string. This is a guaranteed **½ to 1 mark** deduction in practicals.
> 3. **Writing `mongodb://` instead of `mongodb+srv://`** — the `+srv` scheme is mandatory for Atlas. Using the older `mongodb://` will either fail or require a manual replica set connection string.
> 4. **Adding `.env` to GitHub** — this is treated as a serious security violation in KTU practicals; 1 mark is deducted and the candidate may be asked to demonstrate the `.gitignore` fix.
> 5. **Forgetting to whitelist the deployed server's IP** — the most common runtime error in deployment viva. Students should explicitly mention adding the PaaS provider's outbound IP.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **MongoDB Atlas = Managed Cloud MongoDB** (DBaaS) by MongoDB Inc., hosted on **AWS, GCP, Azure**.
- **M0 is FREE forever** → 512 MB, 3-node replica set, ideal for student projects.
- **Default port = 27017**; **TLS is always on**.
- **Setup flow:** Sign up → Create project → Build M0 cluster → Create DB user → Whitelist IP → Copy `mongodb+srv://` URI → Paste in `.env` → Test.
- **Connection string starts with `mongodb+srv://`** — never `mongodb://` for Atlas.
- **URL-encode special characters** (`@` → `%40`, `:` → `%3A`, `/` → `%2F`, `?` → `%3F`, `#` → `%23`) in the password.
- **Never commit `.env` to GitHub** — always add it to `.gitignore`.
- **IP Whitelist `0.0.0.0/0`** = allow any IP (insecure but common in student demos).
- **Authentication = Who are you? (SCRAM, X.509, IAM)**
- **Authorization = What can you do? (RBAC roles: read, readWrite, dbAdmin, userAdmin).**
- **Principle of Least Privilege** = grant minimum required permissions; use database-scoped custom roles.
- **Hierarchy:** Organization → Project → Cluster → Database → Collection → Document (max 16 MB).
- **Backups are NOT available on M0** — upgrade to M10+ for Point-in-Time Recovery.
- **For deployment on Render/Vercel/Railway:** add the PaaS provider's outbound IP to the whitelist, or use `0.0.0.0/0` for student projects.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deploying Web Apps on Heroku
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Deploying Web Apps on Heroku

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heroku** is a cloud-based **Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS)** that enables developers to deploy, run, and scale web applications entirely in the cloud without managing the underlying hardware, operating system, or network infrastructure. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBITT504)**, Heroku is positioned as a representative example of a *managed application platform* supporting multiple runtimes including **Node.js, Ruby on Rails, Python (Django/Flask), Java, PHP, and Go**.

Heroku abstracts the four classical layers of the application stack into developer-managed units:

| Stack Layer | Developer-Managed Unit | Heroku-Managed Unit |
|-------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Hardware / VM | None | Dyno host (Linux container) |
| OS / Runtime | None (implicit) | OS image + Buildpack |
| Application logic | Source code | Compiled slug |
| Data | Add-on (Postgres, Redis) | Storage layer |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Heroku is a Rented Shop in a Mall"**
>
> Imagine you have built a fantastic product (your web app). Instead of buying land, constructing a building, wiring electricity, and installing security (i.e., configuring servers, OS, load balancers, firewalls), you simply **rent a ready-made shop counter in a huge shopping mall** owned by Heroku. You walk in, hand over your product, place it on the shelf, and the mall handles:
> - **Electricity** (compute power)
> - **Security guards** (firewalls, HTTPS)
> - **Customer footfall** (routing/scaling)
> - **Storage rooms** (database add-ons)
>
> You pay only for the counter space and shelves you use (dyno-hours), and if your product becomes viral, the mall instantly gives you more counters (**horizontal scaling**).

## 1.3 Why Heroku in Web Application Development?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Heroku is taught in Module 4 to demonstrate the practical realization of **continuous deployment**, **environment isolation**, and **horizontal scaling** — concepts that are abstract in a localhost development environment but become tangible on a PaaS.

**Key Engineering Metrics (Heroku SLAs):**
- **Dyno sleep threshold:** 30 minutes of inactivity on a *free* or *hobby* web dyno.
- **Routing layer:** Automated HTTP/HTTPS termination at `*.herokuapp.com`.
- **Slug size limit:** 500 MB compressed (web slug).
- **Cold-start latency:** Typically **2 to 5 seconds** for the first request after dyno sleep.
- **Region availability:** Common regions include `us`, `eu`, and `ap` (Asia-Pacific), governed by `HEROKU_REGION`.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Heroku Operational Architecture

Heroku's runtime architecture is composed of four cooperating subsystems. Understanding each subsystem is essential for the **ESE (End Semester Examination)**.

### 2.1.1 The Dyno (Lightweight Linux Container)

> [!NOTE]
> **Dyno** is the fundamental unit of computation on Heroku. Conceptually, it is an **isolated, virtualized user-space container** running on a shared Linux kernel. Each dyno is ephemeral, stateless, and disposable.

- A **web dyno** serves HTTP traffic and participates in the routing mesh.
- A **worker dyno** runs background jobs (queues, cron-like tasks).
- Dynos are **read-only at the filesystem level** except for a transient `/tmp` directory.

### 2.1.2 The Buildpack (Compilation Engine)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Buildpack** is a set of scripts that detects the language/runtime of your pushed source code, fetches the appropriate compiler and dependencies, and produces a **slug** (an executable, compressed image).

Standard buildpacks include: `heroku/nodejs`, `heroku/ruby`, `heroku/python`, `heroku/java`, `heroku/go`, `heroku/php`.

### 2.1.3 The Slug (Compiled Artifact)

The **slug** is the deployable artifact — a tar-compressed, pre-compiled bundle of your application together with all its runtime dependencies. It is the unit that actually runs inside a dyno.

### 2.1.4 The Procfile (Process Declaration)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Procfile** is a plain text file named `Procfile` (no extension) placed in the project root. It declares the command used to start each process type.

**Standard Procfile syntax:**

```
web:    gunicorn app:app --log-file -
worker: celery -A tasks worker --loglevel=info
```

The `web` process type is special — it is the **only** process type that receives HTTP traffic from the router.

### 2.1.5 The Router & Routing Mesh

When a user hits `https://<app-name>.herokuapp.com`, the request enters the **Heroku Router**, a distributed layer-7 load balancer that:
- Terminates TLS (HTTPS).
- Forwards the request to a random healthy **web dyno**.
- Applies a **30-second request timeout** (502 Gateway if exceeded).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Definition / Formula | Key Constraint |
|---------|----------------------|----------------|
| **PaaS** | Platform-as-a-Service | Heroku, Render, Vercel, App Engine |
| **Dyno** | Virtualized Linux container | Stateless, ephemeral |
| **Buildpack** | Language detector + compiler | Auto-detected from source |
| **Slug** | Compiled, compressed artifact | Max **500 MB** |
| **Procfile** | Process declaration file | Must have `web:` entry |
| **Config Var** | Environment variable | `heroku config:set KEY=value` |
| **Add-on** | Managed third-party service | Postgres, Redis, Papertrail |
| **Release** | Immutable deployment version | Auto-rollback on failure |
| **Release Phase** | Pre-boot migration step | Runs once per release |
| **Slug Compiler** | `bin/compile` (in buildpack) | Produces slug from source |
| **Router Timeout** | Hard HTTP timeout | **30 seconds** |
| **Dyno Hour** | Billing unit | 1 dyno × 1 hour = 1 unit |
| **Ephemeral FS** | Local disk not persisted | Use S3 / add-on for storage |
| **Foreman** | Local Procfile runner (legacy) | Replaced by `heroku local` |
| **HEROKU_REGION** | Geographic region env var | `us`, `eu`, `ap` |

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Component

- **Why Buildpacks instead of Docker?** Buildpacks provide **zero-config language detection**, making the platform approachable for beginners. Modernly, Heroku also supports **container registry (Docker)** deployments.
- **Why Ephemeral Filesystem?** Because dynos are disposable, any file written locally is lost on restart, dyno cycling, or deploy. This forces architects to use **external stores** (S3, Postgres) for persistent data.
- **Why Config Vars instead of `.env`?** Config Vars are injected as **real OS-level environment variables** and are visible only to collaborators, making them production-safe.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Heroku is used in industry for: **MVP launches, microservices, webhook receivers, scheduled jobs, REST API backends, and rapid prototyping**. It is *not* typically used for long-running high-performance computing, due to dyno memory caps (max **standard-2x : 1 GB RAM**, performance tiers go up to **14 GB RAM** with extended compute dynos).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Deployment Procedure & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Pre-Deployment Checklist

Before any push to Heroku, the following invariants must hold:

1. A **Git repository** is initialized in the project root.
2. A `Procfile` exists in the project root.
3. The application **binds to the port specified by the `PORT` environment variable** (NOT a hardcoded port like 3000).
4. A `runtime.txt` (Python) or `package.json` (Node) specifies the runtime version.
5. Dependencies are declared (`requirements.txt` / `package.json`).

## 3.2 The Canonical Deployment Workflow (Node.js Example)

Let **A** denote the set of local source files, and let **H** denote the Heroku remote.

### Step 1 — Install the Heroku CLI

The Heroku Command Line Interface is downloaded and added to `PATH`.

```bash
# macOS
brew tap heroku/brew && brew install heroku

# Ubuntu/Debian
curl https://cli-assets.heroku.com/install-apt.sh | sh
```

### Step 2 — Authentication

The CLI exchanges the local public key with Heroku's auth server:

$$
\text{session} = \text{Heroku Auth Server}.\text{login}(\text{email}, \text{API\_key})
$$

**Model command:**

```bash
heroku login
```

This opens a browser-based OAuth flow.

### Step 3 — Create the Heroku Application

Heroku's API provisions a new logical application, generating a unique name and Git remote URL.

```bash
heroku create my-ktu-webapp
```

The Heroku CLI automatically adds a Git remote named `heroku` pointing to:
$$
\text{HEROKU\_REMOTE} = \text{git@heroku.com:my-ktu-webapp.git}
$$

### Step 4 — Configure the Procfile

For a Node.js Express app, the Procfile should read:

```
web: node server.js
```

This tells the dyno manager: *spawn the `web` process by executing `node server.js`*.

### Step 5 — Bind to `PORT` Environment Variable (Critical Pitfall)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 14-Mark Question Pitfall:** Hardcoding `app.listen(3000)` will cause `Application Error` on Heroku, because the Heroku router forwards to the port in `$PORT`, which is dynamically assigned (typically **port 5000 to 65535**).

The correct Node.js snippet is:

$$
\text{const PORT} = \text{process.env.PORT} \;\vert\; 3000
$$

```javascript
const express = require('express');
const app = express();
const PORT = process.env.PORT || 3000;

app.get('/', (req, res) => {
  res.send('Deployed on Heroku!');
});

app.listen(PORT, () => {
  console.log(`Listening on port ${PORT}`);
});
```

### Step 6 — Provision a Database (Optional)

Heroku Postgres is a managed PostgreSQL instance accessed via a `DATABASE_URL` config var.

```bash
heroku addons:create heroku-postgresql:essential-0
heroku config # to inspect DATABASE_URL
```

### Step 7 — Set Environment Variables (Config Vars)

```bash
heroku config:set NODE_ENV=production
heroku config:set JWT_SECRET=my_super_secret_key
heroku config:set API_KEY=abcd1234
```

These are injected at runtime as standard POSIX environment variables and can be read in code as:

```javascript
const secret = process.env.JWT_SECRET;
```

### Step 8 — Deploy via Git Push

The Heroku remote is added implicitly by `heroku create`. The push triggers the **build pipeline**:

$$
\text{git push heroku main} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Buildpack} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Slug} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Release}
$$

```bash
git add .
git commit -m "Initial Heroku deployment"
git push heroku main
```

### Step 9 — Open the Deployed Application

```bash
heroku open
heroku logs --tail   # stream live logs
```

### Step 10 — Scale Dynos

$$
\text{Total Dyno-Hours/month} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{Dynos}_i \times \text{Hours})
$$

```bash
heroku ps:scale web=2 worker=1
```

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Build Pipeline Stages

| Stage | Input | Output | Tooling |
|-------|-------|--------|---------|
| **1. Receive** | `git push` payload | Source tarball | Heroku API |
| **2. Detect** | Source tarball | Buildpack identity | Buildpack detection |
| **3. Compile** | Source + buildpack | Compiled artifacts | `bin/compile` |
| **4. Export** | Compiled artifacts | Slug (tarball) | `bin/compile` |
| **5. Release** | Slug + config vars | Immutable release | Heroku platform |
| **6. Boot** | Release | Running dyno(s) | `Procfile` |

## 3.4 Verification of a Successful Deployment

After `git push heroku main`, the terminal displays the build log. A successful deployment ends with:

$$
\text{verdict} = \begin{cases}
\text{"Build succeeded"} \rightarrow \text{Releasing v} N \\
\text{"Build failed"} \rightarrow \text{! Push rejected, failed to compile}
\end{cases}
$$

Run `heroku ps` to confirm dyno state:

```
=== Dynos:
web.1: up for 5m (standard-1x)
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Deployment Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef dev fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#0D47A1
    classDef heroku fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#4A148C
    classDef ext fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#BF360C
    classDef user fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#1B5E20

    devA[Developer Machine]:::dev
    cliA[Heroku CLI]:::dev
    gitA[Git Push heroku main]:::dev

    herokuA[Heroku API Gateway]:::heroku
    buildA[Buildpack Detection]:::heroku
    compileA[Slug Compiler bin/compile]:::heroku
    slugA[Slug Artifact tar]:::heroku
    releaseA[Release Phase]:::heroku
    dynoA[Web Dyno 1]:::heroku
    dynoB[Web Dyno 2]:::heroku
    routerA[Heroku Router L7 LB]:::heroku

    extA[Heroku Postgres Add-on]:::ext
    extB[Redis Add-on]:::ext
    configA[Config Vars env]:::heroku

    userA[End User Browser]:::user
    tlsA[HTTPS TLS Termination]:::heroku

    devA --> cliA --> gitA --> herokuA
    herokuA --> buildA --> compileA --> slugA --> releaseA
    configA -.injected.-> releaseA
    releaseA --> dynoA
    releaseA --> dynoB
    userA --> tlsA --> routerA
    routerA --> dynoA
    routerA --> dynoB
    dynoA -.SQL.-> extA
    dynoB -.cache.-> extB
```

## 4.2 Heroku Process Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Created: heroku create
    Created --> Building: git push
    Building --> Released: buildpack OK
    Building --> Failed: compile error
    Failed --> Building: new commit
    Released --> Booting: dyno start
    Booting --> Up: web process listening
    Booting --> Crashed: process exited
    Crashed --> Booting: auto-restart (10 retries)
    Up --> Sleeping: 30 min no traffic (eco)
    Sleeping --> Up: incoming request
    Up --> [*]: heroku apps:destroy
```

## 4.3 Git-to-Dyno Data Flow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef src fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef hp fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000
    classDef run fill:#E8EAF6,stroke:#1A237E,color:#000

    A1[Local Source Code]:::src
    A2[package.json]:::src
    A3[Procfile]:::src
    A4[Git Repository]:::src

    B1[Heroku Build Server]:::hp
    B2[Buildpack heroku/nodejs]:::hp
    B3[Slug Compiled Image]:::hp
    B4[Release v1]:::hp

    C1[Dyno Container]:::run
    C2[Node.js Runtime]:::run
    C3[Express App Process]:::run
    C4[PORT env listener]:::run

    A1 --> A4
    A2 --> A4
    A3 --> A4
    A4 -- git push --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 -- deploy --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a Procfile in Heroku? Why is it mandatory for deployment?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Procfile** is a plain text file named exactly `Procfile` (no file extension) placed in the root directory of a Heroku application. It declares the commands used to start each **process type** of the application. It is mandatory because Heroku uses the Procfile to determine which processes to launch inside the dyno after deployment. Each line follows the syntax:

$$
\text{process\_type}: \text{command}
$$

The `web` process type is mandatory for any application that must receive HTTP traffic; the router only forwards requests to a process named `web`. *(3 marks)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any three differences between a PaaS and an IaaS deployment model.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | PaaS (e.g., Heroku) | IaaS (e.g., AWS EC2) |
|--------|---------------------|----------------------|
| OS management | Provider-managed | User-managed |
| Scaling | One-line CLI / GUI | Manual configuration |
| Pricing | Per-dyno-hour | Per-VM-hour + data transfer |
| Skill required | Low — code-first | High — DevOps heavy |
| Example use | Rapid MVPs, REST APIs | Custom infra, legacy apps |

*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Full Procedure Type

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Paper - PBITT504]`**
>
> **(a)** Explain the architecture of Heroku. With a neat diagram, describe the role of **dynos, buildpacks, slugs, and the Procfile** in the deployment pipeline. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Demonstrate, with a Node.js Express application as an example, the **step-by-step procedure to deploy a web app on Heroku**. Include code snippets and CLI commands. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Heroku Architecture (7 marks)**

Heroku's architecture is a layered PaaS comprising the following subsystems:

1. **Dyno** — A lightweight, isolated Linux container that hosts one process. Dynos are **stateless and ephemeral**; local files are wiped on restart. (1 mark)
2. **Buildpack** — A detection + compilation script. Heroku auto-detects the language (e.g., Node.js) and executes `bin/compile` to install dependencies and compile the source. (1 mark)
3. **Slug** — A compressed, executable artifact produced by the buildpack. Slug size is capped at **500 MB**. (1 mark)
4. **Procfile** — A root-level text file that declares process startup commands. The `web` process receives HTTP traffic. (1 mark)
5. **Router** — A layer-7 load balancer that terminates TLS and distributes traffic across web dynos with a **30-second timeout**. (1 mark)
6. **Config Vars** — Injected OS-level environment variables, used for secrets and runtime config. (1 mark)
7. **Add-ons** — Managed third-party services (Postgres, Redis, Papertrail). (1 mark)

---

**(b) Step-by-Step Node.js Deployment (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Initialize the project (1 mark):**

```bash
mkdir ktu-heroku-demo && cd ktu-heroku-demo
npm init -y
npm install express
```

**Step 2 — Create `server.js` (1 mark):**

```javascript
const express = require('express');
const app = express();
const PORT = process.env.PORT || 3000;

app.get('/', (req, res) => {
  res.send('Hello KTU — Deployed on Heroku!');
});

app.listen(PORT, () => {
  console.log(`Server running on port ${PORT}`);
});
```

**Step 3 — Create the `Procfile` (1 mark):**

```
web: node server.js
```

**Step 4 — Initialize Git and commit (1 mark):**

```bash
git init
git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit"
```

**Step 5 — Login and create the Heroku app (1 mark):**

```bash
heroku login
heroku create ktu-webapp-demo
```

**Step 6 — Deploy via Git push (1 mark):**

```bash
git push heroku main
heroku open
heroku logs --tail
```

**Step 7 — Scale the application (1 mark):**

```bash
heroku ps:scale web=2
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Conceptual + Troubleshooting Type

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper - PBITT504]`**
>
> **(a)** Discuss the role of **Heroku add-ons** and **Config Vars** in production deployment. How do they differ from traditional environment-variable `.env` files? *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A deployed Node.js application is throwing `Application Error (H14 — No web processes running)`. Diagnose the issue and provide a fix, with proper CLI commands. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Heroku Add-ons and Config Vars (7 marks)**

**Add-ons (3 marks):**
Add-ons are managed, third-party services that extend Heroku's native capabilities. They are provisioned via `heroku addons:create` and integrate seamlessly via auto-injected **Config Vars** (e.g., `DATABASE_URL` for Postgres, `REDIS_URL` for Redis). Examples include: `heroku-postgresql`, `heroku-redis`, `papertrail` (logging), `newrelic` (APM).

**Config Vars (3 marks):**
Config Vars are Heroku's way of injecting environment variables into the runtime of a dyno. They are:
- Encrypted at rest.
- Visible only to collaborators of the app.
- Accessible in code via `process.env.KEY_NAME` (Node.js), `os.environ.get('KEY_NAME')` (Python), or `ENV['KEY_NAME']` (Ruby).

**Difference from `.env` (1 mark):**
A `.env` file is local, plaintext, and not synced with the platform. Config Vars are **cloud-native**, encrypted, and audited — they are the production-grade equivalent of `.env`.

---

**(b) H14 Error Diagnosis and Fix (7 marks)**

**Error Meaning (2 marks):**
`H14` indicates that **no `web` dyno is running**. The Heroku router cannot forward traffic because the process type `web` is missing or has crashed repeatedly.

**Diagnosis Steps (2 marks):**

```bash
heroku ps                 # check running dynos
heroku logs --tail        # inspect crash stack trace
```

**Likely Root Causes (2 marks):**
1. **No Procfile in repo root** — Heroku could not detect a `web` process.
2. **App listens on a hardcoded port** — `app.listen(3000)` ignores `process.env.PORT`, so the dyno boots but the router cannot reach it.
3. **Syntax error in `server.js`** — dyno crashes immediately.

**Fix (1 mark):**

```bash
# Ensure Procfile exists in project root
echo "web: node server.js" > Procfile

# Ensure server binds to PORT
# Edit server.js:
#   const PORT = process.env.PORT || 3000;

git add . && git commit -m "Fix H14: add Procfile and bind to PORT"
git push heroku main
heroku ps:scale web=1
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do not hardcode the port.** This is the single most common reason for H14 errors and accounts for an estimated **30% of lost marks** in deployment questions.
> - **Do not forget `Procfile`.** A missing Procfile results in a `No such file or directory` build error, not a runtime error.
> - **Do not store secrets in source code.** Always use Config Vars — committing a `JWT_SECRET` to GitHub is a security violation.
> - **Do not use local SQLite or local filesystem** for persistent data on Heroku; use Postgres or an S3-compatible store.
> - **Avoid writing to the local filesystem**, as it is ephemeral and **wiped on every dyno restart**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Must-Know" Items for KTU ESE**

- **PaaS** abstracts OS, hardware, and networking. Heroku is the canonical example. *(1 mark question)*
- **Procfile** is mandatory, lives in the project root, has no file extension, and must declare a `web` process.
- **PORT** must come from `process.env.PORT`, not be hardcoded. *(H14 / H12 trap)*
- **Dyno** is the unit of compute. `web` dynos serve HTTP; `worker` dynos run background jobs.
- **Buildpacks** auto-detect language. For Node.js, `heroku/nodejs` is used.
- **Slug** = compiled artifact; max **500 MB**; produced by the buildpack.
- **Router** is a layer-7 LB with a hard **30-second timeout**.
- **Config Vars** are the production-safe alternative to `.env` files. They are encrypted and per-app.
- **Heroku Postgres** and **Heroku Redis** are first-class managed add-ons, accessed via `DATABASE_URL` and `REDIS_URL`.
- **Ephemeral Filesystem:** any file written to disk on a dyno is lost on restart. Use **S3** or **Postgres** for persistent data.
- **Scaling command:** `heroku ps:scale web=N`.
- **Logs command:** `heroku logs --tail` streams real-time logs.
- **Region selection:** Use `heroku create --region eu` for European data residency.
- **Release Phase:** runs `release: <cmd>` once per release; useful for DB migrations.
- **Common error codes:**
  - `H10` — App crashed.
  - `H12` — Request timeout (>30 s).
  - `H14` — No web dynos running.
  - `H20` — App boot timeout.
  - `R10` — Boot timeout (worker process).
- **Alternatives to Heroku** (frequently asked): Render, Railway, Vercel, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Google App Engine, Fly.io.
- **Key CLI commands to memorize:**
  - `heroku login`
  - `heroku create [app-name]`
  - `heroku config:set KEY=value`
  - `heroku addons:create heroku-postgresql:essential-0`
  - `heroku ps:scale web=N`
  - `heroku logs --tail`
  - `heroku open`
  - `heroku run bash`
  - `heroku apps:destroy`
- **Deployment via container registry** (Docker) is also supported: `heroku container:push web` and `heroku container:release web`.
- **Free dynos** sleep after 30 minutes of inactivity; **Eco** and **Basic** dynos do not sleep.
- **HEROKU_REGION** env var indicates the region of the running dyno.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project Suggestions: Countdown Clock, To-Do List, Rock Paper Scissors Game, Shopping Cart Page
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBITT504 - WEB APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Project Suggestions: Countdown Clock, To-Do List, Rock Paper Scissors Game, Shopping Cart Page # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What Are These "Project Suggestions"? In the KTU 2024 Scheme for **PBITT504 – Web Application Development**, Module 4 is the **practical capstone module**. The University does not assign a single monolithic project. Instead, it prescribes **four mini-project scaffolds**, each chosen so that a student exercises a *different* pillar of client-side web engineering: 1. **Countdown Clock** → exercises the *temporal/clock* and *Date API* domain. 2. **To-Do List (Task Manager)** → exercises *CRUD on DOM* + *persistent storage*. 3. **Rock Paper Scissors Game** → exercises *game state, randomization, and conditional logic*. 4. **Shopping Cart Page** → exercises *array-based data modeling, totals computation, and UI re-rendering*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Verbatim:** *"Mini-project suggestions: a real-world problem to be solved using JavaScript — e.g., Countdown Clock, To-Do List, Rock–Paper–Scissors, Shopping Cart."* > The University expects students to **implement at least one** of these end-to-end, but the End-Semester Exam (ESE) tests the *concepts* demonstrated by all four. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogies — Learning the "Why" Behind Each Project | # | Project | Real-World Analogy | Core Engineering Skill Drilled | |---|---------|---------------------|--------------------------------| | 1 | **Countdown Clock** | A digital watch counting down to New Year's Eve | Time arithmetic, interval-based UI updates | | 2 | **To-Do List** | Sticky notes on a fridge that survive a power-cut (localStorage) | DOM CRUD + browser persistence | | 3 | **Rock Paper Scissors** | The childhood "odds-on" hand game | Random number generation + state machines | | 4 | **Shopping Cart** | The trolley you push through a supermarket aisle | Array of objects + computed totals | > [!NOTE] > All four projects share a common spine: **HTML for skeleton, CSS for skin, JavaScript for brain**. The JavaScript brain is where the marks lie. --- ## 1.3 The Three Universally Tested Concepts Regardless of which project a student picks, the examiner tests exactly three competencies: - **DOM Manipulation** — `document.querySelector`, `createElement`, `appendChild`, `innerHTML`, `textContent`. - **Event Handling** — `addEventListener('click', ...)`, form `submit`, keyboard events. - **State Persistence** — `localStorage.setItem` / `getItem` (or `sessionStorage`). > [!TIP] > **High-Yield Insight:** If you master these three for *any one* project, you can answer 80% of the Part-B questions for *all four*. The exam is concept-agnostic, not project-specific. --- ## 1.4 Glossary of Mandatory JavaScript Building Blocks > [!NOTE] > The following APIs are **non-negotiable** for Module 4 — every one of them appears in at least one of the four projects. - **`new Date()`** — Captures the current moment; used heavily in the Countdown Clock. - **`Date.now()`** — Returns milliseconds since the Unix epoch (`1970-01-01T00:00:00Z`); the standard for measuring *elapsed* time. - **`setInterval(fn, ms)`** — Repeatedly calls `fn` every `ms` milliseconds; the heartbeat of any clock. - **`clearInterval(id)`** — Stops a running interval; prevents memory leaks. - **`Math.random()`** — Returns a float in `[0, 1)`; the basis of the RPS game's randomness. - **`Math.floor()`** — Truncates to an integer; combined with `Math.random()` for dice-style selection. - **`localStorage`** — Browser key-value store (~5–10 MB limit); persists across tabs and restarts. - **`JSON.stringify()` / `JSON.parse()`** — Convert JS objects to/from strings for `localStorage`. - **`Array.prototype.push / splice / filter / map / reduce`** — The cart and to-do list rely on these. - **`addEventListener(event, handler)`** — Binds user actions to code. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The Common Architecture — "MVC-Lite" for Vanilla JS Every one of the four projects fits into a **Model–View–Controller (MVC)-Lite** pattern, which KTU examiners love because it tests architectural thinking: - **Model** → Plain JavaScript variables / arrays / objects (e.g., `let cart = []`). - **View** → The HTML DOM rendered via `innerHTML` or `createElement`. - **Controller** → Event handlers that mutate the Model and re-render the View. ``` User Click → Event Listener (Controller) → Mutate Model → Re-render View ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Pet Phrase:** "Explain how the View stays in sync with the Model." > **Ideal Answer:** "After every state change, a single `render()` function is called that rebuilds the visible UI from the Model array, ensuring a single source of truth." --- ## 2.2 Project 1 — The Countdown Clock (Temporal Logic) ### 2.2.1 The Math Foundation A countdown clock computes the **delta** between a *target* time and the *current* time: $$\Delta t = T_{\text{target}} - T_{\text{now}}$$ If $\Delta t \le 0$, the event has elapsed and the UI must display **`"EXPIRED"`**. ### 2.2.2 Conversion from Milliseconds to Human Units The total $\Delta t$ arrives in **milliseconds**. To display days/hours/minutes/seconds: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{days} &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{\Delta t}{1000 \times 60 \times 60 \times 24} \right\rfloor \\ \text{hours} &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{\Delta t}{1000 \times 60 \times 60} \right\rfloor \bmod 24 \\ \text{minutes} &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{\Delta t}{1000 \times 60} \right\rfloor \bmod 60 \\ \text{seconds} &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{\Delta t}{1000} \right\rfloor \bmod 60 \end{aligned} $$ The `mod 24 / mod 60` step is what **prevents `48:00:00`** and forces `02:00:00` instead. ### 2.2.3 The Heartbeat A `setInterval(updateClock, 1000)` ticks once per second. **Never** use `setTimeout` recursively for a clock — it drifts. --- ## 2.3 Project 2 — The To-Do List (CRUD + Persistence) ### 2.3.1 The Four Verbs of CRUD | Operation | JS Equivalent | Typical Trigger | |-----------|---------------|-----------------| | **C**reate | `tasks.push(item)` | User clicks "Add" | | **R**ead | `render()` loops over `tasks` | Page load + every mutation | | **U**pdate | `tasks[i].done = !tasks[i].done` | User clicks the checkbox | | **D**elete | `tasks.splice(i, 1)` | User clicks the trash icon | ### 2.3.2 The Persistence Trinity The three-line incantation every KTU student must memorize: ``` SAVE → localStorage.setItem('tasks', JSON.stringify(tasks)); LOAD → tasks = JSON.parse(localStorage.getItem('tasks')) || []; AUTO → Call SAVE at the END of every mutating function. ``` > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Forgetting that `localStorage` only stores **strings**. If you call `setItem` with an array, `getItem` will return the string `"[object Object],[object Object]"` — and the page silently breaks. --- ## 2.4 Project 3 — Rock Paper Scissors (Probability + State Machine) ### 2.4.1 The Probability Engine The computer's "move" is just an integer from `0` to `2`: $$ m_{\text{comp}} = \left\lfloor \text{Math.random()} \times 3 \right\rfloor $$ Each integer maps to `{ 0: 'rock', 1: 'paper', 2: 'scissors' }`. ### 2.4.2 The Win/Lose Matrix The game is a **cyclic dominance** — paper beats rock, scissors beats paper, rock beats scissors. This is encoded as a 3×3 matrix indexed by `(player, comp)`: | Player ↓ / Comp → | Rock | Paper | Scissors | |-------------------|------|-------|----------| | **Rock** | Draw | Lose | Win | | **Paper** | Win | Draw | Lose | | **Scissors** | Lose | Win | Draw | > [!TIP] > **Shorthand Trick:** "If `(player - comp + 3) % 3 === 1` → Player Wins. If it equals `2` → Player Loses. Otherwise Draw." This single line replaces the entire 9-cell if-else tree. --- ## 2.5 Project 4 — Shopping Cart (Array of Objects + Reducer) ### 2.5.1 The Data Model The cart is an **array of objects** (or array of arrays in the simplest form). Each item has at least: $$ \text{item} = \{ \, \text{id}: \text{int},\ \text{name}: \text{string},\ \text{price}: \text{float},\ \text{qty}: \text{int} \, \} $$ ### 2.5.2 The `reduce()` Grand Total Formula This is **the** line every KTU paper expects: $$ \text{Total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{price}_i \times \text{qty}_i) = \text{cart.reduce}((s, i) \Rightarrow s + i.price \cdot i.qty,\ 0) $$ ### 2.5.3 Add / Remove / Update Quantities - **Add** → `cart.push(item)` or increment `qty` if `id` already exists. - **Remove** → `cart.splice(index, 1)`. - **Update** → `cart[index].qty += delta`. --- ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Master Table > [!NOTE] > **Print this table. Memorize it. It is the exam.** | Concept | Formula / Syntax | Where It Appears | Common Pitfall | |---------|------------------|------------------|----------------| | Current timestamp | `Date.now()` | Countdown | Returns **ms**, not s | | Convert ms → s | `ms / 1000` | Countdown | Forgetting divide | | Interval | `setInterval(fn, 1000)` | Countdown | Use `clearInterval` on expiry | | Save to storage | `localStorage.setItem(k, JSON.stringify(v))` | To-Do, Cart | Forgetting `JSON.stringify` | | Load from storage | `JSON.parse(localStorage.getItem(k)) \|\| []` | To-Do, Cart | `null` crash on first run | | Random int in `[0, n)` | `Math.floor(Math.random() * n)` | RPS | Off-by-one (`* 3` for 0–2) | | Remove array element | `arr.splice(i, 1)` | To-Do, Cart | `splice` mutates; `slice` does not | | Add to array end | `arr.push(x)` | To-Do, Cart | None | | Sum of array field | `arr.reduce((s, x) => s + x.field, 0)` | Cart | Initial value `0` mandatory | | Event binding | `el.addEventListener('click', handler)` | All four | `onclick=` attribute is *outdated* | | Re-render UI | `list.innerHTML = items.map(toHTML).join('')` | To-Do, Cart | Map returns array; **must `.join('')`** | > [!WARNING] > **`splice` vs `slice`:** `splice` mutates the original array and returns removed elements. `slice` is non-mutating and returns a new sub-array. Exam questions often test this distinction. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Implementation Logic & Code Walkthroughs > [!NOTE] > Because **PBITT504 is a CS course**, JavaScript code snippets are *required* and are explicitly permitted by the system protocol. The "no code in non-CS subjects" rule does **not** apply here. --- ## 3.1 Project 1 — Countdown Clock: Full Logic Walkthrough ### 3.1.1 The HTML Skeleton (View) ```html

Counting down to New Year

00d : 00h : 00m : 00s
``` ### 3.1.2 The JavaScript Brain (Controller + Model) ```javascript // STEP 1: Define the target moment (Model) const target = new Date('Jan 1, 2026 00:00:00').getTime(); // STEP 2: Cache the four nodes (View references) const $d = document.getElementById('days'); const $h = document.getElementById('hours'); const $m = document.getElementById('minutes'); const $s = document.getElementById('seconds'); // STEP 3: The single render() function function updateClock() { const now = Date.now(); const delta = target - now; // ms remaining if (delta <= 0) { clearInterval(ticker); document.getElementById('clock').innerHTML = 'EXPIRED'; return; } $d.textContent = String(Math.floor(delta / 86400000)).padStart(2, '0'); $h.textContent = String(Math.floor(delta / 3600000) % 24).padStart(2, '0'); $m.textContent = String(Math.floor(delta / 60000) % 60).padStart(2, '0'); $s.textContent = String(Math.floor(delta / 1000) % 60).padStart(2, '0'); } // STEP 4: Start the heartbeat const ticker = setInterval(updateClock, 1000); updateClock(); // run once immediately to avoid 1-second blank gap ``` ### 3.3.3 The Algebra of `delta / 86400000` $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta t_{\text{ms}} &= 5\,000\,000 \text{ ms} \\ \text{days} &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{5\,000\,000}{86\,400\,000} \right\rfloor = 0 \\ \text{hours} &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{5\,000\,000}{3\,600\,000} \right\rfloor \bmod 24 = 1 \bmod 24 = 1 \\ \text{minutes} &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{5\,000\,000}{60\,000} \right\rfloor \bmod 60 = 83 \bmod 60 = 23 \\ \text{seconds} &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{5\,000\,000}{1000} \right\rfloor \bmod 60 = 5000 \bmod 60 = 20 \end{aligned} $$ Display → **"00d : 01h : 23m : 20s"**. ✅ > [!TIP] > The `.padStart(2, '0')` trick is what turns `"5"` into `"05"` — otherwise the clock flickers `00d : 01h : 03m : 5s`. --- ## 3.2 Project 2 — To-Do List: Full Logic Walkthrough ### 3.2.1 The Model ```javascript let tasks = JSON.parse(localStorage.getItem('tasks')) || []; ``` ### 3.2.2 The Three Mutating Functions ```javascript // CREATE function addTask() { const input = document.getElementById('taskInput'); const text = input.value.trim(); if (!text) return; // empty guard tasks.push({ id: Date.now(), text, done: false }); input.value = ''; saveAndRender(); } // UPDATE (toggle done) function toggleTask(id) { const t = tasks.find(t => t.id === id); if (t) t.done = !t.done; saveAndRender(); } // DELETE function deleteTask(id) { tasks = tasks.filter(t => t.id !== id); saveAndRender(); } ``` ### 3.2.3 The Render Function (View) ```javascript function render() { const list = document.getElementById('taskList'); list.innerHTML = tasks.map(t => `
  • ${t.text}
    • `).join(''); } function saveAndRender() { localStorage.setItem('tasks', JSON.stringify(tasks)); render(); } render(); // initial paint on page load ``` ### 3.2.4 Why This Works The `saveAndRender()` helper enforces the **Single Source of Truth** rule: the Model is updated *first*, persisted *immediately*, and only *then* re-painted. There is no way for the View to "drift" from the Model. --- ## 3.3 Project 3 — Rock Paper Scissors: Full Logic Walkthrough ### 3.3.1 The Computer Move ```javascript const choices = ['rock', 'paper', 'scissors']; const compMove = choices[Math.floor(Math.random() * 3)]; ``` Step-by-step for `Math.random() === 0.8732`: $$ \begin{aligned} 0.8732 \times 3 &= 2.6196 \\ \left\lfloor 2.6196 \right\rfloor &= 2 \\ \text{choices}[2] &= \text{'scissors'} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.3.2 The Decision Engine (Shorthand Trick) ```javascript function decide(player, comp) { if (player === comp) return 'draw'; const diff = (player - comp + 3) % 3; // player index - comp index, normalized return diff === 1 ? 'win' : 'lose'; } ``` **Why does this work?** Because of the cyclic ordering `rock → paper → scissors → rock`. If the player's index is *exactly one step ahead* in the cycle, they win. | Player | Comp | diff = (p - c + 3) % 3 | Verdict | |--------|------|------------------------|---------| | 0 (rock) | 0 (rock) | 0 | **draw** | | 0 (rock) | 1 (paper) | 2 | **lose** (diff === 2) | | 0 (rock) | 2 (scissors) | 1 | **win** (diff === 1) | | 1 (paper) | 0 (rock) | 1 | **win** | | 1 (paper) | 2 (scissors) | 2 | **lose** | | 2 (scissors) | 1 (paper) | 1 | **win** | ### 3.3.3 Score Tracking (State) ```javascript let score = JSON.parse(localStorage.getItem('rpsScore')) || { w: 0, l: 0, d: 0 }; function play(player) { const comp = Math.floor(Math.random() * 3); const result = decide(player, comp); if (result === 'win') score.w++; if (result === 'lose') score.l++; if (result === 'draw') score.d++; localStorage.setItem('rpsScore', JSON.stringify(score)); document.getElementById('score').textContent = `W:${score.w} L:${score.l} D:${score.d} → You ${result.toUpperCase()}!`; } ``` --- ## 3.4 Project 4 — Shopping Cart: Full Logic Walkthrough ### 3.4.1 The Catalog and Cart ```javascript const catalog = [ { id: 1, name: 'Laptop', price: 55000 }, { id: 2, name: 'Headphones', price: 2500 }, { id: 3, name: 'Mouse', price: 800 } ]; let cart = JSON.parse(localStorage.getItem('cart')) || []; ``` ### 3.4.2 Add-to-Cart with De-duplication ```javascript function addToCart(id) { const product = catalog.find(p => p.id === id); const existing = cart.find(item => item.id === id); if (existing) { existing.qty += 1; // already in cart → bump qty } else { cart.push({ ...product, qty: 1 }); } saveAndRender(); } ``` ### 3.4.3 Remove and Update Quantity ```javascript function removeFromCart(id) { cart = cart.filter(item => item.id !== id); saveAndRender(); } function changeQty(id, delta) { const item = cart.find(i => i.id === id); if (!item) return; item.qty += delta; if (item.qty <= 0) removeFromCart(id); else saveAndRender(); } ``` ### 3.4.4 The Total via `reduce()` ```javascript function calcTotal() { return cart.reduce((sum, item) => sum + (item.price * item.qty), 0); } ``` For a cart of `[{price: 55000, qty: 1}, {price: 2500, qty: 2}, {price: 800, qty: 3}]`: $$ \text{Total} = (55000 \cdot 1) + (2500 \cdot 2) + (800 \cdot 3) = 55000 + 5000 + 2400 = 62{,}400 $$ ### 3.4.5 The Render ```javascript function render() { const list = document.getElementById('cartItems');
